Introduction and Front Matter
beginner
5 min
The Author's Opening Supplication and Intentions
The author invokes Allah and declares his intentions for studying and teaching.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the significance of the author's opening prayer and the multiple intentions behind studying and teaching this commentary.

Core Idea
A scholar's work begins with sincere intention and a prayer for divine assistance.

In Islamic tradition, every important undertaking begins with a supplication and a clear declaration of purpose. The author of this commentary opens with a heartfelt prayer that acknowledges Allah's omniscience and enumerates the noble intentions that drive his scholarly work. This lesson unpacks the layers of that prayer, revealing the spiritual and pedagogical framework that shapes the entire commentary.

Guided Path
1
Read the prayer carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyze it. Then read the translation to grasp its comprehensive scope.
Notice the reverent tone and the listing of intentions.
2
Acknowledge Allah's omniscience
The author begins by presenting himself before Allah 'before every breath, glance, and twinkling of the eye.' Reflect on what this profound acknowledgment teaches about the author's state of mind.
This sets the spiritual context for the entire work.
3
Identify the intentions
After 'I intend' (نويت), the author lists multiple purposes. Find each one and think about how they connect to the study of 'Aqeedah.
Examples: seeking Allah, spreading knowledge, reviving the Shariah.
4
Reflect on the chain of knowledge
The author mentions entering the 'chain of knowledge' and being counted among conveyers of revelation. Consider how this shows the communal and transmitted nature of Islamic scholarship.
This connects the author to the Prophet and the Ummah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
اللهم إني أقدم إليك بين يدي كل نفس ولمحة وطرفة يعرف بها أهل السماوات وأهل الأرض، وكل شيء هو في علمك كائن أو قد كان. أقدم لك بين يدي ذلك كله.. نويت بالتعلم وجد الله تعالى، ونشر العلم، وتعليمه، وبث الفوائد الشرعية، وتبليغ أحكام الله تعالى، والازدياد من العلم، وإحياء الشرع الشريف، ودوام ظهور الحق، وتدمير الباطل، وإظهار الصواب والرجوع إلى الحق، والاجتماع على ذكر الله تعالى، والدعاء للمسلمين، وللسلف الصالحين، ودوام خير الأمة، بكثرة علمائها، واعتناء ثوابهم، وتحصيل ثواب من ينتهي إليه هذا العلم، وبركة دعائهم لي وترحمهم علي، ودخولي في سلسلة العلم بين رسول الله صلى الله عليه وآله وصحبه وسلم، وبينهم، وعدادي في جملة مبلغي الوحي، وأحكامه، وإزالة الجهل عن نفسي وعن غيري اللهم تعالى.
O Allah, I present to You before every breath, glance, and twinkling of the eye by which the inhabitants of the heavens and the inhabitants of the earth know, and everything that in Your knowledge is coming to be or has already been. I present to You before all of that... I intend by learning the finding of Allah Almighty, and spreading knowledge, and teaching it, and disseminating religious benefits, and conveying the rulings of Allah Almighty, and increasing in knowledge, and reviving the noble Shariah, and the permanence of the appearance of truth, and the destruction of falsehood, and manifesting correctness and returning to truth, and gathering upon the remembrance of Allah Almighty, and supplication for the Muslims, and for the righteous predecessors, and the continuance of the good of the Ummah through the abundance of its scholars, and cherishing their rewards, and attaining the reward of those to whom this knowledge reaches, and the blessing of their supplication for me and their mercy upon me, and my entry into the chain of knowledge between the Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and grant him peace and his family and companions) and them, and my being counted among the conveyers of revelation and its rulings, and the removal of ignorance from myself and from others, O Allah Almighty.
Source pages p. 5

Commentary
This passage is a personal invocation and declaration of intention by the author or commentator at the beginning of the work. It begins with a profound prayer acknowledging Allah's omniscience over all moments and beings, then lists a series of sincere intentions for engaging in this scholarly endeavor. The intentions include seeking closeness to Allah, spreading and teaching knowledge, reviving the Shariah, supporting truth, and benefiting the Muslim community. The author also seeks to be connected to the chain of transmission from the Prophet and to be counted among those who convey divine revelation and its rulings. The passage reflects the traditional Islamic practice of beginning any important task with a supplication and a clear statement of pious purpose.

Key Terms
نويت
جد الله تعالى
نشر العلم
إحياء الشرع
سلسلة العلم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think this prayer is merely formulaic and does not reflect genuine intention.
Correction: The author's careful choice of words and the comprehensive list of intentions show deep spiritual commitment and a clear pedagogical purpose, setting the tone for the entire commentary.

Checkpoint
What is the primary purpose of the author's opening prayer and intention?
A) To show off his eloquence
B) To seek divine assistance and express sincere intentions for learning and teaching
C) To list all his teachers
D) To criticize other scholars

Key Takeaway
The author's prayer encapsulates the sincere, community-oriented spirit that should accompany all Islamic learning.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Before you open any book or begin any lesson, do you pause to clarify your intention to Allah? What is one intention from the author's prayer that you have never consciously made? Will you make it right now?
?
The author seeks to be 'counted among the conveyers of revelation.' When you learn, do you see yourself as a link in a chain passing on truth to others, or do you learn only for yourself? How would your learning change if you embraced this responsibility?
?
This prayer includes 'removal of ignorance from myself and from others.' Where in your life have you been content with ignorance—about your faith, your duties, or your Lord? What specific step will you take today to remove it?

Editor's Methodology and Corrections
Editor explains corrections and additions in his edition.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the careful methodology used by the editor to correct and enhance the present edition of al-Bahja al-Saniyya.

Core Idea
Scholarly editions are built on honest correction, clear annotation, and transparent methods.

Before diving into the theological content of al-Bahja al-Saniyya, it is helpful to understand how this particular edition was prepared. The editor explains his approach to correcting errors and filling gaps in the original Indonesian print, relying on the foundational commentary by Ahmad al-Dardir. This preface gives us a window into the scholarly care that underlies the text you are about to study.

Guided Path
1
Read the editor's preface
Begin by reading the full Arabic passage and its translation to grasp the editor's overall process.
2
Identify the key methodological steps
Look for the four main actions the editor took: relying on an old edition, correcting errors, supplying omissions, and adding a biography and layout improvements.
These steps form the backbone of the editorial methodology.
3
Understand the annotation symbols
Note how the editor uses the margin for errors and brackets for omissions. This is a standard scholarly practice.
Brackets [] indicate inserted text.
4
Reflect on the purpose
Ask yourself why the editor goes into such detail. The goal is transparency and trustworthiness.
This connects to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد اعتمدتُ في طبعتي هذه على طبعة قديمة طبعت في إندونيسيا، وقد وقعت لهم أخطاء وبعض سقط استدركته هنا، وقد ساعدني على استدراكه فضل الله تعالى، وعنايتي بـ«شرح الخريدة لسيدي أبي البركات أحمد الدردير، رَضِوَاللَّهُ عَنْهُ. أما الأخطاء فنبهت على الأهم منها في مواضعها، ذاكرًا الصواب في الأصل، ومحشيّا بالخطأ في الهامش. وأما مواضع السقط فزدتها في الأصل بين معقوفتين هكذا []، وقد أفدتها من شرح الخريدة» لسيدي أحمد الدردير، ومن مراجعة الكتب التي اعتمد عليها الشارح رحمة الله وقدَّمتُ العمل بترجمة موجزة للشيخ اقتبستها مما كتبه تلميذه وخريجه معالي الأستاذ الدكتور عبد الوهاب إبراهيم أبو سليمان، حفظه الله، عضو هيئة كبار العلماء بالمملكة العربية السعودية. ومما غيّرتُه في هذه الطبعة عن سابقتها أن جعلت أبيات الخريدة في أعلى الصفحات مع تمييز ما يشرحه المصنف بالخط الأسود الثقيل. وقد علقت تعليقات يسيرة استفدتها من شرح الخريدة البهية»، ومن التعليقات عليه. وفي الختام أسأل الله تعالى أن أكون قد وفقت في عملي، كما أسأله أن يجعله وسائر أعمالي خالصا لوجهه الكريم، وأن يرزقني علمًا نافعًا، ورزقا طيبًا واسعا، إنه على ذلك قدير وبالإجابة جدير. وصل اللهم على سيدنا محمد وعلى آله وصحبه وسلم.
And I relied in this edition of mine on an old edition printed in Indonesia, and they had errors and some omissions that I corrected here, and the grace of Allah Almighty helped me to correct them, and my attention to [the commentary] 'Sharh al-Kharida' by my master Abi al-Barakat Ahmad al-Dardir, may Allah be pleased with him. As for the errors, I alerted to the most important of them in their places, mentioning the correct in the original, and annotating with the error in the margin. And as for the places of omission, I added them in the original between brackets like this [], and I benefited from 'Sharh al-Kharida' by my master Ahmad al-Dardir, and from reviewing the books on which the commentator relied, may Allah have mercy on him. And I prefaced the work with a brief biography of the Shaykh, which I quoted from what was written by his student and graduate, His Excellency Professor Dr. Abd al-Wahhab Ibrahim Abu Sulayman, may Allah preserve him, member of the Council of Senior Scholars in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. And among what I changed in this edition over its predecessor is that I placed the verses of al-Kharida at the top of the pages, distinguishing what the author explains with thick black font. And I added brief comments that I benefited from 'Sharh al-Kharida al-Bahiyya' and from the comments on it. And in conclusion, I ask Allah Almighty that I have succeeded in my work, and I ask Him to make it and all my deeds sincere for His noble face, and to grant me beneficial knowledge and good abundant provision; indeed, He is powerful over that and worthy of answering. And may Allah bless our Master Muhammad and his family and companions and grant them peace.
Source pages p. 7

Commentary
This passage is an editorial preface explaining the methodology used in preparing this edition. The editor states that he based his work on an old Indonesian edition, which contained errors and omissions. He corrected these with Allah's help and by referring to al-Dardir's commentary 'Sharh al-Kharida'. For errors, he noted the correct reading in the text and the error in the margin; for omissions, he inserted the missing text in brackets. He also included a short biography of the author (al-Mashshāt) taken from a work by one of the author's students. A key change from the previous edition is that the editor now places the verses of the original poem (al-Kharida) at the top of each page, highlighting in bold black the portions of the poem that the commentary discusses. The editor also added brief annotations drawn from al-Dardir's commentary and other sources. The passage concludes with a prayer for acceptance and blessing.

Key Terms
طبعة قديمة
سقط
شرح الخريدة
معقوفتين
هامش
أصل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The editor rewrote the text entirely.
Correction: The editor only corrected errors and supplied omissions, preserving the original content and structure.

Checkpoint
What method did the editor use to indicate errors in the old edition?
A) He deleted errors and replaced them with correct text.
B) He noted the correct reading in the original and the error in the margin.
C) He added a footnote explaining the error without marking the original.
D) He ignored errors that were not important.

Key Takeaway
The editor's methodology ensures transparency and fidelity to the original while correcting errors and omissions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The editor thanks Allah for helping him correct errors. When you make a mistake in your understanding of the Deen, do you humbly seek correction from reliable sources, or do you stubbornly cling to your error? What step will you take this week to verify a piece of Islamic knowledge you currently hold?
?
The editor meticulously distinguishes his additions from the original. When you convey Islamic teachings, do you clearly separate your own opinions from the transmitted texts, or do you mix them carelessly? How can you ensure you are not adding or omitting unintentionally?
?
The editor relies on a specific commentary (Sharh al-Kharida) and other works. In your pursuit of religious knowledge, do you rely on primary scholarly works and qualified teachers, or do you pick from random online sources? Will you commit to learning from a recognized text and teacher starting today?

Introduction and Front Matter
beginner
4 min
The Author's Introduction and Purpose
The author introduces himself and states his purpose.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the author's humble approach and his sincere intention in writing this commentary for the benefit of others and for his own afterlife reward.

Core Idea
A scholar's work is an act of hope and worship, seeking divine reward.

Every great book begins with an introduction that reveals the author's heart. Here, Hasan al-Mashat opens his commentary with humility and a clear purpose: to serve the readers and store up treasure for the Day of Judgment. Though the text is damaged, his sincere tone still shines through.

Guided Path
1
Read the author's self-introduction
Notice how he describes himself as 'al-faqir al-rajī'—the poor one, the hopeful. This sets a tone of humility.
Even in corruption, the key phrase is clear.
2
Identify his stated purpose
He hopes for benefit for the servants (readers) and that the commentary becomes a provision for the Hereafter.
This is not just a scholarly exercise; it is an act of worship.
3
Reflect on the blessings invoked
Note the blessings on the Prophet and the reliance on Allah. The author frames his work within a spiritual context.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(أما بعد) فيقول الفقير الراجي الفوز على الصراط المستقيم حسن محمد [؟] المشاط كان الله له وأصلح في الدارين [؟] [؟] منظومة [؟] [؟] منظومة مباركة [؟] [؟] [؟] [؟] [؟] [؟] [؟] أرجو الله تعالى به نفع العباد وأن يكون في ذخيرة يوم المعاد [؟] وبالله اعتمادي وصلى الله وسلم على سيدنا [؟] في إصلاحه وما قصدت إلا اقترابه آمين.
(To proceed): The poor one, hoping for success on the straight path, Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat – may Allah be with him and rectify his affairs in both worlds – [unclear] [unclear] poem [unclear] [unclear] blessed poem [unclear] [unclear] [unclear] I hope from Allah Almighty benefit for the servants and that it be a provision for the Day of Return [unclear] and upon Allah is my reliance, and may Allah bless and grant peace to our master [unclear] in its correction, and I intended only its drawing near. Amen.
Source pages p. 12

Commentary
This passage is the author's introduction following the basmalah and hamdalah. It begins with 'Amma ba'd' (to proceed), a conventional transition. The author identifies himself as 'al-faqir al-rajī' (the poor one, the hopeful) and states his name: Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat, adding a prayer for divine aid. He then mentions that this work is a commentary on a blessed poem (al-Kharidat al-bahiyya), though the exact phrasing is unclear due to OCR corruption. He explicitly states his hope that Allah will grant benefit to readers through this commentary and that it will be a stored treasure for the Day of Reckoning. The introduction concludes with a prayer of reliance on Allah and blessings on the Prophet, affirming that his only aim was to make the poem accessible. Despite the poor OCR, the author's humble tone and pedagogical purpose are evident.

Key Terms
الفقير الراجي
الصراط المستقيم
ذخيرة يوم المعاد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The author's introduction is merely a formulaic opening with no real personal meaning.
Correction: The author explicitly states his sincere hope for divine reward and benefit to readers, showing his introduction is an expression of his spiritual intent, not just a formality.

Checkpoint
What does the author hope for from writing this commentary?
A) Fame and recognition
B) Financial gain
C) Benefit for the servants (readers) and reward in the afterlife
D) To criticize other scholars

Key Takeaway
The author's introduction reveals his humble reliance on Allah and his sincere hope that his commentary benefits readers and brings him reward in the afterlife.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you begin any act of learning, do you consciously intend it as a means of drawing closer to Allah and benefiting others, or is it merely for personal ambition? Will you pause before your next study session to renew that intention?
?
The author calls himself 'the poor one, the hopeful'—do you truly feel your spiritual poverty before Allah in your daily life, or do you rely on your own knowledge and deeds? How can you cultivate that awareness of need today?
?
If this very lesson you are reading were to be your only provision on the Day of Return, would it be enough? What concrete step can you take now to ensure your learning is stored as a treasure for that day?

Introduction and Front Matter
beginner
4 min
Author's Introduction to Al-Kharidat al-Bahiyya
al-Dardir opens his creedal poem with praise, blessings, and introduction.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the structure and purpose of the introductory verses of al-Dardir's creedal poem.

Core Idea
A small poem can contain great knowledge if it distills the essence.

This lesson opens the study of Al-Kharidat al-Bahiyya by introducing the author, Imam Ahmad al-Dardir, and his poetic creed. The verses you are about to read are not just praise; they establish the author's humility, the poem's name, and its claim to be a concise yet comprehensive summary of Islamic theology. Approach this passage as a window into the author's intention.

Guided Path
1
Read the opening verses
Read the Arabic text and translation carefully, noting the flow from basmala to praise to blessings to introduction.
Notice the rhyme and meter of the poem.
2
Identify the author's self-description
Find how al-Dardir describes himself in the first line and what that tells you about his attitude.
He calls himself 'the one hoping for mercy'.
3
Understand the poem's title and description
The author names the poem and describes it as small but rich. Reflect on what this means for the study ahead.
The title 'Al-Kharidat al-Bahiyya' means 'The Splendid Virgin Pearl'.
4
Note the closing prayer
The author ends with a hope for acceptance and forgiveness. Consider what this reveals about the purpose of the work.
This is a common pattern in Islamic scholarly works.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم
يَقُولُ رَاجِي رَحْمَةَ القَدِيرِ * أَيْ أَحْمَدُ الْمَشْهُورُ بالدَّرْدِيرِ
الْحَمْدُ للهِ العَلِيِّ الوَاحِدِ * العَالِمِ الفَرْدِ الغَنِيُّ الْمَاجِدِ
وَأَفْضَلُ الصَّلاةِ والتَّسْلِيم * عَلَى النَّبِيِّ الْمُصْطَفَى الكَريم
وَآلِهِ وَصَحْبِهِ الأَطْهَارَ * لَا سِيَّمَا رَفِيقِهِ فِي الغَارِ
وَهَذِهِ عَقِيدَةٌ سَنِيَّة * سَمَّيْتُهَا الْخَرِيدَةَ البَهِيـ
لَطِيفَةٌ صَغِيرَةٌ فِي الْحَجْمِ * لَكِنَّهَا كَبِيرَةٌ فِي العِلم
تَكْفِيكَ عِلْمًا إِنْ تُرِدْ أَنْ تَكْتَفِي * لأَنَّهَا بِزُبْدَةِ الفَنِّ تَفِي
وَالله أَرْجُو فِي قَبولِ العَمَلِ * وَالنَّفْعَ مِنْهَا ثُمَّ غَفْرَ الزَّلَل
In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. Says the one hoping for the mercy of the Powerful: i.e., Ahmad, known as al-Dardir. Praise be to Allah, the High, the One, the All-Knowing, the Unique, the Self-Sufficient, the Glorious. And the best of prayer and peace be upon the chosen, noble Prophet, and upon his pure family and companions, especially his companion in the cave. And this is a sound creed; I have named it 'Al-Kharidat al-Bahiyya (The Splendid Virgin Pearl).' Delicate, small in size, but great in knowledge. It suffices you with knowledge if you wish to be content, for it fulfills with the cream of the art. And I hope from Allah acceptance of the deed, benefit from it, then forgiveness of slips.
Source pages p. 13

Commentary
This passage is the opening of the creedal poem (al-Kharidat al-Bahiyya). The author, Ahmad al-Dardir, begins by invoking the Basmala and identifying himself as the one hoping for God's mercy. He praises God with attributes of majesty and sends blessings upon the Prophet Muhammad, his family, and especially Abu Bakr (the companion in the cave). He then introduces his work, calling it a 'sound creed' and naming it 'al-Kharidat al-Bahiyya.' He notes that although it is small in size, it contains great knowledge, being a condensed summary of the essence of the discipline. He concludes by expressing hope that God will accept his effort, grant benefit, and forgive any shortcomings.

Key Terms
الخريدة البهية
الدردير

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What does the author say about the size of his creedal poem?
A) It is large in both size and knowledge.
B) It is small in size but great in knowledge.
C) It is medium-sized but contains little knowledge.
D) He does not mention its size.

Key Takeaway
Al-Kharidat al-Bahiyya is a small poem that claims to contain the essence of Islamic creed, authored by Ahmad al-Dardir with humility and hope for divine acceptance.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you open a book of knowledge, do you start with the same spirit as al-Dardir—seeking acceptance and fearing slips—or do you rush ahead without acknowledging your need for God’s forgiveness? How can you change your intention today?
?
al-Dardir calls his small poem 'great in knowledge.' Have you ever dismissed a concise work of faith as too simple or shallow? Will you now commit to approaching summaries with the reverence they deserve, recognizing they may contain the cream of the art?
?
The author praises Allah with specific names: al-Ali (the High), al-Wahid (the One), al-Ghani (the Self-Sufficient). Which of these names challenges the way you currently live? What would change if you truly felt that Allah is self-sufficient and you are not?

Introduction and Front Matter
beginner
4 min
Opening of al-Kharida al-Bahiyya
The author invokes Allah, describes his poem al-Kharida al-Bahiyya and its purpose.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the author's purpose and the nature of his poem al-Kharida al-Bahiyya as a concise yet comprehensive Sunni creed.

Core Idea
A small poem can contain the essence of a great science.

Imam al-Dardir opens his creedal poem with praise of Allah and the Prophet, and introduces his work. This lesson explores the opening verses to understand the author's intent and the unique value of this concise text.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the English translation to grasp the overall meaning.
Notice the poetic rhyme and rhythm.
2
Identify the author's self-introduction
The author mentions himself as 'hoping for the mercy of the All-Powerful' and identifies as Ahmad al-Dardir. This shows his humility and reliance on Allah.
3
Examine the praise of Allah and the Prophet
He praises Allah with several attributes (e.g., al-'Ali, al-Wahid) and sends blessings on the Prophet, his family, and especially Abu Bakr.
These attributes are foundational in Islamic theology.
4
Analyze the description of the poem
He names the poem 'al-Kharida al-Bahiyya', describes it as small in size but great in knowledge, and says it suffices because it contains the 'cream of the art'.
This paradox emphasizes efficiency.
5
Reflect on the concluding supplication
He ends by hoping for Allah's acceptance of his work, benefit, and forgiveness of slips. This shows his sincerity and awareness of human fallibility.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
يَقُولُ رَاجِي رَحْمَةَ القَدِيرِ * أَي أَحْمَدُ الْمَشْهُورُ بالدَّرْدِيرِ * الْحَمْدُ للهِ العَلِيِّ الوَاحِدِ * العَالِمِ الفَرْدِ الغَنِيِّ الْمَاجِدِ * وَأَفْضَلُ الصَّلاةِ والتَّسْلِيم * عَلَى النَّبِيِّ الْمُصْطَفَى الكَريم * وَآلِهِ وَصَحْبِهِ الأَطْهَارَ * لَا سِيَّمَا رَفِيقِهِ فِي الغَارِ * وَهَذِهِ عَقِيدَةٌ سَنِيَّة * سَمَّيْتُهَا الْخَرِيدَةَ البَهِيَّةَ * لَطِيفَةٌ صَغِيرَةٌ فِي الْحَجْمِ * لَكِنَّهَا كَبِيرَةٌ فِي العِلم * تَكْفِيكَ عِلْمًا إِنْ تُرِدْ أَنْ تَكْتَفِي * لأَنَّهَا بِزُبْدَةِ الفَنِّ تَفِي * والله أَرْجُو فِي قَبولِ العَمَلِ * والنَّفْعَ مِنْهَا ثُمَّ غَفْرَ الزَّلَل
He says, hoping for the mercy of the All-Powerful, that is Ahmad known as al-Dardir: Praise be to Allah, the High, the One, the Knower, the Unique, the Rich, the Glorious. And the best of prayers and peace be upon the chosen noble Prophet, and upon his pure family and companions, especially his companion in the cave. And this is a Sunni creed; I have named it al-Kharida al-Bahiyya. It is delicate, small in size, but great in knowledge. It suffices you with knowledge if you wish to be satisfied, because it is abundant with the cream of the art. And I hope from Allah for acceptance of the deed, and benefit from it, and then forgiveness of slips.
Source pages p. 14

Commentary
This passage is the opening section of the poem. It begins with the author mentioning himself as 'hoping for the mercy of the All-Powerful' and identifies himself as Ahmad al-Dardir. He then praises Allah with attributes (al-'Ali, al-Wahid, al-'Alim, al-Fard, al-Ghani, al-Majid) and sends blessings upon the Prophet Muhammad, his family, and companions, specifically mentioning Abu Bakr as the companion in the cave. Next, he names and describes his poem: 'al-Kharida al-Bahiyya' (the brilliant virgin pearl), stating that despite its small size, it contains great knowledge and suffices the reader as it contains the essence of the art (the science of kalam). He concludes by supplicating to Allah for acceptance of his work, benefit, and forgiveness of mistakes.

Key Terms
الخريدة البهية
عقيدة سنية
زبدة الفن

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: A small poem cannot contain deep theology.
Correction: The poem deliberately condenses the essence of kalam into a concise form; its size belies its depth.

Checkpoint
What does the author name his creedal poem?
A) al-Durra al-Bahiyya
B) al-Kharida al-Bahiyya
C) al-‘Aqida al-Sunniyya
D) al-Bahja al-Saniyya

Key Takeaway
Al-Dardir's al-Kharida al-Bahiyya is a concise yet comprehensive Sunni creed, and he hopes for divine acceptance.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have in your hands a poem that claims to contain the cream of the art. Are you content to pass over its verses lightly, or will you wrestle with each line until it reshapes your understanding of Allah?
?
Al-Dardir hopes for acceptance of his deed. When you teach or share this creed, do you feel the same urgency for Allah's acceptance, or do you treat it as mere academic exercise?
?
The poem is small in size but great in knowledge. What does that say about your own attention span? Will you allow the brevity to deceive you into shallow learning?

Introduction to the Commentary on Al-Kharidat al-Bahiyya
The commentator praises Tawhid and introduces his commentary.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the purpose and features of Shaykh Hasan al-Mashat's commentary on al-Dardir's poem 'Al-Kharidat al-Bahiyya'.

Core Idea
The science of Tawhid is the noblest, and this commentary makes it accessible.

This lesson introduces you to the opening of Shaykh Hasan al-Mashat's commentary. The commentator begins by praising Allah and affirming the high status of the science of Tawhid. He then introduces the famous poem 'Al-Kharidat al-Bahiyya' and explains the distinctive features of his own commentary, which is designed for beginners.

Guided Path
1
Read the opening praise
Read the Arabic passage from the beginning down to 'وبعد'. Notice the traditional formula of praise and blessing.
2
Understand the importance of Tawhid
The commentator states that Tawhid is the noblest science. Identify why: it is connected to Allah and His messengers, and it teaches the three rational judgments.
These judgments are: necessary, impossible, and possible.
3
Identify the poem and its commentator
Find the name of the poem, its author, and the name of the commentator whose work this is. Note the date of completion.
The date is 1385 AH.
4
Note the commentary's features
The commentator describes his work as concise, easy, clear, and with vocalization. Reflect on why these features are beneficial for beginners.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الحمد لله رب العالمين، تفضل علينا بنعم لا تعد ولا تحصى وأولها نعمة معرفة أصول الدين، والصلاة والسلام على رسوله الأمين الذي أرسله تعالى رحمة للخلق أجمعين، وأظهر دينه القويم على كل دين وعلى آله وأصحابه الطيبين الطاهرين والتابعين بإحسان المجاهدين لكل ضال غاو مبين. وبعد، فإن علم التوحيد من أشرف العلوم وأعلاها قدرًا لتعلقه بالله تعالى وبرسله الكرام عليهم الصلاة والسلام فبه يعرف الطالب ما يجب في حق مولانا تعالى وما يستحيل، وما يجوز، وما يجب في حق رسله عليهم الصلاة والسلام، وما يستحيل وما يجوز. ومن أحسن المنظومات المؤلفة في هذا الفن وأوفاها ، وأكثرها شهرة بين أيدي الطلاب منظومة الخريدة البهية» لسيدي الإمام العلامة الرباني أبي البركات أحمد ابن محمد العدوي المالكي المشهور بالدردير رَضَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُ ، وقد شرحها الناظم نفسه رحمه الله، وحشى عليه جمع من طلابه ومن علماء الأزهر الأنور. وكان من جملة الشروح على هذا النظم المبارك شرح شيخ مشايخنا العلامة الفقيه القاضي سيدي حسن المشاط المكي المالكي رَحِمَهُ اللهُ، وقد انتهى الشيخ من تأليفه سنة ١٣٨٥ من الهجرة النبوية. وهو شرح مختصر يصلح للمبتدئين في هذا الفن راعى فيه الشيخ سهولة الأسلوب، ووضوح العبارة، وضبط الكلمات الصعبة، وهي ميزة لا توجد في أصله، فجاء لبنة أصيلة ركينة في صرح هذا العلم.
Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds, Who has bestowed upon us blessings that cannot be counted or enumerated, the first of which is the blessing of knowing the foundations of religion. And prayers and peace be upon His trustworthy Messenger, whom He sent as a mercy to all creation, and manifested His straight religion above all religions, and upon his family and his good and pure Companions, and the Followers in righteousness, the fighters against every manifest erring misleader. To proceed: Indeed, the science of Tawhid (monotheism) is the noblest of sciences and the highest in rank because of its connection to Allah Almighty and His noble messengers, peace and blessings be upon them. Through it, the student knows what is necessary (wajib) with respect to our Master Almighty, what is impossible (mustahil), and what is possible (ja'iz); and what is necessary with respect to His messengers, what is impossible, and what is possible. Among the best and most complete didactic poems composed in this field, and the most famous among students, is the poem 'Al-Kharidat al-Bahiyya' by my master, the Imam, the erudite, the divine, Abu al-Barakat Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-'Adawi al-Maliki, known as al-Dardir, may Allah be pleased with him. He himself (the poet) explained it, may Allah have mercy on him, and a group of his students and scholars of al-Azhar al-Anwar wrote glosses upon it. Among the commentaries on this blessed poem is the commentary of the shaykh of our shaykhs, the erudite jurist and judge, my master Hasan al-Mashat al-Makki al-Maliki, may Allah have mercy on him. The shaykh completed its authorship in the year 1385 of the Prophetic Hijra. It is a concise commentary suitable for beginners in this field; the shaykh took care in it of ease of style, clarity of expression, and the vocalization of difficult words—a feature not found in its original. Thus it came as a solid, foundational brick in the edifice of this science.
Source pages p. 6

Commentary
This passage is the introduction to the commentary. It begins with the traditional praise of Allah and blessings upon the Prophet, then immediately asserts the supreme status of the science of Tawhid (theology) because it deals with knowledge of Allah and His messengers. The commentator notes that this science teaches the student the three rational judgments regarding Allah (necessity, impossibility, and possibility) and the same three regarding the prophets. He then introduces the famous poem 'Al-Kharidat al-Bahiyya' by Imam al-Dardir, stating that the poet himself wrote a commentary and many scholars added glosses. Among the commentaries is the one by Shaykh Hasan al-Mashat, the author of this present work. He provides the date of completion (1385 AH) and highlights the commentary's features: it is concise, easy in style, clear in expression, and includes vowelization of difficult words—qualities that make it ideal for beginners. He concludes by metaphorically calling it a solid brick in the building of this science.

Key Terms
علم التوحيد
الخريدة البهية
الدردير
حسن المشاط
شرح مختصر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think the commentary is only a summary of al-Dardir's work, but it is actually a unique commentary with added features like vocalization not found in the original.
Correction: The commentary is not just a summary; it includes ease of style, clarity, and vocalization of difficult words, which are not present in the original poem.

Checkpoint
According to the introduction, what does the science of Tawhid enable the student to know?
A) Only the obligatory duties
B) The necessary, impossible, and possible regarding Allah and His messengers
C) The history of Islamic sects
D) The details of Islamic law

Key Takeaway
The science of Tawhid is the foundation of Islamic belief, and this commentary by Shaykh Hasan al-Mashat provides an accessible entry point for beginners.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The commentator calls the knowledge of the foundations of religion the first blessing. When did you last pause to thank Allah specifically for teaching you about Him? Will you do that today after this lesson?
?
If Tawhid is the noblest science because it connects you directly to Allah, how much of your study time is actually devoted to it compared to other subjects? What would it look like to truly prioritize it?
?
This commentary was written for beginners. Are you willing to admit you are a beginner in Tawhid? What one concrete step will you take this week to study it with the humility and seriousness it deserves?

Praise of Al-Kharida al-Bahiyya and Its Commentary
Praise of a poem and its commentary for beginners.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the significance of Al-Kharida al-Bahiyya in the study of tawhid and the distinctive features of al-Mashat's commentary that make it ideal for beginners.

Core Idea
Al-Kharida al-Bahiyya is a foundational poem; al-Mashat's commentary unlocks it for beginners.

This passage introduces us to a celebrated poem in the Islamic creed, Al-Kharida al-Bahiyya, and praises its commentary by Shaykh Hasan al-Mashat. The author emphasizes the poem's completeness and popularity, then highlights how the commentary makes the content accessible through clear style and explanations. Let's explore this passage to appreciate its role in the traditional learning path.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then its translation. Notice the flow of praise and the introduction of the commentary.
The author uses terms like 'أحسن' and 'أوفاها' to indicate superlative praise.
2
Identify the main elements
Identify the poem's author, its title, and the commentator. Note the qualities attributed to each work.
The poem is by al-Dardir; the commentary is by al-Mashat.
3
Understand the commentary's features
Focus on the description of al-Mashat's commentary: it is concise, easy in style, clear in expression, and fixes difficult words. Consider why these features are important for beginners.
The passage says these features are 'a merit not found in the original'.
4
Reflect on the educational value
Think about why the author calls the commentary 'a solid foundational brick.' How does this commentary serve the science of tawhid?
This connects to the traditional method of building knowledge step by step.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومن أحسن المنظومات المؤلفة في هذا الفن وأوفاها ، وأكثرها شهرة بين أيدي الطلاب منظومة الخريدة البهية» لسيدي الإمام العلامة الرباني أبي البركات أحمد ابن محمد العدوي المالكي المشهور بالدردير رَضَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُ ، وقد شرحها الناظم نفسه رحمه الله، وحشى عليه جمع من طلابه ومن علماء الأزهر الأنور. وكان من جملة الشروح على هذا النظم المبارك شرح شيخ مشايخنا العلامة الفقيه القاضي سيدي حسن المشاط المكي المالكي رَحِمَهُ اللهُ، وقد انتهى الشيخ من تأليفه سنة ١٣٨٥ من الهجرة النبوية. وهو شرح مختصر يصلح للمبتدئين في هذا الفن راعى فيه الشيخ سهولة الأسلوب، ووضوح العبارة، وضبط الكلمات الصعبة، وهي ميزة لا توجد في أصله، فجاء لبنة أصيلة ركينة في صرح هذا العلم.
And among the best versified works composed in this art, the most complete and the most famous among students is the versified work 'Al-Kharida al-Bahiyya' by my master, the imam, the all-knowing saint, Abu al-Barakat Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-‘Adawi al-Maliki, known as al-Dardir, may Allah be pleased with him. The author himself explained it, may Allah have mercy on him, and a group of his students and scholars of al-Azhar al-Anwar wrote glosses upon it. Among the commentaries on this blessed poem is the commentary of the teacher of our teachers, the all-knowing jurist, the judge, my master Hasan al-Mashat al-Makki al-Maliki, may Allah have mercy on him. The shaykh finished composing it in the year 1385 of the Prophet's hijra. It is a concise commentary suitable for beginners in this art; the shaykh observed in it ease of style, clarity of expression, and fixation of difficult words—a merit not found in the original work. Thus it became a solid foundational brick in the edifice of this science.
Source pages p. 6

Commentary
This passage praises the poem 'Al-Kharida al-Bahiyya' by Ahmad al-Dardir, calling it one of the best and most complete works in the field of tawhid, very popular among students. The author himself wrote a commentary, and many scholars wrote glosses. Among the commentaries, the one by Hasan al-Mashat is highlighted. It is described as concise, suitable for beginners, with easy style, clear expression, and explanations of difficult words—features not found in the original. The passage concludes that this commentary serves as a solid foundation for the science.

The Commentator's Purpose: Making Tawhid Accessible
Al-Mashat introduces himself, praises God, and explains his motivation for writing this commentary.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the commentator's motivation and method in writing this abridged commentary on al-Dardir's poem 'al-Kharida al-Bahiyya'.

Core Idea
Commentary is an act of devotion and accessibility in a time of declining knowledge.

The commentary begins with a classical invocation and then reveals the author's heart: a humble scholar seeing the need to revive interest in theology. This introduction sets the stage for the entire work, showing us that the author writes not for fame but for benefit and proximity to God.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and note its structure
The passage follows the traditional format: basmala, praise of God, blessings on the Prophet, self-introduction, and the reason for writing. Identify each part.
This structure is a hallmark of Islamic scholarly works.
2
Identify the key problem and solution
Al-Mashat observes that despite al-Dardir's excellent poem and commentary, ambitions have weakened and knowledge is declining. His solution is to write a simple, accessible commentary.
The problem is not the original work but the state of the community.
3
Notice the humility and spiritual intention
Al-Mashat describes himself as 'the poor one hoping for success on the path' and expresses hope that this work be a provision for the Day of Judgment. This shows his sincere intention.
This humility is a common trope but here it reflects genuine piety.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم الحمد لله حمدا يوافي ما تزايد من النعم والشكر له على ما أولانا من الفضل والكرم والصلاة والسلام على أكرم العرب والعجم، المبعوث إلى سائر الأمم، سيدنا محمد وعلى آله وصحابته وأمته أفضل الأمم. أما بعد، فيقول الفقير الرَّاجي الفوز على الصراط (۱) ، حسن محمد عباس المَشاط، كان الله له، وأصلح في الدارين أحواله لما رأيت منظومة أبي البركات سيدي أحمد الدردير المسماة بـ «الخريدة البهية في علم التوحيد كافية في موضوعها منظومة مباركة مستوفاة كلّ ما يطلب من المكلّف في هذا الفن، وقد شرحها الناظم رحمه الله تعالى شرحًا تَقَرّ به العيون، وتنشرح به الصدور، غير أن الهمم تقاصرت، وأصبح شأن العلم مُدْبِرًا، والناس عنه معرضون فقصدت التقرّب والتقريب بوضع تعليق بسيط كالشرح الموجز لهذه المنظومة المباركة، رجاء النفع بها وبمؤلفها الذي حاز في العلوم أوفى نصيب، فكتبتُ بحسب ما يتيسر هذا التعليق، أرجو الله تعالى به نفع العباد، وأن يكون لي ذخيرة يوم المعاد، بمنه وكرمه.
In the name of God, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. Praise be to God, a praise that matches the increasing blessings, and gratitude to Him for what He has bestowed upon us of favor and generosity. And blessings and peace be upon the most noble of Arabs and non-Arabs, the one sent to all nations, our master Muhammad, and upon his family, his companions, and his community, the best of communities. To proceed: the poor one hoping for success on the path (1), Hasan Muhammad 'Abbas al-Mashat, may God be with him and rectify his affairs in both worlds, says: When I saw the poem of Abu al-Barakat, my master Ahmad al-Dardir, named 'al-Kharida al-Bahiyya fi 'Ilm al-Tawhid' — sufficient in its subject, a blessed poem encompassing all that is required of the accountable person in this art — and its author, may God have mercy on him, had explained it with an explanation that delights the eyes and expands the breasts, yet ambitions have diminished, the status of knowledge has declined, and people have turned away from it, I intended to draw near and to make it accessible by producing a simple commentary, like an abridged explanation of this blessed poem, hoping for benefit through it and through its author, who attained the fullest share in the sciences. So I wrote this commentary to the extent that was facilitated, asking God Almighty thereby to benefit the servants and that it be a provision for me on the Day of Return, by His grace and generosity.
Source pages p. 18

Commentary
This passage is the commentator's introduction, which follows the classical pattern of opening with the basmala, praise of God, and blessings on the Prophet. The commentator then identifies himself as 'the poor one hoping for success on the path' (al-faqir al-rajī al-fawz 'alā al-sirāt) — a humble title — and gives his full name: Hasan Muhammad 'Abbas al-Mashat. He asks God to be with him and to rectify his affairs in both worlds. The core of the introduction is the explanation of why he wrote this commentary. He tells us that he saw the poem of Ahmad al-Dardir, 'al-Kharida al-Bahiyya fi 'Ilm al-Tawhid,' and he extols it: it is sufficient in its subject matter, a blessed poem that covers everything required of the mukallaf (the responsible person) in the field of tawhid. Al-Dardir himself had already written a commentary on his own poem, which was excellent and delightful. But despite that, people's ambitions have weakened, knowledge is in decline, and people are turning away from it. Therefore, al-Mashat aimed to draw near to God and to make knowledge accessible (al-taqarrub wa al-taqrīb) by writing a simple commentary (ta'liq basīt) in the form of an abridged explanation (sharḥ mūjaz) of this blessed poem. He hopes for benefit both from the commentary and from its original author, and he wishes that this work be a provision for him on the Day of Judgment. The introduction thus establishes the commentator's humility, his respect for the original text and its author, his awareness of the sad state of contemporary scholarship, and his pedagogical intention to make tawhid accessible.

Key Terms
الخريدة البهية
منظومة
التعليق
الشرح الموجز
المكلّف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That al-Mashat's commentary is an independent work.
Correction: Al-Mashat explicitly states it is a simple explanatory comment on al-Dardir's poem and existing commentary.

Checkpoint
According to the introduction, what motivated al-Mashat to write this commentary?
A) Because al-Dardir's poem was incomplete
B) Because people's ambitions had weakened and knowledge was declining
C) Because he disagreed with al-Dardir's theology
D) Because he wanted to write a longer commentary than al-Dardir's

Key Takeaway
Al-Mashat wrote this commentary to revive interest in tawhid by making al-Dardir's poem accessible in a time of scholarly neglect.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Al-Mashat saw that 'ambitions have diminished and people have turned away from knowledge.' In your own life, where do you see this decline? What concrete step will you take this week to reverse it?
?
Al-Mashat wrote his commentary 'to draw near to God and to make it accessible.' Is your pursuit of knowledge primarily for God's pleasure or for worldly gain? What would change if you truly intended it for God?
?
If you witnessed a revival of interest in tawhid, would you be among those who benefit or among those who turn away? What are you doing right now to ensure you are not neglectful?

Method of Learning

Al-Mashat's Preface: Motivation for the Commentary
Al-Mashat explains his reasons for writing the commentary.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Al-Mashat's motivation for writing his commentary on al-Kharidah al-Bahiyyah and the context of declining scholarly engagement.

Core Idea
Al-Mashat wrote to revive learning, not merely to repeat.

Al-Mashat opens his commentary with praise and then explains his purpose. He observed that despite al-Dardir's excellent poem and its own commentary, people's enthusiasm for knowledge had waned. So he wrote a concise explanation to make the teachings accessible. This lesson explores his preface and its significance.

Guided Path
1
Read the preface passage
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Notice the formal structure: basmalah, praise, then the author's statement of intent.
Pay attention to the phrase 'الهمم تقاصرت' (aspirations diminished) – it signals the problem.
2
Identify the author's stated motivation
Look for the specific reasons Al-Mashat gives for writing. He mentions that although al-Dardir's own commentary was excellent, people were turning away from knowledge. His intention was to draw near to God and make knowledge accessible.
He says 'فقصدت التقرّب والتقريب' (I intended to draw near and to simplify).
3
Note the author's humility
Observe how Al-Mashat describes himself: 'الفقير الراجي الفوز على الصراط' (the poor one hoping for success). He also invites readers to correct any mistakes, showing his sincere intent.
This humility is a common feature of Islamic scholarly writings.
4
Reflect on the context
Consider the broader context: the decline of knowledge ('أصبح شأن العلم مُدْبِرًا'). Why might such a preface be important for understanding the purpose of the commentary?
This preface sets the tone for the entire work – it is not just a formality.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم
الحمد لله حمدا يوافي ما تزايد من النعم والشكر له على ما أولانا من الفضل والكرم والصلاة والسلام على أكرم العرب والعجم، المبعوث إلى سائر الأمم، سيدنا محمد وعلى آله وصحابته وأمته أفضل الأمم.
أما بعد، فيقول الفقير الرَّاجي الفوز على الصراط ، حسن محمد عباس المَشاط، كان الله له، وأصلح في الدارين أحواله لما رأيت منظومة أبي البركات سيدي أحمد الدردير المسماة بـ «الخريدة البهية في علم التوحيد كافية في موضوعها منظومة مباركة مستوفاة كلّ ما يطلب من المكلّف في هذا الفن، وقد شرحها الناظم رحمه الله تعالى شرحًا تَقَرّ به العيون، وتنشرح به الصدور، غير أن الهمم تقاصرت، وأصبح شأن العلم مُدْبِرًا، والناس عنه معرضون فقصدت التقرّب والتقريب بوضع تعليق بسيط كالشرح الموجز لهذه المنظومة المباركة، رجاء النفع بها وبمؤلفها الذي حاز في العلوم أوفى نصيب، فكتبتُ بحسب ما يتيسر هذا التعليق، أرجو الله تعالى به نفع العباد، وأن يكون لي ذخيرة يوم المعاد، بمنه وكرمه.
وما رأيت أيها المطالع فيه على خطأ أو خَطَل، فإنِّي أذنتُ في إصلاحه، وما قصدت إلا الخير، وبالله اعتمادي، وعليه استنادي، وصلى الله وسلم على سيدنا محمد وآله وأصحابه، وأهل وُدّه واقترابه، آمين.
In the name of God, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. Praise be to God, a praise that matches the increasing blessings, and gratitude to Him for what He has bestowed upon us of favor and generosity. And blessings and peace be upon the most noble of Arabs and non-Arabs, sent to all nations, our master Muhammad, and upon his family, his Companions, and his nation, the best of nations. To proceed: the poor one hoping for success on the Path, Hasan Muhammad Abbas al-Mashat – may God be with him and rectify his conditions in both abodes – says: when I saw the poetic composition of Abu al-Barakat Sayyidi Ahmad al-Dardir, titled 'al-Kharidah al-Bahiyyah fi Ilm al-Tawhid', sufficient in its subject, a blessed composition encompassing all that is required of the responsible person in this art, and the author, may God have mercy on him, had explained it with an explanation that pleases the eyes and expands the breasts, yet aspirations have diminished, the status of knowledge has declined, and people are turning away from it, I intended to draw near and to simplify by composing a simple comment like a concise explanation of this blessed composition, hoping for benefit from it and from its author who attained the fullest share in the sciences. So I wrote this comment as much as was facilitated, asking God Almighty thereby to benefit the servants and that it be a provision for me on the Day of Return, by His bounty and generosity. And if you, O reader, see any mistake or idle talk in it, I have permitted its correction. I intended nothing but good. Upon God is my reliance, and on Him is my support. And may God bless and grant peace to our master Muhammad, his family, his Companions, and those who love and draw near to him. Amen.
Source pages p. 18

Commentary
This passage is the author's preface (muqaddimah) in which he states his purpose for writing the commentary. He begins with the basmalah and hamdalah, then explains that he saw al-Dardir's poem 'al-Kharidah al-Bahiyyah' as a sufficient and blessed work covering all required matters of tawhid. Although al-Dardir himself had already written a full commentary, people's zeal for learning had diminished and knowledge was declining. Therefore, al-Mashat sought to bring himself closer to God and to make knowledge accessible by writing a brief commentary (ta'liq basit) as a concise explanation (sharh mujaz). He hopes for benefit for himself and others. He then asks the reader to correct any mistakes he finds, since his intention is only good. He ends with a prayer for blessings on the Prophet and his close ones.

Key Terms
تعليق
شرح موجز
منظومة
الخريدة البهية
علم التوحيد
المكلّف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the preface is merely a formal dedication without substantive content.
Correction: The preface reveals the author's pedagogical motivation and his concern about the decline of learning, showing it is integral to understanding the commentary's purpose.

Checkpoint
What was the main reason al-Mashat wrote this commentary on al-Kharidah al-Bahiyyah?
A) Because he found errors in al-Dardir's original poem.
B) Because al-Dardir's own commentary was too long and unhelpful.
C) Because people's enthusiasm for learning had declined and he wanted to make knowledge accessible.
D) Because he was asked by al-Dardir's students to write an explanation.

Key Takeaway
Al-Mashat's preface shows that his commentary was a response to the decline of learning, aiming to revive accessible knowledge.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you see people around you turning away from religious knowledge, do you feel a personal responsibility to respond, or do you remain indifferent? What would al-Mashat's attitude compel you to do today?
?
Al-Mashat wrote a concise commentary to make knowledge accessible. What is one step you can take this week to make a single beneficial teaching easier for someone else to understand, even if it requires sacrifice on your part?
?
If you were to examine your own relationship with learning, would you say you are part of those who have 'diminished aspirations' or those who revive knowledge? What concrete change would prove otherwise?

The Chain of Transmission (Ijazah) of This Work
Enumerating the ijazah chains for the work

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the author's chains of transmission connect this work to earlier authoritative scholars, ensuring the continuity of Islamic knowledge.

Core Idea
Chains of transmission guarantee authentic knowledge transfer across generations.

In traditional Islamic learning, a work’s authenticity is often validated by its chain of transmission (isnad). The author includes multiple ijazahs to show the reliable journey of this knowledge from earlier masters. This passage lists the second, third, and fourth chains, linking to renowned scholars like Zakariyya al-Ansari and Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic and its translation carefully. Notice the names and how they connect.
The passage uses 'عن' to indicate transmission.
2
Identify the chains
Look for the phrases 'وأما الثاني', 'وأما الثالث والرابع'. Each introduces a different chain. List the names in each chain.
3
Reflect on the significance
Why would the author include these chains? How does this practice ensure reliability?
This connects to the broader concept of isnad in Islamic scholarship.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأما الثاني فعن الشمس محمد بن محمد عقيلة، عن الله المذكورين في إسناده منهم الشيخ الكبير ، محدث الحجاز الشهير، بن سالم البصري، عن أئمة منهم: الشمس البابلي، عن النور عبد الزيادي، عن الشهاب الرملي عن شيخ الإسلام زكريا الأنصاري وأبي الفضل جلال الدين السيوطي وغيرهم. وأما الثالث والرابع، فمن أئمة منهم: مسند الحجاز عبد الله البصري المذكور» (۱). الحسيني، الدمياطي الشافعي، المعروف بابن الميت، وبالبرهان الشامي، المتوفى سنة (١١٤٠).
As for the second (way of transmission), it is from al-Shams Muhammad ibn Muhammad 'Uqayla, from Allah? (the mentioned ones in his chain, among them the great shaykh, the famous muhaddith of Hijaz, Ibn Salim al-Basri, from imams including: al-Shams al-Babili, from al-Nur 'Abd al-Ziyadi, from al-Shihab al-Ramli, from Shaykh al-Islam Zakariyya al-Ansari and Abu al-Fadl Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti and others. As for the third and fourth, they are from imams including: Musnid al-Hijaz 'Abdullah al-Basri al-madhkur. Al-Husayni, al-Dimyati al-Shafi'i, known as Ibn al-Mayyit, and al-Burhan al-Shami, died 1140 AH.
Source pages p. 21

Commentary
This passage details the chains of transmission (ijazah) through which the work was received. It mentions the second path from al-Shams Muhammad ibn Muhammad 'Uqayla, then through the great muhaddith of Hijaz Ibn Salim al-Basri, then from al-Shams al-Babili, al-Nur 'Abd al-Ziyadi, al-Shihab al-Ramli, and then from Shaykh al-Islam Zakariyya al-Ansari and Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti. The third and fourth paths include Musnid al-Hijaz 'Abdullah al-Basri. Then it lists another scholar: al-Husayni, al-Dimyati al-Shafi'i, known as Ibn al-Mayyit and al-Burhan al-Shami, who died in 1140 AH.

Key Terms
الإسناد (chain)
الاجازة (ijazah)
مسند الحجاز (Musnid al-Hijaz)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Chains of transmission are just a formality and don't affect the reliability of a text.
Correction: In Islamic scholarship, the chain (isnad) is critical for authenticating hadith and transmitted knowledge. A reliable chain confirms that the text was accurately passed down from the original author or teacher.

Checkpoint

Key Takeaway
The author's inclusion of multiple ijazah chains demonstrates the careful preservation and authenticated transmission of Islamic knowledge.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The author took great care to list multiple chains of transmission for this work. Do you take the same care in verifying the sources of your Islamic knowledge? Will you commit to finding a teacher or a reliable chain before accepting any new teaching?
?
If you were to trace back your own Islamic knowledge to its sources, could you name the teachers or texts from whom you learned? What does the absence of such a chain say about the authenticity of your understanding?
?
The scholars in these chains dedicated their lives to preserving and transmitting knowledge. Are you equally dedicated to ensuring that what you believe is based on sound, authenticated sources? What is one step you can take this week to connect with a reliable chain of learning?

The Poem's Small Size but Vast Knowledge
The commentator describes the poem's small size but vast knowledge, listing its contents.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the structure and contents of this concise theological poem and why its small size does not limit its profound knowledge.

Core Idea
A small poem can contain the essentials of Islamic creed and proofs.

This lesson opens the commentary on the poem's first line. The commentator immediately addresses a potential misconception: a small book cannot be profound. He defines the terms, lists the poem's contents, and explains why it is actually great in knowledge. You will see how this brief poem covers the essential categories of Islamic theology and leads the reader from blind imitation to verified faith.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and its translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Notice how the commentator defines 'latifa' and 'saghira fi al-hajm' before listing the contents.
The passage is from the sharh, not the matn itself.
2
Identify the poem's contents
Find the list of what the poem contains: the three categories for God and messengers, proofs, transmitted matters, Sufism, and the benefits of tawhid. Count them.
3
Understand the correction of misunderstanding
The commentator explains that 'small in size' might wrongly imply little knowledge. He corrects this by saying the poem is 'great in knowledge'. Reflect on why this correction is important.
This connects to the learning objective.
4
Reflect on the goal of the poem
The passage ends by stating the purpose: to move from taqlid (blind imitation) to tahqiq (verification). Consider how the poem's contents achieve this.
This is the ultimate aim of studying kalam.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
لَطِيفَةٌ صَغِيرَةٌ فِي الْحَجْمِ * لَكِنَّهَا كَبِيرَةٌ فِي الْعِلْمِ. [١] لَطِيفَةٌ مِنْ لَطُفَ كَكَرُمَ دَقَّ أَوْ رَقَّ فَاللَّطِيفُ الصَّغِيرُ الْحَجْمِ أَوِ الرَّقِيقُ وَالشَّفَّافُ الَّذِي لَا يَحْجُبُ مَا وَرَاءَهُ كَالزُّجَاجِ. صَغِيرَةٌ فِي الْحَجْمِ بِفَتْحِ الْحَاءِ الْمُهْمَلَةِ وَسُكُونِ الْجِيمِ أَيِ الْقَدْرِ. وَهَذِهِ الْمَنْظُومَةُ الْمُبَارَكَةُ إِحْدَى وَسَبْعُونَ بَيْتًا تَشْتَمِلُ عَلَى مَا يَجِبُ لِلَّهِ وَمَا يَجُوزُ وَمَا يَسْتَحِيلُ. وَكَذَلِكَ لِلرُّسُلِ الْكِرَامِ. وَعَلَى الْبَرَاهِينِ وَالسَّمْعِيَّاتِ. وَعَلَى جُمْلَةٍ مِنَ التَّصَوُّفِ. وَخُتِمَتْ بِمَا اشْتَمَلَتْ عَلَيْهِ كَلِمَةُ التَّوْحِيدِ مِنَ الْفَوَائِدِ. [٢] وَلَمَّا كَانَ قَوْلُهُ صَغِيرَةٌ فِي الْحَجْمِ مُوهِمًا أَنَّهَا قَلِيلَةُ الْعِلْمِ اسْتَدْرَكَ بِدَفْعِ هَذَا التَّوَهُمِ بِقَوْلِهِ لَكِنَّهَا كَبِيرَةٌ أَيْ عَظِيمَةٌ فِي الْعِلْمِ وَذَلِكَ لِمَا اشْتَمَلَتْ عَلَى مَا ذُكِرَ مِنَ الْعَقَائِدِ الْحَقَّةِ الْوَاجِبِ عَلَى الْمُكَلَّفِ أَنْ يَتَعَلَّمَهَا وَالْبَرَاهِينِ الْقَطْعِيَّةِ الَّتِي يَخْرُجُ بِهَا مِنْ رِبْقَةِ التَّقْلِيدِ إِلَى نُورِ التَّحْقِيقِ حَتَّى لَا يَكُونَ فِي إِيمَانِهِ خِلَافٌ.
Delicate, small in size, but great in knowledge. [1] 'Latifa' from latufa like karuma: it means thin or fine; so 'latif' is small in size or thin and transparent that does not hide what is behind it like glass. 'Saghira fi al-hajm' with fatḥa on ḥā and sukūn on jīm, meaning in quantity. And this blessed poem is seventy-one verses, containing what is obligatory for God, what is possible, and what is impossible, and similarly for the noble messengers, and on proofs and transmitted matters, and on a portion of Sufism, and it concludes with the benefits contained in the word of tawḥīd. [2] And since his saying 'small in size' might give the impression that it has little knowledge, he corrected this misunderstanding by saying 'but it is great' i.e. mighty in knowledge, because it contains the aforementioned true creeds that the responsible person must learn, and the decisive proofs by which he emerges from the confines of blind imitation into the light of verification, so that there is no disagreement in his faith.
Source pages p. 25

Commentary
This passage explains the first line of the poem. The commentator starts by defining 'latifa' as small or transparent, and 'saghira fi al-hajm' as small in quantity. He notes the poem has 71 verses. Then he lists its contents: the necessary, possible, and impossible for God and for the messengers; proofs (burāhin); transmitted matters (samʿiyyāt); some Sufism; and benefits of the word of tawḥīd. In [2], he addresses a potential misunderstanding: the small size might imply limited knowledge, but the poem is actually great in knowledge, containing essential true beliefs and decisive proofs that lead from blind imitation (taqlīd) to verification (taḥqīq), ensuring no disagreement in faith.

صغيرة في الحجم
ما يجب لله
ما يجوز
ما يستحيل
براهين
سمعيات
تصوف
تقليد
تحقيق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That a small book or poem must contain little knowledge due to its size.
Correction: The poem is actually great in knowledge because it contains the essential true beliefs and decisive proofs that lead to verified faith.

Checkpoint

B) 71
C) 72

Key Takeaway
Despite having only 71 verses, this poem covers the core areas of Islamic creed and provides proof-based faith, moving the learner from blind imitation to verification.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

The Poem's Sufficiency and Its Core Content
The commentator explains the poem's sufficiency and its essence of the art of creed.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the commentator's explanation of how the poem suffices for knowledge of Islamic creed and what is meant by 'the cream of the art'.

Core Idea
A short poem can suffice as a foundation for the entire science of Islamic creed.

The commentator begins by unpacking the poet's claim that this poem is enough for the seeker of knowledge. He explains that it is not just any poetry—it contains the very essence (zubda) of the discipline, which is the science of tawḥīd. This passage clarifies the scope and purpose of the poem.

Guided Path
1
Read the lines and translation
Start by reading the two lines of poetry and their literal translation to get the overall claim made by the poet and commentator.
Notice the rhyme and brevity of the original Arabic.
2
Understand the key terms
Focus on the terms 'taktafi' (be content) and 'zubdat al-fann' (cream of the art). The commentary defines them in detail.
These are technical terms in didactic poetry that indicate completeness.
3
Identify the contents of the poem
The passage states that the poem contains the three rational categories (obligatory, possible, impossible) and the transmitted matters. Find those in the Arabic and translation.
This shows the poem covers the core of ʿilm al-kalām.
4
Consider the implication
Reflect on why the commentator emphasizes sufficiency. What does this tell you about the purpose and intended audience of the poem?
The poem is meant as a comprehensive summary for beginners.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
تَكْفِيكَ عِلْمًا إِنْ تُرِدْ أَنْ تَكْتَفِي * لِأَنَّهَا بِزُبْدَةِ الْفَنِّ تَفِي. [٣] تَكْفِيكَ هَذِهِ الْمَنْظُومَةُ عِلْمًا فِي دِينِكَ إِنْ تُرِدْ أَنْ تَكْتَفِي وَالْجُمْلَةُ مَقُولَةُ الْقَوْلِ وَتَكْتَفِي مِنَ الْكِفَايَةِ وَهِيَ الِاسْتِغْنَاءُ أَنْ يَكْفِيَكَ الْعِلْمُ الْمُسْتَفَادُ مِنْهَا إِنْ تُرَدْ بِضَمِّ التَّاءِ أَيْ تَقْصِدْ أَنْ تَكْتَفِيَ بِهَا عَنْ غَيْرِهَا مِنَ الْمُطَوَّلَاتِ. [٤] وَذَلِكَ لِأَنَّهَا بِزُبْدَةِ الْفَنِّ وَهُوَ فَنُّ عَقَائِدِ الْإِيمَانِ وَيُسَمَّى عِلْمَ التَّوْحِيدِ وَعِلْمَ أُصُولِ الدِّينِ وَعِلْمَ الْعَقَائِدِ وَتَفِي مِنْ وَفَى يَفِي مِثْلُ وَقَى يَقِي أَصْلُهُ تَوْفِي. وَذَلِكَ لِمَا اشْتَمَلَتْ عَلَيْهِ الْمَنْظُومَةُ مِنَ الْوَاجِبِ وَالْجَائِزِ وَالْمُسْتَحِيلِ فِي حَقِّ اللَّهِ تَعَالَى وَفِي حَقِّ الرُّسُلِ عَلَيْهِمُ الصَّلَاةُ وَالسَّلَامُ وَمِنَ السَّمْعِيَّاتِ.
It suffices you with knowledge if you wish to be content, because it fulfills with the cream of the art. [3] This poem suffices you with knowledge in your religion if you wish to be content. The sentence is a saying, and 'taktafī' is from al-kifāya which is self-sufficiency: that the knowledge derived from it suffices you if you intend (with ḍamma on tā) i.e. you intend to be content with it instead of other lengthy works. [4] That is because it is with the cream of the art, which is the art of the creeds of faith, also called the science of tawḥīd, the science of the principles of religion, and the science of creeds; and 'tafī' from wafā yafī, like waqā yaqī, its origin is tawfī. And that is because the poem contains the obligatory, the possible, and the impossible with respect to God the Exalted and with respect to the messengers, peace be upon them, and from the transmitted matters.
Source pages p. 26

Commentary
This passage explains the second and third lines of the poem. The commentator first explains 'takfīka ʿilman in turid an taktafī': the poem suffices you with knowledge if you seek to be content, meaning you don't need longer works. The phrase 'in turid' has a ḍamma on tā (turād) meaning you intend. Then he explains 'bizubdati al-fanni tafī': 'zubda' is the cream or best part of the art—the art of creedal faith, known as ʿilm al-tawḥīd, uṣūl al-dīn, or ʿilm al-ʿaqāʾid. 'Tafī' means fulfills or suffices, from wafā. The poem fulfills its purpose because it contains the necessary, possible, and impossible for God and the messengers, and transmitted matters (samʿiyyāt). Thus it covers the core of the discipline.

Key Terms
تكفيك علما
تكتفي
زبدة الفن
فن عقائد الإيمان
علم التوحيد
علم أصول الدين
تفي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that this poem makes other books unnecessary for all knowledge.
Correction: The commentator clarifies that the poem suffices for the essential knowledge of the fundamentals (the 'cream' of the art), not for all detailed scholarship.

Checkpoint
What is meant by 'zubdat al-fann' (cream of the art)?
A) The most difficult part of poetry
B) The essence of the art of creedal faith
C) The longest chapter in the poem
D) A type of Sufi practice

Key Takeaway
The poem covers the essential principles of Islamic creed, making it sufficient for the seeker who wishes to grasp the fundamentals without lengthy works.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
This poem claims to suffice you with knowledge of creed. Have you actually internalized the categories of obligatory, possible, and impossible regarding Allah and His messengers, or do you rely on vague notions? What step will you take today to test your understanding of these categories?
?
The commentator says the poem contains the cream of the art. If you were asked to distill your own belief about Allah into these very categories, could you? Perhaps your faith is more scattered than you think. Will you memorize these three judgments and apply them to your daily thoughts about God?
?
The poem is sufficient, yet you have access to it. Why do you still feel uncertain about core beliefs? Is it because you haven't engaged with it deeply? What specific question about God's attributes do you still avoid, and why?

The Poem's Greatness Despite Its Small Size
Commentary on the poem's claim of being small yet vast in knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the author of al-Kharida al-Bahiyya claims his poem is small in size yet vast in knowledge, and how its content provides sufficiency in creedal matters.

Core Idea
A small poem can contain the cream of essential Islamic beliefs.

The commentator anticipates a natural reaction: a short poem cannot contain much knowledge. He immediately corrects this assumption by revealing the poem's rich content. This lesson unpacks how the poem's small size belies its comprehensive coverage of true beliefs, definitive proofs, and the core categories of Islamic theology, making it a sufficient source for the discerning student.

Guided Path
1
Recognize the potential misunderstanding
Notice that the phrase 'small in volume' could mislead one into thinking the poem lacks substance. The commentator addresses this head-on.
This is a common pedagogical technique: anticipate and refute objections.
2
Understand the content that makes it great
The greatness lies in including true beliefs every accountable person must learn, along with definitive proofs that free one from blind imitation.
These proofs are what elevate faith from taqlid to tahqiq (verification).
3
Grasp the concept of sufficiency
The poem is said to suffice if one wishes to rely on it instead of longer works. This is not arrogance but a claim about its concentrated usefulness.
The word 'taktafi' implies self-sufficiency in knowledge.
4
Identify the three categories of content
The poem covers the obligatory, possible, and impossible regarding Allah and the messengers, plus sam'iyyat (scriptural matters). This is the 'cream of the art'.
These categories form the backbone of Islamic theology (ilm al-kalam).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
[٢] ولما كان قوله: صغيرة في الحجم» موهما أنها قليلة العلم استدرك بدفع هذا التّوهم بقوله: (لكنّها كبيرة) أي: عظيمة (في العلم)، وذلك لما اشتملت على ما ذُكر من العقائد الحقة، الواجب على المكلف أن يتعلمها والبراهين القطعية التي يخرج بها من ربقة التقليد إلى نور التحقيق، حتى لا يكون في إيمانه خلاف. [۳] ( تكفيك) هذه المنظومة (علما) في دينك (إن تُرِد أن تكتفي)، والجملة مقولة القول، وتكتفي من الكفاية وهي الاستغناء، أن يكفيك العلم المستفاد منها إن تُرد» بضم التاء، أي: تقصد أن تكتفي بها عن غيرها من المطولات. [٤] وذلك لأنها ( بزبدة) بخلاصة الفن)، وهو فن عقائد الايمان، ويسمى علم التوحيد، وعلم أصول الدين، وعلم العقائد، (تفي) من وَفَى يفي، مثل وَقَى يقي أصله توفي. وذلك لما اشتملت عليه المنظومة من الواجب، والجائز، والمستحيل في حق الله تعالى، وفي حق الرسل عليهم الصلاة والسلام، ومن السمعيات.
[2] And when his statement 'small in volume' suggested that it is little in knowledge, he corrected by repelling this assumption with his statement: ('but it is great') meaning: magnificent (in knowledge), and that is because it includes what was mentioned of true beliefs, which the legally responsible person must learn, and the definitive proofs by which he emerges from the shackles of blind imitation into the light of verification, so that there may be no opposition in his faith. [3] (This poem suffices) you (with knowledge) in your religion (if you wish to be content), and the sentence is the statement of the saying, and 'taktafi' comes from 'kifayah' which is self-sufficiency, that the knowledge derived from it suffices you 'in turid' with a damma on the ta, meaning: you intend to be content with it instead of other lengthy works. [4] And that is because it (is) (with the cream) of the cream of the art), and it is the art of the beliefs of faith, and it is called the science of tawhid, the science of the fundamentals of religion, and the science of beliefs, (it fulfills) from wafa yafi, like waqa yaqi, its original is tufi. And that is because the poem includes the obligatory, the possible, and the impossible with respect to Allah the Exalted, and with respect to the Messengers, peace be upon them, and from the sam'iyyat (matters known through revelation).
Source pages p. 26

Commentary
This passage is a commentary explaining the meaning of the poet's line 'small in volume but great in knowledge.' The commentator begins by noting that the phrase 'small in volume' might mislead one into thinking the poem contains little knowledge. He immediately refutes this by explaining that 'great in knowledge' refers to its immense benefit, as it includes true beliefs that every legally responsible person must learn, and definitive proofs that free the believer from blind imitation (taqlid) and lead to verification (tahqiq), ensuring certainty in faith. Next, he explains the phrase 'it suffices you with knowledge if you wish to be content' (تكفيك علما إن ترد أن تكتفي). He analyzes the grammar: 'taktafi' is from kifayah meaning self-sufficiency; the conditional 'in turid' means if you intend to rely solely on this poem instead of longer works. Finally, he explains that this sufficiency is because the poem contains the cream (zubdah) of the art of Islamic beliefs—also called the science of tawhid, usul al-din, or aqa'id—and it fulfills (tufi) its purpose by covering the three categories: obligatory (wajib), possible (ja'iz), and impossible (mustahil) regarding Allah and the messengers, as well as sam'iyyat (scriptural matters like the afterlife). Thus the commentator unfolds the poet's claim step by step, addressing potential misunderstandings and clarifying the poem's comprehensive scope.

Key Terms
العقائد الحقة
البراهين القطعية
التقليد
التحقيق
زبدة الفن
الواجب والجائز والمستحيل
السمعيات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: A short poem cannot contain substantial theological knowledge.
Correction: The poem's small size belies its comprehensive coverage of essential beliefs, definitive proofs, and the core categories of Islamic theology, making it a valuable resource.

Checkpoint
According to the commentator, what does the poet mean by 'great in knowledge'?
A) The poem contains many pages of detailed arguments.
B) The poem includes true beliefs, definitive proofs, and the three categories of attributes for Allah and the messengers.
C) The poem is famous and widely studied.
D) The poem is written by a great scholar.

Key Takeaway
The poem's small size is deceptive; its concentrated content of true beliefs, definitive proofs, and the three theological categories makes it a sufficient guide in creedal matters.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever judged a source of knowledge by its size or length, assuming that more pages mean more value? How has this lesson challenged that assumption in your own heart, and what small but weighty text could you commit to studying deeply instead of always seeking longer ones?
?
The passage says the poem allows you to 'emerge from the shackles of blind imitation into the light of verification.' In which area of your faith are you still content to follow without understanding? What would it cost you today to demand the definitive proof for one belief you hold?
?
If this small poem truly suffices in knowledge for the one who is content, why do you often feel insufficient in your own faith? Is it lack of knowledge or lack of contentment? What would change if you believed that what you already know—if it is the cream of truth—is enough to make your faith certain?
