The Ten Principles: Definition, Subject, Names
Introduces the ten principles and details the first three.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the purpose of the ten principles and how the first three—definition, subject, and names—help identify and distinguish a science.

Core Idea
The first three principles answer: What is this science? What is its subject? How is it named?

Before diving into any science, scholars established a set of principles to define it. This lesson introduces the concept of the ten principles and focuses on the first three: definition (al-hadd), subject (al-mawdu'), and names (al-asma'). These establish the identity of the science.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage about the ten principles
Read the Arabic passage that introduces the ten principles and the first three. Focus on the purpose and each principle's role.
2
Identify the first three principles
Find the names of the first three principles in the passage: al-hadd, al-mawdu', al-asma'. Note how each is defined.
The text states: 'by the definition… by the subject… by the names'.
3
Understand the role of definition
The definition tells the student what he is seeking. It gives the essence of the science.
4
Understand how subject and names distinguish the science
The subject distinguishes one science from another, as all sciences share a common genus but differ by subject. Names similarly serve to differentiate.
The passage says 'all sciences are one genus' – a key point.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(۱) للعلوم مبادئ عشرة ذكرها العلماء لتعين الطلاب على الولوج في العلوم فبالحد يعرفُ الطالب ما هو ساع في طلبه، وبالموضوع يمتاز له ذلك العلم من غيره، لأن العلوم كلها جنس ، وإنما تفترق بالموضوعات، وبالأسماء كذلك يتميز العلم عن غيره، وبالفائدة يَقْوَى الباعث على الطلب، وبالفضل يعرفُ شَرف هذا العلم فيزاد باعثه على تحصيله وبالنسبة إلى ما عداه من العلوم يعرف موقعه بين العلوم وبالواضع، - والمقصود بالواضع: هو المُدَوّن، وإلا فالعلوم كلّها من رسول الله صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ ، والمدوّن جامع لذلك كله عن رسول الله صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ وصحابته وتابعيهم حتى عصره ـ يعرف المؤسس وواضع البذرة الأولى للعلم، وباستمداده يعرف مأخذ العلم، وأيضًا هذا له تعلق بالواضع باعتبار أنه جمع علم من سبقه إلى سول الله صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ ، فيعلم أنه ليس منشئًا للعلم ومبتدعا له، وإنما هو جامع ومُشَهُرٌ لما اندثر أو غاب عن العقول، وبحكم الشارع فيه يعرف مقدار احتياجه لهذا العلم وجوبًا، وندبًا، وإباحة، وعدم احتياجه له إن كان في حيز الكراهة والحرمة وبمسائله يعرف تفصيلات موضوعاته. انظر مقدمة شرح الخريدة» (ص۲۹).
(1) For sciences, there are ten principles mentioned by scholars to help students enter into the sciences. By the definition (al-hadd), the student knows what he is seeking. By the subject (al-mawdu'), the science is distinguished from others, because all sciences are one genus and are only differentiated by their subjects. By the names (al-asma'), similarly the science is distinguished from others. By the benefit (al-fa'idah), the motivation for seeking it becomes strong. By the virtue (al-fadl), the honor of this science is known, so one's motivation to acquire it increases. By the relation (al-nisbah) to other sciences, its position among sciences is known. By the founder (al-wadi')—and by 'founder' is meant the codifier (al-mudawwin); otherwise, all sciences come from the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and the codifier is the one who collected all of that from the Messenger of Allah, his Companions, and their followers up to his own time—the founder and the one who placed the first seed of the science is known. By its source (istikmad), the origin of the science is known; and also this is related to the founder in that he collected the knowledge of those before him up to the Messenger of Allah, so it is known that he is not the originator or innovator of the science, but rather a collector and publicizer of what had been lost or hidden from minds. By the ruling of the Lawgiver (hukm al-shari') regarding it, the extent of one's need for this science is known—whether obligatory, recommended, permissible, or unnecessary if it falls into the realm of disliked or forbidden. By its issues (masa'il), the details of its subjects are known. See the introduction of Sharh al-Kharidah (p. 29).
Source pages p. 27

Commentary
This passage is a footnote explaining the ten principles (mabadi' 'asharah) that scholars use to define and understand any science. The commentator lists and describes each principle: (1) al-hadd (definition) helps the student know what he is studying; (2) al-mawdu' (subject) distinguishes one science from another; (3) al-asma' (names) also distinguish it; (4) al-fa'idah (benefit) motivates the seeker; (5) al-fadl (virtue) shows the science's honor; (6) al-nisbah (relation) shows its position relative to other sciences; (7) al-wadi' (founder) identifies the codifier (mudawwin)—clarifying that the true source of all knowledge is revelation from the Prophet, and the codifier merely collects and systematizes; (8) istimdad (source) reveals the origin of the science; (9) hukm al-shari' (legal ruling) indicates whether studying it is obligatory, recommended, etc.; (10) al-masa'il (issues) show the detailed questions of the subject. The commentator emphasizes that the 'founder' is not an innovator but a compiler who resurrects forgotten knowledge. This explanation serves as a methodological framework for properly situating the science of tawhid within the broader tradition.

Key Terms
الحد
الموضوع
الأسماء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what distinguishes one science from another?
A) Its benefit
B) Its subject
C) Its founder
D) Its rulings

Key Takeaway
The first three principles of any science are its definition, subject, and name, which together identify and distinguish it from other sciences.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever studied a subject without first knowing its definition and subject? What confusion did that cause, and how might this lesson prevent that in your pursuit of Islamic knowledge?
?
If someone asked you to define the science of 'Aqeedah and name its subject, could you? Take a moment to commit these to heart, because your understanding shapes your worship.

The Motivational Principles: Benefit, Virtue, Relation
Explains the benefit, virtue, and relation of a science.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the principles of benefit, virtue, and relation motivate the student and situate a science within the broader tradition.

Core Idea
Benefit drives effort; virtue increases desire; relation shows position.

After establishing a science's identity, scholars turned to principles that motivate study and place the science among others. This lesson covers three principles: benefit (al-fa'idah), virtue (al-fadl), and relation (al-nisbah) to other sciences.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on the next three principles
Read the Arabic passage that explains benefit, virtue, and relation. Notice how each builds motivation.
2
Understand benefit (al-fa'idah)
Benefit strengthens the student's motivation to seek the science. It answers 'What do I gain?'
3
Understand virtue (al-fadl)
Virtue reveals the honor of the science, increasing the student's desire to acquire it.
4
Understand relation (al-nisbah)
Relation shows the science's position among other sciences, helping contextualize its importance.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(۱) للعلوم مبادئ عشرة ذكرها العلماء لتعين الطلاب على الولوج في العلوم فبالحد يعرفُ الطالب ما هو ساع في طلبه، وبالموضوع يمتاز له ذلك العلم من غيره، لأن العلوم كلها جنس ، وإنما تفترق بالموضوعات، وبالأسماء كذلك يتميز العلم عن غيره، وبالفائدة يَقْوَى الباعث على الطلب، وبالفضل يعرفُ شَرف هذا العلم فيزاد باعثه على تحصيله وبالنسبة إلى ما عداه من العلوم يعرف موقعه بين العلوم وبالواضع، - والمقصود بالواضع: هو المُدَوّن، وإلا فالعلوم كلّها من رسول الله صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ ، والمدوّن جامع لذلك كله عن رسول الله صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ وصحابته وتابعيهم حتى عصره ـ يعرف المؤسس وواضع البذرة الأولى للعلم، وباستمداده يعرف مأخذ العلم، وأيضًا هذا له تعلق بالواضع باعتبار أنه جمع علم من سبقه إلى سول الله صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ ، فيعلم أنه ليس منشئًا للعلم ومبتدعا له، وإنما هو جامع ومُشَهُرٌ لما اندثر أو غاب عن العقول، وبحكم الشارع فيه يعرف مقدار احتياجه لهذا العلم وجوبًا، وندبًا، وإباحة، وعدم احتياجه له إن كان في حيز الكراهة والحرمة وبمسائله يعرف تفصيلات موضوعاته. انظر مقدمة شرح الخريدة» (ص۲۹).
(1) For sciences, there are ten principles mentioned by scholars to help students enter into the sciences. By the definition (al-hadd), the student knows what he is seeking. By the subject (al-mawdu'), the science is distinguished from others, because all sciences are one genus and are only differentiated by their subjects. By the names (al-asma'), similarly the science is distinguished from others. By the benefit (al-fa'idah), the motivation for seeking it becomes strong. By the virtue (al-fadl), the honor of this science is known, so one's motivation to acquire it increases. By the relation (al-nisbah) to other sciences, its position among sciences is known. By the founder (al-wadi')—and by 'founder' is meant the codifier (al-mudawwin); otherwise, all sciences come from the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and the codifier is the one who collected all of that from the Messenger of Allah, his Companions, and their followers up to his own time—the founder and the one who placed the first seed of the science is known. By its source (istikmad), the origin of the science is known; and also this is related to the founder in that he collected the knowledge of those before him up to the Messenger of Allah, so it is known that he is not the originator or innovator of the science, but rather a collector and publicizer of what had been lost or hidden from minds. By the ruling of the Lawgiver (hukm al-shari') regarding it, the extent of one's need for this science is known—whether obligatory, recommended, permissible, or unnecessary if it falls into the realm of disliked or forbidden. By its issues (masa'il), the details of its subjects are known. See the introduction of Sharh al-Kharidah (p. 29).

Commentary
This passage is a footnote explaining the ten principles (mabadi' 'asharah) that scholars use to define and understand any science. The commentator lists and describes each principle: (1) al-hadd (definition) helps the student know what he is studying; (2) al-mawdu' (subject) distinguishes one science from another; (3) al-asma' (names) also distinguish it; (4) al-fa'idah (benefit) motivates the seeker; (5) al-fadl (virtue) shows the science's honor; (6) al-nisbah (relation) shows its position relative to other sciences; (7) al-wadi' (founder) identifies the codifier (mudawwin)—clarifying that the true source of all knowledge is revelation from the Prophet, and the codifier merely collects and systematizes; (8) istimdad (source) reveals the origin of the science; (9) hukm al-shari' (legal ruling) indicates whether studying it is obligatory, recommended, etc.; (10) al-masa'il (issues) show the detailed questions of the subject. Checkpoint
What does the principle of 'al-fadl' (virtue) accomplish?
A) It distinguishes the science from others.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you study any subject, do you actively consider its benefit (al-fā'idah) for your life and afterlife? How often do you ask yourself, 'What will I gain from this knowledge?' – and does that question ever drive you to study harder?
?
The virtue (al-faḍl) of a science is not just about status but about its honor. Which science do you currently pursue with a sense of its honor and nobility, and which do you study only out of necessity? What would change if you saw every beneficial science as noble?
?
You now know that every discipline has a relation (al-nisbah) to others. Can you map your own studies: which science is foundational, which is dependent, and which is the ultimate goal? Does your current learning sequence reflect that order, or do you need to re-prioritize?

Foundational Principles: Founder, Source, Ruling, Issues
Explains the founder, source, legal ruling, and issues of a science.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the final four principles—founder, source, legal ruling, and issues—and how they ground a science in revelation and practice.

Core Idea
A science's true source is revelation; the founder is its codifier, not originator.

The last four principles deal with the origin, legitimacy, and content of a science. This lesson covers the founder (al-wadi'), the source (al-istimdad), the ruling of the Lawgiver (hukm al-shari'), and the issues (al-masa'il). A key clarification is that the founder is the codifier, not the inventor.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on the last four principles
Read the Arabic passage that explains founder, source, ruling, and issues. Pay attention to the clarification about the founder.
2
Understand who the founder (al-wadi') is
The founder is the codifier (mudawwin) who collects and systematizes knowledge from the Prophet and earlier generations. He is not an originator.
The passage explicitly says: 'the founder is the codifier; otherwise, all sciences come from the Messenger of Allah.'
3
Understand the source (al-istimdad)
The source shows where the science derives its material, linking it back to revelation.
4
Understand the legal ruling (hukm al-shari')
The ruling indicates whether studying the science is obligatory, recommended, permissible, or not, based on its utility.
The text mentions: 'obligatory, recommended, permissible, and unnecessary if disliked or forbidden.'
5
Understand the issues (al-masa'il)
Issues are the detailed questions that arise from the subject, forming the content of the science.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(۱) للعلوم مبادئ عشرة ذكرها العلماء لتعين الطلاب على الولوج في العلوم فبالحد يعرفُ الطالب ما هو ساع في طلبه، وبالموضوع يمتاز له ذلك العلم من غيره، لأن العلوم كلها جنس ، وإنما تفترق بالموضوعات، وبالأسماء كذلك يتميز العلم عن غيره، وبالفائدة يَقْوَى الباعث على الطلب، وبالفضل يعرفُ شَرف هذا العلم فيزاد باعثه على تحصيله وبالنسبة إلى ما عداه من العلوم يعرف موقعه بين العلوم وبالواضع، - والمقصود بالواضع: هو المُدَوّن، وإلا فالعلوم كلّها من رسول الله صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ ، والمدوّن جامع لذلك كله عن رسول الله صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ وصحابته وتابعيهم حتى عصره ـ يعرف المؤسس وواضع البذرة الأولى للعلم، وباستمداده يعرف مأخذ العلم، وأيضًا هذا له تعلق بالواضع باعتبار أنه جمع علم من سبقه إلى سول الله صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ ، فيعلم أنه ليس منشئًا للعلم ومبتدعا له، وإنما هو جامع ومُشَهُرٌ لما اندثر أو غاب عن العقول، وبحكم الشارع فيه يعرف مقدار احتياجه لهذا العلم وجوبًا، وندبًا، وإباحة، وعدم احتياجه له إن كان في حيز الكراهة والحرمة وبمسائله يعرف تفصيلات موضوعاته. انظر مقدمة شرح الخريدة» (ص۲۹).
(1) For sciences, there are ten principles mentioned by scholars to help students enter into the sciences. By the definition (al-hadd), the student knows what he is seeking. By the subject (al-mawdu'), the science is distinguished from others, because all sciences are one genus and are only differentiated by their subjects. By the names (al-asma'), similarly the science is distinguished from others. By the benefit (al-fa'idah), the motivation for seeking it becomes strong. By the virtue (al-fadl), the honor of this science is known, so one's motivation to acquire it increases. By the relation (al-nisbah) to other sciences, its position among sciences is known. By the founder (al-wadi')—and by 'founder' is meant the codifier (al-mudawwin); otherwise, all sciences come from the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and the codifier is the one who collected all of that from the Messenger of Allah, his Companions, and their followers up to his own time—the founder and the one who placed the first seed of the science is known. By its source (istikmad), the origin of the science is known; and also this is related to the founder in that he collected the knowledge of those before him up to the Messenger of Allah, so it is known that he is not the originator or innovator of the science, but rather a collector and publicizer of what had been lost or hidden from minds. By the ruling of the Lawgiver (hukm al-shari') regarding it, the extent of one's need for this science is known—whether obligatory, recommended, permissible, or unnecessary if it falls into the realm of disliked or forbidden. By its issues (masa'il), the details of its subjects are known. See the introduction of Sharh al-Kharidah (p. 29).
Source pages p. 27

Commentary
This passage is a footnote explaining the ten principles (mabadi' 'asharah) that scholars use to define and understand any science. The commentator lists and describes each principle: (1) al-hadd (definition) helps the student know what he is studying; (2) al-mawdu' (subject) distinguishes one science from another; (3) al-asma' (names) also distinguish it; (4) al-fa'idah (benefit) motivates the seeker; (5) al-fadl (virtue) shows the science's honor; (6) al-nisbah (relation) shows its position relative to other sciences; (7) al-wadi' (founder) identifies the codifier (mudawwin)—clarifying that the true source of all knowledge is revelation from the Prophet, and the codifier merely collects and systematizes; (8) istimdad (source) reveals the origin of the science; (9) hukm al-shari' (legal ruling) indicates whether studying it is obligatory, recommended, etc.; (10) al-masa'il (issues) show the detailed questions of the subject. The commentator emphasizes that the 'founder' is not an innovator but a compiler who resurrects forgotten knowledge. This explanation serves as a methodological framework for properly situating the science of tawhid within the broader tradition.

Key Terms
الواضع
المدوّن
الاستمداد
حكم الشارع
المسائل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The 'founder' of a science is its originator or inventor.
Correction: The text explicitly states that the founder is the codifier who collects knowledge from the prophetic tradition; all sciences ultimately come from revelation, not human invention.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the role of the 'founder' (al-wadi') of a science?
A) To invent new knowledge
B) To codify and publicize existing knowledge from prophetic sources
C) To be the first teacher of the science
D) To write the rules of the science

Key Takeaway
The final four principles—founder, source, legal ruling, and issues—establish a science's prophetic origin, legal status, and detailed content.

Hope and Action: True Rajā' versus Wishful Thinking
Explanation of the poet's supplication and definition of true hope.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of true hope (rajā') in Islamic spirituality and how it is distinguished from mere wishful thinking (umniyya) by the necessity of accompanying action.

Core Idea
True hope is not wishful thinking; it is heart's attachment to good while actively taking means.

This lesson examines the eighth verse of the poem, where the poet expresses his hope for Allah's acceptance of his work and its benefit. The commentary by Shaykh al-Mishshāt clarifies the spiritual concept of hope (rajā') and quotes Ibn 'Aṭā'illāh to emphasize that genuine hope must be paired with action. We will explore the definitions and implications.

Guided Path
1
Read the verse and its grammatical emphasis
Begin by reading the verse (line 8) and note the grammatical construction: 'Allah' is placed before the verb to specify that hope is directed only to Him.
The accusative case (ikhtiṣāṣ) highlights exclusivity.
2
Understand the definitions of 'acceptance' and 'hope'
The commentator defines 'acceptance' (qabūl) as being pleased with something and not rejecting it. Then 'hope' (rajā') is defined linguistically as expectation and technically as the heart's attachment to obtaining the desired while taking the means.
The technical definition includes action.
3
Learn Ibn 'Aṭā'illāh's distinction
Ibn 'Aṭā'illāh states: 'Hope is what is accompanied by action, otherwise it is a wish (umniyya).' This distinguishes genuine hope from blameworthy wishful thinking.
Action is the key differentiator.
4
Reflect on the poet's compassion
The poet hopes his work benefits all who engage with it, showing compassion derived from the Prophet's character.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٨ | واللهَ أَرْجُو فِي قَبولِ العَمَلِ * والنَّفْعَ مِنْهَا ثُمَّ غَفْرَ الزَّلَلِ

شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي

دعاء وتضرع | (والله) بالنصب على الاختصاص مُقَدِّم على عامله، وهو (أرجو) أي: لا أرجو إلا الله (في قبول العمل الذي منه نظم هذه العقيدة، وقبول الشيء: الرضا به وعدم ردّه، والرجاء ـ بالمد لغة: الأمل، وعُرفًا: تعلُّق القلب بحصول المرغوب فيه مع الأخذ في الأسباب. قال سيدي أحمد بن عطاء رَضِوَاللَّهُ عَنْهُ في «الحكم» (۱): «الرجاء ما قارنه عمل ، وإلا فهو أمنية»، أي: طمع، وهو مذموم. (و) أرجوه النفع منها أي بها، لكل من قرأها، أو طالعها، أو حصلها، أو كتبها، كما قال الناظم في شرحه»(۲)، وهذا من كمال شفقة الناظم على من له أدنى اتصال به، وهو خُلُقٌ شريف مصدره خُلُقِ سيد الوجود الرؤوف الرحيم عَلَيْهِ الصَّلَاةُ وَالسَّلَامُ.
8. And upon Allah I hope for acceptance of the deed and benefit from it, then forgiveness of slips. Commentary by Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mishshāt al-Maliki al-Malikī: Supplication and humility. (And Allah) in the accusative as a specificational object, placed before its verb, which is (I hope), i.e., I hope only from Allah (in acceptance of the deed which includes the composition of this creed, and acceptance of something: being pleased with it and not rejecting it. And hope (rajā') – in language: expectation; in technical terms: the heart's attachment to obtaining the desired while taking the means. My master Ahmad ibn 'Aṭā'illāh, may Allah be pleased with him, said in al-Ḥikam: 'Hope is what is accompanied by action, otherwise it is a wish,' i.e., ambition, and it is blameworthy. (And) I hope for benefit from it, i.e., from it, for everyone who reads it, looks at it, studies it, or writes it, as the versifier said in his commentary. And this is from the perfection of the versifier's compassion for anyone with the slightest connection to him, and it is a noble character originating from the character of the Master of Existence, the Kind, the Merciful, peace and blessings be upon him.
Source pages p. 31

Commentary
This passage is a verse of poetry followed by a detailed exegetical commentary. The verse (line 8 of the poem) is a supplication: 'And upon Allah I hope for acceptance of the deed and benefit from it, then forgiveness of slips.' The commentator, Shaykh Hasan al-Mishshāt, begins by parsing the grammar: 'Allah' is in the accusative case as a specificational object (ikhtiṣāṣ) placed before its verb 'arjū' to emphasize that the poet hopes only from Allah, not from anyone else. He then defines the term 'acceptance' (qabūl) as being pleased with something and not rejecting it. Next, he defines 'hope' (rajā') both linguistically—meaning expectation—and technically in the spiritual path: the heart's attachment to obtaining what is desired while taking the necessary means. He then quotes Ibn 'Aṭā'illāh al-Sakandarī from his work al-Ḥikam, stating that true hope must be accompanied by action; otherwise it is merely 'wishful thinking' (umniyya) or ambition (ṭama'), which is blameworthy. The commentator then explains that the poet hopes for benefit from his work for all who engage with it—whether by reading, studying, or writing it—reflecting the poet's compassion. This compassion is a noble character trait derived from the character of the Prophet Muhammad, who is described as 'Kind, Merciful.' Thus, the passage defines key theological and spiritual concepts and emphasizes the importance of combining hope with action.

Key Terms
رجاء
أمنية
قبول
اختصاص
تعلق القلب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That hoping for good without taking action is sufficient in Islam.
Correction: True hope (rajā') must be accompanied by action; without action it is merely wishful thinking (umniyya) which is blameworthy.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what distinguishes true hope (rajā') from wishful thinking (umniyya)?
A) Hope is only for the Hereafter.
B) Hope must be accompanied by action.
C) Hope is only for prophets.
D) Wishful thinking is commendable.

Key Takeaway
Genuine hope in Allah is always accompanied by sincere action; without action, it is mere wishful thinking.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Is your hope for Allah's acceptance backed by concrete action today, or are you just waiting for it to happen? What one action will you take this week that proves your hope is genuine?
?
The poet's compassion extended to anyone who even reads his work. How does your hope for Allah's mercy affect how you treat others? Will you pray for those who benefit from your efforts?
?
Ibn 'Aṭā'illāh said, 'Hope is what is accompanied by action, otherwise it is a wish.' Which area of your life have you been merely wishing for change instead of taking the means? What will you start doing today to turn that wish into true hope?

True Hope vs. Wishful Thinking in Islam
Mashaat explains the poet's line on hope, acceptance, and forgiveness, focusing on the definition of hope.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the distinction between true hope (rajā') and mere wishful thinking (umniyya) according to Islamic theology, and why hope must be coupled with action.

Core Idea
Hope without action is just a wish.

In this passage, Shaykh Hasan al-Mashshāt unpacks the poet's invocation for acceptance and forgiveness by first clarifying what true hope means. He quotes the sage Ibn 'Aṭā' to draw a sharp line between hope that motivates action and idle wishing. This distinction is foundational for understanding Islamic spirituality and the balance between hope and fear.

Guided Path
1
Read the poet's line and commentary opening
Read the Arabic and translation, noting the grammar of 'Wa-llāha' and the definitions of acceptance and hope.
Notice the linguistic vs. technical definition of rajā'.
2
Analyze the quote from Ibn 'Aṭā'
Identify the key difference: hope is accompanied by action, wish is not. Reflect on why this distinction matters.
The quote is from al-Ḥikam.
3
Consider practical implications
True hope is practical and leads to effort. How does this apply to your own religious practice?
Think about the balance between reliance on God and personal effort.
4
Reflect on the story of the great Imam
Read the story about the Imam who, despite his knowledge, feared Allah's plot and did not rely on his intellect. This illustrates humility that accompanies true hope.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واللهَ أَرْجُو فِي قَبولِ العَمَلِ * والنَّفْعَ مِنْهَا ثُمَّ غَفْرَ الزَّلَلِ
شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي
دعاء وتضرع
(والله) بالنصب على الاختصاص مُقَدِّم على عامله، وهو (أرجو) أي: لا أرجو إلا الله (في قبول العمل الذي منه نظم هذه العقيدة، وقبول الشيء: الرضا به وعدم ردّه، والرجاء ـ بالمد لغة: الأمل، وعُرفًا: تعلُّق القلب بحصول المرغوب فيه مع الأخذ في الأسباب.
قال سيدي أحمد بن عطاء رَضِوَاللَّهُ عَنْهُ في «الحكم» (۱): «الرجاء ما قارنه عمل ، وإلا فهو أمنية»، أي: طمع، وهو مذموم. (و) أرجوه النفع منها أي بها، لكل من قرأها، أو طالعها، أو حصلها، أو كتبها، كما قال الناظم في شرحه (۲)، وهذا من كمال شفقة الناظم على من له أدنى اتصال به، وهو خُلُقٌ شريف مصدره خُلُقِ سيد الوجود الرؤوف الرحيم عَلَيْهِ الصَّلَاةُ وَالسَّلَامُ .
أن هذا مقام عظيم لا يتهيأ إلا لمثل هذا الإمام، وليس يُسْمَح به لكل أحد، فإن غائلته تخشى إلا على من بَرّز في العلوم، وبلغ في صحة الذهن مبلغ هذا الرجل العظيم، فأرشد إلى أن الذي ينبغي عدم الخوض في هذا واستعمال دين العجائز، ثم أشار إلى أنه مع بلوغه هذا المبلغ، وأخذه الحق عن الاجتهاد والبصيرة لا يأمن مكر الله، بل يعتقد أن الحق إن لم يدركه بلطفه ويختم له بكلمة الإخلاص فالويل له ولا ينفعه إذ ذاك علومه وإن كانت مثل مدد البحر.
فانظر هذه الحكاية ما أحسنها وأدلها على عظمة هذا الإمام، وتسليمه لربه تعالى، وتفويضه الأمر إليه، وعدم اتكاله على علومه، ثم تعجب بعدها من جاهل يفهم منها غير المراد ثم يخبط خبط عشواء.
(۱) (ص٥١) بشرح محمد حياة السندي.
(۲) (ص ۱۳۷).
(ثم) أي: وأرجوه (غَفْرَ) بفتح الغين المعجمة، وتسكين الفاء، أي: ستر (الزَّلَل) جمع زلة وهى المعاصي، وسترها إما محوها من الصحف أو بعدم المؤاخذة بها، وذلك مقتضى سعة كرم ربنا تَبَارَكَ وَتَعَالَى.
And I hope in Allah for acceptance of the deed and benefit from it, then forgiveness of slips. Explanation by Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashshāt al-Mālikī al-Makkī. Supplication and humility. (Wa-llāha) is in the accusative for specification, preceding its operator, which is (arjū), meaning: I do not hope except in Allah (for acceptance of the deed from which this creed is composed; and acceptance of a thing is being pleased with it and not rejecting it; and ar-rajā' – with long alif, linguistically: hope, and in conventional usage: the attachment of the heart to obtaining what is desired while engaging in the means. Master Aḥmad ibn ‘Aṭā’ – may Allah be pleased with him – said in al-Ḥikam (1): 'Hope is that which is accompanied by action, otherwise it is a mere wish,' i.e., covetousness, which is blameworthy. (And) I hope for benefit from it, i.e., through it, for everyone who reads it, or studies it, or memorizes it, or writes it, as the poet said in his commentary (2). This is from the perfection of the poet's compassion for anyone who has the slightest connection to him, and it is a noble character trait derived from the character of the Master of Existence, the Compassionate, the Merciful – may blessings and peace be upon him. That this is a great station that is not attained except by the likes of this Imām, and it is not permitted for everyone, for its harm is feared except for one who has excelled in the sciences and reached in soundness of intellect the level of this great man. So he guided to the fact that what is proper is not to delve into this and to use the religion of old women. Then he indicated that even with his reaching this level and taking the truth by ijtihād and insight, he is not safe from the plot of Allah. Rather, he believes that if the truth does not reach him by His gentleness and He does not seal for him with the word of sincerity, then woe to him, and his sciences – even if they were like the waves of the sea – will not benefit him at that time. So look at this story: how beautiful it is and how it indicates the greatness of this Imām, his submission to his Lord – exalted is He – his entrustment of the matter to Him, and his lack of reliance on his sciences. Then be amazed after it at an ignorant person who understands from it other than what is intended and then strikes blindly. (1) (p. 51) with the commentary of Muḥammad Ḥayāt al-Sindī. (2) (p. 137). (Thumma) i.e., and I hope for (ghafra) – with fatḥa on the ghayn and sukūn on the fā – i.e., covering (az-zalal) plural of zallat, which are acts of disobedience; and covering them either by erasing them from the scrolls or by not taking to account for them. And that is required by the vastness of the generosity of our Lord – blessed and exalted is He.
Source pages p. 31

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on the poet's line: 'And I hope in Allah for acceptance of the deed, and benefit from it, then forgiveness of slips.' The commentator, Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashshāt, explains each part. He begins by parsing the grammar of 'Wa-llāha' (and Allah) as an accusative of specification, emphasizing that hope is directed exclusively to Allah. Then he defines 'acceptance' (qabūl) as being pleased with a thing and not rejecting it, and 'hope' (rajā') as linguistically 'expectation' and in conventional usage the heart's attachment to obtaining a desired object while taking the necessary means. To clarify the nature of hope, he quotes Master Aḥmad ibn ‘Aṭā’ (may Allah be pleased with him) from al-Ḥikam: 'Hope is what is accompanied by action; otherwise it is a mere wish' (umniyya), which is blameworthy covetousness. This distinction is crucial: true hope is not idle wishing but is coupled with effort. Next, he explains that the poet hopes for benefit from the creed (the poem) for anyone who reads, studies, memorizes, or writes it, as the poet mentioned in his own commentary. This shows the poet's deep compassion and noble character, imitating the character of the Prophet (ﷺ), the Compassionate and Merciful. The commentator then relates a story about a great Imam (likely an earlier scholar) who reached the highest level of knowledge but still feared Allah's plot and did not rely on his own learning. The Imam advised that laypeople should adhere to 'the religion of old women' (simple faith) and avoid delving into theological intricacies. Even with his own mastery, the Imam recognized that without Allah's grace and a good end, knowledge is worthless. This story illustrates true humility and submission to Allah. The commentator admires the story then bemoans ignorant people who misunderstand it and act foolishly. Finally, he explains 'then forgiveness of slips' (thumma ghafra az-zalal). 'Ghafr' means covering, and 'zalal' (plural of zallat) means sins. Covering sins can be either by erasing them from the records or by not punishing for them. This is a consequence of Allah's abundant generosity.

Key Terms
رجاء
أمنية
قبول
غفر
زلل
عمل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Hope in God means just wishing and waiting for God's mercy without effort.
Correction: True Islamic hope must be accompanied by action; otherwise it is blameworthy wishful thinking (umniyya).

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what distinguishes true hope (rajā') from a mere wish (umniyya)?
A) True hope is accompanied by action, while a mere wish is not.
B) True hope is only for scholars, while a mere wish is for everyone.
C) True hope is for worldly gain, while a mere wish is for the afterlife.
D) True hope is always fulfilled, while a mere wish is not.

Key Takeaway
True hope is a dynamic state that combines trust in God with personal effort, unlike idle wishing.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Think about the hopes you carry for your afterlife—your desire for Paradise, forgiveness, and acceptance. Are these hopes actually driving you to action today, or are they just comfortable wishes? What is one concrete step you will take this week to turn a wish into genuine hope?
?
The great Imam feared that his vast knowledge would be worthless without Allah's seal of sincerity. What is the thing you rely on most—your prayers, your charity, your Islamic knowledge, your career? Does that reliance actually make you feel entitled to Allah's mercy? How will you combine your effort with humble dependence on Him?
?
If you were to die at this very moment, would your hope in Allah's mercy be stronger than your fear of being among the losers? What specific change in your daily attitude or action does this lesson demand of you starting tomorrow?

Method of Learning

The Fruits of Sincere Knowledge
The commentator enumerates the conditions for tasting the sweetness of knowledge and the role of sincerity.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the conditions for attaining the sweetness of sacred knowledge and the role of sincerity in unlocking divine reward.

Core Idea
Sacred knowledge yields its sweetness only when sought with sincerity and action.

Sidi ‘Abd al-Raḥmān al-Mashāṭ now turns to the reward of those who truly taste sacred knowledge. He describes not just the benefit but the conditions that unlock it: receiving from the experts, preserving, sincerity, and acting upon what one learns. This passage serves as a spiritual guide for the seeker of knowledge.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing, then read the literal translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that the passage moves from tasting knowledge to conditions to a secret.
2
Identify the conditions for tasting knowledge
The passage lists three conditions for being given the sweetness of knowledge. Find them and write them down.
3
Ponder the secret of sincerity
The passage ends by stating that sincerity is a secret between the servant and his Lord. Reflect on why this matters for the seeker of knowledge.
This connects to the learning objective about sincerity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فمن تَذَوّق العلم الشريف وأُعطي حلاوة العلوم والمعارف، فقد أُعطي خير الدنيا والآخرة، وهذا إنما يكون عن: تلقي العلم من العارفين به والقيام بحفظه والإخلاص في طلبه، فإن عَمِل بما عَلِم وَرَّثه الله عِلْمٌ ما لم يكن يعلمه، والإخلاص هو سر بين العبد وربه لا يطلع عليه ملك فيكتبه، ولا شيطان فيفسده
So whoever tastes sacred knowledge and is given the sweetness of the sciences and knowledges, he has been given the good of this world and the Hereafter. That only happens by: receiving knowledge from those who know it, and preserving it, and sincerity in seeking it. For if he acts upon what he knows, Allah will bequeath to him knowledge of what he did not know. And sincerity is a secret between the servant and his Lord; no angel sees it to record it, and no devil to spoil it.
Source pages p. 34

Commentary
This passage discusses the reward of tasting sacred knowledge. It states that one who tastes sacred knowledge and is given its sweetness has been given the good of both worlds. The conditions for that are: receiving knowledge from those who possess it, preserving it, and sincerity in seeking it. If one acts upon what he knows, God gives him knowledge of what he did not know. Sincerity is a secret between the servant and God, unknown to angels or devils.

Key Terms
العلم
الإخلاص
اللذة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Acquiring any knowledge by any means brings reward.
Correction: The text specifies that knowledge must be sought with sincerity, preservation, and from experts, and must be acted upon.

Checkpoint
What is the secret between the servant and his Lord according to this passage?
A) Knowledge
B) Preservation
C) Sincerity
D) Action

Key Takeaway
Tasting sacred knowledge and gaining its reward requires sincerity, proper transmission, and action upon that knowledge.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You say you seek knowledge for Allah, but when you study alone, do you ever find yourself wanting to be seen as knowledgeable by others? What would it take for you to make your sincerity so hidden that no angel could record it? Make a concrete plan to check your intention before each study session today.
?
Of the three conditions mentioned – receiving from experts, preserving, and sincerity – which one are you actively neglecting right now? If you are honest, what is the single step you will take today to address that neglect? Write it down and commit to it.
?
If acting on what you know truly unlocks knowledge you don't yet have, what is a piece of Islamic knowledge you possess but are not implementing? Why are you holding back? What would change in your life if you started acting on it tonight? Decide now to take one small action within the next hour.

The Definition and Nature of Tasawwuf
Defining tasawwuf as a science and practice, and its relation to Shari'a, Tariqa, Haqiqa.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the dual definition of tasawwuf as a science and a practice, its subject matter, goal, and its relationship to Shari'a, Tariqa, and Haqiqa.

Core Idea
Tasawwuf is the science of purifying the heart and acquiring Muhammadan character through practice.

This lesson establishes tasawwuf as the noblest of sciences, one that depends on knowledge of faith. It provides a formal definition that distinguishes tasawwuf as a science (principles for heart rectification) and as a practice (cautious adherence). It also clarifies the key triad: Shari'a (rulings), Tariqa (the path), and Haqiqa (spiritual secrets).

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the continuous Arabic passage once to get a sense of its flow. Then read the literal English translation. Notice the footnote definition and the concluding clarification of Shari'a, Tariqa, and Haqiqa.
The passage begins with an introductory praise and then gives the definition.
2
Identify the two aspects of the definition
The passage defines tasawwuf in two ways: as a science ('ilman) and as a practice ('amalan). Find each definition and note what each emphasizes.
The definition as science focuses on principles; as practice focuses on actions.
3
Note the goal and subject matter
The passage states the goal (ghaya) and subject (mawdu') of tasawwuf. The goal is both worldly (rectification) and otherworldly (high ranks). The subject is the Muhammadan character traits.
These elements complete the formal definition of a science.
4
Understand the triad: Shari'a, Tariqa, Haqiqa
The passage explains that tasawwuf as practice is Tariqa, Shari'a is the set of rulings, and Haqiqa is the inner secrets and result of the path. Reflect on how these three relate.
Haqiqa is obtained after purification of the heart.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وهذا شروع من الناظم نفعنا الله بعلومه، وأمدنا بإمداداته وأسراره في فن التصوف (١) الذي هو أشرف العلوم، وبه صلاح القلب، وسائر الحواس مرتب على معرفة عقائد الإيمان، لأنه لا يُمكن السير إلى الله تعالى إلا بعد معرفتها، وهذا من بعض فوائد هذا العلم.
(١) في المطبوعة «التوحيد»، وحَدُّ التصوف عِلمًا: هو عِلْمٌ بأصول يُعرف به صلاح القلب، وسائر الحواس وعملا هو الأخذ بالأحوط من المأمورات، واجتناب المنهيات والاقتصار على الضروريات من المباحات وغايته: صلاح القلب وسائر الحواس في الدنيا، والفوز بأعلى المراتب في العُقْبَى. وموضوعه الأخلاق المحمدية من حيث التخلق بها.
واعلم أن التصوف بمعنى العمل، هو الطريقة، وأما الشريعة فهي الأحكام التي وردت عن الشارع المعبر عنها بالدين، وأما الحقيقة: فهي أسرار الشريعة ونتيجة الطريقة، فهي علوم ومعارف تحصل لقلوب السالكين بعد صفائها من كدرات الطباع البشرية.
This is a beginning from the author—may Allah benefit us with his knowledge and supply us with his effusions and secrets—in the art of tasawwuf, which is the noblest of sciences. Through it comes the rectification of the heart and all the senses, and it is dependent upon knowledge of the beliefs of faith, for it is not possible to journey to Allah Most High except after knowing them. This is one of the benefits of this science.
(1) In the printed edition: [it says] 'at-tawheed'; and the definition of tasawwuf as a science: it is a knowledge of principles by which the rectification of the heart and all the senses is known. As a practice, it is taking the most precautionary [approach] from the commanded matters, avoiding the prohibited matters, and limiting to the necessities from the permissible matters. Its goal: the rectification of the heart and all the senses in this world, and attaining the highest ranks in the Hereafter. Its subject is the Muhammadan character traits from the perspective of acquiring them. And know that tasawwuf in the sense of practice is the path (tariqa); as for the Shari'a, it is the rulings that came from the Lawgiver, expressed by religion; and as for the Reality (haqiqa), it is the secrets of the Shari'a and the result of the path. These are sciences and gnoses that are obtained in the hearts of the wayfarers after their purification from the impurities of human nature.
Source pages p. 104

Commentary
This passage introduces the topic of tasawwuf (Sufism) as a noble science. The commentator first praises the author and states that tasawwuf is the most honorable of sciences because it leads to the rectification of the heart and senses, but it depends on prior knowledge of the beliefs of faith (aqa'id al-iman). Then in a footnote, he provides a formal definition. He distinguishes two aspects: (1) as a science ('ilman): it is a body of principles by which one knows how to rectify the heart and senses; (2) as a practice ('amalan): it is taking the most precautionary rulings in commands, avoiding prohibitions, and limiting to necessities in permissible matters. The goal (ghaya) is spiritual rectification in this world and highest ranks in the hereafter. The subject matter (mawdu') is the Muhammadan character traits (akhlaq muhammadiyya) in terms of acquiring them. Then he clarifies the relationship between Shari'a, Tariqa, and Haqiqa: Shari'a is the set of rulings, Tariqa is the practical path of tasawwuf, and Haqiqa is the spiritual secrets and results that arise from purifying the heart.

Key Terms
التصوف
الطريقة
الشريعة
الحقيقة
الغاية
الموضوع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Tasawwuf is only about asceticism without scholarly grounding.
Correction: This definition shows tasawwuf is a science with principles and a specific subject matter, not mere asceticism.

Checkpoint

A) Principles of logic
B) Muhammadan character traits
C) Knowledge of God's essence
D) Rules of jurisprudence

Key Takeaway
Tasawwuf is both a science of principles for heart rectification and a practice of cautious adherence, leading to spiritual realities.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have learned that tasawwuf is the science of rectifying the heart and senses. When you examine your heart right now, what is one impurity—like pride, envy, or heedlessness—that you know is present? What is one principle from this lesson you will use tonight to begin purifying it?
?
The passage states that tasawwuf depends on correct beliefs of faith. Is there any belief about Allah, the Prophet, or the unseen that you have not fully settled in your heart? How might that uncertainty be blocking your spiritual progress, and what step will you take this week to resolve it?
?
Shari'a, Tariqa, Haqiqa: which of these three are you most neglecting? Are you fasting and praying but ignoring the inner work (Tariqa), or seeking spiritual experiences without obeying Allah's commands (Shari'a)? Commit to one specific action today to restore balance.

Etiquettes of Dhikr: The Adab of Remembrance
The author enumerates five etiquettes of dhikr and provides a detailed supplication.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the essential etiquettes that accompany the remembrance of Allah and their importance for spiritual purification.

Core Idea
Dhikr without adab is like a body without a soul.

The passage begins by urging the student to increase in remembrance of the kalima of tawhid. But this is not merely about repetition; the heart must be prepared through specific etiquettes. The author then lists five key practices that transform dhikr from a mechanical act into a means of spiritual purification. Let's explore these etiquettes as taught by the great scholar.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify the etiquettes
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice that after the initial command, the author lists several etiquettes. Count them and see how each one is described.
The etiquettes are: tawba, tahara, turning to Allah, istighfar, and salawat.
2
Understand the importance of adab
Reflect on why the author emphasizes performing dhikr 'with adab'. How does this affect the quality of remembrance?
3
Memorize the Sayyid al-Istighfar
The passage quotes the best formula for seeking forgiveness. Commit it to memory, as it is a powerful supplication.
It begins with 'Allahumma anta Rabbi...'.
4
Consider the role of salawat
Note that the author instructs to send abundant blessings on the Prophet while facing the qibla. Think about why this is included among the etiquettes.
It connects the dhikr of tawhid to the love of the Prophet.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
إذا علمت ذلك ( فأكثرن) بهمزة القطع المفتوحة، ونون التوكيد الخفيفة من ذكرها أي كلمة الإسلام، (بالأدب) أي: مع القيام بالأدب. وعليك أيها الطالب الصالح الإكثار من ذكر كلمة التوحيد لا إله إلا الله محمد رسول الله، فإنه لا شيء أقرب لصفاء القلب من كثرة ذكرها، مع القيام بالآداب التي منها: تجديد التوبة، مما وقع فيه من المخالفة، والخواطر الرديئة. وأن يتطهر من الحدث والخبث. وأن يتوجه إلى الله عَزَّوَجَلَّ برغبة لتحصل له الجمعية في الذكر. وأن يستغفر الله تعالى بما تيسر بأي صيغة كانت، وأفضلها سيد الاستغفار، وهو (اللهم أنت ربي لا إله إلا أنت خلقتني وأنا عبدك، وأنا على عهدك ووعدك ما استطعت أعوذ بك من شر ما صنعت، أبوء لك بنعمتك علي وأبوء بذنبي فاغفر لي فإنه لا يغفر الذنوب إلا أنت). ويكثر من الصلاة والسلام على النبي صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ مع استقبال القبلة.
When you know that, then increase—with a fat-ha on the alif of the imperative and a light nun of emphasis—in remembrance of it, i.e., the word of Islam, with adab (etiquette), i.e., with observance of etiquette. And it is incumbent upon you, O righteous student, to increase in remembrance of the word of tawhid: 'La ilaha illa Allah, Muhammadun Rasul Allah'. For nothing is closer to the purification of the heart than frequent remembrance of it, while observing the etiquettes which include: renewing repentance from what one has fallen into of violations and evil thoughts; and purifying oneself from ritual impurity and physical impurity; and turning to Allah Almighty with desire so that focus in remembrance is attained; and seeking forgiveness from Allah Almighty as much as is easy, using any formula, the best of which is Sayyid al-Istighfar: 'O Allah, You are my Lord, there is no god but You, You created me and I am Your servant, and I am upon Your covenant and promise as much as I am able. I seek refuge in You from the evil of what I have done. I acknowledge Your favor upon me and I acknowledge my sin, so forgive me, for none forgives sins except You.' And he should increase in sending blessings and peace upon the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, while facing the qibla.
Source pages p. 104

Commentary
This passage begins by explaining the verse 'fa-akthir min dhikriha bi al-adab' (so increase in remembrance of it with etiquette). It clarifies that 'fa-akthir' is an imperative with emphasis, and 'dhikriha' refers to the word of Islam (the kalima). 'Bi al-adab' means with observance of proper conduct. The author then directly addresses the student, urging frequent recitation of the testimony of faith, 'La ilaha illa Allah, Muhammadun Rasul Allah', because nothing purifies the heart more than that. However, this dhikr must be accompanied by specific etiquettes, which are then listed: 1) renewing repentance (tawba) from sins and bad thoughts, 2) ritual purification (tahara) from hadath (minor impurity) and khabath (physical impurity), 3) turning to Allah with sincere desire to achieve presence of heart (jama'iyya) in dhikr, 4) seeking forgiveness (istighfar) with any formula, the best being Sayyid al-Istighfar (the master prayer for forgiveness), which is quoted in full, 5) sending abundant blessings and peace upon the Prophet while facing the qibla. These etiquettes are presented as essential for the effectiveness of dhikr. The passage is a practical guide, emphasizing that the external actions prepare the heart for spiritual realization.

Key Terms

الأدب
التوبة
الاستغفار
سيد الاستغفار
الصلاة على النبي
الجمعية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Dhikr is merely mechanical repetition of words
Correction: This passage shows that dhikr must be accompanied by adab, including repentance, purification, and inner presence, making it a holistic act of worship.

Checkpoint

A) Astaghfirullah al-azim
B) Sayyid al-Istighfar as quoted
C) Subhanallah wa bihamdihi

Key Takeaway
The effectiveness of dhikr depends on observing its etiquettes: repentance, purification, heartfelt turning to Allah, seeking forgiveness, and honoring the Prophet.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you say 'La ilaha illa Allah', do you pause to consider if your heart is truly turning away from everything else? What would it take to make that moment a true renewal of your covenant with Allah?
?
Repentance is listed first among the etiquettes. Is there a specific sin or bad habit you have been tolerating that you know weakens your dhikr? When will you sincerely repent and remove that barrier?
?
Facing the qibla and sending blessings on the Prophet is the final etiquette. Have you ever felt a genuine connection to the Prophet in your dhikr? What would change if you consciously imagined standing before him as you send salawat?

Pre-Dhikr Etiquettes: Repentance, Purification, and Turning to Allah
Al-Mashat lists pre-dhikr etiquettes: repentance, purification, turning to Allah, seeking forgiveness, sending blessings, facing qibla.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the spiritual and physical preparations required before dhikr, including repentance, purification, seeking forgiveness with the best formula, and facing the qibla.

Core Idea
Dhikr requires preparation of heart and body: repentance, purity, and sincere turning to Allah.

Before engaging in the remembrance of Allah, a Muslim must prepare both inwardly and outwardly. Imam al-Mashat outlines a series of etiquettes that ensure the heart is present and the act is accepted. This lesson presents his guidance on these essential preparations.

Guided Path
1
Identify the prerequisites for dhikr
Read the passage carefully and list all the etiquettes mentioned for preparing for dhikr. Notice the sequence.
There are five main etiquettes, some with subdetails.
2
Understand the importance of repentance and purification
Repentance cleanses the heart from sins, while purification (tahara) cleanses the body. Both are necessary for a proper dhikr.
The passage emphasizes renewing repentance (tawba) and purifying from hadath and khabath.
3
Learn the best formula for seeking forgiveness
The passage quotes Sayyid al-Istighfar in full. Reflect on its meaning and why it is considered the best.
This supplication acknowledges Allah's lordship, one's servitude, and seeks forgiveness.
4
Apply the etiquettes to your own practice
Think about how you can incorporate these etiquettes before your daily dhikr or prayers. Which ones are you already doing? Which need improvement?
The author stresses adherence for those seeking the path of the folk (Sufis).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
تجديد التوبة، مما وقع فيه من المخالفة، والخواطر الرديئة. وأن يتطهر من الحدث والخبث. وأن يتوجه إلى الله عَزَّوَجَلَّ برغبة لتحصل له الجمعية في الذكر. وأن يستغفر الله تعالى بما تيسر بأي صيغة كانت، وأفضلها سيد الاستغفار، وهو (اللهم أنت ربي لا إله إلا أنت خلقتني وأنا عبدك، وأنا على عهدك ووعدك ما استطعت أعوذ بك من شر ما صنعت، أبوء لك بنعمتك علي وأبوء بذنبي فاغفر لي فإنه لا يغفر الذنوب إلا أنت). ويكثر من الصلاة والسلام على النبي صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ مع استقبال القبلة. وقد ذكر الناظم رحمه الله تعالى كثيرًا من هذه الآداب، فعليك بها إن أردت طريق القوم.
Renewing repentance from what has occurred of violations and bad thoughts. And that one purify oneself from ritual impurity and physical impurity. And that one turn to Allah, Mighty and Majestic, with desire so that concentration in dhikr may be attained. And that one seek forgiveness from Allah, the Exalted, with whatever is easy, in any formula, the best of which is the master of seeking forgiveness: 'O Allah, You are my Lord, there is no god but You, You created me and I am Your servant, and I am upon Your covenant and promise as much as I am able, I seek refuge in You from the evil of what I have done, I acknowledge Your blessing upon me and I acknowledge my sin, so forgive me for none forgives sins except You.' And that one send many blessings and peace upon the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, while facing the qibla. And the author (al-nazim), may Allah have mercy on him, has mentioned many of these etiquettes, so adhere to them if you desire the path of the folk.
Source pages p. 105

Commentary
This passage describes the preliminary etiquettes (adab) that one should observe before beginning the dhikr of Allah. First, one must renew repentance from any sins and bad thoughts. Second, one must purify oneself from ritual impurity (hadath) and physical impurity (khabath). Third, one must turn to Allah with sincere desire so that concentration (jam'iyya) in dhikr may be attained. Fourth, one should seek forgiveness with any formula, the best being sayyid al-istighfar, which the author quotes in full. Fifth, one should send many blessings on the Prophet while facing the qibla. The author notes that the poet (al-nazim) has mentioned many of these etiquettes, and he commands the reader to adhere to them if they seek the path of the beloved folk (the Sufis).

C) Sayyid al-Istighfar

Key Takeaway

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
The author says these etiquettes are for 'whoever desires the path of the folk.' Do you truly desire that path? If these preparations seem heavy, what does that reveal about the sincerity of your desire for closeness to Allah? Will you take one step today to prepare properly?

Etiquettes of Dhikr: During and After
Al-Mashat details the etiquettes to observe during and after the dhikr session.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the proper etiquettes to observe while performing dhikr and after its completion, as taught by the spiritual masters.

Core Idea
Dhikr is not just words; its power lies in intention, posture, and stillness to receive divine influxes.

In this passage, Sheikh al-Mashat outlines the etiquettes that accompany the remembrance of Allah. These guidelines transform the dhikr from a mere verbal repetition into a spiritual journey. We learn the importance of purification of intention, posture, and the concluding formula, as well as the essential stillness after the dhikr to allow divine blessings to settle in the heart.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyze it. Then read the translation to get an overall sense of the etiquettes.
Notice the repeated use of 'wa an' which introduces each etiquette.
2
Identify the key etiquettes
List the etiquettes mentioned: intention, banishing distractions, posture, eye closure, pronouncing the kalima, concluding formula, post-dhikr silence.
These are all presented as imperatives or conditions for proper dhikr.
3
Reflect on the spiritual significance
For each etiquette, ask yourself why it matters spiritually. For example, closing the eyes helps illuminate the heart; silence allows waridat to settle.
The passage explicitly states reasons for some etiquettes.
4
Apply the etiquettes
Consider how you can incorporate these etiquettes into your own dhikr practice. Which ones do you already observe? Which need improvement?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقوة، وأن يكون ذكره رغبة في مرضات الله ومحبته، وامتثالاً لأمره، لا لرياء، ولا سمعة، ولا لطلب أمر دنيوي، أو أخروي، وأن ينفي الأكوان من قلبه، لأن ملاحظة شيءٍ منها قاطع عن الله تعالى، ولولا أن للشيخ مدخلا في السير ما سوغوا له ملاحظته في حال البداية، وأن يجلس كجلوسه في التشهد، إلا لتعب فيجوز التربع، وأن يغمض عينيه، لأن له تأثيرًا في تنوير القلب، وأن يبتدئ بـ«لا» جهة اليمين، ويرجع بـ «إله»، ويختم بـ «الله» جهة اليسار مشيرًا إلى قلبه، فإذا أراد ختم الذكر ختمه بـ «محمد رسول الله». وأما الآداب البعدية: فإنه يسكت ويسكن بخشوع، فإن للذكر وارداتٍ ترد على قلب الذاكر، ولا يتمكن الوارد من القلب إلا بذلك.
And strength, and that his remembrance should be out of desire for Allah's good pleasure and love, and compliance with His command, not for show, nor for reputation, nor for seeking a worldly or otherworldly matter. And that he banish existences from his heart, because observing anything from them cuts one off from Allah, the Exalted; were it not that the shaykh has an entrance in the journey, they would not have allowed him to observe it at the beginning stage. And that he sit like his sitting in the tashahhud, unless there is fatigue, then cross-legged is permissible. And that he close his eyes, because it has an effect in illuminating the heart. And that he begin with 'la' from the right side, and return with 'ilaha', and end with 'Allah' from the left side, pointing to his heart. Then when he wishes to conclude the dhikr, he ends it with 'Muhammad Rasul Allah'. As for the post-dhikr etiquettes: then he remains silent and still with humility, for the dhikr has influxes (waridat) that descend upon the heart of the rememberer, and the influx cannot settle in the heart except by that.
Source pages p. 106

Commentary
This passage details the etiquettes to be observed during and after the dhikr. First, the intention must be pure: desire for Allah's pleasure and love, compliance with His command, not for show or worldly gain. Second, one must banish all thoughts of created things from the heart, as they distract from Allah. However, the author notes the exception that a shaykh may allow a beginner to observe the shaykh's presence. Third, the posture: sit as in tashahhud (on the left foot, right foot upright), but if tired, cross-legged is allowed. Fourth, close eyes to aid illumination of the heart. Fifth, the specific method of pronouncing the kalima: begin with 'la' (of la ilaha) from the right side, return with 'ilaha', and end with 'Allah' from the left, pointing to the heart. To end the dhikr, say 'Muhammad Rasul Allah'. Post-dhikr: remain silent and still with humility so that the divine influxes (waridat) can settle in the heart.

Key Terms
إخلاص
نفي الأكوان
تشهد
واردات
ذكر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The physical movements and postures in dhikr are merely ritualistic and have no deeper meaning.
Correction: The passage explains that these actions have spiritual effects, such as closing the eyes to illuminate the heart, and the posture aids in allowing divine influxes to settle.

Checkpoint
How does one conclude the dhikr according to this passage?
A) With 'Allahu Akbar'
B) With 'Subhanallah'
C) With 'Muhammad Rasul Allah'
D) With 'Alhamdulillah'

Key Takeaway
The effectiveness of dhikr depends on proper intention, physical posture, and post-dhikr stillness to allow divine influxes to enter the heart.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sit for dhikr, do you genuinely banish all thoughts of your work, family, and worries from your heart, or do you let them linger? What would it take for you to truly empty your heart so that only Allah remains? Will you commit to a 5-minute practice of nafi al-akwan before your next dhikr?
?
The passage says closing your eyes illuminates your heart. Have you ever experienced the difference between dhikr with eyes open and closed? What stops you from closing your eyes—fear of losing control, or mere habit? Can you try closing your eyes in your next dhikr and see if your heart feels more present?
?
After dhikr, do you rush off to the next task, or do you remain silent and still to receive the waridat? Honestly, how often have you missed these divine influxes because you were impatient? Will you make a covenant with yourself to sit in stillness for at least one minute after every dhikr session, even if nothing seems to happen at first?

Etiquette of Dhikr: Inward Sincerity and Outward Posture
Author lists recommended etiquette for dhikr, both before and after.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the recommended etiquette for performing dhikr, including the inner state of sincerity and the outer postures and movements, as well as the practice after completing the dhikr.

Core Idea
Dhikr requires both inward sincerity and outward posture to receive spiritual influx.

This lesson presents the proper etiquette for dhikr as described by the author. It covers both the pre-conditions—such as sincerity, detachment from worldly thoughts, and specific sitting and eye positions—and the post-dhikr practice of stillness to allow the spiritual influx (warid) to settle in the heart. These guidelines help the practitioner remain focused and receptive.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without overanalyzing. Then read the English translation to get a clear sense of the instructions.
2
Identify the pre-etiquette points
List the recommended actions before starting the dhikr: sincerity, detachment from created things, sitting posture, closing eyes, and the specific movement of the tongue from right to left.
Notice that some points are about inner state and others about outer form.
3
Note the post-etiquette
Read the last sentence about remaining silent and still after dhikr, and understand why it is important.
The author explains that dhikr brings spiritual influences that need stillness to settle.
4
Reflect on the purpose
Ask yourself why these etiquettes are recommended. They are not arbitrary but designed to maximize spiritual benefit and minimize distraction.
The core idea is that correct form aids inner focus.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأن يكون ذكره رغبة في مرضات الله ومحبته، وامتثالا لأمره، لا لرياء، ولا سمعة، ولا لطلب أمر دنيوي، أو أخروي، وأن ينفي الأكوان من قلبه، لأن ملاحظة شيءٍ منها قاطع عن الله تعالى، ولولا أن للشيخ مدخلا في السير ما سؤغوا له ملاحظته في حال البداية، وأن يجلس كجلوسه في التشهد، إلا لتعب فيجوز التربع، وأن يغمض عينيه، لأن له تأثيرًا في تنوير القلب، وأن يبتدئ بـ«لا» جهة اليمين، ويرجع بـ «إله» ، ويختم بـ «الله» جهة اليسار مشيرًا إلى قلبه، فإذا أراد ختم الذکر ختمه بـ «محمد رسول الله». وأما الآداب البعدية: فإنه يسكت ويسكن بخشوع، فإن للذكر واردات ترد على قلب الذاكر، ولا يتمكن الوارد من القلب إلا بذلك.
And that his remembrance (dhikr) be out of desire for Allah's pleasure and love, and in compliance with His command, not out of hypocrisy, nor for reputation, nor to seek a worldly or otherworldly matter, and that he remove (the perception of) created things from his heart, because noticing any of them cuts one off from Allah the Exalted. Were it not that the shaykh has an entry (method) in the spiritual journey, they would not have permitted him to notice them at the beginning stage. And that he sit like his sitting in the tashahhud, except if tired, then cross-legged is permissible. And that he close his eyes, because it has an effect in illuminating the heart. And that he begin with 'la' from the right side, and return with 'ilaha', and seal with 'Allah' from the left side, pointing to his heart. If he wishes to end the dhikr, he ends it with 'Muhammad Rasul Allah'. As for the after-etiquette: he remains silent and still with humility, for indeed dhikr has influences (waridat) that descend upon the heart of the one remembering, and the influence does not settle in the heart except through that.
Source pages p. 106

Commentary
This passage is a set of instructions for the etiquette (adab) of dhikr. It first describes the pre-conditions: sincerity and detachment from worldly motives. The practitioner must remove all thoughts of created things from his heart. The passage then lists the physical postures: sitting like in tashahhud, closing the eyes, and the specific movement of the tongue 'la ilaha illa Allah' from right to left to the heart. Ending the dhikr with 'Muhammad Rasul Allah' is recommended. Finally, after the dhikr, one should be silent and still to allow the spiritual influx (warid) to settle in the heart.

Key Terms
الذكر
الآداب السابقة
الآداب البعدية
الواردات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think the specific physical postures are mandatory and must be observed strictly without exception.
Correction: The passage indicates that these are recommended etiquettes; for example, cross-legged sitting is permissible if one is tired, showing flexibility.

Checkpoint
What should be done after completing the dhikr according to the after-etiquette?
A) Repeat the dhikr aloud
B) Stand up immediately
C) Remain silent and still with humility
D) Recite the Quran

Key Takeaway
Proper dhikr combines sincere intention, detachment from distractions, specific physical postures, and post-dhikr stillness to allow spiritual influx to settle in the heart.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you engage in dhikr, is your heart truly desiring Allah's pleasure, or are you merely going through motions out of habit or to feel spiritual? What would it take for you to purify your intention before every dhikr session starting today?

?
After you finish dhikr, do you rush to your phone, work, or other distractions, or do you allow yourself to sit in stillness for the spiritual influx to settle? What fear or discomfort keeps you from that silence, and will you commit to five minutes of stillness after your next dhikr?

Balancing Fear and Hope
Author explains the necessity of both fear and hope, and when to predominate each.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the spiritual states of fear (khawf) and hope (raja') are both necessary, and when each should be given predominance based on one's state of health.

Core Idea
Fear and hope are like a bird's two wings; both needed, but balance changes with life's stage.

This passage from Al-Bahja Al-Saniyya offers practical spiritual advice: every believer must possess both fear of Allah's power and hope in His mercy. Using the metaphor of a bird's wings, the author emphasizes that neither state can be abandoned. The crucial lesson is knowing when to let one dominate over the other—fear during health to curb sins, hope near death to foster reliance on Allah's mercy.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic passage aloud or silently. Then read the English translation to grasp the meaning.
Pay attention to the key terms: الخوف, الرجاء, تغليب.
2
Understand the necessity of both states
The author states that both fear and hope are necessary for every person, likening them to the two wings of a bird. Without one, one falls. Reflect on why both are essential.
This counteracts the misconception that one can rely solely on hope or solely on fear.
3
Learn the rule for predominance
The passage gives a clear rule: in health, fear should dominate; near death, hope should dominate. Understand the wisdom behind this timing.
The purpose of predominating fear in health is to remove follies from the heart.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الخوف والرجاء وغلب أيها الذاكر لمولاك جل وعلا في حال صحتك الخوف من الله تعالى، وسطوته وقهره، على الرجاء في رحمته وعفوه، وهما حالتان لا بد منهما لكل إنسان، ولا يخلو منهما أحد سلك الطريق أو لا، كما أنهما مثل جناحي الطائر، من فقد أحدهما سقط. إلا أنه في حال الصحة ينبغي تغليب جانب الخوف على جانب الرجاء، إذ به تزول الرعونات عن القلب بمشيئة الله تعالى، فإذا نزل به المرض وأشرف على الموت ينبغي تغليب جانب الرجاء.
Fear and hope, and predominate, O you who remember your Lord, Mighty and Majestic, in your state of health, fear of Allah the Exalted, His overpowering and His force, over hope in His mercy and pardon. Both are states necessary for every person, and no one is free from them, whether he traverses the path or not. They are like the two wings of a bird; whoever loses one of them falls. However, in the state of health, it is appropriate to make the side of fear predominate over the side of hope, for by it, follies are removed from the heart by the will of Allah the Exalted. And if illness befalls him and he nears death, it is appropriate to make the side of hope predominate.
Source pages p. 107

Commentary
This passage is a directive on the spiritual states of fear (khawf) and hope (raja'). The author states that both are essential for every person, comparing them to the two wings of a bird—without one, one falls. The key teaching is the timing of predominance: during health, fear should be stronger to prevent sins and follies; near death, hope should prevail to foster trust in Allah's mercy. This balances the believer's psychology.

Key Terms
الخوف
الرجاء
تغليب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One should always fear Allah or always hope in His mercy.
Correction: Both fear and hope are necessary, but their predominance depends on one's state: fear in health, hope near death.

Checkpoint
When should hope (raja') be given more weight than fear (khawf)?
A) When one is healthy
B) When one is about to die
C) Always
D) Never

Key Takeaway
Fear and hope are essential spiritual states; fear should dominate in health to prevent sins, and hope should dominate near death to foster trust in Allah's mercy.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
In your current state of health, which wing is stronger—fear or hope? Be honest: do you rely more on fear of Allah to stay away from sin, or do you presume upon His mercy? How has that balance affected your actions this past week? This lesson commands you to let fear predominate in health. Will you commit to recalling Allah's power and justice daily to guard against follies? Write one specific area where you need to increase fear.
?
When you imagine yourself on your deathbed, does the thought of Allah's mercy bring you peace, or do you feel anxiety? The author instructs that hope should predominate near death. Are you cultivating that hope now, while you have time? What specific aspects of Allah's mercy do you need to remind yourself of to strengthen your hope? Take a moment to repent sincerely and resolve to increase your good deeds as a sign of hope.
?
The analogy of two wings: if you lost one, you would fall. Which wing in your life is weaker—fear or hope? What concrete step can you take today to strengthen that wing, based on the guidance in this passage about the appropriate predominance for your current state? Do not let this pass; the balance determines your spiritual flight.
