The World: Definition and Its Temporality
Commentator defines the world and states its temporality and dependence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of the world in Islamic theology and why its temporality and dependence are considered certain proofs for the existence of Allah.

Core Idea
The world is everything besides Allah, and its temporality proves it needs a Creator.

After establishing the framework of rational judgments, the commentator turns to the path that leads to knowledge of Allah: the temporal origination of the world. This passage defines what is meant by 'the world' and establishes two essential attributes—temporality and dependence—that form the basis for the classical proof of God's existence. Let us read the text and unpack its meaning step by step.

Guided Path
1
Read and locate the key terms
Begin by reading the Arabic passage and its translation. Identify the terms 'عالم' (world), 'حادث' (temporal), and 'مفتقر' (dependent). Notice how the commentator explains them.
The word 'عالم' here means everything other than Allah.
2
Understand the definition of 'world'
The commentator explicitly defines the world as 'ما سوى الله تعالى' (everything other than Allah). He further divides it into substances and accidents. Grasp these categories, as they are foundational.
Substances (جواهر) subsist by themselves; accidents (أعراض) subsist in substances.
3
Examine the two attributes: temporal and dependent
The passage states that the world is 'حادث' (temporal) and 'مفتقر' (dependent) without doubt. 'Temporal' means existing after non-existence; 'dependent' means it needs an originator. Note that 'dependent' follows logically from 'temporal'.
'Without doubt' (من غير شك) indicates certainty, though the commentator notes it includes conjecture and illusion.
4
Grasp the implied proof
The commentator provides evidence: perceived changes in the world and its need for an originator. These observations demonstrate temporality and dependence, which point to Allah as the Creator.
This is the classic argument from contingency.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولما فرغ الناظم رحمه الله تعالى من بيان أقسام الحكم العقلي ووجوب معرفة الله تعالى على كل مكلف، أخذ في بيان الطريق الموصل إلى معرفة الله تعالى، وهو حدوث هذا العالم فقال: (ثم) لما عرفت ما يجب عليك أيها المكلف شرعًا من حدوث العالم دليل على حق الله، وما يجوز، وما يستحيل، فـ (اعلمن) بنون التوكيد الخفيفة، معرفة الله تعالى للحث والتنبيه (بأن هذا العالما) المشاهد بجميع أجزائه، والألف فيه لإطلاق القافية. وفسر العالم بقوله: (أي: ما سوى الله تعالى العلي العالما) بكسر اللام، ونصبه على القطع للمدح، من جوهر وهو ما قام بنفسه أو عرض وهو ما قام بغيره من الجواهر، كالألوان. (من غير شك) يتعلّق بقوله: (حادث) الواقع خبرًا عن «أن هذا العالما» أي موجود بعد عدم، يعني: أن حدوث هذا العالم ثابت بغير شك، لما يشاهده من التغيرات ولأنه محتاج إلى موجد يوجده من العدم إلى حيز الوجود، وليس إلا الله تعالى، وإليه أشار بقوله: (مفتقر) وهو خبر بعد خبر، وإن كان لازما للأول. حقيقة الشك: التردد في الطرفين على السواء، ومراده به هنا مطلق التردد، الشامل للظن: وهو الطرف الراجح، والوهم: وهو المرجوح.
When the poet, may Allah have mercy on him, finished explaining the divisions of the rational judgment and the obligation of knowing Allah Almighty upon every legally responsible person, he began to explain the way leading to knowledge of Allah Almighty, which is the temporal origination of this world, and said: (Then) when you, O legally responsible person, know what is obligatory upon you legally regarding the temporal origination of the world as a proof of the right of Allah, and what is possible, and what is impossible, so know (with the light nun of emphasis) for encouragement and alertness (that this world) observed in all its parts, the alif being for the release of the rhyme. And he interpreted the world by his saying: (i.e., everything other than Allah, the High, the Knower) with kasra on the lam, and accusative as a disconnected [apposition] for praise, including substance which is that which subsists by itself, or accident which is that which subsists in other than itself among substances, like colors. (Without doubt) relates to his saying: (temporal) which is the predicate of 'that this world', i.e., existing after non-existence, meaning that the temporal origination of this world is established without doubt, because of the changes observed in it, and because it needs an originator to bring it from non-existence into the realm of existence, and that is none but Allah Almighty, and to that he alluded by his saying: (dependent), which is a second predicate, even if it is necessary to the first. The reality of doubt: hesitation between the two sides equally, and his intent here is absolute hesitation, including conjecture which is the preponderant side, and illusion which is the weaker side.
Source pages p. 41

Commentary
This passage is an explanatory introduction to the proof from temporal origination. The commentator first sets the context: after discussing rational judgments and the obligation of knowing Allah, the poet moves to the way that leads to knowledge—namely, the temporal origination of the world. He then glosses the poet's line 'Then know that this world' (ثم اعلمن بأن هذا العالما). The 'then' indicates sequence after learning the rational judgments. The emphatic 'know' (اعلمن) with the light nun is used for encouragement. 'This world' refers to the entire observed universe, and the final alif is poetic license. The poet interprets 'world' as 'everything besides Allah', praising Allah with 'the High, the Knower' as an incidental description. The commentator explains that 'everything besides Allah' includes substances (jawahir) which subsist by themselves and accidents (a‘rad) which subsist in substances, like colors. Then he explains the phrase 'without doubt it is temporal and dependent' (من غير شك حادث مفتقر). 'Without doubt' qualifies 'temporal', meaning the world's temporality is certain, evidenced by perceived changes and its need for an originator. 'Temporal' means existing after non-existence. 'Dependent' is a second predicate, logically consequent to temporality. The footnote clarifies the meaning of 'shakk' (doubt) as hesitation, but here it broadly includes any uncertainty, whether preponderant (zann) or weak (wahm).

Key Terms
عالم
حادث
مفتقر
شك
جوهر
عرض

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think 'without doubt' means absolute certainty without any possibility of consideration, but the commentator explains it as a general term covering even conjectural uncertainty.
Correction: The term 'شك' here includes absolute hesitation, encompassing both preponderant conjecture (ظن) and weak illusion (وهم), not just perfect certainty.

Checkpoint
According to the commentator, what does the term 'world' (العالم) refer to?
A) The celestial spheres only
B) Everything that exists, including Allah
C) Everything other than Allah, including substances and accidents
D) Only visible physical bodies

Key Takeaway
The world is everything other than Allah, consisting of substances and accidents, and it is certain that the world is temporal and dependent, which necessitates a Creator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that every single thing in your life – your health, your wealth, your relationships – is temporal and dependent on Allah at every moment, what would you do differently today? Name one specific attachment you would loosen.
?
The passage says the world is 'without doubt' temporal, yet many of us live as if this world is permanent. When was the last time you genuinely felt the temporality of something you love? How did that feeling change your perspective or actions?
?
Consider one 'substance' (a stable thing) and one 'accident' (a changing quality) in your life. How does recognizing their dependence on Allah challenge your sense of control or self-reliance? What can you do today to actively acknowledge that dependence?

The Greatness of Tawhid and the Nature of Disbelief
Al-Sawi states the greatness of tawhid and the absence of shirk among jinn.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the science of tawhid is named after the attribute of tawhid and the unique distinction between humans and jinn regarding shirk.

Core Idea
Tawhid is the greatest attribute; only some humans commit shirk, not the jinn.

In this short 'fā'idah,' Shaykh Ahmad al-Ṣāwī highlights the supreme importance of the science of tawhid. He explains that it is named after tawhid because tawhid itself is the most important of all attributes. He then makes a remarkable observation about disbelief: shirk is found only among some humans, while jinn as a whole are free from it. This passage sets the stage for understanding the central position of tawhid in Islamic theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the English translation. Notice how al-Sawi structures his statement: first the reason for naming, then the exception about humans and jinn.
The passage is a 'fā'idah' (benefit) cited from al-Sawi.
2
Grasp the main point about tawhid's importance
Identify why al-Sawi calls tawhid 'the most important of attributes.' Understand that the science is named after this attribute because it is foundational.
3
Understand the unique claim about jinn
Focus on the distinction: only some humans are disbelievers due to shirk, whereas the jinn collectively do not associate partners with Allah. If a jinn is a disbeliever, it is for another reason.
This point corrects a common assumption that jinn can be polytheists.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فائدة
قال العلامة سيدي أحمد الصاوي: «هذه الصفة أهم الصفات، ولذا سمي علم التوحيد بها، ولم يكفر بضدها إلا بعض الإنس، وأما الجن برمتهم فلا يعتقدون الشرك لله سبحانه وتعالى، وإنما الكافر منهم كافر بغير الشرك».
Benefit: The scholar Sayyidi Ahmad al-Sawi said: 'This attribute is the most important of attributes, and thus the science of tawhid is named after it, and only some humans were deemed disbelievers for its opposite. As for the jinn, all of them do not believe in associating partners with Allah, glorified and exalted is He; rather, the disbeliever among them is a disbeliever due to something other than shirk.'
Source pages p. 50

Commentary
This passage is a fā'idah (benefit) cited by the author from the commentary of Ahmad al-Ṣāwī. Al-Ṣāwī explains why ʿilm al-tawḥīd (the science of divine unity) is named after the attribute of divine unity (tawḥīd) itself: because tawḥīd is the most important of all attributes—it is the foundational principle of faith. He then notes a striking fact: disbelief (kufr) due to shirk (associating partners with Allah) is found only among some humans (ins), not among the jinn. The jinn, as a whole species, do not fall into shirk; if any jinn are disbelievers, it is for reasons other than shirk. This distinction underscores the gravity of shirk for humans and the unique status of tawḥīd as the core of the Islamic creed.

Key Terms
علم التوحيد
الشرك
الإنس
الجن
الكفر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some people think that jinn can also be polytheists like humans, committing shirk.
Correction: Al-Sawi clarifies that jinn as a whole never associate partners with Allah; their disbelief, if any, is due to reasons other than shirk.

Checkpoint
According to al-Sawi, why is the science of tawhid named after the attribute of tawhid?
A) Because it is the easiest attribute to understand.
B) Because tawhid is the most important attribute and the basis of the science.
C) Because only humans discuss this attribute.
D) Because the jinn were the first to study it.

Key Takeaway
Tawhid is the greatest attribute, and shirk is a uniquely human failing; jinn are not guilty of it.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If tawḥīd is the greatest attribute, what does your daily routine show about your attachment to it? Are you willing to identify and abandon any hidden shirk you might be committing, even if it feels comfortable?
?
The jinn, despite their disobedience, do not commit shirk. What does this tell you about the severity of shirk for humans? Have you ever considered that your own sins might include forms of shirk (like showing off) without realizing it? How will you start checking your intentions today?
?
This lesson states that shirk is a uniquely human failing. Does that make you feel more accountable? What specific step can you take this week to strengthen your tawḥīd and protect yourself from even subtle shirk?

The First Sentence: Divine Attributes and Creation
Commentary linking the first sentence of the shahada to divine attributes and creation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the first part of the shahada ('La ilaha illa Allah') logically implies the entirety of divine attributes and the creation of the world.

Core Idea
The declaration 'لا إله إلا الله' contains the entire doctrine of God and creation.

This lesson unpacks the profound theological content embedded in the very first phrase of the Islamic testimony of faith. The commentator explains that the name 'Allah' points to the Necessary Existent and Creator, and from that, a full range of divine attributes and cosmological beliefs are logically deduced. We will see how the concise statement actually entails a complete system of tawhid.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once through, then its translation. Notice that the commentator first establishes that the entire shahada contains all rulings, then focuses on the first sentence.
2
Identify the implications of 'Allah' as Necessary Existent
The passage says that 'Allah' refers to the self that is necessarily existent (الذات الواجب الوجود). From this, it derives attributes of negation (صفات السلوب) except oneness, and transcendence above accidents.
Necessary existence implies that He cannot be non-existent, cannot be compound, etc.
3
Examine the implications of 'Creator of the world'
Being the Creator entails attributes of power, will, knowledge, life, oneness, the temporal origination of the world, and negation of cause and nature. Try to see how each follows from the concept of creating.
This shows the depth of the simple phrase.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويَنْطَوِي فِي كِلْمَةِ الإِسْلام * ما قَدْ مَضَى مِنْ سَائِرِ الأَحْكَام
وإسكان اللام، أي: الكلمة الدالة على الإسلام، وهي لا إله إلا الله محمد رسول الله (ما) قد مضى ذكره من سائر) أي: جميع (الأحكام) الإلهيات والنبويات والسمعيات، ولذلك اعتبر الشارع هذه الكلمة الجليلة للدخول في الإسلام وترجمة لما في القلب، ولم يقبل من أحد الإسلام إلا بها.
وبيان ذلك ما قاله الناظم في شرحه(۱) على سبيل الاختصار المفيد، وهو أن الله عَلم على الذات الواجب الوجود الخالق للعالم، وقد دلّت هذه الجملة على حصر الألوهية فيه تعالى، وظاهر أن ذلك يتضمن جميع ما ذكر، فإن وجوب الوجود يتضمن صفات السلوب ما عدا الوحدانية، والتنزه عن الأعراض في الأفعال والأحكام، وكونه خالقا للعالم يتضمن القدرة، والإرادة والعلم، والحياة، والوحدانية، وحدوث العالم بأسره (۲)، ونفي العلة، والطبيعة. وأما الجملة الثانية، وهو قولنا: (محمد رسول الله) فقد دلت على ثبوت الرسالة لنبينا صلى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ ، وذلك يستلزم صدقه في كل ما أخبر عنه، وأمانته، وتبليغه الرسالة للعباد، وكل ما أمر به، وفطانته، والرسول لا يكون إلا معصوما، واستحالة أضدادها عليه صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ، وجواز كل ما لا يؤدي إلى نقص في علو مرتبته من الأعراض البشرية.
صدقه يستلزم الإيمان بكل ما جاء به، ومن ذلك إرسال ووجوب الرسل، وهو يستلزم ما يجب في حقهم، وما يستحيل، وما يجوز، والإيمان بالكتب السماوية، واليوم الآخر، والحساب، وما تقدّم من جميع السمعيات.
And it is contained in the word of Islam all that has passed of the various rulings. With sukun of the lam, i.e., the word indicating Islam, which is 'there is no god but Allah, Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah' ('ma' [that which] has passed of the various (i.e., all) rulings: the divine matters, prophetic matters, and revealed matters. Therefore the Lawgiver considered this noble word for entering Islam and as a translation of what is in the heart, and He accepted Islam from no one except with it. And the explanation of that is what the author said in his commentary(1) by way of useful brevity: that Allah is a sign for the essence that is necessarily existent, the Creator of the world, and this sentence has indicated the exclusive attribution of divinity to Him, may He be exalted, and it is apparent that that includes all that has been mentioned. For necessary existence includes attributes of negation except oneness (wahdaniyya), and transcendence above incidental attributes in actions and rulings, and His being Creator of the world includes power, will, knowledge, life, oneness, the temporal origination of the entire world (2), and the negation of cause and nature. As for the second sentence, which is our saying: 'Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah,' it has indicated the establishment of prophethood for our Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and that necessitates his truthfulness in everything he informed about, his trustworthiness, his conveying the message to the servants, and everything he commanded, and his intelligence, and the Prophet can only be infallible, and the impossibility of their opposites upon him, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and the permissibility of everything from human incidental attributes that does not lead to a deficiency in the loftiness of his rank. His truthfulness necessitates faith in everything he brought, and from that is the sending and necessity of messengers, and it necessitates what is obligatory regarding them, what is impossible, and what is possible, and faith in the heavenly books, the Last Day, the reckoning, and all that has preceded of the revealed matters.
Source pages p. 102

Commentary
This passage is an explanatory commentary on the poetic line that states the word of Islam contains all the rulings previously discussed. The commentator begins by clarifying the phrase 'كلمة الإسلام' (the word of Islam), identifying it as the shahada 'لا إله إلا الله محمد رسول الله'. He then explains that this word encompasses all the rulings that were mentioned earlier in the poem: the divine matters (الإلهيات) concerning God, the prophetic matters (النبويات) concerning prophets, and the revealed matters (السمعيات) concerning what is heard from revelation. The Lawgiver made this word the criterion for entering Islam, as it translates the belief of the heart and is not accepted otherwise.

Next, the commentator provides a concise breakdown of how the two sentences of the shahada imply the full set of doctrines. The first sentence 'لا إله إلا الله' indicates that divinity (al-uluhiyya) is exclusive to God. The name 'الله' refers to the self that is necessarily existent (الذات الواجب الوجود), the Creator of the world. From this, several implications follow: 
- 'وجوب الوجود' (necessary existence) entails the attributes of negation (صفات السلوب) such as not being non-existent, except the attribute of oneness (الوحدانية) which is not a negation but an affirmation. It also entails transcendence above accidents (عوارض) in actions and rulings.
- 'كونه خالقا للعالم' (His being Creator of the world) entails the attributes of power, will, knowledge, life, oneness, the temporal origination of the entire world (حدوث العالم), and the negation of cause (العلة) and nature (الطبيعة).

The second sentence 'محمد رسول الله' establishes the prophethood of Muhammad (peace be upon him). This in turn necessitates his truthfulness, trustworthiness, conveying the message, intelligence, and infallibility (العصمة). It also implies the impossibility of their opposites and the permissibility of human attributes that do not detract from his high rank.

Finally, the truthfulness of the Prophet necessitates faith in everything he brought, which includes the sending of messengers, the necessity of belief in them, the knowledge of what is obligatory, impossible, and possible regarding them, as well as faith in the divine books, the Last Day, the reckoning, and all other revealed matters (السمعيات). Thus, the passage demonstrates how the concise word of Islam actually contains the entirety of Islamic creed.

Key Terms
كلمة الإسلام
لا إله إلا الله
الإلهيات
وجوب الوجود
صفات السلوب
الوحدانية
حدوث العالم
نفي العلة
نفي الطبيعة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The shahada is merely a verbal formula with no detailed doctrinal content.
Correction: This passage shows that the first sentence alone contains a complete system of beliefs about God and creation.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is NOT derived from the first sentence of the shahada according to the passage?
A) Power and will of God
B) Infallibility of prophets
C) Temporal origination of the world
D) Negation of cause and nature

Key Takeaway
The first sentence of the shahada logically entails all essential beliefs about God and the origin of the world.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite 'لا إله إلا الله', do you consciously affirm that God alone is the Creator, and that everything else is brought into existence by Him? How would your daily actions change if you truly lived with that awareness every moment?
?
This lesson shows that the shahada entails the negation of cause and nature. Have you ever attributed something to 'luck' or 'natural forces' while forgetting that God is the direct cause? Will you commit to recognizing God's hand in every event from now on?
?
If the shahada contains the belief that the world had a beginning, does your heart truly accept that your own existence is a deliberate act of creation, not a random accident? How should that certainty reshape your purpose in life starting today?

The Second Sentence: Prophethood and Revealed Matters
Commentary on how the second sentence of the shahada implies prophethood and all revealed matters.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the second part of the shahada ('Muhammad Rasul Allah') implies the full doctrine of prophethood and all revealed matters (sam'iyyat).

Core Idea
The declaration 'محمد رسول الله' encapsulates the entire creed concerning prophets and the unseen.

This lesson continues the commentary's demonstration that the shahada is not just a formula but a complete doctrinal system. Here we focus on the second sentence: that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah. The commentator shows that this simple statement logically entails the truthfulness, trustworthiness, intelligence, and infallibility of the Prophet, and through his truthfulness, it necessitates faith in everything he brought, including all matters of the unseen, the books, the Last Day, and the like.

Guided Path
1
Read the second part of the passage
Read the Arabic and translation for the second sentence discussion. Notice how the commentator moves from the statement to its logical implications.
2
Identify the attributes implied by prophethood
The passage lists truthfulness, trustworthiness, conveying, intelligence, and infallibility. Also note the opposites are impossible, and permissible human attributes that don't diminish his rank.
These are the necessary, impossible, and possible attributes of prophets.
3
Trace the chain to revealed matters
The truthfulness of the Prophet requires belief in everything he brought, which includes the sending of messengers, belief in them, their attributes, the books, the Last Day, and all sam'iyyat.
This shows how the shahada covers the entire field of prophetic and revealed doctrines.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويَنْطَوِي فِي كِلْمَةِ الإِسْلام * ما قَدْ مَضَى مِنْ سَائِرِ الأَحْكَام
وإسكان اللام، أي: الكلمة الدالة على الإسلام، وهي لا إله إلا الله محمد رسول الله (ما) قد مضى ذكره من سائر) أي: جميع (الأحكام) الإلهيات والنبويات والسمعيات، ولذلك اعتبر الشارع هذه الكلمة الجليلة للدخول في الإسلام وترجمة لما في القلب، ولم يقبل من أحد الإسلام إلا بها.
وبيان ذلك ما قاله الناظم في شرحه(۱) على سبيل الاختصار المفيد، وهو أن الله عَلم على الذات الواجب الوجود الخالق للعالم، وقد دلّت هذه الجملة على حصر الألوهية فيه تعالى، وظاهر أن ذلك يتضمن جميع ما ذكر، فإن وجوب الوجود يتضمن صفات السلوب ما عدا الوحدانية، والتنزه عن الأعراض في الأفعال والأحكام، وكونه خالقا للعالم يتضمن القدرة، والإرادة والعلم، والحياة، والوحدانية، وحدوث العالم بأسره (۲)، ونفي العلة، والطبيعة. وأما الجملة الثانية، وهو قولنا: (محمد رسول الله) فقد دلت على ثبوت الرسالة لنبينا صلى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ ، وذلك يستلزم صدقه في كل ما أخبر عنه، وأمانته، وتبليغه الرسالة للعباد، وكل ما أمر به، وفطانته، والرسول لا يكون إلا معصوما، واستحالة أضدادها عليه صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ، وجواز كل ما لا يؤدي إلى نقص في علو مرتبته من الأعراض البشرية.
صدقه يستلزم الإيمان بكل ما جاء به، ومن ذلك إرسال ووجوب الرسل، وهو يستلزم ما يجب في حقهم، وما يستحيل، وما يجوز، والإيمان بالكتب السماوية، واليوم الآخر، والحساب، وما تقدّم من جميع السمعيات.
And it is contained in the word of Islam all that has passed of the various rulings. With sukun of the lam, i.e., the word indicating Islam, which is 'there is no god but Allah, Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah' ('ma' [that which] has passed of the various (i.e., all) rulings: the divine matters, prophetic matters, and revealed matters. Therefore the Lawgiver considered this noble word for entering Islam and as a translation of what is in the heart, and He accepted Islam from no one except with it. And the explanation of that is what the author said in his commentary(1) by way of useful brevity: that Allah is a sign for the essence that is necessarily existent, the Creator of the world, and this sentence has indicated the exclusive attribution of divinity to Him, may He be exalted, and it is apparent that that includes all that has been mentioned. For necessary existence includes attributes of negation except oneness (wahdaniyya), and transcendence above incidental attributes in actions and rulings, and His being Creator of the world includes power, will, knowledge, life, oneness, the temporal origination of the entire world (2), and the negation of cause and nature. As for the second sentence, which is our saying: 'Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah,' it has indicated the establishment of prophethood for our Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and that necessitates his truthfulness in everything he informed about, his trustworthiness, his conveying the message to the servants, and everything he commanded, and his intelligence, and the Prophet can only be infallible, and the impossibility of their opposites upon him, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and the permissibility of everything from human incidental attributes that does not lead to a deficiency in the loftiness of his rank. His truthfulness necessitates faith in everything he brought, and from that is the sending and necessity of messengers, and it necessitates what is obligatory regarding them, what is impossible, and what is possible, and faith in the heavenly books, the Last Day, the reckoning, and all that has preceded of the revealed matters.
Source pages p. 102

Commentary
This passage is an explanatory commentary on the poetic line that states the word of Islam contains all the rulings previously discussed. The commentator begins by clarifying the phrase 'كلمة الإسلام' (the word of Islam), identifying it as the shahada 'لا إله إلا الله محمد رسول الله'. He then explains that this word encompasses all the rulings that were mentioned earlier in the poem: the divine matters (الإلهيات) concerning God, the prophetic matters (النبويات) concerning prophets, and the revealed matters (السمعيات) concerning what is heard from revelation. The Lawgiver made this word the criterion for entering Islam, as it translates the belief of the heart and is not accepted otherwise.

Next, the commentator provides a concise breakdown of how the two sentences of the shahada imply the full set of doctrines. The first sentence 'لا إله إلا الله' indicates that divinity (al-uluhiyya) is exclusive to God. The name 'الله' refers to the self that is necessarily existent (الذات الواجب الوجود), the Creator of the world. From this, several implications follow: 
- 'وجوب الوجود' (necessary existence) entails the attributes of negation (صفات السلوب) such as not being non-existent, except the attribute of oneness (الوحدانية) which is not a negation but an affirmation. It also entails transcendence above accidents (عوارض) in actions and rulings.
- 'كونه خالقا للعالم' (His being Creator of the world) entails the attributes of power, will, knowledge, life, oneness, the temporal origination of the entire world (حدوث العالم), and the negation of cause (العلة) and nature (الطبيعة).

The second sentence 'محمد رسول الله' establishes the prophethood of Muhammad (peace be upon him). This in turn necessitates his truthfulness, trustworthiness, conveying the message, intelligence, and infallibility (العصمة). It also implies the impossibility of their opposites and the permissibility of human attributes that do not detract from his high rank.

Finally, the truthfulness of the Prophet necessitates faith in everything he brought, which includes the sending of messengers, the necessity of belief in them, the knowledge of what is obligatory, impossible, and possible regarding them, as well as faith in the divine books, the Last Day, the reckoning, and all other revealed matters (السمعيات). Thus, the passage demonstrates how the concise word of Islam actually contains the entirety of Islamic creed.

Key Terms
محمد رسول الله
النبويات
السمعيات
الرسالة
العصمة
الكتب السماوية
اليوم الآخر
الحساب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The statement 'Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah' only affirms his role, with no further doctrinal implications.
Correction: The passage shows that it entails a complete set of beliefs about prophets and the unseen, including infallibility and all revealed doctrines.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, which attribute is NOT directly derived from the phrase 'Muhammad Rasul Allah'?
A) Truthfulness of the Prophet
B) Infallibility of the Prophet
C) Oneness of God
D) Conveying the message

Key Takeaway
The second sentence of the shahada logically entails the complete doctrine of prophethood and all revealed matters.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite 'Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah,' do you actually believe everything he taught—including the reality of the Last Day and the reckoning—even when those beliefs demand you change how you live today? What is one teaching you have been avoiding acting on, and what will you do about it this week?
?
The passage says the Prophet's truthfulness necessitates faith in everything he brought. Is there any teaching of the Prophet that you secretly doubt or rationalize away? How would your life look different if you fully surrendered to that teaching starting now?
?
You claim to love the Prophet, but loving him means accepting his infallibility and trusting his message completely. Is there an area of your life where you rely on your own opinion over what the Prophet commanded? What would it cost you to submit, and are you willing to pay that price?

True and False Hope in God's Mercy
Commentary on the line 'غلب الخوف على الرجاء' distinguishing true hope from wishful thinking.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the difference between true hope (al-rajā' al-ṣādiq) and false hope (al-rajā' al-kādhib), and why hope must be accompanied by action.

Core Idea
Hope without action is wishful thinking, not true hope.

Many assume that hoping for God's mercy is always praiseworthy, but Islamic scholars distinguish between genuine hope and mere wishful thinking. In this passage, Shaykh al-Mashshāt explains that true hope is tied to action—either repentance or good deeds—while hope without effort is blameworthy. Let's explore this crucial distinction.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Notice how the commentator builds his argument.
2
Understand the hadith reference
The Prophet ﷺ commands having good opinion of Allah. This sets the context: hope is encouraged, but only when paired with action.
3
Identify the three cases
The passage describes three scenarios: the repentant sinner, the doer of good deeds, and the one who does nothing. Which are true hope and which is false?
4
Distinguish true from false
Focus on the key phrase: 'hope and good opinion [are valid] when accompanied by action, otherwise it is wishful thinking.' This is the core distinction.
5
Apply the lesson
Reflect on your own spiritual life. Are you actively repenting and doing good while hoping, or merely wishing without effort?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وغلب الخوف على الرجاء * وسر لمولاك بلا تناء | ٦٠ شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي: على الخوف، لأنه حال القدوم على الكريم الذي لا تتخطاه الآمال، ولقوله صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ: «لا يموتن أحدكم إلا وهو يحسن الظن بالله تعالى»(۱)، أو كما قال. واعلم أن الرجاء والظن الحسن فيما قارنه عمل، وإلا فهو أمنية، الفرق بين الرجاء الصادق والكاذب: وذلك أن العبد يسيئ إلى مولاه بالذنب والتقصير فيتوب ويرجع فيرجو قبول توبته أو يعمل عملا صالحًا لله تعالى، فهذا يرجو قبوله، والثالث لا يعمل ولا يتوب ويقول: «أرجو من الله كذا وكذا، فهذا هو الرجاء الكاذب المذموم.
And overcome fear with hope * And journey to your Lord without distance | 60 Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashshāt al-Malikī al-Mālikī: [The phrase] 'overcome fear with hope' because it is the state of coming to the Generous One Whom hopes do not exceed, and due to his saying, may God bless him and grant him peace: 'Let none of you die except while having good opinion of God Almighty,' or as he said. And know that hope and good opinion [are valid] when accompanied by action, otherwise it is wishful thinking. The difference between true hope and false hope: that is, the servant sins against his Lord by sin and shortcoming, then repents and returns and hopes for acceptance of his repentance, or performs a righteous deed for God Almighty, so this one hopes for its acceptance; and the third does not act nor repent and says: 'I hope from God such and such,' so this is the blameworthy false hope.
Source pages p. 108

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on the line 'And overcome fear with hope' from the poem. The commentator begins by explaining that fear is overcome by hope because it is the proper state when approaching the Generous God, whose mercy exceeds all hopes. He then cites a hadith from Muslim: 'Let none of you die except while having good opinion of God Almighty.' He then clarifies that true hope and good opinion are only praiseworthy when accompanied by action; without action, it is mere wishful thinking (umniyya). He distinguishes three cases: 1) the servant sins, repents, and hopes for acceptance – this is true hope; 2) the servant does good deeds and hopes for acceptance – also true hope; 3) the servant neither acts nor repents but merely says 'I hope from God such and such' – this is blameworthy false hope. The commentary thus guards against the misconception that hoping for God's mercy without effort is sufficient.

Key Terms
الرجاء
الظن الحسن
الأمنية
الرجاء الصادق
الرجاء الكاذب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Hoping for God's mercy is always praiseworthy regardless of one's actions.
Correction: Hope is only praiseworthy when accompanied by action; without action, it is blameworthy wishful thinking (umniyya).

Checkpoint
According to the commentator, what is the difference between true hope and false hope?
A) True hope is accompanied by action; false hope is without action.
B) True hope is for this world; false hope is for the afterlife.
C) True hope is from fear; false hope is from love.
D) True hope is certain; false hope is doubtful.

Key Takeaway
True hope is always accompanied by action—repentance or good deeds—while hope without action is blameworthy wishful thinking.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you say 'I hope Allah forgives me,' is that hope actually driving you to change something in your life today, or is it just a comfortable phrase that lets you stay where you are?
?
The hadith says to have good opinion of Allah at death—but if you died right now, would your good opinion be based on your actions and repentance, or on an empty wish? What needs to change so that your hope is honest?
?
You know the third category: those who do nothing and say 'I hope.' Is there any area in your life where you have been content with that false hope? Will you commit to one concrete action of repentance or obedience before the end of this week?

The Spiritual Journey: Heart's Attachment and Self-Discipline
Commentary defining the spiritual journey as inner transformation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the spiritual journey to God is an inner transformation of the heart, not a physical movement through space.

Core Idea
The journey to God is inward, not physical; it is the heart's attachment and self-discipline.

This brief commentary on a poetic line from the Kharīda dismantles any literal, physical understanding of 'journeying' to God. The commentator, Shaykh al-Mashshāt, replaces spatial movement with two clear spiritual realities: the heart's turning towards Allah and the soul's opposition to its own desires. Let's unpack these essential elements of the spiritual path.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Begin by reading the original Arabic line and its commentary, then the translation. Focus on the key terms 'سير' and 'بلا تناء'.
The passage is short but dense in meaning.
2
Identify what 'without distance' means
Notice that the commentator explains 'بلا تناء' as not straying from the straight path, not as literal distance. This reframes the journey as a moral and spiritual direction.
3
Grasp the definition of 'journeying' (al-sayr)
The core definition: it is the heart's attachment to Allah combined with opposing the soul's desires. These two elements define the inner journey.
Memorize this definition as a key concept in Islamic spirituality.
4
Reflect on the concluding prayer
The commentator ends with a prayer for guidance on the straight path. This shows that the journey is a gift from Allah, not merely a human effort.
Note the humility of the scholar in asking for divine assistance.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وَسِر لِمَوْلاكَ بِلا تَنَاءِ | شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي: (وَسِر) أيها السالك (المولاك) سيرًا حثيثا (بلا تناء) أي: بغير تباعد عن الطريق المستقيم إلى الله تعالى، والسير: عبارة عن تعلّق قلب العبد بمولاه تبارك وتعالى مع مخالفة النفس في شهواتها، رزقنا الله ذلك، ووفقنا للسير في الطريق المستقيم الموصل إلى ربنا تبارك وتعالى بمنه وكرمه.
And journey to your Lord without distance | Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashshāt al-Malikī al-Mālikī: '(And journey) O traveler (to your Lord) a swift journey (without distance), i.e., without straying from the straight path to God Almighty. And journeying (al-sayr) is an expression for the attachment of the servant's heart to his Lord, Blessed and Exalted, along with opposing the soul in its desires. May God grant us that, and guide us to journey on the straight path that leads to our Lord, Blessed and Exalted, by His grace and generosity.
Source pages p. 108

Commentary
This passage comments on the line 'And journey to your Lord without distance.' The commentator explains 'journey' (sir) as a swift journey, and 'without distance' as not straying from the straight path. He then defines 'journeying' (al-sayr) as a term for the heart's attachment to God combined with opposing the soul's desires. The passage concludes with a prayer: that God grant this journey and guide to the straight path that leads to Him. The emphasis is that the spiritual journey is internal and moral, not physical.

Key Terms
السير
الطريق المستقيم
تعلّق القلب
مخالفة النفس

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The journey to God involves physical travel to a specific place, like visiting a saint's tomb or going on a pilgrimage.
Correction: The commentator explicitly states that 'without distance' means not straying from the straight path, and defines journeying as an internal state of heart attachment and self-opposition, not physical movement.

Checkpoint
According to the commentator, what does 'journeying' (al-sayr) mean?
A) Physical travel to holy places.
B) The heart's attachment to God while opposing the soul's desires.
C) Reciting the name of God repeatedly.
D) Performing extra acts of worship.

Key Takeaway
The spiritual journey to Allah is an inner process: attach your heart to Him and oppose your soul's desires—that is the path without distance.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Reflect on your daily life: Where is your heart most attached—to Allah or to worldly pleasures? What specific desire of your soul have you been avoiding opposing? Commit to one concrete action today to oppose it.
?
You have learned that the spiritual journey is not physical distance but inner transformation. Is there an area where you have been moving physically (like rushing through prayer) but your heart is far? What will you do tonight to reattach your heart to Allah in that action?
?
The journey requires both heart attachment and self-discipline. Which of these two do you neglect more, and why? Write down one small step you will take this week to strengthen the weaker side.

Renewing Repentance: Never Despair of Allah's Mercy
Commentary explaining the renewal of repentance and refuting despair.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that repentance is not nullified by returning to sin and must be renewed each time, based on the story of Hasan al-Basri and the Quranic verse.

Core Idea
Repentance is never wasted; renew it even after repeated sins.

This passage from al-Bahja al-Saniyya explains a key line of the Kharida: 'Renew repentance for sins.' The commentator uses an analogy, a story with Hasan al-Basri, and a Quranic verse to show that even if a believer falls into sin again after repenting, his repentance remains valid and he must renew it. This lesson explores that comforting and motivating teaching.

Guided Path
1
Read the opening analogy
Notice the comparison: no land means no vegetation; no repentance means no spiritual state or station.
This sets the stage for the necessity of repentance.
2
Understand the exchange with Hasan al-Basri
A man observes that believers sin, repent, and then sin again. Hasan explains that this is the believer's nature – constantly returning to Allah.
This addresses a common concern about repeated sin.
3
Derive the ruling on renewal
The commentator concludes that returning to sin does not nullify repentance; rather, one must renew it each time, even a thousand times a day.
4
Read the Quranic verse and poet's line
Allah loves those who repent frequently. The poet's line reinforces the theme.
Connect the verse to the obligation of renewal.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وجَدِّدِ التَّوْبَةَ للأوْزَارِ . لا تَيْأَسَنُ مِنْ رَحمةِ الغَفَّارِ | شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي: كما أن من لا أرض له لا نبات له كذلك من لا توبة له لا حال له، ولا مقام. قال رجل للحسن البصري رحمه الله تعالى: «العبد يذنب ثم يتوب ويستغفر، وإذا هو يرجع إلى الذنب، فقال له: «ذلك شأن المؤمن يذنب، ثم يتوب ويستغفر ويرجع إلى مولاه تبارك وتعالى»، أي: ولو عاد إلى الذنب في اليوم الواحد مرارًا. وأُخِذ من ذلك أن التوبة لا تنتقض بالرجوع إلى الذنب، ولو رجعت إليه في اليوم ألف مرة، ويجب تجديدها عند كل رجوع، قال تعالى: إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ التَّوَّابِينَ وَيُحِبُّ الْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ (١)، ولذلك قال الناظم تغمده الله برحمته: «وجدد التوبة للأوزار»، والعبد لا يزال مُقصرًا فقيرًا لمولاه، ليس له من يرجع في أحواله إلا إليه. أركان التوبة ولتعلم أن للتوبة أركانا ثلاثة: الندم على ما وقع فيه من المخالفات. والعزم على أن لا يعود لمثله. والإقلاع عن الذنب في الحال. فيجب الكف عن استتمام الزنا، وشرب الخمر، وعن أذى الناس ويجب رَدّ المظالم إلى أهلها واستسماح المظلوم إن أمكن، وإلا استغفر له وتصدق له بما يمكنه، فإن الله تعالى وهو الغني إذا علم منك الصدق أرضى عنك خصماءك.
And renew repentance for sins. Do not despair of the mercy of the All-Forgiving. | Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashshāt al-Malikī al-Mālikī: Just as one who has no land has no vegetation, likewise one who has no repentance has no state (ḥāl) nor station (maqām). A man said to al-Ḥasan al-Baṣrī, may God have mercy on him: 'The servant sins, then repents and seeks forgiveness, and then he returns to sin.' He said to him: 'That is the matter of the believer: he sins, then repents and seeks forgiveness, and returns to his Lord, Blessed and Exalted' – meaning, even if he returns to sin many times in a single day. And it is derived from that that repentance is not nullified by returning to sin, even if one returns to it a thousand times a day, and it is obligatory to renew it at every return. God Almighty said: 'Indeed, God loves those who repent and loves those who purify themselves' (2:222). Therefore the poet, may God envelop him in His mercy, said: 'And renew repentance for sins.' And the servant is always falling short and poor before his Lord; he has no one to turn to in his states except Him. The pillars of repentance: And know that repentance has three pillars: 1) regret for the violations that occurred; 2) determination not to return to the like of it; 3) desisting from the sin immediately. So one must refrain from completing fornication, drinking wine, and harming people, and it is obligatory to return wrongs to their owners and seek the pardon of the wronged if possible, otherwise pray for forgiveness for him and give charity on his behalf as much as possible. For God Almighty – and He is the Rich – if He knows sincerity from you, He will satisfy your adversaries on your behalf.
Source pages p. 109

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on the line 'And renew repentance for sins' and the following line 'Do not despair of the mercy of the All-Forgiving.' The commentator begins with an analogy: just as land is necessary for vegetation, repentance is necessary for any spiritual state or station. He then narrates an exchange with al-Ḥasan al-Baṣrī: a man observed that a believer sins, repents, and then sins again. Al-Ḥasan replied that this is the believer's way – he constantly returns to his Lord even if he returns to sin many times a day. From this, the commentator derives that repentance is not invalidated by returning to sin, and it must be renewed upon each return. He cites Quran 2:222: 'Indeed, God loves those who repent.' Then he states the three pillars of repentance: regret (nadam), determination to not return (ʿazm), and immediate desistance (iqlāʿ). He elaborates that one must stop sinful acts like fornication or drinking and must also restore rights to the wronged or seek forgiveness and give charity on their behalf if direct reconciliation is impossible. The passage concludes with the reassurance that if God sees sincerity, He will satisfy the wronged parties.

Key Terms
التوبة
تجدّد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that sinning after repentance nullifies it completely, so there is no point in repenting again.
Correction: The commentator explicitly states that repentance is not nullified by returning to sin; rather, it must be renewed each time.

Checkpoint
According to the commentator, how many times can one repent after sinning again?
A) Only once.
B) Up to three times.
C) As many times as needed, even a thousand.
D) Only if the sin is major.

Key Takeaway
Repentance is not invalidated by repeating sin; rather, it must be renewed each time, and Allah loves those who repent frequently.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you fall into the same sin after repenting, do you feel your previous repentance was wasted? How does this lesson challenge that feeling, and what will you do the next time you sin?
?
Think of a sin you have committed many times. Have you ever felt too ashamed to repent again? This lesson says to renew repentance and not despair. Will you repent right now, even if it's the thousandth time?
?
The servant is always in need of Allah. In what area of your life are you still trying to rely on your own strength instead of turning back to Allah in repentance? What is one step you can take today to return to Him?

What Is Kalam?
beginner
5 min
The Three Pillars of Repentance
Enumeration and explanation of the three pillars of repentance and related duties.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will know the three pillars of repentance (regret, determination, immediate desistance) and the practical obligations that follow, including restoring rights and seeking pardon.

Core Idea
True repentance rests on regret, resolve, and immediate renunciation of sin.

After establishing the need to renew repentance, the commentator explains its essential components. Repentance is not just words; it has three pillars: regret for the sin, firm determination not to return, and immediate cessation. This lesson unpacks each pillar and the related duties of restoring rights and seeking forgiveness from those wronged.

Guided Path
1
Read the three pillars
The commentator lists three pillars: regret (al-nadam), determination (al-ʿazm), and immediate desistance (al-iqlāʿ).
This is the heart of the lesson.
2
Examine each pillar in detail
Reflect on what each means: regret is heartfelt sorrow; determination is a firm resolve; immediate desistance means stopping at once.
3
Consider the practical implications
The commentator explains that one must stop sinful acts like fornication and drinking, and also restore rights to the wronged or seek their pardon.
If direct pardon is impossible, one should supplicate and give charity on their behalf.
4
Trust in Allah's justice
The passage ends with hope: if Allah sees sincerity, He will satisfy the adversaries on your behalf.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وجَدِّدِ التَّوْبَةَ للأوْزَارِ . لا تَيْأَسَنُ مِنْ رَحمةِ الغَفَّارِ | شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي: كما أن من لا أرض له لا نبات له كذلك من لا توبة له لا حال له، ولا مقام. قال رجل للحسن البصري رحمه الله تعالى: «العبد يذنب ثم يتوب ويستغفر، وإذا هو يرجع إلى الذنب، فقال له: «ذلك شأن المؤمن يذنب، ثم يتوب ويستغفر ويرجع إلى مولاه تبارك وتعالى»، أي: ولو عاد إلى الذنب في اليوم الواحد مرارًا. وأُخِذ من ذلك أن التوبة لا تنتقض بالرجوع إلى الذنب، ولو رجعت إليه في اليوم ألف مرة، ويجب تجديدها عند كل رجوع، قال تعالى: إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ التَّوَّابِينَ وَيُحِبُّ الْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ (١)، ولذلك قال الناظم تغمده الله برحمته: «وجدد التوبة للأوزار»، والعبد لا يزال مُقصرًا فقيرًا لمولاه، ليس له من يرجع في أحواله إلا إليه. أركان التوبة ولتعلم أن للتوبة أركانا ثلاثة: الندم على ما وقع فيه من المخالفات. والعزم على أن لا يعود لمثله. والإقلاع عن الذنب في الحال. فيجب الكف عن استتمام الزنا، وشرب الخمر، وعن أذى الناس ويجب رَدّ المظالم إلى أهلها واستسماح المظلوم إن أمكن، وإلا استغفر له وتصدق له بما يمكنه، فإن الله تعالى وهو الغني إذا علم منك الصدق أرضى عنك خصماءك.
And renew repentance for sins. Do not despair of the mercy of the All-Forgiving. | Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashshāt al-Malikī al-Mālikī: Just as one who has no land has no vegetation, likewise one who has no repentance has no state (ḥāl) nor station (maqām). A man said to al-Ḥasan al-Baṣrī, may God have mercy on him: 'The servant sins, then repents and seeks forgiveness, and then he returns to sin.' He said to him: 'That is the matter of the believer: he sins, then repents and seeks forgiveness, and returns to his Lord, Blessed and Exalted' – meaning, even if he returns to sin many times in a single day. And it is derived from that that repentance is not nullified by returning to sin, even if one returns to it a thousand times a day, and it is obligatory to renew it at every return. God Almighty said: 'Indeed, God loves those who repent and loves those who purify themselves' (2:222). Therefore the poet, may God envelop him in His mercy, said: 'And renew repentance for sins.' And the servant is always falling short and poor before his Lord; he has no one to turn to in his states except Him. The pillars of repentance: And know that repentance has three pillars: 1) regret for the violations that occurred; 2) determination not to return to the like of it; 3) desisting from the sin immediately. So one must refrain from completing fornication, drinking wine, and harming people, and it is obligatory to return wrongs to their owners and seek the pardon of the wronged if possible, otherwise pray for forgiveness for him and give charity on his behalf as much as possible. For God Almighty – and He is the Rich – if He knows sincerity from you, He will satisfy your adversaries on your behalf.
Source pages p. 109

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on the line 'And renew repentance for sins' and the following line 'Do not despair of the mercy of the All-Forgiving.' The commentator begins with an analogy: just as land is necessary for vegetation, repentance is necessary for any spiritual state or station. He then narrates an exchange with al-Ḥasan al-Baṣrī: a man observed that a believer sins, repents, and then sins again. Al-Ḥasan replied that this is the believer's way – he constantly returns to his Lord even if he returns to sin many times a day. From this, the commentator derives that repentance is not invalidated by returning to sin, and it must be renewed upon each return. He cites Quran 2:222: 'Indeed, God loves those who repent.' Then he states the three pillars of repentance: regret (nadam), determination to not return (ʿazm), and immediate desistance (iqlāʿ). He elaborates that one must stop sinful acts like fornication or drinking and must also restore rights to the wronged or seek forgiveness and give charity on their behalf if direct reconciliation is impossible. The passage concludes with the reassurance that if God sees sincerity, He will satisfy the wronged parties.

Key Terms
التوبة
الندم
العزم
الإقلاع
أركان التوبة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to the commentator, what are the three pillars of repentance (arkān al-tawba)?
A) Regret, determination, and immediate desistance.
B) Fasting, prayer, and charity.
C) Confession, penance, and absolution.
D) Seeking forgiveness, avoiding sin, and doing good deeds.

Key Takeaway
Repentance is built on three pillars: regret, determination, and immediate desistance, along with restoring rights to others.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sin against someone, do you truly seek their pardon as the pillar of 'returning wrongs to their owners' demands, or do you merely ask Allah for forgiveness while ignoring the person you wronged? What is one relationship you need to repair today?
?
The pillar of 'immediate desistance' requires you to stop sinning instantly. Is there a sin you have been delaying giving up, telling yourself 'I'll quit later'? What is holding you back from stopping now?
?
The passage ends with the hope that Allah will satisfy your adversaries if He sees your sincerity. Do you believe that sincere repentance can truly undo the harm caused, even when you cannot make amends directly? How does this affect your view of past wrongs you cannot fix?

The Resilience of Repentance
Commentary explains that repentance is not invalidated by returning to sin.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why returning to sin after repentance does not invalidate the previous repentance, and that one must renew repentance each time.

Core Idea
Repentance is never wasted; renew it every time you fall.

This passage from al-Bahja al-Saniyya addresses a common concern: if I repent but later sin again, was my repentance accepted? The commentary uses a beautiful analogy and a story from Hasan al-Basri to reassure that repentance remains valid and must be renewed.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation to get the meaning.
Notice the analogy at the beginning.
2
Understand the analogy of land and plants
The author compares a person without repentance to land without soil. Think about why repentance is essential for spiritual growth.
This analogy sets the stage.
3
Learn from the story of Hasan al-Basri
A man asks about a believer who repeatedly sins and repents. Hasan al-Basri says this is the believer's way – he always returns to his Lord.
Even multiple returns in one day are covered.
4
Grasp the principle: repentance is not invalidated
The commentary derives that repentance is not nullified by returning to sin. Instead, one must renew it each time. The Quran verse supports this.
This is the key takeaway.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي أن من لا أرض له لا نبات له كذلك من لا توبة له لا حال له، ولا مقام. قال رجل للحسن البصري رحمه الله تعالى: «العبد يذنب ثم يتوب ويستغفر، وإذا هو يرجع إلى الذنب، فقال له: «ذلك شأن المؤمن يذنب، ثم يتوب ويستغفر ويرجع إلى مولاه تبارك وتعالى»، أي: ولو عاد إلى الذنب في اليوم الواحد مرارًا. وأُخِذ من ذلك أن التوبة لا تنتقض بالرجوع إلى الذنب، ولو رجعت إليه في اليوم ألف مرة، ويجب تجديدها عند كل رجوع، قال تعالى: إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ التَّوَّابِينَ وَيُحِبُّ الْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ (١)، ولذلك قال الناظم تغمده الله برحمته: «وجدد التوبة للأوزار»، والعبد لا يزال مُقصرًا فقيرًا لمولاه، ليس له من يرجع في أحواله إلا إليه.
Explanation by Sheikh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashshāṭ al-Māliki: Indeed, one who has no land has no plants; likewise, one who has no repentance has no state and no station. A man said to al-Ḥasan al-Baṣrī, may Allah have mercy on him: 'The servant sins, then repents and seeks forgiveness, and then he returns to sin.' He said to him: 'That is the affair of the believer: he sins, then repents and seeks forgiveness, and returns to his Lord, Blessed and Exalted.' That is, even if he returns to sin multiple times in a single day. And it is derived from this that repentance is not invalidated by returning to sin, even if one returns to it a thousand times in a day, and it is obligatory to renew it at every return. Allah Most High said: 'Indeed, Allah loves those who repent and loves those who purify themselves' (Sūrat al-Baqara, 2:222). Therefore, the author, may Allah envelope him in His mercy, said: 'And renew repentance for sins.' And the servant remains deficient and poor to his Lord; he has no one to turn to in his states except Him.
Source pages p. 109

Commentary
This passage is an exposition on the nature of repentance, using a simile and a historical anecdote. The author begins by drawing an analogy: just as land without soil produces no plants, a person without repentance has no spiritual state or station. This underscores that repentance is the foundation of the spiritual life. He then relates a story about a man who asked al-Ḥasan al-Baṣrī about a servant who repeatedly sins, repents, and then sins again. Al-Ḥasan replied that this is characteristic of the believer—he keeps returning to his Lord, even if he falls back into sin multiple times in one day. The author extracts a principle from this: repentance is not invalidated by returning to sin, no matter how often one returns, and it becomes obligatory to renew repentance each time one falls. He supports this with the Qur'anic verse that Allah loves those who repent. The author then ties this back to the original poem, emphasizing that the servant is perpetually deficient and dependent on Allah, having no refuge other than Him.

Key Terms
توبة
رجوع
تجديد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That returning to sin after repentance invalidates the previous repentance.
Correction: The commentary explicitly states that repentance is not invalidated by returning to sin; rather, one must renew it.

Checkpoint
According to the commentary, what is the ruling on a person who repents but later returns to the same sin?
A) Their previous repentance is invalidated.
B) They must seek forgiveness only once.
C) Their repentance is not invalidated, and they must renew it.
D) They are considered disbelievers.

Key Takeaway
Repentance remains valid even after repeated sin; renew it sincerely each time.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sin after repenting, do you feel your previous repentance was worthless? How does this passage challenge that feeling, and what will you do the next time you fall?
?
How often do you consciously renew your repentance for the same sin? If rarely, what holds you back from constantly turning to Allah as described here?
?
The passage ends by reminding you that you are always deficient and poor to your Lord. How does this truth affect your attitude toward your repeated sins—will you now see them as opportunities to return to Allah rather than reasons for despair? What specific change will you make today in how you handle your next failure?

What Is Kalam?
beginner
4 min
The Three Pillars of Repentance
Author enumerates the three pillars of repentance and their requirements.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three essential components of repentance according to Islamic theology and the practical requirements of making amends.

Core Idea
True repentance requires regret, resolve, and immediate action.

Repentance (tawba) is a central concept in Islamic spirituality, but it is often misunderstood as merely feeling sorry. The author clarifies that repentance has three distinct pillars, each with specific demands. This passage lays out the foundational structure that every believer must know to repent sincerely.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage attentively
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Pay attention to the list format and the author's elaboration after the three pillars.
2
Identify the three pillars
Find the three terms: الندم, العزم, الإقلاع. Notice the specific conditions for each pillar.
The third pillar includes immediate cessation and making amends.
3
Understand the obligation of restoring rights
The passage details that if the sin involved injustice, one must return rights and seek forgiveness. Consider the implications for communal harmony.
4
Reflect on the role of divine sincerity
The passage ends with the assurance that Allah will reconcile adversaries if He sees sincerity. This highlights the spiritual dimension of repentance.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أركان التوبة ولتعلم أن للتوبة أركانا ثلاثة: الندم على ما وقع فيه من المخالفات. والعزم على أن لا يعود لمثله. والإقلاع عن الذنب في الحال. فيجب الكف عن استتمام الزنا، وشرب الخمر، وعن أذى الناس ويجب رَدّ المظالم إلى أهلها واستسماح المظلوم إن أمكن، وإلا استغفر له وتصدق له بما يمكنه، فإن الله تعالى وهو الغني إذا علم منك الصدق أرضى عنك خصماءك.
The pillars of repentance: Know that repentance has three pillars: regret over the violations that occurred, resolve not to return to the like of it, and immediate abandonment of the sin. So it is obligatory to refrain from completing adultery, drinking wine, and harming people, and it is obligatory to return wrongs to their owners and to seek the forgiveness of the wronged person if possible; otherwise, seek forgiveness for them and give charity on their behalf as much as possible. For Allah Most High, Who is the Self-Sufficient, if He knows sincerity from you, He will make your adversaries content with you.
Source pages p. 109

Commentary
This passage defines the three essential components (arkān) of repentance. The author states that repentance has three pillars: (1) remorse (nadam) for the sins committed, (2) a firm resolve (ʿazm) not to return to that sin in the future, and (3) immediate cessation (iqlaʿ) of the sinful act. After listing these, the author elaborates on the practical application of the third pillar: one must immediately stop the sin, such as refraining from completing adultery, drinking wine, or harming others. Additionally, if the sin involved injustice toward others (ẓulm), the person must restore rights to their owners and seek the forgiveness of the wronged party. If that is not possible, one should make supplication and give charity on their behalf. The author concludes by emphasizing that if Allah sees sincerity, He will cause one's adversaries to be satisfied, even without direct reconciliation.

Key Terms
أركان التوبة
الندم
العزم
الإقلاع
رد المظالم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think repentance is just feeling regret, without stopping the sin or making amends.
Correction: The passage clearly shows that repentance has three pillars including immediate cessation and restoring rights, so it is a comprehensive act.

Checkpoint
What is the third pillar of repentance according to the passage?
A) Regret and remorse.
B) Resolve not to return to sin.
C) Immediate abandonment of the sin.
D) Seeking forgiveness from others.

Key Takeaway
Repentance is not complete without regret, resolve to change, and immediate action to stop the sin and make amends.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever regretted a sin but continued in it? What is holding you back from immediate cessation today? Commit to stopping it now.
?
Is there someone you have wronged that you have not yet sought forgiveness from? What is stopping you from reaching out to them before tomorrow? Write down the step you will take.
?
When you sin, do you rush to make amends with others, or do you only focus on your private repentance with Allah? How can you practice the third pillar—returning rights and seeking forgiveness—more actively starting today?

Do Not Despair of Allah's Mercy
Commentary on not despairing of mercy with Qur'anic proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that despairing of Allah's mercy is forbidden and equated with disbelief, and that renewing repentance is a powerful act against Satan.

Core Idea
Despair is disbelief; mercy encompasses everything.

This lesson examines the command not to despair of Allah's mercy. The commentator explains that despair is a trait of disbelievers and that renewing repentance is particularly hard on Satan. The passage grounds this in a Qur'anic verse and in the vastness of divine mercy.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the passage once to grasp the overall teaching. Notice the direct address 'Do not despair' and the explanation that follows.
The passage begins with the poem line and then the commentary.
2
Identify the Qur'anic proof
The commentator quotes verse 12:87 from Surah Yusuf. Understand that this verse establishes the ruling: only disbelievers despair of Allah's relief.
This is a key evidence that despair is a serious offense.
3
Reflect on Satan's reaction
The commentator mentions that nothing is harder on Satan than a believer renewing repentance. This shows the importance of never giving up on turning back to Allah.
Repentance is a weapon against despair.
4
Consider the scope of mercy
The passage says 'the mercy of our Lord encompasses everything.' This universal mercy is the reason why despair is unjustified.
No sin is too great for Allah's mercy.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(ولا تيأسن) أي: لا تقنط من رحمة الغفار) أي: الستار للذنوب، فإن رحمة ربنا وسعت كلّ شيء، قال الشارح رحمه الله تعالى: «وليس أشد على الشيطان من تجديد المؤمن للتوبة قال تعالى: إِنَّهُ لَا يَيْأَسُ مِن رَّوْحِ اللَّهِ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْكَافِرُونَ (١)، (٢). واعلم أن أصول الطريق الموصل إلى الله عشرة، ذكرها الناظم في الطريق الموصل إلى الله شرحه» (۳) مفصلة وموضحة، وأشار لها هنا في النظم مبتدئا بالتوبة، ومُتَنيا بالشكر للمنعم الحقيقي جَلَّ وَعَلا فقال: وكن) أيها السالك الطريق إلى الله تعالى ( على آلائه جمع إلى كمعى وأمعاء، أي: كن على نعمه التي أنعمها عليك، كنعمة الإيمان وهو أجلها، والعلم والسمع والبصر إلى ما لا نصل فيه إلى عد وإحصاء، وإن تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَةَ اللهِ لَا تُحْصُوهَا (٤) . (شكورًا) أي: كثير الشكر ، وهو صرف العبد جميع ما أنعم الله به إلى ما خلق لأجله، فهو يرجع إلى: اعتقاد الجنان، بأن يعتقد أن لا نعمة في الوجود إلى من الله الكريم الموجود.
('And do not despair') i.e., do not lose hope of the mercy of the All-Forgiving, i.e., the One who conceals sins, for the mercy of our Lord encompasses everything. The commentator, may Allah have mercy on him, said: 'Nothing is more severe upon Satan than the renewing of repentance by the believer.' Allah Most High said: 'Indeed, no one despairs of the relief of Allah except the disbelieving people' (Sūrat Yūsuf, 12:87). Know that the foundations of the path leading to Allah are ten; the author mentioned them in 'The Path Leading to Allah' with explanation, elaborated and clarified, and he alluded to them here in the poem, beginning with repentance and concluding with gratitude to the true Benefactor, Exalted and High. He said: ('And be') O traveler on the path to Allah Most High, ('upon His blessings') [ālā'ih] plural like akma' and am'a', i.e., be upon His favors that He has bestowed upon you, such as the blessing of faith—which is the greatest—and knowledge, hearing, and sight, to the extent that we cannot reach the count and enumeration of them: 'And if you should count the favors of Allah, you could not enumerate them' (Sūrat an-Naḥl, 16:18). ('Grateful') i.e., abundantly grateful, and it is the servant's directing all that Allah has bestowed upon him to that for which it was created. So it goes back to: belief in the heart, by believing that there is no blessing in existence except from Allah, the Generous, the Existing.
Source pages p. 110

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on two lines of the poem: 'And do not despair of the mercy of the All-Forgiving' and 'And be grateful for His blessings.' The commentator first explains that despair is forbidden because Allah's mercy is vast, and nothing is harder on Satan than a believer renewing repentance, citing the verse from Sūrat Yūsuf. He then notes that the author of the poem has outlined ten principles of the spiritual path, which are detailed in another work, and here in the poem he begins with repentance and ends with gratitude. The commentator then explains the meaning of 'being grateful': it is to direct all of Allah's blessings toward the purpose for which they were created. He breaks this down into three aspects (though only one is mentioned here due to the text cutting off): the first is belief in the heart (iʿtiqād al-janān), which is to believe that every blessing in existence comes only from Allah. The passage ends before completing the list, but the implication is that gratitude also involves verbal expression and physical action.

Key Terms
تيأس
رحمة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that despairing of mercy is just a minor sin or that certain sins are too great for Allah to forgive.
Correction: The passage equates despair with disbelief (kufr) by citing the verse that only disbelievers despair, and it affirms that mercy encompasses everything, so no sin is beyond forgiveness.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, who are the only people who despair of Allah's relief?
A) Those who commit major sins.
B) The disbelieving people.
C) Those who have not repented.
D) The hypocrites.

Key Takeaway
Despairing of Allah's mercy is a sign of disbelief, and believers should never lose hope, for His mercy encompasses all.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Think of a sin you have committed repeatedly. Is there a part of you that says, 'I've done it too many times, Allah won't forgive me'? How does the verse 'Indeed, no one despairs of the relief of Allah except the disbelieving people' confront that voice? Will you repent right now?
?
The commentator says nothing is harder on Satan than a believer renewing repentance. What is one area of your life where you have been avoiding repentance because of shame or hopelessness? What concrete step will you take today to renew your repentance and break Satan's hold?
?
If you truly believe that 'the mercy of your Lord encompasses everything,' what does that imply about the biggest sin you have committed? Are you willing to let go of your despair and place your complete trust in His mercy, even for that sin?
