What Is Kalam?
beginner
6 min
The Meaning of Gratitude: Heartfelt Belief
Commentary defining gratitude and its first component: inner belief.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that true gratitude (shukr) begins with the inner conviction that every blessing comes from Allah alone.

Core Idea
Gratitude starts with believing all blessings are from Allah.

This lesson unpacks the definition of gratitude given by the commentator. He explains that being grateful (shakūr) involves directing all blessings to their purpose, starting with inner conviction that every favor comes from Allah alone. This foundational aspect is belief in the heart (iʿtiqād al-janān).

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the passage that begins with 'واعلم أن أصول الطريق' and ends with the definition. Notice the mention of ten principles and then the focus on gratitude.
The passage transitions from the ten principles to the specific command to be grateful.
2
Identify the command and its object
The poem line says 'And be grateful for His blessings.' The commentator explains 'upon His blessings' as enumerating various favors, with faith being the greatest.
The blessings include faith, knowledge, hearing, sight – countless gifts.
3
Grasp the definition of shukr (gratitude)
The commentator defines shukr as 'directing all that Allah has bestowed to that for which it was created.' Then he breaks it down into components, starting with 'belief in the heart.'
This shows gratitude is not just words or actions but includes inner conviction.
4
Understand 'belief in the heart'
The passage specifies that belief in the heart is to believe that no blessing exists except from Allah. This is the first and foundational aspect of gratitude.
Without this inner belief, verbal or physical gratitude is incomplete.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(ولا تيأسن) أي: لا تقنط من رحمة الغفار) أي: الستار للذنوب، فإن رحمة ربنا وسعت كلّ شيء، قال الشارح رحمه الله تعالى: «وليس أشد على الشيطان من تجديد المؤمن للتوبة قال تعالى: إِنَّهُ لَا يَيْأَسُ مِن رَّوْحِ اللَّهِ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْكَافِرُونَ (١)، (٢). واعلم أن أصول الطريق الموصل إلى الله عشرة، ذكرها الناظم في الطريق الموصل إلى الله شرحه» (۳) مفصلة وموضحة، وأشار لها هنا في النظم مبتدئا بالتوبة، ومُتَنيا بالشكر للمنعم الحقيقي جَلَّ وَعَلا فقال: وكن) أيها السالك الطريق إلى الله تعالى ( على آلائه جمع إلى كمعى وأمعاء، أي: كن على نعمه التي أنعمها عليك، كنعمة الإيمان وهو أجلها، والعلم والسمع والبصر إلى ما لا نصل فيه إلى عد وإحصاء، وإن تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَةَ اللهِ لَا تُحْصُوهَا (٤) . (شكورًا) أي: كثير الشكر ، وهو صرف العبد جميع ما أنعم الله به إلى ما خلق لأجله، فهو يرجع إلى: اعتقاد الجنان، بأن يعتقد أن لا نعمة في الوجود إلى من الله الكريم الموجود.
('And do not despair') i.e., do not lose hope of the mercy of the All-Forgiving, i.e., the One who conceals sins, for the mercy of our Lord encompasses everything. The commentator, may Allah have mercy on him, said: 'Nothing is more severe upon Satan than the renewing of repentance by the believer.' Allah Most High said: 'Indeed, no one despairs of the relief of Allah except the disbelieving people' (Sūrat Yūsuf, 12:87). Know that the foundations of the path leading to Allah are ten; the author mentioned them in 'The Path Leading to Allah' with explanation, elaborated and clarified, and he alluded to them here in the poem, beginning with repentance and concluding with gratitude to the true Benefactor, Exalted and High. He said: ('And be') O traveler on the path to Allah Most High, ('upon His blessings') [ālā'ih] plural like akma' and am'a', i.e., be upon His favors that He has bestowed upon you, such as the blessing of faith—which is the greatest—and knowledge, hearing, and sight, to the extent that we cannot reach the count and enumeration of them: 'And if you should count the favors of Allah, you could not enumerate them' (Sūrat an-Naḥl, 16:18). ('Grateful') i.e., abundantly grateful, and it is the servant's directing all that Allah has bestowed upon him to that for which it was created. So it goes back to: belief in the heart, by believing that there is no blessing in existence except from Allah, the Generous, the Existing.
Source pages p. 110

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on two lines of the poem: 'And do not despair of the mercy of the All-Forgiving' and 'And be grateful for His blessings.' The commentator first explains that despair is forbidden because Allah's mercy is vast, and nothing is harder on Satan than a believer renewing repentance, citing the verse from Sūrat Yūsuf. He then notes that the author of the poem has outlined ten principles of the spiritual path, which are detailed in another work, and here in the poem he begins with repentance and ends with gratitude. The commentator then explains the meaning of 'being grateful': it is to direct all of Allah's blessings toward the purpose for which they were created. He breaks this down into three aspects (though only one is mentioned here due to the text cutting off): the first is belief in the heart (iʿtiqād al-janān), which is to believe that every blessing in existence comes only from Allah. The passage ends before completing the list, but the implication is that gratitude also involves verbal expression and physical action.

Key Terms
شكر
آلاء
شكور
اعتقاد الجنان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think gratitude (shukr) is only saying 'thank you' or performing physical acts of worship.
Correction: The passage shows that gratitude begins with inner belief (iʿtiqād al-janān) that all blessings come from Allah. This is foundational; without it, external expressions are incomplete.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the first aspect of gratitude (shukr)?
A) Speaking words of thanks.
B) Giving charity.
C) Believing in the heart that all blessings come from Allah.
D) Performing extra prayers.

Key Takeaway
Gratitude is not just words; it begins with the firm belief in the heart that every blessing is from Allah alone.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that every good thing in your life—your health, wealth, and even your faith—is from Allah alone? When was the last time your heart actually felt that gratitude, not just your tongue?
?
If gratitude begins in the heart, what hidden ingratitude are you carrying without realizing it? Take a moment to identify one blessing you have been treating as ordinary and commit to a specific act of thankfulness for it today.
?
The blessing of faith is the greatest, yet it can become routine. Ask yourself: if you lost it, how desperate would you be to regain it? Now, let that urgency transform your heart's gratitude into action—how will you use your faith today to serve its purpose?

What Is Kalam?
beginner
5 min
The Supplication for Protection from Cutters and the Heart's Diseases
Explains the supplication for protection from obstacles and lists heart diseases.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the meaning of the supplication 'Rabbi la taqta'ni 'anka bi qati'' and the two categories of cutters that can separate a servant from Allah.

Core Idea
The heart's diseases are the most dangerous cutters from Allah.

This passage presents a powerful supplication taught to the spiritual seeker. It defines the concept of 'qawati'' (cutters) as anything that distracts from worship, and highlights that inner heart diseases like envy and hypocrisy are among the most serious obstacles. As you read, note how the commentator broadens the understanding of what can separate us from Allah.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation carefully
First, read the Arabic passage without analysis, then read the English translation. Let the words of the supplication and the commentator's explanation sink in.
Notice the phrase 'بذل وانكسار' (lowliness and brokenness) describing the servant's state.
2
Identify the proper state for this supplication
The passage begins by describing the state of the servant: lowliness and brokenness. Understand why this state is emphasized and how it relates to the answer of supplication.
Allah is with the broken-hearted, as mentioned.
3
Define 'qawati'' (cutters)
The commentator defines 'qawati'' as every trial (fitna) that distracts the servant from worship and its duties. Pay attention to the examples given.
The cutters include both external attachments and internal diseases.
4
Recognize the inner heart diseases listed
The passage lists four specific heart diseases: spite (hiqd), envy (hasad), hypocrisy (riya'), and self-admiration ('ujb). Reflect on why these are considered cutters.
These are internal obstacles that are often overlooked.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(وقُل) أيها العبد السالك متضرعًا لمولاك حال كونك متلبسًا (بذل وانكسار، فإن الله عند المنكسرة قلوبهم، وهو من دواعي الإجابة: (رب لا تقطعني عنك بقاطع من القواطع، وهي كل فتنة تشغل العبد عن العبودية، والقيام بوظائفها، من تعلق بمال أو ولد أو جاه، ومن القواطع عن الله تعالى: الأمراض القلبية، كالحقد والحسد، والرياء، والعُجب.
And say, O the traveling servant, supplicating to your Lord while you are adorned with lowliness and brokenness, for Allah is with those whose hearts are broken, and that is among the means of answer: 'Lord, cut me not off from You by any cutter from the cutters, and they are every trial that occupies the servant from worship and performing its duties, from attachment to wealth or child or status, and among the cutters from Allah Almighty are heart diseases, such as spite, envy, hypocrisy, and self-admiration.'
Source pages p. 121

Commentary
This passage is an explanation of the supplication 'Rabbi la taqta'ni 'anka bi qati''. The commentator begins by describing the proper state for this supplication: the servant should be in a state of lowliness and brokenness before his Lord, because Allah is with those whose hearts are broken, and that state is a cause for the answer of the supplication. Then he defines 'qawati' (cutters) as every trial (fitna) that distracts the servant from worship and fulfilling its duties. He gives examples of such distractions: attachment to wealth, children, or status. He then specifies that among the most dangerous cutters are inner heart diseases: spite, envy, hypocrisy, and self-admiration. The point is that the believer should ask Allah to protect him from everything that cuts him off from his Lord, both external distractions and internal vices.

Key Terms
القواطع
العبودية
الأمراض القلبية
الحقد
الحسد
الرياء
العُجب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think 'cutters' refer only to physical sins or outward distractions.
Correction: The passage emphasizes that inner heart diseases like spite, envy, hypocrisy, and self-admiration are also major cutters from Allah.

Checkpoint
What are the 'qawati' (cutters) that the servant asks Allah to protect him from, according to this passage?
A) Only physical sins like theft and adultery
B) Every trial that distracts from worship, including heart diseases like spite and envy
C) Only attachment to wealth and children
D) Only diseases of the body

Key Takeaway
The supplication 'Rabbi la taqta'ni' asks Allah to protect from everything that distracts from worship, especially inner diseases of the heart.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Which of the heart diseases mentioned in this passage—spite, envy, hypocrisy, or self-admiration—do you find secretly residing in your heart right now? Will you sincerely repent to Allah and beg Him to purify you before you sleep tonight?
?
Are you attached to your wealth, your children, or your status in a way that distracts you from the duties of worship? What specific attachment will you begin to let go of this week to draw closer to Allah?
?
The passage says Allah is with the broken-hearted—those who are lowly and broken before Him. Is your heart truly broken and humble before Allah, or are you proud and self-sufficient? What one act of humility will you perform today to nurture that brokenness?

What Is Kalam?
beginner
5 min
The Most Radiant Secret and the Two Levels of Certainty
Explaining the divine light and two levels of certainty

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the meaning of the divine light (al-sirr al-abha) that enables discernment, and the distinction between 'ilm al-yaqin (knowledge by proof) and haqq al-yaqin (knowledge by direct witnessing).

Core Idea
Certainty comes in degrees: proof, sight, and direct experience.

This lesson unpacks a beautiful supplication asking for the 'most radiant secret'—a divine light that distinguishes truth from falsehood. The commentator explains this as a spiritual illumination granted to the believer. He then clarifies that certainty is not a single state but has two levels: knowledge through proof and knowledge through direct witnessing, each with a different closeness to reality.

Guided Path
1
Read the supplication and its meaning
Read the Arabic supplication 'wa la tahrimni min sirrika al-abha' and its literal translation. Notice the plea for a divine secret.
The verb 'tahrimni' can be read two ways, but both mean 'do not prevent me'.
2
Understand the divine light as criterion
The 'secret' is defined as a divine light (nur ilahi) that enables the servant to distinguish truth from falsehood, like the furqan mentioned in the Quran.
This light is placed in the heart, granting spiritual discernment.
3
Distinguish the two levels of certainty
Identify 'ilm al-yaqin (knowledge by proof) and haqq al-yaqin (knowledge by direct witnessing). See how they differ in proximity to the truth.
The example of fire illustrates this: smoke inference vs. sight vs. direct contact.
4
Reflect on the example of fire
Consider the three scenarios: inferring fire from smoke, seeing fire from a distance, and being in the fire. Map each to a level of certainty.
This makes the abstract distinction concrete.
5
Note the condition for answered supplication
The passage concludes that supplication is beneficial when accompanied by humility at noble times like before dawn and after prayers.
This shows the practical dimension of the lesson.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(ولا تحرمني) أي: وقُل بذل رب لا تحرمني - بفتح التاء، ثلاثي من حرم، أو بضمها من أحرم - أي: لا تمنعني. (من) إعطاءك سرك (الأبهى من كل نور، قال المؤلف: «والمراد به النور الإلهي الذي يفرق به العبد بين الحق والباطل في نفس الأمر المشار إليه بقوله تعالى: ﴿ يَأَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا إِن تَتَّقُوا اللَّهَ يَجْعَل لَّكُمْ فرقانا ﴾». أي نورًا في قلوبكم تميزون به بين الحق والباطل على ما هو عليه في نفس الأمر، وذلك أن علم اليقين هو معرفة الأشياء بالبرهان، قال تعالى: ﴿ لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ ﴾. وحق اليقين معرفتها بالمشاهدة، من غير مخالطة ولا ممازجة، قال تعالى: ﴿ فَنُزُلُ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَتَصْلِيَةُ جَحِيمٍ إِنَّ هَذَا لَهُوَ حَقُّ الْيَقِينِ ﴾، فليس من استدل على وجود نار برؤية الدخان كمن شاهدها على بعد، وليس من شاهدها كمن خالطها وعلم وقودها، وما هي عليه. (المزيل للعمى) نعت ثان للسر، أي سرك الموصوف بالأنوار، والمزيل للعمى، أي: عن الجهل وطمس البصيرة والعياذ بالله، وفيه دليل على أن الدعاء ينفع وهو مما لا شك فيه لكن بشرط أن يكون مصاحبًا للذل والخضوع في الأوقات الشريفة، كالأسحار، ودبر الصلوات.
And deprive me not—that is, say with lowliness: O Lord, deprive me not (with fath of the ta, triliteral from harama, or with damm from ahrama), i.e., do not prevent me. From bestowing upon me Your secret (the most radiant over every light). The author said: 'What is meant is the divine light by which the servant distinguishes between truth and falsehood in themselves, indicated by His saying: O you who believe, if you fear Allah, He will grant you a criterion (furqan).' That is, a light in your hearts by which you distinguish between truth and falsehood as they are in themselves. That is because 'ilm al-yaqin is knowledge of things by proof; He said: If you knew with certainty of knowledge. And haqq al-yaqin is knowing them by direct witnessing, without intermixing or blending; He said: Then accommodation of boiling water and roasting in hellfire; indeed, this is the truth of certainty. So the one who infers the existence of fire by seeing smoke is not like the one who sees it from a distance, and the one who sees it is not like the one who mixes with it and knows its fuel and what it is. (The remover of blindness) is a second attribute of the secret, i.e., Your secret described with lights, and the remover of blindness, i.e., from ignorance and dullness of insight—Allah forbid. And in it is evidence that supplication is beneficial, which is beyond doubt, but on condition that it is accompanied by lowliness and humility in noble times, such as before dawn and after prayers.
Source pages p. 121

Commentary
This passage explains the supplication 'wa la tahrimni min sirrika al-abha' (and deprive me not of Your most radiant secret). The commentator first clarifies the verb: it means 'do not prevent me.' Then he defines the 'secret' as the divine light (nur ilahi) by which the servant distinguishes between truth and falsehood as they are in themselves, citing the Qur'anic verse about 'furqan' (criterion) from Surah al-Anfal. He elaborates that this light is placed in the heart, enabling discernment. He then distinguishes two levels of certainty: 'ilm al-yaqin (knowledge of certainty) which is knowledge through proof (burhan), and haqq al-yaqin (truth of certainty) which is direct witnessing (mushahada) without any intermediation. He gives an example: inferring fire from smoke is like 'ilm al-yaqin; seeing fire from afar is a higher level; mixing with fire and knowing its fuel is like haqq al-yaqin. Then he explains the phrase 'al-muzil li-l-'ama' (the remover of blindness) as a second attribute of the secret: it removes ignorance and dullness of insight. Finally, he notes that this shows supplication is beneficial, provided it is accompanied by lowliness and humility at noble times like before dawn and after prayers.

Key Terms
السر الأبهى
النور الإلهي
الفرقان
علم اليقين
حق اليقين
المشاهدة
البرهان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think certainty is a single monolithic level, but the passage shows three degrees: knowledge by proof, sight, and direct experience.
Correction: The text explicitly illustrates that inferring fire from smoke, seeing it from afar, and mixing with it are not the same. Similarly, 'ilm al-yaqin and haqq al-yaqin are distinct, and the example reveals three gradations.

Checkpoint
What is the difference between 'ilm al-yaqin and haqq al-yaqin according to this passage?
A) 'Ilm al-yaqin is from the Qur'an, haqq al-yaqin from the Sunnah
B) 'Ilm al-yaqin is knowledge by proof, haqq al-yaqin is knowledge by direct witnessing
C) 'Ilm al-yaqin is stronger than haqq al-yaqin
D) There is no difference

Key Takeaway
Certainty is not monolithic; it has degrees, from knowledge by proof to direct experience, each bringing one closer to the truth.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read about 'ilm al-yaqin' and 'haqq al-yaqin', which level do you honestly pray for? Are you content with reasoning about Allah's existence, or do you yearn for the direct experience that removes all doubt? What is stopping you from seeking that higher certainty tonight in your du'a?
?
The supplication asks for a 'secret' that removes blindness. In what area of your life are you still spiritually blind, and why haven't you begged Allah to remove it with the same urgency as this du'a? Write one area down and commit to making this specific du'a with lowliness before dawn tomorrow.
?
The author says supplication benefits only if accompanied by lowliness and humility. Think of your last du'a: did you feel that lowliness, or was it routine? What would it take for you to weep before Allah tonight over your certainty?

Textual and Editorial Notes
beginner
4 min
Understanding the Editor's Methodology
The editor describes his editorial methodology and changes made in this edition.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the editorial decisions behind this edition of al-Bahja al-Saniyya and how to identify corrections in the text.

Core Idea
Understanding the editor's method helps you read the text critically.

This preface explains the editorial process used in preparing this edition. Knowing which changes were made and why will help you appreciate the reliability of the text and engage with it more intelligently. The editor mentions corrections of errors, filling in omissions, and improvements in layout.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation to understand the editor's statements.
Notice the structure: he states his methodology, then lists changes.
2
Identify the types of corrections
The editor distinguishes between errors (أخطاء) and omissions (سقط). Note how he marks each.
Errors are noted in the margin; omissions are added in brackets.
3
Understand the layout changes
He placed the verses at the top and used bold for explained parts. This helps in following the commentary.
4
Reflect on the purpose
The editor prays for acceptance. Consider the scholarly care in producing a reliable text.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
البهجة السنية وقد اعتمدتُ في طبعتي هذه على طبعة قديمة طبعت في إندونيسيا، وقد وقعت لهم أخطاء وبعض سقط استدركته هنا، وقد ساعدني على استدراكه فضل الله تعالى، وعنايتي بـ«شرح الخريدة لسيدي أبي البركات أحمد الدردير، رَضِوَاللَّهُ عَنْهُ. أما الأخطاء فنبهت على الأهم منها في مواضعها، ذاكرًا الصواب في الأصل، ومحشيّا بالخطأ في الهامش. وأما مواضع السقط فزدتها في الأصل بين معقوفتين هكذا []، وقد أفدتها من شرح الخريدة» لسيدي أحمد الدردير، ومن مراجعة الكتب التي اعتمد عليها الشارح رحمة الله وقدَّمتُ العمل بترجمة موجزة للشيخ اقتبستها مما كتبه تلميذه وخريجه معالي الأستاذ الدكتور عبد الوهاب إبراهيم أبو سليمان، حفظه الله، عضو هيئة كبار العلماء بالمملكة العربية السعودية. ومما غيّرتُه في هذه الطبعة عن سابقتها أن جعلت أبيات الخريدة في أعلى الصفحات مع تمييز ما يشرحه المصنف بالخط الأسود الثقيل. عليه. وقد علقت تعليقات يسيرة استفدتها من شرح الخريدة البهية»، ومن التعليقات وفي الختام أسأل الله تعالى أن أكون قد وفقت في عملي، كما أسأله أن يجعله وسائر أعمالي خالصا لوجهه الكريم، وأن يرزقني علمًا نافعًا، ورزقا طيبًا واسعا، إنه على ذلك قدير وبالإجابة جدير. وصل اللهم على سيدنا محمد وعلى آله وصحبه وسلم.
Al-Bahja al-Saniyya. And I relied in this my print on an old edition printed in Indonesia; they had errors and some omissions which I corrected here. I was aided in correction by the grace of Allah the Exalted and my study of 'Sharh al-Kharida' by my master Abu al-Barakat Ahmad al-Dardir, may Allah be pleased with him. As for the errors, I drew attention to the most important of them in their places, mentioning the correct version in the original, and [the erroneous] footnoted in the margin. As for the omissions, I added them in the original between brackets like this [], and I benefited from 'Sharh al-Kharida' by my master Ahmad al-Dardir, and from reviewing the books upon which the commentator, the mercy of Allah, relied. And I presented the work with a brief biography of the shaykh, which I borrowed from what his student and graduate, His Excellency Professor Dr. ‘Abd al-Wahhab Ibrahim Abu Sulayman, may Allah preserve him, a member of the Council of Senior Scholars in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, wrote. Among the changes I made in this edition over its predecessor is that I placed the verses of al-Kharida at the top of the pages, distinguishing what the author explains in bold black type. upon him. And I made brief comments, which I benefited from 'Sharh al-Kharida al-Bahiyya' and from the glosses. And in conclusion, I ask Allah the Exalted that I have been successful in my work, and I ask Him to make it and all my deeds purely for His noble face, and to grant me beneficial knowledge, good and ample provision. Indeed, He is powerful over that and worthy of response. O Allah, bless our master Muhammad and his family and companions and grant them peace.
Source pages p. 7

Commentary
This passage is the editor's preface describing the methodology used in preparing this edition. The editor states that he relied on an old Indonesian edition, corrected its errors and supplied omissions. He added missing text in brackets, taken from al-Dardir's own commentary. He included a biographical sketch and changed the layout to show the poem verses at the top with explanations in bold. He also added brief marginal notes. The passage concludes with a prayer for divine acceptance.

Key Terms
الطبعة
السقط
الأخطاء
الشروح

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What did the editor add in brackets in the original text?
A) His own comments
B) Missing parts from the original commentary
C) Biographical notes
D) Variant readings

Key Takeaway
The editor's detailed methodology helps ensure the reliability of this edition by clearly marking corrections and layout changes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read a religious text, do you ever consider the meticulous editorial work behind it? Are you content to trust it blindly, or do you seek to understand the methodology that ensures its reliability? How can you mirror the editor's care in your own learning and teaching of Islamic knowledge?
?
The editor corrected errors and filled omissions with painstaking effort. In your own life, do you address your mistakes and gaps in knowledge with the same diligence, or do you let them pass? What could you start doing today to be more precise in your understanding and practice of your faith?

Life of Imam al-Mashat
beginner
4 min
Author's Biography: Name, Lineage, and Early Life
The biography introduces the author's full name, lineage, birth, and early scholastic career.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the background and scholarly lineage of the author, Ahmad Hasan al-Mashshāt.

Core Idea
Knowing the author's life illuminates the context of his theological work.

Before diving into the theological content of al-Bahja al-Saniyya, it is valuable to know something about its author. This biographical sketch introduces us to Ahmad Hasan al-Mashshāt, a Maliki scholar and judge from Mecca. His training under prominent scholars and his own teaching career shaped the commentary we are about to study.

Guided Path
1
Read the full biography
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation to get an overview of al-Mashshāt's life.
Pay attention to the many titles listed at the beginning.
2
Identify his scholarly titles
Notice the titles: 'al-Allama, al-Muhaddith, al-Usuli, al-Faqih, al-Qadi'. Each indicates a branch of Islamic learning.
These titles show the breadth of his scholarship.
3
Trace his lineage
His lineage goes back to Abd Manaf ibn Qusayy of Quraysh. Reflect on why lineage matters in Islamic scholarly tradition.
4
Map his education and teachers
List the schools and teachers mentioned: al-Sawlatiyya, Muhammad al-Khidr al-Jikani, etc. Consider the network of scholars he studied under.
5
Follow his career progression
He taught for 30 years, served on a commission, traveled, and became a judge. This shows his long dedication to learning and law.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ترجمة المؤلف اسمه ونسبه هو العلامة المحدث، الأصولي، الفقيه، القاضي، أبو أحمد حسن بن محمد بن عباس بن علي بن عبد الواحد المشاط، المكي المالكي، المنافي، نسبة الى عبد مناف ابن قصي القرشي. مولده ونشأته: ولد في الثالث من شهر شوال سنة ۱۳۱۷ من الهجرة النبوية، بحي القرارة، وأسرته من الأسر العلمية المكية العريقة التي أنجبت عددًا من العلماء. ألحقه والده للدراسة بالكتاتيب، ثم بالمدرسة الصولتية سنة ١٣٢٩ ، وجرى تعيينه مساعدًا للمدرسين، وهو قريب من سن التخرج، وبعد تخرجه عمل بنفس المدرسة مدرسًا، مدة ثلاثين سنة، ونال شهادة الجدارة منها عام ١٣٣٦، ودرس بالمسجد الحرام، ولازم علمائه، والتقى بعدد كبير من العلماء الواردين إلى البلد الحرام. فمن أساتذته: محمد الخضر بن مايابي الجكني وعمر حمدان المحرسي، ولازم المشايخ محمد حبيب الله الشنقيطي، ووالده الشيخ محمد المشاط. وفي عام ١٣٦١ عُيّن عضوًا في هيئة التمييز، وكان كعادة العلماء متورعًا عن هذه المسألة، حتى انحلت الهيئة عام ١٣٦٤ ، فسافر على إثر ذلك في رحلة مدتها عام تقريبًا إلى السودان ومصر وسوريه ولبنان، والتقى بعلمائها. في عام ١٣٦٥ عُيّن وكيلًا لرئيس المحكمة الشرعية، وظل بها مدة عامين حتى صار قاضيًا بها.
Translation of the author. His name and lineage: He is the eminent scholar, hadith specialist, usuli, jurist, judge, Abu Ahmad Hasan ibn Muhammad ibn Abbas ibn Ali ibn Abd al-Wahid al-Mashshāt, al-Makki al-Maliki, al-Manafi, in relation to Abd Manaf ibn Qusayy al-Qurashi. His birth and upbringing: He was born on the third of Shawwal in the year 1317 of the Prophet's Hijra, in the al-Qarara district, and his family is one of the ancient Meccan scholarly families that produced a number of scholars. His father enrolled him in the kuttab for study, then in al-Sawlatiyya school in the year 1329, and he was appointed as an assistant to the teachers while close to graduation age, and after graduation he worked as a teacher in the same school for thirty years, and he obtained a certificate of merit from it in the year 1336, and he taught in the Sacred Mosque, and frequently attended its scholars, and met a large number of scholars coming to the sacred land. Among his teachers: Muhammad al-Khidr ibn Mayabi al-Jikani and Umar Hamdan al-Muharrisi, and he frequented the shaykhs Muhammad Habib Allah al-Shanqiti and his father Shaykh Muhammad al-Mashshāt. In the year 1361 he was appointed a member of the Discrimination Commission, and as was the habit of scholars, he was scrupulous regarding this matter, until the commission was dissolved in the year 1364, so he travelled thereafter on a journey of about a year to Sudan, Egypt, Syria, and Lebanon, and met its scholars. In the year 1365 he was appointed deputy head of the Sharia court, and remained there for two years until he became a judge there.
Source pages p. 8

Commentary
This passage is a biographical sketch of the author, Ahmad Hasan al-Mashshāt. It begins by listing his full name, titles (eminent scholar, hadith specialist, usuli, jurist, judge), and lineage tracing back to Abd Manaf ibn Qusayy of the Quraysh, indicating his noble ancestry. He was born in Mecca's al-Qarara district on 3 Shawwal 1317 AH (approximately 1900 CE). His family was a scholarly family. His father sent him first to the kuttab (Quran school) and then to al-Sawlatiyya school in 1329 AH. He was made a teaching assistant before graduation, and after graduating he taught there for thirty years, earning a certificate of merit in 1336 AH. He also studied at the Sacred Mosque, attending the lessons of many scholars. The text then lists some of his teachers: Muhammad al-Khidr al-Jikani, Umar al-Muharrisi, Muhammad Habib Allah al-Shanqiti, and his own father Muhammad al-Mashshāt. Later, in 1361 AH, he was appointed to the Discrimination Commission, but he was cautious about the role, and after the commission dissolved in 1364, he traveled to Sudan, Egypt, Syria, and Lebanon for about a year, meeting their scholars. In 1365 AH he became deputy head of the Sharia court, and after two years became a judge.

القاضي

القرشي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think al-Mashshāt was a Sufi shaykh.
Correction: He is clearly a Maliki scholar and judge, as indicated by his titles and career.

Checkpoint

A) 1317
B) 1329
C) 1361
D) 1365

Key Takeaway
The author's scholarly pedigree and long teaching career establish his authority in the Maliki tradition.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The author taught at al-Sawlatiyya for thirty years without interruption. What is one distraction or obstacle in your life that prevents you from dedicating consistent time to seeking Islamic knowledge? What concrete step can you take this week to remove it?
?
When appointed to the Discrimination Commission, al-Mashshāt was 'متورعًا' (scrupulous) about the role. Think of a position or responsibility you currently hold or desire. Are you compromising any of your values for it? Write down one way you can practice scrupulousness (waraʿ) in that role starting today.

Life of Imam al-Mashat
beginner
5 min
The Author's Biography: From Birth to Legacy
Biography of the author: name, lineage, birth, education, career, and works.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the life, education, career, and scholarly contributions of Shaykh Hasan ibn Muhammad al-Mishshāt, the author of al-Bahja al-Saniyya.

Core Idea
A scholar's life reflects the breadth of Islamic learning and service.

This lesson introduces you to the author of the text you are about to study. Knowing his background helps you appreciate his authority and the context of his works. The passage covers his noble lineage, his rigorous education in Mecca, his long teaching career, his roles as a judge and legal advisor, and his prolific authorship.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the full Arabic passage and its English translation. Notice the structure: it begins with name and lineage, then birth and education, then career milestones, and finally a numbered list of works.
2
Identify key events
Focus on important dates: birth in 1317 AH, entry into al-Sawlatiyya in 1329, certificate of merit in 1336, and judicial appointments in 1361, 1365, 1372.
3
Study the list of works
Examine the nine authored works. Note the variety: hadith, sira, usul al-fiqh, hadith terminology, Hajj, Ramadan, and the present commentary.
These works show his specialization and breadth.
4
Reflect on his journey
Consider how his life – from student to teacher, judge, and author – prepared him to write this commentary. His experiences enriched his scholarly perspective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ترجمة المؤلف اسمه ونسبه هو العلامة المحدث، الأصولي، الفقيه، القاضي، أبو أحمد حسن بن محمد بن عباس بن علي بن عبد الواحد المشاط، المكي المالكي، المنافي، نسبة الى عبد مناف بن قصي القرشي. مولده ونشأته: ولد في الثالث من شهر شوال سنة ۱۳۱۷ من الهجرة النبوية، بحي القرارة، وأسرته من الأسر العلمية المكية العريقة التي أنجبت عددًا من العلماء. ألحقه والده للدراسة بالكتاتيب، ثم بالمدرسة الصولتية سنة ١٣٢٩ ، وجرى تعيينه مساعدًا للمدرسين، وهو قريب من سن التخرج، وبعد تخرجه عمل بنفس المدرسة مدرسًا، مدة ثلاثين سنة، ونال شهادة الجدارة منها عام ١٣٣٦، ودرس بالمسجد الحرام، ولازم علمائه، والتقى بعدد كبير من العلماء الواردين إلى البلد الحرام. فمن أساتذته: محمد الخضر بن مايابي الجكني وعمر حمدان المحرسي، ولازم المشايخ محمد حبيب الله الشنقيطي، ووالده الشيخ محمد المشاط. وفي عام ١٣٦١ عُيّن عضوًا في هيئة التمييز، وكان كعادة العلماء متورعًا عن هذه المسألة، حتى انحلت الهيئة عام ١٣٦٤ ، فسافر على إثر ذلك في رحلة مدتها عام تقريبًا إلى السودان ومصر وسوريه ولبنان، والتقى بعلمائها. في عام ١٣٦٥ عُيّن وكيلًا لرئيس المحكمة الشرعية، وظل بها مدة عامين حتى صار قاضيًا بها. وفي عام ١٣٧٢ عُيّن عضوا في مجلس الشورى، وما لبث أن أعيد إلى سلك القضاء معاونًا لرئيس المحكمة، واستمر فيها حتى قبلت استقالته في عام ١٣٧٥ ففرح كثيرًا. ولم ينقطع عن التدريس في المدرسة الصولتيه، ودرس المسجد الحرام، وقد تخرّج على يديه ولازمه علماء وأعيان كثيرون. من مؤلفاته: (١) الثبت الكبير ، ذكر فيه المؤلف شيوخه ومروياته. (٢) إنارة الدّجى في مغازي خير الورى، ذكر فيه مغازي وسرايا الرسول صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ ، وهو شرح لمنظومة الشيخ أحمد بن محمد البدوي الشنقيطي. (٣) الجواهر الثمينة في بيان أدلة عالم المدينة، في علم أصول الفقه. (٤) رفع الأستار عن محيا مخدرات طلعة الأنوار في علم آثار النبي المختار، وهو شرح المنظومة «طلعة الأنوار للسيد عبد الله بن إبراهيم العلوي، وهو مختصر من ألفية الحافظ العراقي في مصطلح الحديث. (٥) التقريرات السنية على المنظومة البيقونية، وهو شرح ماتع للمنظومة البيقونية في مصطلح الحديث. (٦) إسعاف أهل الإسلام بوظائف الحج إلى بيت الله الحرام، ذكر فيه المؤلف آيات وأحاديث مختارة متعلقة بمناسك الحج. (٧) إسعاف أهل الإيمان بوظائف شهر رمضان شرح فيه المؤلف مجموعة من الأحاديث الصحيحة الواردة في مسائل الصيام. (٨) البهجة السنية في شرح الخريدة البهية، وهو كتابنا هذا. (٩) أربعون حديثًا في الترغيب والترهيب، ختمها بحديث الحسنين في شمائل جدهما المحمدية
Translation of the author. His name and lineage: He is the eminent scholar of hadith, usul, fiqh, judge, Abu Ahmad Hasan ibn Muhammad ibn Abbas ibn Ali ibn Abd al-Wahid al-Mishshāt, al-Makki al-Maliki, al-Manafi, attribution to Abd Manaf ibn Qusayy al-Qurashi. His birth and upbringing: He was born on the third of Shawwal in the year 1317 of the Prophetic Hijra, in the al-Qarara district, and his family is among the ancient scholarly Meccan families that produced a number of scholars. His father enrolled him for study in the kuttabs, then in the al-Sawlatiyya school in 1329, and he was appointed as a teacher's assistant, and he was close to graduation age, and after his graduation he worked at the same school as a teacher for thirty years, and obtained a certificate of merit from it in 1336, and he taught at the Sacred Mosque, and studied with its scholars, and met many scholars visiting the Sacred Land. Among his teachers: Muhammad al-Khidr ibn Mayabi al-Jikini and Umar Hamdan al-Mahrasi, and he attended the lessons of the shaykhs Muhammad Habib Allah al-Shinqiti and his father Shaykh Muhammad al-Mishshāt. In 1361 he was appointed a member of the Commission of Distinction, and as was the custom of scholars he was wary of this matter, until the commission was dissolved in 1364, whereupon he traveled for about a year to Sudan, Egypt, Syria, and Lebanon, and met its scholars. In 1365 he was appointed deputy to the head of the Sharia Court, and remained there for two years until he became a judge there. In 1372 he was appointed a member of the Consultative Council, and soon he was returned to the judiciary as assistant to the head of the court, and he remained there until his resignation was accepted in 1375, and he was very happy. He did not cease teaching at the al-Sawlatiyya school and teaching at the Sacred Mosque, and many scholars and notables graduated under him and attended his lessons. Among his authored works: (1) al-Thabt al-Kabîr, in which he mentions his shaykhs and narrations. (2) Inārat al-Dujā fī Maghāzī Khayr al-Warā, in which he mentions the military expeditions and campaigns of the Messenger of God, peace be upon him, and it is a commentary on the poem of Shaykh Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Badawi al-Shinqiti. (3) al-Jawāhir al-Thamīna fī Bayān Adillat 'Ālim al-Madīna, in the science of the principles of fiqh. (4) Raf' al-Asār 'an Muhayyā Mukhaddarāt Tal'at al-Anwār fī 'Ilm Āthār al-Nabī al-Mukhtār, which is a commentary on the poem 'Tal'at al-Anwār' of Sayyid 'Abd Allah ibn Ibrahim al-'Alawī, and it is an abridgment of the Alfiyya of al-Hāfiz al-'Irāqī in the science of hadith terminology. (5) al-Taqrīrāt al-Saniyya 'alā al-Manẓūma al-Bayqūniyya, which is a valuable commentary on the Bayqūniyya poem in hadith terminology. (6) Is'āf Ahl al-Islām bi-Waẓā'if al-Hajj ilā Bayt Allāh al-Harām, in which he mentions selected Quranic verses and hadiths related to the rites of Hajj. (7) Is'āf Ahl al-Īmān bi-Waẓā'if Shahr Ramaḍān, in which he explains a collection of authentic hadiths on issues of fasting. (8) al-Bahja al-Saniyya fī Sharḥ al-Kharīda al-Bahiyya, which is this book of ours. (9) Arba'ūn Hadīthan fī al-Targhīb wa al-Tarhīb, concluding with the hadith of al-Hasanayn on the virtues of their grandfather Muhammad.
Source pages p. 8

Commentary
This passage is a biography of the author, Shaykh Hasan ibn Muhammad al-Mishshāt. It begins with his full name and lineage, tracing his ancestry back to Abd Manaf ibn Qusayy al-Qurashi, emphasizing his noble Meccan and scholarly heritage. It then describes his birth on 3 Shawwal 1317 AH in the al-Qarara neighborhood of Mecca. His family was a well-known scholarly family. His father first enrolled him in kuttabs (traditional Quranic schools) and then at al-Sawlatiyya School in 1329 AH. He progressed quickly, becoming a teacher's assistant before graduation, and after graduating he taught at the same school for thirty years, obtaining a certificate of merit in 1336 AH. He also studied at the Sacred Mosque, attending the circles of its scholars, and met many visiting scholars. The passage lists some of his most prominent teachers: Muhammad al-Khidr al-Jikini, Umar al-Mahrasi, and he closely studied with Shaykh Muhammad Habib Allah al-Shinqiti and his own father, Shaykh Muhammad al-Mishshāt. The biography then details his judicial career: in 1361 he was appointed to the Commission of Distinction, but he was hesitant about this role, and when the commission was dissolved in 1364, he took a year-long journey to Sudan, Egypt, Syria, and Lebanon, meeting scholars there. In 1365 he became deputy head of the Sharia Court, and after two years became a judge. In 1372 he joined the Consultative Council, but soon returned to the judiciary as assistant to the head of the court, until his resignation was accepted in 1375, which brought him great joy. Throughout, he continued teaching at al-Sawlatiyya School and the Sacred Mosque, and many scholars and notables studied under him. Finally, the passage enumerates his nine major authored works, covering hadith, sira, usul al-fiqh, hadith terminology, Hajj, Ramadan, and the present commentary on al-Kharida al-Bahiyya. This biography establishes the author's scholarly authority and the context for his contributions.

Key Terms
شهادة الجدارة
هيئة التمييز
المدرسة الصولتية
المسجد الحرام
الثبت الكبير

Key Takeaway
Shaykh Hasan al-Mishshāt was a distinguished scholar of Mecca whose life encompassed teaching, judging, and authoring numerous works, including this commentary.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Al-Mishshāt taught at the same school for thirty years without seeking fame or a more prestigious post. What is the one place or role in your life where you are called to be steadfast for decades, and how does that prospect challenge your desire for recognition?
?
He traveled to meet scholars in Sudan, Egypt, Syria, and Lebanon. Are you actively seeking knowledge from diverse sources and teachers, or have you become complacent with what is easy and familiar? What step will you take this week to seek a new source of Islamic learning?
?
Al-Mishshāt resigned from his judicial post and was 'very happy.' Do you cling to positions of authority for status or security? Would you be genuinely joyful if you were freed from a role you thought you needed? What does his contentment without office say about your own attachments?

Life of Imam al-Mashat
beginner

The Life of the Author: Career and Death
Biographical sketch of the author's career and death

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the major events in the author's life, including his judicial appointments, teaching roles, and his death.

Core Idea
A scholar's life of service and teaching.

This lesson provides a biographical sketch of the author of al-Bahja al-Saniyya, highlighting his distinguished career in the judiciary and his dedication to teaching at the Sacred Mosque and al-Madrasa al-Sawlatiyya. We will also learn about the circumstances of his death.

Guided Path
1
Read the biography
Carefully read the passage about the author's life from his appointment to his death.
2
Identify key roles
Note his positions: member of the Consultative Council, deputy chief judge, teacher.
He returned to teaching after resignation.
3
Consider the date
His death on 7 Shawwal 1399 AH marks the end of a scholarly life.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
البهجة السنية وفي عام ١٣٧٢ عُيّن عضوا في مجلس الشورى، وما لبث أن أعيد إلى سلك القضاء معاونًا لرئيس المحكمة، واستمر فيها حتى قبلت استقالته في عام ١٣٧٥ ففرح كثيرًا. ولم ينقطع عن التدريس في المدرسة الصولتيه، ودرس المسجد الحرام، وقد تخرّج على يديه ولازمه علماء وأعيان كثيرون.
من مؤلفاته:
(١) الثبت الكبير، ذكر فيه المؤلف شيوخه ومروياته.
(٢) إنارة الدّجى في مغازي خير الورى، ذكر فيه مغازي وسرايا الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم، وهو شرح لمنظومة الشيخ أحمد بن محمد البدوي الشنقيطي.
(٣) الجواهر الثمينة في بيان أدلة عالم المدينة، في علم أصول الفقه.
(٤) رفع الأستار عن محيا مخدرات طلعة الأنوار في علم آثار النبي المختار، وهو شرح لمنظومة «طلعة الأنوار» للسيد عبد الله بن إبراهيم العلوي، وهو مختصر من ألفية الحافظ العراقي في مصطلح الحديث.
(٥) التقريرات السنية على المنظومة البيقونية، وهو شرح ماتع للمنظومة البيقونية في مصطلح الحديث.
(٦) إسعاف أهل الإسلام بوظائف الحج إلى بيت الله الحرام، ذكر فيه المؤلف آيات وأحاديث مختارة متعلقة بمناسك الحج.
(٧) إسعاف أهل الإيمان بوظائف شهر رمضان شرح فيه المؤلف مجموعة من الأحاديث الصحيحة الواردة في مسائل الصيام.
(٨) البهجة السنية في شرح الخريدة البهية، وهو كتابنا هذا.
(٩) أربعون حديثًا في الترغيب والترهيب، ختمها بحديث الحسنين في شمائل جدهما المحمدية.
(١٠) الحدود البهية في الحدود المنطقية، رسالة عبارة عن أسئلة وأجوبة في علم المنطق، طبعت بعناية تلميذه الدكتور عبد الوهاب أبو سليمان المالكي، وغيرها.
وفاته
توفي الشيخ رحمه الله تعالى في يوم الأربعاء السابع من شهر شوال سنة ۱۳۹۹.
وقد أكرمني الله تعالى بالرواية عن عدة من تلاميذه أجلهم عندي، وأقربهم إلى قلبي سيدي وأستاذي العلامة المحدث الشيخ محمد عوامة حفظه الله تعالى وأدامه.
(۱) ترجم للمؤلف ترجمة مُوسَّعَة تلميذه فضيلة الأستاذ الدكتور عبد الوهاب أبو سليمان، في مقدمة تحقيقه لكتاب «الجواهر الثمينة» (ص ١٧: ٧٢) ومنها اقتبست هذه الترجمة المختصرة.
Al-Bahja al-Saniyya: In the year 1372 he was appointed a member of the Consultative Council, and soon after he was returned to the judiciary as deputy chief judge, and he remained in it until his resignation was accepted in the year 1375, and he was very happy. He did not cease teaching at al-Madrasa al-Sawlatiyya, and taught at the Sacred Mosque, and many scholars and notables graduated under him and accompanied him. Among his works: (1) Al-Thabt al-Kabir, in which the author mentioned his teachers and his narrations. (2) Inarat al-Duja fi Maghazi Khayr al-Wara, in which he mentioned the military expeditions and campaigns of the Messenger of God, peace be upon him, and it is a commentary on the poem of Sheikh Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Badawi al-Shinqiti. (3) Al-Jawahir al-Thamina fi Bayan Adillat 'Alim al-Madina, in the science of Usul al-Fiqh. (4) Raf' al-Astar 'an Muhayya Mukhadarat Tul'at al-Anwar fi 'Ilm Athar al-Nabi al-Mukhtar, which is a commentary on the poem 'Tul'at al-Anwar' of al-Sayyid 'Abdullah ibn Ibrahim al-'Alawi, and it is an abridgment of Alfiya of al-Hafiz al-'Iraqi in the terminology of hadith. (5) Al-Taqrirat al-Saniyya 'ala al-Manzuma al-Bayquniyya, which is a delightful commentary on al-Manzuma al-Bayquniyya in the terminology of hadith. (6) Is'af Ahl al-Islam bi Waza'if al-Hajj ila Bayt Allah al-Haram, in which the author mentioned selected verses and hadiths related to the rituals of Hajj. (7) Is'af Ahl al-Iman bi Waza'if Shahr Ramadan, in which the author explained a collection of authentic hadiths on matters of fasting. (8) Al-Bahjat al-Saniyya fi Sharh al-Kharidat al-Bahiyya, which is this book of ours. (9) Arba'un Hadithan fi al-Targhib wa al-Tarhib, concluding with the hadith of al-Hasanayn on the noble characteristics of their grandfather Muhammad. (10) Al-Hudud al-Bahiyya fi al-Hudud al-Mantiqiyya, a treatise consisting of questions and answers in logic, printed by the care of his student Dr. 'Abd al-Wahhab Abu Sulayman al-Maliki, and others. His death: The Sheikh, may God have mercy on him, died on Wednesday, the seventh of Shawwal, year 1399. And God Almighty has honored me with narration (riwaya) from several of his students, the most esteemed of them to me and the closest to my heart, my master and teacher, the scholar and muhaddith Sheikh Muhammad 'Awwama, may God preserve him and prolong him. (1) His student, His Excellency Professor Dr. 'Abd al-Wahhab Abu Sulayman, provided an extensive biography of the author in the introduction to his critical edition of the book 'Al-Jawahir al-Thamina' (pp. 17-72), and from it I have abridged this biography.
Source pages p. 9

Commentary
This passage is a biographical sketch of the author of this commentary (al-mu'allif), likely the commentator Mashat. It opens with his appointment to the Consultative Council in 1372 AH and his subsequent return to the judiciary as deputy chief judge, a post he held until his resignation in 1375 AH. It emphasizes his continued teaching at al-Madrasa al-Sawlatiyya and the Sacred Mosque, and notes that many scholars and notables studied under him. The passage then lists ten of his works, covering a range of Islamic sciences: hadith (collections and terminologies), fiqh (usul, Hajj, Ramadan), siyar, logic, and the current book itself (number 8). The list is followed by his death date: Wednesday, 7 Shawwal 1399 AH. Finally, the author (the one writing this biography) mentions that he has received riwaya (narrative license) from several of the deceased scholar's students, particularly Shaykh Muhammad 'Awwama, and acknowledges that the biography is abridged from a more extensive one by Dr. 'Abd al-Wahhab Abu Sulayman in his edition of Al-Jawahir al-Thamina.

Key Terms
الثبت الكبير
إنارة الدجى
الخريدة البهية
البهجة السنية
الرواية
المحدث

D) 1370 AH

Key Takeaway
The author was a respected judge and teacher who died in 1399 AH.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The author found great joy in leaving his high-ranking judicial position. Is there a position or status you are holding onto that, if you let go for Allah, would bring you deeper contentment? What would it take to release it?
?
The author dedicated his life to teaching at the Sacred Mosque and al-Madrasa al-Sawlatiyya. What spiritual legacy are you building right now? If your life ended next year, what would you leave behind that truly matters for the Hereafter?
?
His death came after a life of service. Are you living each day as if your appointment with death is certain and possibly near? What is one thing you will change today to align your actions with that reality?

Life of Imam al-Mashat
beginner
6 min
The Author's Writings and Scholarly Legacy
Enumeration of the author's ten works and mention of riwaya.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will become familiar with the ten works authored by the scholar, covering various Islamic disciplines, and learn about the chain of transmission (riwaya) from his students.

Core Idea
A prolific scholar with works on hadith, fiqh, siyar, and logic.

This lesson presents the impressive list of works written by the author, ranging from hadith collections and commentaries to works on Hajj and Ramadan. It also highlights the author's connection to later scholars through riwaya, emphasizing the continuity of Islamic learning.

Guided Path
1
Read the list of works
Go through the ten titles and note their subjects.
They cover hadith, usul al-fiqh, siyar, logic, and more.
2
Notice the variety
See how the author contributed to multiple fields.
Work 8 is this very commentary.
3
Reflect on the riwaya
Read the final note about the author receiving riwaya from students of the deceased scholar.
This shows the living chain of transmission.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
البهجة السنية وفي عام ١٣٧٢ عُيّن عضوا في مجلس الشورى، وما لبث أن أعيد إلى سلك القضاء معاونًا لرئيس المحكمة، واستمر فيها حتى قبلت استقالته في عام ١٣٧٥ ففرح كثيرًا. ولم ينقطع عن التدريس في المدرسة الصولتيه، ودرس المسجد الحرام، وقد تخرّج على يديه ولازمه علماء وأعيان كثيرون.
من مؤلفاته:
(١) الثبت الكبير، ذكر فيه المؤلف شيوخه ومروياته.
(٢) إنارة الدّجى في مغازي خير الورى، ذكر فيه مغازي وسرايا الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم، وهو شرح لمنظومة الشيخ أحمد بن محمد البدوي الشنقيطي.
(٣) الجواهر الثمينة في بيان أدلة عالم المدينة، في علم أصول الفقه.
(٤) رفع الأستار عن محيا مخدرات طلعة الأنوار في علم آثار النبي المختار، وهو شرح لمنظومة «طلعة الأنوار» للسيد عبد الله بن إبراهيم العلوي، وهو مختصر من ألفية الحافظ العراقي في مصطلح الحديث.
(٥) التقريرات السنية على المنظومة البيقونية، وهو شرح ماتع للمنظومة البيقونية في مصطلح الحديث.
(٦) إسعاف أهل الإسلام بوظائف الحج إلى بيت الله الحرام، ذكر فيه المؤلف آيات وأحاديث مختارة متعلقة بمناسك الحج.
(٧) إسعاف أهل الإيمان بوظائف شهر رمضان شرح فيه المؤلف مجموعة من الأحاديث الصحيحة الواردة في مسائل الصيام.
(٨) البهجة السنية في شرح الخريدة البهية، وهو كتابنا هذا.
(٩) أربعون حديثًا في الترغيب والترهيب، ختمها بحديث الحسنين في شمائل جدهما المحمدية.
(١٠) الحدود البهية في الحدود المنطقية، رسالة عبارة عن أسئلة وأجوبة في علم المنطق، طبعت بعناية تلميذه الدكتور عبد الوهاب أبو سليمان المالكي، وغيرها.
وفاته
توفي الشيخ رحمه الله تعالى في يوم الأربعاء السابع من شهر شوال سنة ۱۳۹۹.
وقد أكرمني الله تعالى بالرواية عن عدة من تلاميذه أجلهم عندي، وأقربهم إلى قلبي سيدي وأستاذي العلامة المحدث الشيخ محمد عوامة حفظه الله تعالى وأدامه.
(۱) ترجم للمؤلف ترجمة مُوسَّعَة تلميذه فضيلة الأستاذ الدكتور عبد الوهاب أبو سليمان، في مقدمة تحقيقه لكتاب «الجواهر الثمينة» (ص ١٧: ٧٢) ومنها اقتبست هذه الترجمة المختصرة.
Al-Bahja al-Saniyya: In the year 1372 he was appointed a member of the Consultative Council, and soon after he was returned to the judiciary as deputy chief judge, and he remained in it until his resignation was accepted in the year 1375, and he was very happy. He did not cease teaching at al-Madrasa al-Sawlatiyya, and taught at the Sacred Mosque, and many scholars and notables graduated under him and accompanied him. Among his works: (1) Al-Thabt al-Kabir, in which the author mentioned his teachers and his narrations. (2) Inarat al-Duja fi Maghazi Khayr al-Wara, in which he mentioned the military expeditions and campaigns of the Messenger of God, peace be upon him, and it is a commentary on the poem of Sheikh Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Badawi al-Shinqiti. (3) Al-Jawahir al-Thamina fi Bayan Adillat 'Alim al-Madina, in the science of Usul al-Fiqh. (4) Raf' al-Astar 'an Muhayya Mukhadarat Tul'at al-Anwar fi 'Ilm Athar al-Nabi al-Mukhtar, which is a commentary on the poem 'Tul'at al-Anwar' of al-Sayyid 'Abdullah ibn Ibrahim al-'Alawi, and it is an abridgment of Alfiya of al-Hafiz al-'Iraqi in the terminology of hadith. (5) Al-Taqrirat al-Saniyya 'ala al-Manzuma al-Bayquniyya, which is a delightful commentary on al-Manzuma al-Bayquniyya in the terminology of hadith. (6) Is'af Ahl al-Islam bi Waza'if al-Hajj ila Bayt Allah al-Haram, in which the author mentioned selected verses and hadiths related to the rituals of Hajj. (7) Is'af Ahl al-Iman bi Waza'if Shahr Ramadan, in which the author explained a collection of authentic hadiths on matters of fasting. (8) Al-Bahjat al-Saniyya fi Sharh al-Kharidat al-Bahiyya, which is this book of ours. (9) Arba'un Hadithan fi al-Targhib wa al-Tarhib, concluding with the hadith of al-Hasanayn on the noble characteristics of their grandfather Muhammad. (10) Al-Hudud al-Bahiyya fi al-Hudud al-Mantiqiyya, a treatise consisting of questions and answers in logic, printed by the care of his student Dr. 'Abd al-Wahhab Abu Sulayman al-Maliki, and others. His death: The Sheikh, may God have mercy on him, died on Wednesday, the seventh of Shawwal, year 1399. And God Almighty has honored me with narration (riwaya) from several of his students, the most esteemed of them to me and the closest to my heart, my master and teacher, the scholar and muhaddith Sheikh Muhammad 'Awwama, may God preserve him and prolong him. (1) His student, His Excellency Professor Dr. 'Abd al-Wahhab Abu Sulayman, provided an extensive biography of the author in the introduction to his critical edition of the book 'Al-Jawahir al-Thamina' (pp. 17-72), and from it I have abridged this biography.
Source pages p. 9

Commentary
This passage is a biographical sketch of the author of this commentary (al-mu'allif), likely the commentator Mashat. It opens with his appointment to the Consultative Council in 1372 AH and his subsequent return to the judiciary as deputy chief judge, a post he held until his resignation in 1375 AH. It emphasizes his continued teaching at al-Madrasa al-Sawlatiyya and the Sacred Mosque, and notes that many scholars and notables studied under him. The passage then lists ten of his works, covering a range of Islamic sciences: hadith (collections and terminologies), fiqh (usul, Hajj, Ramadan), siyar, logic, and the current book itself (number 8). The list is followed by his death date: Wednesday, 7 Shawwal 1399 AH. Finally, the author (the one writing this biography) mentions that he has received riwaya (narrative license) from several of the deceased scholar's students, particularly Shaykh Muhammad 'Awwama, and acknowledges that the biography is abridged from a more extensive one by Dr. 'Abd al-Wahhab Abu Sulayman in his edition of Al-Jawahir al-Thamina.

?

If the answer is zero, what concrete step will you take this week to begin producing something that will outlive you?
?
The author not only authored books but also received riwaya (permission) from scholars, connecting him to the chain of transmission. Do you have any connection to a living scholar through whom you can trace your knowledge back to the Prophet? If not, why haven’t you sought one? Will you let this lesson pass without taking action to find a teacher?

Life of Imam al-Mashat
beginner
4 min
The Author's Passing and Scholarly Legacy
Biographical note on author's death and student works.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the biographical details of Sheikh Mashat's death and the scholarly connections that preserved his works.

Core Idea
A scholar's legacy lives on through his students and their works.

This brief passage provides a personal glimpse into the life and death of Sheikh Mashat, the author of the Kharidah. It identifies one of his treatises, records the precise date of his passing, and highlights the chain of transmission through his students, particularly the notable scholar Muhammad 'Awwamah. Understanding this context deepens our appreciation of the text's journey through generations.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic passage and its literal English translation. Notice the structure: it first mentions a work, then the death date, then the transmitter's connection.
The footnote is part of the passage and provides source for the biography.
2
Identify key information
Focus on the date of death (7th Shawwal 1399 AH) and the name of the scholar who preserved his works (Dr. Abd al-Wahhab Abu Sulayman). Also note the mention of Sheikh Muhammad 'Awwamah as a transmitter.
3
Reflect on the significance
Consider why such biographical details are included in a kalam textbook. They establish the chain of transmission (isnad) and build trust in the text's authenticity.
This is a common feature in classical Islamic scholarly works.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الحدود البهية في الحدود المنطقية، رسالة عبارة عن أسئلة وأجوبة في علم المنطق، طبعت بعناية تلميذه الدكتور عبد الوهاب أبو سليمان المالكي، وغيرها.
وفاته
توفي الشيخ رحمه الله تعالى في يوم الأربعاء السابع من شهر شوال سنة ۱۳۹۹ (۱).
وقد أكرمني الله تعالى بالرواية عن عدة من تلاميذه أجلهم عندي، وأقربهم إلى قلبي سيدي وأستاذي العلامة المحدث الشيخ محمد عوامة حفظه الله تعالى وأدامه.
(۱) ترجم للمؤلف ترجمة مُوسَّعَة تلميذه فضيلة الأستاذ الدكتور عبد الوهاب أبو سليمان، في مقدمة تحقيقه لكتاب «الجواهر الثمينة» (ص ۱۷: ۷۲ ومنها اقتبست هذه الترجمة المختصرة.
Al-Hudud al-Bahiyyah fi al-Hudud al-Mantiqiyyah, a treatise consisting of questions and answers in logic, printed under the supervision of his student Dr. Abd al-Wahhab Abu Sulayman al-Maliki and others. His death: The Sheikh, may Allah have mercy on him, passed away on Wednesday, the seventh of Shawwal, in the year 1399 (1). And Allah has honored me with transmission (riwaya) from several of his students; the most distinguished among them and closest to my heart is my master and teacher, the scholar and muhaddith Sheikh Muhammad 'Awwamah, may Allah preserve him and prolong his benefit. (1) His student, His Excellency Professor Dr. Abd al-Wahhab Abu Sulayman, wrote an extensive biography of the author in the introduction to his critical edition of the book "al-Jawahir al-Thaminah" (pp. 17-72), from which this brief biography is excerpted.
Source pages p. 10

Commentary
This passage is a biographical note about the author's death and his scholarly connections. It begins by mentioning a work by the author titled 'Al-Hudud al-Bahiyyah fi al-Hudud al-Mantiqiyyah', a logic treatise. Then it states the date of his death: Wednesday, 7th Shawwal 1399 AH. Next, the writer (probably the editor or a student) mentions that he has received transmission (riwaya) from several of the author's students, particularly praising Sheikh Muhammad 'Awwamah. Finally, a footnote indicates that a more extensive biography was written by the author's student Dr. Abd al-Wahhab Abu Sulayman in his edition of 'al-Jawahir al-Thaminah'. This note serves to provide context about the author's life and the chain of transmission of his works.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you died today, what specific book, teaching, or influence would you leave behind that could be transmitted by your students or peers? Are you actively building that legacy, or are you assuming it will happen by itself?
?
The narrator says of his teacher: 'the most distinguished among them and closest to my heart.' Which of your teachers holds that place in your heart, and what concrete steps have you taken to truly learn from them and honor their transmission?
?
The author's works were preserved by a student who edited and published them. Is there a teacher or scholar from whom you have received knowledge that you have not yet put into a form that can benefit others? Commit yourself to writing down, sharing, or recording what you have learned.

Life of Imam al-Mashat
beginner
4 min
Biography of Ahmad al-Dardir and Explanation of the Poem's Opening
Al-Mashat introduces the poet and explains the first line of the poem, then gives a biography.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the significance of al-Dardir's comprehensive scholarship and his role as a teacher, as well as the meaning of the opening line of his poem.

Core Idea
Ahmad al-Dardir combined both rational and transmitted sciences, embodying the ideal Sunni scholar.

Before diving into the theological content of al-Kharidat, al-Mashat introduces the poet himself, Ahmad al-Dardir. This lesson unpacks the opening line of the poem, where al-Dardir expresses his hope for God's mercy, and then details his lineage, education, and teachers. Understanding the author's background helps us appreciate the depth of the work.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage carefully, then the English translation. Note the poet's invocation and the commentator's explanation.
Pay attention to how the commentator breaks down the first line.
2
Examine al-Dardir's full name and lineage
The passage provides the poet's full name: Abu al-Barakat Sayyidi Ahmad ibn Sayyidi Muhammad al-Dardir al-ʿAdawi. Notice the nisbah and what it signifies.
Al-ʿAdawi refers to Banu ʿAdi, a Qurayshi tribe.
3
Reflect on his scholarly description
The commentator describes al-Dardir as 'the imam who combines the intellectual and transmitted sciences.' Consider why this combination is important in Islamic theology.
This reflects the ideal of a comprehensive scholar.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
هو ترجمة الناظم : يقول راجي رحمة القدير أي: الله سبحانه وتعالى أي: أحمد اسم المؤلف، فهو الإمام الجامع بين المعقول والمنقول والفروع والأصول، مربي المريدين، وناشر ألوية العلم والمعرفة على المستفيدين، أبو البركات سيدي أحمد ابن سيدي محمد الدردير، العدوي، بفتح العين نسبة إلى بني عدي قرية عظيمة من قرى الصعيد، من بني عدي القبيلة المشهورة من قريش. قال سيدي محمد السباعي، وهو أحد تلامذة الشيخ رحمه الله تعالى: «ولد الناظم سنة سبع وعشرين بعد المئة والألف، وقد تربّى تربية حسنة، ولما أكمل قراءة القرآن عن ظهر قلب شرع في طلب العلوم حتى حَقَّقَ الفنون، واقتبس من أنوارها، وتضلّع من أنهارها، أخذ عن أئمة أعلام، لهم في العلم أعلى مقام، منهم العالم المُتَفَنّن سيدي علي الصعيدي، العدوي، والشيخ الكبير سيدي عمر الطحلاوي.
This is the biography of the poet: He says, 'I hope for the mercy of the Almighty' meaning God, glorified and exalted be He; 'I, Ahmad' is the name of the author. So he is the imam who combines the intellectual and transmitted sciences, the subsidiary and fundamental matters, the educator of seekers, the one who spreads the banners of knowledge and wisdom among those who benefit. He is Abu al-Barakat Sayyidi Ahmad ibn Sayyidi Muhammad al-Dardir, al-ʿAdawi – with fatha on the ʿayn – attributed to Banu ʿAdi, a great village in Upper Egypt, from Banu ʿAdi, the famous tribe of Quraysh. Sayyidi Muhammad al-Sabai, who is one of the disciples of the shaykh, may God have mercy on him, said: 'The poet was born in the year 1127 AH. He was raised well. When he completed reading the Quran by heart, he began seeking knowledge until he mastered the arts, drew from their lights, and imbibed from their rivers. He studied under leading imams of high rank in knowledge, among them the versatile scholar Sayyidi ʿAli al-Saʿidi al-ʿAdawi and the great shaykh Sayyidi ʿUmar al-Tahlawi.'
Source pages p. 19

Commentary
This passage is a combination of an explanation of the first line of al-Dardir's poem and a biographical notice (tarjamah) of the poet. The commentator begins with the heading 'This is the biography of the poet' and then unpacks the line 'I hope for the mercy of the Almighty' (yaqulu raji rahmat al-qadir) word by word: 'ragi' means 'I hope', 'rahmat al-qadir' means 'the mercy of the Almighty, that is God'. Then he identifies 'Ahmad' as the author's name. He then gives a comprehensive description of al-Dardir's scholarly stature: he combined the rational and transmitted sciences, the subsidiary and fundamental matters, he was a spiritual educator and a disseminator of knowledge. His full name and lineage are given: Abu al-Barakat Sayyidi Ahmad ibn Sayyidi Muhammad al-Dardir al-ʿAdawi, with an explanation of the nisbah 'al-ʿAdawi' referring to Banu ʿAdi, a village in Upper Egypt, and that the tribe is part of Quraysh. Then a quote from al-Sabai, one of al-Dardir's students, provides the birth year (1127 AH) and details of his education: after memorizing the Quran, he pursued knowledge until he mastered various disciplines, studying under prominent scholars such as ʿAli al-Saʿidi and ʿUmar al-Tahlawi.

Key Terms
ترجمة الناظم
أبو البركات
الدردير
العدوي
المعقول والمنقول
جامع بين المعقول والمنقول

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: A biography is just a list of names and dates.
Correction: The biography emphasizes al-Dardir's comprehensive scholarship, combining both rational and transmitted sciences, which is key to understanding his work.

Checkpoint
What is the significance of the nisbah 'al-ʿAdawi' for Ahmad al-Dardir?
A) It indicates he was from the tribe of ʿAd, an ancient Arab tribe.
B) It refers to the village of Banu ʿAdi in Upper Egypt, and the tribe is from Quraysh.
C) It means he was a follower of the school of ʿAdi ibn Hatim.
D) It is a misspelling of 'al-ʿUdawi' meaning 'one who sings'.

Key Takeaway
Ahmad al-Dardir was a comprehensive scholar who combined rational and transmitted sciences, born in 1127 AH, and studied under prominent teachers like ʿAli al-Saʿidi and ʿUmar al-Tahlawi.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just read about al-Dardir's pursuit of both rational and transmitted sciences. Which of these two areas have you been neglecting in your own learning? What specific step will you take this week to address that gap?
?
Al-Dardir studied under recognized masters. Who are the reliable scholars you are currently learning from? If you are learning alone, how will you ensure you are not misinterpreting the religion?
?
The poem's opening phrase: 'I hope for the mercy of the Almighty.' How much is the hope of Allah's mercy a driving force in your daily actions, not just in your words? What would change in your behaviour if that hope were truly alive in your heart?

Life of Imam al-Mashat
beginner
4 min
Introducing the Author: Al-Dardir and His Opening Words
Commentator explains the opening line and introduces the author al-Dardir.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the identity and scholarly stature of Imam al-Dardir as introduced in the opening line of his poem.

Core Idea
The opening line reveals the author's humility and his comprehensive knowledge.

The commentator begins by explaining the poet's self-introduction. The line 'He says, hoping for the mercy of the Almighty, i.e. Ahmad, known as al-Dardir' is not a separate statement but the poet identifying himself. This lesson uncovers who al-Dardir was and why his work is significant.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic line and translation
Start by reading the opening verse and its translation to grasp the literal meaning.
2
Understand 'hoping for the mercy of the Almighty'
Reflect on why the author begins with this phrase—it shows his humility and reliance on Allah.
3
Identify the author's full name and lineage
The commentator gives the author's full name: Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Dardir al-'Adawi. Note the nisba 'al-'Adawi' and its origin.
4
Recognize the description 'combining rational and transmitted sciences'
The author is described as an imam who masters both 'ma'qul' (rational) and 'manqul' (transmitted) sciences, as well as principles and branches.
5
Appreciate the tribal affiliation to Quraysh
The lineage traces back to Bani 'Adi of Quraysh, indicating noble descent.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
يَقُولُ رَاجِي رَحْمَةَ القَدِيرِ * أَي: أَحْمَدُ الْمَشْهُورُ بالدَّرْدِيرِ
ترجمة الناظم | يقول راجي رحمة القدير) أي: اللَّه سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَى (أي: أحمد) اسم المؤلف، فهو الإمام الجامع بين المعقول والمنقول والفروع والأصول، مربي المريدين، وناشر ألوية العلم والمعرفة على المستفيدين، أبو البركات سيدي أحمد ابن سيدي محمد الدردير، العدوي، بفتح العين نسبة إلى بني عدي قرية عظيمة من قرى الصعيد، من بني عدي القبيلة المشهورة من قريش.
He says, hoping for the mercy of the Almighty, i.e. Ahmad, known as al-Dardir. Translation of the poet: He says, hoping for the mercy of the Almighty, i.e. Allah glorified and exalted, (i.e. Ahmad) is the name of the author. He is the Imam combining the rational and transmitted (sciences), the branches and the foundations, the nurturer of seekers, and the spreader of the banners of knowledge and gnosis to the beneficiaries. Abu al-Barakat, my master Ahmad ibn my master Muhammad al-Dardir, al-‘Adawi, with the fatha on the ‘ayn, in relation to Bani ‘Adi, a great village in Upper Egypt, from Bani ‘Adi, the well-known tribe of Quraysh.
Source pages p. 19

Commentary
This passage is both a poetic line and its commentary. The commentator begins by quoting the opening verse of the poem: 'He says, hoping for the mercy of the Almighty, i.e. Ahmad, known as al-Dardir.' The verse is a self-introduction by the author. The commentator then explains each part: 'hoping for the mercy of the Almighty' means seeking Allah's mercy; 'Ahmad' is the author's first name, and 'al-Dardir' is his family name. He then provides a comprehensive description of the author as an imam who combines both rational and transmitted sciences (ma‘qūl and manqūl), as well as the detailed branches (furū‘) and fundamental principles (uṣūl). He also describes him as a spiritual guide (murabbī) for seekers and a disseminator of knowledge. Finally, he gives the author's full name and lineage: Abu al-Barakat Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Dardir al-‘Adawi, explaining that the nisba 'al-‘Adawi' comes from the tribe of Bani ‘Adi, a subdivision of Quraysh, indicating their noble origin.

Key Terms
القدير
الجامع بين المعقول والمنقول
العدوي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The opening line 'He says, hoping for the mercy of the Almighty' is a separate comment by someone else.
Correction: The line is the poet's own self-identification; the commentator explains it as part of the poem.

Checkpoint
What is the meaning of 'al-‘Adawi' in the author's nisba?
A) A village in Upper Egypt
B) A tribe of Quraysh
C) A title meaning 'the just'
D) A school of jurisprudence

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Dardir was a renowned scholar who combined both rational and transmitted sciences, and his poem begins with a humble invocation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Al-Dardir begins by calling himself 'hoping for the mercy of the Almighty'. Do you truly live with that hope, or are you relying on your own knowledge and deeds? What would change if you genuinely saw yourself as always in need of Allah's mercy?
?
The commentator says al-Dardir combined both rational and transmitted sciences. In what area of your religious knowledge are you neglecting one side? How can you take a concrete step this week to balance your learning?
?
Al-Dardir is described as a spiritual guide (murabbi) for seekers. Who are you currently teaching or influencing? Are you actively sharing knowledge, or are you keeping it to yourself? What one thing can you do to become a better guide for those around you?
