Life of Imam al-Mashat
beginner
8 min
Biography of Ahmad al-Dardir: Birth, Education, and Teachers
Biography of al-Dardir from a student's account, including teachers, spiritual legacy, and a teacher's profile.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the key biographical details of Ahmad al-Dardir's early life, education, teachers, and spiritual legacy.

Core Idea
Al-Dardir's biography shows how traditional Islamic learning combines external scholarship with spiritual inheritance.

This lesson presents a student's account of Imam Ahmad al-Dardir's life. It covers his birth, upbringing, pursuit of knowledge, and the eminent scholars he studied under. The passage also reveals a spiritual dimension through the 'great Muhammadan inheritance' and a prophetic dream, illustrating the holistic nature of traditional Islamic education.

Guided Path
1
Read the student's account of al-Dardir's birth and early education
Start with the opening statement from Sayyidi Muhammad al-Siba'i, which mentions al-Dardir's birth year, good upbringing, and completion of Quran memorization.
Notice that the biography is transmitted through a student.
2
Identify the teachers mentioned and their chains
List the teachers named: 'Ali al-Sa'idi, 'Umar al-Tahlawi, Shams al-Din al-Hifnawi, and others. Note the chains of transmission provided.
The chains connect al-Dardir to earlier authorities.
3
Understand the 'great Muhammadan inheritance'
Focus on the phrase 'al-mirath al-akbar al-muhammadi' received from Imam al-Hifnawi. This refers to spiritual knowledge and blessings from the Prophet.
This is a key concept in Sufi tradition.
4
Reflect on the prophetic dream and general ijazas
Consider the dream where the Prophet gives glad tidings, and the fact that al-Dardir received general ijazas from multiple teachers.
The dream affirms his spiritual rank.
5
Examine the biographical details of Shaykh 'Umar al-Tahlawi
Read the final paragraph from 'Aja'ib al-Athar, which gives additional information about one of al-Dardir's teachers, including his lineage, study, teaching posts, and character.
This exemplifies the detail in biographical literature.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال سيدي محمد السباعي، وهو أحد تلامذة الشيخ رحمه الله تعالى: «ولد الناظم سنة سبع وعشرين بعد المئة والألف، وقد تربّى تربية حسنة، ولما أكمل قراءة القرآن عن ظهر قلب شرع في طلب العلوم حتى حَقَّقَ الفنون، واقتبس من أنوارها، وتضلّع من أنهارها، أخذ عن أئمة أعلام، لهم في العلم أعلى مقام، منهم العالم المُتَفَنّن سيدي علي الصعيدي، العدوي، والشيخ الكبير سيدي عمر الطحلاوي.
والأول عن سيدي محمد الصغير، عن سيدي عبد الباقي الزرقاني عن سيدي على الأجهوري.
والثاني عن الشهاب أحمد النفراوي.
وأخذ أيضًا عن الإمام العارف شمس الدين محمد بن سالم الحفناوي الشافعي، ونفع به، وعنه تلقى الميراث الأكبر المحمدي، وقد رأى بعض الصالحين رسول الله صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ فَبَشِّره بأن الإمام الدردير أُعطِي ما لا عين رأت ولا أُذُنٌ سمعت، كيف لا وقد جمع علم الشريعة المطهرة عن الشمس الحفناوي المذكور، وعن الشيخ الصعيدي، وعن الشهاب أحمد الملوي، وعن الشمس محمد بن محمد الدفري، وكلّ منهم أجازه إجازة عامة، وأخذ عن آخرين أيضًا وأجازوه.
أما الأول فعن الشمس محمد ابن الميّت، عن مشايخه الذين أثبتهم في «ثبته»، منهم: النور الشَّبْرَامَلَّسي، والبرهان الكوراني، وغيرهم.
لكن في ترجمته من عجائب الآثار أنه عمر، وهو ابن علي بن يحيى ابن مصطفى الطحلاوي المالكي الأزهري، تفقه على الشيخ سالم النفراوي، وحضر دروس الشيخ أحمد الملوي والشبراوي، والبليدي، وسمع الحديث على الشهاب أحمد البابلي، وتمهر في الفنون، ودَرَّس بالجامع الأزهر، وبالمشهد الحسيني، كان للناس فيه اعتقاد حسن، وعليه هيبة، ووقار، وسكون، ولكلامه وقع في القلوب، توفي سنة ١١٨١.
Sayyidi Muhammad al-Siba‘i, who is one of the students of the Shaykh—may Allah have mercy on him—said: 'The poet was born in the year 1127 AH. He was raised well, and when he completed the recitation of the Quran by heart, he began seeking knowledge until he mastered the disciplines, borrowed from their lights, and saturated himself from their rivers. He took from eminent imams, who have the highest station in knowledge, among them the versatile scholar Sayyidi ‘Ali al-Sa‘idi al-‘Adawi, and the great shaykh Sayyidi ‘Umar al-Tahlawi. The first (chain) is from Sayyidi Muhammad al-Saghir, from Sayyidi ‘Abd al-Baqi al-Zurqani, from Sayyidi ‘Ali al-Ajhuri. The second (chain) is from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Nafrawi. He also took from the arif Imam Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Salim al-Hifnawi al-Shafi‘i, and benefited from him, and from him he received the great Muhammadan inheritance. Some righteous people saw the Messenger of Allah—Allah bless him and grant him peace—and he gave him the good news that Imam al-Dardir had been given what no eye has seen nor ear has heard. How could it not be, when he had compiled the pure Sharia science from the mentioned Shams al-Hifnawi, and from al-Shaykh al-Sa‘idi, and from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Malawi, and from Shams Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Dafri, and each of them gave him a general license (ijāza ‘āmma). He also took from others and they licensed him. As for the first (chain), it is from Shams Muhammad ibn al-Mayyit, from his shaykhs whom he recorded in his Thabt, among them al-Nur al-Shabramallisi, and al-Burhan al-Kurani, and others. However, in his translation from ‘Aja'ib al-Athar that he is ‘Umar, and he is the son of ‘Ali ibn Yahya ibn Mustafa al-Tahlawi al-Maliki al-Azhari. He studied jurisprudence under al-Shaykh Salim al-Nafrawi, attended the lessons of al-Shaykh Ahmad al-Malawi, al-Shabrawi, and al-Bilidi, and heard hadith from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Babili, and became proficient in the disciplines. He taught at al-Azhar Mosque and at the Mashhad al-Husayni. People had good belief in him, and he had awe, dignity, and calmness, and his speech had an impact on hearts. He died in the year 1181.'
Source pages p. 19

Commentary
This passage is a biographical narrative about the author Ahmad al-Dardir. It begins by quoting one of his students, Muhammad al-Siba‘i, who provides key details: al-Dardir was born in 1127 AH, received a good upbringing, memorized the Quran, and then pursued knowledge until he mastered various fields. He studied under renowned scholars, specifically naming ‘Ali al-Sa‘idi and ‘Umar al-Tahlawi, then provides two chains of transmission from these teachers to earlier authorities. The commentator adds that al-Dardir also studied under Shams al-Din al-Hifnawi, through whom he received the 'great Muhammadan inheritance,' an esoteric spiritual legacy. A dream of some righteous person is mentioned, in which the Prophet ﷺ gave glad tidings that al-Dardir would receive immense blessings. This is attributed to his compilation of knowledge from many teachers, each of whom gave him a general ijāza. Finally, the commentator clarifies the identity of one of the teachers, ‘Umar al-Tahlawi, citing the biographical work ‘Ajā'ib al-Āthār: his full name, lineage, his own teachers, his teaching career at al-Azhar and the Husayni shrine, his praiseworthy character, and his death in 1181 AH.

Key Terms
الميراث الأكبر المحمدي
إجازة عامة
عجائب الآثار

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The dream of the Prophet is a literal promise of worldly wealth.
Correction: The dream is a spiritual affirmation of al-Dardir's high station, referring to unseen blessings, not material wealth.

Checkpoint
In what year was Ahmad al-Dardir born according to this biography?
A) 1127 AH
B) 1181 AH
C) 1117 AH
D) 1137 AH

Key Takeaway
Al-Dardir's life exemplifies the integration of exoteric knowledge (zahir) and esoteric blessing (batin) in traditional Islamic scholarship.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Al-Dardir combined the blessings of many teachers. Are you learning from a single source, or are you actively seeking multiple righteous teachers to deepen your understanding of Islam? What is one step you can take this week to diversify your learning?
?
The Prophet ﷺ gave glad tidings to al-Dardir in a dream. If you were to see the Prophet tonight, what would he say about your current pursuit of knowledge? What would you need to change starting tomorrow?
?
Al-Dardir received the great Muhammadan inheritance through a human teacher. Do you have a living teacher from whom you are truly receiving the spirit of the deen, or are you only studying books? What would it take for you to find or deepen that connection?

Life of Imam al-Mashat

The Opening Supplication of Imam al-Dardir
The poet identifies himself in the opening line.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the opening line of Imam al-Dardir's poem and the role of the commentator.

Core Idea
Humility marks the beginning of al-Dardir's theological poem.

This lesson starts at the very beginning of the text. The poet introduces himself with a phrase that sets the tone of supplication. We will look at this line and what it tells us about the author.

Guided Path
1
Read the opening line
Read the Arabic line carefully. Notice the rhyme and the self-identification.
The poet uses a humble epithet.
2
Identify the poet
The poet is Ahmad al-Dardir, a prominent scholar. The 'meaning' clarifies that the 'one who hopes' is al-Dardir.
3
Note the commentator
Shaykh Hasan al-Mashat is the commentator. He begins by signaling that he is explaining al-Dardir's poem.
The commentary is known as al-Bahja al-Saniyya.

يَقُولُ رَاجِي رَحْمَةَ القَدِيرِ * أَي أَحْمَدُ الْمَشْهُورُ بالدَّرْدِيرِ
شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي
والأول عن سيدي محمد الصغير، عن سيدي عبد الباقي الزرقاني، عن سيدي على الأجهوري.
والثاني عن الشهاب أحمد النفراوي.
وأخذ أيضًا عن الإمام العارف شمس الدين محمد بن سالم الحفناوي الشافعي، ونفع به، وعنه تلقى الميراث الأكبر المحمدي، وقد رأى بعض الصالحين رسول الله صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ فَبَشِّره بأن الإمام الدردير أُعطِي ما لا عين رأت ولا أُذُنٌ سمعت، كيف لا وقد جمع علم الشريعة
المطهرة عن الشمس الحفناوي المذكور، وعن الشيخ الصعيدي، وعن الشهاب أحمد الملوي، وعن الشمس محمد بن محمد الدفري، وكلّ منهم أجازه إجازة عامة، وأخذ عن آخرين أيضًا وأجازوه.

سنة (۱۱۸۱).

ولد الناظم بالصعيد سنة سبع وعشرين بعد المئة والألف (۲) وتوفي رحمه الله تعالى سنة واحد بعد المئتين والألف، ليلة الجمعة لثمان خلت من ربيع الأول، ودفن بمسجده الكائن بالكعكيين بجوار سيدي يحيى بن عقب رحم الله الجميع، وأنالنا بهم المقام الرفيع. المشهور بالدردير بفتح الدال الأولى، وكسر الثانية، بينهما راء ساكنة، قال الناظم في شرحه» (۳) : وكذا اشتهر أولاد الجد كلهم

سنة (١١٤٠).

(۲) كرر الشارح المولد هنا أيضًا لتثبيته في ذهن الطلاب، وخاصة أنهم
يهتمون بحفظ عام الوفاة أكثر.
(۳) شرح الخريدة البهية (ص۱۱۸).
He who hopes for the mercy of the Almighty says * meaning Ahmad, known as al-Dardir. Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat al-Maliki al-Maliki. And the first (chain) is from Sayyidi Muhammad al-Saghir, from Sayyidi Abd al-Baqi al-Zarqani, from Sayyidi Ali al-Ajhuri. And the second is from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Nafrawi. And he also took from the Imam, the gnostic, Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Salim al-Hifnawi al-Shafi'i, and benefited from him, and from him he received the greatest Muhammadan inheritance. And some righteous person saw the Messenger of God, may God bless him and grant him peace, and he gave him glad tidings that Imam al-Dardir was given what no eye has seen nor ear has heard. How could it not be, for he gathered the pure knowledge of the Sharia from the aforementioned Shams al-Hifnawi, and from al-Shaykh al-Sa'idi, and from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Milawi, and from Shams Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Dufri, and each of them granted him a general permission (ijaza), and he also took from others and they granted him permission. As for the first (chain mentioned), it is from Shams Muhammad ibn al-Mayyit, from his teachers whom he recorded in his 'Thabt', among them al-Nur al-Shabramallisi, and al-Burhan al-Kurani, and others. However, in his biography from 'Aja'ib al-Athar' (1/338) that he (i.e. Ibn al-Mayyit) is 'Umar, and he is Ibn Ali ibn Yahya ibn Mustafa al-Tahlawi al-Maliki al-Azhari. He studied fiqh under Shaykh Salim al-Nafrawi, and attended the lessons of Shaykh Ahmad al-Milawi, al-Shabrawi, and al-Bilaydi, and heard hadith from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Babili, and became proficient in the arts, and taught at al-Azhar Mosque and at the Mashhad al-Husayni. People had good belief in him, and he had gravity, dignity, and calmness, and his speech had an impact on hearts. He died in the year 1181. (Footnote 1: Its name is 'al-Jawahir al-Ghawali fi al-Asanid al-Awali', summarized from 'al-Umam' by al-Kurani; Ibn al-Mayyit is Abu Hamid Muhammad ibn Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Badiri...). Al-Bahja al-Saniyya. 1. He who hopes for the mercy of the Almighty * meaning: Ahmad, known as al-Dardir. = Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat al-Maliki al-Maliki ha. The imams — and as for the second, it is from Shams Muhammad ibn Muhammad 'Uqayla, from God? those mentioned in his chain, among them the great Shaykh, the famous hadith scholar of the Hijaz, ibn Salim al-Basri, from imams including: Shams al-Babili, from Nur Abd al-Ziyadi, from Shihab al-Ramli, from Shaykh al-Islam Zakariyya al-Ansari and Abu al-Fadl Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti, and others. And as for the third and fourth, they are from imams including: the Musnid of the Hijaz Abd Allah al-Basri al-madhkur (1). The poet (al-nazim) was born in Upper Egypt in the year 1127 (2) and died, may God have mercy on him, in the year 1201, on the night of Friday, the eighth night of Rabi' al-Awwal, and was buried in his mosque located in al-Ka'kiyyin, near Sayyidi Yahya ibn 'Uqab. May God have mercy on them all and grant us the lofty station through them. He is known as al-Dardir, with a fatha on the first dal, a kasra on the second, with a sukun on the ra' between them. The poet (al-nazim) said in his commentary (3): 'Likewise all the descendants of the grandfather became famous with this epithet.' Al-Husayni, al-Dimyati al-Shafi'i, known as Ibn al-Mayyit and al-Burhan al-Shami, died in the year 1140. (1) (Hashiya al-Siba'i, pp. 17-18). (2) The commentator repeated the birth here to fix it in the minds of the students, especially since they are concerned with memorizing the year of death more. (3) Sharh al-Kharida al-Bahiyya, p. 118.
Source pages p. 20

Commentary
This passage is a biographical introduction to Imam Ahmad al-Dardir, the author of the poem being explained. The passage begins with the opening line of al-Dardir's poem: 'He who hopes for the mercy of the Almighty * meaning Ahmad, known as al-Dardir.' The commentator, Shaykh Hasan al-Mashat, then provides al-Dardir's chains of transmission (isnad) for his knowledge. He mentions two main chains: the first through Sayyidi Muhammad al-Saghir, al-Zarqani, and al-Ajhuri; the second through Shihab Ahmad al-Nafrawi. It then notes that al-Dardir also studied under the great gnostic Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Salim al-Hifnawi, from whom he received the 'greatest Muhammadan inheritance.' A remarkable incident is related: a righteous person saw the Prophet (saw) in a dream and gave glad tidings that al-Dardir had been given what no eye has seen nor ear heard, because he had gathered the pure knowledge of the Sharia from many teachers. The passage then details al-Dardir's teachers: al-Hifnawi, al-Sa'idi, al-Milawi, and al-Dufri, all of whom granted him general permission (ijaza). It then provides more detail on the first chain, tracing it back to Shams Muhammad ibn al-Mayyit and his teachers, listing al-Shabramallisi, al-Kurani, and others. There is a biographical note on Ibn al-Mayyit himself, taken from 'Aja'ib al-Athar': his full name, his teachers, his teaching positions, and his death year (1181). A footnote explains the title of Ibn al-Mayyit's work. On the next page, the opening line is repeated, and the passage continues with the second chain through Shams Muhammad ibn Muhammad 'Uqayla, listing the chain up to al-Babili, al-Ziyadi, al-Ramli, and then Zakariyya al-Ansari and al-Suyuti. The third and fourth chains are mentioned as being from imams including Abd Allah al-Basri. Finally, the passage gives the birth and death dates of al-Dardir: born in Upper Egypt in 1127 AH, died in 1201 AH on a Friday night in Rabi' al-Awwal, buried in his mosque. It explains the pronunciation of 'al-Dardir' and notes that all descendants of the grandfather were known by that epithet. The passage ends with a footnote clarifying that the commentator repeated the birth year to help students remember. Overall, this passage establishes al-Dardir's scholarly credentials through multiple authoritative chains and a prophetic vision.

Key Terms
الناظم
الدردير

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What does the poet call himself in the opening line?
A) One who hopes for the mercy of the Almighty
B) A scholar of the Maliki school
C) A descendant of the Prophet
D) A companion of the Prophet

Key Takeaway
The opening line reveals the poet's humility and his well-known name.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you begin any work, do you start with a recognition of your need for God's mercy, or do you rely on your own ability? What would change if you adopted al-Dardir's opening line as your own?
?
Al-Dardir describes himself as 'hoping for mercy', not as a scholar. Where in your life do you act as if you are self-sufficient rather than dependent on Allah's mercy? What one step can you take today to embody humility?
?
The poet identifies himself not by his achievements but by his state of hope. How often do you introduce yourself to others based on your status rather than your reliance on Allah? How can you shift that?

Life of Imam al-Mashat

Al-Dardir's Teachers and the Prophetic Vision
Lists al-Dardir's teachers and relates a prophetic vision.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the main teachers of Imam al-Dardir and the significance of the prophetic vision about him.

Core Idea
A prophetic vision confirmed al-Dardir's extraordinary rank.

This lesson delves into the scholarly lineage of Imam al-Dardir. We learn about his teachers and a visionary event that underscores his spiritual and scholarly status.

Guided Path
1
Identify the two main chains
The passage begins with two chains. Note the names.
These are early teachers.
2
Notice the special teacher al-Hifnawi
Al-Hifnawi is described as a gnostic from whom al-Dardir received a great inheritance.
3
Understand the vision
A righteous person saw the Prophet and received glad tidings about al-Dardir. This vision testifies to al-Dardir's high station.
The vision is not a dream by al-Dardir himself.
4
List al-Dardir's other teachers
The passage names al-Sa'idi, al-Milawi, and al-Dufri, all of whom gave general ijaza.
General ijaza means permission to transmit all their knowledge.

يَقُولُ رَاجِي رَحْمَةَ القَدِيرِ * أَي أَحْمَدُ الْمَشْهُورُ بالدَّرْدِيرِ
شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي
والأول عن سيدي محمد الصغير، عن سيدي عبد الباقي الزرقاني، عن سيدي على الأجهوري.
والثاني عن الشهاب أحمد النفراوي.
وأخذ أيضًا عن الإمام العارف شمس الدين محمد بن سالم الحفناوي الشافعي، ونفع به، وعنه تلقى الميراث الأكبر المحمدي، وقد رأى بعض الصالحين رسول الله صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ فَبَشِّره بأن الإمام الدردير أُعطِي ما لا عين رأت ولا أُذُنٌ سمعت، كيف لا وقد جمع علم الشريعة
المطهرة عن الشمس الحفناوي المذكور، وعن الشيخ الصعيدي، وعن الشهاب أحمد الملوي، وعن الشمس محمد بن محمد الدفري، وكلّ منهم أجازه إجازة عامة، وأخذ عن آخرين أيضًا وأجازوه.
أما الأول فعن الشمس محمد ابن الميّت، عن مشايخه الذين أثبتهم في «ثبته» (۱)، منهم: النور الشَّبْرَامَلَّسي، والبرهان الكوراني، وغيرهم.

سنة (۱۱۸۱).
(۱) واسمه الجواهر الغوالي في الأسانيد العوالي» لخصه من «الأمم» للكوراني، وابن الميت هو أبو حامد محمد بن محمد بن محمد البديري، =
البهجة السنية
١ يَقُولُ رَاجِي رَحْمَةَ القَدِيرِ * أَي: أَحْمَدُ الْمَشْهُورُ بالدَّرْدِيرِ
=

الأئمة وأما الثاني فعن الشمس محمد بن محمد عقيلة، عن
الله
المذكورين في إسناده منهم الشيخ الكبير ، محدث الحجاز الشهير، بن سالم البصري، عن أئمة منهم: الشمس البابلي، عن النور عبد الزيادي، عن الشهاب الرملي عن شيخ الإسلام زكريا الأنصاري وأبي الفضل جلال الدين السيوطي وغيرهم.
وأما الثالث والرابع، فمن أئمة منهم: مسند الحجاز عبد الله البصري المذكور» (۱).
ولد الناظم بالصعيد سنة سبع وعشرين بعد المئة والألف (۲) وتوفي رحمه الله تعالى سنة واحد بعد المئتين والألف، ليلة الجمعة لثمان خلت من ربيع الأول، ودفن بمسجده الكائن بالكعكيين بجوار سيدي يحيى بن عقب رحم الله الجميع، وأنالنا بهم المقام الرفيع. المشهور بالدردير بفتح الدال الأولى، وكسر الثانية، بينهما راء ساكنة، قال الناظم في شرحه» (۳) : وكذا اشتهر أولاد الجد كلهم
بهذا اللقب.
الحسيني، الدمياطي الشافعي، المعروف بابن الميت، وبالبرهان الشامي، المتوفى
سنة (١١٤٠).
(۱) (حاشية السباعي» (ص ۱۷، ۱۸).
(۲) كرر الشارح المولد هنا أيضًا لتثبيته في ذهن الطلاب، وخاصة أنهم
يهتمون بحفظ عام الوفاة أكثر.
(۳) شرح الخريدة البهية (ص۱۱۸).
He who hopes for the mercy of the Almighty says * meaning Ahmad, known as al-Dardir. Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat al-Maliki al-Maliki. And the first (chain) is from Sayyidi Muhammad al-Saghir, from Sayyidi Abd al-Baqi al-Zarqani, from Sayyidi Ali al-Ajhuri. And the second is from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Nafrawi. And he also took from the Imam, the gnostic, Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Salim al-Hifnawi al-Shafi'i, and benefited from him, and from him he received the greatest Muhammadan inheritance. And some righteous person saw the Messenger of God, may God bless him and grant him peace, and he gave him glad tidings that Imam al-Dardir was given what no eye has seen nor ear has heard. How could it not be, for he gathered the pure knowledge of the Sharia from the aforementioned Shams al-Hifnawi, and from al-Shaykh al-Sa'idi, and from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Milawi, and from Shams Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Dufri, and each of them granted him a general permission (ijaza), and he also took from others and they granted him permission. As for the first (chain mentioned), it is from Shams Muhammad ibn al-Mayyit, from his teachers whom he recorded in his 'Thabt', among them al-Nur al-Shabramallisi, and al-Burhan al-Kurani, and others. However, in his biography from 'Aja'ib al-Athar' (1/338) that he (i.e. Ibn al-Mayyit) is 'Umar, and he is Ibn Ali ibn Yahya ibn Mustafa al-Tahlawi al-Maliki al-Azhari. He studied fiqh under Shaykh Salim al-Nafrawi, and attended the lessons of Shaykh Ahmad al-Milawi, al-Shabrawi, and al-Bilaydi, and heard hadith from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Babili, and became proficient in the arts, and taught at al-Azhar Mosque and at the Mashhad al-Husayni. People had good belief in him, and he had gravity, dignity, and calmness, and his speech had an impact on hearts. He died in the year 1181. (Footnote 1: Its name is 'al-Jawahir al-Ghawali fi al-Asanid al-Awali', summarized from 'al-Umam' by al-Kurani; Ibn al-Mayyit is Abu Hamid Muhammad ibn Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Badiri...). Al-Bahja al-Saniyya. 1. He who hopes for the mercy of the Almighty * meaning: Ahmad, known as al-Dardir. = Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat al-Maliki al-Maliki ha. The imams — and as for the second, it is from Shams Muhammad ibn Muhammad 'Uqayla, from God? those mentioned in his chain, among them the great Shaykh, the famous hadith scholar of the Hijaz, ibn Salim al-Basri, from imams including: Shams al-Babili, from Nur Abd al-Ziyadi, from Shihab al-Ramli, from Shaykh al-Islam Zakariyya al-Ansari and Abu al-Fadl Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti, and others. And as for the third and fourth, they are from imams including: the Musnid of the Hijaz Abd Allah al-Basri al-madhkur (1). The poet (al-nazim) was born in Upper Egypt in the year 1127 (2) and died, may God have mercy on him, in the year 1201, on the night of Friday, the eighth night of Rabi' al-Awwal, and was buried in his mosque located in al-Ka'kiyyin, near Sayyidi Yahya ibn 'Uqab. May God have mercy on them all and grant us the lofty station through them. He is known as al-Dardir, with a fatha on the first dal, a kasra on the second, with a sukun on the ra' between them. The poet (al-nazim) said in his commentary (3): 'Likewise all the descendants of the grandfather became famous with this epithet.' Al-Husayni, al-Dimyati al-Shafi'i, known as Ibn al-Mayyit and al-Burhan al-Shami, died in the year 1140. (1) (Hashiya al-Siba'i, pp. 17-18). (2) The commentator repeated the birth here to fix it in the minds of the students, especially since they are concerned with memorizing the year of death more. (3) Sharh al-Kharida al-Bahiyya, p. 118.
Source pages p. 20

Commentary
This passage is a biographical introduction to Imam Ahmad al-Dardir, the author of the poem being explained. The passage begins with the opening line of al-Dardir's poem: 'He who hopes for the mercy of the Almighty * meaning Ahmad, known as al-Dardir.' The commentator, Shaykh Hasan al-Mashat, then provides al-Dardir's chains of transmission (isnad) for his knowledge. He mentions two main chains: the first through Sayyidi Muhammad al-Saghir, al-Zarqani, and al-Ajhuri; the second through Shihab Ahmad al-Nafrawi. It then notes that al-Dardir also studied under the great gnostic Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Salim al-Hifnawi, from whom he received the 'greatest Muhammadan inheritance.' A remarkable incident is related: a righteous person saw the Prophet (saw) in a dream and gave glad tidings that al-Dardir had been given what no eye has seen nor ear heard, because he had gathered the pure knowledge of the Sharia from many teachers. The passage then details al-Dardir's teachers: al-Hifnawi, al-Sa'idi, al-Milawi, and al-Dufri, all of whom granted him general permission (ijaza). It then provides more detail on the first chain, tracing it back to Shams Muhammad ibn al-Mayyit and his teachers, listing al-Shabramallisi, al-Kurani, and others. There is a biographical note on Ibn al-Mayyit himself, taken from 'Aja'ib al-Athar': his full name, his teachers, his teaching positions, and his death year (1181). A footnote explains the title of Ibn al-Mayyit's work. On the next page, the opening line is repeated, and the passage continues with the second chain through Shams Muhammad ibn Muhammad 'Uqayla, listing the chain up to al-Babili, al-Ziyadi, al-Ramli, and then Zakariyya al-Ansari and al-Suyuti. The third and fourth chains are mentioned as being from imams including Abd Allah al-Basri. Finally, the passage gives the birth and death dates of al-Dardir: born in Upper Egypt in 1127 AH, died in 1201 AH on a Friday night in Rabi' al-Awwal, buried in his mosque. It explains the pronunciation of 'al-Dardir' and notes that all descendants of the grandfather were known by that epithet. The passage ends with a footnote clarifying that the commentator repeated the birth year to help students remember. Overall, this passage establishes al-Dardir's scholarly credentials through multiple authoritative chains and a prophetic vision.

Key Terms
الإجازة العامة
السند
الشيخ

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The prophetic vision was seen by al-Dardir himself.
Correction: The passage states that a righteous person (some righteous person) saw the Prophet and delivered the glad tidings to al-Dardir; it was not al-Dardir's own dream.

Checkpoint
Who saw the Prophet in the vision described in this passage?
A) Imam al-Dardir
B) A righteous person
C) Shaykh al-Hifnawi
D) The commentator

Key Takeaway
Al-Dardir's scholarly authority is affirmed through multiple chains and a prophetic vision.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you actively trace the chain of your religious knowledge back to the Prophet? If not, what steps can you take this week to learn from a teacher with a verified chain?
?
A righteous person was given glad tidings about al-Dardir's rank. If the Prophet were to testify about you, what would he say? What are you doing today to make that testimony possible?
?
Al-Dardir gathered knowledge from many teachers, not just one. Are you content with the bare minimum of learning, or are you willing to seek knowledge from multiple qualified sources until you build a solid foundation?

Life of Imam al-Mashat
advanced
8 min
Detailed Chains of Transmission from Ibn al-Mayyit and Others
Provides detailed chains of transmission with biographical notes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the detailed chains of transmission that connect Imam al-Dardir to earlier scholars.

Core Idea
Al-Dardir's knowledge is traced through multiple reliable chains to the great scholars of Islam.

This lesson takes a closer look at the specific names in al-Dardir's chains. We will see how his teachers connect him to earlier authorities like Zakariyya al-Ansari and al-Suyuti.

Guided Path
1
Read the first chain section
The first chain goes through Shams Muhammad ibn al-Mayyit. Read his biography.
Ibn al-Mayyit was a scholar who died in 1181.
2
Understand the footnote
The footnote identifies the book Ibn al-Mayyit wrote about his chains.
3
Move to the second chain
The second chain goes through Muhammad 'Uqayla and reaches al-Suyuti.
Note the famous names: al-Ramli, Zakariyya al-Ansari, al-Suyuti.
4
Note the third and fourth chains
Both involve Abd Allah al-Basri, a hadith scholar of the Hijaz.
These chains add further authority.

(۳) شرح الخريدة البهية (ص۱۱۸).
He who hopes for the mercy of the Almighty says * meaning Ahmad, known as al-Dardir. Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat al-Maliki al-Maliki. And the first (chain) is from Sayyidi Muhammad al-Saghir, from Sayyidi Abd al-Baqi al-Zarqani, from Sayyidi Ali al-Ajhuri. And the second is from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Nafrawi. And he also took from the Imam, the gnostic, Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Salim al-Hifnawi al-Shafi'i, and benefited from him, and from him he received the greatest Muhammadan inheritance. And some righteous person saw the Messenger of God, may God bless him and grant him peace, and he gave him glad tidings that Imam al-Dardir was given what no eye has seen nor ear has heard. How could it not be, for he gathered the pure knowledge of the Sharia from the aforementioned Shams al-Hifnawi, and from al-Shaykh al-Sa'idi, and from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Milawi, and from Shams Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Dufri, and each of them granted him a general permission (ijaza), and he also took from others and they granted him permission. As for the first (chain mentioned), it is from Shams Muhammad ibn al-Mayyit, from his teachers whom he recorded in his 'Thabt', among them al-Nur al-Shabramallisi, and al-Burhan al-Kurani, and others. However, in his biography from 'Aja'ib al-Athar' (1/338) that he (i.e. Ibn al-Mayyit) is 'Umar, and he is Ibn Ali ibn Yahya ibn Mustafa al-Tahlawi al-Maliki al-Azhari. He studied fiqh under Shaykh Salim al-Nafrawi, and attended the lessons of Shaykh Ahmad al-Milawi, al-Shabrawi, and al-Bilaydi, and heard hadith from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Babili, and became proficient in the arts, and taught at al-Azhar Mosque and at the Mashhad al-Husayni. People had good belief in him, and he had gravity, dignity, and calmness, and his speech had an impact on hearts. He died in the year 1181. (Footnote 1: Its name is 'al-Jawahir al-Ghawali fi al-Asanid al-Awali', summarized from 'al-Umam' by al-Kurani; Ibn al-Mayyit is Abu Hamid Muhammad ibn Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Badiri...). Al-Bahja al-Saniyya. 1. He who hopes for the mercy of the Almighty * meaning: Ahmad, known as al-Dardir. = Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat al-Maliki al-Maliki ha. The imams — and as for the second, it is from Shams Muhammad ibn Muhammad 'Uqayla, from God? those mentioned in his chain, among them the great Shaykh, the famous hadith scholar of the Hijaz, ibn Salim al-Basri, from imams including: Shams al-Babili, from Nur Abd al-Ziyadi, from Shihab al-Ramli, from Shaykh al-Islam Zakariyya al-Ansari and Abu al-Fadl Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti, and others. And as for the third and fourth, they are from imams including: the Musnid of the Hijaz Abd Allah al-Basri al-madhkur (1). The poet (al-nazim) was born in Upper Egypt in the year 1127 (2) and died, may God have mercy on him, in the year 1201, on the night of Friday, the eighth night of Rabi' al-Awwal, and was buried in his mosque located in al-Ka'kiyyin, near Sayyidi Yahya ibn 'Uqab. May God have mercy on them all and grant us the lofty station through them. He is known as al-Dardir, with a fatha on the first dal, a kasra on the second, with a sukun on the ra' between them. The poet (al-nazim) said in his commentary (3): 'Likewise all the descendants of the grandfather became famous with this epithet.' Al-Husayni, al-Dimyati al-Shafi'i, known as Ibn al-Mayyit and al-Burhan al-Shami, died in the year 1140. (1) (Hashiya al-Siba'i, pp. 17-18). (2) The commentator repeated the birth here to fix it in the minds of the students, especially since they are concerned with memorizing the year of death more. (3) Sharh al-Kharida al-Bahiyya, p. 118.
Source pages p. 20

Commentary
This passage is a biographical introduction to Imam Ahmad al-Dardir, the author of the poem being explained. The passage begins with the opening line of al-Dardir's poem: 'He who hopes for the mercy of the Almighty * meaning Ahmad, known as al-Dardir.' The commentator, Shaykh Hasan al-Mashat, then provides al-Dardir's chains of transmission (isnad) for his knowledge. He mentions two main chains: the first through Sayyidi Muhammad al-Saghir, al-Zarqani, and al-Ajhuri; the second through Shihab Ahmad al-Nafrawi. It then notes that al-Dardir also studied under the great gnostic Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Salim al-Hifnawi, from whom he received the 'greatest Muhammadan inheritance.' A remarkable incident is related: a righteous person saw the Prophet (saw) in a dream and gave glad tidings that al-Dardir had been given what no eye has seen nor ear heard, because he had gathered the pure knowledge of the Sharia from many teachers. The passage then details al-Dardir's teachers: al-Hifnawi, al-Sa'idi, al-Milawi, and al-Dufri, all of whom granted him general permission (ijaza). It then provides more detail on the first chain, tracing it back to Shams Muhammad ibn al-Mayyit and his teachers, listing al-Shabramallisi, al-Kurani, and others. There is a biographical note on Ibn al-Mayyit himself, taken from 'Aja'ib al-Athar': his full name, his teachers, his teaching positions, and his death year (1181). A footnote explains the title of Ibn al-Mayyit's work. On the next page, the opening line is repeated, and the passage continues with the second chain through Shams Muhammad ibn Muhammad 'Uqayla, listing the chain up to al-Babili, al-Ziyadi, al-Ramli, and then Zakariyya al-Ansari and al-Suyuti. The third and fourth chains are mentioned as being from imams including Abd Allah al-Basri. Finally, the passage gives the birth and death dates of al-Dardir: born in Upper Egypt in 1127 AH, died in 1201 AH on a Friday night in Rabi' al-Awwal, buried in his mosque. It explains the pronunciation of 'al-Dardir' and notes that all descendants of the grandfather were known by that epithet. The passage ends with a footnote clarifying that the commentator repeated the birth year to help students remember. Overall, this passage establishes al-Dardir's scholarly credentials through multiple authoritative chains and a prophetic vision.

السند
الشيخ حسن المشاط

Key Takeaway

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The passage traces al-Dardir's teachers back to Zakariyya al-Ansari and al-Suyuti. When you consider the sources of your own Islamic knowledge, how confident are you that they are connected to authentic chains? What will you do this week to verify the credibility of at least one scholar you follow?
?
Ibn al-Mayyit's biography mentions that his words had a 'impact on hearts.' Do you have a teacher whose words move you? If not, what is one step you can take to seek out such a teacher?
?
The detailed recording of chains shows that scholars took immense care to preserve their lineage of learning. Are you taking equal care to preserve and transmit the knowledge you have? Write down one specific action you will take to pass on what you have learned to someone else.

The Birth, Death, and Legacy of Imam al-Dardir
Provides birth/death dates and pronunciation of al-Dardir's name.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will know the birth and death dates of Imam al-Dardir, where he is buried, and the correct pronunciation of his name.

Core Idea
Al-Dardir's life spanned the 12th and 13th centuries AH, and his name has a precise pronunciation.

This lesson provides the final pieces of al-Dardir's biography: his birth and death, burial place, and the proper way to say his name. These details help cement his identity for students.

Guided Path
1
Read the birth and death details
Note the year 1127 for birth and 1201 for death. The passage gives the exact day and burial location.
The commentator repeated the birth year to aid memorization.
2
Learn the pronunciation of 'al-Dardir'
The name has a fatha on dāl, kasra on rā', and a sukun on the rā' between them.
This clarifies a common mispronunciation.
3
Note the additional information
The passage also mentions another scholar, Ibn al-Mayyit, and provides footnotes.
Footnotes give source references and pedagogical notes.

He who hopes for the mercy of the Almighty says * meaning Ahmad, known as al-Dardir. Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat al-Maliki al-Maliki. And the first (chain) is from Sayyidi Muhammad al-Saghir, from Sayyidi Abd al-Baqi al-Zarqani, from Sayyidi Ali al-Ajhuri. And the second is from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Nafrawi. And he also took from the Imam, the gnostic, Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Salim al-Hifnawi al-Shafi'i, and benefited from him, and from him he received the greatest Muhammadan inheritance. And some righteous person saw the Messenger of God, may God bless him and grant him peace, and he gave him glad tidings that Imam al-Dardir was given what no eye has seen nor ear has heard. How could it not be, for he gathered the pure knowledge of the Sharia from the aforementioned Shams al-Hifnawi, and from al-Shaykh al-Sa'idi, and from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Milawi, and from Shams Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Dufri, and each of them granted him a general permission (ijaza), and he also took from others and they granted him permission. As for the first (chain mentioned), it is from Shams Muhammad ibn al-Mayyit, from his teachers whom he recorded in his 'Thabt', among them al-Nur al-Shabramallisi, and al-Burhan al-Kurani, and others. However, in his biography from 'Aja'ib al-Athar' (1/338) that he (i.e. Ibn al-Mayyit) is 'Umar, and he is Ibn Ali ibn Yahya ibn Mustafa al-Tahlawi al-Maliki al-Azhari. He studied fiqh under Shaykh Salim al-Nafrawi, and attended the lessons of Shaykh Ahmad al-Milawi, al-Shabrawi, and al-Bilaydi, and heard hadith from al-Shihab Ahmad al-Babili, and became proficient in the arts, and taught at al-Azhar Mosque and at the Mashhad al-Husayni. People had good belief in him, and he had gravity, dignity, and calmness, and his speech had an impact on hearts. He died in the year 1181. (Footnote 1: Its name is 'al-Jawahir al-Ghawali fi al-Asanid al-Awali', summarized from 'al-Umam' by al-Kurani; Ibn al-Mayyit is Abu Hamid Muhammad ibn Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Badiri...). Al-Bahja al-Saniyya. 1. He who hopes for the mercy of the Almighty * meaning: Ahmad, known as al-Dardir. = Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat al-Maliki al-Maliki ha. The imams — and as for the second, it is from Shams Muhammad ibn Muhammad 'Uqayla, from God? those mentioned in his chain, among them the great Shaykh, the famous hadith scholar of the Hijaz, ibn Salim al-Basri, from imams including: Shams al-Babili, from Nur Abd al-Ziyadi, from Shihab al-Ramli, from Shaykh al-Islam Zakariyya al-Ansari and Abu al-Fadl Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti, and others. And as for the third and fourth, they are from imams including: the Musnid of the Hijaz Abd Allah al-Basri al-madhkur (1). The poet (al-nazim) was born in Upper Egypt in the year 1127 (2) and died, may God have mercy on him, in the year 1201, on the night of Friday, the eighth night of Rabi' al-Awwal, and was buried in his mosque located in al-Ka'kiyyin, near Sayyidi Yahya ibn 'Uqab. May God have mercy on them all and grant us the lofty station through them. He is known as al-Dardir, with a fatha on the first dal, a kasra on the second, with a sukun on the ra' between them. The poet (al-nazim) said in his commentary (3): 'Likewise all the descendants of the grandfather became famous with this epithet.' Al-Husayni, al-Dimyati al-Shafi'i, known as Ibn al-Mayyit and al-Burhan al-Shami, died in the year 1140. (1) (Hashiya al-Siba'i, pp. 17-18). (2) The commentator repeated the birth here to fix it in the minds of the students, especially since they are concerned with memorizing the year of death more. (3) Sharh al-Kharida al-Bahiyya, p. 118.

Commentary
This passage is a biographical introduction to Imam Ahmad al-Dardir, the author of the poem being explained. The passage begins with the opening line of al-Dardir's poem: 'He who hopes for the mercy of the Almighty * meaning Ahmad, known as al-Dardir.' The commentator, Shaykh Hasan al-Mashat, then provides al-Dardir's chains of transmission (isnad) for his knowledge. It then notes that al-Dardir also studied under the great gnostic Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Salim al-Hifnawi, from whom he received the 'greatest Muhammadan inheritance.' A remarkable incident is related: a righteous person saw the Prophet (saw) in a dream and gave glad tidings that al-Dardir had been given what no eye has seen nor ear heard, because he had gathered the pure knowledge of the Sharia from many teachers. The passage then details al-Dardir's teachers: al-Hifnawi, al-Sa'idi, al-Milawi, and al-Dufri, all of whom granted him general permission (ijaza). It then provides more detail on the first chain, tracing it back to Shams Muhammad ibn al-Mayyit and his teachers, listing al-Shabramallisi, al-Kurani, and others. There is a biographical note on Ibn al-Mayyit himself, taken from 'Aja'ib al-Athar': his full name, his teachers, his teaching positions, and his death year (1181). A footnote explains the title of Ibn al-Mayyit's work. It explains the pronunciation of 'al-Dardir' and notes that all descendants of the grandfather were known by that epithet. The passage ends with a footnote clarifying that the commentator repeated the birth year to help students remember. Overall, this passage establishes al-Dardir's scholarly credentials through multiple authoritative chains and a prophetic vision.

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The name 'al-Dardir' is pronounced with a different vowel pattern (e.g., al-Dardīr with long vowels).
Correction: The passage specifies the correct pronunciation: fatha on the first dāl, kasra on the second, and sukun on the rā'.

The Biography and Nickname of Ahmad al-Dardir
Biography of the author with origin of his nickname

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the key biographical details of Ahmad al-Dardir and the origin of his famous nickname.

Core Idea
A scholar's nickname can reveal his tribal roots.

Before diving into the theological content of al-Kharida, it is valuable to know the author. This lesson presents the basic facts of Ahmad al-Dardir's life and the story behind his nickname. Understanding his background helps us appreciate the text as a product of its time and place.

Guided Path
1
Read the biographical facts
Identify the birth and death dates and burial location. Note the exact dates mentioned.
The author was born in 1127 AH and died in 1201 AH.
2
Learn the nickname's pronunciation
The passage gives the vowel markings for 'al-Dardir'. Pay attention to the fatha on the first dal, kasra on the second, and sukun on the ra.
3
Discover the story behind the name
Read al-Siba'i's account of how the nickname originated. What was the circumstance involving the grandfather's wife and a man named al-Dardir?
4
Reflect on the significance
Consider why such biographical details are preserved. How does knowing the author's background affect your reading of his work?
This helps contextualize the author's heritage.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولد الناظم بالصعيد سنة سبع وعشرين بعد المئة والألف وتوفي رحمه الله تعالى سنة واحد بعد المئتين والألف، ليلة الجمعة لثمان خلت من ربيع الأول، ودفن بمسجده الكائن بالكعكيين بجوار سيدي يحيى بن عقب رحم الله الجميع، وأنالنا بهم المقام الرفيع. المشهور بالدردير بفتح الدال الأولى، وكسر الثانية، بينهما راء ساكنة، قال الناظم في شرحه : وكذا اشتهر أولاد الجد كلهم بهذا اللقب. قال العلامة السباعي: وسببه أن جد الشيخ كانت حاملا به والدته، وأضافهم رجل من مشايخ عربان محارب، يقال له: الدردير، فوضعت أمه في تلك الليلة فلقبوه بذلك».
The author was born in al-Sa'id in the year 1127 AH and died, may Allah have mercy on him, in the year 1201 AH, on the night of Friday, the eighth night of Rabi' al-Awwal, and was buried in his mosque located in al-Ka'kiyyin near Sidi Yahya ibn 'Uqb, may Allah have mercy on them all, and grant us through them a lofty station. He is known as al-Dardir with a fatha on the first dal, a kasra on the second, with a silent ra between them. The author said in his commentary: 'Likewise, all the grandchildren of the grandfather became known by this nickname.' Al-Siba'i said: 'The reason is that the grandfather's wife was pregnant with the Sheikh, and a man from the Arab shaykhs of Muharib, called al-Dardir, added them (took them in?), so his mother gave birth that night, and they nicknamed him thus.'
Source pages p. 21

Commentary
This passage provides biographical details about the author, Ahmad al-Dardir, and the origin of his nickname. It states his birth in 1127 AH in Upper Egypt, his death in 1201 AH, and his burial place. It then explains the nickname 'al-Dardir': the author himself in his commentary states that all descendants of the grandfather became known by this name. Al-Siba'i adds a story that the grandfather's wife was pregnant with the Sheikh when a man named al-Dardir gave them shelter or affiliation, and the child was born that night, so they called him al-Dardir.

Key Terms
الدردير (al-Dardir)
الناظم (the author)
السباعي (al-Siba'i)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the nickname al-Dardir is an insult or derogatory term.
Correction: The name al-Dardir is a tribal affiliation, not an insult. It was given due to the family's connection to a man named al-Dardir of the Arab tribe of Muharib.

Checkpoint
Where was Ahmad al-Dardir born?
A) In Cairo
B) In Upper Egypt (al-Sa'id)
C) In Mecca
D) In Damascus

Key Takeaway
Ahmad al-Dardir, born in 1127 AH in Upper Egypt, earned his nickname from a tribal affiliation with a man named al-Dardir, as recorded by al-Siba'i.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
What does the precise recording of al-Dardir's death—exact night and burial location—teach you about the value of scholars in Islam? Are you living in a way that would make your own end worth recording?
?
The author's nickname came from a tribal affiliation, not from any personal attribute. In your life, what defines you: your lineage, your achievements, or your character? Which one will truly honor you after you are gone?
?
How does the fact that a simple act of hospitality (being added by al-Dardir) gave rise to a lasting legacy challenge you to treat others with generosity, even those who may become greater than you?

Life of Imam al-Mashat

The Opening Praise and the Prophet's Nobility
The author opens with hamd and salawat and cites the hadith on the Prophet's chosen lineage.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the author opens his commentary with praise and establishes the Prophet's nobility through a hadith on his chosen lineage.

Core Idea
The Prophet's nobility is proven by divine selection through the purest lineages.

In this opening passage, the author begins with the traditional praise of Allah and blessings upon the Prophet. He then immediately asserts the Prophet's supreme rank over all creation, including other prophets, and supports this with a sahih hadith that traces the Prophet's lineage through successive divine choices. This sets the tone for the commentary's reverence for the Prophet.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation to grasp the meaning.
Notice the rhythmic opening phrases and the hadith at the end.
2
Identify the three parts
The passage contains three elements: praise of Allah (hamd), blessings upon the Prophet (salawat), and the supporting hadith. Find each part.
3
Understand the argument
The hadith is cited as evidence for the claim that the Prophet is the best of creation. See how the lineage choices show divine selection.
Each 'choice' (istifa) narrows down to the noblest lineage.
4
Reflect on the significance
Why does the author begin with this hadith? It establishes the Prophet's rank from the outset and grounds it in revelation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ العَلِيِّ الوَاحِدِ * العَالِمِ الفَرْدِ الغَنِيُّ الْمَاجِدِ وَأَفْضَلُ الصَّلاةِ والتَّسْلِيمِ عَلَى النَّبِيِّ الْمُصْطَفَى الكَرِيمِ الْمُخْتَارِ فَضْلِ سَيِّدِنَا وَلِ اللهِ مِنْ بَيْنِ الْخَلْقِ بِالسِّيَادَةِ وَالشَّرَفِ الْعَظِيمِ عَلَى جَمِيعِ الْعَالَمِ حَتَّى الْأَنْبِيَاءِ وَالرُّسُلِ، لِلْحَدِيثِ الصَّحِيحِ: «إِنَّ اللهَ اصْطَفَى كِنَانَةَ مِنْ وَلَدِ إِسْمَاعِيلَ، وَاصْطَفَى قُرَيْشًا مِنْ كِنَانَةَ، وَاصْطَفَى مِنْ قُرَيْشٍ بَنِي هَاشِمٍ، وَاصْطَفَانِي مِنْ بَنِي هَاشِمٍ، فَأَنَا خِيَارٌ مِنْ خِيَارٍ مِنْ خِيَارٍ»
Praise be to Allah, the High, the One, the All-Knowing, the Unique, the Self-Sufficient, the Glorious. And the most excellent of prayers and peace be upon the chosen noble Prophet, the selected, the merit of our master and friend of Allah? from among creation in sovereignty and great honor over all the worlds, even the prophets and messengers, due to the sound hadith: 'Indeed Allah chose Kinana from the descendants of Ishmael, and He chose Quraysh from Kinana, and He chose from Quraysh the Banu Hashim, and He chose me from Banu Hashim, so I am the best from the best from the best.'
Source pages p. 22

Commentary
This passage is the opening of the commentary, where the author begins with the basmala and hamdala followed by salawat upon the Prophet. He then states that the Prophet (peace be upon him) is the best of creation, superior to all prophets and messengers, and supports this with the hadith in which the Prophet says that Allah chose the best from each lineage until He chose him. This hadith establishes the Prophet's nobility and superiority.

Key Terms
الحمد
الصلاة
التسليم
اصطفى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Prophet's nobility is merely a matter of tribal pride or human ancestry.
Correction: The hadith shows that nobility comes from divine choice (istifa), not mere lineage. Allah actively selected each successive group, culminating in the Prophet.

Checkpoint
According to the hadith cited, which group did Allah choose first from the descendants of Ishmael?
A) Banu Hashim
B) Quraysh
C) Kinana
D) Ishmael

Key Takeaway
The Prophet's superiority is established by Allah's successive choices of the noblest lineages, culminating in the Prophet himself.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read that Allah chose the Prophet from the best of lineages, does your heart fully accept that he is truly the most noble of all creation, or do you still find yourself comparing him to other figures in your mind? What would change in your daily life if you truly believed that he is the best from the best from the best?
?
The Prophet said, 'I am the best from the best from the best.' How does this statement challenge your own pride or humility? Do you ever feel that your own lineage, background, or achievements make you better than others? How can you use this hadith to purify your heart from such feelings?
?
If the Prophet's nobility came entirely from Allah's choice—not from his own effort—what does that teach you about your own standing with Allah? Are you asking Allah to grant you nobility through His selection, or are you relying on your own deeds? Take a moment to make dua asking Allah to choose you for His nearness.

The Nickname 'al-Dardir': A Biographical Note
Al-Sabai narrates the origin of the nickname 'al-Dardir'.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the historical reason behind the nickname 'al-Dardir' as recorded by al-Sabai.

Core Idea
A nickname born from a night of hospitality.

Before diving into theological arguments, it is often helpful to know something about the author whose work we study. This short passage from al-Sabai gives us a glimpse into the origins of the nickname 'al-Dardir', showing how a personal event in the author's family history led to the name by which he became known.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic text and its translation. Notice that it is a direct quotation from al-Sabai.
The passage is very short, so take time to parse each word.
2
Identify the key figures
Identify who is speaking (al-Sabai), who is being described (the grandfather of the author), and the man named al-Dardir.
The author's grandfather is the one whose birth is being narrated.
3
Understand the nickname's origin
The nickname came from the man who hosted the author's pregnant grandmother. The child was born that night and was called al-Dardir after the host.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال العلامة السباعي: وسببه أن جد الشيخ كانت حاملا به والدته، وأضافهم رجل من مشايخ عربان محارب، يقال له: الدردير، فوضعت أمه في تلك الليلة فلقبوه بذلك
Al-Allama al-Sabai said: 'Its reason is that the grandfather of the Sheikh (the author) - his mother was pregnant with him, and a man from the elders of the Arab tribe of Muharib, called al-Dardir, hosted them, so his mother gave birth that night, and they nicknamed him by that (i.e., al-Dardir).'
Source pages p. 22

Commentary
This passage is a direct quotation from al-Sabai explaining the origin of the nickname 'al-Dardir' for the author of the matn (the poem al-Kharida). It relates that the author's grandfather (or the author himself?) was born when his mother was hosted by a man named al-Dardir, so he was called al-Dardir after him. This is a biographical note about the author's lineage.

Key Terms
السباعي
الدردير
اللقب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to al-Sabai, why was the author nicknamed 'al-Dardir'?
A) He was born in the desert
B) He was hosted by a man named al-Dardir
C) His mother gave birth while hosted by al-Dardir
D) He was from the Muharib tribe

Key Takeaway
The nickname 'al-Dardir' originated from the man who hosted the author's grandmother on the night his grandfather was born.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
What small act of kindness have you received that left a lasting mark on your life or identity? How can you be a 'Dardir' to someone in need today?
?
Your name—whether given, earned, or inherited—shapes how others see you. Does your name honor a good deed or a virtue? If not, what kind of legacy do you want your life to narrate?
?
If hospitality can lead to a centuries-old legacy like 'al-Dardir', what legacy is your daily treatment of strangers building?

The Prophet's Noble Lineage and its Obligation
The author details the Prophet's noble lineage and urges its memorization.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Prophet's lineage from Muhammad to al-Nadr and why memorizing this lineage is a personal duty.

Core Idea
Knowing the Prophet's lineage is a duty; it is shameful to be ignorant of it.

This passage from Sanusi’s commentary presents the full lineage of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) from his father up to his ancestor al-Nadr, with a discussion of the name 'Quraysh.' The author then stresses that preserving this noble lineage is obligatory because it identifies the Prophet and his pure origins. A poetic couplet underscores the honor of this lineage.

Guided Path
1
Read the lineage list
Read the Arabic lineage from 'Muhammad ibn Abdullah' to 'ibn al-Nadr' carefully. Notice the chain of noble ancestors.
The list includes 11 generations from Muhammad to al-Nadr.
2
Examine the note on Quraysh
The author mentions two opinions: the most correct is that Fihr is the gatherer of Quraysh, but the majority say al-Nadr. Understand this subtle point.
The lineage ends at al-Nadr, who is identified as Quraysh by the majority.
3
Reflect on the obligation
The author says 'يتعين حفظ هذا النسب الشريف' — it is incumbent to preserve this lineage. Consider why it would be shameful not to know it.
This connects to the idea of honoring the Prophet by knowing his roots.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فهو سيدنا محمد بن عبد الله بن عبد المطلب بن هاشم بن عبد مناف بن قصي بن كلاب بن مرة بن كعب بن لؤي بن غالب بن فهر بن مالك بن النضر، وهو قريش على القول الأكثر. أمَا قُرَيشُ فَالأَصَحُ فِهْرُ جِمَاعُها، والأكْثَرُونَ النَّضْرُ (۱) يقول العبد الفقير الرحمة ربه كان الله له: يتعين حفظ هذا النسب الشريف، إذ به يُعرف سيدنا محمد صلى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ، وأصوله الكرام المطهرة، ويقبح بالإنسان ألا يعرف نسب سيده. نَسَبٌ تَحْسِبُ العُلَا بِحُلَاهُ قَلَّدَتْهَا نُجُومَهَا الْجُوزَاءُ مِنْ سَاجِدٍ لِسَاجِدٍ تَقَلَّبًا * صَلَّى عَلَيْهِ اللَّهُ مَا هَبَ الصَّبَا
So he is our master Muhammad ibn Abdullah ibn Abd al-Muttalib ibn Hashim ibn Abd Manaf ibn Qusayy ibn Kilab ibn Murra ibn Ka'b ibn Lu'ayy ibn Ghalib ibn Fihr ibn Malik ibn al-Nadr, and he is Quraysh according to the more prevalent opinion. As for Quraysh, the most correct (opinion) is that Fihr is their gatherer, but the majority (says) al-Nadr. The poor servant hoping for his Lord's mercy - may Allah be with him - says: The preservation of this noble lineage is incumbent, for by it our master Muhammad, may Allah bless him and give him peace, is known, and his noble and pure origins, and it is shameful for a person not to know the lineage of his master. A lineage whose beauty you would think the high (stars) have adorned with its ornaments, Gemini has necklaced its stars, from one prostrating to another prostrating in alternation. May Allah bless him as long as the east wind blows.
Source pages p. 23

Commentary
This passage is a biographical and exhortatory section. The author states the Prophet's full lineage from Muhammad up to al-Nadr, with a note on the disagreement whether Quraysh is Fihr or al-Nadr. He then emphasizes the obligation to preserve this noble lineage because it identifies the Prophet and his pure ancestry, and it is disgraceful for a person to be ignorant of it. He ends with a couplet of poetry praising the Prophet's lineage.

Key Terms
نسب
قريش
فهر
النضر
يتعين

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think knowing the Prophet's lineage is optional or unimportant.
Correction: The author states it is imperative (يتعين) and shameful to be ignorant of it, emphasizing its importance for knowing the Prophet and honoring his origins.

Checkpoint
According to the text, what is the most correct opinion about the ancestor who gathered Quraysh?
A) Al-Nadr
B) Fihr
C) Qusayy
D) Hashim

Key Takeaway
Memorizing the Prophet's lineage is an incumbent duty for every believer.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you know the Prophet's lineage by heart? If not, what is keeping you from fulfilling this duty that the author calls 'incumbent'?
?
When was the last time you felt shame (يُقبح) for not knowing something about your Prophet? How can you remove that ignorance today?
?
How does the fact that the Prophet's lineage is traceable all the way to a noble ancestry affect your love for him and your desire to follow him in every aspect of your life?

5 min
The Prophet's Noble Lineage
Commentator presents the Prophet's genealogy and emphasizes memorizing it.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Prophet Muhammad's genealogy as presented by the commentator and why memorizing it is considered obligatory.

Core Idea
Knowing the Prophet's lineage is a duty for every believer.

This passage presents the noble lineage of Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) in detail, leading from his father Abdullah back to an-Nadr. The commentator then resolves a scholarly dispute about who constitutes Quraysh before emphasizing that memorizing this lineage is not merely recommended but obligatory. Let's explore the text and its significance.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then refer to the translation. Notice the flow from the Prophet's father up to an-Nadr.
The passage is dense; read slowly.
2
Identify the lineage chain
List the names from the Prophet down to an-Nadr. The passage gives a continuous chain.
Count the generations: 14 names including the Prophet.
3
Understand the Quraysh dispute
The passage mentions two views on who constitutes Quraysh. Note the difference between Fihr and an-Nadr.
The commentator sides with Fihr as the more correct unifier.
4
Grasp the ruling on memorization
The commentator uses the word 'يتعين' (it is obligatory) for memorizing the lineage. Reflect on why this is emphasized.
This connects to respect and love for the Prophet.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فهو سيدنا محمد بن عبد الله بن عبد المطلب بن هاشم بن عبد مناف بن قصي بن كلاب بن مرة بن كعب بن لؤي بن غالب بن فهر بن مالك بن النضر، وهو قريش على القول الأكثر. أما قريش فالأصح فهر جماعها، والأكثرون النضر. يقول العبد الفقير لرحمة ربه كان الله له: يتعين حفظ هذا النسب الشريف، إذ به يعرف سيدنا محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم، وأصوله الكرام المطهرة، ويقبح بالإنسان ألا يعرف نسب سيده.
So he is our master Muhammad son of Abdullah son of Abdulmutallib son of Hashim son of Abd Manaf son of Qusayy son of Kilab son of Murrah son of Ka'b son of Lu'ayy son of Ghalib son of Fihr son of Malik son of an-Nadr, and he is Quraysh according to the more prevalent view. As for Quraysh, the most correct is that Fihr is their unifier, while the majority say an-Nadr. The poor servant hoping for his Lord's mercy—may Allah be his—says: It is obligatory to memorize this noble lineage, for by it our master Muhammad (peace and blessings be upon him) is known, and his noble and pure origins; and it is disgraceful for a person not to know the lineage of his master.
Source pages p. 23

Commentary
This passage presents the lineage of the Prophet Muhammad (peace and blessings be upon him) in its genealogical detail, tracing it back to an-Nadr. It then clarifies a scholarly dispute about who constitutes 'Quraysh': the stronger opinion says Fihr is the unifying ancestor, while the majority view points to an-Nadr. The commentator then states his own view as a 'poor servant' that memorizing this noble lineage is an obligation (yuta'ayyan), because through it the Prophet is known, and it is shameful for a believer not to know the lineage of his master (i.e., the Prophet). The passage thus emphasizes the importance of preserving and knowing the Prophet's genealogy as an act of respect and love.

Key Terms
نسب
قريش
فهر
النضر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Knowing the Prophet's lineage is optional or only for scholars.
Correction: The commentator explicitly states it is obligatory (يتعين) and disgraceful not to know it.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the ruling on memorizing the Prophet's lineage?
A) It is recommended but not required.
B) It is obligatory (يتعين).
C) It is only for the scholars.
D) It is optional.

Key Takeaway
Memorizing the Prophet's noble lineage is an act of love and respect, regarded as obligatory by the commentator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If it is disgraceful not to know the lineage of his master, how do you feel about your own knowledge of the Prophet's family? Will you commit to learning and teaching his entire lineage this week?
?
When you claim to love the Prophet, what concrete steps have you taken to truly know him beyond a few basic facts? Does your effort reflect the depth of your love?
?
The commentator says 'يتعين حفظ هذا النسب الشريف'. What might be holding you back from memorizing it? Are you willing to start today with just the first ten names?

Defining the Prophet's Family and Companions
Commentator defines the terms 'Al' and 'Sahb' and their scope.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the specific definitions of 'Al' (family) and 'Sahb' (companions) as given by the commentator, and the theological principle that all companions are trustworthy.

Core Idea
'Al' refers to believing relatives; 'Sahb' are all believing companions, all trustworthy.

In this lesson, we examine the commentator's precise definitions of the Prophet's family (Al) and companions (Sahb) as they appear in the blessing formula. These definitions are not merely linguistic but carry theological weight, especially the affirmation that all companions are trustworthy. Understanding these terms helps us appreciate the reverence shown to the Prophet's household and companions in Sunni Islam.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without pausing. Then read the English translation to grasp the basic meaning.
Notice that the passage is compact and each term is defined precisely.
2
Identify the definitions of 'Al'
Find where the commentator defines 'Al'. Note the two different contexts: general (believing relatives) and supplication (all followers). Also note the alternative opinion restricting to the pious.
The term 'آل' has a scope that depends on context.
3
Identify the definition of 'Sahb'
Find the definition of 'صحب'. Notice the conditions: belief in the Prophet and meeting him during his prophethood, even if only once.
This definition includes all who met the Prophet with faith, regardless of duration.
4
Understand the principle of 'Udul'
Read the final sentence: 'وهم عدول كلهم لا يشتبه.' This declares that all companions are trustworthy and upright. Reflect on why this is important for accepting hadith and Islamic history.
This doctrine protects the integrity of the chain of narration.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وآله معطوف على النبي، أي: وعلى آله، وهم أقاربه المؤمنون من بني هاشم والمطلب، وفي مقام الدعاء كما هنا: اتباعه عليه الصلاة والسلام مطلقًا، وقيل: الأتقياء من أمته. وصحبه وهم من آمن به، واجتمع معه، أوان حمل الدعوة ولو مرة واحدة. وهم عدول كلهم لا يشتبه.
And his family (آل) is conjoined to 'the Prophet', i.e., and upon his family, and they are his believing relatives from Banu Hashim and Banu al-Muttalib, and in the context of supplication as here: his followers (peace and blessings be upon him) absolutely, and it is said: the pious ones of his nation. And his companions (صحبه) are those who believed in him and met him at the time of carrying the call (i.e., during his prophethood) even once. And they are all trustworthy ('udul), there is no doubt.
Source pages p. 23

Commentary
This passage defines two key terms from the opening blessing: 'آل' (family) and 'صحب' (companions). It explains that 'آل' refers to the believing relatives from the clans of Hashim and al-Muttalib, and in the context of du'a (supplication) it can include all followers of the Prophet. Another opinion restricts it to the pious of his community. 'صحب' refers to those who believed in the Prophet and met him during his prophetic mission, even if only once. The commentator concludes by affirming the well-known principle that all companions are 'udul (trustworthy and upright), a doctrine that prevents slandering them.

Key Terms
آل
صحب
عدول
بني هاشم
بني المطلب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think 'Al' includes all descendants of the Prophet regardless of faith.
Correction: The commentator restricts 'Al' to believing relatives from Banu Hashim and Banu al-Muttalib, and in supplication to followers, but not all descendants.

Checkpoint
How does the commentator define 'صحب' (companions)?
A) Anyone who lived during the Prophet's time.
B) Those who believed in the Prophet and met him at least once during his prophethood.
C) Only the close relatives of the Prophet.
D) All Muslims after the Prophet.

Key Takeaway
The commentator defines 'Al' as believing relatives (and followers in supplication) and 'Sahb' as all believing companions, affirming that all companions are trustworthy without exception.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you send blessings upon the Prophet and his family, do you consciously include all believing followers, or do you limit it to blood relatives? How does this definition expand your love and connection to the entire Ummah? Commit to including all believers in your supplication today.
?
The passage asserts that every single Companion is trustworthy without exception. Is there any Companion whose narration you secretly doubt? Examine your heart: do you fully accept this principle, or have you been influenced by criticisms that contradict this settled doctrine? Resolve to uphold the honor of the Companions in your speech and belief.
?
Knowing that the Prophet's family includes those who believed and followed him, not merely by lineage, how will you increase your love and emulation of the pious from his family? What specific actions can you take to align yourself with them?

The Virtue of Abu Bakr in the Cave
Commentary explaining the phrase 'especially his companion in the cave' and narrating the cave incident.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the commentator highlights Abu Bakr's presence in the cave as a proof of his supreme virtue among the companions.

Core Idea
The cave story reveals Abu Bakr's unique status as the Prophet's companion in times of trial.

This passage from the commentary explains a line of the poem that refers to 'his companion in the cave.' The commentator identifies that companion as Abu Bakr as-Siddiq and uses the dramatic story of the Hijrah to demonstrate why Abu Bakr is the best of the companions. As you read, notice how the narrative serves a theological purpose.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic and English to get the full story and commentary.
Pay attention to the opening phrase about superiority.
2
Identify the key claim
The commentator says Abu Bakr is the best of the companions. Find where that is stated.
It is in the phrase 'إذ هو أفضلهم على الإطلاق'.
3
Analyze the cave narrative
Notice how the story builds tension and how the Prophet's reassurance 'لا تحزن' is a divine sign.
4
Connect to the poem's purpose
Why does the poet specifically mention 'especially his companion in the cave'?
Think about the intended effect on the audience.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أفضلية الصديق لا سيما رفيقه عليه الصلاة والسلام في الغار. أي: غار جبل ثور، وهو سيدنا أبو بكر الصديق رضي الله عنه. خصه بالذكر مع دخوله في عموم الأصحاب: تنويها بعظم شأنه، إذ هو أفضلهم على الإطلاق. والغار: هو ثقب في جبل ثور بالمسفلة في طريق مكة إلى اليمن على نحو ساعة بالمشي على القدم دخله رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم وأبو بكر حين خرجا مهاجرين من مكة إلى المدينة المنورة، ومكثا فيه ثلاثة أيام، وذهب المشركون في طلبهما، واقتفوا أثرهما حتى جاءوا إلى الغار، فانقطع الأثر، فجعلوا يفتشون حتى قال بعضهم: انظروا الغار، فقالوا: ليس في الغار أحد، ولو نظروا أدنى نظرة لرأوهما، فاشتد الكرب على أبي بكر رضي الله عنه خوفًا على رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم، وقال: إنهم لو نظروا تحت أقدامهم لرأونا، فقال النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم: لا تحزن إن الله معنا.
The superiority of as-Siddiq, especially his companion (peace and blessings be upon him) in the cave. That is: the cave of Mount Thawr, and he is our master Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him). He singled him out for mention despite his inclusion in the generality of the companions: to highlight his great stature, for he is the best of them absolutely. The cave is: an aperture in Mount Thawr at al-Musfalah on the road from Mecca to Yemen, about an hour's walk on foot. The Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings be upon him) and Abu Bakr entered it when they set out emigrating from Mecca to Medina, and they stayed in it for three days. The polytheists went in search of them and followed their tracks until they came to the cave, and the tracks were cut off. They began searching until one of them said: 'Look into the cave.' They said: 'There is no one in the cave.' If they had looked the least look, they would have seen them. The distress intensified upon Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him) out of fear for the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings be upon him), and he said: 'If they were to look under their feet, they would see us.' The Prophet (peace and blessings be upon him) said: 'Do not grieve; indeed Allah is with us.'
Source pages p. 23

Commentary
This passage explains the phrase 'لا سيما رفيقه في الغار' (especially his companion in the cave) from the poem. The commentator first identifies the companion as Abu Bakr as-Siddiq, then explains why he is singled out: even though he is already included among the companions, his special mention is to highlight his supreme virtue, as he is the best of the companions absolutely. The passage then provides a vivid narrative of the cave incident: the cave of Mount Thawr, the Hijrah, the polytheists' pursuit, the miraculous cutting off of tracks, the search that missed them despite proximity, and Abu Bakr's fear and the Prophet's reassurance with the verse 'لا تحزن إن الله معنا'. The story serves as a proof of Abu Bakr's unparalleled status and divine protection.

Key Terms
الصديق
أبو بكر
الغار
جبل ثور
الهجرة
لا تحزن

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The cave story is just a historical anecdote with no theological significance.
Correction: The commentator uses the cave story to establish Abu Bakr's doctrinal superiority as the best companion and to show divine protection and approval.

Checkpoint
Why does the commentator say Abu Bakr was singled out for mention despite being included in the general companions?
A) Because he was the only one with the Prophet in the cave.
B) To highlight his great stature as the best of the companions.
C) Because the poem required a specific name.
D) To show that he was more afraid than others.

Key Takeaway
The cave incident is not just a historical event but a proof of Abu Bakr's unparalleled status as the Prophet's companion and his supreme virtue among the companions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you find yourself in a situation of extreme fear or anxiety, do you truly believe that 'Allah is with you'? What would it take for you to let go of panic and trust in His protection like the Prophet did?
?
Abu Bakr's fear was not for himself but for the Prophet. In your moments of worry, is your concern primarily for yourself, or for those you love? How can you cultivate a heart that prioritizes the well-being of others over your own comfort?
?
The commentator affirms that Abu Bakr is the best of the companions absolutely. Do you accept this with full conviction, or do you find yourself hesitant? What is the source of your hesitation, and how can you align your heart with the consensus of Ahl al-Sunna?
