The Excellence of Abu Bakr: Companion in the Cave
Explains the virtue of Abu Bakr through the cave event and Quranic proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Abu Bakr is uniquely honored as the Prophet's companion in the cave, how the Qur'an affirms this, and why denying it amounts to disbelief.

Core Idea
Denying Abu Bakr's companionship in the cave is denying the Qur'an.

This passage unpacks a line from the poem that singles out Abu Bakr as 'the companion in the cave.' The commentator narrates the dramatic hijrah story and emphasizes that this companionship is not merely historical but is firmly established in the Qur'an. Consequently, rejecting this event means rejecting a Qur'anic truth, which is an act of disbelief.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the full Arabic passage once, then read the translation. Notice the flow from poem citation to commentary to story to doctrinal conclusion.
Pay attention to the use of Quran 9:40.
2
Identify the purpose of singling out Abu Bakr
The commentator explains that Abu Bakr is mentioned specifically, though already included among the companions, to highlight his supreme status. Find the phrase that states he is 'the most excellent of them absolutely.'
3
Understand the cave incident and the miracle
Read the story of the hijrah: how the polytheists searched but were miraculously prevented from seeing them. Note Abu Bakr's fear and the Prophet's reassurance.
Allah blinded both their physical and spiritual sight.
4
Grasp the Qur'anic proof and its implication
The passage cites Quran 9:40 as direct evidence of Abu Bakr's companionship. It then states that denying this companionship is denying the Qur'an, which constitutes disbelief.
This is a serious doctrinal point, not just a historical fact.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وَآلِهِ وَصَحْبِهِ الأطْهَارِ * لَا سِيَّمَا رَفِيقِهِ فِي الغَارِ
شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي
أي: غار جبل ثور، وهو سيدنا أبو بكر الصديق رضي اللهُ عَنْهُ. خَصَّهُ بالذكر مع دخوله في عموم الأصحاب: تنويها بعظم شأنه، إذ هو أفضلهم على الإطلاق.
والغار: هو ثقب في جبل ثور بالمسفلة في طريق مكة إلى اليمن على نحو ساعة بالمشي على القَدَم دخله رسول الله صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ وأبو بكر حين خرجا مهاجرين من مكة إلى المدينة المنورة، ومكثا فيه ثلاثة أيام، وذهب المشركون في طلبهما، واقتفوا أثرهما حتى جاءوا إلى الغار، فانقطع الأثر، فجعلوا يفتشون حتى قال بعضهم: انظروا الغار»، فقالوا: ليس في الغار أحد»، ولو نظروا أدنى نظرة لرأوهما، فاشتد الكرب على أبي بكر رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُ خوفًا على رسول الله صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ، وقال: «إنهم لو نظروا تحت أقدامهم لرأونا»، فقال النبي صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ: «لا تحزن إن الله معنا» (۱).
فأعمى الله أبصارهم كما أعمى بصائرهم، ومزيّة هذا الغار لم تكن لغير هذا الصديق انفرد بها عن سائر الصحب الكرام، فهو صاحبه في الغار، كما هو صاحبه في جميع مراحل الحياة، وفي البرزخ وما بعده. وأثبت القرآن صحبته بذكر يُتْلَي إِذْ يَقُولُ لِصَحِيهِ، لَا تَحْزَنْ إِنَّ اللهَ مَعَنا (۱) ، فمن أنكرها فقد أنكر القرآن، ومن أنكره أو شيئًا منه فقد كفر.
(۱) سورة التوبة، الآية (٤٠).
And his pure family and companions, especially his companion in the cave. Explanation by Sheikh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashhat al-Makki al-Maliki: i.e. the cave of Mount Thawr, and he is our master Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, may Allah be pleased with him. He singled him out for mention while he is included in the generality of the companions: to honor his great status, for he is the most excellent of them absolutely. And the cave: it is a hole in Mount Thawr in the lower part on the road from Mecca to Yemen, about an hour's walking on foot. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ and Abu Bakr entered it when they went out as emigrants from Mecca to Medina, and they stayed in it three days. The polytheists went in search of them, and they followed their tracks until they came to the cave, and the tracks stopped. They began to search until one of them said: 'Look at the cave.' They said: 'There is no one in the cave.' And if they had looked any look they would have seen them. So the distress intensified for Abu Bakr, may Allah be pleased with him, out of fear for the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, and he said: 'If they looked under their feet they would see us.' So the Prophet ﷺ said: 'Do not grieve, indeed Allah is with us.' (1) So Allah blinded their sights as He blinded their insights. And the merit of this cave was not for other than this Siddiq; he was singled out by it from the rest of the noble companions. Thus he is his companion in the cave, just as he is his companion in all stages of life, and in the Barzakh and after it. And the Qur'an established his companionship with a recited mention: when he says to his companion: 'Do not grieve, indeed Allah is with us.' (1) So whoever denies it has denied the Qur'an, and whoever denies it or anything from it has disbelieved. (1) Surah at-Tawbah, verse 40.
Source pages p. 24

Commentary
This passage explains the line 'لاسيما رفيقه في الغار' (especially his companion in the cave) from the poem. The commentator identifies the companion as Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, noting that he is singled out for mention despite being included among all companions, to honor his supreme status as the most excellent of them. The passage then narrates the story of the hijrah: the Prophet and Abu Bakr hid in the cave of Mount Thawr for three days, the polytheists searched but were miraculously prevented from seeing them, and Abu Bakr's fear was calmed by the Prophet's words 'لا تحزن إن الله معنا' (Do not grieve, indeed Allah is with us). The commentator adds that Allah blinded the polytheists' physical and spiritual sight. He emphasizes that this distinction belongs exclusively to Abu Bakr, making him the Prophet's companion in the cave as in all stages of life and in the afterlife. Crucially, the Qur'an itself attests to this companionship in a recited verse (Quran 9:40). Therefore, denying this companionship is tantamount to denying the Qur'an, and denying any part of the Qur'an constitutes disbelief. The passage thus elevates the historical event to a matter of faith.

Key Terms
أبو بكر الصديق
الغار
صحبته
القرآن

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think the cave incident is just an interesting historical event without doctrinal weight.
Correction: The passage makes clear that the Qur'an itself affirms Abu Bakr as the companion, so denying it is denying the Qur'an and constitutes disbelief.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the consequence of denying that Abu Bakr was the Prophet's companion in the cave?

B) It is disbelief because it denies the Quran
C) It is a historical error
D) It is an acceptable difference of opinion

Key Takeaway
The companionship of Abu Bakr in the cave is divinely attested in the Qur'an, and denying it constitutes disbelief.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read that denying Abu Bakr's companionship is kufr, do you feel a surge of conviction or a subtle hesitation? Examine your heart: is there any part of your belief in the Qur'an that you treat as negotiable? Will you today renew your commitment to accept every verse of the Qur'an without reservation, even when it challenges your pride or ignorance?
?
The Prophet comforted Abu Bakr with 'Do not grieve, Allah is with us.' Are you living as if that promise applies to you right now in your current struggle? What grief or fear are you clutching onto that prevents you from leaning fully into that divine companionship? What would it mean for you to truly believe that Allah is with you in this moment?
?
If someone were to ask you to defend the belief that Abu Bakr is the companion in the cave, could you do so with the same certainty that the Qur'an is the word of Allah? Or do you rely on hearsay? This lesson ties your acceptance of a historical fact to your iman. Do you internalize that your faith is tested by such details? Will you take the time to memorize the Arabic verse and the reasoning so that you can stand firm when questioned?

Jubba'i and Ash'ari's Conversion
The author gives a brief biography of Abu Ali al-Jubba'i and notes Ash'ari's student-teacher relationship and conversion.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the biographical background of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, his early tutelage under Abu Ali al-Jubba'i, and his subsequent return to the Sunni creed.

Core Idea
Ash'ari was once a Mu'tazili student before his conversion to Sunni Islam.

This passage from al-Bahja al-Saniyya provides a brief biographical note on two key figures: the Mu'tazili theologian Abu Ali al-Jubba'i and the renowned Sunni theologian Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari. It highlights Ash'ari's early association with Jubba'i and his eventual rejection of Mu'tazili doctrine in favor of the creed of the Companions. Understanding this background helps clarify the emergence of Ash'ari theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic carefully along with the literal translation. Note the flow of the narrative, focusing on the sequence of scholars.
The beginning mentions Jubba'i coming after Wasil, establishing a chain.
2
Identify the key figures and their relationship
The passage states that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was a student of Abu Ali al-Jubba'i in his youth. Recognize that this is a teacher-student relationship with a significant conversion.
Ash'ari is known for eventually refuting his teacher's doctrines.
3
Examine Ash'ari's full lineage
The author provides the complete lineage of Ash'ari tracing back to the Companion Abu Musa al-Ash'ari. This underscores his noble ancestry and connection to the early Muslim community.
This lineage is important for establishing his credibility.
4
Understand the misconception addressed
This passage guards against the misconception that Ash'ari remained a Mu'tazili. Instead, it emphasizes his clear return to the Sunni creed after realizing the corruption of Mu'tazilism.
The commentary highlights this refutation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وجاء بعد واصل أبو على الجُبَّائي (۱)، وكان أبو الحسن الأشعري في صغره تلميذا له في العقائد إلى أن ظهر له فساد مذهبه فرجع إلى ما عليه الجماعة من الصحابة والتابعين، وتلقاه منهم بالقبول أئمة الدين. وأبو الحسن هو علي بن إسماعيل بن أبي بشر، واسمه: إسحاق ابن سالم بن إسماعيل بن عبد الله بن موسى بن بلال بن أبي بردة بن أبي موسى الأشعري الصحابي. (۱) أبو على الجُبَّائي: هو محمد بن عبد الوهاب البصري، ولد سنة (٢٣٥هـ)، من أئمة المعتزلة، ورئيس علماء الكلام في عصره، وإليه تنسب الطائفة (الجُبَّائية)، له مقالات وآراء تفرد بها في المذهب، وله كتاب «الأصول»، و«النهي عن المنكر»، و«الاجتهاد»، و«الأسماء والصفات»، ردّ عليه الأشعري، توفي سنة (۳۰۳هـ). ترجمته من: سير أعلام النبلاء» (١٨٣/١٤)، و«الأعلام» (٢٥٦/٦).
And after Wasil came Abu Ali al-Jubba'i (1). Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari in his youth was his student in theology until the corruption of his doctrine became clear to him, so he returned to what the Community (jamāʿa) of the Companions and Followers were upon, and the imams of the religion received it from him with acceptance. Abu al-Hasan is Ali ibn Isma'il ibn Abi Bishr, and his name is Ishaq ibn Salim ibn Isma'il ibn Abd Allah ibn Musa ibn Bilal ibn Abi Burda ibn Abi Musa al-Ash'ari the Companion. (1) Abu Ali al-Jubba'i: he is Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab al-Basri, born in the year 235 AH, one of the imams of the Mu'tazila, and the chief of the theologians of his time. To him is attributed the sect (al-Jubba'iyya). He had articles and opinions unique to him in the school, and he has the books al-Usul, al-Nahy 'an al-Munkar, al-Ijtihad, and al-Asma' wa al-Sifat. Al-Ash'ari refuted him. He died in the year 303 AH. His biography is from Siyar A'lam al-Nubala' (14/183) and al-A'lam (6/256).
Source pages p. 29

Commentary
This passage provides a biographical note about the succession of scholars after Wasil. It mentions Abu Ali al-Jubba'i, a leading Mu'tazili theologian, and notes that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was initially his student. However, Ash'ari later saw the corruption of the Mu'tazili doctrine and returned to the creed of the Companions and Followers (the Ahl al-Sunna). The author then gives the full lineage of Ash'ari back to the Companion Abu Musa al-Ash'ari. The footnote gives further details about Jubba'i's life and works, including his birth, death, and major writings, and notes that Ash'ari refuted him.

أبو الحسن الأشعري
الجُبَّائية

Correction: The passage clearly states that Ash'ari returned to the Sunni creed after realizing the corruption of Mu'tazili doctrine.

Key Takeaway

?
The text says Ash'ari returned 'to what the Community of Companions and Followers were upon.' Are you truly aligned with that same creed, or have you adopted ideas that contradict it? Before tonight, pick one belief you hold and compare it directly to what the Companions and Followers were upon—are you standing with them or with something else?

Rational Judgments
beginner
4 min
The Three Rational Judgments and Their Application
The poet enumerates the three rational judgments and states the obligation to know them in God and prophets.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three rational judgments (wujub, istihala, jawaz) and why it is obligatory to know them as applied to Allah and His messengers.

Core Idea
Knowing Allah requires knowing what is necessary, impossible, and permissible regarding Him.

The poet lays out the basic categories of rational judgment that underpin all theological reasoning. These three categories — necessity, impossibility, and permissibility — are not abstract; they directly determine what we can affirm or deny about Allah and His prophets. This passage introduces them and establishes the obligation to know them.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic poem carefully, then its literal translation. Let the structure and rhythm sink in before analyzing.
The poem uses rhyme and meter; pay attention to key terms.
2
Identify the three categories
Find the three divisions of rational judgment: wujub (necessity), istihala (impossibility), jawaz (permissibility). Notice they are listed explicitly.
3
Understand the obligation
The poet states that knowing these categories in relation to Allah and the prophets is legally obligatory (wajib shar'an). Reflect on why this knowledge is essential for a mukallaf.
The same three categories apply to the prophets.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أَقْسَامُ حُكْمِ العَقْلِ لا مَحَالَةَ * هِيَ : الوُجُوبُ، ثُمّ الاسْتِحَالَةُ
ثُمَّ الجَوَازُ، ثَالِثُ الأَقْسَامِ * فَافْهَمْ مَحْضَ لَذَّةِ الأَنَامِ
وَوَاجِبٌ شَرْعًا عَلَى الْمُكَلَّفِ * مَعْرِفَةُ اللَّهِ العَلِيِّ فَاعْرِفِ
أَيْ: يَعْرِفُ الوَاجِبَ وَالْمُحَالا * مَعَ جَائِزٍ فِي حَقِّهِ تَعَالَى
وَمِثْلُ ذَا فِي حَقٍّ رُسُلِ اللهِ * عَلَيْهِم تَحِيَّةُ الإِلَهِ
The divisions of the judgment of reason, necessarily, are: necessity (al-wujub), then impossibility (al-istihala), then permissibility (al-jawaz), the third division. So understand the pure pleasure of mankind. And it is legally obligatory upon the legally responsible person (al-mukallaf) to know Allah, the High, so know [that]; meaning: he knows the necessary and the impossible along with the permissible regarding Him, Exalted. And the like of this regarding the right of the messengers of God, upon them be the greeting of God.
Source pages p. 14

Commentary
This passage presents the three rational judgments (aḥkām al-ʿaql) that are the foundation of theological reasoning: necessity (wujūb), impossibility (istiḥāla), and permissibility (jawāz). The poet then states that it is a legal obligation (wājib sharʿan) upon every responsible person (mukallaf) to know God, which entails knowing these three categories as they apply to God: what is necessary for Him, what is impossible for Him, and what is permissible for Him. The same obligation applies to knowing the messengers of God, i.e., one must know what is necessary, impossible, and permissible regarding them.

Key Terms
الوجوب
الاستحالة
الجواز
المكلف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that the three rational judgments apply only to God and not to the prophets.
Correction: The text explicitly extends the same obligation to the prophets: 'And the like of this regarding the right of the messengers of God.' Thus, one must know the necessary, impossible, and permissible concerning them as well.

Checkpoint
What is legally obligatory (wājib sharʿan) upon the mukallaf according to this passage?
A) To memorize the names of the prophets.
B) To know the three rational judgments in relation to God and the prophets.
C) To engage in theological debate.
D) To learn the Arabic language.

Key Takeaway
The three rational judgments are the essential categories for knowing what must be, what cannot be, and what may be attributed to Allah and His messengers.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly know what is necessary, impossible, and permissible regarding Allah and His messengers, or have you been content with a vague belief that hasn't been articulated? What specific step will you take today to transform this knowledge from an abstraction into a living certainty?
?
The poet calls the knowledge of these three categories 'the pure pleasure of mankind'. When did you last experience intellectual delight in contemplating the logical perfection of Allah's being? If never, will you pause now to sense the beauty of a necessity that has no possibility of non-existence?
?
You bear a legal obligation to know these judgments about the prophets. Has your heart ever wavered at a prophetic report because you doubted their truthfulness? How does knowing that lying is impossible for them compel you to submit to their message without reservation?

Module 6 · Lesson 2

Rational Judgments
beginner
4 min
Definitions of Wajib, Mustahil, and Ja'iz
The poet defines the three rational judgments in terms of essence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the precise definitions of the three rational judgments (wajib, mustahil, ja'iz) as they relate to essence.

Core Idea
The three rational judgments are defined by whether essence can accept existence or non-existence.

This lesson establishes the foundational categories of rational judgment in Islamic theology, distinguishing between what is necessary, impossible, and possible in terms of essence. The poet provides crisp definitions that serve as the basic grammar for all subsequent theological reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the poetic definitions
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Notice the rhyme and rhythm. Then read the literal translation to understand the meaning.
The passage uses poetic meter, so some words are shortened.
2
Understand al-wājib al-ʿaqli
The first line defines the rationally necessary: that which does not accept non-existence in its essence. This means its existence is intrinsic and cannot be negated.
The phrase 'fa-ibtahil' (so supplicate) is a poetic exhortation to God.
3
Understand al-mustaḥīl
The second line defines the rationally impossible: that which does not accept existence in its essence. This is the opposite of the necessary.
4
Understand al-jāʾiz
The third line defines the rationally permissible: that which accepts both existence and non-existence in its essence. Neither is necessary or impossible.
5
Reflect on essence-based definitions
Notice that these definitions are absolute and based on the essence itself, not on external factors. This is key for applying them to Allah and creation.
This distinction prevents relativism in theological reasoning.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فَالوَاجِبُ العَقْلِيُّ: مَا لَمْ يَقْبَلْ * الانْتِفَا فِي ذَاتِهِ فَابْتَهِلْ
وَالْمُسْتَحِيلُ: كُلُّ مَا لَمْ يَقْبَلْ * فِي ذَاتِهِ الثُّبُوتَ ضِدُّ الأَوَّلِ
وَكُلُّ أَمْرٍ قَابِلٍ لِلانْتِفَا * وَلِلثُّبُوتِ جَائِزٌ بِلا خَفَا
So the rational necessary (al-wājib al-ʿaqliyy) is that which does not accept non-existence (al-intifā) in its essence, so supplicate. And the impossible (al-mustaḥīl) is everything that does not accept existence (al-thubūt) in its essence, the opposite of the first. And every matter that accepts non-existence and existence is permissible (jāʾiz) without concealment.
Source pages p. 14

Commentary
This passage provides the precise definitions of the three rational judgments as they relate to essence. The rational necessary (al-wājib al-ʿaqli) is defined as that which cannot be conceived as absent from its essence, i.e., its non-existence is impossible. The impossible (al-mustaḥīl) is the opposite: that which cannot be conceived as existing in its essence, i.e., its existence is impossible. The permissible (al-jāʾiz) is that which can be conceived as either existing or not existing in its essence, i.e., neither existence nor non-existence is necessary or impossible. These definitions are the foundational tools for reasoning about God and creation.

Key Terms
الواجب العقلي
المستحيل
الجائز
الانتفا
الثبوت

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that 'wājib' and 'mustaḥīl' are relative to something else, but the definitions here are absolute based on essence.
Correction: The definitions explicitly refer to the essence itself, not to external factors. For example, the necessary is that which does not accept non-existence in its essence, meaning its existence is absolute, not dependent on context.

Checkpoint
According to the definition, what is al-mustaḥīl (the rationally impossible)?
A) What does not accept non-existence in its essence.
B) What does not accept existence in its essence.
C) What accepts both existence and non-existence.
D) What is required by law.

Key Takeaway
The three rational judgments are absolute, essence-based categories: necessary (cannot be absent), impossible (cannot exist), and permissible (can be either).

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that God's existence is rationally necessary and cannot be denied, what would you change about how you prioritize your relationship with Him today?
?
Think of something in your life—a relationship, a possession, a status—that you treat as if it cannot be lost (wājib). Where have you mistakenly invested the certainty that belongs only to God?
?
Your entire life is in the category of jā'iz: it could have not existed, and every moment it continues by God's permission. How would you live differently if you felt the full weight of that contingency and gratitude?

The Three Rational Judgments and Their Definitions
Sanusi classifies rational judgments into wajib, mustahil, ja'iz and defines each.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three rational judgments (necessary, impossible, possible) and their definitions as foundational for kalam.

Core Idea
The three rational judgments form the basic grammar of theological reasoning.

Sanusi begins by laying the conceptual groundwork for all theological discourse. Before we can reason about Allah and His messengers, we need a shared vocabulary of what is rationally necessary, impossible, and possible. This passage introduces those three categories and defines each one.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Sanusi uses a poetic, rhyming style to convey these foundational concepts.
2
Identify the three categories
The passage presents three rational judgments: necessary (wujub), impossible (istihala), and possible (jawaz). Find each one and notice how the author defines them later.
3
Study the definitions
Sanusi defines each judgment: the necessary is what cannot not exist, the impossible is what cannot exist, and the possible is what can exist or not. Connect each definition to its name.
The word 'النُّبُوتَ' in the definition of mustahil is unusual but means 'existence' here.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أَقْسَامُ حُكْمِ العَقْلِ لا مَحَالَهُ * هِي : الوُجُوبُ، ثُمّ الاسْتِحَالَة * ثُمَّ الْجَوازُ، ثَالِثُ الأَقْسَامِ * فَافْهَمْ مُحْتَ لَذَّةَ الأَنْهَامِ * ووَاجِبٌ شَرْعًا على الْمُكَلَّفَ * مَعرِفَةُ اللَّهِ العَلِيِّ فاعْرِفِ * أَيْ: يَعرِفُ الواجِبَ والْمُحَالا * مَعْ جَائِز فِي حَقِّهِ تَعَالَى * ومِثْلُ ذَا فِي حَقٌّ رُسُلِ اللهِ * عَليهِم تَحِيَّةُ الإِلَهِ * فَالوَاجِبُ العقليُّ: مَا لَم يَقبَل * الانْتِفَا فِي ذاتِه فَابْتَهل * والْمُسْتَحِيلُ: كُلُّ مَا لَم يَقْبَلَ * فِي ذَاتِهِ النُّبُوتَ ضِدُّ الأَوَّلِ * وكُلُّ أَمْرٍ قَابِل لِلانْتِفَاً * وَلِلنُّبُوتِ جَائِز بِلا خَفَا
The divisions of the judgment of reason inevitably are: necessity (al-wujub), then impossibility (al-istihala), then possibility (al-jawaz), the third division. So understand, filled with the delight of minds. And it is legally obligatory upon the morally responsible person to know Allah the High; know that. That is: to know the necessary and the impossible together with the possible with respect to Him, exalted. And likewise regarding the messengers of Allah, upon them the greeting of Allah. So the rational necessary (al-wajib al-'aqli) is: what does not accept nullification (al-intifa') in its essence, so be devoted. And the impossible (al-mustahil) is: everything that does not accept in its essence 'al-nubut', the opposite of the first. And every matter that accepts nullification and 'al-nubut' is possible, without concealment.
Source pages p. 14

Commentary
This passage classifies the three rational judgments (ahkam al-'aql) that are foundational in kalam. The author states the three divisions: necessary (wujub), impossible (istihala), and possible (jawaz). He then explains that it is a legal obligation for the mukallaf to know these judgments with respect to Allah and His messengers. Then he defines each: The rationally necessary (al-wajib al-'aqli) is what does not accept non-existence (al-intifa') in its essence; it must exist. The rationally impossible (al-mustahil) is what does not accept existence in its essence; it cannot exist. The rationally possible (al-ja'iz) is what accepts both existence and non-existence. Note: The word 'al-nubut' in the definition of mustahil is unusual; in standard kalam it is 'al-'adam' for non-existence or 'al-wujud' for existence. The author uses 'al-nubut' which might be a rhyme variant or a corruption. The sense is clear: the impossible is that which cannot exist.

Key Terms
حكم العقل
الوجوب
الاستحالة
الجواز
الواجب العقلي
المستحيل
الجائز

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The rational judgment 'possible' means weakly possible or improbable.
Correction: In kalam, 'possible' (ja'iz) means logically contingent—that which accepts both existence and non-existence without contradiction, not a measure of likelihood.

Checkpoint
What is the definition of the rationally necessary (al-wajib al-'aqli)?
A) What accepts non-existence in its essence
B) What does not accept existence in its essence
C) What does not accept non-existence in its essence
D) What accepts both existence and non-existence

Key Takeaway
The three rational judgments are necessary, impossible, and possible; each has a distinct logical status that forms the basis of all theological reasoning.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If knowing Allah is a rational necessity (wajib) according to this lesson, what does that say about the state of your own conviction? Are you treating the knowledge of Allah as an optional extra in your life?
?
The lesson states that it is legally obligatory upon the mukallaf to know the necessary, impossible, and possible with respect to Allah. When did you last actively reflect on these categories in your personal relationship with Allah? Will you, right now, pause and ask yourself: Do I truly believe it is impossible for Allah to be unjust?
?
Every matter that accepts both existence and non-existence is 'possible' (ja'iz) — including your own life, your health, your wealth. Does your daily anxiety reflect that you believe these are contingent on Allah's will, or do you act as if they are necessary (wajib) and permanent?

Rational Judgments
beginner
4 min
The Three Rational Judgments: Wajib, Mustahil, Jaiz
Defines the three rational judgments and explains each

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three rational judgments (wajib, mustahil, jaiz) and their definitions, and why they are foundational to belief in Allah and His messengers.

Core Idea
The three rational judgments are the exhaustive categories for all that is conceivable.

This lesson introduces the foundational classification of rational judgments in Islamic theology. Imam al-Sanusi's poetic lines lay out the three categories that reason inevitably recognizes: the necessarily existent, the impossible, and the possible. The commentary explains each judgment succinctly, stressing that understanding these is essential for proper faith. Let us examine the passage and its explanation.

Guided Path
1
Read the poetic line and its explanation
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that the poetic line emphasizes inevitability ('la mahalah').
2
Identify the three judgments and their definitions
The passage presents three rational judgments: al-wujub, al-istihalah, and al-jawaz. Find each definition: wujub is 'adam qabul al-intifa' (non-acceptance of non-existence), istihalah is 'adam qabul al-thubut' (non-acceptance of existence), and jawaz is qabul al-thubut wa al-intifa' (acceptance of both existence and non-existence).
These definitions are mutually exclusive and exhaustive.
3
Understand why these three are exhaustive and necessary
The commentator explains that it is inevitable (la mahalah) that the divisions are exactly three, no less, no more. This is because any rational judgment about existence falls into one of these categories.
This is a logical necessity, not a theological opinion.
4
Reflect on the connection to faith
The commentator states that true understanding of these divisions is the basis for belief in Allah and His messengers. The address is to anyone who can understand, and the ultimate source of understanding is Allah.
The note on al-'aql defines the intellect and its role.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أقسام الحكم العقلي أَقْسَامُ حُكْمِ العَقْلِ لا مَحَالَهُ * هي: الوُجُوبُ، ثُمَّ الاسْتِحَالَة ثُمَّ الْجَوازُ، ثَالِثُ الأَقْسَامِ * فَافُهُمْ مُحْتَ لَذَّةَ الأَنْهَامِ قال الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي أقسام حكم العقل ثلاثة، فهو خبر محذوف لـ «أقسام»، يدل عليه ما يأتي، لا محالة) بفتح الميم، أي: لا حيلة ولا انفكاك من كونها ثلاثة الوجوب، والاستحالة، والجواز، لا أقل، ولا أكثر، كما قال: تعريف أقسام (هي الوجوب) وهو عدم قبول الانتفاء، ثم الاستحالة) بالدرج وحذف الهمزة للوزن، وهو عدم قبول الثبوت، (ثم الجواز) وهو (ثالث الأقسام) وهو قبول الثبوت والانتفاء. فافهم) هذه الأقسام الثلاثة حق معرفتها، فإن على معرفتها مدار الإيمان بالله تعالى ورسله عليهم الصلاة والسلام، وليس الخطاب مرادا به مُعَيّنًا، بل هو موجّه لكُلّ من يتأتّى منه الفهم، والمدار والمعوّل عليه هو الفهم حقا، وهو الفهم عن الله عَزَّوَجَلَّ، نسأل الله تعالى أن يرزقنا ذلك بمنه وكرمه، ولذا قال الناظم رحمه الله تعالى: (مُنحت) بالبناء للمفعول، أي: أعطيت (لَذَّة) أي: حلاوة (الأفهام) بفتح الهمزة جمع لفهم، أما بالكسرة فهو التفهم، وليس هنا بمراد. (العقل: سر رُوْحَاني، تُدْرِكُ به النفس العلوم الضرورية والنظرية، ومحله القلب، ونوره في الدماغ، وابتداؤه من حين نفخ الروح في الجنين، وأول كماله عند البلوغ. انظر شرح الخريدة» (ص١٤٤).
The divisions of rational judgment inevitably are: al-wujub (necessity), then al-istihalah (impossibility), then al-jawaz (possibility), the third division. So understand, you have been granted the delight of understandings. Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mishshāt al-Mālikī said: The divisions of rational judgment are three, it is the predicate of a deleted subject for 'aqsam indicated by what follows, inevitably (la mahala) with fatha on mim, i.e., no alternative and no separation from them being three: wujub, istihalah, jawaz, no less, no more, as he said: Definition of the divisions: (they are al-wujub) which is the non-acceptance of non-existence, then al-istihalah, by dropping the hamza for meter, which is the non-acceptance of existence, (then al-jawaz) which is (the third division) and it is the acceptance of both existence and non-existence. (So understand) these three divisions properly, for upon knowing them revolves belief in Allah and His messengers, peace be upon them, and the address is not intended for a specific person, but is directed to everyone who can understand, and the pivot and reliance is on true understanding, which is understanding from Allah, may He be glorified and exalted, we ask Allah to grant us that by His grace and generosity, and therefore the versifier, may Allah have mercy on him, said: (Munihat) passive voice, i.e., you were given (ladhdha) i.e., sweetness (al-afham) with fatha on hamza, plural of fahm, while with kasra it is tafahhum, and it is not intended here. (Al-‘aql: a spiritual secret by which the soul perceives necessary and theoretical sciences, and its place is the heart, and its light is in the brain, and its beginning is from the time the spirit is blown into the fetus, and its first perfection is at puberty. See Sharh al-Kharida (p.144).
Source pages p. 33

Commentary
This passage is a classification and definition of the three rational judgments (al-ahkam al-‘aqliyya). It begins with a poetic line stating that the divisions are inevitably three: al-wujub (necessity), al-istihalah (impossibility), and al-jawaz (possibility). The commentary then explains each: al-wujub is defined as non-acceptance of non-existence (i.e., the thing must exist), al-istihalah as non-acceptance of existence (i.e., the thing cannot exist), and al-jawaz as acceptance of both existence and non-existence (i.e., it is contingent). The commentator emphasizes that understanding these three divisions is fundamental to faith in Allah and His messengers, and that this understanding comes from Allah. The note on al-‘aql defines the intellect as a spiritual faculty that perceives necessary and theoretical knowledge, located in the heart but with its light in the brain, beginning at the blowing of the spirit and reaching perfection at puberty.

Key Terms
حكم عقلي
وجوب
استحالة
جواز
عقل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that the three rational judgments are merely conventional categories that could be fewer or more.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that they are inevitable (la mahalah), meaning they are logically exhaustive and necessary; reason itself imposes these three categories.

Checkpoint
What is the definition of al-istihalah according to this passage?
A) Non-acceptance of existence
B) Non-acceptance of non-existence
C) Acceptance of both existence and non-existence
D) Necessity of existence

Key Takeaway
The three rational judgments—wajib, mustahil, jaiz—are exhaustive and define all possible relations of reason to existence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Before this lesson, did you ever consider that your own existence is merely possible (jaiz)? If it is possible for you not to exist, then what should be your attitude toward the One who did not make you nonexistent? Will you now thank Him for the gift of existence and use it in obedience, or will you continue as if you had a right to exist?
?
The lesson says that upon knowing these three divisions revolves belief in Allah and His messengers. Do you truly know these divisions in your heart, or are they just terms you memorize? What would change in your daily life if you constantly reminded yourself that some things are impossible for Allah (like injustice) and some are necessary (like His mercy)? Will you start today to think of Allah's attributes in terms of wajib, mustahil, and jaiz?
?
The passage calls understanding from Allah a gift. Have you ever sincerely asked Allah to grant you true understanding of these rational judgments? If not, will you make a du'a tonight, humbly asking for it?

Rational Judgments
beginner
5 min
The Three Rational Judgments Defined
Sanusi enumerates the three rational judgments and the commentator defines each.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definitions of the three rational judgments (wajib, mustahil, ja'iz) and their foundational role in Islamic theology.

Core Idea
The three rational judgments are the basic categories for all theological reasoning.

Before making any claim about Allah or creation, we need a shared vocabulary for what is rationally necessary, impossible, or possible. This passage from Sanusi's poem, with its commentary, establishes these three categories and stresses that mastering them is the foundation of faith.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Recite the verse and commentary aloud if possible. Notice the concise style of the poem and the explanatory prose of the commentary.
The poem's line numbers are given in the text.
2
Identify the three judgments
From the commentary, extract the definitions of al-wajib, al-mustahil, and al-ja'iz. Write them in your own words.
Wajib: cannot be negated; Mustahil: cannot be affirmed; Ja'iz: can be either.
3
Understand the importance
The commentator says that upon knowing these three divisions revolves faith in Allah and His messengers. Reflect on why this classification is so central.
Think about how all theological claims fall into one of these categories.
4
Test your understanding
Try to classify simple statements: e.g., 'Allah exists' is wajib; 'Allah is ignorant' is mustahil; 'It will rain tomorrow' is ja'iz.
This connects the abstract definitions to concrete examples.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أَقْسَامُ حُكْمِ العَقْلِ لا مَحَالَهُ * هي: الوُجُوبُ، ثُمَّ الاسْتِحَالَة ۱۰ ثُمَّ الْجَوازُ، ثَالِثُ الأَقْسَامِ * فَافُهُمْ مُحْتَ لَذَّةَ الأَنْهَامِ

حد شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي

أقسام حكم العقل (۱)) ثلاثة، فهو خبر محذوف لـ «أقسام»، يدل عليه ما يأتي، لا محالة) بفتح الميم، أي: لا حيلة ولا انفكاك من كونها ثلاثة الوجوب، والاستحالة، والجواز، لا أقل، ولا أكثر، كما قال:

تعريف أقسام (هي الوجوب) وهو عدم قبول الانتفاء، ثم الاستحالة) بالدرج الحكم العقلي وحذف الهمزة للوزن، وهو عدم قبول الثبوت، (ثم الجواز) وهو (ثالث الأقسام) وهو قبول الثبوت والانتفاء.

فافهم) هذه الأقسام الثلاثة حق معرفتها، فإن على معرفتها مدار الإيمان بالله تعالى ورسله عليهم الصلاة والسلام، وليس الخطاب مرادا به مُعَيّنًا، بل هو موجّه لكُلّ من يتأتّى منه الفهم، والمدار والمعوّل عليه هو الفهم حقا، وهو الفهم عن الله عَزَّوَجَلَّ، نسأل الله تعالى أن يرزقنا ذلك بمنه وكرمه، ولذا قال الناظم رحمه الله تعالى: (مُنحت) بالبناء للمفعول، أي: أعطيت (لَذَّة) أي: حلاوة (الأفهام) بفتح الهمزة جمع لفهم، أما بالكسرة فهو التفهم، وليس هنا بمراد.
The divisions of rational judgment, necessarily, are: obligation, then impossibility 10, then possibility, the third of the divisions. So understand, you have been granted the pleasure of understandings. [Commentary:] The divisions of rational judgment (are) three; it is a predicate omitted for 'divisions', indicated by what comes, necessarily, with a fatha on the mim, i.e., no evasion and no detachment from it being three: obligation, impossibility, and possibility, no less and no more, as he said. Definition of the divisions (they are obligation), and it is the lack of acceptance of negation, then impossibility (with dropping the hamza for the meter), and it is the lack of acceptance of affirmation, (then possibility) and it is (the third of the divisions) and it is the acceptance of affirmation and negation. So understand these three divisions, truly knowing them, for upon their knowledge revolves faith in Allah Almighty and His messengers, peace be upon them, and the address is not meant for a particular person, but it is directed to everyone from whom understanding can be obtained, and the basis and reliance is on understanding truly, and it is understanding from Allah Almighty, we ask Allah Almighty to grant us that by His bounty and generosity. Thus the poet, may Allah have mercy on him, said: (I have been granted) in the passive voice, i.e., I was given (pleasure) i.e., sweetness (of understandings) with a fatha on the hamza, plural of fahm, but with a kasra it means tafahhum (deliberate understanding), and it is not intended here.
Source pages p. 33

Commentary
This passage defines the three rational judgments. The poet opens by stating that the divisions of rational judgment are necessarily three: wajib (obligatory), mustahil (impossible), and ja'iz (possible). The commentator explains each: wajib is that which cannot be negated (i.e., its existence is necessary), mustahil is that which cannot be affirmed (i.e., its existence is impossible), and ja'iz is that which can be both affirmed and negated (i.e., contingent). The commentator then emphasizes that understanding these three divisions is the foundation of faith in God and His messengers, and the address is to anyone who can comprehend. He then explains the poet's line 'you have been granted the pleasure of understandings' as referring to the sweetness of true knowledge.

Key Terms
العقل
الوجوب
الاستحالة
الجواز
الحكم العقلي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: These rational judgments are merely abstract philosophical categories with no practical religious significance.
Correction: The commentator explicitly states that upon knowing these three divisions revolves faith in Allah and His messengers, making them essential for correct theology.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is the definition of al-ja'iz (the possible)?
A) That which cannot be negated
B) That which cannot be affirmed
C) That which can be both affirmed and negated
D) That which is necessary

Key Takeaway
The three rational judgments — wajib, mustahil, and ja'iz — are the fundamental categories for all theological reasoning about Allah and creation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that Allah's existence is rationally necessary—that it is impossible for Him not to exist? If this certainty is real, how does it change the way you obey Him when no one is watching?
?
The text says that faith revolves on understanding these divisions. Have you ever examined whether your faith rests on rational conviction or on blind imitation (taqlid)? What would it take for you to move from inherited belief to personally affirmed certainty?
?
Your own existence is possible (ja'iz)—it could have been otherwise and can end at any moment. Does this contingency humble you? How would your day change if you lived with the conscious awareness that you are not necessary, but entirely dependent on the Necessary Being every moment?

Rational Judgments
beginner
4 min
Knowledge of the Three Divisions as Rational Judgment
Imam al-Haramayn equates knowledge of the three divisions with the rational judgment.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Imam al-Haramayn equates knowledge of the three rational categories (wajib, mustahil, ja'iz) with the very rational judgment itself.

Core Idea
Knowing the three rational judgments is what it means to be rational.

Imam al-Haramayn makes a bold claim: the rational judgment is not something separate from knowing these three categories; it is that very knowledge. In this passage, he emphasizes that a person who does not grasp these divisions cannot be considered truly rational. The author then explains that this knowledge can be in summary form, and it is based on understanding necessity, impossibility, and possibility.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Notice how Imam al-Haramayn's statement is direct and emphatic.
The passage uses 'فائدة' to introduce an important point.
2
Identify Imam al-Haramayn's claim
Find the sentence where he says knowledge of the three divisions is the rational judgment itself. Notice the phrase 'نفس الحكم العقلي'.
3
Understand the consequence
Imam al-Haramayn says that whoever does not know these divisions is not rational. Reflect on the seriousness of this statement.
4
Consider the author's explanation
The author clarifies that this knowledge can be summary, and it is based on knowing the necessity of the necessary, etc. Understand the link between these categories and intellect.
5
Reflect on the foundational importance
Ask yourself: Why is this passage placed at the beginning of a kalam text? How does it set the stage for all subsequent theological reasoning?
This connects to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فائدة * قال إمام الحرمين وجماعة إن معرفة هذه الأقسام الثلاثة (1) نفس الحكم العقلي، فمن لم يعرفها فليس بعاقل، والمراد بالمعرفة ولو إجمالا، كما في «حاشية» السيد محمد السباعي) يقول العبد الفقير عفا الله عنه: فينبغي الاعتناء بهذه الأقسام، والقول بأن معرفتها هي العقل بناء على أنه العلم بوجوب الواجبات وجواز الجائزات واستحالة المستحيلات.

(1) ( ص ٥٤ )
Benefit: Imam al-Haramayn and a group said: Verily, knowledge of these three divisions (1) is the very rational judgment. So whoever does not know them is not rational. And what is meant by knowledge is even if in summary, as in the 'Hashiyah' of al-Sayyid Muhammad al-Sibāʿī. The poor servant, may Allah pardon him, says: It is befitting to take care of these divisions, and the statement that knowledge of them is intellect is based upon it being the knowledge of the obligatoryness of the obligatory, the possible-ness of the possible, and the impossibility of the impossible. (1) (p. 54).
Source pages p. 34

Commentary
This passage presents a benefit from Imam al-Haramayn that knowledge of these three divisions (wajib, mustahil, ja'iz) is itself the rational judgment. So whoever does not know them is not rational. The author adds that this knowledge can be in summary form, and this is based on the idea that intellect is knowledge of the necessity of the necessary, the possibility of the possible, and the impossibility of the impossible.

Correction: Imam al-Haramayn states that knowledge of the three divisions is the very rational judgment; without it, one is not rational.

Checkpoint

A) Knowledge of the three divisions
B) Knowledge of God
C) Knowledge of the Prophet

Key Takeaway
The rational judgment is nothing other than the knowledge of the three categories: necessary, impossible, and possible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you consider yourself rational simply because you can reason about daily matters, or have you truly internalized the three categories—necessary, impossible, and possible—in your understanding of God? What would change if you consciously applied them in every thought about your faith?
?
You have now learned that true rationality is knowing the necessity of the necessary, the impossibility of the impossible, and the possibility of the possible. Examine your beliefs: Is there any attribute of God you implicitly treat as possible when it is actually necessary, or vice versa? How will you correct this starting today?
?
If you were asked to justify your belief in God to a skeptic using only these three categories, could you do it? What step will you take this week to make that knowledge so firm that it becomes second nature?

The Three Rational Judgments Are Rationality Itself
Mashat presents a benefit on the identity of rational judgment with its three divisions.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that knowledge of the three rational judgments (obligation, impossibility, permissibility) is itself rational judgment, and why this principle is foundational in kalam.

Core Idea
Knowing the three rational judgments is what it means to be rational.

This brief benefit from Imam al-Haramayn establishes a cornerstone of Islamic theology: there is no gap between rationality and knowing the three categories. Understanding this identity helps you see why kalam begins with these divisions — they are not optional knowledge but the very substance of rational thought.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage slowly, then the English translation. Notice the strong claim: knowledge of these three divisions is the rational judgment itself.
2
Understand the claim of identity
Imam al-Haramayn says that the three rational judgments are not separate from rational judgment — they are its very essence. Reflect on how this ties knowledge and rationality together.
The author adds that even general (ijmali) knowledge suffices.
3
Reflect on the implication
If someone does not know these divisions, they are not truly rational. Think about why this might be a strong statement and what it says about the importance of these categories in Islam.
This connects to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فائدة
قال إمام الحرمين وجماعة إن معرفة هذه الأقسام الثلاثة (1) نفس الحكم العقلي، فمن لم يعرفها فليس بعاقل، والمراد بالمعرفة ولو إجمالا، كما في «حاشية» السيد محمد السباعي) يقول العبد الفقير عفا الله عنه: فينبغي الاعتناء بهذه الأقسام، والقول بأن معرفتها هي العقل بناء على أنه العلم بوجوب الواجبات وجواز الجائزات واستحالة المستحيلات.
(1) ( ص ٥٤ )
Benefit. Imam al-Haramayn and a group said: Verily, knowledge of these three divisions is the very rational judgment itself. So whoever does not know them is not rational. And what is meant by knowledge is even if general, as in the 'Hashiyah' of Sayyid Muhammad al-Siba'i. The poor servant, may Allah pardon him, says: One should pay attention to these divisions. And the statement that knowledge of them is the intellect is based on the fact that it is knowledge of the obligation of obligations, the permissibility of permissible things, and the impossibility of impossible things.
Source pages p. 34

Commentary
This passage is a benefit (fa'idah) that conveys a key principle in kalam: the three rational judgments—obligation (wujub), impossibility (istihalah), and permissibility (jawaz)—are not merely known by the rational intellect; rather, knowing them is itself the rational judgment (al-hukm al-'aqli). The author quotes Imam al-Haramayn and others to assert that anyone who lacks this knowledge is not truly rational ('aql). The knowledge required is at least general (ijmalan). The author then emphasizes the importance of studying these divisions, explaining that identifying knowledge of them with the intellect means that the intellect is precisely the faculty that knows what is necessary, what is impossible, and what is permissible.

Key Terms
الأقسام الثلاثة
الحكم العقلي
وجوب
جواز
استحالة
العارف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that 'rational judgment' is separate from knowledge of these divisions.
Correction: The passage clarifies that knowledge of the three divisions is itself the rational judgment, not something derived from it.

Checkpoint
According to the benefit, what is the relationship between the three divisions and rational judgment?
A) The three divisions are derived from rational judgment.
B) Knowledge of the three divisions is itself the rational judgment.
C) Rational judgment is independent of the three divisions.
D) The three divisions are only known through revelation.

Key Takeaway
Knowledge of the three rational judgments is none other than rational judgment itself.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that without knowing the three rational judgments you are not rational? What does that say about your daily pursuit of knowledge?
?
If even a general understanding of these categories suffices for rationality, what stops you from acquiring that understanding today?
?
Reflect on a time you thought about God's actions. Did you consciously apply the judgments of obligation, impossibility, or permissibility, or did you let assumptions guide you? How will you change that starting now?

The Three Rational Categories as the First Obligation
Sanusi states the first obligation is knowledge of the three categories regarding God.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the first obligation upon every accountable Muslim is to know God by grasping what is necessary, impossible, and possible for Him.

Core Idea
Knowing God means knowing three categories: necessary, impossible, and possible.

This lesson introduces the foundational principle of Islamic theology: the first thing we must know about Allah is not His essence, but the rational categories that govern what can be said about Him. Sanusi explains that all obligations depend on this knowledge, and then defines it as knowledge of the wajib, muhal, and ja'iz. Through this passage, we learn both the content of our required knowledge and the method for obtaining it through mutawatir reports.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice that Sanusi begins by stating this knowledge is the first obligation.
The passage includes two footnotes that expand on the concept.
2
Identify the three categories
The passage defines knowledge of God as knowing what is necessary (wajib), impossible (muhal), and possible (ja'iz) regarding Him. These are the three rational judgments.
These categories are the backbone of kalam.
3
Understand why this is the first obligation
Sanusi says all other obligations depend on this knowledge. Reflect on why correct belief must precede action.
Without knowing what is necessary about Allah, worship might be misdirected.
4
Distinguish between types of knowledge
Footnote (1) distinguishes between knowledge from mutawatir (necessary) and from ahad reports (speculative). Understand that the obligation here is to have certain knowledge.
The hadith in footnote (2) shows the proper response to doubts about creation.
5
Apply the lesson
Consider how the three categories shape your understanding of Allah's attributes. Use them as a framework for faith.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وهذه المعرفة أول واجب على المكلّف، إذ جميع الواجبات متوقفة عليها، فمعرفة الله هنا عبارة عن معرفة ما يجب في حقه تعالى، وما يستحيل، وما يجوز، وهذا الذي نُكلّف به، أما غيره فلا نُكلّف به. ولذلك قال الناظم رحمه الله تعالى: (أي يعرف أي: معرفة الله؛ أن الأمور الواجب يعرف المكلف: (۱) وأما اعتقاد مشروعيتها وطلبها ، فهو علم ضروري، لأنه مستفاد من الخبر المتواتر. (۲) وهو ما حثنا عليه النبي صلى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ حين قال: «لا يَزالُ النَّاسُ يتسَاءَلُون حتى يقال: هذا خَلَقَ اللهُ الخَلَقَ فَمَنْ خلق الله ؟ فمن وجد من ذلك شيئًا، فليقل: آمنتُ بالله» رواه مسلم في صحيحه» (ح١٣٤) من حديث أبي هريرة رضي اللهُ عَنْهُ.
And this knowledge is the first obligation upon the accountable, since all obligations depend upon it. So knowledge of God here is an expression of knowing what is necessary (wajib) regarding Him, exalted, and what is impossible (muhal), and what is possible (ja'iz). And this is what we are tasked with; as for other than that, we are not tasked. Therefore, the versifier, may Allah have mercy on him, said: (i.e., he knows, meaning knowledge of God; that the matters the accountable must know: (1) And as for the belief in its legitimacy and its seeking, it is necessary knowledge because it is derived from the mutawatir report. (2) And it is what the Prophet ﷺ urged us with when he said: 'People will keep asking until it is said: This is Allah's creation, so who created Allah? Whoever finds anything of that should say: I believe in Allah.' Narrated by Muslim in his Sahih (hadith 134) from the hadith of Abu Hurayra, may Allah be pleased with him.
Source pages p. 36

Commentary
This passage explains what is actually required of the accountable person in terms of knowledge of God. The commentator states that this knowledge (ma'rifa) is the very first obligation, because all other obligations depend on it. The knowledge that is required is not the essence of God (which is impossible), but rather knowledge of three categories regarding God: what is necessary for Him (wajib), what is impossible for Him (muhal), and what is possible for Him (ja'iz). This tripartite division is the core of Islamic theology (kalam). The commentator then quotes the poet (the author of the original poem) who says that the mukallaf must know these three. The passage then includes two footnotes: the first clarifies that the belief in the legitimacy of something (like the Eid prayer) is not knowledge because it is not based on certain proof, but rather on isolated reports; however, if it is based on mutawatir (consensus-based) reports, it becomes necessary knowledge. The second footnote provides a hadith in which the Prophet instructs believers who are troubled by thoughts about the origin of creation to simply say 'I believe in Allah' – emphasizing the limitations of human inquiry into divine matters.

Key Terms
واجب
محال
جائز
أول واجب
معرفة الله
خبر متواتر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that knowing God means understanding His essence or nature.
Correction: The passage clearly states that the required knowledge is only of the three categories (wajib, muhal, ja'iz), not of His essence.

Checkpoint
What are the three categories that the mukallaf must know regarding God?
A) Wajib, muhal, ja'iz
B) Wajib, mustahabb, mubah
C) Qadim, hadith, mumkin
D) Tawhid, 'adl, nubuwwa

Key Takeaway
The first and most essential duty of every Muslim is to know Allah through the three rational judgments: necessary, impossible, and possible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever been content to simply believe in Allah without knowing what is necessary, impossible, and possible for Him? How might this gap affect the strength of your faith and your ability to defend it?
?
When you face doubt or intellectual challenges about your faith, do you immediately resort to 'I just believe' like the Prophet instructed, or do you also engage in understanding the rational categories? Which approach does this lesson prioritize, and how will you change?
?
Imagine someone asks you: 'Who created God?' Would you be able to explain that this question is invalid because it assumes a possible status for Allah that is actually impossible? How will you prepare to answer such questions after this lesson?

The Three Rational Categories for Allah and His Messengers
Sanusi's disciple defines the three rational categories for Allah and extends them to prophets.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three rational categories (wājib, muḥāl, jā'iz) as they apply to Allah and His messengers.

Core Idea
The three rational judgments define what must, cannot, or may be attributed to Allah.

This lesson establishes the foundational definitions of wājib, muḥāl, and jā'iz in relation to Allah. These three categories are the basic building blocks of theological reasoning in Sunni Islam. The passage also affirms that the same categories apply to prophets, preserving the coherence of divine and prophetic attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic text once without worrying about details. Let the poetic rhythm and the definitions sink in.
The first line is the matn (verse); the rest is commentary.
2
Identify the three definitions
Find the definitions of wājib, muḥāl, and jā'iz. Notice that each is defined in terms of existence (thubūt) and non-existence (intifā').
3
Extend to prophets
The passage ends by stating that the same categories apply to the messengers of Allah. Reflect on why this extension is important for theology.
It ensures that Allah's messengers are also described consistently.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أي: يَعرِفُ الواجِبَ والْمُحَالا * مَـعْ جَائِز فِي حَقِّهِ تَعَالَى ومِثْلُ ذَا فِي حَقُّ رُسُلِ اللَّهِ * عَليهِم تَحِيَّةُ الإِلَهِ
حد شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي
(الواجب) في حقه تعالى وهو الثابت له تعالى الذي لا يقبل الانتفاء (۱). والمحالا أي: المستحيل في حقه تعالى، وهو ما لا يقبل الثبوت والألف فيه للإطلاق (۲). (مع) معرفة (جائز في حقه تعالى) وهو ما يقبل الثبوت والانتفاء. ومثل ذا أي معرفة مثل ذا أي المذكور من الواجب وما عطف عليه، في حق رُسل الله) بإسكان السين عند تميم عملًا بقاعدة قوله: وكُلُّ فُعْل بسكون العين * كاليسر والعُشر ونحو أُذن فَضَمُّ عَيْنِهِ يُرَى اتْبَاهَا * لِفَائِهِ عَنْ أَسَد قَد شَاءَا وفعل كعُنُقِ وطنبِ تَسْكِينُهُ إِلى تَمِيم انسبِ عليهم) بتحريك الميم بالضم أو الكسر للوزن (تحيَّةُ الإله) تَبَارَكَ وَتَعَالَى.
(۱) الانتفاء هو العدم والزوال، فالواجب حينئذ هو الأمر الثابت الذي لا يقبل الزوال.
(٢) أي: إطلاق الصوت بالقافية.
That is: he knows the necessary and the impossible along with the possible regarding Him, Exalted is He, and likewise that regarding the messengers of Allah—upon them be the greeting of Allah. Commentary by Sheikh Ḥasan Muḥammad al-Mishshāt al-Makkī al-Mālikī: (The necessary) regarding Him, Exalted is He, is that which is established for Him, which does not admit of non-existence (1). (And the impossible) means the impossible regarding Him, Exalted is He, and it is that which does not admit of existence, and the alif here is for absolute [rhyme] (2). (Along with) knowing (the possible regarding Him, Exalted is He), and it is that which admits both existence and non-existence. And likewise that, i.e., knowing the same, i.e., the aforementioned necessary and what is conjoined with it, regarding the messengers of Allah—with the sīn made sākina according to Tamīm, acting on the rule of his saying: 'Every fu‘l with sukun of the 'ayn...' etc. —upon them (with the mīm voweled with ḍamma or kasra for the meter) (the greeting of Allah) Blessed and Exalted is He. (1) Non-existence is absence and removal; thus the necessary is the established matter that does not admit of removal. (2) That is, the releasing of the voice in rhyme.
Source pages p. 37

Commentary
This passage provides the technical definitions of the three rational judgments as applied to Allah and then extends them to prophets. The wājib is defined as that which cannot be absent from Him, the muḥāl as that which cannot exist for Him, and the jā'iz as that which can either exist or not exist for Him. The footnote clarifies that 'non-existence' means absence and removal. Then the passage states that the same categories apply to prophets, and it includes some grammatical notes about the recitation of the verse. The definitions are fundamental to the science of kalām.

Key Terms
واجب
محال
جائز
الانتفاء
الثبوت

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Theological categories are the same as logical possibility (e.g., anything not contradictory is possible).
Correction: In kalam, these categories are specific to what can be attributed to Allah and prophets, not general logical categories.

Checkpoint
What is the definition of 'al-muḥāl' regarding Allah?
A) What must exist
B) What cannot exist
C) What can exist or not
D) What is known through revelation

Key Takeaway
The three rational categories—wājib, muḥāl, and jā'iz—define what must, cannot, and may be attributed to Allah and His messengers respectively.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever, even silently, assumed something about Allah that is actually impossible for Him? How does knowing the category of muḥāl protect your faith from such misconceptions?
?
When you face trials, do you truly attribute to Allah the possibility (jā'iz) of both giving and withholding, or do you silently demand from Him what you think He must do?
?
The lesson applies these categories to messengers as well. Is your trust in the prophets consistent with affirming that they are free from both impossible and necessary human limitations? Where do you struggle to accept their complete truthfulness?

The Definition and Scope of the Intellectual Wajib
Definition and scope of intellectual wajib with attribute categories

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the precise definition of the intellectual wajib and how it comprehensively covers all categories of divine attributes.

Core Idea
The wajib is what cannot be non-existent, encompassing essence and all attributes.

The commentator begins by explaining the first of the three rational judgments: the wajib. He unpacks the concise poetic definition to show that it encompasses not just existence but all positive and negative attributes of Allah. This lesson establishes a foundational concept for kalam.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition
Read the Arabic line and commentary defining al-wajib al-'aqli. Note the phrase 'ما لم يقبل الانتفا في ذاته'.
The poet uses a shortened form 'الانتفا' for poetic meter.
2
Understand the scope
The commentator explains that the definition applies to essences, attributes, and relations. Find examples: essence of Allah, His existence, eternity, power.
3
Identify the categories included
List the categories of attributes included: salbiyya (negative), ma'ani (meanings), ma'nawiyya (relational), and nafsiyya (essential).
4
Notice what it excludes
The definition is designed to exclude mustahil. Ask yourself why mustahil does not fit.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فَالوَاجِبُ العقليُّ: مَا لَم يَقبَلِ * الانْتِفَا فِي ذاتِه فَابْتَهِل | ١٤ والْمُسْتَحِيلُ: كُلُّ مَا لَم يَقْبَل * فِي ذَاتِهِ النُّبُوتَ ضِدُّ الأَوَّلِ ١٥ شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي 2020 والمستحيل، والجائز التي تجب معرفتها فقال: (فالواجب العقلي من ذات أو صفة، أو نسبة كذات الله تعالى الواجب العقلي وكوجوده، وقدّمه، وكثبوت القدرة الله تعالى في قولنا: «الله قادر»، هو (ما) أي: الأمر الثابت الذي لم يقبل الانتفا) بالقصر للضرورة، أي: لا يقبل الزوال في ذاته، بقطع النظر عن علم الله تعالى وقدرته، وهذا التعريف يشمل صفات السلوب، وهي القدم، والبقاء، والمخالفة.... إلى آخره.. ويشمل صفات المعاني، كالقدرة والصفات المعنوية، ككونه تعالى قادرًا، والصفة النفسية، وهو الوجود، فلذلك كان هذا التعريف للواجب جامعا لأفراده كلها لشموله لصفات المعاني والمعنوية والسلبية، مانع من دخول الغير كالمستحيل (۱). (فابتهل بكسر اللام أي تضرع لله تعالى ليعطيك، ويصل بك إلى معرفة ما ينفعك، فإنك بهذا الخُلُقِ الشريف، وهو التضرع إلى الله تعالى، والخضوع بين يديه تصل إلى المقامات، وتفيض عليك من الله تعالى الفيوضات، أَهلنا الله تعالى لذلك. (والمستحيل) أي: تعريفه، هو (كل ما) أي: أمر من ذات، أو صفة الله المستحيل العقلي (۱) والواجب العقلي على قسمين ضروري وهو ما لا يتوقف على نظر واستدلال، كالتَّحَيُّز للجزم، أي: أخذه قدر ذاته من الفراغ. ونظري: وهو ما توقف على ما ذُكر، كالقِدَم لله تعالى، فكل منهما لا يقبل الانتفاء لذاته.
So the intellectual wajib: that which does not accept non-existence in its essence, so supplicate (14). And the mustaḥīl: everything that does not accept existence in its essence, the opposite of the first (15). Commentary of Sheikh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashshāt al-Makki al-Maliki 2020: And the mustaḥīl and the ja'iz which must be known, so he said: (So the intellectual wajib, from essence or attribute, or relation, like the essence of Allah, Most High, and His existence, and His eternity, and like the establishment of power for Allah, Most High, in our statement 'Allah is Powerful', is (mā) i.e., the established matter that has not accepted non-existence – with shortening for necessity – i.e., it does not accept removal in its essence, regardless of the knowledge of Allah and His power. And this definition includes the attributes of negation, which are pre-eternity, everlastingness, and dissimilarity... etc. And it includes the attributes of meanings, like power, and the relational attributes (al-ma'nawiyya), like His being Powerful, and the essential attribute (al-nafsiyya), which is existence. Therefore, this definition of al-wajib is comprehensive for all of its individuals due to its inclusion of attributes of meanings, relational, and negative, preventing the entry of other than it like al-mustahīl (1). (Fābtahil) with kasr of the lām i.e., supplicate to Allah that He may give you and connect you to knowledge of what benefits you, for by this noble character, which is supplication to Allah and humility before Him, you reach stations, and flow upon you from Allah the divine effusions; may Allah enable us for that. (And al-mustahīl) i.e., its definition is (kullu mā) i.e., something from essence or attribute of Allah – the intellectual mustaḥīl. (1) And the intellectual wajib is of two types: necessary (ḍarūrī), which does not depend on reflection and inference, like occupation of space for a body, i.e., taking its essence from void; and theoretical (naẓarī), which depends on what was mentioned, like pre-eternity for Allah, Most High, for each of them does not accept non-existence by itself.
Source pages p. 38

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on two lines of the poem that define the intellectual wajib and mustahil. The commentator first quotes the poem: 'al-wajib al-'aqli is what does not accept non-existence in its essence' and 'al-mustahil is what does not accept existence in its essence.' He then explains that the definition of al-wajib al-'aqli applies to essences, attributes, and relations, giving examples from Allah's essence, existence, eternity, and power. The definition is comprehensive, covering attributes of negation (like pre-eternity), attributes of meanings (like power), relational attributes (like being powerful), and the essential attribute (existence). It excludes al-mustahil. The commentator then explains the word 'fābtahil' from the poem as a supplication. He then defines al-mustahil al-'aqli as anything from essence or attribute that does not accept existence. Finally, he notes that al-wajib al-'aqli is divided into necessary (ḍarūrī), which requires no reflection (e.g., spatial occupation for a body), and theoretical (naẓarī), which requires reflection (e.g., pre-eternity of Allah). Both are necessary in essence.

Key Terms
الواجب العقلي
الانتفا
الصفات السلوب
الصفات المعاني
الصفات المعنوية
الصفة النفسية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that 'wajib' only applies to existence or to positive attributes.
Correction: The passage shows that wajib covers negative attributes (e.g., pre-eternity), attributes of meanings, relational attributes, and the essential attribute (existence).

Checkpoint
According to the passage, which of the following is NOT included in the definition of intellectual wajib?
A) Attributes of negation (salbiyya)
B) Attributes of meanings (ma'ani)
C) The impossible (mustahil)
D) The essential attribute (nafsiyya)

Key Takeaway
The intellectual wajib is a comprehensive category that includes all divine attributes under the criterion of necessary existence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think of Allah's power, do you treat it as a necessary reality or as something contingent on your own experience? How would your du'a change if you fully internalized that His power is wajib like His existence?
?
The lesson says the intellectual wajib includes even relational attributes. Does your heart feel the necessity of Allah's being 'al-Mighty' as firmly as it feels the necessity of His existence? What might this reveal about the depth of your tawhid?
?
If you truly believed that every attribute of Allah cannot be absent, would you ever feel that He is not listening, not powerful enough, or not merciful? What does that say about your current state of certainty?

The Intellectual Mustahil and the Division of Wajib
Definition of mustahil and classification of wajib into necessary and theoretical

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of the intellectual mustahil and the two types of intellectual wajib: necessary (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī).

Core Idea
The mustahil is what cannot be, and the wajib is either self-evident or requires proof.

After defining the wajib, the commentator defines the mustahil as its opposite: that which cannot accept existence. Then he introduces a crucial division of the wajib into what is known without reflection (necessary) and what requires inference (theoretical). This distinction is essential for understanding how we know Allah's attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition of mustahil
Read the poem line and commentary defining al-mustahil al-'aqli. Note the phrase 'ما لم يقبل النبوت في ذاته'.
'النَّبُوت' means existence.
2
Understand the division of wajib
The commentator divides al-wajib into two types: necessary (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī). Find the examples given for each.
Necessary: a body's occupation of space. Theoretical: pre-eternity of Allah.
3
Compare the two types
Ask yourself what makes something necessarily known versus requiring reflection. How does this relate to the attributes of Allah?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فَالوَاجِبُ العقليُّ: مَا لَم يَقبَلِ * الانْتِفَا فِي ذاتِه فَابْتَهِل | ١٤ والْمُسْتَحِيلُ: كُلُّ مَا لَم يَقْبَل * فِي ذَاتِهِ النُّبُوتَ ضِدُّ الأَوَّلِ ١٥ شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي 2020 والمستحيل، والجائز التي تجب معرفتها فقال: (فالواجب العقلي من ذات أو صفة، أو نسبة كذات الله تعالى الواجب العقلي وكوجوده، وقدّمه، وكثبوت القدرة الله تعالى في قولنا: «الله قادر»، هو (ما) أي: الأمر الثابت الذي لم يقبل الانتفا) بالقصر للضرورة، أي: لا يقبل الزوال في ذاته، بقطع النظر عن علم الله تعالى وقدرته، وهذا التعريف يشمل صفات السلوب، وهي القدم، والبقاء، والمخالفة.... إلى آخره.. ويشمل صفات المعاني، كالقدرة والصفات المعنوية، ككونه تعالى قادرًا، والصفة النفسية، وهو الوجود، فلذلك كان هذا التعريف للواجب جامعا لأفراده كلها لشموله لصفات المعاني والمعنوية والسلبية، مانع من دخول الغير كالمستحيل (۱). (فابتهل بكسر اللام أي تضرع لله تعالى ليعطيك، ويصل بك إلى معرفة ما ينفعك، فإنك بهذا الخُلُقِ الشريف، وهو التضرع إلى الله تعالى، والخضوع بين يديه تصل إلى المقامات، وتفيض عليك من الله تعالى الفيوضات، أَهلنا الله تعالى لذلك. (والمستحيل) أي: تعريفه، هو (كل ما) أي: أمر من ذات، أو صفة الله المستحيل العقلي (۱) والواجب العقلي على قسمين ضروري وهو ما لا يتوقف على نظر واستدلال، كالتَّحَيُّز للجزم، أي: أخذه قدر ذاته من الفراغ. ونظري: وهو ما توقف على ما ذُكر، كالقِدَم لله تعالى، فكل منهما لا يقبل الانتفاء لذاته.
So the intellectual wajib: that which does not accept non-existence in its essence, so supplicate (14). And the mustaḥīl: everything that does not accept existence in its essence, the opposite of the first (15). Commentary of Sheikh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashshāt al-Makki al-Maliki 2020: And the mustaḥīl and the ja'iz which must be known, so he said: (So the intellectual wajib, from essence or attribute, or relation, like the essence of Allah, Most High, and His existence, and His eternity, and like the establishment of power for Allah, Most High, in our statement 'Allah is Powerful', is (mā) i.e., the established matter that has not accepted non-existence – with shortening for necessity – i.e., it does not accept removal in its essence, regardless of the knowledge of Allah and His power. And this definition includes the attributes of negation, which are pre-eternity, everlastingness, and dissimilarity... etc. And it includes the attributes of meanings, like power, and the relational attributes (al-ma'nawiyya), like His being Powerful, and the essential attribute (al-nafsiyya), which is existence. Therefore, this definition of al-wajib is comprehensive for all of its individuals due to its inclusion of attributes of meanings, relational, and negative, preventing the entry of other than it like al-mustahīl (1). (Fābtahil) with kasr of the lām i.e., supplicate to Allah that He may give you and connect you to knowledge of what benefits you, for by this noble character, which is supplication to Allah and humility before Him, you reach stations, and flow upon you from Allah the divine effusions; may Allah enable us for that. (And al-mustahīl) i.e., its definition is (kullu mā) i.e., something from essence or attribute of Allah – the intellectual mustaḥīl. (1) And the intellectual wajib is of two types: necessary (ḍarūrī), which does not depend on reflection and inference, like occupation of space for a body, i.e., taking its essence from void; and theoretical (naẓarī), which depends on what was mentioned, like pre-eternity for Allah, Most High, for each of them does not accept non-existence by itself.

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on two lines of the poem that define the intellectual wajib and mustahil. He then explains that the definition of al-wajib al-'aqli applies to essences, attributes, and relations, giving examples from Allah's essence, existence, eternity, and power. According to the passage, which of the following is an example of a theoretical (naẓarī) intellectual wajib?

?

?

?
If the wajib includes truths you do not yet understand but must accept once proven, are you actively seeking that proof, or are you content with ignorance? What step will you take this week to move from theoretical knowledge to conviction?
