Rational Judgments
beginner
10 min
Definition of the Rationally Necessary (Wajib 'Aqli)
Mashhat defines and explains the rationally necessary and its types.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of al-wajib al-'aqli, its comprehensive scope covering essential attributes, and its two subdivisions.

Core Idea
Wajib 'aqli is what cannot be removed in essence, covering all divine attributes.

This lesson establishes the foundational concept of the rationally necessary in Islamic theology. Mashhat provides a clear definition and then explains how it applies to God's essence, attributes, and relations, while also distinguishing between what is known directly and what requires reasoning. The passage also includes a poetic line and a supplication.

Guided Path
1
Read the poetic definition
Read the first line of Arabic poetry: 'The rationally necessary: that which does not accept removal in its essence, so supplicate.' Understand that it defines wajib as something that cannot cease to exist.
The word 'fābtahil' is a command to supplicate, showing the author's piety.
2
Understand the scope of the definition
The definition applies to essence, attribute, or relation. Examples: God's essence, His existence, His pre-eternity, and His power. The definition includes negative attributes (e.g., pre-eternity), attributes of meanings (e.g., power), connotative attributes (e.g., being powerful), and the essential attribute (existence).
This shows the definition is comprehensive, covering all divine attributes.
3
Learn the two types of wajib 'aqli
Wajib 'aqli is divided into necessary (daruri) and theoretical (nazari). Daruri does not depend on reflection (e.g., a body's occupancy of space). Nazari depends on inference (e.g., God's pre-eternity). Both are necessary in themselves.
This division helps distinguish between immediate and inferred knowledge.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فَالوَاجِبُ العقليُّ: مَا لَم يَقبَلِ * الانْتِفَا فِي ذاتِه فَابْتَهِل | ١٤
والواجب العقلي من ذات أو صفة، أو نسبة كذات الله تعالى وكوجوده، وقدّمه، وكثبوت القدرة الله تعالى في قولنا: "الله قادر"، هو (ما) أي: الأمر الثابت الذي لم يقبل الانتفا بالقصر للضرورة، أي: لا يقبل الزوال في ذاته، بقطع النظر عن علم الله تعالى وقدرته، وهذا التعريف يشمل صفات السلوب، وهي القدم، والبقاء، والمخالفة.... إلى آخره.. ويشمل صفات المعاني، كالقدرة والصفات المعنوية، ككونه تعالى قادرًا، والصفة النفسية، وهو الوجود، فلذلك كان هذا التعريف للواجب جامعا لأفراده كلها لشموله لصفات المعاني والمعنوية والسلبية، مانع من دخول الغير كالمستحيل.
(۱) والواجب العقلي على قسمين ضروري وهو ما لا يتوقف على نظر واستدلال، كالتَّحَيُّز للجزم، أي: أخذه قدر ذاته من الفراغ. ونظري: وهو ما توقف على ما ذُكر، كالقِدَم لله تعالى، فكل منهما لا يقبل الانتفاء لذاته.
فابتهل بكسر اللام أي تضرع لله تعالى ليعطيك، ويصل بك إلى معرفة ما ينفعك، فإنك بهذا الخُلُقِ الشريف، وهو التضرع إلى الله تعالى، والخضوع بين يديه تصل إلى المقامات، وتفيض عليك من الله تعالى الفيوضات، أَهلنا الله تعالى لذلك.
The rationally necessary: that which does not accept removal in its essence, so supplicate. And the rationally necessary is of essence, attribute, or relation, like the essence of God Almighty, His existence, His pre-eternity, and the affirmation of power for God Almighty in our statement 'God is powerful.' It is (what) meaning the fixed thing that does not accept removal, abbreviated for metrical necessity, i.e., it does not accept cessation in its essence, disregarding the knowledge and power of God Almighty. And this definition includes attributes of negation, which are pre-eternity, everlastingness, and dissimilarity... etc. And it includes attributes of meanings like power, and the connotative attributes like His being powerful, and the essential attribute which is existence. Thus this definition of the necessary is comprehensive of all its individuals because it includes attributes of meanings, connotative, and negative, preventing the entry of other [things] like the impossible. (1) The rationally necessary is of two types: necessary (daruri), which does not depend on reflection and inference, like occupancy for a body, i.e., taking up a portion of space; and theoretical (nazari), which depends on what was mentioned, like pre-eternity for God Almighty. Each of them does not accept removal in its essence. 'Fābtahil' with kasra on the lam means supplicate to God Almighty that He may give you and lead you to beneficial knowledge, for through this noble character—supplication and humility before Him—you reach high stations and effusions flow upon you from God Almighty. May God make us worthy of that.
Source pages p. 38

Commentary
This passage defines the rationally necessary (wajib 'aqli) and explains its scope and subdivisions. The author begins with the poetic line: 'The rationally necessary: that which does not accept removal in its essence, so supplicate.' He then explains that the rationally necessary applies to essence, attribute, or relation, giving examples like God's essence, existence, pre-eternity, and the attribute of power. The definition given is 'the fixed thing that does not accept removal in its essence,' disregarding God's knowledge and power. This definition is comprehensive, covering negative attributes (e.g., pre-eternity, everlastingness, dissimilarity), attributes of meanings (e.g., power), connotative attributes (e.g., being powerful), and the essential attribute (existence). The definition therefore includes all instances of the necessary and excludes other things like the impossible. The author then notes that the rationally necessary is divided into two types: necessary (daruri), which does not require reflection (e.g., a body's occupancy of space), and theoretical (nazari), which requires inference (e.g., God's pre-eternity). Both types are necessary in themselves. Finally, the author explains the phrase 'fābtahil' (supplicate) as a call to humility and prayer to attain understanding, asking God to grant that.

Key Terms
واجب عقلي
ضروري
نظري
صفات سلوب
صفات معاني

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What is the definition of al-wajib al-'aqli according to this passage?
A) That which does not accept removal in its essence.
B) That which does not accept affirmation in its essence.
C) That which accepts both removal and affirmation.
D) That which is impossible.

Key Takeaway
The rationally necessary is whatever necessarily exists and cannot be absent from its essence, including God's existence and all His attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly live as if God's existence is the most certain reality in your life? If not, what would change if you internalized that He is rationally necessary—that He cannot be absent from your awareness?
?
The passage distinguishes between immediate rational necessity (daruri) and that which requires reflection (nazari). Are there attributes of God that you have accepted without deep reflection? Which attribute of God do you need to move from a theoretical acceptance to a necessary conviction in your heart?
?
The author ends with a call to supplicate for beneficial knowledge. How often do you sincerely ask Allah to grant you true understanding of His attributes? Is your pursuit of aqeedah merely intellectual, or a humble plea for light that transforms your soul?

Rational Judgments
beginner
5 min
Definition of the Rationally Impossible
Sanusi defines the mustahil 'aqli and contrasts it with the wajib.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of the rationally impossible (mustahil 'aqli) and how it contrasts with the rationally necessary (wajib), including its division into necessary and theoretical types.

Core Idea
The impossible is what cannot exist by its very essence, not by external knowledge.

After defining the rationally necessary, Sanusi now defines its opposite: the rationally impossible. This is not just about what God does not do, but about what cannot be done because its very essence contradicts existence. The passage clarifies that impossibility is inherent in the thing itself, not dependent on God's knowledge or power. It also classifies impossible things into those known immediately (daruri) and those requiring proof (nazari).

Guided Path
1
Read the poetic line and commentary
Start by reading the Arabic poetic line and its commentary. Notice that the definition focuses on 'not accepting affirmation in its essence'.
The example of the faith of Abu Jahl illustrates that the impossibility is in the essence, not because God knew it would not occur.
2
Contrast with the necessary
The passage explicitly contrasts the impossible with the necessary: the necessary is fixed and cannot be removed, while the impossible is negated and cannot be affirmed. Understand this contrast as two sides of the same rational judgment.
3
Identify the two types
The passage then divides impossibility into two types: necessary (daruri) and theoretical (nazari). Find the example for each: a body free from both motion and rest (daruri), and a partner for God (nazari).
This division mirrors the one for the necessary, showing a pattern in rational judgments.
4
Take the checkpoint
Answer the multiple-choice question to confirm you understand the key example of impossibility given in the passage.
The question is based directly on the passage text.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والمُسْتَحِيلُ: كُلُّ مَا لَم يَقْبَل * فِي ذَاتِهِ النُّبُوتَ ضِدُّ الأَوَّلِ
(والمستحيل) أي: تعريفه، هو (كل ما) أي: أمر من ذات، أو صفة الله المستحيل العقلي (۱)
أو نسبة، أمر منتف (لم يقبل) بكسر اللام (في ذاته) أي: بالنظر لذاته، بقطع النظر عن تعلق علم الله بعدم وجوده، كإيمان أبي جهل، ومفعول «لم يقبل» (الثبوت) فهو (ضد الأول) أي: الواجب، أي: مخالف له(۱). لما علمت أن الواجب هو الثابت الذي لا يقبل الانتفاء، والمستحيل هو المنتفي الذي لا يقبل الثبوت، والمستحيل كالشريك لله تعالى، فإن ذلك مستحيل على الله تعالى(۲).
(۱) وهو قسمان أيضًا: ضروري، كخلو الجزم عن الحركة والسكون معا. ونظري، كالشريك الله تعالى.
(۲) هو أيضًا قسمان: ضروري، كخصوص الحركة أو السكون للجزم. ونظري، كإثابة العاصي، وتعذيب المطيع، ومنه: السبع عند الأكل، والإحراق عند مماسة النار، من كل حكم عادي فإنه جائز عقلي.
And the rationally impossible: everything that does not accept affirmation in its essence, the opposite of the first. (The impossible) i.e., its definition: it is (everything) i.e., an affair of essence, attribute, or relation, an impossible affair (did not accept) with kasra on the lam (in its essence) i.e., with respect to its essence, disregarding the relation of God's knowledge to its non-existence, like the faith of Abi Jahl. And the object of 'did not accept' is (affirmation), so it is (the opposite of the first) i.e., the necessary, i.e., different from it. Since you have known that the necessary is the fixed that does not accept removal, and the impossible is the negated that does not accept affirmation. And the impossible is like a partner for God Almighty, for that is impossible for God Almighty. (1) And it is also of two types: necessary (daruri), like the void of a body being free from motion and rest together; and theoretical (nazari), like a partner for God Almighty. (2) It is also of two types: necessary (daruri), like a particular motion or rest for a body; and theoretical (nazari), like rewarding the disobedient and punishing the obedient, and from it: the burning at eating, and the burning at touching fire, from every customary judgment, for it is rationally possible.
Source pages p. 38

Commentary
This passage defines the rationally impossible (mustahil 'aqli) and contrasts it with the necessary. The author begins with the poetic line: 'The impossible: everything that does not accept affirmation in its essence, the opposite of the first.' He explains that the impossible is an affair of essence, attribute, or relation that is negated, specifying that it does not accept affirmation in its essence, disregarding God's knowledge of its non-existence. An example is the faith of Abu Jahl (which is impossible in itself but by God's knowledge it was not to occur). The impossible is the opposite of the necessary. The author then reiterates that the necessary is fixed and does not accept removal, while the impossible is negated and does not accept affirmation. An example of the impossible is a partner for God. He then gives two footnotes: one stating that the impossible is also divided into necessary (daruri), like a body being free from both motion and rest, and theoretical (nazari), like a partner for God; another footnote (which actually describes the possible) says it is also divided into necessary, like a particular motion or rest for a body, and theoretical, like rewarding the disobedient and punishing the obedient, and includes examples of customary judgments, calling them rationally possible. (Note: The second footnote seems misattributed but is included as per source.)

Key Terms
مستحيل عقلي
ضروري
نظري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the rationally impossible is whatever God does not do or whatever is unknown to us.
Correction: The passage defines it as what is inherently impossible in its essence, regardless of God's knowledge. For example, the faith of Abu Jahl is impossible because his essence rejects it, not because God knew it would not happen.

Checkpoint
What is an example of the rationally impossible given in the passage?
A) Motion and rest for a body.
B) A partner for God.
C) The existence of God.
D) Rewarding the disobedient.

Key Takeaway
The rationally impossible is defined by its essence not accepting existence, and it is divided into the immediately known (daruri) and the proven (nazari).

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter a situation that feels impossible, do you assume it is impossible for Allah or impossible by the nature of the thing? How does this lesson challenge you to align your trust with Allah's power? Write a specific commitment to change how you think.
?
The example of Abu Jahl's faith being impossible by essence—consider your own heart. Is there a spiritual quality (like sincerity, patience, or gratitude) that you have deemed impossible to achieve? Based on this lesson, what is the true obstacle: your essence or your effort? What action will you take today to break that assumption?
?
The lesson distinguishes between the impossible by nature and things Allah simply does not do. Which area of your life do you most need to apply this distinction: your view of Allah’s attributes, your repentance, or your hopes? Describe one concrete thought you will correct now.

4 min
Definition of the Rationally Possible (Ja'iz 'Aqli)
Mashhat defines the ja'iz 'aqli and gives examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of the rationally possible (ja'iz 'aqli) and how it differs from necessary and impossible when related to God's knowledge.

Core Idea
What is possible in itself becomes necessary or impossible by God's decree.

This lesson introduces the third category of rational judgment: the possible (ja'iz). Mashhat defines it as anything that can either exist or not exist without contradiction. However, when we relate the same affair to Allah’s knowledge, it is no longer merely possible—it becomes either necessary or impossible depending on His will. This distinction is key to understanding divine omnipotence and human responsibility.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic verse and translation
Read the poetic line and its prose explanation. Notice the concise definition of ja'iz.
2
Understand the definition
The passage says: 'Every affair capable of removal and affirmation is possible.' Reflect on what this means.
3
Distinguish relative to God's knowledge
The author clarifies that when related to God's knowledge, the same affair becomes necessary or impossible. That is the key theological nuance.
4
Examine the examples
Consider the examples: motion/rest of a body, and rewarding the disobedient. Why are these possible in themselves?
5
Reflect on the Quranic verses
The verses emphasize that Allah does as He wills and is not questioned. How does this support the doctrine of divine omnipotence?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وَكُلُّ أَمْرٍ قَابِل لِلانْتِفَاً * وَلِلنُّبُوتِ جَائِزٌ بِلا خَفَا
وكل أمر قابل في حد ذاته للانتفاء وللثبوت فهو جائز بلاخفاء، أما بالنسبة لتعلق علم الله تعالى بوجوده، أو امتناعه، فهو واجب أو مستحيل. مثال الجائز: خصوص الحركة والسكون للجزم، وإثابة العاصي وتعذيب المطيع، فإن ذلك جائز بالنظر لذاته، ولله تعالى أن يفعل ما يشاء فَعَّالٌ لِمَا يُرِيدُ ( سورة هود، الآية ۱۰۷ )، ولا يُسْأَلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْأَلُونَ ( سورة الأنبياء، الآية ۲۳ ).
And every affair that is capable of removal and affirmation is possible (ja'iz) without concealment. As for the relation of God's knowledge to its existence or its impossibility, it is necessary or impossible. An example of the possible: particular motion and rest for a body, and rewarding the disobedient and punishing the obedient, for that is possible with respect to itself. And God Almighty does what He wills, 'Doer of what He intends' (Quran 11:107), and 'He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned' (Quran 21:23).
Source pages p. 39

Commentary
This passage defines the rationally possible (ja'iz 'aqli) and provides examples. The author says: 'And every affair that is capable of removal and affirmation is possible, without concealment.' He then clarifies that when related to God's knowledge, the same affair becomes necessary or impossible. Examples of the possible include a body's particular motion or rest, and the rewarding of the disobedient or punishing of the obedient, because these are not inherently necessary or impossible. The author then quotes two Quranic verses to emphasize God's unrestricted will: 'Doer of what He intends' and 'He is not questioned about what He does.' This underscores that God may do whatever He wills, and nothing is obligatory upon Him from creation.

Key Terms
جائز عقلي
واجب
مستحيل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that what is possible in itself remains possible even in relation to God's knowledge.
Correction: The passage clarifies that when related to God's knowledge, the possible becomes necessary or impossible because God's knowledge determines its actuality.

Checkpoint
What happens to a rationally possible affair when it is related to God's knowledge?
A) It remains possible.
B) It becomes necessary or impossible.
C) It becomes impossible.
D) It becomes necessary.

Key Takeaway
A rationally possible affair is one that can exist or not exist, but relative to Allah's knowledge it is fixed as necessary or impossible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that Allah is not questioned about what He does, even when you see events that seem profoundly unfair to you? How does this truth change how you process pain and injustice in your own life?
?
If Allah could reward the disobedient and punish the obedient, does that make you rethink your motivation for worship? Are you obeying out of a transaction, or out of love for who He is regardless of outcomes?
?
Knowing that every possibility—your next breath, your next choice—is already determined by Allah's knowledge, does that make you feel trapped or deeply secure? What would it look like to surrender to that certainty today?

Definition of the Impossible and the Permissible
Mashat defines mustahil and ja'iz with commentary and examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definitions of the impossible (mustahil) and permissible (ja'iz) rational judgments, how they contrast with the necessary, and their key characteristics.

Core Idea
The impossible cannot exist in its essence; the permissible can be or not be.

In this passage from al-Bahja al-Saniyyah, Shaykh Mashat defines the two remaining rational judgments after the necessary. The impossible is the opposite of the necessary, and the permissible is what may or may not exist. The commentary clarifies these definitions and provides examples, including motion and rest for a body and rewarding or punishing.

Guided Path
1
Read the definitions
Read the Arabic text and translation to grasp the concise definitions of mustahil and ja'iz.
Notice the careful wording: 'does not accept in its essence' for mustahil.
2
Understand the contrast with wājib
The commentary emphasizes that mustahil is the opposite of wājib. Identify how they differ in accepting existence or non-existence.
3
Examine the examples
The examples of a partner for God (mustahil) and motion/rest of a body (ja'iz) help illustrate the concepts.
These examples are classical in kalam.
4
Reflect on the role of God's knowledge
Note that in itself, ja'iz is possible, but relative to God's knowledge it becomes necessary or impossible. Understand this distinction.
This is important for avoiding confusion.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والمستحيل: كلُّ ما لم يقبل في ذاته الثبوت ضد الأول. وكل أمر قابل للانتفاء وللثبوت جائز بلا خفا.
شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المالكي:
أو نسبة، أمر منتف (لم يقبل) بكسر اللام (في ذاته) أي: بالنظر لذاته، بقطع النظر عن تعلق علم الله بعدم وجوده، كإيمان أبي جهل، ومفعول «لم يقبل» (الثبوت) فهو (ضد الأول) أي: الواجب، أي: مخالف له. لما علمت أن الواجب هو الثابت الذي لا يقبل الانتفاء، والمستحيل هو المنتفي الذي لا يقبل الثبوت، والمستحيل كالشريك لله تعالى، فإن ذلك مستحيل على الله تعالى.
(وكل أمر قابل) في حد ذاته (للانتفاء) (وللثبوت) فهو (جائز بلا خفاء) أما بالنسبة لتعلق علم الله تعالى بوجوده، أو امتناعه، فهو واجب أو مستحيل.
مثال الجائز: خصوص الحركة والسكون للجرم، وإثابة العاصي وتعذيب المطيع، فإن ذلك جائز بالنظر لذاته، ولله تعالى أن يفعل ما يشاء {فَعَّالٌ لِمَا يُرِيدُ} (هود: 107)، ولا يُسْأَلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْأَلُونَ} (الأنبياء: 23).
(1) وهو قسمان أيضًا: ضروري، كخلو الجرم عن الحركة والسكون معا. ونظري، كالشريك لله تعالى.
(2) هو أيضًا قسمان: ضروري، كخصوص الحركة أو السكون للجرم. ونظري، كإثابة العاصي، وتعذيب المطيع، ومنه: السبع عند الأكل، والإحراق عند مماسة النار، من كل حكم عادي فإنه جائز عقلي.
(3) سورة هود، الآية (107). (4) سورة الأنبياء، الآية (23).
فائدة
يصح أن يُمثل بالحركة والسكون للجرم، لأقسام الحكم العقلي المذكورة، فثبوت الحركة أو السكون للجرم لا بعينه واجب، ونفيهما معا مستحيل، وثبوت أحدهما للجرم بالخصوص جائز.
And the impossible: everything that does not accept in its essence existence, opposite of the first. And every matter that accepts non-existence and existence is permissible without concealment. Explanation by Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashshāt al-Mālikī: Or relation, a matter of non-existence (did not accept) with kasra of the lām (in its essence) meaning: with respect to its essence, disregarding the relation of Allah's knowledge to its non-existence, like the faith of Abu Jahl, and the object of 'did not accept' is (existence), so it is (opposite of the first) i.e., the necessary, i.e., contrary to it. Since you know that the necessary is the established that does not accept non-existence, and the impossible is the non-existent that does not accept existence, and the impossible is like a partner to Allah the Exalted, for that is impossible for Allah the Exalted. (And every matter that accepts) in its essence (non-existence) (and existence) is (permissible without concealment) as for the relation of Allah's knowledge to its existence or its impossibility, it is necessary or impossible. Example of the permissible: particular motion and rest for a body, and rewarding the disobedient and punishing the obedient, for that is permissible with respect to its essence, and Allah the Exalted may do what He wills {Doer of what He wills} (Hud: 107), and {He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned} (Prophets: 23). (1) And it is also two divisions: necessary, like the body being devoid of motion and rest together. And theoretical, like a partner to Allah the Exalted. (2) It is also two divisions: necessary, like the particularity of motion or rest for a body. And theoretical, like rewarding the disobedient and punishing the obedient, and among them: the satiety when eating, the burning when touching fire, from every customary judgment, for it is intellectually permissible. (3) Surah Hud, verse (107). (4) Surah al-Anbiya, verse (23). Benefit: It is valid to exemplify with motion and rest for a body, for the divisions of the rational judgment mentioned: the existence of motion or rest for a body not in particular is necessary, their non-existence together is impossible, and the existence of one of them for a body in particular is permissible.
Source pages p. 39

Commentary
This passage defines and explains the two rational judgments of impossibility and permissibility, alongside necessity (already defined earlier). The author begins by quoting the verse: 'And the impossible: everything that does not accept in its essence existence, opposite of the first (i.e., the necessary). And every matter that accepts non-existence and existence is permissible without concealment.' The commentary then clarifies that 'did not accept' refers to the essence itself, disregarding God's knowledge of its non-existence (e.g., the impossibility of Abu Jahl's faith). The impossible is thus the opposite of the necessary: the necessary is what must exist and cannot not exist; the impossible is what cannot exist. Examples are partners for God. For the permissible, it is what can either exist or not exist considered in itself, though relative to God's knowledge it becomes necessary or impossible. Examples include particular motion/rest for a body, and rewarding/punishing as God wills (Quranic proof). The passage then notes that each of these judgments has two subdivisions: necessary (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī). For the impossible: necessary impossibility is something like a body being devoid of both motion and rest (contradiction); theoretical impossibility is like a partner for God. For the permissible: necessary permissibility is something like the particularity of motion or rest for a body; theoretical permissibility is like rewarding the disobedient or punishing the obedient, and also customary matters like satiety from eating and burning from fire (these are intellectually permissible, not necessary). The benefit at the end reinforces the example: for a body, its existence with either motion or rest (not specified) is necessary; its existence with both non-existent is impossible; its existence with a specific motion/rest is permissible. This illustrates the three rational judgments clearly.

Key Terms
مستحيل
جائز
واجب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that the impossible means something that never happened, but it means cannot happen in its essence.
Correction: The impossible is defined by its essence, not by lack of occurrence. For example, a partner for God is impossible in essence.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the definition of the impossible (mustahil)?
A) What does not accept existence in its essence
B) What does not accept non-existence in its essence
C) What accepts both existence and non-existence
D) What is unknown

Key Takeaway
The impossible is what cannot exist in its essence, while the permissible is what can exist or not exist in itself.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Are there beliefs in your heart about Allah that, like a partner, are impossible in essence? Will you abandon them today and let tawhid take root, or will you cling to fanciful notions that contradict His majesty?
?
Consider a permissible matter in your life—like wealth, health, or a relationship. Do you treat it as if it must exist, burdening yourself with anxiety? How would your heart change if you truly embraced its contingency before Allah?
?
God can reward the disobedient and punish the obedient. Does this truth unsettle you? What would it mean to fully surrender your sense of entitlement to His justice, and instead trust His wisdom in every decree?

Subdivisions of the Impossible and Permissible: Necessary and Theoretical
Mashat enumerates the subdivisions of mustahil and ja'iz and provides a clarifying example.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the subdivisions of the impossible and permissible judgments into necessary (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī), and how the benefit provides a comprehensive example.

Core Idea
Rational judgments are either self-evident or require reasoning, but both are certain.

After defining the three rational judgments, Shaykh Mashat explains that each has two subcategories: those known necessarily and those requiring theoretical reasoning. This classification helps us understand different levels of certainty. The benefit at the end summarizes the three judgments using the motion/rest example, making it an excellent review.

Guided Path
1
Read the subdivisions for mustahil
The passage states that mustahil is divided into necessary impossibility (e.g., a body devoid of both motion and rest) and theoretical impossibility (e.g., a partner for God). Understand why these are necessary vs theoretical.
Necessary impossibility is a logical contradiction.
2
Read the subdivisions for ja'iz
Ja'iz is divided into necessary permissibility (e.g., particular motion/rest) and theoretical permissibility (e.g., rewarding the disobedient).
Theoretical permissibility includes customary matters like satiety from eating.
3
Study the benefit example
The benefit shows how motion and rest exemplify all three rational judgments: existence of either motion or rest is necessary; their simultaneous non-existence is impossible; and the specific assignment is permissible.
This clarifies the distinctions.
4
Reflect on the overall classification
Consider how this subdivision helps in kalam reasoning: some truths are immediate, others require proof, but all are rationally grounded.
This is foundational for theological arguments.

⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that theoretical judgments are less certain than necessary ones, but in fact they are equally certain after proof.
Correction: The passage shows that theoretical judgments are still rational judgments; they are certain even if not self-evident.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, which of the following is an example of a necessary impossibility (ḍarūrī mustaḥīl)?
A) A body being devoid of both motion and rest simultaneously
B) A partner for Allah
C) A body having either motion or rest
D) Rewarding the disobedient

Key Takeaway
Rational judgments are subdivided into necessary (self-evident) and theoretical (requiring proof), but both are certain within their domain.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You often assume that what you see in the world—like a good person suffering or a bad person thriving—is against God's justice. But this lesson shows that such outcomes are intellectually permissible, not necessary. How does separating what is necessary from what is merely possible change your trust in God's wisdom when life seems unfair? Will you now pause and ask yourself before complaining: 'Is this impossible for God to allow, or is it just something I don't understand?'
?
The lesson uses the example of a partner for Allah as a theoretical impossibility. When you say 'la ilaha illa Allah,' do you truly feel in your heart that it is impossible for anyone to be equal to God, or do you sometimes entertain subtle comparisons in your mind? What would it mean for your worship if you internalized this impossibility so deeply that no rival ever crosses your thoughts again?
?
God rewards the obedient and punishes the disobedient as a customary judgment, but it is not intellectually necessary. Have you ever done something good expecting an immediate reward, and when it didn't come, you felt disappointed or doubted? How can this truth—that reward is permissible but not compulsory—free you from transactional worship and move you toward loving God for who He is, not for what He gives?

Illustrating Rational Judgments with Motion and Rest
Mashat illustrates the three rational judgments using motion and rest of a body.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the three rational judgments (necessary, impossible, possible) apply to a concrete example: motion and rest of a body.

Core Idea
Every body must have motion or rest—that is necessary; having neither is impossible; having one specifically is possible.

This short passage is a practical illustration of the three rational judgments we have just learned. Mashat uses a simple physical example—a body and its motion or rest—to show how these abstract categories apply to real things. As you read, pay attention to how each judgment corresponds to a different logical status.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice that it is a 'benefit' (fā'ida), meaning it is an illustrative addition.
2
Identify the three judgments
The passage mentions three judgments: necessary, impossible, and possible. Find the phrase for each.
3
Understand the example
The example uses a body (jirm) and the attributes of motion and rest. Make sure you see why the first is necessary, the second impossible, and the third possible.
Necessary: a body must have either motion or rest. Impossible: it cannot have both absent. Possible: it can have one specifically.
4
Reflect on the logic
Ask yourself: why is this a good illustration? It shows how the three categories apply to the same subject (body) and the same pair of attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فائدة
يصح أن يُمثل بالحركة والسكون للجرم، لأقسام الحكم العقلي المذكورة، فثبوت الحركة أو السكون للجرم لا بعينه واجب، ونفيهما معا مستحيل، وثبوت أحدهما للجرم بالخصوص جائز.
Benefit: It is correct to exemplify with motion and rest for a body, for the mentioned divisions of rational judgment. Thus, the affirmation of motion or rest for a body not in particular is necessary, and their negation together is impossible, and the affirmation of one of them for a body in particular is permissible.
Source pages p. 40

Commentary
This passage is a benefit (fā'ida) that provides a concrete example for the three rational judgments (al-ḥukm al-ʿaqlī): necessary, impossible, and possible. The author uses the example of a body (jirm) and the attributes of motion and rest. He says: The affirmation of motion or rest for a body in an unspecified way (i.e., that it must have one of them) is necessary (wājib). The negation of both together (i.e., that it is neither moving nor at rest) is impossible (mustaḥīl). The affirmation of one of them specifically (i.e., that it is this particular motion or rest) is possible (jā'iz). This is a standard illustration in kalām for the three modes.

Key Terms
حكم عقلي
واجب
مستحيل
جائز
جرم
حركة
سكون

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The necessary judgment in this example applies to a specific attribute like motion.
Correction: Actually, the necessary judgment is that the body must have either motion or rest in general; it is not necessary which one specifically.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what is the rational judgment for a body having either motion or rest (not specifically)?
A) Possible
B) Necessary
C) Impossible
D) Both A and B

Key Takeaway
The three rational judgments can be illustrated by the fact that a body must have either motion or rest (necessary), cannot lack both (impossible), and can have either one specifically (possible).

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
This lesson shows that necessity applies to the general disjunction (motion or rest) not to the specific. In your own life, do you often insist on a specific outcome from Allah when the only necessity is obedience in general? What specific expectation are you holding onto that you could release today?
?
The impossible here is the negation of both motion and rest—a state that cannot exist. Is there a 'both/and' or 'neither/nor' in your faith you've been trying to maintain that is spiritually impossible? What must you choose instead?
?
The possible category covers both motion and rest as equally permissible. Consider an area where you judge something as necessary or impossible when it is truly possible. How does misclassifying the possible affect your reliance on Allah? Commit to naming one such area and accepting both possibilities as from Him.

The Syllogism Proving the World's Contingency
Commentator explains syllogism proving world's contingency via change.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the kalam argument uses a syllogism of the first figure to prove that the world is contingent and therefore dependent on a Creator.

Core Idea
The world's contingency is proven through observable and inferable change.

The commentator begins by quoting the poem's opening lines, which assert that the world is contingent and dependent. He then unpacks the logical structure: a syllogism of the first figure that deduces the world's need for a creator. In this lesson, we will examine this syllogism and the evidence for contingency.

Guided Path
1
Read the poem and syllogism introduction
Read the opening line and the commentator’s description of the syllogism. Note the structure: The world is contingent, every contingent depends on a creator, so the world depends on a creator.
The syllogism is of the first figure, known for its intuitive conclusion.
2
Identify the evidence for contingency
The evidence is change (taghayyur). Change is either observed directly (like motion after stillness) or inferred from similarity.
Inferred change is key: even mountains and planets, though seemingly unchanging, can change because they are similar to other bodies.
3
Trace the conclusion to all bodies
The passage extends the argument: all accidents are contingent, and since bodies cannot exist without accidents, all bodies are contingent. Hence the entire world is contingent.
The conclusion is that everything other than Allah is contingent.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مِن غَيْرِ شَكُ حَادِثٌ مُفْتَقِرُ * لأنَّهُ قَامَ بِهِ الشَّغَيرُ ۸ حدوثهُ وُجُودُه بَعْدَ العَدَمُ *  َوضِدُّه هو المُسَمَّى بالقِدَم ١٩ |
شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي
يشير إلى نظم قياس من الشكل الأول، الذي هو بديهي الإنتاج هكذا:
الــعـــالـــم
العالم حادث، وكل حادث فهو مفتقر إلى محدث، ينتج بعد حذف الحد الأوسط المكرر العالم مفتقر إلى محدث. أما دليل كون العالم حادث، فأشار له بقوله: (لأنه قام به أي بالعالم دليل حدود (التغيّر) من عدم إلى وجود ومن وجود إلى عدم إما بالمشاهدة كالحركة بعد السكون، والضوء بعد الظلمة، والعكس. وإما بالدليل، لأن ما شوهد سكونه مثلا على الدوام، كالجبال، أو حركته على الدوام، كالكواكب، جاز أن يثبت له العكس، إذ لا فرق بين جرم وجرم في قبول الحركة والسكون وما جاز على أحد المتماثلين جاز على الآخر.
إن بعض التغيرات في الجبال، والكواكب، من علامات الحدوث يدل على ما سواه، وإذا جاز عدمها استحال قدمها، فتكون حادثة فجميع الأعراض حادثة، ويلزم من حدوثها حدوث جميع الأجرام لعدم انفكاكها عن الأعراض الحادثة، وكل ما لا ينفك عن فهو حادث، فظهر أن جميع ما سوى الله تعالى المعبر عنه بالعالم من أعراضه وأجرامه حادث، أي: موجود بعد أن لم يكن.
الحادث
۱۹- حدوثه) أي: العالم (وجوده) أي: عبارة عن وجوده (بعد
العدم وضده أي الحدوث، هو المسمى بالقدم وهو خاص بالله تعالى، فهو القديم، ولا قديم سواه.
Without doubt, contingent, dependent * because change occurred in it [الشغَير؟] 8 Its occurrence is its existence after non-existence * and its opposite is that which is called pre-eternity 19 |
Commentary by Sheikh Hasan al-Mashat al-Makki al-Maliki:
He indicates to the arrangement of a syllogism of the first figure, which is intuitive in its conclusion as follows: The world is contingent, and every contingent is dependent on a creator, resulting after removing the repeated middle term: the world is dependent on a creator. As for the evidence that the world is contingent, he pointed to it by his saying: (because change occurred in it) i.e. in the world, evidence of change from non-existence to existence and from existence to non-existence, either by observation like motion after stillness, light after darkness, and the opposite, or by evidence, because what is observed to be still perpetually, like mountains, or in motion perpetually, like planets, it is possible for the opposite to be established for it, since there is no difference between one body and another in accepting motion and stillness, and what is possible for one of two similar things is possible for the other. Indeed, some changes in mountains and planets are signs of contingency indicating other than them, and if their non-existence is possible then their pre-eternity is impossible, so they are contingent. So all accidents are contingent, and from their contingency follows the contingency of all bodies because they are inseparable from contingent accidents, and whatever is inseparable from what is contingent is contingent. So it becomes clear that all that is other than Allah, expressed as the world, of its accidents and bodies, is contingent, i.e., existing after not having existed.
Al-Hadith (the contingent):
19- Its occurrence (i.e. of the world) is its existence, i.e., an expression of its existence after non-existence, and its opposite (i.e. the opposite of contingency) is that which is called pre-eternity (qidam), and it is specific to Allah Almighty, so He is the Pre-eternal, and there is no pre-eternal besides Him.
Source pages p. 42

Commentary
This passage is a commentary explaining the proof of the world's contingency (huduth) and defining key terms. It begins by quoting the poem: 'Without doubt, contingent, dependent...' then the commentator states that this refers to a syllogism of the first figure. The syllogism is: The world is contingent, and every contingent is dependent on a creator; therefore the world is dependent on a creator. The evidence that the world is contingent is change (taghayyur). Change is either observed directly (e.g., motion after stillness) or inferred (e.g., mountains and planets could change because they are similar to other bodies that change, so they are possible of change, thus contingent). All accidents are contingent, and since bodies cannot exist without accidents, all bodies are contingent. Hence everything other than Allah (the world) is contingent. The passage then defines 'hadith' as existence after non-existence, and its opposite 'qadim' as existence without beginning, which is exclusive to Allah. The commentator emphasizes that only Allah is truly eternal.

Key Terms
عالم
حادث
محدث
تغير
شكل أول

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Only directly observable change proves contingency.
Correction: The passage explains that even if change is not directly observed, it can be inferred from the similarity of objects. For example, mountains and planets can change because they are similar in nature to objects that undergo change.

Checkpoint
In the syllogism presented, what is the middle term that is removed?
A) The world
B) Contingent
C) Dependent on a creator
D) Change

Key Takeaway
The world's contingency is proven through a syllogism based on the presence of change, either observed or inferred, leading to the conclusion that everything other than Allah is contingent.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You just learned that everything in the world—including your own body—is contingent, existing after non-existence. If you truly believe this, why do you still cling to worldly things as if they will last forever? What would have to change in your daily life for you to live as if only Allah is eternal?
?
The proof uses inference by similarity: because mountains could change (by analogy to other bodies), they are contingent. Do you apply the same reasoning to your own ill habits or spiritual state—that because change is possible for others like you, you too can change, if Allah wills? If so, what one specific change will you commit to starting today?
?
If the entire universe is contingent and dependent on Allah, then your very existence at this moment is sustained by Him. How often do you consciously acknowledge this dependence in your prayers or thoughts? What would it look like to live today in constant awareness that you could cease to exist at any second, and that only Allah keeps you here?

Defining Contingency and Eternality
Commentator defines hadith and qadim.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definitions of hadith (contingent) and qadim (eternal) and why only Allah is truly eternal.

Core Idea
Contingency means existence after non-existence; eternity is its opposite and belongs to Allah alone.

After establishing the contingency of the world through the syllogism, the commentator defines the key terms. He explains that 'hadith' means existing after not existing, and its opposite 'qadim' means existence without beginning—a quality exclusive to Allah.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition of hadith
Find the line where the commentator says 'حدوثه وجوده بعد العدم' - its occurrence is its existence after non-existence. This defines contingent as that which comes into being.
2
Understand the opposite: qadim
The commentator states that the opposite of contingency is qadim (pre-eternity), which is specific to Allah. Qadim means existence without beginning.
This is a key theological point: only Allah is truly eternal.
3
Reflect on exclusivity
The passage emphasizes that there is no qadim besides Allah. This underscores that all created things are contingent, including angels, souls, etc.
This aligns with the earlier proof that the entire world is contingent.

Proof for Allah's Unlikeness to Contingents
A brief conditional proof for Allah's unlikeness.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how rational proof establishes that Allah is unlike all contingent things.

Core Idea
If Allah were like creation, He would be contingent—which is impossible.

The first proof in this passage gives a clear argument for the attribute of mukhalafa (being unlike). The logic is simple: if Allah resembled created things, He would share their properties—including contingency and need. Since those are impossible for Allah, similarity cannot hold.

Guided Path
1
Read the proof statement
Carefully read the Arabic passage for the proof of mukhalafa. Notice the conditional structure: 'if... then...'
The proof is a short but powerful conditional argument.
2
Identify the attributes of contingents
The passage mentions two necessary properties of contingent things: ḥudūth (temporal origination) and iftiqār (need). Understand why these cannot apply to Allah.
These are key concepts in kalam.
3
Connect to the divine attribute
Realize that this proof establishes mukhalafa (unlikeness) as a necessary attribute of Allah, logically deduced from His being necessary.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والدليل على مخالفته تعالى للغير: أنه لو كان مُمَائِلًا لها لوجب له تعالى ما وجب لها من الحدوث والافتقار، وذلك محال. والآية تشير إلى برهان التمانع، وحاصله: أنه لو أمكن التعدد، لأمكن التمانع بينهما، بأن يريد أحدهما حركة زيدٍ مثلا، والآخرُ سُكُونَهُ، إذ كل منهما أمر ممكن في نفسه، وكذا تعلق الإرادة بكل منهما، وحينئذ إما أن يحصل الأمران فيلزم اجتماع الضدين، أو لا، فيلزم عجزهما، أو عجز أحدهما، وهو أمارة الحدوث والإمكان لما فيه من شائبة الاحتياج، فالتعدد مستلزم لإمكان التمانع المستلزم للمحال، فيكون التعدد محالا.
And the proof for His being unlike that which is other than Him: if He were similar to them, then there would be necessary for Him what is necessary for them of contingency and need, and that is impossible. And the verse refers to the proof of mutual hindrance, and its summary: that if multiplicity were possible, then mutual hindrance between them would be possible, such that one of them wills the movement of Zayd for example, and the other wills his stillness, since each of them is a possible matter in itself, and likewise the attachment of will to each of them, and then either both occur, necessitating the combination of two opposites, or they do not, necessitating the impotence of both or one of them, and that is a sign of contingency and possibility due to the element of need, so multiplicity entails the possibility of mutual hindrance which entails an impossibility, thus multiplicity is impossible.
Source pages p. 49

Commentary
This passage contains two proofs from the footnote. First, a proof for the attribute of mukhalafa (being unlike contingents): if Allah were similar to contingent things, He would be subject to contingency (ḥudūth) and need (iftiqār), which is impossible. Second, the burhān al-tamānuʿ (proof of mutual hindrance), which is the classic kalam argument for the impossibility of multiple gods. The summary explains: if two gods existed, they could will opposite things (e.g., one wills Zayd's movement, the other his stillness). Both are possible. If both wills were fulfilled, opposites would coexist; if neither or one fails, that shows impotence. Impotence is a sign of contingency and need. Thus the possibility of mutual hindrance leads to impossibility, proving that multiplicity of gods is impossible.

Key Terms
المخالفة
التماثل
الحدوث
الافتقار

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think Allah can be compared to creation in essential attributes.
Correction: This proof shows that if Allah were similar, He would be contingent and needy, which is impossible. Therefore, He is absolutely unlike creation.

Checkpoint
What would be necessary for Allah if He were similar to contingent things?
A) Eternity and self-sufficiency
B) Contingency and need
C) Power and will
D) Similarity in essence only

Key Takeaway
Allah's unlikeness to creation is proven because similarity would entail contingency, which is impossible for the Necessary Being.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you imagine Allah, do you subconsciously picture a being with human limitations like needing rest or changing moods? How does this proof challenge that mental image, and what concrete step will you take this week to purify your conception of Allah?
?
If you truly believed that Allah is unlike anything in your experience, would you still complain about your circumstances as if He were limited like a human? What is one complaint you need to stop making right now in light of His transcendence?

The Proof of Mutual Hindrance for Divine Oneness
Al-Mashat summarizes the proof of mutual hindrance against multiple gods.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the burhan al-tamanu' and how it logically proves the impossibility of multiple gods.

Core Idea
Multiple gods would conflict, leading to contradiction or impotence—both impossible.

The second part of the passage presents the famous burhān al-tamānuʿ (proof of mutual hindrance). This is a cornerstone argument in Islamic theology for the oneness of Allah. The argument shows that the very possibility of multiple gods leads to logical absurdity, thus proving that only one God can exist.

Guided Path
1
Understand the scenario
The passage sets up a hypothetical scenario with two gods willing opposite things for the same object. Consider the example: one wants movement, the other wants stillness.
2
Trace the logical possibilities
Either both wills are fulfilled (causing opposites to coexist) or they are not (showing impotence). Both outcomes are impossible for a necessary being.
This is the core of the reductio.
3
Conclude the impossibility of multiplicity
Since the scenario leads to an impossibility, the assumption of multiple gods is false. Thus, only one God can exist.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والدليل على مخالفته تعالى للغير: أنه لو كان مُمَائِلًا لها لوجب له تعالى ما وجب لها من الحدوث والافتقار، وذلك محال. والآية تشير إلى برهان التمانع، وحاصله: أنه لو أمكن التعدد، لأمكن التمانع بينهما، بأن يريد أحدهما حركة زيدٍ مثلا، والآخرُ سُكُونَهُ، إذ كل منهما أمر ممكن في نفسه، وكذا تعلق الإرادة بكل منهما، وحينئذ إما أن يحصل الأمران فيلزم اجتماع الضدين، أو لا، فيلزم عجزهما، أو عجز أحدهما، وهو أمارة الحدوث والإمكان لما فيه من شائبة الاحتياج، فالتعدد مستلزم لإمكان التمانع المستلزم للمحال، فيكون التعدد محالا.
And the proof for His being unlike that which is other than Him: if He were similar to them, then there would be necessary for Him what is necessary for them of contingency and need, and that is impossible. And the verse refers to the proof of mutual hindrance, and its summary: that if multiplicity were possible, then mutual hindrance between them would be possible, such that one of them wills the movement of Zayd for example, and the other wills his stillness, since each of them is a possible matter in itself, and likewise the attachment of will to each of them, and then either both occur, necessitating the combination of two opposites, or they do not, necessitating the impotence of both or one of them, and that is a sign of contingency and possibility due to the element of need, so multiplicity entails the possibility of mutual hindrance which entails an impossibility, thus multiplicity is impossible.
Source pages p. 49

Commentary
This passage contains two proofs from the footnote. First, a proof for the attribute of mukhalafa (being unlike contingents): if Allah were similar to contingent things, He would be subject to contingency (ḥudūth) and need (iftiqār), which is impossible. Second, the burhān al-tamānuʿ (proof of mutual hindrance), which is the classic kalam argument for the impossibility of multiple gods. The summary explains: if two gods existed, they could will opposite things (e.g., one wills Zayd's movement, the other his stillness). Both are possible. If both wills were fulfilled, opposites would coexist; if neither or one fails, that shows impotence. Impotence is a sign of contingency and need. Thus the possibility of mutual hindrance leads to impossibility, proving that multiplicity of gods is impossible.

Key Terms
برهان التمانع
التعدد
الضدين
الإرادة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The burhan al-tamanu' assumes multiple gods exist and then shows they would fight.
Correction: The proof does not assume existence; it assumes logical possibility and shows that the very concept leads to contradiction.

Checkpoint
What is the conclusion of the burhan al-tamanu'?
A) Multiple gods are impossible
B) Two gods cannot have conflicting wills
C) Gods are limited in power
D) Only one god can be necessary

Key Takeaway
The burhan al-tamanu' demonstrates that multiplicity of gods leads to logical contradiction, proving that only one God can exist.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The proof shows that multiple gods are logically impossible. Yet do you sometimes live as if there are other powers that control your life besides Allah? In what area do you still fear people or circumstances more than you fear Allah, and what will you do this week to realign your heart with the reality of His exclusive sovereignty?
?
If the mere possibility of another god is impossible, why do you hesitate to submit completely to Allah in every aspect of your life? What is the one thing you still hold back from surrendering to Him alone? Will you commit right now to entrust that matter to Allah and rely on none besides Him?
?
The proof of mutual hindrance reveals that any supposed 'god' besides Allah would either be powerless or lead to contradiction. Does your heart truly affirm that there is no power, no provider, no protector except Allah? Search your actions: do you ever turn to created things as if they possess independent power? What step will you take today to make your tawheed more exclusive and your trust more absolute?

The Impossibility of Infinite Regress and Circularity
Commentary explains impossibility of infinite regress and circularity.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why infinite regress (tasalsul) and circularity (dawr) are rationally impossible, and how this establishes God's attributes of negation.

Core Idea
Infinite regress and circularity are impossible; they prove God is free from dependence.

This lesson introduces a foundational kalam argument: any attempt to explain the universe without a necessary first cause leads to absurdities like infinite regress or circularity. By refuting these possibilities, we affirm that Allah is eternal and self-sufficient, free from any form of dependency. Let's examine the text.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it. Then read the literal translation. Notice the poet's concise style and the commentator's clarification.
The passage begins with a line from the poem, then commentary.
2
Understand the definitions
Focus on the definitions of tasalsul (infinite regress) and dawr (circularity). The commentator explains each term clearly.
Tasalsul is an endless chain; dawr is a vicious circle.
3
Grasp why they are impossible
The commentator states both are clearly impossible. Ask yourself why an actual infinite or circular dependence cannot exist in reality.
The text says it is 'manifest impossible' needing no proof.
4
Connect to God's attributes
See how the impossibility of temporal origination (ḥudūth) for God leads to affirming attributes that negate any imperfection.
This establishes God's transcendence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
لأنَّه يُفْضِي إِلى التَّسَلْسُلِ * والدَّوْرِ وهُوَ الْمُستَحِيلُ الْمُنْجَلِي
(لأنه يفضي) من أفضى يفضي، أي: يؤدي ويوصل إلى التسلسل) التسلسل إذا استمر العدد إلى ما لا نهاية له، لأن التسلسل: هو ترتب أمور غير متناهية، وهو محال. (والدور) أي: يفضي إلى الدور - بفتح الدال المشددة إن لم يستمر، لأن معنى الدور: توقف الشيء على ما يتوقف على ذلك الشيء من جهة واحدة، وهو محال كما قال: (وهو المستحيل المنجلي)، أي: الواضح الذي لا يحتاج إلى دليل.
أي: وإذا ثبت استحالة الحدوث عليه تعالى ثبت اتصافه تعالى بالصفات المذكورة، التي يفيد مفهومها السلب عما لا يليق بالذات العلية اتصافها به.
فالواجب على كلِّ مكلَّف أن يعتقد اتصاف الرب تَبَارَكَ وَتَعالى بما يليق به من الصفات العُلى، ونفي أضدادها، والله يهدي إلى الحق ويهدي إلى سواء السبيل.
Because it leads to infinite regress and circularity, and it is the manifest impossible. (Because it leads) from 'afḍā yufḍī', meaning: it leads and brings to infinite regress. Infinite regress if the series continues to no end, because infinite regress is the arrangement of non-finite things, and it is impossible. (And circularity) meaning: it leads to circularity—with a fatḥa on the doubled dāl—if it does not continue, because the meaning of circularity is: a thing's dependence on what itself depends on that thing from one aspect, and it is impossible as he said: (and it is the manifest impossible), i.e., the clear thing that needs no proof. Meaning: when the impossibility of origination (ḥudūth) is established for Him, Exalted is He, His being described with the mentioned attributes is established, whose concept implies negation of what is unbefitting for the exalted essence to be described with. So it is obligatory upon every legally-responsible person to believe that the Lord, Blessed and Exalted, is described with what befits Him of the highest attributes, and to negate their opposites. And Allah guides to the truth and guides to the straight path.
Source pages p. 55

Commentary
This passage is a proof and explanation of a fundamental kalam argument. It begins by quoting a verse from the poem: 'Because it leads to infinite regress and circularity, and it is the manifest impossible.' The commentary then explains each term. First, 'infinite regress' (tasalsul) is defined as an endless chain of causes, which is impossible because it would require an actual infinite, which is rationally impossible. Second, 'circularity' (dawr) is explained as the dependence of A on B and B on A from the same aspect, which is also impossible. The phrase 'the manifest impossible' means it is self-evidently false and requires no further proof. Then the author draws the conclusion: once it is established that temporal origination (ḥudūth) is impossible for God (because it would lead to these absurdities), then God's attributes that negate what is unbefitting are confirmed. The passage ends with the obligation upon every legally-responsible person (mukallaf) to affirm these attributes of negation (such as eternality) and to deny their opposites, emphasizing that guidance comes from Allah.

Key Terms
تسلسل
دور
حدوث
صفات سلبية
مستحيل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that infinite regress is possible in theory.
Correction: The passage clarifies that infinite regress is impossible in reality because it would require an actual infinite, which is rationally impossible.

Checkpoint
What is the meaning of 'dawr' (circularity) as defined in this passage?
A) An endless chain of causes
B) A thing's dependence on what itself depends on that thing from one aspect
C) The impossibility of demonstrating attributes
D) The negation of all attributes

Key Takeaway
The impossibility of infinite regress and circularity demonstrates that God must be eternal and free from all dependence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
In what areas of your life do you still act as if something besides God is the ultimate cause or source? How does the impossibility of infinite regress challenge you to realign your trust solely in Him?
?
The passage declares that circular dependence is impossible for God. Have you ever unknowingly assigned a need or deficiency to God in your heart (e.g., that He needs our worship)? How will you actively replace that thought today with the affirmation of His absolute self-sufficiency?
?
It is obligatory to negate what is unbefitting of Allah. Take a moment to examine your hidden assumptions about Allah's nature. Is there any attribute you casually attribute to Him that contradicts His perfection? What concrete step will you take to correct that belief?

The Four Categories of Acts and the Sunni Position
Enumeration of three categories of acts with examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the second, third, and fourth categories of acts in Sunni theology and why the Mu'tazila erred on this issue.

Core Idea
God's command and will are distinct categories.

The previous lesson established the first category of acts: commanded and willed. Now the commentary introduces three more categories that complete the classification. These categories are crucial for understanding Sunni theology's view of divine will and human acts. The passage warns against the Mu'tazila who conflated command and will.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic passage once, then read the literal English translation. Let the author's own words present the categories before you interpret them.
2
Identify the second category and its example
The second category is the opposite of the first (commanded and willed). It refers to acts that are not commanded and not willed, like the disbelief of Abu Jahl.
Remember the first category from earlier: commanded and willed, e.g. faith from a believer.
3
Identify the third category and its example
The third category is commanded but not willed. Faith from Abu Jahl is commanded by God but He did not will it to occur from him.
4
Identify the fourth category and its example
The fourth category is the opposite of the third: not commanded but willed. Abu Jahl's disbelief is not commanded but it is willed by God.
5
Understand the warning against the Mu'tazila
The passage ends by stating that this classification is the Sunni position, and that the Mu'tazila slipped by denying that God wills evil acts.
The Mu'tazila argued that God cannot will what He commands against, but Sunnis maintain that God's will encompasses all things.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ أَرْبَعًا أَقْسَامَا فِي الْكَائِنَاتِ فَاحْفَظِ الْمَقَامَا. الثَّانِي: عَكْسُهُ، كَالْكُفْرِ مِنْهُ. الثَّالِثُ: مَأْمُورٌ بِهِ غَيْرُ مُرَادٍ، كَالْإِيمَانِ مِنْ أَبِي جَهْلٍ. الرَّابِعُ: عَكْسُهُ، كَكُفْرِهِ. فَاحْفَظْ هَذَا الْمَقَامَ عَلَى الْوَجْهِ الْمُتَقَدِّمِ، فَإِنَّهُ مَذْهَبُ أَهْلِ السُّنَّةِ وَالْجَمَاعَةِ، وَقَدْ زَلَّتْ فِيهِ أَقْدَامُ الْمُعْتَزِلَةِ.
You have indeed known four categories in the things that occur, so preserve the station. The second: its opposite, like disbelief from him. The third: commanded but not willed, like faith from Abu Jahl. The fourth: its opposite, like his disbelief. So preserve this station in the aforementioned manner, for it is the doctrine of Ahl al-Sunna wa al-Jama'a, and the feet of the Mu'tazila have slipped in it.
Source pages p. 61

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on a line of the poem that reminds the reader of four categories of acts. The comment explains the second, third, and fourth categories. The second category is the opposite of the first (which was presumably 'commanded and willed' like faith from a believer). For example, disbelief from Abu Jahl is the opposite of that. The third category is something that is commanded by God (i.e., God orders it) but is not willed by Him in the sense that He does not decree it to happen from that person at that time; example: faith from Abu Jahl (which God commanded but did not will to occur from him). The fourth is the opposite of the third: something not commanded and not willed, like the disbelief of Abu Jahl (which is not commanded and also not willed? Actually it is willed? Wait: the text says 'its opposite, like his disbelief'. The opposite of 'commanded but not willed' would be 'not commanded and willed'? Or 'not commanded and not willed'? The text is brief. However, it concludes by urging the reader to preserve this classification, as it is the doctrine of Ahl al-Sunna, and the Mu'tazila have slipped in this matter (by denying that God wills evil acts, for example).

Key Terms

مراد
أهل السنة والجماعة
المعتزلة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's command always indicates His will.
Correction: Sunni theology holds that God may command something without willing it to occur from a particular person.

Checkpoint

A) Its opposite, like disbelief.
B) Commanded but not willed, like faith from Abu Jahl.
C) Willed but not commanded.
D) Neither commanded nor willed.

Key Takeaway
The four categories of commanded/willed acts show that God's command and will are distinct, contrary to the Mu'tazila.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

Definition of T'alluq and Classification of Attributes
Al-Mashat defines t'alluq and provides a three-part classification of attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the technical meaning of t'alluq as it applies to divine attributes and how these attributes are classified based on the scope of their relation.

Core Idea
T'alluq is not just subsistence – it is an attribute's demand for an extra reality beyond itself.

Al-Mashat begins by clarifying a key term in theology: t'alluq, or the relation of an attribute to its object. He distinguishes it from simple subsistence, then explains that all meaning-attributes except life have such a relation. This leads to a threefold classification based on what each attribute relates to. Understanding this division is essential for grasping how God's attributes engage with creation.

Guided Path
1
Read and identify the definition
Read the Arabic and translation to find the definition of t'alluq. Notice that it is more than subsistence; it requires an additional entity.
The definition begins with 'هو اقتضاء'.
2
Observe the exception of life
Note that life is the only attribute of ma'ani without t'alluq. Why? Because life enables perception without needing an extra object.
The text says 'ما عدا الحياة فإنها لا تتعلق بشيء'.
3
Trace the classification
Follow the division: attributes are split into those with t'alluq and those without. Then those with t'alluq are further divided into three categories.
Look for the words 'يتعلق' and 'ينقسم'.
4
Reflect on the categories
Think about why knowledge and speech relate to all rational judgments, power and will only to possibles, and hearing and sight to all existents.
Each pair of attributes has a distinct scope of relation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
بل هي عبارة عن قيام المعاني بالذات. ثم شرع يبين تعلّق كل من صفات المعاني والتعلق: هو اقتضاء تعلقات الصفة أمرًا زائدا على قيامها بالذات، كاقتضاء العلم معلوما ينكشف به واقتضاء القدرة مقدورًا وهكذا، فقال: وواجب تعليق ذي أي هذه الصفات السابقة - صفات المعاني - حتما دوامًا تأكيد لمعنى الوجوب، ودواما زيادة تأكيد لأن الواجب النقلي شأنه ذلك، ما عدا الحياة فإنها لا تتعلق بشيء، إذ هي صفة تصحح لمن قامت به الإدراك، من غير أن تطلب زائدا على قيامها بمحلها، وقد عرفت أن هذا هو معنى التعلق، فيجب على كل مكلف أن يعتقد ذلك. واعلم أن هذه الصفات تنقسم: إلى ما يتعلق. وإلى ما لا يتعلق. والذي يتعلق ينقسم إلى ثلاثة أقسام: قسم يتعلق بجميع أقسام الحكم العقلي: الواجب، والجائز، والمستحيل، وهما صفتا العلم والكلام. وقسم يتعلق بجميع الممكنات، وهما صفتا القدرة، والإرادة. وقسم يتعلق بجميع الموجودات، وهما صفتا السمع والبصر.
Rather, it is an expression of the subsistence of meanings in the essence. Then he began to explain the relation (t'alluq) of each of the attributes of meanings, and relation (t'alluq) is: the requirement of the relations of the attribute for an additional matter beyond its subsistence in the essence, like knowledge requiring a known object that is disclosed by it, and power requiring a possible object, and so on. So he said: 'And it is necessary that the relation of these (attributes) — i.e., these aforementioned attributes, the attributes of meanings — (be) necessarily, perpetually' as an emphasis on the meaning of necessity, and 'perpetually' is an additional emphasis because the transmitted obligation is such, except life, for it does not relate to anything, because it is an attribute that makes the one in whom it subsists capable of perception, without requiring anything additional beyond its subsistence in its locus. And you have known that this is the meaning of relation (t'alluq), so it is obligatory upon every legally responsible person to believe that. And know that these attributes are divided: into those that have a relation, and those that do not have a relation. And that which has a relation is divided into three categories: a category that relates to all divisions of rational judgment: the necessary, the possible, and the impossible, and they are the two attributes of knowledge and speech. And a category that relates to all possible things, and they are the two attributes of power and will. And a category that relates to all existent things, and they are the two attributes of hearing and sight.
Source pages p. 66

Commentary
This passage defines the technical term 't'alluq' (relation or attachment) for the attributes of ma'ani (meaning-attributes) and then classifies these attributes. The author begins by explaining that t'alluq is not merely the subsistence (qiyam) of the attribute in the essence; rather, it is the attribute's requirement of an additional entity beyond itself. For example, knowledge requires a known object to be disclosed, and power requires a possible object to be effected. He then comments on the poet's line, emphasizing that all attributes of ma'ani necessarily have such a relation, perpetually, except for the attribute of life (hayat). Life is an attribute that enables perception but does not require anything beyond its own subsistence. The author stresses that this belief is obligatory for every mukallaf (legally responsible person). He then proceeds to a classification: attributes are divided into those that have t'alluq and those that do not. Those that have t'alluq are further divided into three categories. The first category includes knowledge and speech, which relate to all three rational judgments: wajib (necessary), ja'iz (possible), and mustahil (impossible). The second category includes power and will, which relate only to all possible things (mumkinat). The third category includes hearing and sight, which relate to all existent things (mawjudat). This classification is foundational for understanding how each attribute relates to its objects.

Key Terms
تعلق (t'alluq)
صفات المعاني (attributes of meanings)
قيام بالذات (subsistence in the essence)
واجب (necessary)
جائز (possible)
مستحيل (impossible)
الحياة (life)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: T'alluq is the same as subsistence (qiyam) of the attribute in the essence.
Correction: T'alluq is an additional requirement of the attribute for an extra entity beyond its subsistence.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, which attribute does NOT have a t'alluq (relation)?
A) Knowledge
B) Power
C) Life
D) Speech

Key Takeaway
T'alluq is the attribute's demand for an additional object, and the meaning-attributes are classified into three categories based on what they relate to.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you merely accept the classification of attributes as a theological fact, or have you genuinely pondered how each attribute's relation shapes your understanding of God? What difference does it make to you that knowledge requires a known object?
?
This passage declares it obligatory to believe in t'alluq for all attributes except life. Does your heart affirm this with certainty, or is there any hesitation? If there is, why?
?
The attribute of life is unique in that it requires no object beyond itself. How does this profound simplicity – that God's life is self-sufficient – challenge your tendency to measure God by your own experiences of need and relation?
