Definition of al-Ja'iz and Its Subdivisions
The author defines the third division (al-ja'iz) and explains its types: ijad, tark, ishqa', is'ad.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of al-ja'iz (the permissible) as it applies to Allah and its four main subdivisions: ijad, tark, ishqa', and is'ad.

Core Idea
Allah's actions are all permissible; He is not obligated to do anything.

This passage introduces the third and final rational judgment: al-ja'iz, or the permissible. The author defines it and then categorises the types of actions that fall under this category in relation to Allah. Understanding this concept is crucial for developing a correct theology of divine freedom and power.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic text carefully, then read the literal translation. Notice the key terms and their definitions.
2
Identify the definition of al-ja'iz
Find where the author defines al-ja'iz. Note that it includes both creating (ijad) and leaving (tark) possible things.
3
Distinguish between ijad and tark
Understand that ijad refers to the attachment of power to create, while tark is simply refraining from creating. Both are equally permissible.
4
Recognize ishqa' and is'ad as subtypes of ijad
Note that ishqa' (making miserable) and is'ad (making happy) are specific instances of ijad, emphasised because of their importance.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم شرع الناظم رحمه الله تعالى في بيان القسم الثالث، وهو الجائز، فقال: (وجائز في حقه تعالى) (الإيجاد)، والخلق للممكنات، سواء وجدت أو لم توجد. والإيجاد هو تعلق القدرة بوجود المقدور، فإن تعلقت بالحياة سمي: إحياء، وبالموت سمي: إماته، وبالمرزوق سمي: رَزْقًا - بفتح الراء ، وهذه التعلقات هي المسماة بصفات الأفعال (١). (والترك) بالرفع عطف على «الإيجاد»، أي: يجوز في حقه تعالى إيجاد الممكنات وتركها يعني أن إيجاد كل ممكن أو تركه أمر جائز في حقه تعالى (٢) ، إن شاء فعل، وإن شاء لم يفعله. قال المؤلف: ومن ذلك : بعثة الرسل عليهم الصلاة والسلام، فإن بعثتهم جائزة وليست بواجبة على الله تعالى، وكذلك إثابة العاصي، وتعذيب المطيع (٣). (والإشقاء) وهو خلق الكفر في العبد والعياذ بالله تعالى، ويسمى الخذلان - بكسر الخاء، والإضلال. (والإسعاد) - بكسر الهمزة مصدر أسعد، وهو خلق الطاعة في العبد، ويسمى بالهداية والتوفيق. والتَنصِيصُ على الإشقاء والإسعاد ، وإن كانا داخلين في الإيجاد: لمزيد الإيضاح والاهتمام بشأنها.
والهوامش:
(١) اعلم أن أسماءه تعالى على أقسام أسماء صفات، كالقادر والمريد والعالم والحي ... إلى آخره السبعة، وأسماء أفعال، كالخالق والرازق والمحيي والمميت.
(٢) في المطبوعة في حقه تعالى إيجاد إن شاء فعل.
(٣) شرح الخريدة» (ص ٢٣٥).
Then the author (may Allah have mercy on him) began explaining the third division, which is the permissible (al-ja'iz). He said: (Ja'iz in His right is) (al-ijad): creation of possible things, whether they exist or not. Al-ijad is the attachment of power (qudra) to the existence of the intended object. If it attaches to life, it is called giving life (ihya'); if to death, it is called causing death (imata); if to provision, it is called providing (rizq) with fatha on the ra'. These attachments are what are called attributes of actions (sifat al-af'al). (And al-tark) with damm is conjuncted to al-ijad, meaning: it is permissible in His right to create possible things and to leave them, meaning that creating or leaving each possible is a permissible matter in His right; if He wills, He does it, and if He wills, He does not do it. The author said: Among that is sending messengers, peace be upon them, for sending them is permissible and not obligatory upon Allah, and likewise rewarding the disobedient and punishing the obedient. (And al-ishqa') is creating disbelief (kufr) in the servant — may Allah protect — and it is called khidhlan (with kasr of the kha') and idlal. (And al-is'ad) with kasr of the hamza, is the verbal noun of as'ada, and it is creating obedience (ta'a) in the servant, and it is called guidance (hidaya) and tawfiq. And specifying ishqa' and is'ad, even though they are included in ijad, is for more clarification and concern about them. Footnotes: (1) Know that His names are of two types: names of attributes, like al-Qadir, al-Murid, al-'Alim, al-Hayy... up to the seven, and names of actions, like al-Khaliq, al-Raziq, al-Muhyi, al-Mumit. (2) In the printed version: 'in His right to create if He wills He does.' (3) From Sharh al-Kharida, p. 235.
Source pages p. 74

Commentary
This passage defines the third rational division (al-ja'iz) as it applies to Allah. The author explains that it includes creation (ijad) and leaving (tark), and then specifies two subtypes: making miserable (ishqa') and making happy (is'ad), which are included in ijad but emphasized because of their importance. The commentary also gives examples: sending messengers, rewarding sinners, punishing the obedient — all are permissible, not obligatory. The footnotes clarify that the names of Allah related to actions correspond to these attachments of power, and note a variant reading in the printed version.

Key Terms
جائز
إيجاد
ترك
إشقاء
إسعاد
صفات الأفعال
خذلان
إضلال
هداية
توفيق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that Allah is obliged to do certain things, such as always sending messengers or always rewarding the obedient.
Correction: The passage clarifies that sending messengers, rewarding the disobedient, and punishing the obedient are all permissible (ja'iz), not obligatory upon Allah. He does as He wills.

Checkpoint
What is the definition of al-ijad according to this passage?
A) Creating something from nothing
B) The attachment of power to the existence of the intended object
C) Giving life to the dead
D) Providing sustenance

Key Takeaway
All of Allah's actions are permissible; nothing is obligatory upon Him, and He is free to create or leave, to make happy or miserable.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If Allah is not obligated to do anything for you, how does that change the way you view your own prayers and supplications? Are you truly relying on His mercy rather than your own deeds?
?
The passage states that Allah may create disbelief in a servant. If you ever find your faith weak, does this truth make you fear that He might leave you, or does it drive you to seek His guidance more earnestly?
?
Since Allah's actions are all permissible and He does as He wills, are you willing to accept whatever He decides for you—whether happiness or misery—without questioning His justice? How can you cultivate that submission in your heart today?

4 min
The Contingency of the World: Proof of a Creator
Sanusi proves the world is contingent (hadith) and thereby necessitates a Creator.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the classical kalam proof that the world is temporally originated (hadith) and therefore requires a Creator, as articulated by Sanusi.

Core Idea
Change proves the world is not eternal; everything that begins needs a Maker.

Sanusi opens his proof by stating a fundamental truth: the world around us is not self-sufficient. It changes, it began, and it points beyond itself. In this passage, he lays out the logical steps from observing change to affirming the necessary existence of Allah. This is the cornerstone of the cosmological argument in Ash'ari theology.

Guided Path
1
State the thesis
Read the first line where Sanusi declares the world is without doubt temporally originated (hadith) and dependent (muftaqir). This is the claim to be proven.
2
Identify the evidence
Notice the reason given: 'because change has occurred in it.' Change is the observable proof that the world is not eternal.
3
Define key terms
Sanusi defines hadith as existence after non-existence, and qidam as its opposite. Understand these definitions clearly.
4
Connect effect to cause
The principle that every effect (athar) points to an effective cause (mu'aththir) is stated. Trace how the world as an effect necessitates a cause.
5
Reflect on the conclusion
The cause (the Creator) must have the attribute of existence necessarily. Reflect on the logic that leads from contingency to necessity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثُمَّ اعْلَمَنْ بِأنَّ هذا العَالَمَا * أني مَا سِوى اللهِ العَلِيِّ العَالِمَا * مِن غَيْرِ شَكٍّ حَادِثٌ مُفْتَقِرُ * لأَنَّهُ قَامَ بِهِ التَّغَيرُ * حُدُوتُهُ وُجُودُه بَعْدَ العَدَمَ * وضِدُّهُ هو الْمُسَمَّى بالقِدَمَ * فاعْلَمْ بأنَّ الوَصْفَ بالوُجُودِ * مِنْ وَاجِبَاتِ الوَاحِدِ الْمَعْبُودِ * إِذْ ظَاهِرُ بأنَّ كُلَّ أَثَرِ * يَهْدِي إِلَى مُؤثرٍ فَاعْتَبِرِ
Then know that this world, everything other than Allah the High, the Knower, is without doubt temporally originated (hadith) and dependent (muftaqir), because change has occurred in it. Its origination (huduth) is its existence after non-existence. Its opposite is what is called pre-eternity (qidam). So know that the attribute of existence (al-wujud) is among the necessary attributes of the One who is worshipped. For it is apparent that every effect (athar) points to an effective cause (mu'aththir), so reflect.
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage provides the classic kalam proof for the existence of God based on the contingency of the world. The author first asserts that the world (everything besides Allah) is without doubt 'hadith' (temporally originated) and 'muftaqir' (dependent/needy), because it undergoes change. Change implies that the world had a beginning. He defines 'huduth' as existence after non-existence, and its opposite as 'qidam' (pre-eternity). Since the world is hadith, its existence is not necessary in itself; therefore it requires a cause that gives it existence. That cause must be described with the attribute of existence necessarily. The author then states the principle that every effect (athar) leads to a cause (mu'aththir), which is the basis of the cosmological argument. This is a concise statement of the proof from contingency (dafil al-huduth).

Key Terms
حدوث
قدم
حَادِث
مُفْتَقِر
أثر
مؤثر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The world being 'hadith' means it is only a few thousand years old.
Correction: Hadith means temporally originated, i.e., not eternal. It does not specify a young age; it simply means the world began to exist after not existing, regardless of the time scale.

Checkpoint
What is the direct evidence given in the passage that the world is temporally originated (hadith)?
A) It is large and complex
B) It has many parts
C) Change occurs in it
D) It is dependent on Allah

Key Takeaway
The contingency of the world—its change and coming into existence—demonstrates the necessity of a Creator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You witness change every day—your body aging, your circumstances shifting. This passage says change proves the world is not eternal. If you truly internalized that you are a 'hadith' being, brought into existence after nothing, how would it change the way you cling to your possessions, plans, and reputation? Today, what is one thing you can let go of because it is not eternal?
?
The passage says 'every effect points to an effective cause.' Think of the last blessing you received—a meal, a moment of peace, a success. Did you see it as an effect pointing to the Giver, or did you take it as automatic? If you were to live the next 24 hours consciously seeing everything as an effect pointing to Allah, what would be different in your gratitude, your humility, and your fear of losing Him?
?
If this universe is contingent—needy at every moment—then your very existence is dependent on Allah right now, not just at the beginning. Does that unsettle you? Do you prefer to feel self-sufficient? What would it look like to live today in utter dependence on Allah, as a 'muftaqir' who cannot breathe without His permission? How will you remind yourself of that every hour?

The Proof for the World's Contingency
Sanusi gives a rational proof for the world's contingency.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how change (taghayyur) proves the world's origination (ḥudūth) and its dependence on a creator.

Core Idea
Change is the rational proof that everything besides God is contingent.

In this lesson, we encounter a foundational kalām argument: the world is originated and dependent because it undergoes change. This concise proof from Imam Sanusi's poem establishes that nothing in creation is self-sufficient. Let's read the passage and see how a single observation—change—leads to the conclusion that the world needs a creator.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic text and its literal translation. Notice the command 'Then know' which signals an important conclusion.
2
Identify the key terms
Find the terms ḥādith (originated), muftaqir (dependent), taghayyur (change), ḥudūth (origination), and qidam (eternality). These are fundamental in kalām.
3
Analyze the proof
The author says: because change occurs in the world, the world is originated and dependent. Ask yourself: why does change imply contingency?
Change indicates that something is not self-sufficient; it requires an external cause for its states.
4
Define the key concepts
Origination (ḥudūth) is existence after non-existence; its opposite is eternality (qidam). Understand these definitions precisely.
5
Connect to the existence of God
Reflect on how this proof forces a rational mind to accept the need for a Creator who is eternal and unchanging.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثُمَّ اعْلَمَنْ بِأنَّ هذا العَالَمَا * أني مَا سِوى اللهِ العَلِيِّ العَالِمَا مِن غَيْرِ شَكٍّ حَادِثٌ مُفْتَقِرُ * لأَنَّهُ قَامَ بِهِ التَّغَيرُ حُدُوتُهُ وُجُودُه بَعْدَ العَدَمَ * وضِدُّهُ هو الْمُسَمَّى بالقِدَمَ
Then know that this world, everything other than God, the High, the All-Knowing, is without doubt originated and dependent, because change occurs in it. Its origination is its existence after non-existence, and its opposite is what is called eternal.
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage is a concise proof for the world's contingency (ḥudūth). The author begins by commanding the reader to know that the entire universe, everything besides God, is 'originated' (ḥādith) and 'dependent' (muftaqir). The proof given is that change (taghayyur) occurs in the world—change is a sign of dependence on something else. The author then defines 'origination' (ḥudūth) as existence after non-existence, and contrasts it with 'eternality' (qidam), which is its opposite. This establishes the Ashʿarī principle that the world is contingent and requires a creator.

Key Terms
حَادِث
مُفْتَقِر
التَّغَير
حُدُوت
قِدَم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The proof for the world's createdness is based only on empirical observation of change.
Correction: The argument is rational: change is a logical property of the world, not merely an observed fact. The world's very nature entails change, which implies contingency.

Checkpoint
What is the proof given for the world's origination (ḥudūth) in this passage?
A) The world came after nothing.
B) Change occurs in the world.
C) The world is dependent on God.
D) The world is unique.

Key Takeaway
The world's change proves its originatedness, which necessitates an eternal, unchanging Creator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
This passage teaches that everything besides God is marked by change and therefore dependent. When you look at your own life, where is your security anchored? Are you investing your hopes in things that inevitably change—your health, relationships, wealth—or in the Eternal One who does not change? What would it cost you to shift that anchor today, and what would you gain?
?
The proof defines your existence as 'being after non-being.' You were nothing, then you were. Does that truth unsettle you? How should it change the way you spend your time, the attachments you hold, and the gratitude you show to the One who brought you from nothing?
?
If change is the sign of contingency, then your own experiences of change—the good and the hard—are constant reminders that you are not self-sufficient. In what ways do you try to deny this dependence? How can you let the changes you face drive you not to despair but to reliance on the only Unchanging Reality?

The Permissibility of God's Actions
Poetic statement of divine permissibility and rejection of obligation

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Ash'ari position that God's actions are not subject to obligation, and why imposing obligations on God is considered improper.

Core Idea
God's actions are free; He is not obligated to do anything.

This lesson introduces a foundational Ash'ari principle: God's actions are not bound by external obligations. The poet lists four specific actions that are permissible for God, and then rebukes those who claim God must do what is right. Let's examine the text and its implications for understanding divine freedom.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the two lines of poetry carefully. Notice the structure: first line lists permissible actions, second line warns against imposing obligation.
The rhyme and meter (rajaz) help memorization.
2
Identify the key terms
Find the words for 'permissible', 'existence', 'leaving', 'making miserable', 'making happy', and 'obligatory'. Understand their theological weight.
The term 'jā'iz' is central – it means not obligatory and not impossible.
3
Understand the four actions
The poet says that creation, non-creation, causing misery, and causing happiness are all permissible for God. Reflect on why these are chosen.
These cover the full spectrum of divine action regarding human destiny.
4
Analyze the condemnation
The second line says that saying 'doing what is right' is obligatory on God is 'bad manners'. Why is it a matter of adab and not just a theological error?
This highlights the Ash'ari view that God's transcendence precludes external obligation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وجَائِزُ فِي حَقِّهِ الإِيجَادُ * وَالتَّرْكُ والإشـقَـاء والإِسْعَادُ وَمَنْ يَقُلْ فِعْلُ الصَّلاحِ وَجَبًا * عَلَى الإِلَهِ قَدْ أَسَاءَ الأَدَبَا
And permissible in His right is giving existence, and leaving [i.e., not creating], and making miserable, and making happy. And whoever says that doing what is right is obligatory upon God has shown bad manners.
Source pages p. 16

Commentary
This passage asserts the Ashʿarī position that God's actions are not subject to obligation. It lists four actions that are jā'iz (permissible) for God: existence (ījād) meaning creating, leaving (tark) meaning not creating, making miserable (ishqā'), and making happy (is'ād). The author then condemns those who say that doing what is morally right (ṣalāḥ) is obligatory (wājib) upon God, calling it 'bad manners' (asā'a al-adab). This is a rejection of the Mu'tazilī doctrine that God must do what is best (al-aṣlaḥ). For Ashʿarīs, God's actions are not constrained by external obligation.

Key Terms
جائز
الإيجاد
الترك
الإشقاء
الإسعاد
فعل الصلاح
وجب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That 'permissible' here means God has moral permission to do evil or that He can act unjustly.
Correction: The term 'jā'iz' in this context means that God's actions are not constrained by any external obligation; it does not imply that God does evil. God is all-wise and all-just, but His justice is not defined by human standards.

Checkpoint
What does the author say about someone who claims that doing what is right (ṣalāḥ) is obligatory upon God?
A) Such a person is a disbeliever.
B) They have shown bad manners.
C) They are correct in belief.
D) They are merely mistaken but not blameworthy.

Key Takeaway
God's actions are not subject to external obligation; it is improper to claim that God must do anything.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever find yourself expecting God to treat you a certain way because you think you deserve it? How does this passage challenge that assumption?
?
When you face suffering, do you question God’s justice because you assume He should have prevented it? Based on this lesson, how should you respond to that internal question?
?
If God is not obligated to reward good, then what is the basis of your hope for Paradise? Does this make you more reliant on His mercy than on your deeds, and how should that change your daily life?

Proof of the World's Contingency through Change
Mashat proves the world's contingency (ḥudūth) through change, needing a creator.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the observed change in the world leads to the conclusion that the world is contingent (ḥādith) and therefore requires a creator, namely Allah.

Core Idea
Change in the world proves it is not eternal, but created.

This lesson introduces the central proof of kalam for God's existence: the argument from the contingency of the world. The poet defines the world as everything other than God and points to its constant change as evidence that it cannot be self-sufficient. By following this reasoning, you will see how Islamic theology builds a logical bridge from creation to the Creator.

Guided Path
1
Understand the context
The poem begins after establishing rational judgments. Note that this proof is the next step in knowing God.
The author uses 'then' to connect to previous material.
2
Define the world
The author explains that the world is everything other than God, consisting of substances (jawāhir) and accidents (aʿrāḍ). This definition is crucial for the argument.
The world includes all created entities.
3
Identify the evidence from change
The poem states that change occurs in the world. Any changing thing cannot be eternal; it must have a beginning (ḥudūth).
Observed change is direct proof of contingency.
4
Draw the conclusion
A contingent thing needs a creator (muftaqir). The creator is none other than Allah, as the world points to its maker.
The poet uses 'dependent' as a second predicate.
5
Clarify the term 'doubt'
The footnote explains that there is no real doubt; the proof is certain.
Doubt here means hesitation, but the proof is decisive.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الطريق الموصل إلى معرفة الله تعالى
ثُمَّ اعْلَمَنْ بِأنَّ هذا العَالَمَا * أني مَا سِوى اللهِ العَلِيِّ العَالِمَا
مِن غَيْرِ شَكُ حَادِثٌ مُفْتَقِرُ * لأنَّهُ قَامَ بِهِ التَّغَيُّرُ
ولما فرغ الناظم رحمه الله تعالى من بيان أقسام الحكم العقلي ووجوب معرفة الله تعالى على كل مكلف، أخذ في بيان الطريق الموصل إلى معرفة الله تعالى، وهو حدوث هذا العالم فقال:
فقال: (ثم) لما عرفت ما يجب عليك أيها المكلف شرعًا من حدوث العالم دليل على حق الله، وما يجوز ، وما يستحيل، فـ (اعلمن) بنون التوكيد الخفيفة، معرفة الله تعالى للحث والتنبيه ( بأن هذا العالما) المشاهد بجميع أجزائه، والألف فيه لإطلاق القافية.
وفسر العالم بقوله: (أي: ما سوى الله تعالى (العلي العالما) بكسر اللام، ونصبه على القطع للمدح، من جوهر وهو ما قام بنفسه أو عرض وهو ما قام بغيره من الجواهر، كالألوان.
من غير شك (۱) يتعلّق بقوله: (حادث) الواقع خبرًا عن «أن هذا العالما» أي موجود بعد عدم، يعني: أن حدوث هذا العالم ثابت بغير شك، لما يشاهده من التغيرات ولأنه محتاج إلى موجد يوجده من العدم إلى حيز الوجود، وليس إلا الله تعالى، وإليه أشار بقوله: (مفتقر) وهو خبر بعد خبر، وإن كان لازما للأول، وكلام الناظم رحمه الله تعالى
(۱) حقيقة الشك: التردد في الطرفين على السواء، ومراده به هنا مطلق التردد، الشامل للظن: وهو الطرف الراجح، والوهم: وهو المرجوح.
The path leading to knowledge of God Almighty
Then know that this world * i.e., everything other than God, the High, the Knowing
Without doubt, it is contingent and dependent * because change occurs in it.
And when the author (may God have mercy on him) finished explaining the divisions of rational judgment and the obligation of knowing God for every accountable person, he began to explain the path leading to knowledge of God Almighty, which is the contingency of this world, saying:
He said: (Then) after you know what is incumbent upon you, O accountable person, legally, from the contingency of the world as a proof for the truth of God, and what is possible, and what is impossible, so know (with the light nun of emphasis) knowledge of God, for encouragement and alerting, (that this world) observed in all its parts, and the alif is for the release of the rhyme.
And he interpreted the world by his saying: (i.e., everything other than God, the High, the Knowing) with kasra on the lam, and it is in the accusative as a detached praise, consisting of substance (jawhar) which is what subsists by itself, or accident (ʿaraḍ) which is what subsists in other than itself of substances, such as colors.
Without doubt (1) connects to his saying: (contingent) which is the predicate of 'that this world', i.e., existing after non-existence, meaning that the contingency of this world is established without doubt, because of the changes witnessed in it and because it is in need of an originator to bring it from non-existence into the realm of existence, and that is none but God Almighty, and to this he pointed by his saying: (dependent) which is a second predicate, although it is necessary for the first. And the author's words...
(1) The reality of doubt is hesitation between two sides equally, and here it is intended as absolute hesitation, including conjecture (ẓann): which is the preponderant side, and illusion (wahm): which is the less preponderant.
Source pages p. 41

Commentary
This passage is a proof (dalīl) for the existence of God through the contingency of the world (ḥudūth al-ʿālam). The author first states that after establishing the rational judgments, the poem now turns to the proof. He defines the world (al-ʿālam) as everything other than God, and divides it into substances (jawāhir) and accidents (aʿrāḍ). The proof: The world is observed to undergo change (taghayyur); anything that changes is contingent (ḥādith), meaning it exists after non-existence. A contingent thing needs a creator (muʿmin) to bring it into existence. The creator must be God alone, as He is the only one capable. The proof also includes a clarification of the term 'doubt' (shakk) in the footnote, explaining that it means absolute hesitation, including conjecture and illusion, but here it is used to assert certainty that the world is contingent without any doubt.

Key Terms
طريق
معرفة الله
عالم
جوهر
عرض
حادث
مفتقر
تغير
شك

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the proof is simply 'the world changes, so God exists' without the intermediate step that change implies temporal origination (ḥudūth).
Correction: The argument requires establishing that change proves the world is not eternal (ḥādith), and only then does it need a creator. The proof is not a direct leap from change to God.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the main evidence for the world's contingency?
A) The world is composed of atoms
B) The world undergoes change
C) The world is not God
D) The world is eternal

Key Takeaway
The argument from change establishes the world's contingency, which in turn proves the existence of God as its creator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You see change every day: your body ages, seasons turn, technology evolves. Yet do you live as if this world is permanent? What would change in your daily priorities if you truly believed that every changing thing is a sign that it was created by God and is utterly dependent on Him? Will you today stop and thank the Creator for one changing thing you usually take for granted?
?
This lesson proves the world’s contingency through change. But what about your own heart? Have you observed its changes—from faith to doubt, from certainty to confusion? If the world's change proves its need for a Creator, what does your own inner change prove? Are you relying on something other than God to stabilize your soul? What step can you take to anchor your heart in the Unchanging One?
?
The author says the world is ‘without doubt’ contingent. But do you sometimes act as if the world is self-sufficient? When you worry about your future, are you forgetting that everything in this world, including your very existence, is contingent and dependent on God every moment? What is one thing you can entrust to God today that you’ve been clutching as if it were eternal?

Allah's Existence
intermediate
10 min
Proof of the World's Temporality from Change
The commentator presents the syllogistic proof that the world is temporal because of change, concluding that all besides Allah is temporal.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the temporality of the world is proven through the observation of change and the principle that whatever is inseparable from temporal things is itself temporal.

Core Idea
The world's constant change proves it cannot be eternal—only Allah is eternal.

The commentator presents the central proof for the temporality of the world: the argument from change. This proof is foundational in Islamic theology because it establishes that the universe had a beginning and therefore requires a Creator. By carefully examining the evidence of change in both observable and seemingly stable phenomena, the commentator builds a logical case that everything other than Allah is temporal.

Guided Path
1
Read the syllogism outline
Understand the structure of the deductive argument presented in the first figure: the world is temporal, every temporal thing depends on a cause, therefore the world depends.
Notice the middle term is 'temporal' and is repeated in both premises.
2
Examine direct evidence of change
See how the commentator uses observable examples like motion after rest and light after darkness to show change from non-existence to existence or vice versa.
These are empirical proofs that some things are temporal.
3
Consider inferential evidence for stable objects
Understand that objects like mountains or planets, even if they seem permanent, can be proven temporal because they could change—there is no essential difference between bodies in accepting motion or rest.
This extends the proof to everything in the world.
4
Trace the conclusion
Follow how the temporality of accidents leads to the temporality of all bodies, and finally that everything besides Allah is temporal.
The opposite of temporality, pre-eternity, is unique to Allah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
من غير شك حادث مفتقر * لأنه قام به التغير ٨ حدوثه وجوده بعد العدم * وضده هو المسمى بالقدم ١٩ يشير إلى نظم قياس من الشكل الأول، الذي هو بديهي الإنتاج هكذا: العالم حادث، وكل حادث فهو مفتقر إلى محدث، ينتج بعد حذف الحد الأوسط المكرر العالم مفتقر إلى محدث. أما دليل كون العالم حادث، فأشار له بقوله: (لأنه قام به أي بالعالم التغير) من عدم إلى وجود ومن وجود إلى عدم إما بالمشاهدة كالحركة بعد السكون، والضوء بعد الظلمة، والعكس. وإما بالدليل، لأن ما شوهد سكونه مثلا على الدوام، كالجبال، أو حركته على الدوام، كالكواكب، جاز أن يثبت له العكس، إذ لا فرق بين جرم وجرم في قبول الحركة والسكون وما جاز على أحد المتماثلين جاز على الآخر. إن بعض التغيرات في الجبال، والكواكب، من علامات الحدوث يدل على ما سواه، وإذا جاز عدمها استحال قدمها، فتكون حادثة فجميع الأعراض حادثة، ويلزم من حدوثها حدوث جميع الأجرام لعدم انفكاكها عن الأعراض الحادثة، وكل ما لا ينفك عن فهو حادث، فظهر أن جميع ما سوى الله تعالى المعبر عنه بالعالم من أعراضه وأجرامه حادث، أي: موجود بعد أن لم يكن. (حدوثه) أي: العالم (وجوده) أي: عبارة عن وجوده (بعد العدم وضده أي الحدوث، هو المسمى بالقدم وهو خاص بالله تعالى، فهو القديم، ولا قديم سواه.
Without doubt it is temporal and dependent * because change has occurred in it. Its temporality is its existence after non-existence * and its opposite is called pre-eternity. He indicates by this the formulation of a syllogism of the first figure, which is evident in its conclusion, as follows: The world is temporal, and every temporal thing is dependent on a temporal originator, so after eliminating the repeated middle term the conclusion is: the world is dependent on a temporal originator. As for the proof that the world is temporal, he indicated it by his saying: (because change has occurred in it, i.e., in the world) from non-existence to existence and from existence to non-existence, either by observation like motion after rest, and light after darkness, and the opposite; or by evidence, because what is observed to be constantly at rest, like mountains, or constantly in motion, like the planets, it is possible that the opposite could be established for it, since there is no difference between one body and another in accepting motion and rest, and what is possible for one of two similar things is possible for the other. Indeed, some changes in mountains and planets are signs of temporality indicating to what is other than it, and if their non-existence is possible then their pre-eternity is impossible, so they are temporal. So all accidents are temporal, and from their temporality follows the temporality of all bodies because they are inseparable from temporal accidents, and everything that is inseparable from temporal things is temporal. So it became clear that everything other than Allah Almighty, expressed as the world, including its accidents and bodies, is temporal, i.e., existing after it was not. (Its temporality) i.e., of the world (is its existence) i.e., its existence (after non-existence) and its opposite, i.e., of temporality, is called pre-eternity, which is specific to Allah Almighty, for He is the Pre-eternal, and no pre-eternal other than Him.
Source pages p. 42

Commentary
This passage is a proof of the world's temporality (ḥudūth) based on change (taghayyur). The commentator explains that the poet's line 'because change has occurred in it' provides the evidence. He first outlines a syllogism in the first figure (the classical form): the world is temporal, and every temporal thing is dependent on a temporal originator, therefore the world is dependent. The proof that the world is temporal is change, which is a transition from non-existence to existence or vice versa. This change is observed directly in some things (e.g., motion after rest, light after darkness) and inferred in others by analogy. For things that appear constant, like mountains at rest or planets in motion, it is possible that they could change because there is no inherent difference between bodies in their capacity to accept motion or rest. If something could cease to exist, then it cannot be eternal; thus it must be temporal. Since all accidents (a‘rāḍ) are temporal, and bodies are never free from accidents, bodies must also be temporal. Therefore everything other than Allah—the entire world, both substances and accidents—is temporal, meaning it exists after not existing. Finally, the commentator explains the opposite of temporality: pre-eternity (qidam), which is unique to Allah. So the proof establishes that everything besides Allah is contingent and dependent on a creator.

Key Terms
تغير
حدوث
قدم
قياس
عرض
جوهر
محدث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Only things that are observed to change are temporal; stable objects like mountains could be eternal.
Correction: The commentator argues that even stable objects could change (since no body is inherently prevented from accepting motion/rest), so their possibility of change proves they are not eternal.

Checkpoint
How does the commentator prove that seemingly stable things like mountains are temporal?
A) By direct observation of their constant motion
B) By arguing that they could change since there is no difference between bodies in accepting motion and rest
C) By claiming that all bodies are made of fire and water
D) By quoting a verse from the Quran

Key Takeaway
The world's temporality is proven by the presence of change, which shows that everything besides Allah is created and dependent.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just learned that everything in the world—including your own body, your health, your thoughts, and your circumstances—is constantly changing. If your entire existence is renewed every moment by Allah, where do you find the arrogance to think that tomorrow belongs to you? Pause and list one specific area of your life where you behave as if you are in control. What would it mean to truly hand that over to the One who brings you into being each instant? Commit to one concrete action today that acknowledges your absolute dependence on Him.
?
The proof from change shows that even things that appear permanent—like mountains—are temporal because they could change. Reflect: what in your life have you been treating as 'eternal'—a relationship, a skill, a status, a belief about yourself? If it can change, it is not Allah. What fear arises when you consider that it might be taken away? Now, ask yourself: Are you more attached to that creation than to the Creator? Tonight, pray for the strength to love Him above all that passes away.
?
The argument relies on analogy: what is possible for one body is possible for another. Do you apply this same logic to yourself? If you see someone else fall into sin or suffer a trial, do you think 'that could never happen to me'? The lesson shows that nothing is inherently immune to change. Let this humble you. Think of one person you have judged for a weakness. Write down what you share with them in your capacity to change. Tonight, thank Allah that He has not yet tested you with that specific trial, and ask Him to keep you firm.

Allah's Existence

The Rational Proof from Artifacts to the Creator
Rational proof from artifacts to the Necessary Existence

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rational proof from artifacts (effects) to the existence of the Necessary Being and its implications for worship.

Core Idea
Every artifact points to its maker; creation points to the Creator.

This lesson presents the foundational rational proof for God's existence used in Islamic theology. The commentator explains that every effect (artifact) requires an effecter (maker), and invites reflection on the universe. This leads to the recognition of God as the Necessary Existence and humanity's purpose of worship.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then the translation. Notice the compressed style of the original matn (the poetic line) and the explanatory commentary.
The passage begins with a line of poetry that serves as the proof statement.
2
Understand key terms: أثر, مؤثر, صنعة
Identify these three terms in the Arabic. أثر (effect/artifact) is anything made, مؤثر (effecter/maker) is the one who makes it, and صنعة (craftsmanship/artifact) is a specific instance of an effect.
These terms are central to the rational argument.
3
Recognize the logical structure
The proof is a simple conditional: every artifact (صنعة) indicates a maker (صانع). The commentator states that an artifact is inconceivable without a maker. This is the core of the argument from design.
This is not a scientific observation but a rational necessity.
4
Apply the logic to the universe
The passage then calls for reflection (فاعتبر) on the heavens and earth, citing Quranic verses that creation is not in vain. This extends the argument from individual artifacts to the entire cosmos.
The verses reinforce that creation has purpose and a Creator.
5
Identify the conclusions about God
From the reflection, one learns that Allah is the Necessary Existence (الواجب الوجود), the Sovereign, and the only One worthy of worship. This links the proof to tawheed.
The proof does not just show a designer but the Necessary Being.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
إذ ظاهر بأن كل أثر يهدي إلى مؤثر فاعتبر حد شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي وجود الله ثم ذكر البرهان العقلي على وجوده تعالى بوجود صفته جَلَّ وَعَلا الدليل على فقال: (إذ ظاهر بأن كل أثر أي صنعة ( يهدي) بفتح الياء ، يدل (إلى) مؤثر) أي: على صانعه، إذ لا تعقل صنعة بدون صانع. وإذا علمت أن كل صنعة تدل على وجود صانعها (فاعتبر) وتأمل في ملكوت السموات والأرض، ودقائق الحكم، سبحانك ما خلقت هذا باطلا ، قال الله تعالى: ﴿ وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَعِبِينَ مَا خَلَقْنَهُمَا إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ ﴾ ، فتعلم بذلك أن الله تعالى هو الواجب الوجود المالك المعبود، فتهتدي بذلك إلى ما خلقت لأجله وَمَا خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ . قال الناظم في شرحه: (ثم تترقى إلى وفور حبّه وشكره، فيترتب على ذلك تفجير ينابيع الحكمة من قلبك، وتقعد في مقعد صدق عند ربك». اهـ. ولنذكر لك شيئًا من ذلك ليكمل لك الاعتبار وتقيس عليه غيره حسبما أمر الناظم رحمه الله تعالى، وننقل لك كلام سيدي أبي الفضل أحمد محمد بن عبد الكريم السَّكَنْدَري في «التنوير في إسقاط بن التدبير» لمناسبته لما قال رحمه الله تعالى:
Since it is apparent that every effect indicates an effecter, so reflect. Commentary by Sheikh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat al-Makki al-Maliki: The existence of God. Then he mentioned the rational proof for His existence, Mighty and Majestic, with the reality of His attribute. He said: (Since it is apparent that every effect, i.e., artifact, indicates, with the fatha on the ya, points to an effecter) i.e., to its maker, for an artifact is inconceivable without a maker. And if you know that every artifact indicates the existence of its maker, (then reflect) and contemplate the dominion of the heavens and the earth and the subtle wisdom. Glory be to You, You did not create this in vain. Allah Almighty said: {And We did not create the heavens and the earth and what is between them in play; We did not create them except with truth, but most of them do not know.} So by that you learn that Allah Almighty is the Necessary Existence, the Sovereign, the Worshipped, and you are guided thereby to what you were created for: {And I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me.} The author said in his commentary: 'Then you ascend to the abundance of His love and gratitude, and as a result fountains of wisdom gush forth from your heart, and you sit in a seat of truth with your Lord.' End. And we will mention something of that to complete your reflection and to analogize upon it according to what the author commanded, and we will transmit the words of Sayyidi Abu al-Fadl Ahmad Muhammad ibn Abd al-Karim al-Sakandari in 'al-Tanwir fi Isqat al-Tadbir' due to its appropriateness to what the author said.
Source pages p. 44

Commentary
This passage is a proof for God's existence using the argument from design (from artifacts to a maker). It begins with the poetic line: 'Since it is apparent that every effect indicates an effecter, so reflect.' The commentator explains that this is the rational proof: every artifact (industry) points to its maker, because an artifact cannot be conceived without a maker. He then instructs the reader to contemplate the heavens and earth and the wisdom therein, citing Quranic verses that creation is not in vain. From this, one concludes that God is the Necessary Existence, the Owner, the Worshipped. This then leads to recognizing the purpose of creation: worship. The author's commentary adds that this recognition leads to love, gratitude, and wisdom. Finally, the commentator promises to give further examples to complete the reflection, quoting another scholar.

Key Terms
أثر
مؤثر
صنعة
صانع
البرهان العقلي
الواجب الوجود

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The argument from design only proves a generic designer, not the Necessary Existence of the Islamic God.
Correction: The passage explicitly concludes that Allah is the Necessary Existence (الواجب الوجود), the Sovereign, and the only one worthy of worship, thus affirming tawheed.

Checkpoint
According to the proof, what does every artifact (صنعة) indicate?
A) The skill of the artisan.
B) The existence of a maker.
C) The material it is made of.
D) The purpose of creation.

Key Takeaway
Every created thing points to a Creator, who is the Necessary Existence and the sole object of worship.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just read that every artifact points to its maker. Look around you right now—what artifacts are within your sight? Do you truly accept that they point to a Maker, or do you live as if the universe is self-made? Will you consciously affirm this connection today?
?
The passage calls you to reflect on the dominion of the heavens and earth. When was the last time you actually paused to contemplate the creation, not out of habit but to feel the presence of the Creator? Will you set a time today, even for two minutes, to do this?
?
The author says this reflection leads to love, gratitude, and wisdom gushing from the heart. Are you satisfied with a dry, intellectual belief, or do you long for that living relationship? What small step will you take this week to move from knowing the proof to tasting the reality?

Allah's Existence

The Rational Proof from Effect to Cause
Rational proof from effect to cause with reflection on creation

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rational proof for God's existence based on the self-evident principle that every effect requires an efficient cause, and how this leads to reflection on creation and worship.

Core Idea
Every artifact points to its maker – this is the foundation of rational belief in God.

This lesson introduces a classical rational argument for God's existence rooted in the observable fact that every artifact (sin'a) must have a maker. The commentator presents the proof concisely, then expands it by inviting reflection on the heavens and earth. By internalizing this argument, you will strengthen your intellectual conviction in the Creator and understand the ultimate purpose of your existence.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the literal translation. Let the author's flow come through before interpreting.
The passage is concise; note the key terms 'athar' and 'mu'aththir'.
2
Identify the core premise and its justification
Find the statement that every effect indicates a cause. Notice that the commentator treats this as self-evident (zāhir) and provides a brief explanation: an artifact is inconceivable without a maker.
This premise is the foundation of the entire proof.
3
See how the proof expands through reflection and Quranic verses
After establishing the premise, the passage calls you to reflect on the heavens and earth. Two Quranic verses are quoted to reinforce that creation is not in vain and that its purpose is worship.
Reflection (i'tibār) is the act of drawing lessons from observation.
4
Understand the conclusion and its implications
The passage concludes that God is the Necessary Existence, the Owner, the Worshipped. It then ties this to human purpose: worship. The author's commentary adds that this leads to love, gratitude, and wisdom.
The proof is not merely intellectual; it transforms the heart.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وجود الله
ثم ذكر البرهان العقلي على وجوده تعالى بوجود صفته جَلَّ وَعَلا الدليل على فقال: (إذ ظاهر بأن كل أثر أي صنعة ( يهدي) بفتح الياء ، يدل (إلى) مؤثر) أي: على صانعه، إذ لا تعقل صنعة بدون صانع. وإذا علمت أن كل صنعة تدل على وجود صانعها (فاعتبر) وتأمل في ملكوت السموات والأرض، ودقائق الحكم، سبحانك ما خلقت هذا باطلا، قال الله تعالى: ﴿ وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَعِبِينَ مَا خَلَقْنَهُمَا إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ ﴾ (۱) ، فتعلم بذلك أن الله تعالى هو الواجب الوجود المالك المعبود، فتهتدي بذلك إلى ما خلقت لأجله وَمَا خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ (۲). قال الناظم في شرحه» (۳) : (ثم تترقى إلى وفور حبّه وشكره، فيترتب على ذلك تفجير ينابيع الحكمة من قلبك، وتقعد في مقعد صدق عند ربك». اهـ.
The existence of God. Then he mentioned the rational proof for His existence, may He be exalted, by the existence of His attribute, may He be glorified and exalted, evidence for it, so he said: (Since it is apparent that every effect [athar], that is, every artifact [sin'a], leads [with the fatha on the ya'] to an efficient cause [mu'aththir]) i.e., to its maker, for an artifact is inconceivable without a maker. And when you know that every artifact indicates the existence of its maker, (then reflect [i'tabar]) and contemplate the dominion of the heavens and the earth and the subtleties of wisdom. Glory be to You, You did not create this in vain. God Almighty said: {And We did not create the heavens and the earth and what is between them in play; We did not create them except with truth, but most of them do not know} (Surah ad-Dukhan, 38-39). So by that you know that God Almighty is the Necessary Existence, the Owner, the Worshipped, and you are guided by that to what you were created for: {And I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me} (Surah adh-Dhariyat, 56). The author said in his commentary: (Then you ascend to the abundance of His love and gratitude, and there follows from that the bursting forth of springs of wisdom from your heart, and you sit in a seat of truth with your Lord). End.
Source pages p. 44

Commentary
This passage presents a rational proof for the existence of God based on the principle that every effect (athar) or artifact (sin'a) necessarily indicates a cause (mu'aththir) or maker. The commentator begins by quoting the poetic line: 'Since it is apparent that every effect leads to an efficient cause, then reflect.' He explains that this is self-evident: no artifact can exist without a maker. He then expands the proof by inviting the reader to contemplate the heavens and earth, quoting Quranic verses (44:38-39 and 51:56) to reinforce that creation is not in vain and that its purpose is worship. The proof concludes that God is the Necessary Existence (al-wajib al-wujud), the Owner, and the Worshipped. The author (al-nazim) then adds that reflection leads to love, gratitude, and wisdom. The passage is a classic kalam argument from contingency or teleology, grounding faith in rational observation.

Key Terms
أثر
مؤثر
صنعة
اعتبار
واجب الوجود

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the proof assumes a causal principle without justification, but the passage treats the principle as self-evident.
Correction: The commentator explicitly says 'it is apparent' (zāhir) and explains that an artifact without a maker is inconceivable. The proof is not based on a borrowed axiom but on immediate rational intuition.

Checkpoint
What is the core premise of the rational proof presented in this passage?
A) The universe is eternal and uncreated.
B) Every effect or artifact points to an efficient cause.
C) God's attributes are known only through scripture.
D) Humans are created for worship, which is the goal of reflection.

Key Takeaway
The rational proof from effect to cause establishes that the universe requires a Creator, and reflecting on it leads to worship of God as the Necessary Existence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you look at the sky or your own body, do you honestly see them as mere accidents with no maker? If not, why do you live as if they have no purpose?
?
The proof says every artifact points to its maker. Your own existence is an artifact—what does it point to? Have you ever truly sat with that question?
?
If you truly believed that God is the Necessary Existence, the Owner, the Worshipped, would your priorities for tomorrow be different? What would change?

The Purpose of Reflection and the Early Stages of Creation
Introduction to the reflection and the first stages of human creation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the early stages of human formation, from sperm in the loins to placement in the womb, demonstrate God's meticulous management and care.

Core Idea
Every stage of your origin is a direct act of God's providence.

This passage opens a lengthy reflection on human creation as evidence of divine care. The author introduces a quote from al-Sakandari that traces the human beginning from a sperm-drop in the loins, through preservation across generations, to the womb. Each detail is presented not as mere biology but as a sign of God's continuous management and mercy.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the passage carefully, noting the introduction by the commentator and the beginning of al-Sakandari's reflection.
Pay attention to the repeated phrase 'tawallāka bi-tadbīrihi'.
2
Identify the early stages
List the stages mentioned: sperm in loins, preservation through ancestors, transfer to womb, and the womb as a receptive place.
Each stage is introduced with 'then' (thumma).
3
Reflect on the concept of tadbīr
Consider how the word 'tadbīr' (management) frames the entire passage. Why is it emphasized?
This is the central theme of divine providence.

⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولنذكر لك شيئًا من ذلك ليكمل لك الاعتبار وتقيس عليه غيره حسبما أمر الناظم رحمه الله تعالى، وننقل لك كلام سيدي أبي الفضل أحمد محمد بن عبد الكريم السَّكَنْدَري في «التنوير في إسقاط بن التدبير» (٤) لمناسبته لما قال رحمه الله تعالى: إن الله عَزَّوَجَلَّ جعلك أيها الإنسان نطفة مستودعة في الأصلاب وتولاك بتدبيره هنالك، حافظا لك وحافظا لما أنت فيه، موصلا لك المدد بواسطة من أنت فيه من الآباء إلى أبيك آدم، ثم قذفك في رحم الأم، فتولاك بحسن تدبيره حينئذ، وجعل الرحم قابلة لك أرضًا يكون فيها نباتك، ومستودعًا تعطى فيها حياتك، ثم جمع بين النطفتين وألف بينهما، فكنت عنهما، لما بنيت عليه الحكمة الإلهية من أن الوجود كله مبني على سر الازدواج، ثم جعلك بعد النطفة مضغة. ثم فتق سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَى في المضغة صورتك، وأقام بنيَتَكَ، ثم نفخ فيك الروح بعد ذلك، ثم غذاك دم الحيض في رحم الأم، وأجرى عليك رزقه من قبل أن يُخرجك إلى الوجود، ثم أبقاك في رحم الأم حتى قويت أعضاءك، واشتدت أركانك ، ليهيئك إلى البروز إلى ما قسم لك أو عليك، وليبرزك إلى دار يتعرف فيها بفضله وعدله إليك. ثم لما أنزلك إلى الأرض عِلم سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَى أنك لا تستطيع خشونات المطاعم، وليس لك أسنان، ولا أرجاء تستعين بها على ما أنت طاعم فأجرى الثديين بالغذاء اللطيف، ووكل بها مستحث الرحمة في قلب الأم، كلما وقف اللبن عن البروز استحثته الرحمة التي جعلها لك في الأم، مستحئًا لا يفتر، ومستنهضا لا يقصر. ثم إنه شغل الأب والأم بتحصيل مصالحك، والرأفة عليك، والنظر بعين المودة منهما إليك، وما هي إلا رأفة ساقها إليك، وإلى
And we will mention to you some of that to complete for you reflection and to measure others by it according to what the author, may God have mercy on him, commanded, and we will quote for you the words of my master Abu al-Fadl Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Abd al-Karim al-Sakandari in 'al-Tanwir fi Isqat al-Tadbir' (4) because of its relevance to what he said, may God have mercy on him: Indeed God, may He be glorified and exalted, made you, O human being, a sperm-drop stored in the loins, and took charge of you with His management there, preserving you and preserving what you are in, conveying to you the supply through the mediation of those from the fathers to your father Adam, then He cast you into the womb of the mother, and took charge of you with His good management then, and made the womb receptive to you as an earth in which your growth occurs, and a repository in which your life is given, then He brought together the two drops and joined them together, so you came from them, because of what divine wisdom is built upon, that all existence is built on the secret of pairing, then He made you after the drop a clot (mudgha). Then He, glorified and exalted, opened in the clot your form, and erected your structure, then He breathed into you the spirit after that, then He fed you with menstrual blood in the mother's womb, and supplied you with His provision before bringing you out into existence, then He kept you in the mother's womb until your limbs became strong and your pillars became firm, to prepare you for emerging to what has been apportioned for you or against you, and to bring you out to an abode in which He makes Himself known through His bounty and justice to you. Then when He brought you down to the earth, He, glorified and exalted, knew that you cannot handle rough foods, and you have no teeth, nor limbs with which you can help yourself regarding what you eat, so He caused the two breasts to flow with delicate nourishment, and entrusted to them a stirrer of mercy in the mother's heart, whenever the milk stops flowing, the mercy that He placed for you in the mother stirs it, a stirrer that does not slacken, and a raiser that does not fall short. Then He occupied the father and mother with obtaining your interests, and compassion for you, and looking at you with the eye of affection from them to you, and it is nothing but compassion drawn to you, and to...
Source pages p. 44

Commentary
This passage is an extended illustrative argument for divine providence through a detailed narration of human creation. The author introduces it as a reflection (iʿtibār) to complement the previous rational proof. He quotes al-Sakandari, who describes the stages: the sperm-drop stored in loins, preservation through ancestors, transfer to the womb, the joining of male and female drops based on the 'secret of pairing' in existence, development into a clot (mudgha), formation of the body, breathing of the spirit, nourishment via menstrual blood, growth until strong enough to be born, and then after birth, the provision of milk through the mother's breasts, which are stimulated by divinely placed mercy. Finally, God engages the parents in caring for the child. The passage emphasizes that every detail is a direct act of God's management (tadbīr) and mercy (rahma), concluding that the parents' love is itself a mercy drawn from God. The incomplete ending suggests the argument continues, but the portion is sufficient to show the teleological and providential design in human life.

Key Terms
نطفة
تدبير
صلب
رحم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Human creation is a purely natural process without divine involvement.
Correction: This passage shows that every step, from the sperm to the womb, is explicitly attributed to God's direct management and care.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, where was the sperm-drop initially stored?
A) In the womb
B) In the loins
C) In the mother's heart
D) In the earth

Key Takeaway
The earliest stages of human existence are not random but are personally managed by God.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
How often do you pause to consider that your very existence—from a single sperm-drop to your birth—was personally and directly managed by God? What would change in your daily gratitude if you truly internalized this fact?
?
If God took charge of you even before you were formed, how can you now trust Him with your current worries and future plans? Are there areas of your life where you still act as if you are self-sufficient?
?
The passage describes the womb as 'receptive earth' for your growth. In what ways are you making your heart a receptive earth for God's guidance? What barriers do you need to remove?

Development in the Womb: From Pairing to Preparation for Birth
Detailed enumeration of embryonic development stages as divine acts.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the stages of embryonic development mentioned in this passage as signs of divine wisdom and care.

Core Idea
Your formation from a drop to a complete human is a miracle of divine artistry.

Continuing the reflection, the passage now details the miraculous processes inside the womb. It begins with the joining of the male and female drops, based on the universal principle of pairing, then describes the transformation into a clot, the formation of the body, the breathing of the spirit, and the nourishment through menstrual blood. Each step is governed by God's management to prepare the child for life outside.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the passage carefully, noting the sequence of stages.
Look for the word 'then' (thumma) as a marker of progression.
2
Identify the key stages
List the stages: pairing of drops, clot, form, spirit, nourishment, growth.
3
Reflect on the purpose
Consider the final goal: to prepare for emergence and to be in a place where God's bounty and justice are known.
This shows the teleological nature of the process.

?

?

?

Divine Provision After Birth: Milk and Parental Care
Description of postnatal divine provision through breastfeeding and parental compassion.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the post-birth provisions of milk and parental compassion are presented as continued acts of divine mercy.

Core Idea
Even after birth, God's direct care continues through milk and the mercy He places in parents.

The reflection concludes with God's care after birth. Recognizing the infant's inability to eat solid food, God provides milk through the mother's breasts, and instills mercy in her heart to stimulate breastfeeding. Moreover, He engages both parents in loving care, which is ultimately a mercy drawn from God Himself. This shows that divine providence does not end at birth but continues throughout life.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Focus on the details of breastfeeding and the role of mercy.
Note the description of the infant's helplessness.
2
Analyze the mechanism of milk flow
Identify how the passage describes the cause of milk flow – a 'stirrer of mercy' in the mother's heart.
This is not a biological reflex but a divine arrangement.
3
Reflect on parental love
Consider the statement that parental compassion is 'nothing but compassion drawn to you' from God.
This emphasizes that all love ultimately comes from God.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what did God place in the mother's heart to ensure milk flow?
A) A biological instinct
B) A stirrer of mercy
C) A physical pump
D) The father's assistance

Key Takeaway

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recall your mother's sleepless nights and tireless care for you as an infant, do you truly see that as Allah's mercy flowing through her, or do you still reduce it to mere biology? How does this lesson change your gratitude towards her and towards Allah?
?
If every cry of a baby is met with a rush of milk from the mother's breast because Allah placed a 'stirrer of mercy' in her heart, what does that tell you about how He responds to your own cries of need? When was the last time you cried to Him with the same helplessness as an infant?
?
The passage says that even the compassion of your parents is 'compassion drawn to you' from Allah. Have you ever thanked Allah for making your parents love you? Or have you taken their love as something you deserve? What would change if you started seeing every kind word from them as a direct gift from Allah?

Allah's Existence
beginner
4 min
Every Effect Points to an Effecter
Introduction of the principle and its application to early human creation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the foundational principle that every observable effect in the universe requires an intelligent effecter, and see how this is demonstrated through the earliest stages of human creation.

Core Idea
Every effect is a signpost leading directly to its Maker.

This lesson introduces the central axiom of the author's argument: every effect (أثر) points to an effecter (مؤثر). The commentary begins by tracing this principle through the earliest stages of human life, from the sperm in the loins to the formation in the womb, showing that even the secret of pairing in creation reflects divine wisdom. Read the passage to grasp how the author establishes this foundational idea.

Guided Path
1
Read the poetic line and opening commentary
Start with the highlighted line and the first paragraphs of the commentary. Notice how the author addresses you directly ('O human') to make the argument personal.
The principle is stated in the poetry and then unpacked.
2
Identify the stages mentioned
Trace the sequence: sperm in loins, protection, delivery to womb, combination of gametes. Each stage is an effect that demands an agent.
3
Reflect on the 'secret of pairing'
The author mentions that all existence is built on the secret of pairing (سر الازدواج). Consider how this universal pattern points to a wise designer.
This connects to the broader kalam argument from design.
4
Connect to the learning objective
Summarise how the principle of effect→effecter is already evident in these early stages.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
إِذْ ظَاهِرٌ بأَنَّ كُلَّ أَثَرِ * يَهْدِي إِلَى مُؤَنَّرٍ فَاعْتَبِرِ
شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي
إن الله عَزَّوَجَلَّ جعلك أيها الإنسان نطفة مستودعة في الأصلاب وتولاك بتدبيره هنالك، حافظا لك وحافظا لما أنت فيه، موصلا لك المدد بواسطة من أنت فيه من الآباء إلى أبيك آدم، ثم قذفك في رحم الأم، فتولاك بحسن تدبيره حينئذ، وجعل الرحم قابلة لك أرضًا يكون فيها نباتك، ومستودعًا تعطى فيها حياتك، ثم جمع بين النطفتين وألف بينهما، فكنت عنهما، لما بنيت عليه الحكمة الإلهية من أن الوجود كله مبني على سر الازدواج، ثم جعلك بعد النطفة مضغة. ثم فتق سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَى في المضغة صورتك، وأقام بنيَتَكَ، ثم نفخ فيك الروح بعد ذلك، ثم غذاك دم الحيض في رحم الأم، وأجرى عليك رزقه من قبل أن يُخرجك إلى الوجود، ثم أبقاك في رحم الأم حتى قويت أعضاءك، واشتدت أركانك ، ليهيئك إلى البروز إلى ما قسم لك أو عليك، وليبرزك إلى دار يتعرف فيها بفضله وعدله إليك. ثم لما أنزلك إلى الأرض عِلم سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَى أنك لا تستطيع خشونات المطاعم، وليس لك أسنان، ولا أرجاء تستعين بها على ما أنت طاعم فأجرى الثديين بالغذاء اللطيف، ووكل بها مستحث الرحمة في قلب الأم، كلما وقف اللبن عن البروز استحثته الرحمة التي جعلها لك في الأم، مستحئًا لا يفتر، ومستنهضا لا يقصر. ثم إنه شغل الأب والأم بتحصيل مصالحك، والرأفة عليك، والنظر بعين المودة منهما إليك، وما هي إلا رأفة ساقها إليك، وإلى العباد في مظاهر الآباء والأمهات تعريفا بالوداد، وفي حقيقة الأمر ما كَفَلَك إلا ربوبيته وما حضنك إلا إلهيته، ثم ألزم الأب القيام بك إلى حين البلوغ، وأوجب عليه ذلك رأفة منه بك، ثم رفع قلم التكليف عنك إلى أوان تكمّل الإفهام، وذلك عند الاحتلام، ثم إلى أن صرت كهلًا لم يقطع عنك نوالا ولا فضلا، ثم إذا انتهيت إلى الشيخوخة، ثم إذا قدمت عليه، ثم إذا حشرت إليه، ثم إذا أقامك بين يديه، ثم إذا سلمك من عقابه، ثم إذا أدخلك دار ثوابه، ثم إذا كشف عنك وجود حجابه، وأجلسك مجلس أوليائه وأحبابه، قال تعالى: ﴿ إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَنَهَرٍ فِي مَقْعَدِ صِدْقٍ عِندَ مَلِيكٍ مُّقْتَدِرٍ ﴾. فلأي إحسانه تشكر، ولأي آلائه تذكر، واستمع قوله تعالى: ﴿وَمَا بِكُم مِّن نِعْمَةٍ فَمِنَ اللَّهِ﴾، تعلم أنك لم تخرج ولن تخرج عن إحسانه، ولن يعدوك وجود فضله وامتنانه، وإن أردت البيان في تقلبات أطوارك فاستمع ما قال الله سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَى : ﴿ وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَنَ مِن سُلَالَةٍ مِّن طِينٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلْتَهُ نُطْفَةً فِي قَرَارٍ مَّكِينٍ ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا النُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْمُضْغَةَ عِظَامًا فَكَسَوْنَا الْعِظَمَ لَحْمًا ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَهُ خَلْقًا وَاخَرَّ فَتَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخَلِقِينَ ثُمَّ إِنَّكُم بَعْدَ ذَلِكَ لَمَيِّتُونَ * ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ تُبْعَثُونَ ﴾، تبدو لك بوارقها، وتبسط عليك شوارقها، وفي
Since it is apparent that every effect guides to an effecter, so consider. Commentary of Sheikh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashshāt al-Makki al-Mālikī. Indeed, God, mighty and majestic, made you, O human, a sperm deposited in the loins and tended to you with His governance there, protecting you and protecting what you are in, delivering to you supply by means of those among whom you are from the fathers to your father Adam. Then He cast you into the womb of the mother, so He tended to you with His good governance then, and made the womb capable for you as a land in which is your growth, and a repository in which you are given your life. Then He combined the two gametes and united them, and you came from them, because divine wisdom is built upon the fact that all existence is built on the secret of pairing. Then He made you after the sperm a clot. Then He, glorified and exalted, split open in the clot your form, and erected your structure, then breathed into you the spirit after that. Then He nourished you with menstrual blood in the womb of the mother, and flowed upon you His provision before He brought you out to existence. Then He kept you in the womb of the mother until your limbs strengthened and your pillars became firm, to prepare you to emerge to what is apportioned for you or against you, and to bring you out to an abode in which He makes known His bounty and justice to you. Then when He brought you down to the earth, He, glorified and exalted, knew that you cannot bear harsh foods, and you have no teeth, nor limbs to help you with what you eat. So He made the two breasts flow with delicate nourishment, and entrusted with them a stimulator of mercy in the mother's heart; whenever milk stops flowing, the mercy which He placed for you in the mother stimulates it, a stimulator that does not slacken, and an urge that does not fall short. Then He occupied the father and mother with securing your interests, and compassion for you, and looking at you with the eye of affection from them. It is nothing but compassion brought to you, and to the servants in the manifestations of fathers and mothers as a definition of love. In reality, nothing sponsored you but His lordship, and nothing nursed you but His divinity. Then He obliged the father to support you until puberty, and made that obligatory on him out of compassion from Him to you. Then He lifted the pen of obligation from you until the time of completion of understanding, which is at the time of emission. Then until you became an adult, He did not cut off from you any gift or favor. Then when you reach old age, then when you come to Him, then when you are gathered to Him, then when He makes you stand before Him, then when He saves you from His punishment, then when He admits you to the abode of His reward, then when He removes from you the veil of His hijab, and seats you in the seat of His saints and beloved ones. He, exalted, said: 'Indeed, the righteous will be within gardens and rivers in a seat of truth near an Omnipotent King.' So for which of His beneficence do you thank, and for which of His favors do you remember? And listen to His, exalted, saying: 'And whatever blessing you have is from God.' You learn that you have never left and will never leave His beneficence, and the existence of His favor and grace will never miss you. And if you want an explanation in the fluctuations of your stages, listen to what God, glorified and exalted, said: 'And certainly did We create man from an extract of clay. Then We placed him as a sperm-drop in a firm lodging. Then We made the sperm-drop into a clinging clot, and We made the clot into a lump [of flesh], and We made the lump into bones, and We clothed the bones with flesh; then We brought him forth as another creation. So blessed is God, the best of creators. Then indeed, after that you are to die. Then indeed, you will be resurrected on the Day of Resurrection.' The lightning flashes appear to you, and the flashes spread over you, and in...
Source pages p. 45

Commentary
This passage consists of an introductory poetic line and a lengthy commentary that serves as an extended proof of God's existence and attributes through the observation of human creation and divine care. The commentator begins by stating the principle from the poem: that every observable effect (أثر) points to an effecter (مؤثر). The commentary then proceeds to argue this principle by tracing the stages of human life from conception to the afterlife, each stage being an effect that requires an intelligent and powerful agent. The commentary follows a chronological order. First, it describes how God prepares the human being as a sperm in the loins of fathers, then moves it to the womb, and combines the two gametes. This is linked to the divine wisdom that all existence is built on the secret of pairing (سر الازدواج). Next, the commentary details the development in the womb: clot, lump, bones, clothing with flesh, and the breathing of the spirit. Then it describes birth and the provision of milk through the mother's breasts, which are stimulated by mercy. It then speaks of the parents' care, which is ultimately from God's lordship and divinity. The commentary then moves to the stages of life: childhood, puberty, adulthood, old age, and then the events after death: being brought to God, standing before Him, being saved from punishment, admitted to Paradise, and ultimately seeing God without veil. The passage concludes with Quranic verses (Surah al-Qamar, Surah an-Nahl, Surah al-Mu'minun) that reinforce the argument that all blessings and the stages of creation are from God alone. The overall structure is a cumulative argument: the entire human life is a series of effects that point to a single, wise, and powerful effecter, namely God. The commentary does not present a separate formal proof but rather uses narrative and Quranic citation to build the case.

Key Terms
أثر (effect)
مؤثر (effecter/agent)
الحكمة الإلهية (divine wisdom)
سر الازدواج (secret of pairing)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that natural processes alone account for human reproduction without need for a divine agent.
Correction: The author insists that each stage, from the preservation of the sperm to the combination of gametes, is an effect that requires an effecter. The 'secret of pairing' indicates design, not chance.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what does the phrase 'secret of pairing' (سر الازدواج) refer to?
A) The union of sperm and egg
B) The divine wisdom that all existence is built on pairing
C) The pairing of mother and father
D) The pairing of body and soul

Key Takeaway
The very beginning of human life is a series of effects that unmistakably point to a wise and powerful Creator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just read that God preserved you as a sperm in your father's loins and guided you through every stage to birth. Have you ever truly thanked Him for that specific preservation? If not, what stops you from making a sincere du'a of gratitude right now, acknowledging His direct care for you?
?
The passage says the womb was made 'a land in which is your growth.' Do you realize that your entire existence depended on that temporary sanctuary? How would your daily worries shrink if you lived with the constant awareness that the same God who prepared that womb for you is now preparing every circumstance of your life?
?
If every effect must have an effecter, and you are an effect, why do you sometimes act as if you are self-sufficient? In what area of your life today do you need to consciously return to the recognition that you are not the source of your own existence? Commit right now to one specific action that acknowledges your dependence on God.

Module 7 · Lesson 12
Next →
Allah's Existence
beginner
5 min
Divine Craftsmanship in the Womb and Birth
Detailed description of embryonic development and breastfeeding as proof of divine agency.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the stages of embryonic development and birth demonstrate the wisdom and power of the Creator.

Core Idea
From clot to infant, every transformation is a miracle of divine design.

In this lesson, the commentary continues tracing the effects of divine action by detailing the formation of the human body in the womb, from clot to bones to spirit, and then the provision of milk through the mother's breasts. Each step is an effect that can only be explained by a wise and benevolent agent. Read on to see the intricate care involved.

Guided Path
1
Read the second section
Begin from 'ثم جعلك بعد النطفة مضغة' and read through the description of the clot, bones, flesh, and spirit.
Notice the precise order of creation.
2
Identify the effects
Each transformation (clot, lump, bones, flesh) is an effect. Ask: Could these happen without a powerful effecter?
3
Reflect on the provision of milk
The commentary describes the milk supply as a mercy-driven mechanism. Note the role of the mother's heart and the stimulation of milk.
This shows not just power but also compassion.
4
Connect to the overarching argument
Summarize how this section reinforces the principle that every effect points to God.

شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي

عِظَامًا فَكَسَوْنَا الْعِظَمَ لَحْمًا ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَهُ خَلْقًا وَاخَرَّ فَتَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخَلِقِينَ ثُمَّ إِنَّكُم بَعْدَ ذَلِكَ لَمَيِّتُونَ * ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ
