Since it is apparent that every effect guides to an effecter, so consider. Commentary of Sheikh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashshāt al-Makki al-Mālikī. Indeed, God, mighty and majestic, made you, O human, a sperm deposited in the loins and tended to you with His governance there, protecting you and protecting what you are in, delivering to you supply by means of those among whom you are from the fathers to your father Adam. Then He cast you into the womb of the mother, so He tended to you with His good governance then, and made the womb capable for you as a land in which is your growth, and a repository in which you are given your life. Then He combined the two gametes and united them, and you came from them, because divine wisdom is built upon the fact that all existence is built on the secret of pairing. Then He made you after the sperm a clot. Then He, glorified and exalted, split open in the clot your form, and erected your structure, then breathed into you the spirit after that. Then He nourished you with menstrual blood in the womb of the mother, and flowed upon you His provision before He brought you out to existence. Then He kept you in the womb of the mother until your limbs strengthened and your pillars became firm, to prepare you to emerge to what is apportioned for you or against you, and to bring you out to an abode in which He makes known His bounty and justice to you. Then when He brought you down to the earth, He, glorified and exalted, knew that you cannot bear harsh foods, and you have no teeth, nor limbs to help you with what you eat. So He made the two breasts flow with delicate nourishment, and entrusted with them a stimulator of mercy in the mother's heart; whenever milk stops flowing, the mercy which He placed for you in the mother stimulates it, a stimulator that does not slacken, and an urge that does not fall short. Then He occupied the father and mother with securing your interests, and compassion for you, and looking at you with the eye of affection from them. It is nothing but compassion brought to you, and to the servants in the manifestations of fathers and mothers as a definition of love. In reality, nothing sponsored you but His lordship, and nothing nursed you but His divinity. Then He obliged the father to support you until puberty, and made that obligatory on him out of compassion from Him to you. Then He lifted the pen of obligation from you until the time of completion of understanding, which is at the time of emission. Then until you became an adult, He did not cut off from you any gift or favor. Then when you reach old age, then when you come to Him, then when you are gathered to Him, then when He makes you stand before Him, then when He saves you from His punishment, then when He admits you to the abode of His reward, then when He removes from you the veil of His hijab, and seats you in the seat of His saints and beloved ones. He, exalted, said: 'Indeed, the righteous will be within gardens and rivers in a seat of truth near an Omnipotent King.' So for which of His beneficence do you thank, and for which of His favors do you remember? And listen to His, exalted, saying: 'And whatever blessing you have is from God.' You learn that you have never left and will never leave His beneficence, and the existence of His favor and grace will never miss you. And if you want an explanation in the fluctuations of your stages, listen to what God, glorified and exalted, said: 'And certainly did We create man from an extract of clay. Then We placed him as a sperm-drop in a firm lodging. Then We made the sperm-drop into a clinging clot, and We made the clot into a lump [of flesh], and We made the lump into bones, and We clothed the bones with flesh; then We brought him forth as another creation. So blessed is God, the best of creators. Then indeed, after that you are to die. Then indeed, you will be resurrected on the Day of Resurrection.' Commentary
This passage consists of an introductory poetic line and a lengthy commentary that serves as an extended proof of God's existence and attributes through the observation of human creation and divine care. The commentator begins by stating the principle from the poem: that every observable effect (أثر) points to an effecter (مؤثر). The commentary then proceeds to argue this principle by tracing the stages of human life from conception to the afterlife, each stage being an effect that requires an intelligent and powerful agent. The commentary follows a chronological order. First, it describes how God prepares the human being as a sperm in the loins of fathers, then moves it to the womb, and combines the two gametes. This is linked to the divine wisdom that all existence is built on the secret of pairing (سر الازدواج). Next, the commentary details the development in the womb: clot, lump, bones, clothing with flesh, and the breathing of the spirit. Then it describes birth and the provision of milk through the mother's breasts, which are stimulated by mercy. It then speaks of the parents' care, which is ultimately from God's lordship and divinity. The commentary then moves to the stages of life: childhood, puberty, adulthood, old age, and then the events after death: being brought to God, standing before Him, being saved from punishment, admitted to Paradise, and ultimately seeing God without veil. The passage concludes with Quranic verses (Surah al-Qamar, Surah an-Nahl, Surah al-Mu'minun) that reinforce the argument that all blessings and the stages of creation are from God alone. The overall structure is a cumulative argument: the entire human life is a series of effects that point to a single, wise, and powerful effecter, namely God. The commentary does not present a separate formal proof but rather uses narrative and Quranic citation to build the case.

Key Terms
نطفة (sperm)
مضغة (clot/lump)
روح (spirit)
دم الحيض (menstrual blood)
رزق (provision)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think embryonic development and breastfeeding are purely natural processes without divine intervention.
Correction: The author describes each step as a direct act of God (He made, He breathed, He nourished, He kept, He made the breasts flow) and attributes the mercy mechanism to divine design.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what does God do after forming the structure of the fetus?
A) He gives it bones
B) He breathes the spirit into it
C) He provides milk
D) He brings it out of the womb

Key Takeaway
The intricate stages of human formation and sustenance are undeniable signs of a wise and compassionate Creator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You were nourished by menstrual blood in the womb—something you would find repulsive now. Does your soul's pride still feel entitled to refuse the humble means of your own creation? How can you cultivate gratitude for the very 'lowly' processes Allah used to bring you into existence?
?
Allah placed a 'mercy stimulator' in your mother's heart that moved her to feed you even when she was exhausted. When was the last time you thanked her for that selflessness? And more importantly, what mercy are you withholding from others that Allah has placed in your capacity to give?
?
Your limbs and teeth were not ready at birth; Allah knew you could only handle milk. In what area of your life are you refusing Allah's gradual provision—demanding solid 'answers' or 'results' before He has built your capacity to handle them? Will you trust His timing and feed on whatever nourishment He provides today, even if it feels small?

Divine Care from Parents to the Afterlife
Continuation of the proof through parental care, life stages, and afterlife, with Quranic backing.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how God's care extends through parents, upbringing, and into the afterlife, making the case for His lordship and mercy.

Core Idea
From the love of parents to the gardens of Paradise, every blessing flows from God alone.

This final section of the commentary traces God's care beyond birth: He enlists parents as instruments of mercy, guides the child through stages of life, and ultimately brings the believer to eternal reward. The passage culminates in Quranic verses that declare all blessings from God. Read it to see how the argument from effects reaches its ultimate conclusion.

Guided Path
1
Read the section on parents
Notice how the author says the parents' care is actually God's lordship and divinity in disguise. The father's obligation is from God's compassion.
2
Trace the stages of life
From childhood to old age, and then the events after death: standing before God, salvation, Paradise, and vision of God.
3
Reflect on the Quranic citations
The author quotes verses from Surah an-Nahl, Surah al-Qamar, and Surah al-Mu'minun to reinforce that all blessings are from God.
4
Summarize the overall argument
Explain how the entire human life, from beginning to end, is a series of effects that point to one Effecter.

تستطيع خشونات المطاعم، وليس لك أسنان، ولا أرجاء تستعين بها على ما أنت طاعم فأجرى الثديين بالغذاء اللطيف، ووكل بها مستحث الرحمة في قلب الأم، كلما وقف اللبن عن البروز استحثته الرحمة التي جعلها لك في بالوداد، وفي حقيقة الأمر ما كَفَلَك إلا ربوبيته وما حضنك إلا إلهيته، ثم ألزم الأب القيام بك إلى حين البلوغ، وأوجب عليه ذلك رأفة منه بك، ثم رفع قلم التكليف عنك إلى أوان تكمّل الإفهام، وذلك عند تعالى: ﴿وَمَا بِكُم مِّن نِعْمَةٍ فَمِنَ اللَّهِ﴾، تعلم أنك لم تخرج ولن تخرج عن إحسانه، ولن يعدوك وجود فضله وامتنانه، وإن أردت البيان في تقلبات أطوارك فاستمع ما قال الله سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَى : ﴿ Since it is apparent that every effect guides to an effecter, so consider. Commentary of Sheikh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashshāt al-Makki al-Mālikī. Indeed, God, mighty and majestic, made you, O human, a sperm deposited in the loins and tended to you with His governance there, protecting you and protecting what you are in, delivering to you supply by means of those among whom you are from the fathers to your father Adam. Then He cast you into the womb of the mother, so He tended to you with His good governance then, and made the womb capable for you as a land in which is your growth, and a repository in which you are given your life. Then He combined the two gametes and united them, and you came from them, because divine wisdom is built upon the fact that all existence is built on the secret of pairing. Then He made you after the sperm a clot. Then He, glorified and exalted, split open in the clot your form, and erected your structure, then breathed into you the spirit after that. Then He nourished you with menstrual blood in the womb of the mother, and flowed upon you His provision before He brought you out to existence. Then He kept you in the womb of the mother until your limbs strengthened and your pillars became firm, to prepare you to emerge to what is apportioned for you or against you, and to bring you out to an abode in which He makes known His bounty and justice to you. Then when He brought you down to the earth, He, glorified and exalted, knew that you cannot bear harsh foods, and you have no teeth, nor limbs to help you with what you eat. So He made the two breasts flow with delicate nourishment, and entrusted with them a stimulator of mercy in the mother's heart; whenever milk stops flowing, the mercy which He placed for you in the mother stimulates it, a stimulator that does not slacken, and an urge that does not fall short. Then He occupied the father and mother with securing your interests, and compassion for you, and looking at you with the eye of affection from them. It is nothing but compassion brought to you, and to the servants in the manifestations of fathers and mothers as a definition of love. In reality, nothing sponsored you but His lordship, and nothing nursed you but His divinity. Then He obliged the father to support you until puberty, and made that obligatory on him out of compassion from Him to you. Then He lifted the pen of obligation from you until the time of completion of understanding, which is at the time of emission. Then until you became an adult, He did not cut off from you any gift or favor. Then when you reach old age, then when you come to Him, then when you are gathered to Him, then when He makes you stand before Him, then when He saves you from His punishment, then when He admits you to the abode of His reward, then when He removes from you the veil of His hijab, and seats you in the seat of His saints and beloved ones. He, exalted, said: 'Indeed, the righteous will be within gardens and rivers in a seat of truth near an Omnipotent King.' So for which of His beneficence do you thank, and for which of His favors do you remember? And listen to His, exalted, saying: 'And whatever blessing you have is from God.' You learn that you have never left and will never leave His beneficence, and the existence of His favor and grace will never miss you. And if you want an explanation in the fluctuations of your stages, listen to what God, glorified and exalted, said: 'And certainly did We create man from an extract of clay. Then We placed him as a sperm-drop in a firm lodging. Then We made the sperm-drop into a clinging clot, and We made the clot into a lump [of flesh], and We made the lump into bones, and We clothed the bones with flesh; then We brought him forth as another creation. So blessed is God, the best of creators. Then indeed, after that you are to die. Then indeed, you will be resurrected on the Day of Resurrection.' The lightning flashes appear to you, and the flashes spread over you, and in...
Source pages p. 45

Commentary
This passage consists of an introductory poetic line and a lengthy commentary that serves as an extended proof of God's existence and attributes through the observation of human creation and divine care. The commentator begins by stating the principle from the poem: that every observable effect (أثر) points to an effecter (مؤثر). The commentary then proceeds to argue this principle by tracing the stages of human life from conception to the afterlife, each stage being an effect that requires an intelligent and powerful agent. The commentary follows a chronological order. First, it describes how God prepares the human being as a sperm in the loins of fathers, then moves it to the womb, and combines the two gametes. This is linked to the divine wisdom that all existence is built on the secret of pairing (سر الازدواج). Next, the commentary details the development in the womb: clot, lump, bones, clothing with flesh, and the breathing of the spirit. Then it describes birth and the provision of milk through the mother's breasts, which are stimulated by mercy. It then speaks of the parents' care, which is ultimately from God's lordship and divinity. The commentary then moves to the stages of life: childhood, puberty, adulthood, old age, and then the events after death: being brought to God, standing before Him, being saved from punishment, admitted to Paradise, and ultimately seeing God without veil. The passage concludes with Quranic verses (Surah al-Qamar, Surah an-Nahl, Surah al-Mu'minun) that reinforce the argument that all blessings and the stages of creation are from God alone. The overall structure is a cumulative argument: the entire human life is a series of effects that point to a single, wise, and powerful effecter, namely God. The commentary does not present a separate formal proof but rather uses narrative and Quranic citation to build the case.

Key Terms
ربوبية (lordship)
إلهية (divinity)
تكليف (obligation/pen of duty)
جنات (gardens)
مليك مقتدر (Omnipotent King)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that parents are the ultimate source of care and that God's role is distant.
Correction: The author insists that it is actually God's lordship and divinity that sponsor and nurse the child; parents are merely veils.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what does God do 'after admitting you to the abode of His reward'?
A) He seats you in the seat of His saints
B) He removes the veil of His hijab
C) He gives you a palace
D) He asks you to thank Him

Key Takeaway
God's care encompasses every stage of existence, from the love of parents to the vision of His face in Paradise.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you reflect on the love and care of your parents, can you honestly say that you see it as a manifestation of God's lordship over you? If not, what is holding you back from that recognition, and what concrete step will you take today to shift your perspective?
?
The passage details God's care from birth through every stage, even into the afterlife. Which stage of your life right now—childhood memories, current responsibilities, or fears of old age—do you most struggle to see as an expression of God's mercy? How will you actively thank Him for that stage tonight before you sleep?
?
You have just read that every blessing comes from God and that you will be asked 'for which of His beneficence do you thank?' Identify one blessing you have been taking for granted or attributing to your own effort. What will you do within the next hour to acknowledge that it is from Him alone?

Causality and Created Things
intermediate
5 min
Refutation of Natural Causality and Inherent Power
Sanusi refutes those who attribute effects to nature, cause, or inherent power, declaring it kufr or bid'a.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sunni theologians reject belief in independent natural causation and inherent powers, and how such views lead to logical impossibilities like infinite regress or circular reasoning.

Core Idea
Only Allah is the true agent; attributing effects to nature or inherent power is kufr or bid'a.

In this lesson, we examine a poetic passage from Sanusi's al-Kharida that refutes false doctrines about causality. The author clearly distinguishes between what constitutes kufr (disbelief) and what constitutes bid'a (innovation). He then provides a rational argument: if creatures had independent causal power, it would lead to impossible consequences like infinite regress (tasalsul) or circularity (dawr).

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic lines once, then the translation. Notice the concise, rhyming style of the matn.
The passage is from a didactic poem, so each line delivers a self-contained point.
2
Identify the three false doctrines and their rulings
Find where Sanusi mentions 'tab'' (nature), 'illa' (cause), and 'quwwa mawdi'a' (inherent power). Note the ruling for each: kufr for the first two, bid'a for the third.
These rulings are based on the principle that only Allah has true causal efficacy.
3
Understand the argument against inherent power
Read the last two lines: if one claims an inherent power, it must itself be temporally originated (hadith), leading to tasalsul and dawr. Ask why these are impossible.
Tasalsul (infinite regress) and dawr (circularity) are both logically invalid in kalam.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومَنْ يَقُلْ بالطَّبْعِ أو بِالعِلَّهُ * فَذاكَ كُفْرٌ عِنْدَ أَهْلِ الْمِله * ومَنْ يَقُلْ بالقُوَّةِ الْمُودَعَةِ * فَذاكَ بِدْعِي فلا تَلْتَفِتِ * لَوْ لَم يَكُنْ مُنْصِفًا بِها لَزِمْ * حُدُوتُهُ وَهُوَ مُحالٌ فَاسْتَقِمْ * لأَنَّهُ يُفْضِي إلى التَّسَلْسُل * والدَّوْرِ وَهُوَ الْمُستَحِيلُ الْمُنْجَلِي
And whoever says by nature (al-tab') or by cause (al-'illa) – that is kufr according to the people of the religion. And whoever says by the inherent power (al-quwwa al-mawdi'a) – that is bid'a, so do not pay attention. If he were not just with it, there would necessarily follow its temporal origination (huduth), which is impossible, so be upright. Because it leads to infinite regress (al-tasalsul) and circularity (al-dawr), which are manifestly impossible.
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage refutes false doctrines about causation. The author declares that attributing effects to 'tab'' (nature) or 'illa' (cause) is kufr (disbelief) because it denies that Allah is the sole effective agent. Similarly, saying there is a 'quwwa mawdi'a' (an inherent power placed in creatures) is bid'a (innovation). The author then argues: if one were not fair with this (i.e., if one denies that effects come directly from Allah), then it would imply that the supposed cause (the 'quwwa') is itself temporally originated (hadith), leading to tasalsul (infinite regress) and dawr (circular reasoning), both impossible. Thus, only Allah is the true agent.

Key Terms
الطبع
العلة
القوة المودعة
التسلسل
الدور

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Refuting natural causality means denying all empirical causation, such as fire burning or water quenching thirst.
Correction: Sanusi's refutation targets the idea that nature or created things have independent causal power apart from Allah. Empirical causation is accepted as a customary pattern upheld by Allah, not as an independent force.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the ruling on saying that effects come from nature (tab')?
A) It is permissible
B) It is bid'a
C) It is kufr
D) It is recommended

Key Takeaway
Attributing causal efficacy to anything other than Allah is either kufr or bid'a, and any such view leads to logical impossibilities like infinite regress or circular reasoning.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
In what areas of your life do you find yourself attributing outcomes to 'luck', 'effort', or 'natural laws' rather than directly to Allah's will? How does this passage expose that mindset as potentially kufr or bid'a, and what concrete step can you take today to realign your belief?
?
If you truly believed that no created thing—including your own strength—has any independent power, how would your prayer, your trust (tawakkul), and your fear of Allah change? What holds you back from that level of certainty?
?
The passage says that inherent power leads to infinite regress. Have you ever felt burdened by the idea that you are the ultimate cause of your own success or failure? How does accepting that all power belongs to Allah alone free you from that burden, and what would it look like for you to live that freedom tomorrow?

Causality and Created Things
beginner
3 min
Refutation of Natural Causation and Deposited Powers
Sanusi condemns two erroneous views on causality.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Ash'ari theology denies causal efficacy to anything other than Allah and classifies belief in natural causality as disbelief and in deposited powers as innovation.

Core Idea
Only Allah has true causal efficacy; all other claims are either kufr or bid'a.

This short but decisive passage from al-Bahja al-Saniyya cuts to the heart of Islamic theology of causality. The author, Imam al-Sanusi, asserts that genuine causal effect belongs exclusively to Allah. He then delivers a sharp ruling: attributing causality to nature or to a created cause constitutes disbelief, while attributing it to a 'deposited power' in creatures is an innovation. This lesson unpacks these rulings and their theological basis.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then read the translation. Notice the uncompromising language.
The passage is in verse, so the phrasing is concise.
2
Identify the two rulings
The passage condemns two views: (1) believing in nature or cause as an independent agent is kufr; (2) believing in a deposited power is bid'a. Notice the different severity.
Kufr is disbelief; bid'a is innovation, less severe but still forbidden.
3
Reflect on the theological principle
Why does Ash'ari theology reject any intermediary causation? Think about Allah's absolute sovereignty and the impossibility of creatures having independent causal power.
This connects to the core idea of tawhid.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والفِعْلِ، فَالتَّأْثِيرُ لَيْسَ إِلَّا * لِلوَاحِدِ القَهَّارِ جَلَّ وعلا ومَنْ يَقُلْ بالطَّبْعِ أو بِالعِلَّهُ * فَذاكَ كُفْرٌ عِنْدَ أَهْلِ الْمِله ومَنْ يَقُلْ بالقُوَّةِ الْمُودَعَةِ * فَذاكَ بِدْعِي فلا تَلْتَفِتِ
And as for action, efficacy belongs only to the One, the Subduer, Mighty and Exalted. And whoever says by nature or by cause, that is unbelief according to the people of the religion. And whoever says by a power deposited [in creatures], that is an innovation, so do not pay attention.
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage denies causal efficacy to anything other than God. The author asserts that true effect (ta'thīr) belongs exclusively to God, the One and Overpowering (al-Qahhār). He then condemns two erroneous views: (1) those who attribute effect to 'nature' (tab') or 'cause' ('illa) are guilty of kufr; (2) those who say creatures have a 'deposited power' (quwwa mūda'a) are guilty of bid'a (innovation). Both positions compromise God's exclusive sovereignty. This is a standard Ashʿarī denial of secondary causality.

Key Terms
التأثير
الطبع
العلة
القوة المودعة
كفر
بدعة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think Ash'aris accept that creatures have real causal power derived from God.
Correction: The passage explicitly calls belief in a deposited power an innovation, showing that no creature has any independent causal efficacy.

Checkpoint
What is the ruling on someone who says that created objects have a deposited power (quwwa mūda'a) causing effects?
A) It is kufr (disbelief).
B) It is bid'a (innovation).
C) It is acceptable as long as they believe in God.
D) It is recommended.

Key Takeaway
In Ash'ari theology, attributing causal efficacy to anything other than Allah is false: if it is a natural or philosophical cause it is disbelief, and if it is a deposited power it is innovation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you succeed in something, do you ever secretly feel it was due to your own skill or effort, forgetting that only Allah has true efficacy? How would your gratitude change if you internalized this lesson fully?
?
Think of a recent trial: did you instinctively blame a person, a system, or 'bad luck' instead of turning to Allah as the sole agent? What concrete step can you take tomorrow to train your heart to see Allah's hand in everything?
?
This passage tells us that even attributing power to a 'deposited' ability in creatures is an innovation. Is there any area in your life where you trust in your own 'deposited power' (like intelligence, wealth, or relationships) rather than in Allah alone? Can you identify one specific reliance you need to repent from today?

Causality and Created Things
intermediate
5 min
Negation of Natural Causality: Allah Alone Creates All Effects
Commentator explains Ash'ari kasb doctrine and denies causal efficacy to created things.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Ash'ari doctrine that all effects are directly created by Allah alone, and that created things have no independent causal power.

Core Idea
Created things have no real effect; Allah alone creates all outcomes.

This passage from the commentary on Al-Kharida explains a central Ash'ari principle: the denial of natural causality. The commentator clarifies that when we say 'fire burns' or 'food satiates,' we do not mean that these things cause the effect by their own power, but rather that Allah creates the effect at the moment of contact. The passage uses the concept of kasb to distinguish human responsibility from divine creation.

Guided Path
1
Understand the meaning of kasb
The passage begins by quoting 'You used to do' and explains human actions are in terms of acquisition (kasb/iktisab), not creation (ijad/ikhtira'). Understand this key distinction: humans acquire actions, Allah creates them.
Kasb is a core Ash'ari concept that reconciles divine omnipotence with human accountability.
2
Note the denial of causal efficacy
The commentator then extends the same principle to all ordinary phenomena: fire, food, water, stars, knife. They have no effect by nature, by causal power, or by a deposited power.
This denial includes three categories: nature (tab'), causal power ('illa), and deposited power (quwwa mawda'a).
3
Recognize the exclusive divine agency
The passage concludes that all effect belongs to Allah alone, by pure choice, upon the existence of these things. This affirms that Allah is the sole agent.
The Quranic verse about Prophet Ibrahim and the fire serves as proof.
4
Reflect on the Quranic proof
The commentator cites the verse 'We said, O fire, be cool and safe' to demonstrate that even fire's burning can be suspended by Allah's command.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ، فذلك من حيث الكسب والاكتساب، لا من حيث الإيجاد والاختراع. فلا تأثير للأمور العادية، فالنار لا تحرق بطبعها، وكذلك الطعام في الشبع، والماء في الرِّي وفي إنبات الزرع، والكواكب في إنضاج الفواكه وغيرها، وكذا السكين في القطع، كل ذلك لا تأثير له لا بالطبع، ولا بالعلة، ولا بقوة أودعها الله فيها، بل التأثير في ذلك كله لله تعالى وحده، بمحض اختياره عند وجود هذه الأشياء، ﴿ قُلْنَا يَا نَارُ كُونِي بَرْدًا وَسَلَامًا ﴾
You used to do, so that is in terms of acquisition and earning, not in terms of creation and invention. Ordinary things have no effect, so fire does not burn by its nature, likewise food in satiation, water in quenching thirst and in causing plants to grow, the stars in ripening fruits and other things, and likewise the knife in cutting; all of that has no effect neither by nature, nor by causal power, nor by a power that Allah deposited in them. Rather, the effect in all of that belongs to Allah alone, by pure choice, upon the existence of these things. He said: 'We said, O fire, be cool and safe.'
Source pages p. 51

Commentary
This passage is an explanatory note on the Ash'ari doctrine of kasb (acquisition) and the negation of natural causality. The commentator begins by quoting part of a Quranic verse 'You used to do' (كنتم تعملون) and explains that human actions are attributed to servants only from the perspective of acquisition (kasb and iktisab), not from the perspective of origination (ijad) or invention (ikhtira'). This is a fundamental distinction in Ash'ari theology: Allah is the sole creator of actions, while humans acquire them. The commentator then extends this principle to all ordinary phenomena: fire does not burn by its nature (tab'), food does not satiate by itself, water does not quench thirst or cause plants to grow, stars do not ripen fruit, knives do not cut. He explicitly denies that these things have any effect—neither by nature, nor by an inherent causal power ('illa), nor by a power that Allah has deposited (quwwa mawda'a) in them. Instead, the effect belongs entirely to Allah alone, by His sheer choice (bi-mahd ikhtiyarihi) at the moment these things exist. This is supported by the verse in which Allah commanded the fire to be cool and safe for Prophet Ibrahim. The passage thus guards against any form of belief that created entities have independent causal efficacy.

Key Terms
الكسب
الاكتساب
الإيجاد
الاختراع
الطبع
العلة
القوة المودعة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Ash'aris deny all causality, even regular patterns of cause and effect.
Correction: Ash'aris affirm causality as a customary pattern (adatullah) but deny that created things have independent causal power. Allah creates effects in conjunction with apparent causes.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the Ash'ari stance on fire burning?
A) Fire burns by its nature (tab').
B) Fire burns by an inherent causal power ('illa).
C) Fire does not burn; Allah creates burning at the moment of contact.
D) Fire burns only when a condition is met and no obstacle is present.

Key Takeaway
Allah alone is the true agent; created things are mere occasions for His direct action.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you experience success—whether in work, relationships, or worship—do you truly attribute the outcome to Allah alone, or does your heart secretly credit your own skill or the 'power' of the means you used? What specific step can you take today to realign your conviction with the truth that Allah alone creates every effect?
?
Think of something you fear—like an illness, financial loss, or rejection. According to this lesson, that thing has no inherent power to harm you; only Allah creates harm. Does your heart live this belief? What practical action can you take this week to trust Allah's direct control over what you fear most?
?
The fire became cool for Ibrahim because Allah commanded it. Consider a situation in your life where circumstances seem fixed or destructive. Are you willing to believe that Allah can reverse any natural outcome? What supplication or reliance (tawakkul) can you practice to make this belief real?

Nature vs Cause: Conditional and Unconditional Effects
A faa'ida distinguishing tab' and 'illa effects in terms of conditionality

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the effect of nature (tab') is conditional and the effect of a cause ('illa) is unconditional, and why this distinction matters for understanding kalam beliefs about causality.

Core Idea
Nature works only when conditions are met; a cause works unconditionally.

This short 'benefit' passage clarifies a key conceptual distinction between two types of supposed causal efficacy that were mentioned earlier as erroneous beliefs. Before we understand why Ash'aris reject both, we need to grasp how they differ according to their advocates. The passage explains that nature (tab') requires conditions and absence of obstacles, while a cause ('illa) does not—it necessitates its effect immediately. Let's read the passage and see the examples.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Notice the examples of fire burning and ring movement.
The phrase 'fi alf farq' may be read differently; consider it emphasizing a vast difference.
2
Identify the two types of effect
The passage contrasts the effect of nature (tab') with the effect of a cause ('illa). For each, note the conditions or lack thereof.
3
Examine the conditions for nature
Nature depends on the existence of a condition (shart) and the absence of an obstacle (mani'). Example: fire burning requires contact and no wetness.
4
Examine the unconditionality of cause
A cause does not depend on any condition or obstacle. Example: movement of ring follows movement of finger without additional conditions.
5
Reflect on the theological implication
Ask yourself why believing in an unconditional cause ('illa) might be considered worse than believing in conditional nature (tab') from an Ash'ari perspective.
The answer lies in how each challenges divine omnipotence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فائدة

الفرق بين تأثير الطبع، وتأثير العلة بعد العلم باشتراكهما في الف فرق بين تأثير الطبع عدم الاختيار أن الطبع يتوقف على وجود الشرط، وانتفاء المانع، وتأثير العلة كالإحراق بالنار فإنه يتوقف على مماسة النار للشيء المحروق، وانتفاء المانع من البلل، ونحوه.

وأما العلة فلا يتوقف تأثيرها على شيء من ذلك، كحركة الخاتم لحركة الإصبع، فكلما وجدت العلة كحركة الإصبع وجد المعلول، وهو حركة الخاتم، ولذا يلزم اقتران العلة بمعلولها دون الطبيعة بمطبوعها، لتخلف شرط أو وجود مانع.
Benefit: The difference between the effect of nature (tab') and the effect of cause ('illa), after knowing their commonality in a thousand differences [or: in the multiplicity of differences], that the effect of nature is that nature depends on the existence of the condition and the absence of the obstacle, and the effect of cause like burning by fire depends on the contact of the fire with the object being burned and the absence of the obstacle of wetness and the like. As for the cause, its effect does not depend on any of that. Like the movement of the ring due to the movement of the finger: whenever the cause (movement of finger) exists, the effect (movement of ring) exists. Hence, the cause is necessarily conjoined with its effect, unlike nature with its natural object, because of the absence of a condition or the presence of an obstacle.
Source pages p. 52

Commentary
This passage is a 'benefit' (faa'ida) that aims to clarify the conceptual difference between two types of supposed causal efficacy: the effect of nature (tab') and the effect of a cause ('illa). These terms were just mentioned in the previous lesson as two heretical beliefs (both rejected by Ahl al-Sunna). Here the commentator gives a technical distinction: the effect of nature (tab') is conditional—it requires the presence of a condition (shart) and the absence of an obstacle (mani'). An example is burning by fire: it requires contact and absence of wetness. The effect of a cause ('illa), on the other hand, is unconditional: it does not depend on anything external; the cause necessitates the effect necessarily and immediately. The example given is the movement of a ring due to the movement of the finger: whenever the finger moves, the ring moves without needing any additional condition. Therefore, a cause is always conjoined with its effect, while nature may be absent due to a missing condition or present obstacle. Note that this distinction is purely explanatory of the concepts; the Ash'ari position is that neither nature nor causes have any real effect—only Allah creates. The passage helps understand why believing in 'illa is considered worse (as in the next line about quwwa mawda'a being bid'ah rather than kufr) because 'illa implies a necessary connection that challenges divine omnipotence more directly.

Key Terms
الطبع
العلة
الشرط
المانع
المعلول

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Thinking that tab' and 'illa are the same kind of causal efficacy.
Correction: They differ in conditionality: tab' is conditional, 'illa is unconditional and necessitates its effect.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is given as an example of the effect of a cause ('illa) in the passage?
A) Fire burning paper when touched
B) Movement of a ring due to finger movement
C) Food satisfying hunger
D) Water quenching thirst

Key Takeaway
The effect of nature is conditional, while the effect of a cause is unconditional and necessarily conjoined with its effect.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face a difficulty, do you instinctively try to remove obstacles or secure conditions, or do you turn directly to Allah as the only true cause? Which action shows real tawheed?
?
If you truly believed that no person, circumstance, or natural process has any independent effect, how would your daily anxieties about health, wealth, or relationships change? What would you start doing differently today?
?
Is your trust in Allah theoretical or practical? Examine a recent situation where you felt helpless: did you complain about missing conditions or obstacles, or did you rely on Allah alone? What needs to change?

Natural vs. Causal Influence
Author distinguishes natural vs. causal influence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the key difference between the effect of a natural disposition (tabʿ) and the effect of a logical cause (ʿilla) in Islamic theology.

Core Idea
Natural influence is conditional; causal influence is necessary.

This passage from al-Bahja al-Saniyya clarifies a crucial distinction in discussions of causality. Before we analyze how things act in the world, we must distinguish between two types of influence: that of a natural disposition (tabʿ), which depends on conditions, and that of a cause (ʿilla), which is unconditional and necessary. The author uses concrete examples to make this abstract distinction tangible.

Guided Path
1
Identify the shared premise
Read the passage and note that both natural disposition and cause produce effects. This commonality sets the stage for the distinction.
2
Contrast the two types of influence
Focus on the difference: natural effect (e.g., fire burning) requires a condition (contact) and absence of obstacles (wetness). Causal effect (e.g., ring moving with finger) requires only the cause itself.
3
Grasp the implication
Understand that a cause is necessarily concurrent with its effect, while a natural disposition may fail due to missing conditions or obstacles. This has implications for attributing actions to God or to secondary causes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فائدة. الفرق بين تأثير الطبع وتأثير العلة بعد العلم باشتراكهما في أن تأثير الطبع يتوقف على وجود الشرط وانتفاء المانع، كالإحراق بالنار فإنه يتوقف على مماسة النار للشيء المحروق وانتفاء المانع من البلل ونحوه. وأما العلة فلا يتوقف تأثيرها على شيء من ذلك، كحركة الخاتم لحركة الإصبع، فكلما وجدت العلة كحركة الإصبع وجد المعلول وهو حركة الخاتم، ولذا يلزم اقتران العلة بمعلولها دون الطبيعة بمطبوعها، لتخلف شرط أو وجود مانع.
A benefit: The difference between the effect of nature (tabʿ) and the effect of a cause (ʿilla) after knowing that they share that the effect of nature depends on the existence of the condition and the absence of an obstacle, like burning by fire: it depends on the contact of the fire with the burned object and the absence of an obstacle such as wetness and the like. As for the cause, its effect does not depend on any of that, like the movement of the ring due to the movement of the finger: whenever the cause, like the movement of the finger, exists, the effect, which is the movement of the ring, exists. Hence the cause necessarily is concurrent with its effect, unlike nature with its natural thing, due to the absence of a condition or the existence of an obstacle.
Source pages p. 52

Commentary
This passage is a technical distinction between two types of influence: the effect of a natural disposition (tabʿ) and the effect of a logical cause (ʿilla). The author first notes that both share the concept of producing an outcome. He then explains the difference: natural effect, like fire burning, requires both the presence of a condition (contact) and the absence of obstacles (wetness). If either is missing, the effect does not occur. In contrast, a causal effect, like a ring moving when the finger moves, is unconditional: whenever the cause exists, the effect necessarily exists. The author concludes that a cause is inseparably concurrent with its effect, whereas a natural disposition can fail due to missing conditions or present obstacles. This distinction is foundational for discussions of causality in kalam.

Key Terms
الطبع
العلة
المعلول
المطبوع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that all causal relationships in the world are necessary and unconditional.
Correction: The author distinguishes natural influence (conditional) from causal influence (necessary). Only the latter is unconditional.

Checkpoint
What does the effect of a cause (ʿilla) not depend on?
A) The absence of an obstacle
B) The existence of a condition
C) The presence of the cause itself
D) Both A and B

Key Takeaway
A natural effect requires conditions and no obstacles; a causal effect follows necessarily from its cause.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Are there areas in your life where you have conditioned your faith or actions on external factors (like feelings or outcomes) rather than trusting in Allah's unconditional will? What would it look like to rely on Him as the only cause that never fails?
?
If your good deeds are like natural influence—requiring conditions of sincerity and absence of pride—how often do you examine those conditions before expecting reward? How does this lesson expose hidden assumptions about your own efficacy?

Ruling on Belief in Deposited Power
Author rules on the theological status of the deposite-power view.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the Ash'ari scholars consider the belief that things act through a deposited power to be an innovation (bid'a) but not unbelief (kufr).

Core Idea
Belief in deposited power is bid'a, not kufr, if divine creation of the servant's power is upheld.

In this short but precise passage, Sanusi addresses a specific theological opinion about causality. Some deviant groups claimed that created things affect others through a power that God deposited in them. This lesson examines why the author labels such a person an 'innovator' rather than an 'unbeliever', and what crucial theological principle prevents the judgment of takfir.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing; then read the translation. Notice the strong language used for the proponents ('people of deviance and stubbornness').
2
Identify the ruling given
Find the word 'بدعي' (innovator) and the statement that it is not said to be kufr. This is the central ruling.
The ruling distinguishes between bid'a and kufr.
3
Understand the reasoning
The author explains why it is not kufr: because they affirm that God creates the servant, his power, and his will. Grasp this reasoning.
This is the key theological principle that saves them from takfir.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومن يقل من أهل الزيغ والعناد: إن هذه الأمور العادية تؤثر (بالقوة المودعة) بفتح الدال اسم مفعول أي بواسطة القوة التي أودعها الله تعالى فيها، فذاك القائل (بدعي) منسوب إلى البدعة التي هي خلاف السنة التي أخذها سلفنا الصالح عن نبينا صلى الله عليه وسلم، ولا يقال: إنه كفر، لأنهم أثبتوا له تعالى خلق العبد وخلق قدرته وإرادته، فصار فعل العبد في الحقيقة مخلوقا له تعالى، فلا تلتفت لهذا القول بل أعرض عنه.
And whoever among the people of deviance and stubbornness says that these customary things cause effects through the deposited power (al-quwwa al-mūdaʿa) — with fath on the dal, a passive participle, meaning by means of the power that God—exalted is He—has deposited in them—then that speaker is an innovator (bidʿī), attributed to innovation (bidʿa) which is contrary to the Sunna taken by our righteous predecessors from our Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him. It is not said that it is unbelief (kufr), because they have affirmed for Him, exalted, the creation of the servant and the creation of his power and will, so the act of the servant becomes in reality created by Him, exalted. So do not pay attention to this saying, rather turn away from it.
Source pages p. 52

Commentary
This passage addresses the theological status of those who claim that natural objects or events influence through a power deposited in them by God. The author first identifies such claimants as 'people of deviance and stubbornness'. He explains that the phrase 'al-quwwa al-mūdaʿa' means a power that God has placed in things. He rules that such a person is an innovator (bidʿī), i.e., one who introduces a doctrine contrary to the Sunna of the Salaf. However, he clarifies that this claim does not amount to unbelief (kufr), because these claimants still affirm that God creates the servant, his power, and his will, so that the servant's action is ultimately God's creation. Thus, they are misguided but not outside the fold of Islam. The author concludes by advising to ignore and turn away from this opinion.

Key Terms
القوة المودعة
بدعي
بدعة
خلق العبد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that any belief implying secondary causation is kufr.
Correction: The author clarifies that as long as God's creation of the servant's power and will is affirmed, it remains bid'a, not kufr.

Checkpoint
Why does the author consider the belief in deposited power to be bid'a rather than kufr?
A) Because it denies God's existence
B) Because it affirms that God creates the servant's power and will
C) Because it rejects the Sunna entirely
D) Because it claims things act by themselves

Key Takeaway
Belief in deposited power is an innovation (bid'a) but not unbelief (kufr), because it still affirms God's creation of the servant's power and will.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever thought that as long as you believe in God's creation, any explanation of how things work is acceptable? How does this ruling challenge that assumption? What will you do to ensure your understanding of causation aligns with the Sunna?
?
The author distinguishes between innovation and unbelief. Does that distinction ever make you complacent about innovations? What is one innovation in belief or practice that you have been ignoring, and what will you do about it?
?
The author says the deposited power view is still innovation because it affirms God's creation. Do you sometimes think that as long as you believe the 'right' things about God, other aspects of your creed don't matter? How will you ensure you are not guilty of innovation in your understanding of causality?

The Four Positions on Natural Causes
Author summarizes four theological positions on natural causes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the four theological positions on the efficacy of natural causes and the orthodox Sunni view that they are mere signs of divine will.

Core Idea
Causes are signs, not agents; only Allah acts.

This lesson summarizes a critical theological discussion on causality. The author presents the correct belief that customary causes have no inherent efficacy, then distinguishes four erroneous views ranging from innovation to outright disbelief. Understanding this classification protects one's faith and clarifies the Sunni position on miracles and divine omnipotence.

Guided Path
1
Learn the orthodox Sunni position
The passage begins by stating the correct belief: customary causes have no effect; they are merely signs and indications of Allah's will, with no necessary connection to their effects.
This is the foundation: causes are not agents.
2
Identify the first deviant view
The first erroneous claim is that causes effect by themselves without any act of God. The author rules this as kufr by consensus.
This position negates divine omnipotence.
3
Identify the second and third deviant views
The second view says causes effect by a power created in them (bid'a). The third view says causes effect by God's permission but with a rational necessary connection – this leads to kufr as it denies miracles.
Bid'a vs. kufr: note the distinction.
4
Understand the Qadariyya view and the final nuance
The Qadariyya (Mu'tazilites) attribute effect to things by means of the power. The Sunni view is that effect is from God because of the power. The preponderant view: effect belongs to God alone at the time of the cause, not by it.
This nuance preserves divine agency while acknowledging customary regularity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وحاصل القول في الأسباب العادية: أنه لا تأثير لها فيما قارنها، وإنما جعل الله تعالى الأسباب علامة ودلالة على ما شاء من الحوادث من غير ملازمة بينها وبين مسبباتها، وهذا هو الواجب اعتقاده، ومعتقده مؤمن حقا. أما من قال: تؤثر بذاتها من غير جعل من الله تعالى، فهو كافر بالإجماع. ومن قال: بقوة خلقها الله فيها، فهو مبتدع. ومن قال: إنها تؤثر بإذن الله وأن بينها وبين ما قارنها ملازمة عقلية، فهو جاهل يؤول اعتقاده ذلك إلى الكفر، فإن ما بالعقل لا يتخلف، ويستلزم هذا القول إنكار المعجزات وما أخبرت به الأنبياء لله وحده وإن كان بواسطة تلك القوة. وأما القدرية فينسبون التأثير لتلك الأشياء بواسطة القوة، ففرق بين الاعتقادين: أي فاعتقاد المعتزلي القدري أن التأثير للأشياء بواسطة القوة، والسني أن التأثير لله لسبب القوة. ومع ذلك فالراجح الأول، وهو أن التأثير له وحده عندها لا بها، وإن جرت العادة بأنه إنما يحصل التأثير عندها.
And the summary of the statement concerning customary causes: that they have no effect in that which they accompany; rather God—exalted—has made causes a sign and an indication of what He wills of events, without necessary connection between them and their effects. And this is what must be believed, and the one who believes it is a true believer. As for one who says they effect by themselves without any making (jaʿl) from God, he is an unbeliever by consensus. And one who says [they effect] by a power which God created in them, he is an innovator. And one who says they effect by God's permission and that between them and what they accompany there is a rational necessary connection, he is ignorant; his belief leads to unbelief, because what is [necessary] by reason cannot be absent, and this statement necessitates denial of miracles and what the prophets informed of—[that all is] for God alone, even if by means of that power. As for the Qadariyya, they attribute effect to those things by means of the power; so there is a difference between the two beliefs: the belief of the Muʿtazilite Qadari that effect belongs to things by means of the power, and the Sunni [belief] that effect belongs to God because of the power. Nevertheless, the preponderant [view] is the first, which is that effect belongs to Him alone at the time of them (the causes), not by them, even if the custom is that effect only occurs at the time of them.
Source pages p. 53

Commentary
This passage is a comprehensive classification of theological positions on the nature of causality. The author begins by stating the correct Sunni position: customary causes have no inherent efficacy; they are merely signs placed by God, with no necessary connection to their effects. He then lists four deviant positions and their rulings:

1. Those who say causes act by themselves (without any divine appointment) — this is kufr by consensus.
2. Those who say causes act through a power created by God in them (the deposited power view) — this is bidʿa (innovation).
3. Those who say causes act by God's permission but that there is a rational necessary connection between cause and effect — this is ignorance that leads to kufr, as it would deny miracles and prophetic reports.
4. The Qadariyya (Muʿtazilites) who attribute effect to things by means of the power — this is contrasted with the Sunni view that effect is from God because of the power.

The author then notes that, despite the Sunni view being that causes are signs, there is another preponderant view within Sunni Islam: that effect belongs to God alone at the time of the cause, not by the cause, even though customarily the effect does not occur except at that time. This nuance preserves divine omnipotence while acknowledging the empirical regularity. The passage thus provides a precise map of beliefs regarding causation, from orthodoxy to outright unbelief.

Key Terms
الأسباب العادية
ملازمة عقلية
القدرية
المعتزلي
بدعة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Sunni view that causes are signs means causes have no role at all in the occurrence of effects.
Correction: The author notes a preponderant view that effect occurs at the time of the cause, but it is attributed to Allah alone, not to the cause itself. This acknowledges empirical regularity while preserving divine agency.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the ruling on one who claims that customary causes effect by themselves without any act of God?
A) He is an innovator
B) He is a true believer
C) He is an unbeliever by consensus
D) He is ignorant but not an unbeliever

Key Takeaway
The orthodox Sunni view is that causes are merely signs of Allah's will; any claim of inherent causal power leads to kufr, while attributing effect to a created power is bid'a.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you plan your day, do you truly see each outcome as coming directly from Allah, or do you implicitly rely on causes as if they have independent power? What would change if you internalized that causes are only signs?
?
If your doctor gave you a medicine, do you think of it as having a property that heals, or do you see it as a sign from Allah that healing may occur? How does this affect your gratitude and trust?
?
This lesson declares that belief in independent causal power leads to kufr. Scrutinize your own heart: in which areas of your life do you feel anxiety when causes fail? What does that reveal about your true belief?

intermediate
6 min
Three Positions on Ordinary Causes
Classification of three positions on ordinary causes with rulings.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three theological positions on ordinary causes and the correct Sunni belief that causes are mere signs, not independent agents.

Core Idea
Causes are signs from Allah, not independent actors.

The relationship between causes and their effects was hotly debated among Muslim theologians. Some claimed causes have intrinsic power, others denied any connection. This passage from al-Bahja al-Saniyya outlines the three main positions and clarifies the orthodox Sunni view, which preserves both divine omnipotence and the reality of miracles.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation once to grasp the overall argument. Notice that it starts with a line of poetry then commentary and a summary section.
2
Identify the three erroneous positions
Look for the three positions listed after the summary: (1) causes effect by themselves, (2) causes effect by a deposited power, (3) causes effect with a necessary connection by God's permission. Note the ruling for each.
The correct Sunni view is given first.
3
Understand the correct Sunni view
Focus on the statement: 'causes have no effect... rather, God made them signs and indications.' This is the obligatory belief. Understand why the other positions are problematic.
4
Reflect on the consequences
Consider why the third position leads to potential disbelief—because it denies miracles and prophetic reports. Note that the first position is outright disbelief, the second is innovation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومَنْ يَقُلْ بالقُوَّةِ الْمُودَعَةِ * فَذاكَ بِدْعِي فلا تَلْتَفِتِ
حد شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي .
أودعها الله تعالى فيها، فذاك) القائل (بدعي) منسوب إلى البدعة التي هي خلاف السنة التي أخذها سلفنا الصالح عن نبينا صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ ، ولا يقال: إنه كفر ، لأنهم أثبتوا له تعالى خلق العبد، وخلق قدرته وإرادته، فصار فعل العبد في الحقيقة مخلوقا له تعالى، فلا تلتفت) لهذا القول، بل أعرض عنه.
خلاصة القول | وحاصل القول في الأسباب العادية:
أنه لا تأثير لها فيما قارنها، وإنما جعل الله تعالى الأسباب علامة ودلالة على ما شاء من الحوادث من غير ملازمة بينها وبين مسبباتها، وهذا هو الواجب اعتقاده، ومعتقده مؤمن حقا. أما من قال: تؤثر بذاتها من غير جعل من الله تعالى، فهو كافر بالإجماع.
ومن قال: بقوة خلقها الله فيها، فهو مبتدع. ومن قال: إنها تؤثر بإذن الله، وأن بينها وبين ما قارنها ملازمة عقلية، فهو جاهل ، يَؤُول اعتقاده ذلك إلى الكفر، فإن ما بالعقل لا يتخلف، ويستلزم هذا القول إنكار المعجزات، وما أخبرت به الأنبياء
من خوارق العادات والمغيبات عنا، كأحوال القبر، وأهوال يوم القيامة.
And whoever says with the deposited power, then that one is an innovator, so do not pay attention. Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat al-Makki al-Maliki: God deposited it in it, so that speaker is an innovator, attributed to innovation which is contrary to the Sunnah that our righteous predecessors took from our Prophet, may God bless him and grant him peace. It is not said that it is disbelief, because they affirmed for Him creation of the servant, and creation of his power and will, so the act of the servant in reality is created by Him. So do not pay attention to this statement, but turn away from it. Summary of the statement and the conclusion regarding ordinary causes: That they have no effect in what is simultaneous with them; rather, God made the causes a sign and an indication of what He wills of events, without any necessary connection between them and their effects. This is what must be believed, and its believer is a true believer. As for the one who says that they have effect by themselves without any act of God, he is a disbeliever by consensus. And the one who says that they have effect by a power that God created in them, he is an innovator. And the one who says that they have effect by God's permission and that there is a necessary connection between them and what is simultaneous with them, he is ignorant, his belief tending to disbelief, because what is by reason does not fail, and this statement necessitates denial of miracles and what the prophets informed of, from miracles that break the norm and the unseen things from us, like the states of the grave and the terrors of the Day of Resurrection.
Source pages p. 53

Commentary
This passage defines the three positions on ordinary causes (الأسباب العادية) and states the correct Sunni belief. It begins by commenting on the verse: 'And whoever says with the deposited power, then that is an innovator, so do not pay attention.' The commentary explains that the speaker who claims a power deposited in causes is an innovator, not a disbeliever, because he still attributes creation to God. Then the passage summarizes the correct view: causes have no inherent effect; God made them mere signs and indications of events, with no necessary connection between them and their effects. This is obligatory to believe, and its adherent is a true believer. Then it lists the three erroneous positions: First, whoever says causes have effect by themselves, without any act of God, is a disbeliever by consensus. Second, whoever says they have effect by a power God created in them is an innovator. Third, whoever says they have effect by God's permission but with a necessary connection is ignorant, and his belief tends to disbelief, because it necessitates the denial of miracles and things revealed by prophets, such as the miracles of the grave and the horrors of the Day of Resurrection. The passage thus clearly distinguishes the Sunni position from those error.

Key Terms
الأسباب العادية
البدعة
القدرية
السُّنّي
الإجماع
الملازمة
المعجزات
خوارق العادات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The third position (effect by permission with necessary connection) is acceptable because it acknowledges God's permission.
Correction: This leads to disbelief because it necessitates a necessary connection that would deny miracles and prophetic reports of the unseen.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the ruling on someone who says that ordinary causes have an effect by themselves without any act of God?
A) He is an innovator
B) He is a disbeliever by consensus
C) He is mistaken but still a believer
D) He is a true Sunni

Key Takeaway
The Sunni view affirms that causes have no independent effect but are signs from Allah, which alone preserves divine omnipotence and the reality of miracles.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you experience a success or a failure today, do you instinctively credit or blame the immediate cause (e.g., your effort, another person) or do you first see it as a sign from Allah? Which position from this lesson does your automatic reaction resemble? Commit to catching yourself and correcting your attribution.
?
The third position denies miracles because it assumes necessary causes. Think of a moment in your life that seemed like a ‘coincidence’ or a ‘natural outcome’ but could have been a divine sign or miracle. Did you dismiss it? What would change in your relationship with Allah if you truly believed He can break His own patterns at any moment?
?
This lesson says believing causes act independently makes one a disbeliever. Is there any area of your life—your income, your health, your relationships—where you act as if the means are self-sufficient? Write a short prayer asking Allah to purify your tawheed in that area.

Divine Attributes

The Five Negative Attributes of Allah
Sanusi enumerates the five negative attributes: qidam, baqa, qiyam bi nafsihi, mukhalafa, wahdaniyya.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the five negative attributes (sifat salbiyya) that negate from Allah what is not befitting, and their significance in affirming Allah's transcendence.

Core Idea
Five negations protect Allah's transcendence: pre-eternity, permanence, self-subsistence, dissimilarity, and oneness.

This passage from al-Bahja al-Saniyya introduces the five negative attributes of Allah. These attributes are not positive descriptions but negations of imperfections. Understanding them is crucial for tawhid, as they safeguard the concept of Allah's absolute perfection and uniqueness.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic poem
Read the Arabic lines carefully, noticing the rhythm and the sequence of attributes. Then read the literal translation.
The poem is written in rajaz meter, a common teaching format.
2
Identify the five attributes
Locate each of the five negative attributes in the poem: qidam, baqa, qiyam bi nafsihi, mukhalafa, and wahdaniyya. Understand that they negate flaws from Allah.
The word 'salbiyya' means negative or privative.
3
Understand the concluding line
The final line states that effective causation (ta'thir) is only for Allah. Reflect on how this connects to the five attributes, especially wahdaniyya (oneness).
This line reinforces tawhid and rejects any other independent cause.
4
Reflect on the meaning of 'tilt al-tuqa'
The phrase 'these are piety' indicates that believing in these attributes is an act of piety. Consider why negating imperfections from Allah is a form of worship.
Piety (tuqa) here refers to safeguarding one's faith by affirming Allah's transcendence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وذِي تُسَمَّى صِفةٌ نَفْسِيَّةَ * ثُمَّ تَلِيهَا خَمْسَةُ سَلْبِيَّةُ * وَهْيَ القِدَمَ بِالذَّاتِ فَاعْلَمُ والبَقَا * قِيامُهُ بِنَفْسِهِ، تِلْتَ التَّقَى * مُخَالِفٌ للغَيْرِ، وَحْدَانِيَّةٌ * فِي الذَّاتِ، أَوْ صِفَاتِهِ العَلِيَّة * والفِعْلِ، فَالتَّأْثِيرُ لَيْسَ إِلَّا * لِلوَاحِدِ القَهَّارِ جَلَّ وعلا
And this is called a self-attribute (sifa nafsiyya), then it is followed by five negative ones (salbiyya): they are: pre-eternity (qidam) of the essence, so know; and permanence (baqa); self-subsistence (qiyamuhu bi nafsihi); these are piety; being different from others (mukhalif lil ghayr); and oneness (wahdaniyya) in essence, or in His high attributes, and in action. So effective causation (al-ta'thir) is only for the One, the All-Dominant, exalted and high.
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage lists the five negative attributes (sifat salbiyya) that negate from Allah what is not befitting. First, it mentions that the attribute of existence is a 'sifa nafsiyya' (self-attribute). Then it lists five negative attributes: (1) qidam: pre-eternity; (2) baqa: permanence; (3) qiyam bi nafsihi: self-subsistence; (4) mukhalafa lil ghayr: dissimilarity to others; (5) wahdaniyya: oneness in essence, attributes, and actions. The line 'tilt al-tuqa' (these are piety) indicates that holding these beliefs is a form of piety. The passage concludes that effective causation (ta'thir) belongs only to Allah, the One, the All-Dominant.

Key Terms
صفة نفسية
صفات سلبية
القدم
البقاء
القيام بالنفس
مخالفة للغير
وحدانية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the five negative attributes are separate entities added to Allah's essence.
Correction: The five negative attributes are pure negations; they do not imply multiplicity in Allah. They simply describe what Allah is not.

Checkpoint
How many negative attributes (sifat salbiyya) does the author list?
A) Three
B) Four
C) Five
D) Seven

Key Takeaway
The five negative attributes protect Allah's transcendence by negating all imperfections, affirming His absolute oneness and sole causation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you feel anxious about your future, do you truly believe that Allah is self-subsistent and needs no support, or do you act as if your security depends on other things? How can you internalize qiyam bi nafsihi so that it changes your trust in Allah alone?
?
The attribute of mukhalafa affirms that Allah is unlike anything. When you imagine Allah in your mind, do you ever unconsciously compare Him to something created? How will you actively purge such images from your thoughts?
?
Wahdaniyya in action means that only Allah creates and controls outcomes. In your daily efforts, do you attribute success or failure to your own skill or to others, forgetting that all effective causation is only for the One? How can you remind yourself of this throughout your day?

Divine Attributes

The Seven Attributes of Ma'ani
Sanusi lists the seven essential attributes of Allah: knowledge, life, power, will, speech, hearing, sight.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the seven essential attributes of Allah (the 'ma'ani') and the distinction between His will and His command.

Core Idea
Seven eternal attributes define Allah's essence; His will is not identical to His command.

Sanusi now turns from the general classification of attributes to the specific list of seven that are called 'attributes of ma'ani.' These are real, eternal qualities that subsist in Allah's essence. The passage also contains an important clarification: Allah's will (irada) is not the same as His command (amr), a distinction that avoids theological confusion.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic poem
Read the Arabic passage slowly, noting the names of the attributes as they appear in the poetic lines.
The poem uses rhyme and meter, so read it aloud if possible.
2
Identify the seven attributes
From the text, list the seven attributes of ma'ani: knowledge, life, power, will, speech, hearing, sight.
3
Understand the qasd-amr distinction
The line 'الْقَصْدُ غَيْرُ الأمرِ' tells us that purpose (qasd) is distinct from command (amr). This clarifies that Allah's will may not always correspond to what He commands.
This prevents confusion about divine decree and command.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
The poem ends by affirming that Allah is al-fa'il (the Agent) and al-mukhtar (the Free Chooser), grounding the attributes in His active freedom.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثُمَّ الْمَعَانِي سَبْعَةُ للرَّائِي * أي عِلْمُهُ الْمُحِيطُ بالأَشْيَاءِ * حَياتُهُ وقُدْرَةٌ إرادة * وكُلُّ شيءٍ كَائِنْ أَرَادَة * وإنْ يَكُنْ بِضِدِّهِ قَدْ أَمَرا * فالقَصْدُ غيرُ الأمرِ فاطرَح المِرَا * فقَد عَلِمتَ أربَعًا أَقْسَامَا * فِي الكَائِنَاتِ فَاحْفَظِ الْمَقَامَا * كَلامُهُ والسَّمْعُ والإِبْصَارُ * فَهُوَ الإِلهُ الفَاعِلُ الْمُخْتَارُ
Then the attributes of ma'ani are seven for the seer: that is His knowledge encompassing all things, His life, power, will, and everything that is He wills. And if He has commanded (amara) something contrary to it (i.e., contrary to His will?), then purpose (al-qasd) is other than command (al-amr), so cast aside dispute. For you have known four divisions in the existent things, so preserve the station. His speech, hearing, and sight – so He is the God, the Agent, the Free Choiser.
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage enumerates the seven attributes of ma'ani (meaningful attributes) that are eternally predicated of Allah. The author says there are seven for the one who reflects (al-ra'i). They are: (1) knowledge ('ilm) encompassing all things, (2) life (hayat), (3) power (qudra), (4) will (irada), (5) speech (kalam), (6) hearing (sam'), (7) sight (basar). The passage includes a parenthetical note: 'And if He has commanded (amara) something contrary to it (i.e., contrary to His will?), then purpose (qasd) is not the same as command, so avoid argument.' This likely distinguishes between God's will (irada) and His command (amr), clarifying that what He wills may not be what He commands (e.g., He wills the existence of disbelievers but does not command their disbelief). The author then says you have known four divisions in the existent things – possibly referring to the earlier classification of the world into four categories? Then he lists speech, hearing, and sight, concluding that Allah is the agent (al-fa'il) and the free chooser (al-mukhtar).

Key Terms
صفات المعاني
العلم
الحياة
القدرة
الإرادة
الكلام
السمع
البصر
القصد
الأمر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: These seven attributes are parts or components of Allah.
Correction: They are eternal aspects of His essence, not parts. They are not separable from Him.

Checkpoint
How many attributes of ma'ani are mentioned?
A) Five
B) Seven
C) Eight
D) Four

Key Takeaway
The seven attributes of ma'ani (knowledge, life, power, will, speech, hearing, sight) are eternal and inherent to Allah, and His will is distinct from His command.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face a trial or a sin you committed, do you truly believe that it occurred by Allah's will? Or do you secretly resent that His command (what He wants you to do) seems different from what He willed? Tonight, before you sleep, identify one specific situation where you have struggled to accept His will, and say 'I submit to Your will even if I do not understand it.'
?
You have learned that Allah has seven attributes. Which of these attributes do you most need to be reminded of in your daily life? For instance, how would remembering His all-encompassing knowledge change how you speak or act when you are alone? Make a concrete plan to reflect on one attribute each day this week.
?
The poem ends by calling Allah 'al-Fa'il al-Mukhtar'—the Agent, the Free Chooser. Do you live as if you are the one in control, or do you actively surrender your choices to Him? What is one decision you are facing right now where you need to recognize His free choice over yours? Will you make a dua tonight asking Him to align your will with His?

Divine Attributes

The Self-Attribute and the Five Negative Attributes
Enumeration of the nafsiyya attribute and five salbiyya attributes

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the meaning of the self-attribute (nafsiyya) and be able to list and explain the five negative attributes of Allah.

Core Idea
The self-attribute is Allah's existence itself; the five negative attributes negate all imperfection.

In this passage, the author introduces the concept of the self-attribute (ṣifa nafsiyya), which is simply Allah's necessary existence. He then enumerates five negative attributes (ṣifāt salbiyya) that negate any possible imperfection from Allah. Understanding these categories is foundational for Islamic theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
First, read the Arabic poetic passage and its English translation to get an overview of the content.
The verse structure uses rhyme for memorization.
2
Identify the self-attribute argument
Find the part where the author states that existence (wujūd) is a necessary attribute of Allah and note the causal proof given.
The proof uses the principle that every effect points to a cause.
3
List the five negative attributes
Identify each of the five negative attributes as they appear: eternal, everlasting, self-subsistence, different from others, oneness.
These are called 'negative' because they deny imperfections.
4
Reflect on why these are called 'negative'
Consider how each attribute negates a potential deficiency in Allah, reinforcing His perfection and uniqueness.
They are not positive attributes added to the essence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فاعْلَمْ بأنَّ الوَصْفَ بالوُجُودِ * مِنْ وَاجِبَاتِ الْوَاحِدِ الْمَعْبُودِ إِذْ ظَاهِرُ بأنَّ كُلَّ أَثَرِ * يَهْدِي إِلَى مُؤثِرٍ فَاعْتَبِرِ وذِي تُسَمَّى صِفةٌ نَفْسِيَّةَ . ثُمَّ تَلِيهَا خَمْسَةُ سَلْبِيَّةُ وَهْيَ القِدَمَ بِالذَّاتِ فَاعْلَمُ والبَقَا * قِيامُهُ بِنَفْسِهِ، تِلْتَ التَّقَى مُخَالِفٌ للغَيْرِ، وَحْدَانِيَّةٌ * فِي الذَّاتِ، أَوْ صِفَاتِهِ العَلِيَّة
Know that the attribute of existence is among the necessities of the One who is worshipped. For it is clear that every effect guides to an efficient cause, so reflect. And this is called the self-attribute. Then come five negative attributes after it: eternal in essence, and know, and everlasting, self-subsistence, that is the path of piety, different from others, oneness in essence or His high attributes.
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage defines the self-attribute (ṣifa nafsiyya) and the five negative attributes (ṣifāt salbiyya). First, the author states that existence (wujūd) is a necessary attribute of God—it is not derived but self-evident. He then provides a causal argument: every effect points to a cause (which is God). Then he introduces the self-attribute (essence) and lists the five negative attributes: qidam (eternality), baqā' (everlastingness), qiyām bi nafsih (self-subsistence), mukhālafa lil-ghayr (difference from creation), and waḥdāniyya (oneness). These are called 'negative' because they negate imperfection from God, affirming His uniqueness.

خمسة سلبية
الوجود
القدم

قيامه بنفسه
مخالف للغير
وحدانية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The self-attribute is an additional attribute distinct from Allah's essence.
Correction: The self-attribute is the essence itself; it is not something added.

Checkpoint

B) Four
C) Five
D) Seven

Key Takeaway
Allah's existence is a necessary self-attribute, and five negative attributes protect His transcendence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly live as if Allah's existence is the most certain reality, or do your daily actions suggest you forget this self-evident truth? How can you make His existence more present in your heart today?
?
Which of the five negative attributes do you find hardest to internalize? For instance, do you ever subconsciously imagine Allah as being like something created? What step will you take to uproot that comparison from your heart?
?
If someone asked you why Allah cannot be limited by time, place, or multiplicity, could you answer from this passage? Commit to memorizing the five negative attributes and their meanings so you can defend God's transcendence when needed.
