Divine Power and Will Encompass All Possible Things
The author explains that divine power and will encompass all possible things, refuting the Mu'tazila.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that divine power and will (qudra and irada) extend to all possible things, including human voluntary actions, and why the Mu'tazila position is considered contrary to piety.

Core Idea
God's power and will cover everything possible — no human action is outside them.

This lesson focuses on a key point of Sunni creed: that God's attributes of power and will are universal in scope. The author contrasts this with the Mu'tazila claim that humans create their own actions independently. Understanding this distinction is crucial for grasping the Islamic doctrine of qadar (divine decree) and refuting deterministic or libertarian extremes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the poetic line and the commentary structure.
2
Identify the main claim
The passage states that power and will relate to all possible things. This is the central dogma being upheld.
The author says this formulation is more concise than al-Laqani's.
3
Understand the Mu'tazila position
The author explains the Mu'tazila claim: God's power does not relate to voluntary actions, and sins are not willed by God because will would necessitate command.
The Mu'tazila use the idea that will implies command to argue God cannot will sin.
4
Connect to 'brother of piety'
The phrase 'O brother of piety' indicates that opposing this creed (like the Mu'tazila) is inconsistent with true piety.
This is a rhetorical device to emphasize the importance of correct belief.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقدرة إرادة تعلقا * بالممكنات كلها هذا أخصر وأوضح من قول البرهان اللقاني: فقدرةً بممكن تعلّقت تعلّقت (۲) بِلا تَنَاهِي مَا بِهِ يا أخا التقى المصاحب والملازم لامتثال أوامر الله، واجتناب نواهيه، وفيه إشارة إلى أن من خالف هذه العقيدة كالمعتزلة ليسوا من أهل التّقى، فإنهم قالوا: إن قدرته تعالى لا تتعلق بأفعال العبد الاختيارية، بل العبد مستقل بخلق فعله الاختياري، وإن بعض أفعاله الاختيارية كالمعاصي ليست بإرادة الله تعالى بناء على أن الإرادة تستلزم الأمر،
And power and will related to all possible things. This is more concise and clear than the statement of Burhan al-Laqani: 'So power related to a possible thing, related without end, O brother of piety.' The brother of piety is the companion and adherent to obeying the commands of Allah and avoiding His prohibitions. And in it is an indication that whoever opposes this creed, like the Mu'tazila, is not among the people of piety. For they said: Indeed His power, Exalted, does not relate to the voluntary actions of the servant, but rather the servant is independent in creating his own voluntary action, and that some of his voluntary actions, like sins, are not by the will of Allah, Exalted, based on the idea that will necessitates command.
Source pages p. 68

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on the line of poetry stating that power and will relate to all possible things. The author notes that this formulation is more concise than al-Laqani's. He then explains that the phrase 'O brother of piety' indicates that those who oppose this doctrine, specifically the Mu'tazila, are not truly pious. The Mu'tazila claim that God's power does not extend to human voluntary actions; instead, humans create their own actions independently. They also claim that some actions like sins are not willed by God because, in their view, will necessitates command (i.e., if God willed it, He would command it, but He commands against sin). The commentary refutes this by implication, affirming that God's power and will encompass all actions, including human voluntary acts.

Key Terms
القدرة
الإرادة
الممكنات
المعتزلة
الأفعال الاختيارية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's power and will do not extend to human voluntary actions, particularly sins.
Correction: According to Sunni creed, God's power and will encompass all possible things, including all human actions, good and bad.

Checkpoint
What do the Mu'tazila claim about God's power and will regarding human voluntary actions?
A) They affirm that God's power extends to all human actions.
B) They deny that God's power relates to voluntary actions; humans create their own.
C) They say God's will is identical to His command.
D) They say God does not know human actions.

Key Takeaway
Divine power and will encompass all possible things, including human voluntary actions, and the Mu'tazila position is inconsistent with piety.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that every action you take, even your sins, occurs by God’s will? How does that make you feel about your own choices? Write a commitment to reflect on this belief in your daily life.
?
If you were to face the temptation to sin, how would remembering that God wills all things but does not command sin change your response? What will you do the next time you are tempted?
?
The Mu'tazila claimed that God cannot will sin because it would contradict His command. Have you ever thought that your sins were outside God's plan? Confess that thought and resolve to accept God's complete sovereignty.

God's Power and Will Encompass All Possible Things
Commentary on a verse affirming God's power and will over all possibles, with refutation of Mu'tazila.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that God's power and will extend to every possible thing, including human actions, and how this refutes the Mu'tazilite position.

Core Idea
God's power and will are universal, covering all contingent things without exception.

This passage from al-Bahja al-Saniyya explains a key verse affirming the comprehensiveness of God's power and will. The commentator, Shaykh Hasan al-Mashat, clarifies a nuance about God's knowledge and then directly refutes the Mu'tazilite view that human acts are independent of God's will. By the end, you will see how this verse serves as a clear statement of Sunni belief.

Guided Path
1
Read the verse and commentary
First, carefully read the Arabic verse and the commentary that follows. The verse is simple: 'And power and will attach to all contingent things.' The commentary expands on this.
Notice the commentator begins with a clarification about God's knowledge before addressing the verse.
2
Understand the knowledge clarification
The commentator explains that God's knowledge attaches to things both generally and in detail, contrary to a claim that it attaches only generally. He also discusses knowledge of the impossible.
This sets the theological framework for understanding divine attributes.
3
Appreciate the verse's clarity
The commentator praises the verse as more concise and clear than an alternative formulation by al-Laqani. Note the implicit call to piety in addressing the reader as 'O pious one.'
4
Identify the Mu'tazilite error
The passage explicitly refutes the Mu'tazila who claim God's power and will do not extend to human free acts. Understand their reasoning and why it is considered corrupt.
The commentator states this belief has already been proven corrupt earlier.

وقدرة إرادة تعلقا * بالممكنات كلها أخا التقى
شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي
يتعلق بالمعلومات إجمالا لإبهامه أنه لا يتعلق بها تفصيلا، كما منع أن يقال: «يتعلق بها إجمالاً وتفصيلا للتناقض، وأوجب أن يقال: يتعلق بها تفصيلًا».
الثاني: قال العلامة السباعي: معنى تعلق علمه تعالى بالمستحيل: علمه تعالى باستحالته، وأنه لو تصور وقوعه لزم منه الفساد، وهذا مما أشار له بعض السلف بقوله: «علم ماكان، وعلم ما يكون، وعلم ما لم يكن إن لو كان كيف يكون»، وهذا يميز علمه تعالى عن علمنا بالمستحيل، والله أعلم (۱).
( وقدرة إرادة تعلقا بالممكنات كلها هذا أخصر وأوضح من قول البرهان اللقاني: فقدرةً بممكن تعلّقت
تعلّقت (۲) بِلا تَنَاهِي مَا بِهِ يا أخا التقى المصاحب والملازم لامتثال أوامر الله، واجتناب نواهيه، وفيه إشارة إلى أن من خالف هذه العقيدة كالمعتزلة ليسوا من أهل التّقى، فإنهم قالوا: إن قدرته تعالى لا تتعلق بأفعال العبد الاختيارية، بل العبد مستقل بخلق فعله الاختياري، وإن بعض أفعاله الاختيارية كالمعاصي ليست بإرادة الله تعالى بناء على أن الإرادة تستلزم الأمر، (۱) (حاشية السباعي» (ص ١٠٦). (۲) «الجوهرة» (ص۱۹۷) مع شرح الصاوي.
وقد تقدم أن هذا الاعتقاد فاسد.
"And power and will attach to all contingent things, O pious one." Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat al-Maliki al-Maliki: "It attaches to known things generally, because of its ambiguity that it does not attach to them in detail, just as he forbade saying: 'It attaches to them generally and in detail' due to contradiction, and required that one say: 'It attaches to them in detail.' Second: al-Allama al-Siba'i said: The meaning of His knowledge, exalted, attaching to the impossible: His knowledge, exalted, of its impossibility, and that if its occurrence were imagined, corruption would necessarily follow from it. This is what some of the Salaf indicated by their saying: 'He knows what was, and He knows what will be, and He knows what was not, if it were to be, how it would be.' And this distinguishes His knowledge, exalted, from our knowledge of the impossible. And God knows best. (And power and will attach to all contingent things: this is more concise and clearer than the saying of al-Burhan al-Laqani: 'So power attaches to a contingent thing, attaches without limit to it.' O pious one, the companion and one who adheres to obeying God's commands and avoiding His prohibitions. And in it is an indication that those who oppose this belief, like the Mu'tazila, are not among the people of piety. For they said: His power, exalted, does not attach to the free acts of the servant, rather the servant is independent in creating his free act, and some of his free acts like sins are not by the will of God, exalted, based on the idea that will necessitates command. And it has already been stated that this belief is corrupt."
Source pages p. 68

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on the poetic verse "Wa qudratun iradatun ta'allaqa bi-l-mumkinati kulliha" (And power and will attach to all contingent things). The commentator, Shaykh Hasan al-Mashat, explains that the verse is affirming that God's power and will encompass all possible things. He first clarifies a nuance regarding God's knowledge: it attaches to things both generally and in detail, contrary to a claim that it only attaches generally. Then he quotes al-Siba'i on the meaning of God's knowledge of the impossible—it is knowledge of its impossibility. The commentator then praises the verse as clearer than an alternative formulation by al-Laqani. He points out that the verse contains an implicit rebuke of the Mu'tazila, who deny that God's power and will extend to human free actions, claiming instead that humans independently create their own acts and that sins are not willed by God because will necessitates command. The commentator concludes that this Mu'tazilite belief has already been shown to be corrupt earlier in the text.

Correction: God's power and will encompass all possible things, including human actions. Humans do not act independently; their actions are within God's power and will.

The Order of Attachment: Power Follows Will, Will Follows Knowledge
Sanusi clarifies the hierarchical attachment of divine attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the hierarchical relationship among God's knowledge, will, and power, and why power depends on will and will on knowledge.

Core Idea
God's power always acts in accordance with His will, which always aligns with His knowledge.

This lesson explores the inner logic of divine action. The author explains that God's attributes do not operate in isolation; rather, they follow a precise order. Before we can understand how God creates or commands, we must see how knowledge, will, and power are interrelated.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Pay attention to the hierarchical language.
Notice the words 'تابع' (dependent) and 'ترتيب' (order).
2
Identify the three attributes and their dependencies
The passage mentions power, will, and knowledge. Note how each one depends on the next. Write down the chain.
3
Understand the meaning of the chain
The meaning is that God does not create unless He wills, and He does not will unless He knows. This shows that God's actions are not arbitrary.
This explains why God's power is not independent.
4
Reflect on the example of faith and disbelief
The example shows that if God knows someone will persist in disbelief, He does not will faith for that person, so faith does not occur. This illustrates the hierarchy.
The example grounds the abstract principle.
5
Consider the theological significance
This order ensures that God's actions are perfectly rational and just, based on His knowledge and will.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ترتيب تعلق واعلم أن تعلق القدرة والإرادة والعلم مرتب، فتعلق القدرة تابع لتعلق الإرادة، وتعلق الإرادة تابع لتعلق العلم.
فالمعنى: أنه تعالى لا يُوجد شيئًا أو يُعْدِمه إلا إذا أراده، ولا يريد إلا إذا علمه، فما علم أنه يكون أراد كونه ووجوده، ثم أبرزه على طبق الإرادة، وما عَلِم أنه لا يكون ولم يرد كونه فلا يوجد وإن أمر به، كالإيمان ممن عَلِم الله أنه يستمر على الكفر حتى الموت.
The order of attachment: Know that the attachment of power, will, and knowledge is ordered: the attachment of power is dependent on the attachment of will, and the attachment of will is dependent on the attachment of knowledge. So the meaning is: He, exalted, does not bring into existence anything or annihilate it unless He wills it, and He does not will unless He knows it. So what He knows will be, He wills its being and existence, then He brings it forth in accordance with the will. And what He knows will not be and He did not will its being, it does not exist even if He commands it, like faith in one whom God knows will persist in disbelief until death.
Source pages p. 69

Commentary
This passage explains the order of attachment among God's attributes of knowledge, will, and power. It is a classification: the attachment of power is subordinate to the attachment of will, and the attachment of will is subordinate to the attachment of knowledge. In other words, God does not create or annihilate anything unless He wills it, and He does not will anything unless He knows it. The commentator provides an example: if God knows that a person will persist in disbelief until death, He does not will that person's faith, and thus faith does not occur for that person, even if God commands it. This hierarchy underscores that God's action is perfectly ordered by His knowledge and will.

تعلق
قدرة
إرادة
علم
تابع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think God's power acts independently of His will and knowledge.
Correction: The passage teaches that power is dependent on will, and will on knowledge. God's actions are always based on His knowledge and will.

Checkpoint
According to the order of attachment, what does the attachment of power depend on?
A) The attachment of knowledge.
B) The attachment of will.
C) The attachment of power is independent.
D) The attachment of will depends on power.

Key Takeaway
The attachment of God's power is dependent on His will, and His will on His knowledge, ensuring perfect order in divine action.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Your faith right now is exactly what God has willed for you, based on His eternal knowledge. Are you content with where you stand, or do you feel a tension between your efforts and the reality of His decree? How might this transform your reliance on Him rather than on yourself?
?
The passage gives the example of a person whom God knows will persist in disbelief until death. You might know someone who rejects faith despite clear truth. How does the fact that God has not willed their belief change the way you view them and your responsibility toward them? Will you plead to Allah to change their hearts, acknowledging that only He can?
?
If every event in your life occurs only because God knew it, willed it, and brought it into being, then every trial has a purpose rooted in His perfect knowledge. Can you truly accept the most painful recent event in your life as part of that ordered chain, or do you resent it? What would it look like to submit completely to that order?

Why God’s Power and Will Do Not Affect the Necessary or Impossible
Proof that God's power and will do not affect the necessary or impossible, using the nature of effect.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God’s power and will are only attached to possible things, not to the necessary or impossible, and why this reflects perfection, not deficiency.

Core Idea
God’s power only affects what can change; the necessary and impossible are beyond effect.

This lesson explores a subtle but important point in Islamic theology: why God’s attributes of power and will do not extend to the necessary (wajib) or the impossible (mustahil). It might seem limiting, but the reasoning shows it is a mark of perfection. By examining the nature of effect and the logical consequences of attaching power to the impossible, we see that only possible things can be objects of divine action. This argument relies on two key principles: tahsil al-hasil (acquisition of the already acquired) and qalb al-haqa'iq (reversal of realities).

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis. Then read the literal translation to grasp the overall argument.
Notice the author's concise style; every phrase serves the proof.
2
Identify the key terms and definitions
Note how the author defines power and will as 'attributes of effect' (صفتي تأثير) and explains the necessary concomitant of an effect.
This definition drives the entire proof.
3
Understand the two logical consequences
For the necessary, attaching power leads to tahsil al-hasil (if bringing into existence) or qalb al-haqa'iq (if annihilating). For the impossible, the consequences are reversed. Grasp why each is absurd.
Tahsil al-hasil means 'obtaining what is already obtained' – a logical contradiction.
4
Appreciate the conclusion and clarification
The passage concludes that God's perfection lies in this non-attachment, and clarifies it is due to the recipient (the necessary and impossible) not the agent.
This guards against misunderstanding God's power as limited.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
سبب عدم تعلق ومفهوم كلام الناظم رحمه الله تعالى: أن القدرة والإرادة لا تتعلقان بالواجب، ولا بالمستحيل، وهو كذلك، لأن القدرة والإرادة لما كانت صفتي تأثير، ومن لازم الأثر وجوده بعد عدم لزم أن ما لا يقبل العدم أصلا وهو الواجب، وما لا يقبل الوجود أصلا وهو المستحيل، لا يصح أن يكون أثرًا للقدرة والإرادة وإلا لزم تحصيل الحاصل على تعلقها بالواجب، بأن تعلقت بإيجاده، ولزم قلب الحقائق إن تعلقت بإعدامه. وكذا يقال في المستحيل يلزم على تعلقها به قلب الحقائق إن تعلقت بإيجاده وتحصل الحاصل إن تعلقت بإعدامه، والجميع محال وباطل، وما أدى إلى ذلك باطل، فالكمال المطلق لله عَزَوَجَلَّ في عدم تعلقها بالواجب والمستحيل.
وليكن منك على بال أن عدم تعلق القدرة والإرادة بما عدا الممكن ليس ذلك لقصور في الفاعل جَلَّ وَعَلَا، بل لنقص في القابل، فإدخال
The reason for non-attachment. And the meaning of the poet's statement, may God have mercy on him: that power and will do not attach to the necessary nor to the impossible, and it is so. Because power and will, since they are attributes of effect, and from the necessary concomitants of an effect is its existence after non-existence, it necessarily follows that what does not admit non-existence at all—that is the necessary—and what does not admit existence at all—that is the impossible—cannot be an effect of power and will. Otherwise, it would entail the acquisition of the already acquired if they attached to the necessary by bringing it into existence, and it would entail the reversal of realities if they attached to annihilating it. And likewise is said regarding the impossible: it would entail the reversal of realities if they attached to bringing it into existence, and the acquisition of the already acquired if they attached to annihilating it. And all of that is impossible and false, and whatever leads to that is false. So the absolute perfection of God, mighty and majestic, lies in the non-attachment of power and will to the necessary and impossible. And let it be well known to you that the non-attachment of power and will to other than the possible is not due to a deficiency in the agent, glory be to Him, but rather a deficiency in the recipient. So entering...
Source pages p. 69

Commentary
This passage provides the reason why God's power and will do not attach to the necessary (wajib) or the impossible (mustahil). It is a proof: power and will are attributes of effect (sifat ta'thir), and effect necessitates existence after non-existence. The necessary cannot cease to exist, and the impossible cannot exist. Hence, attaching power to the necessary to bring it into existence would be 'acquiring the already acquired' (tahsil al-hasil), and attaching it to annihilate the necessary would reverse realities. Similarly for the impossible. Therefore, the absolute perfection of God is shown by the fact that His power and will only attach to possible things. The commentator clarifies that this non-attachment is not due to any deficiency in God, but rather due to the nature of the recipient (the necessary and impossible). The final phrase 'فإدخال' likely begins another point, but the argument as given is complete.

Key Terms
قدرة
إرادة
واجب
مستحيل
ممكن
صفتي تأثير
تحصيل الحاصل
قلب الحقائق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that God's power is limited because it cannot affect the impossible.
Correction: The passage clarifies that non-attachment is due to the nature of the impossible itself—it is not an object that can receive effect—so God's power remains absolute; the deficiency is in the recipient.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, why does God's power not attach to the impossible?
A) Because God's power is limited.
B) Because the impossible cannot exist, so it cannot be an effect.
C) Because the impossible has no effect on God.
D) Because power only attaches to what is commanded.

Key Takeaway
God's power and will are perfect because they are only directed toward what is possible, avoiding logical absurdities like tahsil al-hasil and qalb al-haqa'iq.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever felt frustrated that God did not answer a prayer the way you wanted? How does this lesson help you trust that God's power is perfectly applied only to what is genuinely possible, and that your request might have been impossible by its very nature?
?
Think of a situation where you believed God was ‘unable’ to change something. After studying this lesson, can you see how the limitation might actually be in the object itself, not in God? How does that change your view of God's perfection?
?
The passage says non-attachment to the impossible is not a deficiency in God but in the recipient. Is there an area of your life where you blame God for not acting, when in reality the fault lies in the impossibility of the situation? How can you realign your expectations with this truth?

The Ordered Relationship of God's Attributes
Defines the ordered tacha'luq and proves non-relation to necessary and impossible.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the hierarchical order of God's power, will, and knowledge, and why they do not relate to the necessary and impossible.

Core Idea
God's power and will only relate to the possible, not to the necessary or impossible.

This lesson explores the ordered relationship of God's attributes of power, will, and knowledge. The author explains that power follows will, and will follows knowledge. He then proves that these attributes do not relate to the necessary (God) or the impossible, as that would lead to logical absurdities like acquiring what is already present or inverting realities.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation to grasp the sequence of ideas. Notice the logical progression from order to limits.
The passage contains both a definition and a proof.
2
Understand the order of tacha'luq
Identify that power's tacha'luq is subordinate to will, and will's tacha'luq is subordinate to knowledge. This reflects a causal hierarchy in divine action.
The meaning is clarified by examples: God only creates what He wills, and He only wills what He knows.
3
Grasp the limits of power and will
The author states that power and will do not relate to the necessary (wajib) or the impossible (mustahil). Understand why: they are attributes of effect, and effect requires existence after non-existence.
The necessary cannot cease to exist; the impossible cannot come to exist.
4
Reflect on the proof
The proof uses the concepts of tahsil al-hasil (acquiring what is already acquired) and qalb al-haqa'iq (inversion of realities). Relating power to the necessary would entail one of these absurdities.
The conclusion is that God's absolute perfection lies in non-relation to these two categories.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واعلم أن تعلق القدرة والإرادة والعلم مرتب، فتعلق القدرة تابع لتعلق الإرادة، وتعلق الإرادة تابع لتعلق العلم. فالمعنى: أنه تعالى لا يُوجد شيئًا أو يُعْدِمه إلا إذا أراده، ولا يريد إلا إذا علمه، فما علم أنه يكون أراد كونه ووجوده، ثم أبرزه على طبق الإرادة، وما عَلِم أنه لا يكون ولم يرد كونه فلا يوجد وإن أمر به، كالإيمان ممن عَلِم الله أنه يستمر على الكفر حتى الموت. ومفهوم كلام الناظم رحمه الله تعالى: أن القدرة والإرادة لا تتعلقان بالواجب، ولا بالمستحيل، وهو كذلك، لأن القدرة والإرادة لما كانت صفتي تأثير، ومن لازم الأثر وجوده بعد عدم لزم أن ما لا يقبل العدم أصلا وهو الواجب، وما لا يقبل الوجود أصلا وهو المستحيل، لا يصح أن يكون أثرًا للقدرة والإرادة وإلا لزم تحصيل الحاصل على تعلقها بالواجب، بأن تعلقت بإيجاده، ولزم قلب الحقائق إن تعلقت بإعدامه. وكذا يقال في المستحيل يلزم على تعلقها به قلب الحقائق إن تعلقت بإيجاده وتحصل الحاصل إن تعلقت بإعدامه، والجميع محال وباطل، وما أدى إلى ذلك باطل، فالكمال المطلق لله عز وجل في عدم تعلقها بالواجب والمستحيل. وليكن منك على بال أن عدم تعلق القدرة والإرادة بما عدا الممكن ليس ذلك لقصور في الفاعل جل وعلا، بل لنقص في القابل، فإدخال الجسم الكبير كالجمل في ثقب صغير كسم الخياط مستحيل، لا تتعلق القدرة به لعدم قابليته في الجمل، لكن القادر العظيم إذا أراد صغر الجسم الكبير، وكبر الثقب الصغير حتى خلق له قابلية لذلك، فافهم.
Know that the tacha'luq (relation/engagement) of power, will, and knowledge is ordered: the tacha'luq of power is subordinate to the tacha'luq of will, and the tacha'luq of will is subordinate to the tacha'luq of knowledge. The meaning is: He, exalted, does not bring something into existence or annihilate it except if He wills it, and He does not will except if He knows it. So what He knows will be, He wills its being and existence, then He brings it forth in accordance with the will. And what He knows will not be and He does not will its being, then it does not exist, even if He commands it, like faith from one whom God knows will persist in disbelief until death. And the purport of the author's (may God have mercy on him) speech: that power and will do not relate to the necessary (al-wajib), nor to the impossible (al-mustahil), and it is so. Because power and will, since they are attributes of effect (sifat ta'thir), and from the necessary concomitant of the effect is its existence after non-existence, it necessarily follows that what does not accept non-existence at all – which is the necessary – and what does not accept existence at all – which is the impossible – cannot be an effect of power and will. Otherwise, there would follow tahsil al-hasil (acquisition of the acquired) upon their relation to the necessary by relating to its bringing into existence, and there would follow inversion of realities if they related to its annihilation. Likewise, it is said regarding the impossible: there would follow inversion of realities if they related to its bringing into existence, and tahsil al-hasil if they related to its annihilation. All of that is absurd and false, and what leads to that is false. So absolute perfection for God, mighty and majestic, lies in their non-relation to the necessary and impossible. And let it be known to you that the non-relation of power and will to other than the possible is not due to deficiency in the agent (al-fa'il), exalted and high, but rather due to deficiency in the receiver (al-qabil). For entering a large body, like a camel, into a small hole like a needle's eye is impossible; power does not relate to it due to its lack of receptivity in the camel. But the mighty powerful one, if He wills, can make the large body small and enlarge the small hole until He creates receptivity for it. So understand.
Source pages p. 69

Commentary
This passage is a combined definition and proof. It begins by stating that the tacha'luq (engagement/relation) of God's attributes of power (qudra), will (irada), and knowledge ('ilm) is ordered hierarchically: power follows will, and will follows knowledge. The meaning is that God does not create or annihilate anything unless He wills it, and He does not will anything unless He knows it. Thus, what God knows will occur, He wills its existence and then brings it into being according to His will. What God knows will not occur and He does not will, it does not exist even if He commands it (e.g., faith from someone He knows will die in disbelief). Then the author explains that power and will do not relate to the necessary (wajib) or the impossible (mustahil). The reason is that power and will are attributes of effect (ta'thir), and effect requires existence after non-existence. The necessary (God) does not accept non-existence, and the impossible does not accept existence. If power/will related to the necessary by bringing it into existence, that would be 'tahsil al-hasil' (acquiring what is already present); if by annihilating it, that would invert realities. Similarly for the impossible, relating power to its existence would invert realities, and to its non-existence would be tahsil al-hasil. Both are impossible, so it is perfect for God that power and will do not relate to them. Finally, the author clarifies that this non-relation is not due to deficiency in God (the agent) but due to deficiency in the receiver (the object). He gives the example of a camel entering a needle's eye: it is impossible due to lack of receptivity, not because God is incapable. But if God wills, He can change the object's receptivity. So understand.

Key Terms
تعلق
قدرة
إرادة
علم
واجب
مستحيل
ممكن
تحصيل الحاصل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's power can relate to absolutely anything, including creating another god or making the necessary cease to exist.
Correction: God's power and will only relate to the possible. The necessary (God) cannot be affected, and the impossible cannot be actualized. Relating power to them would lead to logical absurdities like tahsil al-hasil or inversion of realities, which are incompatible with God's perfection.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the order of tacha'luq for power, will, and knowledge?
A) Power follows knowledge, knowledge follows will.
B) Power follows will, will follows knowledge.
C) Will follows power, power follows knowledge.
D) All three are independent of each other.

Key Takeaway
The tacha'luq of power and will is hierarchically ordered and limited to the possible, safeguarding God's perfection from logical absurdities.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
This lesson teaches that God's will is perfectly ordered and does not relate to what is impossible or beyond its scope. Reflect on a time when you insisted that God should answer a prayer in a specific way, perhaps ignoring the signs that His will was otherwise. How does this ordered tacha'luq challenge you to submit to His knowledge and will, even when the outcome seems contrary to your desires? What needs to change in your du'a (supplication) to align with His perfect order?
?
If God's power and will only relate to what is possible (mumkin), then everything that happens is within the realm of possibility. Does this truth bring you comfort or unease? Considering that He knows all possible outcomes and wills only what is best, how does this affect your trust in His decree (qadr) when you face difficulties or disappointments? Are you willing to accept that some impossibilities (like changing the past or forcing belief) are not part of His plan, and that this refusal is actually a mark of His perfection?

Divine Attributes
intermediate
4 min
The Role of the Receiver in Divine Power
Clarifies that non-relation of power to impossible is due to receiver deficiency, not agent deficiency.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the non-relation of God's power to the impossible is due to the receiver's deficiency, not any limitation in God.

Core Idea
Impossibility lies in the receiver, not in the agent.

After establishing that power and will only relate to the possible, the author clarifies a potential misunderstanding. Some might think this implies a deficiency in God's power. The author explains that it is actually due to the deficiency in the receiver (the object). He gives the powerful example of a camel trying to pass through a needle's eye to illustrate this point.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Note that this passage is a clarification and contains an illustrative example.
The passage directly addresses a potential misconception.
2
Understand the distinction
Identify the two possibilities: deficiency in the agent (God) vs. deficiency in the receiver (the object). The author emphasizes it is the latter.
This is crucial for understanding divine attributes.
3
Analyze the camel example
Reflect on why a camel cannot pass through a needle's eye: it is not because of lack of power, but because of the camel's size (lack of receptivity).
The example shows that the object must have the capacity to receive the action.
4
Apply the principle
Understand that God can change the receiver's receptivity if He wills, making the impossible possible by altering the object's nature.
This preserves God's absolute power while explaining why some things are impossible.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

Know that the tacha'luq (relation/engagement) of power, will, and knowledge is ordered: the tacha'luq of power is subordinate to the tacha'luq of will, and the tacha'luq of will is subordinate to the tacha'luq of knowledge. And what He knows will not be and He does not will its being, then it does not exist, even if He commands it, like faith from one whom God knows will persist in disbelief until death. Because power and will, since they are attributes of effect (sifat ta'thir), and from the necessary concomitant of the effect is its existence after non-existence, it necessarily follows that what does not accept non-existence at all – which is the necessary – and what does not accept existence at all – which is the impossible – cannot be an effect of power and will. Likewise, it is said regarding the impossible: there would follow inversion of realities if they related to its bringing into existence, and tahsil al-hasil if they related to its annihilation. Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: If God's power does not relate to the impossible, then God is not all-powerful.
Correction: God's power is absolute; the impossible is impossible due to the receiver's deficiency, not God's. God can change the receiver's nature, making the formerly impossible possible.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, why does God's power not relate to the impossible?
A) Because God is unable to do it.
B) Because the object lacks the necessary receptivity.
C) Because God chooses not to do it.
D) Because it contradicts God's will.

Key Takeaway
The impossibility of certain things does not indicate a limitation in God's power, but rather a lack of receptivity in the object, which God can change if He wills.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face a situation that seems impossible to change, do you secretly attribute that impossibility to God's lack of power over it, or to your own lack of receptivity? How does this lesson expose the real obstacle in your life? This week, name one area where you have given up because you thought 'it's impossible' and ask yourself: Is this truly impossible for God, or have I simply assumed my current state cannot change? Then take one concrete step to alter your receptivity.
?
Have you ever used the phrase 'it's not meant to be' or 'God hasn't willed it' to excuse a failure or avoid effort? How can you distinguish between a genuine impossibility (due to receiver deficiency) and a test from God that requires you to change your own receptivity? Write down one area of your life where you might be hiding behind 'God's will' as an excuse for your own lack of readiness to change.
?
If God can change the camel and the needle to make the impossible possible, what does that mean for the 'impossible' habits, sins, or broken relationships in your life? Are you willing to ask God to change your receptivity — even if it means you must become small or the problem must become large?

Divine Attributes

God's Hearing and Sight: Universality and Transcendence
Defines God's hearing and sight as universal, eternal attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that God's hearing and sight are eternal attributes that encompass every existent, and that they are fundamentally different from human hearing and sight.

Core Idea
God's hearing and sight disclose all existents; ours are limited.

This lesson begins with the poetic line commanding firm belief in God's hearing and sight. The commentary explains that these two attributes relate to every existing thing that is known to God, not just sounds or visible objects. We will see how they differ from our own sensory faculties, which are dependent on physical organs and conditions.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on hearing and sight
Read the Arabic and translation of the first part: 'And be certain that His hearing and sight relate to every existent that is seen...'
Notice the word 'يُرى' is explained as 'known'.
2
Identify the key terms
Find and understand the terms 'سمع' (hearing) and 'بصر' (sight) as attributes of God.
They are eternal and subsisting in the essence.
3
Contrast with human faculties
Read the commentary comparing divine and human hearing/sight. Note the limitations of ours.
Human hearing depends on distance; human sight on direction and conditions.
4
Reflect on transcendence
Ask yourself: Why must God's hearing and sight be different from ours? What does that teach about His perfection?
This affirms divine transcendence (tanzih).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واجزم بأن سمعه والبصرا * تعلقا بكل موجود يرى 40 وكلها قديمة بالذات * لأنها ليست بغير الذات. (واجزم) أي: اعتقد اعتقادًا جازمًا أيها المكلف بأن سمعه تعالى والبصرا بألف الإطلاق تعلقا معًا تعلق انكشاف بكل موجود يرى، أي: يُعلم، أي: بكل موجود معلوم له تعالى. فهما صفتان قديمتان قائمتان بذاته تعالى فسمعه وبصره تعالى يخالفان سمعنا وبصرنا في التعلق، لأن سمعنا يتعلق عادة ببعض موجودات، وهي الأصوات، بشرط عدم البعد جدا، وبصرنا يتعلق عادة ببعض الموجودات، وهي الأجسام وألوانها في جهة مخصوصة، على وجه مخصوص. (وكلها) أي: كل صفات المعاني المتقدمة قديمة بالذات أي: صفاته تعالى قديمة كذاته بذات الله تعالى، فليست بممكنة، بل هي قديمة، وأن قدمها ذاتي بقدم الذات، واستدل على ذلك بقوله: ( لأنها ليست بغير الذات العلية (1) وأما سمعنا وبصرنا فحادثان قائمان بمحل مخصوص، فبصرنا قائم بإنسان العين أو هو قوة مودعة في العصبتين المجوفتين اللتين يتلاقيان ثم يفترقان، كما هو مذهب الحكماء، وسمعنا قائم بالصماخ - أي: ثقب الأذن أو هو قوة قائمة بالعصب المفروش في مقعر الصماخ، والله تعالى منزه عن ذلك، وسمعنا وبصرنا من أسباب علومنا، بخلاف سمعه وبصره تعالى.
And be certain that His hearing and sight relate to every existent that is seen (i.e., known). And all of them are eternal by essence because they are not other than the essence. (Wajzim) i.e., believe with firm belief, O responsible one, that His hearing, exalted, and sight – with the alif of absolute [pronunciation] – relate together a relation of disclosure to every existent that is seen, i.e., known, i.e., to every existent known to Him, exalted. So they are two eternal attributes subsisting in His essence, exalted. So His hearing and sight, exalted, differ from our hearing and sight in relation, because our hearing relates customarily to some existents, namely sounds, on condition of not being too distant, and our sight relates customarily to some existents, namely bodies and their colors, in a specific direction, in a specific manner. (And all of them) i.e., all the previous attributes of meanings (sifat al-ma'ani) are eternal by essence, i.e., His attributes, exalted, are eternal like His essence, by the essence of God, exalted. So they are not possible, rather they are eternal, and their eternality is inherent by the eternality of the essence. And he proved that by his statement: 'Because they are not other than the lofty essence.' (1) As for our hearing and sight, they are originated, subsisting in a specific locus. Our sight subsists in the pupil of the eye, or it is a power deposited in the two hollow nerves that meet then separate, as is the doctrine of the philosophers. And our hearing subsists in the ear canal – i.e., the hole of the ear – or it is a power subsisting in the nerve spread in the concavity of the ear canal. And God, exalted, is transcendent of that. And our hearing and sight are among the causes of our knowledge, unlike His hearing and sight, exalted.
Source pages p. 70

Commentary
This passage is a definition and proof. It begins with the author's line: 'And be certain that His hearing and sight relate to every existent that is seen' – where 'seen' means known. The commentary explains: one must firmly believe that God's hearing (sam') and sight (basar) are two eternal attributes that disclose every existent known to Him. They differ from human hearing and sight. Human hearing relates to sounds not too distant; human sight relates to bodies and colors in a specific direction and manner. Then the author states: 'And all of them are eternal by essence because they are not other than the essence.' The commentary clarifies: 'all of them' refers to all the previously mentioned attributes of meanings (sifat al-ma'ani). They are eternal like God's essence, not possible; their eternality is inherent from the eternality of the essence. The proof is: they are not other than the essence, so they share its eternal nature. A footnote adds: human hearing and sight are originated, subsisting in physical organs (pupil, nerves, ear canal) according to philosophers, and God is transcendent of that. Human hearing and sight cause knowledge, unlike God's.

Key Terms
سمع
بصر
قديم
تعلق
انكشاف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Thinking God's hearing and sight are like human sensory perception, limited to sounds and visible objects in a specific way.
Correction: God's hearing and sight encompass every existent (ma'lūm) without any limitation. They are not dependent on organs or physical conditions.

Checkpoint
What does the commentary say about the relation (tacalluq) of God's hearing and sight?
A) It is limited to sounds and colors.
B) It is a relation of disclosure to every existent known to Him.
C) It depends on physical distance.
D) It only applies to humans.

Key Takeaway
God's hearing and sight are eternal, universal attributes that disclose all existents, completely unlike human senses.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You believe God hears and sees every existent, including your most private thoughts and actions. Are you living with this awareness, or do you often act as if only your limited senses are watching? What specific habit would you need to change to truly honor that God sees and hears everything?
?
Your hearing and sight are confined to sounds at a distance and bodies in a direction. How does this contrast with God's unlimited perception humble you? In what area of your life do you need to submit your limited judgment to God's all-encompassing knowledge?
?
God's hearing and sight are eternal and unchanging—they do not fade or rest. Since you cannot escape His awareness, what sin are you still clinging to secretly, thinking it is hidden? Will you confess it to God and ask His forgiveness before you meet Him?

Divine Attributes
beginner
4 min
The Eternity of All Attributes of Meanings
States the eternity of all attributes of meanings and gives the proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that all attributes of meanings (sifat al-ma'ani) are eternal by essence, and the proof is that they are not other than the essence.

Core Idea
Attributes are eternal because they are not separate from God's essence.

In the previous lesson we saw that hearing and sight are eternal. Now the author extends this to all attributes of meanings (sifat al-ma'ani) – the seven attributes: life, knowledge, will, power, hearing, sight, speech. He states that each is eternal by essence, not possible. The proof is simple yet profound: they are not other than the essence.

Guided Path
1
Read the statement about all attributes
Read the line: 'And all of them are eternal by essence because they are not other than the essence.'
The phrase 'كلها' refers to all previously mentioned attributes of meanings.
2
Understand the proof
The proof is direct: if attributes are not other than the essence, they share the essence's eternal nature.
This avoids the need for a separate argument for each attribute.
3
Contrast with human attributes
Read the footnote about human hearing and sight being originated and physical.
This highlights that divine attributes are not like created ones.
4
Reflect on the implications
Why is it important that attributes are not other than the essence? What does this safeguard against?
It avoids multiplicity of eternals and affirms tawhid.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واجزم بأن سمعه والبصرا * تعلقا بكل موجود يرى 40 وكلها قديمة بالذات * لأنها ليست بغير الذات. (واجزم) أي: اعتقد اعتقادًا جازمًا أيها المكلف بأن سمعه تعالى والبصرا بألف الإطلاق تعلقا معًا تعلق انكشاف بكل موجود يرى، أي: يُعلم، أي: بكل موجود معلوم له تعالى. فهما صفتان قديمتان قائمتان بذاته تعالى فسمعه وبصره تعالى يخالفان سمعنا وبصرنا في التعلق، لأن سمعنا يتعلق عادة ببعض موجودات، وهي الأصوات، بشرط عدم البعد جدا، وبصرنا يتعلق عادة ببعض الموجودات، وهي الأجسام وألوانها في جهة مخصوصة، على وجه مخصوص. (وكلها) أي: كل صفات المعاني المتقدمة قديمة بالذات أي: صفاته تعالى قديمة كذاته بذات الله تعالى، فليست بممكنة، بل هي قديمة، وأن قدمها ذاتي بقدم الذات، واستدل على ذلك بقوله: ( لأنها ليست بغير الذات العلية (1) وأما سمعنا وبصرنا فحادثان قائمان بمحل مخصوص، فبصرنا قائم بإنسان العين أو هو قوة مودعة في العصبتين المجوفتين اللتين يتلاقيان ثم يفترقان، كما هو مذهب الحكماء، وسمعنا قائم بالصماخ - أي: ثقب الأذن أو هو قوة قائمة بالعصب المفروش في مقعر الصماخ، والله تعالى منزه عن ذلك، وسمعنا وبصرنا من أسباب علومنا، بخلاف سمعه وبصره تعالى.
And be certain that His hearing and sight relate to every existent that is seen (i.e., known). And all of them are eternal by essence because they are not other than the essence. (Wajzim) i.e., believe with firm belief, O responsible one, that His hearing, exalted, and sight – with the alif of absolute [pronunciation] – relate together a relation of disclosure to every existent that is seen, i.e., known, i.e., to every existent known to Him, exalted. So they are two eternal attributes subsisting in His essence, exalted. So His hearing and sight, exalted, differ from our hearing and sight in relation, because our hearing relates customarily to some existents, namely sounds, on condition of not being too distant, and our sight relates customarily to some existents, namely bodies and their colors, in a specific direction, in a specific manner. (And all of them) i.e., all the previous attributes of meanings (sifat al-ma'ani) are eternal by essence, i.e., His attributes, exalted, are eternal like His essence, by the essence of God, exalted. So they are not possible, rather they are eternal, and their eternality is inherent by the eternality of the essence. And he proved that by his statement: 'Because they are not other than the lofty essence.' (1) As for our hearing and sight, they are originated, subsisting in a specific locus. Our sight subsists in the pupil of the eye, or it is a power deposited in the two hollow nerves that meet then separate, as is the doctrine of the philosophers. And our hearing subsists in the ear canal – i.e., the hole of the ear – or it is a power subsisting in the nerve spread in the concavity of the ear canal. And God, exalted, is transcendent of that. And our hearing and sight are among the causes of our knowledge, unlike His hearing and sight, exalted.
Source pages p. 70

Commentary
This passage is a definition and proof. It begins with the author's line: 'And be certain that His hearing and sight relate to every existent that is seen' – where 'seen' means known. The commentary explains: one must firmly believe that God's hearing (sam') and sight (basar) are two eternal attributes that disclose every existent known to Him. They differ from human hearing and sight. Human hearing relates to sounds not too distant; human sight relates to bodies and colors in a specific direction and manner. Then the author states: 'And all of them are eternal by essence because they are not other than the essence.' The commentary clarifies: 'all of them' refers to all the previously mentioned attributes of meanings (sifat al-ma'ani). They are eternal like God's essence, not possible; their eternality is inherent from the eternality of the essence. The proof is: they are not other than the essence, so they share its eternal nature. A footnote adds: human hearing and sight are originated, subsisting in physical organs (pupil, nerves, ear canal) according to philosophers, and God is transcendent of that. Human hearing and sight cause knowledge, unlike God's.

Key Terms
صفات المعاني
قديم
ذات
حادث
سمع
بصر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Thinking that God's attributes are separate entities created independently, or that they are accidents that could be originated.
Correction: They are not other than the essence; they are co-eternal with it and not possible.

Checkpoint
What is the proof given that all attributes of meanings are eternal?
A) They are self-subsisting.
B) They are not other than the essence.
C) They are impossible to be originated.
D) They are attributes of meanings.

Key Takeaway
All divine attributes of meanings are eternal by essence because they are not other than the essence itself.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God's attributes are not other than His essence, do you ever think of His mercy or anger as separate from Him? How does that change the way you fear or hope in Him?
?
When you describe God as 'All-Hearing', do you imagine a process like human hearing? What does it mean that His hearing is eternal and without cause? How should that shape your awareness that He hears every whisper?
?
You affirm that God's attributes are eternal—but does your heart truly rest in that, or do you sometimes act as if God's attributes could change or fail? What would change in your trust if you fully believed His power and knowledge are co-eternal with Him?

The Eternality of Divine Speech: Beyond Letters and Sequence
Commentary on the matn line that Allah's speech is not composed of letters or sequence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Allah's speech is not composed of letters or sounds and does not involve temporal sequence, distinguishing it from created human speech.

Core Idea
Divine speech is an eternal attribute, not a sequence of letters or sounds.

In this passage, the commentator clarifies a crucial distinction: Allah's speech (kalam) is unlike human speech. While our speech is created, composed of letters and sounds, and unfolds in time, Allah's speech is an eternal attribute of His essence. This lesson explores the implications of that difference, based on the matn line that negates letters, sounds, and arrangement from divine speech. By understanding this, you will guard against anthropomorphic misconceptions about God's attribute of speech.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic passage and its literal translation. Notice the key terms: حرف (letter), صوت (sound), ترتيب (arrangement), حادث (temporal), قديمة (eternal).
The passage is a commentary on a matn line.
2
Identify what is negated
The passage explicitly negates that divine speech consists of letters, sounds, or temporal arrangement. Underline these negations in the text.
3
Grasp the contrast with human speech
The commentator contrasts divine speech with human speech, which is temporally originated (حادث) and consists of letters and sounds. Reflect on why this contrast is essential for tawheed.
4
Understand the positive affirmation
The passage affirms that divine speech is an attribute of His sacred essence (صفة ذاته المقدسة). This means speech is not an accident but a permanent attribute. How does this affect our understanding of Allah's communication?
5
Apply the lesson
Consider how this knowledge refutes the view that the Quran (as divine speech) is created. The Quran's eternity is defended by this distinction.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
كلامه تعالى ثم لما ذكر سابقا أن من صفات المعاني الكلام، أراد أن يبين أنه ليس ليس ككلام ككلامنا، فإن كلامنا حادث، وهو بحرف وصوت، وكلام الله عَزَّوَجَلَّ صفة قديمة فقال: (ثم الكلام ليس بالحروف والأصوات، (وليس) مُتَلَبّسًا (بالترتيب) والتأخير، (كـ) الكلام الحادث (المألوف) والمعروف لنا، فهو داخل في حيز النفي، أي أنه ليس مثل كلامنا، بل هو صفة ذاته المقدسة في الكلام، فالكلام لا ينحصر في الحروف والأصوات.
His speech, may He be exalted, then when he mentioned previously that among the attributes of meanings is speech, he intended to clarify that it is not like our speech, for our speech is temporally originated (hādith), and it consists of letters and sound, while the speech of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is an eternal attribute. So he said: (Then speech is not from letters and sounds, nor is it clothed with arrangement and delay, like the temporally originated familiar speech known to us), so it enters into the domain of negation, i.e., it is not like our speech, but rather it is an attribute of His sacred essence in terms of speech, so speech is not confined to letters and sounds.
Source pages p. 71

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on the matn line regarding the attribute of speech (kalam). The author explains that after mentioning speech among the attributes of meanings, he wants to clarify that divine speech is not like human speech. Human speech is temporally originated (hādith) and consists of letters and sounds, while Allah's speech is an eternal attribute. The matn line negates that divine speech is composed of letters or sounds, and also negates that it involves arrangement or temporal sequence, as human speech does ('familiar speech'). Thus, divine speech is completely unlike created speech; it is an attribute of Allah's essence and is not limited to letters and sounds. The point is to affirm the eternity of divine speech and distinguish it from the contingent speech of creatures.

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That Allah's speech is similar to human speech, composed of letters and sounds, and uttered in sequence.
Correction: Allah's speech is an eternal attribute devoid of letters, sounds, and temporal order. It is not created and does not resemble human speech.

Checkpoint

C) Because human speech is limited to certain subjects.
D) Because Allah's speech is dictated by angels.

Key Takeaway
Divine speech is an eternal attribute not composed of letters or sounds, unlike temporal human speech.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
Your own speech is temporal, limited, and sequential. How does the utter difference of Allah's speech humble you before Him? Are you willing to let His speech define your life, even when it contradicts your own desires?
?
The passage says divine speech is 'not like the familiar speech known to us'. In what hidden ways do you still imagine Allah speaking like a human? What must you uproot from your heart to truly affirm His transcendence in this attribute?

Divine Attributes

Attributes of Meaning: Additional and Inseparable
Explanation that divine attributes are not identical to essence and are inseparable.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the divine attributes of meaning are additional to the essence yet not distinct entities, and why this does not imply multiple eternal beings.

Core Idea
Divine attributes are real and inseparable, but not separate beings.

Sanusi addresses a subtle point in divine attribute theology: if attributes are real and eternal, do they become independent gods? This passage clarifies that while attributes are additional in meaning, they are not distinct essences. This careful distinction preserves tawhid while affirming God's perfect attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Notice the author's concise argument style.
The passage contains a reductio ad absurdum and a response to an objection.
2
Identify the main claims
The passage makes three points: (1) attributes are inseparable from the essence, (2) they are not identical to the essence, and (3) this does not lead to multiple eternals. Find each claim.
3
Understand the objection and answer
Consider why someone might think attributes being additional implies multiple gods. Then see how Sanusi refutes this by denying distinction (taghāyur) between essence and attributes.
The key concept is 'taghāyur' – distinction in essence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
بمعنى أنها لا تنفك عنها، فلا يعقل قيام الذات بدونها، وليس وجودها في نفسها، وليست بعين الذات، وإلا لزم أن تكون الذات صفة، وأن الحياة عين العلم مثلا، وهو باطل. ولا يلزم من ذلك تعدد القدماء، لأن المحظور إنما هو تعدد القدماء المتغايرة، ونحن نمنع تغير الذات الصفات، والصفات بعضها مع بعض فينتفي التعدد، إذ لا يقبل إلا مع التغاير، فلا يلزم التعدد ولا التكثر ولا قدم الغير، ولا تكثر القدماء. قال العلامة السباعي أثر ذلك: «فعلِم أن مذهب أهل السنة: أن صفات الذات زائدة عليها، قائمة بها، لازمة لها لزوما لا يقبل الانفكاك فهي دائمة الوجود والبقاء، فلا يطرأ [عليها] العدم فهو حي بحياة عالم بعلم قادر بقدرة وهكذا».
Meaning that they [the attributes] are not separable from it [the essence]; thus the subsistence of the essence without them is inconceivable. Their existence is not in themselves, nor are they the same as the essence; otherwise it would necessitate that the essence be an attribute, and that life be identical to knowledge for example, which is false. And from that does not follow multiplicity of the Pre-eternals, because the forbidden thing is only multiplicity of distinct Pre-eternals, and we deny that the essence and the attributes, and the attributes with one another, are distinct; so multiplicity is negated, for it is only accepted with distinction. Thus multiplicity, numerousness, pre-eternality of another, and numerousness of Pre-eternals do not follow. Al-ʿAllāma al-Sibāʿī said following that: 'Know that the madhhab of Ahl al-Sunna is that the attributes of the essence are additional to it, subsisting by it, inseparable from it with an inseparability that does not admit disconnection; so they are perpetual in existence and duration; non-existence does not occur to them. So He is living by a life, knowing by a knowledge, powerful by a power, and so forth.'
Source pages p. 71

Commentary
This passage explains the nature of the divine attributes of meaning (ṣifāt al-maʿānī). The author asserts that these attributes are inseparable from the divine essence—it is inconceivable for the essence to exist without them. However, they are not existent in themselves (i.e., they are not independent substances) nor are they identical to the essence. If they were identical, that would imply that the essence is an attribute, which is absurd, or that life is the same as knowledge, which is also false. The author then addresses a possible objection: if the attributes are additional to the essence, does that entail multiple eternal beings? He answers no, because multiplicity requires distinct (mutaghāyira) entities. The attributes are not distinct from the essence or from each other in a way that would make them separate pre-eternals; they are distinct only in meaning (maʿna). Therefore, the fear of multiple eternals is unfounded. To reinforce this, he quotes al-Sibāʿī, who states the standard Sunni position: the attributes of the essence are additional (zāʾida) to the essence, subsisting by it, inseparable, perpetual, and never subject to non-existence. This is the madhhab of Ahl al-Sunna.

Key Terms
صفات الذات
زيادة
عدم الانفكاك
تعدد القدماء
التغاير

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Saying the attributes are additional to the essence implies multiple eternal gods.
Correction: The attributes are not distinct essences; distinction in meaning alone does not produce multiplicity of eternals.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, why does saying attributes are additional not lead to multiple eternals?
A) Because attributes are identical to essence
B) Because attributes are not distinct essences (mutaghāyira)
C) Because attributes are created
D) Because attributes are independent

Key Takeaway
The Sunni position affirms that God's attributes are real, additional in meaning, inseparable, and eternal, yet they do not compromise the oneness of God.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you call upon Allah as al-‘Alīm (the All-Knowing), do you truly feel that His knowledge is a real, eternal attribute inseparable from Him, or do you reduce it to a mere mental concept? How would your dua change if you internalized that His knowledge is as real as His existence?
?
The lesson argues that attributes are not identical to the essence yet not separate beings. Does this truth comfort you or challenge your mental image of God? Are you willing to accept a mystery that your mind cannot fully reconcile, or do you find yourself leaning toward simplification (denying the attributes) or exaggeration (making them gods)?
?
If you truly believed that Allah’s life, knowledge, and power are inseparable from Him and eternal, what would you change about your reliance on Him today? Identify one specific worry you have and ask yourself: is it because you forget that His power is not a distant concept but a present, inseparable reality?

Divine Speech Is Not Composed of Letters or Sounds
Affirmation that divine speech is a pre-eternal attribute not consisting of letters/sounds or temporal sequence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that divine speech is a pre-eternal attribute distinct from human speech, not composed of letters or sounds, and not subject to temporal sequence.

Core Idea
Allah's speech is an eternal attribute, not bound by letters, sounds, or time.

In this passage, the commentator builds on the earlier mention of speech as an attribute of meaning. He clarifies that divine speech is fundamentally different from human speech. While human speech is created, composed of letters and sounds, and occurs in time, Allah's speech is eternal and transcends these limitations. This distinction is crucial for avoiding anthropomorphic misconceptions.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the negation pattern: 'ليس بالحروف وليس بالترتيب'.
2
Identify the key contrast
The passage contrasts divine speech with human speech. Find the specific features that are denied of divine speech (letters, sounds, arrangement).
Human speech is described as 'حادث' (originated) and 'بالحروف والأصوات'.
3
Understand the theological point
The passage affirms that divine speech is a 'صفة قديمة' (pre-eternal attribute) of the essence. Reflect on why this matters for tawhid.
This guards against anthropomorphism (tashbih).
4
Internalize the distinction
Summarize in your own words: How is Allah's speech different from ours? Why can't we think of it as composed of letters or sounds?
The key is that Allah's speech is not like any created thing.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثُمَّ الكَلامُ لَيْس بالحُروفِ وَلَيْسَ بالترتيب كَالْمَأْلُونِ المخلوقين. كلامه تعالى ثم لما ذكر سابقا أن من صفات المعاني الكلام، أراد أن يبين أنه ليس ليس ككلام ككلامنا، فإن كلامنا حادث، وهو بحرف وصوت، وكلام الله عَزَّوَجَلَّ صفة قديمة فقال: (ثم الكلام ليس بالحروف والأصوات، (وليس) مُتَلَبّسًا (بالترتيب) والتأخير، (كـ) الكلام الحادث (المألوف) والمعروف لنا، فهو داخل في حيز النفي، أي أنه ليس مثل كلامنا، بل هو صفة ذاته المقدسة في الكلام، فالكلام لا ينحصر في الحروف والأصوات.
Then speech is not by letters nor by arrangement, like the customary [speech] of created beings. His speech—may He be exalted—then when he previously mentioned that among the attributes of meaning is speech, he intended to show that it is not like our speech; for our speech is originated, and it is by letter and sound, while the speech of Allah, mighty and majestic, is a pre-eternal attribute. So he said: 'Then speech is not by letters and sounds, nor is it attired by arrangement and delay, like the originated speech that is customary and known to us.' So it falls within the scope of negation, i.e., it is not like our speech; rather it is the attribute of His sacred essence in speech, for speech is not confined to letters and sounds.
Source pages p. 71

Commentary
This passage explains the nature of the divine attribute of speech (al-kalām). The author quotes the verse from al-Kharīda: 'Then speech is not by letters nor by arrangement, like the customary [speech] of created beings.' He then interprets it, explaining that speech is one of the attributes of meaning, and the author aims to distinguish it from human speech. Human speech is originated (ḥādith), composed of letters and sounds, and occurs in temporal sequence. In contrast, Allah's speech is a pre-eternal (qadīm) attribute that is not composed of letters or sounds, nor does it involve temporal arrangement or delay. He emphasizes that divine speech is not like our speech; it is an attribute of the essence, not confined to letters and sounds. The key point is that speech in God is a real attribute, but it is not the speech of created beings—it is transcendent, not limited by physical or temporal properties.

Correction: Divine speech is a pre-eternal attribute not consisting of letters or sounds, thus not anthropomorphic.

B) It is not composed of letters or sounds

Key Takeaway
Allah's speech is an eternal attribute of His essence, not composed of letters or sounds, unlike created human speech.

?

?

How does this passage refute that assumption? What step will you take this week to purify your concept of Allah's attributes from any trace of anthropomorphism?

Proof That the Opposites of Allah's Attributes Are Impossible
Mashāṭ provides a logical proof by conditional that the opposites of Allah's attributes are impossible.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the logical proof that the opposites of Allah's attributes are impossible, based on the entailment of poverty and temporality.

Core Idea
If Allah lacked a perfection, He would have its opposite, which implies need — impossible for the Self-Sufficient.

Mashāṭ presents a concise proof that the opposites of Allah’s attributes cannot exist. The reasoning is conditional: if Allah were not perfect, He would be imperfect; imperfection implies need and contingency, which contradicts His necessary existence. This lesson unpacks that argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
The passage consists of three poem lines followed by a commentary that explains the logic.
2
Follow the conditional argument
Identify the conditional premise: if Allah were not described with lofty attributes, He would be described with their opposites. Notice how the commentary explains that this leads to poverty and temporality, which are impossible.
The proof is not circular; it uses the fact that absence of a perfection implies presence of its opposite.
3
Reflect on the conclusion
Understand that because Allah is self-sufficient and omnipotent, He cannot be characterized by any deficiency. Thus, the opposites of His attributes are impossible.
This reinforces the doctrine of divine perfection.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٤٤ | لأَنَّهُ لَوْ لَمْ يَكُنْ مَوْصُوفا * بِهَا لَكَانَ بِالسِّوَى مَعْرُوفا ٤٥ وكُلُّ مَنْ قَامَ بِهِ سِوَاهَا * فَهْوَ الذِي فِي الفَقْرِ قَدْ تَنَاهَى ٤٦ والوَاحِدُ الْمَعْبُودُ لا يَفْتَقِرُ * لِغَيْرِهِ جَلَّ الغَنِي الْمُقْتَدِرُ شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي . وإنما وجبت له الصفات العُلى واستحال عليه أضدادها: (لأنه) تَبَارَكَ وَتَعَالَى (لو لم يكن موصوفًا بها لكان بالسوى) بكسر السين، وفتح الواو مقصورًا، والألف واللام عوض عن المضاف إليه، ويتعلق بقوله: (معروفًا) أي: لكان معروفًا بسواها، في العجز والجهل إلى آخر المستحيلات، يعني: لو لم يكن متصفًا بالصفات الشامخات لا تصف بأضدادها، لكن اتصافه بأضدادها ،باطل لما يلزم عليه من الافتقار والحدوث، كما قال الناظم رحمه الله تعالى: وكل من قام به سواها فاعل ،قام أي كل من قام به غير الصفات العلى الشامخات من العجز والجهل وغيرهما من المستحيلات، فهو الذي في الفقر والاحتياج إلى من يكمله (قد تناهي) أي: قد تناهى في الفقر والاحتياج، فقوله: «في الفقر يتعلق بقوله: «قد تناهى»، وهو محال، لأنه يؤدي إلى الحدوث. والواو في قول الناظم: ( والواحد) لعلها للاستئناف، أو للحال، أي: والحال أن الله تعالى الواحد الذي هو (المعبود) بحق (لا يفتقر لغيره) بل غيره مفتقر إليه تعالى (جل) وعظم عن ذلك الافتقار الله (الغني) بسكون الياء للوزن أي: الغني عن كل ما سواه، (المقتدر) على كل شيء، وكل شيء هو إلى الغني المقتدر فقير.
44. 'Because if He were not described with them, He would be known by their opposite.' 45. 'And whoever is characterized by other than them, he is the one who has reached the extreme in poverty.' 46. 'And the One, the rightly worshiped, is not in need of others; majestic is the Self-Sufficient, the Omnipotent.' Commentary by Sheikh Hasan Muhammad al-Mishāt al-Mālikī. And the lofty attributes are obligatory for Him and their opposites are impossible for Him: because He, blessed and exalted, if He were not described with them, He would be described by what is other than them using kasra on the sīn and fatḥa on the wāw (al-siwā), alif and lām compensating for the omitted possessive, and it is related to His saying: 'known' — i.e., He would be known by other than them, i.e., by impotence, ignorance, and other impossibilities. That is, if He were not characterized by the lofty attributes, He would be characterized by their opposites. But His characterization by their opposites is invalid because it entails poverty (iftaqār) and temporality (ḥudūth), as the author said: 'And whoever is characterized by other than them' — i.e., whoever is characterized by other than the lofty attributes such as impotence, ignorance, and other impossibilities, then he is the one who has reached the extreme in poverty and need to one who completes him. His saying 'in poverty' is related to his saying 'has reached the extreme' — and that is impossible because it leads to temporality. The wāw in the author's saying: 'and the One' is perhaps for resumption or for the circumstantial state, i.e., the state being that Allah, the One who is rightly worshiped, is not in need of others; rather, others are in need of Him. He is exalted and glorified above that poverty. Allah is the Self-Sufficient (with sukūn on the yā' for meter), i.e., independent of all else, and the Omnipotent over everything, and everything is poor in need of the Self-Sufficient, the Omnipotent.
Source pages p. 73

Commentary
This passage provides the logical proof (burhān) that the opposites of the necessary attributes are impossible for Allah. It is a classification of an argument. The proof is structured as a conditional: if Allah were not described with the lofty attributes (e.g., life, knowledge, power), then He would be described by their opposites (ignorance, impotence, etc.). But being described by those opposites entails poverty (iftaqār) and temporality (ḥudūth), which are impossible for Allah. The poem lines reinforce this: line 44 states the conditional premise, line 45 notes that anyone characterized by deficiencies is in extreme poverty, and line 46 declares that the One God is not in need. The commentator explains that the 'poverty' here means neediness for a perfecter, and this implies contingency, which is impossible for Allah. The wāw in 'and the One' is interpreted as resumptive or circumstantial, emphasizing that Allah is self-sufficient and all others are dependent on Him. The proof does not assume the conclusion; it shows that if Allah lacked a perfection, He would have its opposite, which is a deficiency, but deficiency is impossible for the necessarily existent being.

Key Terms
وجوب
استحالة
صفات عُلى
افتقار
حدوث
غني
مقتدر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The proof is circular: it assumes Allah must have perfect attributes.
Correction: The proof does not assume the conclusion; it uses the principle that absence of a perfection entails its opposite, which implies need and temporality. Since need is impossible for Allah, the conclusion follows logically.

Checkpoint
According to the proof presented, what would follow if Allah were not described with the lofty attributes?
A) He would be described with their opposites, which entail poverty and temporality.
B) He would be described with even more perfect attributes.
C) He would be unknown and unknowable.
D) He would have no attributes at all.

Key Takeaway
The proof shows that any imperfection implies need, which is impossible for Allah, so He must possess all perfections and their opposites cannot exist.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that any deficiency in Allah is impossible because it would entail need, would you ever dare to doubt His knowledge of your hidden acts? How does this lesson confront your secret sins?
?
The proof shows that you are the one in need, not Allah. Are there areas of your life where you act as if Allah depends on your worship or obedience? How can you shift your mindset to worship out of your own need rather than duty?
?
If Allah's opposite attributes are impossible, then His promise of reward and threat of punishment are certain. Is there a command you have been neglecting that this certainty now makes urgent? What will you do about it today?
