Essential Attributes of Prophets
Enumeration of prophetic attributes

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the four essential attributes of prophets and the rational status of their opposites.

Core Idea
Prophets must be trustworthy, truthful, conveying, and intelligent.

After affirming the vision of God, the poem turns to the attributes of all messengers. The author lists four essential qualities and states that their opposites are impossible for prophets. This lesson also explains the permissibility of human actions for them and the purpose of their sending.

Guided Path
1
Identify the four attributes
The passage lists amāna, ṣidq, tablīgh, and faṭāna. Write them down and note their meanings.
These are the wajib (necessary) attributes of prophets.
2
Understand the opposites
The author says their opposites are impossible (mustaḥīl) for prophets. This means prophets cannot be treacherous, dishonest, conceal revelation, or be foolish.
3
Consider permissible actions
The poem mentions that permissible human actions like eating are allowed for prophets, showing they are human but protected from sin.
4
Reflect on the purpose of sending
The sending of prophets is a favor (tafaḍḍul) and mercy (raḥma) from God to the worlds.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واجزمَ أَخِي بِرُؤيَةِ الإلهِ * فِي جَنَّةِ الْخُلْدِ بِلا تَنَاهِي إذ الوقوع جائز بالعقلِ * وَقَدْ أَتَى فِيهِ دَلِيلُ النَّقْل وَصِف جَمِيعَ الرُّسُلِ بِالأَمَانَة . وَالصِّدْقِ والتبليغ والفَطَانَة وَيَسْتَحِيلُ ضِدُّها عَليهِم * وجَائِزٌ كَالأَكْلِ فِي حَقِّهِم إرْسَالُهُم تَفَضُّلٌ وَرَحْمَةً * للعالَمِينَ جَل مُولِي النُّعْمَةَ وَيَلْزَمُ الإِيمَانُ بِالْحِسَابِ * والْحَشْرِ والعِقَابِ والثَّوَابِ والنَّشْرِ والصِّراطِ والْمِيزَانِ * والحوض والنَّيْرَانِ والْجِنَانِ والجِنِّ والأَمْلاكِ ثُمَّ الأَنْبِيَا * والْحُورِ والوِلْدَانِ ثُمَّ الأَوْلِيَا
And be certain, my brother, of seeing God in the Garden of Eternity without end. For its occurrence is permissible by reason, and textual proof has come concerning it. And describe all the messengers with trustworthiness, truthfulness, conveying, and intelligence. And their opposites are impossible regarding them; and permissible are things like eating in their right. Their sending is favor and mercy for the worlds; Exalted is the Bestower of blessing. And belief is necessary in the accounting, the resurrection, punishment, and reward, and the spreading, the path, the balance, the basin, the hellfire, and the gardens, and the jinn, the angels, then the prophets, and the beautiful maidens, and the boys, then the saints.
Source pages p. 16

Commentary
This final passage covers three topics. First, the vision of God (ru'yat Allāh) in Paradise: it is rationally possible and textually established. Second, the attributes of prophets: they must be trustworthy (amāna), truthful (ṣidq), conveying (tablīgh), and intelligent (faṭāna). Their opposites are impossible, while permissible human actions like eating are allowed for them. Their sending (irsāl) is a favor and mercy from God. Third, eschatological beliefs that are necessary: accounting (ḥisāb), resurrection (ḥashr), punishment and reward, spreading (nashr), path (ṣirāṭ), balance (mīzān), basin (ḥawḍ), hellfire (nīrān), gardens (jinān), and also belief in jinn, angels, prophets, beautiful maidens (ḥūr), boys (wildān), and saints (awliyā'). This is a comprehensive list of Sunni creedal points.

Misconception: Some think prophets are not human or have no human needs.
Correction: The passage affirms permissible human actions for prophets.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is NOT listed as an attribute of messengers?
A) Trustworthiness
B) Infallibility
C) Truthfulness
D) Intelligence

Key Takeaway
Prophets must possess four essential attributes, and their opposites cannot occur in them.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
After learning that prophets must be trustworthy, truthful, conveying, and intelligent, examine your own life: Which of these attributes do you fall short in when representing the message of Islam to others? How will you improve in the next week?
?
The passage teaches that sending prophets is a favor and mercy from God. Have you truly felt gratitude for having a prophet who perfectly embodied these attributes? When was the last time you thanked Allah for sending Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him)?
?
If the opposites of these attributes are impossible for prophets, then we can fully rely on their message. Do you rely on the Quran and Sunnah as if they were from God, or do you sometimes doubt? What is one area where you can increase your trust in the prophetic message?

Beliefs in the Afterlife and Unseen Beings
Enumeration of eschatological beliefs and unseen beings

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will know the list of eschatological realities and unseen beings that are obligatory to believe in according to Sunni creed.

Core Idea
Belief in the afterlife details and unseen beings is an essential part of faith.

The final portion of the poem enumerates the eschatological events and beings that must be believed. These include the accounting, resurrection, punishment, reward, the path, balance, basin, hellfire, gardens, and also belief in jinn, angels, prophets, beautiful maidens, boys, and saints. This forms a comprehensive summary of Sunni eschatology.

Guided Path
1
Read the list of eschatological events
The passage begins with al-ḥisāb (accounting), al-ḥashr (resurrection), al-ʿiqāb (punishment), al-thawāb (reward), al-nashr (spreading), al-ṣirāṭ (path), al-mīzān (balance), al-ḥawḍ (basin), al-nīrān (hellfire), al-jinān (gardens). Note each term.
2
Read the list of unseen beings
Then it mentions al-jinn (jinn), al-amlāk (angels), thumma al-anbiyā' (prophets), al-ḥūr (maidens), al-wildān (boys), thumma al-awliyā' (saints).
3
Understand the obligation
The phrase 'yalzamu al-īmānu' indicates that belief in these is necessary. These are all part of the sam'iyyāt (matters known from revelation).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واجزمَ أَخِي بِرُؤيَةِ الإلهِ * فِي جَنَّةِ الْخُلْدِ بِلا تَنَاهِي إذ الوقوع جائز بالعقلِ * وَقَدْ أَتَى فِيهِ دَلِيلُ النَّقْل وَصِف جَمِيعَ الرُّسُلِ بِالأَمَانَة . وَالصِّدْقِ والتبليغ والفَطَانَة وَيَسْتَحِيلُ ضِدُّها عَليهِم * وجَائِزٌ كَالأَكْلِ فِي حَقِّهِم إرْسَالُهُم تَفَضُّلٌ وَرَحْمَةً * للعالَمِينَ جَل مُولِي النُّعْمَةَ وَيَلْزَمُ الإِيمَانُ بِالْحِسَابِ * والْحَشْرِ والعِقَابِ والثَّوَابِ والنَّشْرِ والصِّراطِ والْمِيزَانِ * والحوض والنَّيْرَانِ والْجِنَانِ والجِنِّ والأَمْلاكِ ثُمَّ الأَنْبِيَا * والْحُورِ والوِلْدَانِ ثُمَّ الأَوْلِيَا

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The scale of your deeds will be set up. Does the lightness or heaviness of that scale concern you right now, in what you did today?
?

?
The poem lists beautiful maidens and boys as rewards. Do you live with the longing for paradise as a real, tangible desire, or has it become a distant abstraction? What would change if you truly yearned for it?

Divine Transcendence
beginner
5 min
The Impossibility of Comprehending God's Essence
Sanusi asserts the impossibility of comprehending God's essence and cites Abu Bakr's maxim.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why human intellect cannot fully grasp the essence of Allah and the meaning of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq's statement 'al-'ajz 'an al-idrak idrak'.

Core Idea
Recognising our inability to comprehend God is itself a form of comprehension.

Sanusi now turns to a foundational truth: the divine essence is beyond human grasp. He argues that created beings are inherently limited and cannot attain knowledge of God's reality. To illustrate this, he recounts a profound exchange with Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, which encapsulates the proper attitude of the believer toward the divine.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read Sanusi's statement and the reported dialogue of Abu Bakr. Notice the logical flow from impossibility to the celebrated saying.
2
Identify the key claim
Find the phrase where Sanusi states that knowledge of God's essence is unattainable. Understand why he says it is impossible.
3
Analyse Abu Bakr's answer
Examine the exchange: what does 'I knew my Lord by my Lord' mean? How does it relate to the impossibility of comprehension?
4
Reflect on the paradox
Consider the statement 'Inability to perceive is perception'. How can inability be a form of perception? What does it teach about the limits of human knowledge?
5
Connect to the larger lesson
Understand how this passage fits into the broader theme of divine transcendence (tanzih).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واعلم أن معرفة حقيقة الذات الإلهية العليّة لا سبيل إلى حصولها لعدم إمكان ذلك، فإن الحادث يقصر بالطبع عن عظيم هذا المقام الذات الإلهية ولذلك لما سئل الصدّيق الأكبر سيدنا أبو بكر رَضَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُ بِمَ عرفت ربك؟ قال: عرفت ربي بربي، ولولا ربي ما عرفت ربي، فقيل له: هل يأتي لبشر أن يدركه؟ فقال: «العجز عن الإدراك إدراك) (۲) . اهـ.
And know that knowledge of the reality of the divine Essence, the Most High, there is no way to attain it because it is impossible. For the contingent is by nature deficient before the greatness of this station of the divine Essence. Therefore, when the great Truthful One, our master Abu Bakr, may Allah be pleased with him, was asked: 'By what did you know your Lord?' He said: 'I knew my Lord by my Lord, and were it not for my Lord, I would not have known my Lord.' So it was said to him: 'Can any human perceive Him?' He said: 'Inability to perceive is perception(2).' End.
Source pages p. 36

Commentary
This passage discusses the human inability to comprehend the essence of God. The commentator states that knowledge of the reality of the divine Essence is unattainable because it is inherently impossible. The reason is that the contingent (al-hadith, i.e., created being) is by nature deficient and cannot grasp the greatness of the divine station. He then cites the famous exchange with Abu Bakr al-Siddiq. When asked how he knew his Lord, Abu Bakr replied that he knew his Lord by his Lord; i.e., God's own self-disclosure is the means of knowledge, not human effort. When asked whether any human can perceive God, he answered, 'Inability to perceive is perception' (al-'ajz 'an al-idrak idrak). This paradoxical statement means that recognizing one's incapacity to fully comprehend God is itself a form of comprehension. It acknowledges the limits of human intellect in the face of the divine.

Key Terms
حقيقة الذات
حادث
عجز عن الإدراك
إدراك

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that knowing God means fully understanding His essence or seeing Him intellectually.
Correction: True knowledge of God is recognizing that His essence is incomprehensible; it is 'al-ajz an al-idrak idrak'.

Checkpoint
According to the saying of Abu Bakr quoted here, how does one know God?
A) Through philosophical reasoning
B) Through God's own self-disclosure
C) Through sense perception
D) Through the consensus of scholars

Key Takeaway
The human intellect cannot grasp the essence of Allah; the highest form of knowledge is recognising one's inability to comprehend Him.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Abu Bakr, the most knowledgeable of creation, said, 'I knew my Lord by my Lord.' When you reflect on your own knowledge of Allah, do you truly acknowledge that any true knowledge you have is solely from Him, or do you secretly take pride in your intellect or studies? What specific act of humility can you perform today to remind yourself that your knowledge is a gift?
?
Sanusi states that the contingent being is by nature deficient before the greatness of the divine station. How does your daily life reflect this reality? Do you live as though you are entirely dependent and deficient, or do you act with a sense of self-sufficiency? What habit can you change to embrace your contingency?
?
The highest form of perception, according to this lesson, is recognizing your inability to perceive Allah. Are you comfortable with that limit, or do you feel frustrated by it? What would change in your relationship with Allah if you fully accepted that you can never fully comprehend Him? Can you thank Him for being beyond your grasp?

5 min
The Impossibility of Knowing Allah's Essence
Sanusi asserts the impossibility of knowing Allah's essence and cites Abu Bakr's saying.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why it is impossible to know the reality of Allah's essence and the significance of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq's statement.

Core Idea
The inability to perceive God is itself perception.

This lesson explores the opening of the passage, where the author asserts that knowledge of Allah's essence is unattainable. He supports this with the profound anecdote of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, who teaches us that true knowledge begins with recognizing our limitations.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation once to get an overall sense of the argument.
Notice the emphatic tone: 'لا سبيل إلى حصولها'.
2
Analyze the claim of impossibility
The author states that knowledge of the Divine Essence is impossible because the contingent cannot grasp the infinite. Reflect on this foundational idea.
This sets the stage for what follows.
3
Examine the anecdote of Abu Bakr
Read the story of how Abu Bakr was asked about knowing Allah. Focus on his two statements: 'I knew my Lord by my Lord' and 'The inability to perceive is perception.'
What does 'by my Lord' mean here?
4
Reflect on the phrase 'العجز عن الإدراك إدراك'
Consider the paradoxical meaning: admitting our inability to comprehend is itself a form of knowledge. This is a key insight.
Compare with the Quran: 'They encompass not of His knowledge save what He wills.'
5
Understand the implication for obligation
The passage concludes that this inability is not what we are obliged to know; the obligation is different, as will be seen in the next lesson.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واعلم أن معرفة حقيقة الذات الإلهية العليّة لا سبيل إلى حصولها لعدم إمكان ذلك، فإن الحادث يقصر بالطبع عن عظيم هذا المقام. ولذلك لما سئل الصدّيق الأكبر سيدنا أبو بكر رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُ: بِمَ عرفت ربك؟ قال: عرفت ربي بربي، ولولا ربي ما عرفت ربي. فقيل له: هل يأتي لبشر أن يدركه؟ فقال: «العجز عن الإدراك إدراك». وهذه المعرفة أول واجب على المكلّف، إذ جميع الواجبات متوقفة عليها، فمعرفة الله هنا عبارة عن معرفة ما يجب في حقه تعالى، وما يستحيل، وما يجوز، وهذا الذي نُكلّف به، أما غيره فلا نُكلّف به.
(٢) وهو ما حثنا عليه النبي صلى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ حين قال: «لا يَزالُ النَّاسُ يتسَاءَلُون حتى يقال: هذا خَلَقَ اللهُ الخَلَقَ فَمَنْ خلق الله ؟ فمن وجد من ذلك شيئًا، فليقل: آمنتُ بالله» رواه مسلم في صحيحه (ح١٣٤) من حديث أبي هريرة رضي اللهُ عَنْهُ.
And know that attaining knowledge of the reality of the Divine Essence, the Most High, is impossible to achieve, because that is not possible. For the contingent is by nature incapable of [comprehending] the greatness of this station. Therefore, when the greatest Ṣiddīq, our master Abū Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him), was asked: 'By what did you know your Lord?' He said: 'I knew my Lord by my Lord, and were it not for my Lord, I would not have known my Lord.' It was said to him: 'Can any human attain to Him?' He said: 'The inability to perceive is [itself] perception.' This knowledge is the first obligation upon the responsible person, for all obligations depend upon it. So knowledge of Allah here means knowing what is necessary for Him, what is impossible for Him, and what is possible for Him. This is what we are obligated to [know]; as for other than that, we are not obligated to know it.
(2) That is what the Prophet (peace be upon him) urged us to do when he said: 'People will continue to ask until it is said: Allah created the creation, so who created Allah? Whoever finds something of that, let him say: I believe in Allah.' Narrated by Muslim in his Ṣaḥīḥ (ḥadīth 134) from Abū Hurayra (may Allah be pleased with him).
Source pages p. 36

Commentary
This passage deals with the nature of knowledge of Allah. The author states that it is impossible to know the reality of the Divine Essence because the contingent human intellect cannot grasp the infinite. He supports this with the famous anecdote of Abu Bakr al-Ṣiddīq, who declared that knowing God is through God Himself, and that the inability to comprehend is itself a form of perception. He then concludes that the obligatory knowledge is limited to knowing what is necessary, impossible, and possible for God, and nothing else. The hadith cited warns against excessive questioning about the nature of God.

Key Terms
معرفة الذات
العجز عن الإدراك

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: To know God, one must comprehend His essence.
Correction: The passage states that knowledge of God's essence is impossible; true knowledge is to recognize our inability and then to know His necessary, impossible, and possible attributes.

Checkpoint
What did Abu Bakr al-Ṣiddīq say when asked how he knew his Lord?
A) I knew Him through reason
B) I knew my Lord by my Lord
C) I knew Him through the prophets
D) I knew Him through nature

Key Takeaway
It is impossible to know Allah's essence; the first step is to acknowledge our inability to fully comprehend Him.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you say 'I believe in Allah', do you secretly wish you could fully comprehend Him? How does Abu Bakr's 'العجز عن الإدراك إدراك' challenge your desire for certainty? What will you do when your intellect fails to grasp His wisdom in your life's trials?
?
If the highest form of knowing Allah is admitting your inability to know Him, are you willing to surrender your pride in your own knowledge and reasoning? In what area of your life today can you practice this humble admission before Allah?
?
Abu Bakr said, 'I knew my Lord by my Lord.' Do you rely more on your own efforts to draw close to Allah, or do you actively seek His grace as the source of your knowledge? What specific step will you take this week to ask Him for ma'rifa?

The First Obligation: Knowing Allah's Attributes
Sanusi defines the first obligation as knowing Allah's necessary, impossible, and possible attributes, supported by a hadith.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the first obligation upon every responsible person is to know Allah's necessary, impossible, and possible attributes.

Core Idea
The first obligation is to know what is necessary, impossible, and possible for Allah.

Having established that Allah's essence is unknowable, the author now defines what we are actually obliged to know: the three categories of divine attributes. This lesson unpacks that definition and the supporting hadith warning against excessive speculation.

Guided Path
1
Read the second part of the passage
Read the Arabic and translation for the section on the first obligation and the hadith.
The text connects back to 'this knowledge' meaning the knowledge just discussed.
2
Identify the three categories
The passage mentions three things: what is necessary (wājib), impossible (mustaḥīl), and possible (jāʾiz) for Allah. Underline these terms.
These are the core of Islamic theology.
3
Understand the limitation
The author stresses that we are only obliged to know these, not anything else (like the essence). This restricts theological inquiry.
4
Examine the hadith
Read the Prophet's saying: when people ask 'who created Allah?', we should say 'I believe in Allah.' This supports stopping at the three categories.
The hadith is from Muslim.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واعلم أن معرفة حقيقة الذات الإلهية العليّة لا سبيل إلى حصولها لعدم إمكان ذلك، فإن الحادث يقصر بالطبع عن عظيم هذا المقام. ولذلك لما سئل الصدّيق الأكبر سيدنا أبو بكر رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُ: بِمَ عرفت ربك؟ قال: عرفت ربي بربي، ولولا ربي ما عرفت ربي. فقيل له: هل يأتي لبشر أن يدركه؟ فقال: «العجز عن الإدراك إدراك». وهذه المعرفة أول واجب على المكلّف، إذ جميع الواجبات متوقفة عليها، فمعرفة الله هنا عبارة عن معرفة ما يجب في حقه تعالى، وما يستحيل، وما يجوز، وهذا الذي نُكلّف به، أما غيره فلا نُكلّف به.
(٢) وهو ما حثنا عليه النبي صلى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ حين قال: «لا يَزالُ النَّاسُ يتسَاءَلُون حتى يقال: هذا خَلَقَ اللهُ الخَلَقَ فَمَنْ خلق الله ؟ فمن وجد من ذلك شيئًا، فليقل: آمنتُ بالله» رواه مسلم في صحيحه (ح١٣٤) من حديث أبي هريرة رضي اللهُ عَنْهُ.

For the contingent is by nature incapable of [comprehending] the greatness of this station. Therefore, when the greatest Ṣiddīq, our master Abū Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him), was asked: 'By what did you know your Lord?' He said: 'I knew my Lord by my Lord, and were it not for my Lord, I would not have known my Lord.' It was said to him: 'Can any human attain to Him?' He said: 'The inability to perceive is [itself] perception.' This knowledge is the first obligation upon the responsible person, for all obligations depend upon it. So knowledge of Allah here means knowing what is necessary for Him, what is impossible for Him, and what is possible for Him. This is what we are obligated to [know]; as for other than that, we are not obligated to know it.
(2) That is what the Prophet (peace be upon him) urged us to do when he said: 'People will continue to ask until it is said: Allah created the creation, so who created Allah? Whoever finds something of that, let him say: I believe in Allah.' Narrated by Muslim in his Ṣaḥīḥ (ḥadīth 134) from Abū Hurayra (may Allah be pleased with him).
Source pages p. 36

Commentary
This passage deals with the nature of knowledge of Allah. The author states that it is impossible to know the reality of the Divine Essence because the contingent human intellect cannot grasp the infinite. He supports this with the famous anecdote of Abu Bakr al-Ṣiddīq, who declared that knowing God is through God Himself, and that the inability to comprehend is itself a form of perception. He then concludes that the obligatory knowledge is limited to knowing what is necessary, impossible, and possible for God, and nothing else. B) Knowledge of His names and actions

Only Allah Has True Influence in Creation
Al-Mashat explains the meaning of the line 'al-ta'thīr laysa illā li-l-wāḥid al-qahhār'.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Allah alone is the true creator of all actions, including human voluntary acts, and that human responsibility does not imply independent creative power.

Core Idea
True creative influence belongs only to Allah; human actions are created by Him yet we are accountable.

In this passage, Al-Mashat unpacks the profound statement that 'influence belongs only to the One, the Subduer.' He clarifies that while we experience choice and are commanded to act, the actual creation of our actions is entirely from Allah. This lesson explores how Islamic theology affirms both divine omnipotence and human accountability without contradiction.

Guided Path
1
Read and understand the passage
Read the Arabic and translation carefully. Note how the author defines 'ta'thīr' as invention and bringing into existence.
The opening 'يجب' is part of the commentary structure.
2
Identify the key claim
The passage states that Allah is the only true effective agent. Human power has no creative influence over anything.
This is the core of the lesson.
3
Apply to voluntary actions
See how the author uses examples of movements, stillness, standing, and sitting to show that all are created by Allah.
Even our deliberate actions are not independently caused by us.
4
Examine the Quranic evidence
Look at the two verses: one commanding action (Tawbah 105) and one attributing Paradise to deeds (Zukhruf 72). Consider how they support the doctrine.
These verses show that actions are attributed to us legally.
5
Reflect on the balance
Understand that Allah creates actions yet we are addressed by the Lawgiver. This harmonizes divine omnipotence with human responsibility.
This is a key Ash'ari theological principle.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
يجب ( فالتأثير) أي: الاختراع والإيجاد للأشياء، أي: فإذا علمت أنه المؤثر الحقيقي هو الله تعالى الوحدانية، فاعلم أن التأثير للأشياء (ليس) أي: لا يصلح لأحد (إلا) لله الواحد القهار وحده (جل وعلا)، فلا تأثير لقدرتنا في شيء من أفعالنا الاختيارية، كالحركات والسكنات، والقيام والقعود، بل ذلك كله مخلوق الله تعالى، ونسبة العمل إلينا ومخاطبتنا من قبل الشارع بتحصيله، كقوله تعالى: ﴿ وَقُلِ اعْمَلُواْ فَسَيَرَى اللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُ ﴾، وقوله تعالى: ﴿ وَتِلْكَ الجَنَّةُ التي أُورِثْتُمُوهَا بِمَا الَّتِي
It is necessary (the influence) i.e., invention and bringing into existence of things, i.e., when you know that the true effective agent is Allah, may He be exalted, the unity, then know that influence over things is not valid for anyone except Allah, the One, the Subduer, alone (majestic and exalted). So there is no influence for our power in any of our voluntary actions, such as movements and stillness, standing and sitting, rather all of that is created by Allah, may He be exalted, and the attribution of the action to us and our addressing by the Lawgiver regarding its acquistion, like His saying: 'And say, "Work, and Allah will see your deeds and [so will] His Messenger"' [Tawbah 105], and His saying: 'And that is Paradise which you are made to inherit for what you (used to) do' [Zukhruf 72].
Source pages p. 50

Commentary
This passage is an explanation of the matn line 'al-ta'thīr laysa illā li-l-wāḥid al-qahhār'. The author first defines 'al-ta'thīr' as invention and bringing into existence. He then states that once you know that Allah is the true effective agent (al-mu'aththir al-ḥaqīqī), you must understand that influence over all things belongs exclusively to Allah, the One, the Subduer. He then applies this to human voluntary actions: our power (qudra) has no real creative influence; our movements, stillness, etc., are all created by Allah. However, actions are still attributed to us legally, and we are commanded by the Lawgiver to perform them and are accountable. He supports this with two Qur'anic verses: one commanding action and stating that Allah and His Messenger will see the deed (Tawbah 105), and another stating that Paradise is inherited by what one used to do (Zukhruf 72).

Key Terms
التأثير
الاختراع
الإيجاد
المؤثر الحقيقي
الفعل الاختياري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That human free will implies independent creative power; humans create their own actions.
Correction: In Ash'ari theology, Allah creates all actions, including human voluntary acts. Humans acquire (kasb) their actions and are judged for their choices, but the creative power belongs solely to Allah.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what is the status of human voluntary actions?
A) They are created by human power independently
B) They are created by Allah but attributed to humans legally
C) They are illusions
D) They are partly created by humans and partly by Allah

Key Takeaway
True creative influence belongs only to Allah; human actions are created by Him, yet we are commanded and held accountable for them.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sin, do you subconsciously think it was solely your doing, forgetting that Allah is the creator of that action? How does the truth that Allah creates your actions both relieve and terrify you?
?
If true influence belongs only to Allah, why do you still act as if your own power can secure outcomes without His will? In what area of your life today do you need to stop depending on your own influence and rely on the One who is al-Qahhar?
?
You are commanded to 'work' (Qur'an 9:105), yet your actions are created by Allah. Does this make you more or less diligent in obeying? How does this teaching remove excuses for laziness or despair?

Divine Transcendence
intermediate
5 min
True Agency Belongs to Allah Alone
Mashaat defines tathir as exclusive to Allah and denies causal power to creatures.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that only Allah is the true agent (مؤثر) and that human actions and natural phenomena are created by Him, with human actions attributed only as kasb (acquisition).

Core Idea
True effect belongs solely to Allah; everything else is only a means.

This passage establishes the central kalam doctrine that Allah alone is the creator and agent of all existence. Before discussing how beings relate to actions, we must affirm that no creature has any real causal power. Mashaat defines the key term tathir and clarifies the role of human acquisition.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition of tathir
Identify how Mashaat defines تَأْثِير (effect) as اختراع (invention) and إيجاد (bringing into existence). Note that it is exclusively for Allah.
The word التأثير is the key concept.
2
Understand negation of effect for creatures
See that human voluntary actions (movements, stillness, standing, sitting) are not truly effected by us. They are created by Allah. Our actions are attributed to us as earning (كسب), not as creation.
The Quranic verses are cited to support this distinction.
3
Apply the principle to natural causes
Recognize that fire, water, stars, etc., have no real causal power—not by nature, nor by cause, nor by any power deposited in them. All effect is from Allah alone.
The miracle of Ibrahim's fire shows Allah's direct control.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والفِعْلِ، فَالتَّأثِيرُ لَيْسَ إِلَّا لِلوَاحِدِ القَهَّارِ جَلَّ وعَلا.
يجب
(فالتأثير) أي: الاختراع والإيجاد للأشياء، أي: فإذا علمت أنه المؤثر الحقيقي هو الله تعالى الوحدانية، فاعلم أن التأثير للأشياء (ليس) أي: لا يصلح لأحد (إلا) لله الواحد القهار وحده (جل وعلا)، فلا تأثير لقدرتنا في شيء من أفعالنا الاختيارية، كالحركات والسكنات، والقيام والقعود، بل ذلك كله مخلوق الله تعالى، ونسبة العمل إلينا ومخاطبتنا من قبل الشارع بتحصيله، كقوله تعالى: ﴿ وَقُلِ اعْمَلُواْ فَسَيَرَى اللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُ ﴾، وقوله تعالى: ﴿ وَتِلْكَ الجَنَّةُ التي أُورِثْتُمُوهَا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ ﴾، فذلك من حيث الكسب والاكتساب، لا من حيث الإيجاد والاختراع. فلا تأثير للأمور العادية، فالنار لا تحرق بطبعها، وكذلك الطعام في الشبع، والماء في الرّي وفي إنبات الزرع، والكواكب في إنضاج الفواكه وغيرها، وكذا السكين في القطع، كل ذلك لا تأثير له لا بالطبع، ولا بالعلة، ولا بقوة أودعها الله فيها، بل التأثير في ذلك كله لله تعالى وحده، بمحض اختياره عند وجود هذه الأشياء، ﴿ قُلْنَا يَنَارُ كُونِي بَرْدًا وَسَلَامًا ﴾.
(ومن يقل) من أهل الضلال (بالطبع) أي: بتأثير الطبيعة، بأن يقول ويعتقد أن الأشياء المذكورة تُؤثر بطبعها، (أو) يقل (بالعلة) أي: أو بعلة فيها بأنها في وجود شيء من غير أن يكون له تعالى فيه اختيار، فذاك) أي: القول (كفر) ، ويلزم كفر قائله، لأنه أثبت له تعالى شريكًا، (عند أهل الملة) أي ملة الإسلام.
قال الراغب: «الملة: اسم لما شرعه الله تعالى لعباده على لسان أنبيائه، ليتوصلوا به إلى جواره والفرق بينها وبين الدين أن الملة لا تضاف إلا للنبي الذي تستند إليه، ولا تكاد توجد مضافة إلى الله تعالى، ولا لآحاد الأمة، ولا تستعمل إلا في جملة الشرائع دون آحادها».
And the action? Thus effect is only for the One, the Subduer, majestic and exalted.
It is necessary [to say]: (Effect) i.e., invention and bringing into existence of things. That is, if you know that the real agent is Allah, [you know] His oneness, then know that effect upon things is not suitable for anyone except Allah, the One, the Subduer, alone (majestic and exalted). So there is no effect for our power in any of our voluntary actions, such as movements and stillnesses, standing and sitting; rather, all of that is created by Allah Almighty. And the attribution of action to us and our being addressed by the Lawgiver to acquire it—such as His statement: "And say, 'Work, and Allah will see your work, and His Messenger'" and His statement: "And that Paradise which you have inherited because of what you used to do"—that is from the perspective of earning and acquisition, not from the perspective of creating and inventing. So there is no effect for ordinary things: fire does not burn by its nature, nor food in satiating, nor water in quenching thirst and causing plants to grow, nor the stars in ripening fruits and other things, nor the knife in cutting. All of that has no effect—not by nature, nor by cause, nor by a power deposited in them by Allah. Rather, effect in all of that is for Allah Almighty alone, by His sheer choice upon the existence of these things. "We said, 'O fire, be coolness and peace.'"
(And whoever says) from the people of misguidance (by nature) i.e., attributing effect to nature, by saying and believing that the mentioned things effect by their nature, (or) says (by cause) i.e., or by a cause in them, that they bring about the existence of something without Allah having any choice in it—then that (i.e., the statement) is (disbelief), and the disbelief of its speaker is entailed, because he has affirmed a partner for Allah Almighty, (according to the people of the milla) i.e., the religion of Islam.
Al-Raghib said: 'Milla is a name for what Allah Almighty has legislated for His servants upon the tongues of His prophets, so that they may reach nearness to Him. The difference between it and religion (din) is that milla is only attributed to the prophet upon whom it is based, and it is hardly ever attributed to Allah Almighty, nor to individual members of the community, and it is only used for the entirety of laws, not for individual ones.'
Source pages p. 50

Commentary
This passage is a combination of definition and proof followed by a clear ruling. The author first defines tathīr (effect/agency) as ‘invention and bringing into existence’. He asserts that true effect belongs exclusively to Allah, the One and Subduer. This means that created beings have no real causal power—not in human voluntary actions (like movements, standing, sitting) nor in natural phenomena (like fire burning, water quenching, the sun ripening fruit). Human actions are attributed to us only in the sense of kasb (earning/acquisition), not as creation or invention. The author cites Quranic verses to support this: people are commanded to work, and Paradise is inherited by what they do—but this is acquisition, not origination. He then explicitly refutes those who claim that things act by their nature (tab‘) or by an inherent cause (‘illa). Such a claim elevates creatures to partners with Allah in effect, which constitutes kufr (disbelief) according to the milla of Islam. The passage concludes with a lexical clarification from al-Rāghib al-Iṣfahānī distinguishing milla (a specific religious law revealed through a prophet) from dīn (religion in a broader sense). This reinforces that the standard of judgment here is the Islamic sharia, which holds Allah alone as the ultimate agent.

Key Terms
التأثير
الاختراع
الإيجاد
الكسب
الطبع
العلة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Fire naturally burns and water naturally quenches, independently of Allah.
Correction: Nothing has effect by nature or cause; all effect is from Allah alone, as He said to the fire 'be coolness and peace'.

Checkpoint
Why does the author mention the Quranic verses about working and inheriting Paradise?
A) To prove that humans create their own actions.
B) To show that actions are attributed to us as kasb, not as creation.
C) To demonstrate that natural causes are real.
D) To explain the meaning of milla.

Key Takeaway
Allah alone is the true agent; creatures have no independent causal power.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you experience a success, do you truly attribute it solely to Allah, or do you feel a sense of personal credit as if your effort created the outcome? How would your gratitude change if you lived this lesson moment by moment?
?
Is there a fear in your life—of illness, poverty, or people's harm—that reveals you are trusting in the ‘natural effect’ of these things rather than in the direct control of Allah? Will you consciously surrender that fear today?
?
The passage strips even fire of its burning power. What is one habit or attachment you have that assumes the world works by itself, and how will you replace that assumption with living awareness of Allah's immediate action?

Divine Transcendence
intermediate
4 min
Refutation of Natural Causation and the Ruling of Disbelief
Mashaat refutes attributing effect to nature or cause, rules it as kufr, and defines milla.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why attributing causal power to nature or inherent causes is considered disbelief in Islam, and you will learn the distinction between milla and din.

Core Idea
Claiming that things act by nature or cause is shirk because it partners creatures with Allah in effect.

Having established that only Allah has true effect, Mashaat now confronts those who ascribe effect to nature (طبع) or to inherent causes (علة). He rules that such claims are kufr (disbelief) according to the Islamic milla. The lesson also defines milla, showing it refers to a prophet's law.

Guided Path
1
Identify the two false doctrines
Read the two groups: those who say things act by nature (طبع) and those who say by cause (علة). Note that both imply the thing brings about existence without Allah's choice.
This includes naturalists and philosophers.
2
Understand the ruling of kufr
The author declares such statements are kufr because they affirm a partner for Allah in effect. The ruling is according to the people of milla (Islam).
This shows the gravity of denying Allah's sole agency.
3
Learn the definition of milla
Read al-Raghib's distinction: milla is a law brought by a prophet; it is not attributed to Allah directly, nor to individuals, and refers to the whole set of laws.
This clarifies that the standard is the Prophet's legislation.

Source Text

يجب
(فالتأثير) أي: الاختراع والإيجاد للأشياء، أي: فإذا علمت أنه المؤثر الحقيقي هو الله تعالى الوحدانية، فاعلم أن التأثير للأشياء (ليس) أي: لا يصلح لأحد (إلا) لله الواحد القهار وحده (جل وعلا)، فلا تأثير لقدرتنا في شيء من أفعالنا الاختيارية، كالحركات والسكنات، والقيام والقعود، بل ذلك كله مخلوق الله تعالى، ونسبة العمل إلينا ومخاطبتنا من قبل الشارع بتحصيله، كقوله تعالى: ﴿ وَقُلِ اعْمَلُواْ فَسَيَرَى اللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُ ﴾، وقوله تعالى: ﴿ وَتِلْكَ الجَنَّةُ التي أُورِثْتُمُوهَا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ ﴾، فذلك من حيث الكسب والاكتساب، لا من حيث الإيجاد والاختراع. فلا تأثير للأمور العادية، فالنار لا تحرق بطبعها، وكذلك الطعام في الشبع، والماء في الرّي وفي إنبات الزرع، والكواكب في إنضاج الفواكه وغيرها، وكذا السكين في القطع، كل ذلك لا تأثير له لا بالطبع، ولا بالعلة، ولا بقوة أودعها الله فيها، بل التأثير في ذلك كله لله تعالى وحده، بمحض اختياره عند وجود هذه الأشياء، ﴿ قُلْنَا يَنَارُ كُونِي بَرْدًا وَسَلَامًا ﴾.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When something good or bad happens in your life, do you ever silently attribute it to 'luck', 'nature', or a 'cause' without consciously thanking or attributing it to Allah? Which specific event this week can you rephrase right now to acknowledge Allah's direct action?
?
What casual phrases do you use (e.g., 'the medicine cured me', 'hard work paid off') that might implicitly assign independent causal power to created things? Will you commit to catching yourself and replacing such phrases with ones affirming Allah's sole causality?
?
If you truly believed that every effect—every sneeze, every sunrise, every success—comes directly from Allah without any natural cause, how would your du'aa, your gratitude, and your reliance on means change? What is one practical change you can make starting today?

Divine Transcendence
beginner
4 min
God's Transcendence from Indwelling and Direction
Commentary defines God's transcendence from indwelling and direction with rational proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Allah is transcendent from indwelling (ḥulūl) and direction (jiha), and the rational proof for His non-material nature.

Core Idea
God is free from indwelling and direction because He is non-material.

This passage from the commentary explains two essential aspects of God's transcendence. The author first asserts that Allah is purified from indwelling (ḥulūl) and then from direction (jiha), providing a rational argument that direction is a property of matter, and since God is non-material, He cannot be described with it.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic verse and translation
Read the Arabic passage once carefully, then read the English translation. Notice that the verse line states the doctrine, and the commentary expands on it.
The verse line includes additional terms not fully explained in this excerpt.
2
Understand the concept of ḥulūl
The commentator defines transcendence as purification from ḥulūl (indwelling) and saryān (permeation) in things. Reflect on why that is impossible for the Eternal Being.
Ḥulūl is a belief found in some religions that God dwells within creation.
3
Examine the proof that God has no direction
The commentator argues that direction (jiha) is a property of matter. Since Allah is non-material, He cannot be described with any direction. Consider the analogy of a stone lacking knowledge because it lacks capacity.
4
Address a common misunderstanding
The author refutes the idea that absence of direction implies non-existence. Understand that this logic only applies to contingent things, not to the Necessary Being.
This is a crucial point for tawḥīd.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مُنَزَّهُ عن الْحُلُولِ والجِهَة * والاتصالِ الانْفِصَالِ والسَّفَة
(مُنَزِّه) أي: هو مطهر عن الحلول والسريان في الأشياء، لأنه بعض التنزيهات القديم بالذات كما تقدّم. (و) منزه عن (الجهة) لشيء، لأن الجهة من خواص المادة وأحكامها والله منزه عن المادة وعلائقها، فيكون الحق عَزَّوَجَلَّ غير مادي، لا يجوز أن يتصف بجهة من الجهات، ولا بشيء من المتقابلات، فإن القابل هو المادة، إلا ترى أن الإنسان لابد له أن يكون عالما، أو جاهلًا، ولا يصح ارتفاعهما، لكن الحجر لا يقال له: عالم وجاهل لعدم القابلية، فافهم. واعلم أنه لا يلزم من عدم الجهة العدم، لأن ذلك في الحوادث،
He is transcendent from indwelling and direction, and from connection, disconnection, and foolishness. (Transcendent) meaning: He is purified from indwelling and permeating into things, because that is part of the transcendences of the Eternal in Essence, as has preceded. And He is transcendent from direction (jiha) for a thing, because direction is among the properties of matter and its rulings, and Allah is transcendent from matter and its attachments. So the Truth, Mighty and Majestic, is non-material; it is not permissible that He be described with any direction, nor with any of the opposites, for the recipient is matter. Do you not see that a man must be either knowing or ignorant, and their simultaneous absence is not valid, but a stone is not called knowing or ignorant due to the lack of receptivity? So understand. And know that the absence of direction does not entail non-existence, because that is in the case of contingent things.
Source pages p. 56

Commentary
This passage is a definition and proof of God's transcendence (tanzih) from two particular attributes: indwelling (ḥulūl) and direction (jiha). The verse line states the doctrine, and the commentary explains. First, 'transcendent' means purified from ḥulūl (incarnation or indwelling) and permeation (saryān) in things, because that is impossible for the Eternal. Second, direction (jiha) is explained as a property of matter (mādda); since God is non-material (ghayr māddī), He cannot be described with direction. The author provides a rational analogy: a human must be either knowing or ignorant—these opposites cannot both be absent—but a stone is neither because it lacks capacity. Similarly, God is not subject to direction because He lacks material receptivity. A common objection is that without direction, God would be non-existent. The author refutes this: absence of direction does not imply non-existence, because the relationship of direction to existence applies only to contingent (created) things.

Key Terms
تنزيه
حلول
جهة
مادة
قديم بالذات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: If God has no direction, He is nothing or non-existent.
Correction: The passage clarifies that direction is only a property of matter; its absence does not imply non-existence for the non-material Eternal.

Checkpoint
Why does the commentator argue that God cannot be described with direction (jiha)?
A) Because direction is a property of the human body
B) Because direction is a property of matter, and God is non-material
C) Because direction implies limitation and God is infinite
D) Because direction is a concept that only applies to created beings

Key Takeaway
Allah is free from indwelling and direction because these are properties of matter, and He is non-material.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you pray, do you find yourself unconsciously imagining God in a specific direction—like above the sky? How does this lesson challenge that ingrained habit? What will you do to train your heart to worship a God who is beyond all direction?
?
The analogy shows that a stone is not called knowing or ignorant because it lacks receptivity. Do you ever limit God by comparing Him to human attributes, like thinking He 'feels' anger like we do? Which of God's attributes do you need to purify from human-like limitations?
?
The common misunderstanding is that without direction, God is nothing. Have you ever felt distant from God because you couldn't 'locate' Him? How can the truth of His transcendence actually bring you closer, knowing He is not confined? What step will you take today to deepen that conviction?

p. 56
Module 10 · Lesson 14
Next →
Divine Transcendence
beginner
5 min
Transcendence from Indwelling and Direction
Sanusi negates indwelling and direction from Allah using rational arguments.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Allah must be transcendent from indwelling (hulul) and direction (jihah), based on His eternity and non-materiality.

Core Idea
Direction is a property of matter; Allah is not matter, so He has no direction.

Sanusi begins by negating two fundamental physical attributes from Allah: indwelling (hulul) and direction (jihah). These are among the earliest topics in kalam because they directly oppose anthropomorphic conceptions of God. The passage uses a simple analogy to show that just as a stone cannot be described as knowing or ignorant, so too Allah cannot be described with direction because He is beyond matter and its properties.

Guided Path
1
Read the negation of hulul and jihah
Read the first two lines of the Arabic passage. Notice how the author immediately links these negations to Allah's eternity (qadim) and non-materiality.
2
Understand the analogy of the stone
The author compares the impossibility of attributing direction to Allah to the impossibility of attributing knowledge to a stone. Reflect on why this analogy works.
The stone lacks receptivity (qabiliyyah) for knowledge; Allah lacks receptivity for direction.
3
Clarify the misconception about non-existence
The author explicitly states that denying direction does not mean denying existence. This addresses a common fear that without spatial location, Allah would be nothing.
This clarification is crucial for beginners.
4
Affirm the transcendence from spatial relations
The passage concludes by negating all six spatial directions relative to Allah. Notice that this is not just a negation but a positive affirmation of His transcendence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مُنَزَّه عن الْحُلُولِ والسريان في الأشياء، لأنه بعض التنزيهات القديم بالذات كما تقدّم.
(و) منزه عن (الجهة) لشيء، لأن الجهة من خواص المادة ،وأحكامها والله منزه عن المادة ،وعلائقها، فيكون الحق عَزَّوَجَلَّ غير مادي، لا يجوز أن يتصف بجهة من الجهات، ولا بشيء من المتقابلات، فإن القابل هو المادة، إلا ترى أن الإنسان لابد له أن يكون عالما، أو جاهلًا، ولا يصح ارتفاعهما، لكن الحجر لا يقال له: عالم وجاهل لعدم القابلية، فافهم.
واعلم أنه لا يلزم من عدم الجهة العدم، لأن ذلك في الحوادث، وفي أحكام الماديات ومشروط بالقابلية فلا يقال: إنه فوق الجرم، ولا تحته ولا يمينه ولا شماله، ولا خلفه، ولا أمامه.
(و) مُنَزِّه عن الاتصال)، و(الانفصال)، فلا يقال: إنه متصل بالعالم اتصال الحوادث، ولا منفصل، لأن هذه الأمور من الحادث، والله سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَى قديم ليس بحادث. (و) منزه أيضًا (عن السَّفه وهو وضع الشيء في غير محله، كيف وهو المدبر الحكيم العليم الذي وضع كل شيء في موضعه، والذي أحسن وأعطى كل شيء خلقه ثم هدى. واعلم أن العالم: وهو ما سوى الله تعالى، وإن عَظُم في نفسه فهو في جانب باهر قدرته لا شيء، فكيف يكون العلي الكبير، الغني القدير، حالا، ومتصلا ، أو منفصلا في شيء حقير فقير، هو في نفسه عدم. أما ما جاء في النصوص الثابتة من الآيات القرآنية، والأحاديث الموهمة للتشبيه النبوية مما يوهم شيئًا فهو من المتشابه الذي يجب علينا الإيمان به، مع تفويض معناه وحقيقته إلى الله تعالى: ﴿ فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ زَيْغٌ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ مَا نَشَبَهُ مِنْهُ ابْتِغَاءَ الْفِتْنَةِ وَابْتِغَاءَ تَأْوِيلِهِ، وَمَا يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُ إِلَّا الله وَالرَّسِحُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ يَقُولُونَ عَامَنَّا بِهِ كُلٌّ مِّنْ عِندِ رَبِّنَا وَمَا يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّا أُولُوا الألباب (۱) ، جعلنا الله من أولي الألباب بمنه وكرمه آمين.
Transcendent from indwelling and permeation in things, because it is one of the transcendences of the Eternal in essence, as previously stated. And transcendent from direction to a thing, because direction is a property of matter and its rulings, and Allah is transcendent from matter and its attachments. So the Truth, may He be exalted, is non-material, not permissible to be described with any direction, nor with any of the opposites, for the recipient is matter. Do you not see that a human necessarily is either knowing or ignorant, and their removal is not valid, but a stone is not said to be knowing or ignorant due to lack of receptivity, so understand. And know that the absence of direction does not entail non-existence, because that applies to contingent things and to the rulings of material things and is conditioned by receptivity. So it is not said: He is above the body, nor below it, nor to its right, nor to its left, nor behind it, nor in front of it. And transcendent from connection and disconnection, so it is not said: He is connected to the world as contingent things are connected, nor disconnected, because these matters are of the contingent, and Allah, glorified and exalted, is eternal, not contingent. And also transcendent from foolishness, which is placing a thing in other than its proper place. How could that be, while He is the Wise, Knowing Manager who placed everything in its proper place, and who perfected and gave each thing its creation, then guided. And know that the world, which is everything besides Allah, even if great in itself, is nothing alongside His overwhelming power. So how could the Most High, the Great, the Self-Sufficient, the Powerful be indwelling, connected, or disconnected in a contemptible, poor thing that is in itself non-existence? As for what has come in the established texts from Quranic verses and Prophetic hadiths that give an impression of anthropomorphism, those are of the ambiguous (mutashabih) which we must believe in, while entrusting its meaning and reality to Allah: 'As for those in whose hearts is deviation, they follow what is similar in it, seeking discord and seeking its interpretation. And none knows its interpretation except Allah. And those firmly grounded in knowledge say, "We believe in it; all is from our Lord." And none will remember except those of understanding.' (Quran 3:7) May Allah make us among those of understanding, by His grace and generosity, Amen.
Source pages p. 56

Commentary
This passage is a detailed proof of Allah's transcendence from physical and spatial attributes. The author begins with the poem's line 'Muzannahu 'an al-hulul...' and then explains each term. First, he negates 'hulul' (indwelling) and 'saryan' (permeation) in things, because these are attributes of contingent beings, and Allah is ancient in essence. Second, he negates 'jihah' (direction) by arguing that direction is a property of matter; Allah is non-material, so cannot have direction. He illustrates with the analogy of a stone that is not described as knowing or ignorant because it lacks receptivity. He then clarifies that negating direction does not imply non-existence, as that applies only to contingent things conditioned by receptivity. Third, he negates 'ittisal' (connection) and 'infisal' (disconnection) because these are contingent attributes; Allah is eternal, not contingent. Fourth, he negates 'safah' (foolishness) by affirming Allah's perfect wisdom in placing everything correctly. He then makes a concluding argument: the entire world, despite its size, is nothing before Allah's power; thus it is absurd to think Allah could be dwelling or connected in something so insignificant. Finally, he addresses ambiguous texts that seem to imply anthropomorphism: these are mutashabihat (ambiguous verses/hadiths) which must be believed in without attempting to interpret their literal meaning, following the approach of the righteous predecessors (salaf). He cites Quran 3:7, praising those who say 'We believe in it; all is from our Lord.' He ends with a prayer to be among those of understanding.

Key Terms
حلول
جهة
قابلية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that denying Allah a direction means denying His existence.
Correction: The author explains that direction is a property of matter, and Allah is non-material. Denying direction does not deny existence, as that would only apply to contingent things conditioned by receptivity.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, why can Allah not have direction (jihah)?
A) Because direction would limit His power
B) Because direction is a property of matter, and Allah is non-material
C) Because the Quran says He is above the Throne
D) Because direction implies movement

Key Takeaway
Allah's transcendence from direction is a consequence of His non-materiality and eternity, not a denial of His existence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you make sujood, do you ever imagine that you are prostrating towards a being who is 'up' above the sky? How does this lesson challenge that ingrained tendency? What will you actively do from today to train your heart to worship Allah without assigning any direction?
?
The analogy of the stone is striking: a stone exists but cannot be called 'knowing' because it lacks the capacity for knowledge. Reflect: Are there any attributes you attribute to Allah that actually stem from your own limited human experience? Are you willing to let go of those mental images, even if they feel comforting?
?
If the absence of direction does not mean non-existence, then your relationship with Allah does not depend on spatial proximity. How does that change the way you seek closeness to Him? What concrete steps will you take to seek nearness through obedience and remembrance, rather than through physical orientation?

Module 10 · Lesson 15

Divine Transcendence
beginner
4 min
Transcendence from Connection, Disconnection, and Foolishness
Sanusi negates connection, disconnection, and foolishness, and argues from the insignificance of the world.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Allah is beyond connection/disconnection and foolishness, and how the world's insignificance supports this.

Core Idea
Allah is not connected or disconnected like contingent beings, and He is all-wise.

After negating spatial attributes, the author moves to relational attributes: connection (ittisal) and disconnection (infisal). These are also properties of contingent beings. He then negates foolishness (safah) by affirming Allah's perfect wisdom. The lesson culminates in a powerful argument: the entire world is nothing before Allah's power, so how could He dwell in it?

Guided Path
1
Read the negation of connection and disconnection
Read the part about ittisal and infisal. Notice the reasoning: these are attributes of contingent things, and Allah is eternal.
2
Understand the negation of foolishness
Foolishness is defined as placing something in the wrong place. The author argues that Allah's wisdom prevents this.
This is a positive attribute of wisdom behind the negation.
3
Reflect on the argument from the world's insignificance
The author says the entire universe is nothing compared to Allah's power. Then he poses a rhetorical question: how could Allah be indwelling or connected in something so insignificant?
This is a reductio ad absurdum argument.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مُنَزَّه عن الْحُلُولِ والسريان في الأشياء، لأنه بعض التنزيهات القديم بالذات كما تقدّم.
(و) منزه عن (الجهة) لشيء، لأن الجهة من خواص المادة ،وأحكامها والله منزه عن المادة ،وعلائقها، فيكون الحق عَزَّوَجَلَّ غير مادي، لا يجوز أن يتصف بجهة من الجهات، ولا بشيء من المتقابلات، فإن القابل هو المادة، إلا ترى أن الإنسان لابد له أن يكون عالما، أو جاهلًا، ولا يصح ارتفاعهما، لكن الحجر لا يقال له: عالم وجاهل لعدم القابلية، فافهم.
واعلم أنه لا يلزم من عدم الجهة العدم، لأن ذلك في الحوادث، وفي أحكام الماديات ومشروط بالقابلية فلا يقال: إنه فوق الجرم، ولا تحته ولا يمينه ولا شماله، ولا خلفه، ولا أمامه.
(و) مُنَزِّه عن الاتصال)، و(الانفصال)، فلا يقال: إنه متصل بالعالم اتصال الحوادث، ولا منفصل، لأن هذه الأمور من الحادث، والله سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَى قديم ليس بحادث. (و) منزه أيضًا (عن السَّفه وهو وضع الشيء في غير محله، كيف وهو المدبر الحكيم العليم الذي وضع كل شيء في موضعه، والذي أحسن وأعطى كل شيء خلقه ثم هدى. واعلم أن العالم: وهو ما سوى الله تعالى، وإن عَظُم في نفسه فهو في جانب باهر قدرته لا شيء، فكيف يكون العلي الكبير، الغني القدير، حالا، ومتصلا ، أو منفصلا في شيء حقير فقير، هو في نفسه عدم. أما ما جاء في النصوص الثابتة من الآيات القرآنية، والأحاديث الموهمة للتشبيه النبوية مما يوهم شيئًا فهو من المتشابه الذي يجب علينا الإيمان به، مع تفويض معناه وحقيقته إلى الله تعالى: ﴿ فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ زَيْغٌ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ مَا نَشَبَهُ مِنْهُ ابْتِغَاءَ الْفِتْنَةِ وَابْتِغَاءَ تَأْوِيلِهِ، وَمَا يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُ إِلَّا الله وَالرَّسِحُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ يَقُولُونَ عَامَنَّا بِهِ كُلٌّ مِّنْ عِندِ رَبِّنَا وَمَا يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّا أُولُوا الألباب (۱) ، جعلنا الله من أولي الألباب بمنه وكرمه آمين.
Transcendent from indwelling and permeation in things, because it is one of the transcendences of the Eternal in essence, as previously stated. And transcendent from direction to a thing, because direction is a property of matter and its rulings, and Allah is transcendent from matter and its attachments. So the Truth, may He be exalted, is non-material, not permissible to be described with any direction, nor with any of the opposites, for the recipient is matter. So it is not said: He is above the body, nor below it, nor to its right, nor to its left, nor behind it, nor in front of it. And transcendent from connection and disconnection, so it is not said: He is connected to the world as contingent things are connected, nor disconnected, because these matters are of the contingent, and Allah, glorified and exalted, is eternal, not contingent. And also transcendent from foolishness, which is placing a thing in other than its proper place. How could that be, while He is the Wise, Knowing Manager who placed everything in its proper place, and who perfected and gave each thing its creation, then guided. And know that the world, which is everything besides Allah, even if great in itself, is nothing alongside His overwhelming power. So how could the Most High, the Great, the Self-Sufficient, the Powerful be indwelling, connected, or disconnected in a contemptible, poor thing that is in itself non-existence? Checkpoint
Why does the author negate connection (ittisal) from Allah?
A) Because connection implies physical contact
B) Because connection is a property of contingent things, and Allah is eternal
C) Because the Quran says He is separate
D) Because connection would limit His power

Key Takeaway
Allah's eternity and the world's contingent nature make connection, disconnection, and foolishness impossible for Him.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You just read that the entire universe is 'nothing' compared to Allah's power. Yet how often do you let the size of your problems or the allure of worldly things distract you from His majesty? What would it look like for you to truly internalize the insignificance of the world? Write a concrete commitment to remind yourself of this truth daily for one week.
?
The passage says Allah is free from foolishness—He never places things in the wrong place. Think of a situation in your life where you felt that something was misplaced, unfair, or without wisdom. How does knowing that Allah's placement is always perfect challenge your feelings? What would it mean for you to surrender to His wisdom in that specific situation today?
?
Sanusi argues that it is absurd for the Most High to be 'connected' or 'disconnected' in something contemptible. Yet we sometimes imagine Allah as close to us in a physical or spatial sense. How does this lesson reshape your understanding of closeness to Allah? What non-physical ways can you cultivate closeness to Him that honor His transcendence? Name one practice you will adopt starting today.

Module 10 · Lesson 16

Divine Transcendence
beginner
3 min
Approach to Ambiguous Texts (Mutashabihat)
Al-Mashat explains the Sunni approach to ambiguous texts: belief with tafwid.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that ambiguous Quranic verses and hadiths about Allah's attributes must be believed in without literal interpretation, entrusting their meaning to Allah (tafwid).

Core Idea
We affirm what Allah said about Himself, but we do not interpret it literally.

After establishing that Allah is transcendent from physical and spatial attributes, the author addresses a potential problem: some Quranic verses and hadiths seem to imply anthropomorphism (e.g., Allah's hand, face). These texts are mutashabihat (ambiguous). The correct approach, taught by the Salaf, is to believe in them as true, but to entrust their exact meaning to Allah, avoiding interpretation that would contradict His transcendence.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on mutashabihat
Read the last part of the source that deals with ambiguous texts. Note the author's emphasis on belief and tafwid.
2
Understand the Quranic proof
The author cites Quran 3:7, which speaks about those with deviation following ambiguous verses, and that only Allah knows their true interpretation.
Note the verse includes a pause; the Salaf take the position that 'those firmly grounded in knowledge' stop at 'except Allah'.
3
Apply the lesson to your attitude
Reflect on how you should approach such verses: affirm them without delving into how, trusting Allah's wisdom.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مُنَزَّه عن الْحُلُولِ والسريان في الأشياء، لأنه بعض التنزيهات القديم بالذات كما تقدّم.
(و) منزه عن (الجهة) لشيء، لأن الجهة من خواص المادة ،وأحكامها والله منزه عن المادة ،وعلائقها، فيكون الحق عَزَّوَجَلَّ غير مادي، لا يجوز أن يتصف بجهة من الجهات، ولا بشيء من المتقابلات، فإن القابل هو المادة، إلا ترى أن الإنسان لابد له أن يكون عالما، أو جاهلًا، ولا يصح ارتفاعهما، لكن الحجر لا يقال له: عالم وجاهل لعدم القابلية، فافهم.

(و) مُنَزِّه عن الاتصال)، و(الانفصال)، فلا يقال: إنه متصل بالعالم اتصال الحوادث، ولا منفصل، لأن هذه الأمور من الحادث، والله سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَى قديم ليس بحادث. Third, he negates 'ittisal' (connection) and 'infisal' (disconnection) because these are contingent attributes; Allah is eternal, not contingent. Fourth, he negates 'safah' (foolishness) by affirming Allah's perfect wisdom in placing everything correctly. He then makes a concluding argument: the entire world, despite its size, is nothing before Allah's power; thus it is absurd to think Allah could be dwelling or connected in something so insignificant. Finally, he addresses ambiguous texts that seem to imply anthropomorphism: these are mutashabihat (ambiguous verses/hadiths) which must be believed in without attempting to interpret their literal meaning, following the approach of the righteous predecessors (salaf). He cites Quran 3:7, praising those who say 'We believe in it; all is from our Lord.' He ends with a prayer to be among those of understanding.

Key Terms
متشابه
تفويض
تأويل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that ambiguous verses must be interpreted metaphorically to be understood.
Correction: The author teaches that we do not interpret them at all; we delegate knowledge of their meaning to Allah.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what should we do with ambiguous verses (mutashabihat)?
A) Interpret them metaphorically
B) Ignore them
C) Believe in them without interpreting their literal meaning
D) Change their wording to avoid confusion

Key Takeaway
Ambiguous scriptural texts are to be affirmed as true, with their real meaning left to Allah alone.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read a verse like 'the Most Merciful rose above the Throne,' do you feel pressure to visualize how it happens? Are you willing to say 'I believe it, but the true meaning is with Allah' even if it leaves you without a mental picture?
?
Do you ever find yourself avoiding ambiguous verses because they make you uncomfortable? Will you commit to affirming them with tafwid instead of ignoring them?
?
If a friend asks you what a particular anthropomorphic verse really means, can you lovingly explain that you believe it as true but entrust its meaning to Allah—without feeling embarrassed by not having a 'better' answer?

Divine Transcendence
beginner
6 min
Divine Transcendence from Incarnation, Direction, Connection, Disconnection, and Foolishness
Al-Mashat lists five negations and argues from the world's insignificance.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Allah is negated from incarnation, direction, connection, disconnection, and foolishness, and how the insignificance of the world supports this.

Core Idea
Allah is absolutely transcendent—He cannot be contained, connected, or disconnected like creation.

Al-Mashat begins by affirming that Allah is free from five specific attributes that would imply limitation or similarity to creation. He then reminds us of the world's utter insignificance before Allah's power, making it impossible for Him to be associated with anything created.

Guided Path
1
Read the negations
Read the opening line listing the five negations: hulul, jihah, ittisal, infisal, safah. Notice each term.
These are key concepts in divine transcendence.
2
Examine each negation
Go through the commentary on each negation. Understand why each is impossible for Allah.
Direction and location are properties of material things; connection/disconnection belong to accidents; safah contradicts wisdom.
3
Reflect on the world's insignificance
Read the argument that the entire universe is nothing compared to Allah. See how this reinforces the negations.
If the world is nothing, how can the Creator be contained in it?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مُنَزَّه عن الْحُلُولِ والجِهَةِ * والاتصالِ والانفصالِ والسَّفَةِ. حد شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي: وفي أحكام الماديات مشروط بالقابلية فلا يقال: إنه فوق الجرم، ولا تحته ولا يمينه ولا شماله، ولا خلفه، ولا أمامه. (و) منزه عن الاتصال، والانفصال، فلا يقال: إنه متصل بالعالم اتصال الحوادث، ولا منفصل، لأن هذه الأمور من الحادث، والله سبحانه وتعالى قديم ليس بحادث. (و) منزه أيضًا عن السفه وهو وضع الشيء في غير محله، كيف وهو المدبر الحكيم العليم الذي وضع كل شيء في موضعه، والذي أحسن وأعطى كل شيء خلقه ثم هدى. واعلم أن العالم: وهو ما سوى الله تعالى، وإن عظم في نفسه فهو في جانب باهر قدرته لا شيء، فكيف يكون العلي الكبير، الغني القدير، حالا، ومتصلا، أو منفصلا في شيء حقير فقير، هو في نفسه عدم. أما ما جاء في النصوص الثابتة من الآيات القرآنية، والأحاديث النبوية الموهمة للتشبيه مما يوهم شيئًا فهو من المتشابه الذي يجب علينا الإيمان به، مع تفويض معناه وحقيقته إلى الله تعالى: ﴿ فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ زَيْغٌ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ مَا تَشَابَهَ مِنْهُ ابْتِغَاءَ الْفِتْنَةِ وَابْتِغَاءَ تَأْوِيلِهِ، وَمَا يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُ إِلَّا الله وَالرَّاسِخُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ يَقُولُونَ آمَنَّا بِهِ كُلٌّ مِنْ عِنْدِ رَبِّنَا وَمَا يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّا أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ ﴾ (١)، جعلنا الله من أولي الألباب بمنه وكرمه آمين. (١) سورة آل عمران الآية (٧)، اقتصر الشارح رحمه الله على الطريق الأسلم، وهو طريق سلفنا الصالح، وبقي طريق الخلف، وهو الأعلم، بتعيين محامل السنية.
Transcendent above incarnation (hulul) and direction (jihah), and connection (ittisal) and disconnection (infisal) and foolishness (safah). The commentary of Shaykh Hasan al-Mashat: And in the rulings of material things, it is conditioned by receptivity, so it is not said: He is above the body, nor below it, nor to its right, nor to its left, nor behind it, nor in front of it. And (He is) transcendent above connection and disconnection, so it is not said: He is connected to the world with the connection of accidents, nor disconnected, because these matters are of the created, and Allah, glorified and exalted, is eternal, not created. And (He is) also transcendent above foolishness, which is putting a thing in other than its place. How could that be, while He is the wise, all-knowing manager who has placed everything in its place, and who has given everything its creation and then guided? And know that the world, which is everything other than Allah, even if great in itself, is nothing in the presence of His overwhelming power. So how could the Most High, the Great, the Self-Sufficient, the All-Powerful be incarnate (hallan) or connected or disconnected in a lowly, poor thing that is in itself non-existence? As for what has come in the established texts of Quranic verses and prophetic hadiths that are suggestive of anthropomorphism (tashbih), it is from the ambiguous (mutashabih) which we must believe in, while delegating (tafwid) its meaning and reality to Allah: {As for those in whose hearts is deviation, they follow what is ambiguous from it, seeking discord and seeking its interpretation. But no one knows its interpretation except Allah. And those firmly rooted in knowledge say, 'We believe in it; all is from our Lord.' And none will remember except those of understanding.} (1) May Allah make us among those of understanding, by His grace and generosity, Amen. (1) Surah Al 'Imran, verse 7. The commentator relied on the safer path, the path of our pious predecessors (salaf), and left the path of the successors (khalaf), which is more knowledgeable, in determining sound interpretations (mahamil al-saniyya).
Source pages p. 57

Commentary
This passage is a detailed explanation of divine transcendence (tanzih) from five negative attributes: hulul (incarnation), jihah (direction), ittisal (connection), infisal (disconnection), and safah (foolishness). The commentator first explains that statements like 'above the body' or 'to the right' are impermissible because they imply direction and limitation, which are properties of material things. Direction and location apply only to bodies and accidents; Allah is not a body. Next, he negates connection and disconnection: Allah is not connected to the world as accidents are connected, nor is He disconnected, because both connection and disconnection are attributes of created things, while Allah is eternal. Then he negates safah, defined as placing something improperly; this is impossible for Allah, who is perfectly wise and places everything in its proper place. The passage then broadens to the world's insignificance before Allah's power—the entire universe is nothing compared to Him, so how could He be contained within a lowly thing? Finally, he addresses anthropomorphic texts (mutashabihat) from the Quran and hadith. The correct approach is to affirm them as true while delegating their meaning (tafwid) to Allah, following the way of the salaf. He quotes the verse from Surah Al 'Imran about those with deviation in their hearts who seek figurative interpretation, and the firm stance of the scholars who say 'We believe in it.' The footnote clarifies that the commentator chose the safer path of tafwid over the path of ta'wil (interpretation) followed by some later scholars.

Key Terms
التنزيه
الحلول
الجهة
الاتصال
الانفصال
السفه

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
Which of the following is NOT one of the five negations mentioned in the passage?
A) Hulul
B) Jihah
C) Tashbih
D) Safah

Key Takeaway
Allah is transcendent above all limitations and cannot be associated with properties of created beings.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If Allah is transcendent above all direction and physical location, do you still sometimes imagine Him as somewhere 'up there'? Will you consciously correct that mental image whenever it arises?
?
The lesson says that the entire world is nothing before Allah's power. How does this truth affect your attachment to worldly things? What specific attachment are you willing to loosen today?
?
Allah is described as al-Hakim, the All-Wise, who places everything in its proper place. Consider a situation in your life where you've questioned Allah's wisdom. Are you ready to submit to His placement, even if you don't understand it?

Handling Ambiguous Texts: The Path of Tafwid
Al-Mashat explains the approach to mutashabih texts using tafwid.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the correct Sunni approach to Quranic verses and hadiths that seem to imply anthropomorphism, which is to affirm them while delegating their true meaning to Allah (tafwid).

Core Idea
Believe in ambiguous texts firmly, but leave their true meaning to Allah alone.

After establishing Allah's transcendence, al-Mashat addresses a potential difficulty: what about verses and hadith that seem to attribute human-like qualities to Allah? He explains that these are mutashabih (ambiguous) and that the correct approach is to believe in them as true while delegating their interpretation to Allah, following the path of the salaf.

Guided Path
1
Identify the problem
Read about the texts that suggest anthropomorphism. Understand why they are considered ambiguous.
These texts are from the Quran and sunna and are divinely revealed.
2
Learn the solution: tafwid
The commentator presents the approach of tafwid: we affirm the texts as true without knowing the how, and we leave the meaning to Allah.
This is the safer path, according to the salaf.
3
Study the Quranic verse
Read the verse from Al Imran (7) that distinguishes between those with deviation and those firmly rooted in knowledge.
The firmly rooted say 'We believe in it; all is from our Lord.'
4
Contrast with the path of ta'wil
The footnote mentions that some later scholars (khalaf) engage in interpretation, but the commentator chooses the safer path.
Tafwid is considered more precautionary in matters of Allah's attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

(و) منزه عن الاتصال، والانفصال، فلا يقال: إنه متصل بالعالم اتصال الحوادث، ولا منفصل، لأن هذه الأمور من الحادث، والله سبحانه وتعالى قديم ليس بحادث. (و) منزه أيضًا عن السفه وهو وضع الشيء في غير محله، كيف وهو المدبر الحكيم العليم الذي وضع كل شيء في موضعه، والذي أحسن وأعطى كل شيء خلقه ثم هدى. واعلم أن العالم: وهو ما سوى الله تعالى، وإن عظم في نفسه فهو في جانب باهر قدرته لا شيء، فكيف يكون العلي الكبير، الغني القدير، حالا، ومتصلا، أو منفصلا في شيء حقير فقير، هو في نفسه عدم. أما ما جاء في النصوص الثابتة من الآيات القرآنية، والأحاديث النبوية الموهمة للتشبيه مما يوهم شيئًا فهو من المتشابه الذي يجب علينا الإيمان به، مع تفويض معناه وحقيقته إلى الله تعالى: ﴿ فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ زَيْغٌ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ مَا تَشَابَهَ مِنْهُ ابْتِغَاءَ الْفِتْنَةِ وَابْتِغَاءَ تَأْوِيلِهِ، وَمَا يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُ إِلَّا الله وَالرَّاسِخُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ يَقُولُونَ آمَنَّا بِهِ كُلٌّ مِنْ عِنْدِ رَبِّنَا وَمَا يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّا أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ ﴾ (١)، جعلنا الله من أولي الألباب بمنه وكرمه آمين. (١) سورة آل عمران الآية (٧)، اقتصر الشارح رحمه الله على الطريق الأسلم، وهو طريق سلفنا الصالح، وبقي طريق الخلف، وهو الأعلم، بتعيين محامل السنية.

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that affirming Allah's transcendence requires denying the literal apparent meaning of verses like 'Yadullah' (Hand of Allah).
Correction: The correct view is to affirm the verses as true while delegating their reality to Allah, as the salaf did.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the correct approach towards Quranic verses that seem to imply anthropomorphism?
A) Interpret them figuratively (ta'wil) according to linguistic norms.
B) Deny them as false interpolations.
C) Believe in them while delegating their true meaning to Allah (tafwid).
D) Reject them because they contradict reason.

Key Takeaway
The correct approach to ambiguous texts is to affirm them as true and delegate their meaning to Allah, as the salaf did.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read a verse like 'The hand of Allah is above their hands' (48:10), do you feel a tension between affirming the text and believing in Allah's transcendence? How does tafwid free you from that tension while still holding onto both truths? Commit to reciting 'Aamanna bihi kullun min 'indi Rabbina' the next time you encounter such a verse.
?
Are you ever tempted to 'explain away' ambiguous attributes, thinking that giving a figurative meaning makes faith easier or more acceptable? What would it cost you to simply trust Allah's words and leave their reality to Him? Make a firm intention today to practice tafwid in your heart, not seeking ta'wil out of intellectual pride or fear.
?
The verse condemns 'those in whose hearts is deviation' who follow ambiguous texts 'seeking discord and interpretation.' Is there any area of your faith where you find yourself pushing for a neat explanation rather than submitting with 'We believe in it'? Confess that to Allah and ask for the steadfastness of those firmly rooted in knowledge.
