The Ash'ari Reply to the Mu'tazili Objection on Divine Will
Anecdote of a debate on divine transcendence and will.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Ash'ari scholars reconcile divine will with human disobedience using the distinction between willing and approving.

Core Idea
God wills all that occurs, but He does not approve sin; disobedience is by human choice, not divine coercion.

This lesson presents a famous exchange between a Mu'tazili and an Ash'ari scholar. It illustrates the Ash'ari position that God's will encompasses all events, including sinful acts, without implying His approval. The anecdote shows how the Ash'ari master deftly refutes the Mu'tazili objection by turning the question back on the objector.

Guided Path
1
Read the anecdote carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the sequence of statements and the quick back-and-forth.
This is a real historical exchange between two major scholars.
2
Identify the two positions
What does the Mu'tazili assert in his first statement? What is the Ash'ari's response? Pinpoint the core disagreement about God's will and human action.
The Mu'tazili wants to deny that God wills evil; the Ash'ari affirms God's sovereignty.
3
Analyze the final counter-question
Why does Abu Ishaq ask 'Is our Lord disobeyed by force?' Think about what this question implies about human agency and divine will.
This question exposes a flaw in the Mu'tazili objection.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
لَطِيفَةٌ يُحْكَى أَنَّ عَبْدَ الْجَبَّارِ الْمُعْتَزِلِيَّ قَالَ لِلْأُسْتَاذِ أَبِي إِسْحَاقَ الْإِسْفَرَايِنِيِّ، مِنْ كِبَارِ أَئِمَّةِ أَهْلِ السُّنَّةِ وَالْجَمَاعَةِ، مُعَرِّضًا لَهُ: «سُبْحَانَ مَنْ تَنَزَّهَ عَنِ الْفَحْشَاءِ». فَتَفَطَّنَ الْأُسْتَاذُ وَقَالَ: «سُبْحَانَ مَنْ لَا يَقَعُ فِي مُلْكِهِ إِلَّا مَا يَشَاءُ». فَقَالَ عَبْدُ الْجَبَّارِ: «يُرِيدُ رَبُّنَا أَنْ يُعْصَى». فَقَالَ الْأُسْتَاذُ: «أَيُعْصَى رَبُّنَا قَهْرًا».
A subtle anecdote: It is related that 'Abd al-Jabbar al-Mu'tazili said to the master Abu Ishaq al-Isfara'ini, who is among the great imams of Ahl al-Sunna, alluding to him: 'Glory be to Him who is transcendent above indecency.' The master understood and said: 'Glory be to Him in whose dominion nothing happens except what He wills.' 'Abd al-Jabbar said: 'Our Lord wants to be disobeyed.' The master said: 'Is our Lord disobeyed by force?'
Source pages p. 62

Commentary
This passage is a short anecdote illustrating a theological exchange between a Mu'tazili scholar, 'Abd al-Jabbar, and an Ash'ari scholar (from Ahl al-Sunna), Abu Ishaq al-Isfara'ini. 'Abd al-Jabbar begins by praising God as transcendent above indecency (al-fahsha'), implying that God does not will evil acts. Abu Ishaq rephrases the praise to affirm God's sovereignty: God is transcendent, and nothing occurs in His dominion except what He wills. 'Abd al-Jabbar then retorts: 'So our Lord wants to be disobeyed?' This is a common Mu'tazili objection: if God wills all that happens, then He wills sin. Abu Ishaq replies with a counter-question: 'Is our Lord disobeyed by force?' (i.e., is the disobedience done by compelling God? No, the slave disobeys by his own choice, but that happens within God's will. The question challenges the Mu'tazili inference that willing sin implies approving or coercing it. The Ash'ari position is that God wills sin's occurrence but does not approve it; the human agent has free choice within God's overarching will.

Key Terms
التَّنْزِيه
الْفَحْشَاء
الْمَشِيئَة
الْقَهْر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: If God wills all that happens, then He approves of sin.
Correction: God wills the occurrence of sin without approving it; the human agent chooses disobedience, not God.

Checkpoint
What was the point of Abu Ishaq's final question: 'Is our Lord disobeyed by force?'
A) To affirm that God compels disobedience.
B) To deny human free will.
C) To challenge the Mu'tazili assumption that if God wills sin, He forces it.
D) To assert that God has no will over sin.

Key Takeaway
God's will encompasses all events, but human disobedience is by choice not coercion; God does not approve sin.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sin, do you ever secretly feel that God's will somehow forced you, as if you had no choice? How does Abu Ishaq's question 'Is our Lord disobeyed by force?' cut into that excuse, and what would it mean for you to fully own your sin before God?
?
The Mu'tazili thought they were defending God's holiness by denying He wills evil. Where in your own heart do you think you're 'defending God' but actually shrinking His sovereignty? What part of His will do you struggle to submit to because you're afraid it might contradict His goodness?
?
If you truly believed that God wills every event - including your failures - yet does not approve of your sin, how would your repentance change? Would you still run from Him in shame, or would you run to Him, trusting that He is both in control and merciful?

Divine Transcendence
beginner
4 min
The Principle of Divine Impossibility
Statement of the principle that impossible attributes are opposites of necessary ones.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that what is impossible for Allah is systematically derived as the opposite of His necessary attributes.

Core Idea
Allah's impossibility mirrors His necessity.

After covering the necessary attributes of Allah, the author now turns to what is impossible. The core principle is simple yet profound: every impossible attribute is the direct opposite of a necessary one. This lesson presents that foundational rule and the three categories of necessary attributes it relies on.

Guided Path
1
Read the opening verse
Read the verse from al-Kharīda that states the principle. Notice how it references 'what preceded' (the necessary attributes).
The verse is compact, so read carefully.
2
Identify the three categories
The commentary explains that the impossible attributes are the opposites of three types of necessary attributes: essential (nafsiyya), negative (salbiyya), and attributes of meaning (maʿnawiyya).
These categories were defined earlier.
3
Understand the logical relationship
Understand that for each necessary attribute, its opposite is impossible. This logical pairing is the backbone of the discussion.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويستحيل ضِدُّ مَا تَقَدَّمَا * مِنَ الصِّفاتِ الشَّامِحَاتِ فَاعلَما. ولما فرغ في القسم الأول: وهو ما يجب لله تعالى، أراد أن يشرع في القسم الثاني، وهو ما يستحيل عليه تعالى فقال: (ويستحيل اضداد الصفات ضد ما تقدما بألف الإطلاق، أي: يستحيل كل ما ينافي ما تقدم لك من الصفات النفسية، والصفات السلبية، وصفات المعاني (الشامخات) أي المرتفعات والمُنزّهات عن الحدوث ولوازمه. (فاعلما) أي: فاعلم أنه يستحيل عليه تعالى العدم والحدوث، وطرو العدم وهو الفناء، والمماثلة للحوادث في نحو جرمية، وعرضية وعدم قيامه بنفسه وعدم الوحدانية، بأن يكون ذا كثرة في ذاته، أو صفاته، أو يكون له شريك في فعل من الأفعال. ويستحيل عليه الجهل، والموت والعجز والكراهية، أي: عدم الإرادة، أو صدور شيء من الكائنات بالتعليل أو بالطبع. ويستحيل عليه: البَكَم، والصَّمَم، والعَمَى تعالى الله عن علوا كبيرًا.
And impossible is the opposite of what preceded, from the lofty attributes, so know. And when he finished the first section—which is what is necessary for Allah, exalted—he intended to begin the second section, which is what is impossible for Him, exalted, so he said: 'And impossible are the opposites of the attributes opposite to what preceded, with the alif of absoluteness,' i.e., everything that contradicts what preceded you of the essential attributes, the negative attributes, and the attributes of meaning, the lofty ones, i.e., the elevated and transcendent from origination and its concomitants. (Faʿlama) i.e., so know that it is impossible for Him, exalted: non-existence, origination, the occurrence of non-existence which is annihilation, similarity to originated things in terms of being a body or an accident, not being self-subsistent, and lack of oneness, such that He has multiplicity in His essence or His attributes, or that He has a partner in any of His acts. And it is impossible for Him: ignorance, death, inability, and dislike (karāhiya), i.e., absence of will, or that any of the existents should proceed by (bi-l-taʿlīl) or by (bi-l-ṭabʿ). And it is impossible for Him: dumbness, deafness, and blindness. Exalted is Allah above that, with a great exaltation.
Source pages p. 72

Commentary
This passage begins the discussion of what is impossible (muḥāl) for Allah, which is the second major division of attributes (after the necessary attributes). The author cites the verse from al-Kharīda: 'And impossible is the opposite of what preceded, from the lofty attributes, so know.' He then explains that after completing the section on what is necessary for Allah (wājib), he now enters the section on what is impossible. The statement 'and impossible are the opposites of the attributes' means that every attribute that contradicts the previously mentioned necessary attributes—which include the essential (nafsiyya), negative (salbiyya), and attributes of meaning (maʿnawiyya)—is impossible. He then lists these opposites: non-existence (ʿadam), origination (ḥudūth), perishing (fanāʾ), similarity to originated things (such as being a body or accident), not being self-subsistent (qāʾim bi-nafsih), lack of oneness (i.e., having multiplicity in essence or attributes, or having a partner), and the opposites of the attributes of meaning: ignorance (jahl), death (mawt), inability (ʿajz), and dislike (karāhiya, i.e., absence of will). He also mentions that it is impossible for things to proceed from Him by causal necessity (bi-l-taʿlīl) or by nature (bi-l-ṭabʿ). Finally, he adds the opposites of the negative attributes: dumbness (bakam), deafness (ṣamam), and blindness (ʿamā). All these are impossible for Allah, and He is exalted above them.

Key Terms
يستحيل
ضد
الصفات النفسية
الصفات السلبية
صفات المعاني

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that what is impossible for Allah is arbitrary or unrelated to His necessary attributes.
Correction: This passage teaches that impossibility is logically derived: it is the exact opposite of what is necessary. Thus, understanding necessary attributes directly informs what is impossible.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what are the three categories of necessary attributes that have opposites?
A) Essential, negative, attributes of meaning
B) Essential, positive, attributes of action
C) Negative, attributes of meaning, attributes of essence
D) Essential, attributes of action, attributes of perfection

Key Takeaway
The impossibility of Allah is systematically derived as the opposite of His necessary attributes, which are categorized into essential, negative, and of meaning.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The lesson teaches that impossibility is defined by opposition to necessity. Have you ever, even for a moment, thought of Allah as having an attribute that contradicts His necessity, like weakness or ignorance? How does realizing this principle help you guard your thoughts? Resolve to correct any such thought immediately.
?
You have learned that the necessary attributes are categorized as essential, negative, and of meaning. Which category have you been negligent in reflecting upon? For instance, do you truly internalize that Allah is free from all imperfections (negative attributes)? What step will you take today to deepen your understanding?
?
If you truly believe that the opposite of every necessary attribute is impossible, how would that change the way you rely on Allah? For example, is there any situation where you act as if Allah's power is limited? Commit to affirming His absolute perfection in your heart.

The Enumeration of Attributes Impossible for Allah
Enumeration of the specific attributes impossible for Allah, grouped by category.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to list the specific attributes that are impossible for Allah according to the author's enumeration.

Core Idea
The list of impossible attributes safeguards Allah's absolute perfection.

After establishing the general principle, the author proceeds to enumerate the specific attributes that are impossible for Allah. These are grouped into several categories: those opposing the negative attributes, those opposing the attributes of meaning, and additional sensory impossibilities. This lesson walks through the list.

Guided Path
1
Identify the three groups
Notice that the enumeration is structured. First, attributes opposing the negative attributes (e.g., non-existence, origination). Second, attributes opposing the attributes of meaning (e.g., ignorance, death). Third, sensory opposites (dumbness, deafness, blindness).
This grouping is implicit; try to see it.
2
Understand each item
For each impossible attribute, understand what it means and why it contradicts Allah's perfection.
Focus on terms like 'dislike' (karāhiya) which is absence of will.
3
Connect to necessary attributes
Relate each impossible attribute back to its corresponding necessary attribute from the earlier discussion.
For example, ignorance is opposite of knowledge.

Source Text

And when he finished the first section—which is what is necessary for Allah, exalted—he intended to begin the second section, which is what is impossible for Him, exalted, so he said: 'And impossible are the opposites of the attributes opposite to what preceded, with the alif of absoluteness,' i.e., everything that contradicts what preceded you of the essential attributes, the negative attributes, and the attributes of meaning, the lofty ones, i.e., the elevated and transcendent from origination and its concomitants. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as impossible for Allah in this passage?
A) Death
B) Inability
C) Knowledge
D) Dumbness

The specific impossible attributes for Allah are systematically listed and include non-existence, origination, similarity to creation, ignorance, death, inability, dislike, and sensory deficiencies.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If Allah cannot be ignorant, then His knowledge is perfect and all-encompassing. How does this truth confront the times you have doubted His wisdom in your life? Will you now surrender to His knowledge even when you don't understand?
?
The passage states that Allah cannot have dislike (karāhiya) in the sense of absence of will. This means every event occurs by His will, not by blind fate. When you face a trial, do you blame fate or do you see His will behind it? What needs to change in your heart to accept His decree with contentment?
?
Allah is free from sensory deficiencies like blindness and deafness. He sees and hears everything, including your secret thoughts. If you truly believed this, what would you stop doing in private that you would never do in public?

What Cannot Be: Existence and Oneness
Mashāṭ enumerates the impossible attributes related to existence and oneness.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the attributes that are impossible for Allah regarding His existence and oneness, and why they contradict His necessary attributes.

Core Idea
Allah cannot not exist, cannot be like His creation, and cannot have partners.

After establishing what Allah must necessarily be, the author now turns to what is impossible for Him. These impossibilities are simply the opposites of the necessary attributes. In this lesson, we cover those related to Allah’s existence and unique oneness — non-existence, temporality, annihilation, similarity to creation, dependence, and multiplicity.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Note the phrase 'lofty attributes' (al-ṣifāt al-shāmikhāt).
2
Identify the six impossible attributes
The passage lists six specific impossibilities. Find each one and notice how they are opposites of necessary attributes.
Non-existence, temporality, annihilation, similarity, not self-subsistent, not unique.
3
Reflect on the meaning of 'lofty'
The commentator explains that these attributes are 'elevated and transcendent above temporality and its concomitants'. Think about why temporality is a key concept.
Temporality (ḥudūth) is the first impossibility listed.
4
Consider the opposites
For each impossible attribute, try to recall the corresponding necessary attribute from the previous lesson. For example, non-existence is opposite to existence.
This helps in memorization and understanding.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المستحيل في حقه تعالى ويَسْتَحِيلُ ضِدُّ مَا تَقَدَّمَا * مِنَ الصِّفاتِ الشَّامِحَاتِ فَاعلَما | ٤٣ شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي ولما فرغ في القسم الأول: وهو ما يجب لله تعالى، أراد أن يشرع المستحيل في حقه تعالى في القسم الثاني، وهو ما يستحيل عليه تعالى فقال: (ويستحيل اضداد الصفات ضد ما تقدما بألف الإطلاق، أي: يستحيل كل ما ينافي ما تقدم المنقدمة لك من الصفات النفسية، والصفات السلبية، وصفات المعاني (الشامخات) أي المرتفعات والمُنزّهات عن الحدوث ولوازمه. (فاعلما) أي: فاعلم أنه يستحيل عليه تعالى العدم والحدوث، وطروء العدم وهو الفناء، والمماثلة للحوادث في نحو جرمية، وعرضية وعدم قيامه بنفسه وعدم الوحدانية، بأن يكون ذا كثرة في ذاته، أو صفاته، أو يكون له شريك في فعل من الأفعال. ويستحيل عليه الجهل، والموت والعجز والكراهية، أي: عدم الإرادة، أو صدور شيء من الكائنات بالتعليل أو بالطبع. ويستحيل عليه: البَكَم، والصَّمَم، والعَمَى تعالى الله عن ذلك علوا كبيرًا. (١) في المطبوعة «القدم» وهو خطأ. (٢) الفرق بين الفاعل بالعلة، والفاعل بالطبع أن العلة: لا تتوقف على وجود شرط ولا انتفاء مانع، والطبيعة: تتوقف على ذلك. ومما يدل على بطلانهما: اختلاف أنواع العالم على كثرتها، إذ مفعول العلة والطبيعة لا يختلف.
The impossible with respect to Him, exalted. 'And the opposite of what preceded from the lofty attributes is impossible, so know.' 43. Commentary by Sheikh Hasan Muhammad al-Mishāt al-Makki al-Mālikī. When he finished the first section—which is what is obligatory for Allah—he intended to begin the impossible with respect to Him in the second section, which is what is impossible for Him, so he said: 'And the opposites of the attributes are impossible against what preceded with an alif of absolute negation, i.e., everything that contradicts what preceded for you from the essential attributes, the negative attributes, and the attributes of meanings (the lofty) meaning elevated and transcendent above temporality and its concomitants. So know: i.e., know that it is impossible for Him, exalted, to be non-existence, temporality, the occurrence of non-existence which is annihilation, similarity to contingent things in terms of being a body or an accident, not being self-subsistent, not being unique (i.e., having multiplicity in His essence or attributes, or having a partner in any action). And it is impossible for Him to be ignorant, dead, impotent, or averse (i.e., lacking will), or for anything to proceed from Him by causality or nature. And it is impossible for Him to be mute, deaf, blind—exalted is Allah above that with great exaltation. (1) In the printed edition: 'al-qidam' (eternity) and it is an error. (2) The difference between the agent by causality and the agent by nature is that causality does not depend on the existence of a condition or the absence of an impediment, while nature does. Evidence of their invalidity includes the diversity of the types of the world despite their abundance, because the effect of causality and nature does not differ.'
Source pages p. 72

Commentary
This passage is an enumeration of the attributes that are impossible for Allah. The commentator explains the line from the poem that states the opposite of the previously mentioned necessary attributes are impossible. He clarifies that 'impossible' refers to everything that contradicts the essential, negative, and attributes of meanings, which are described as 'lofty' (shāmikhāt) meaning elevated and free from temporality. He then lists the impossible attributes in detail: non-existence, temporality, annihilation, similarity to contingent beings in being a body or accident, not being self-subsistent, lacking oneness (i.e., having multiplicity or a partner), ignorance, death, impotence, aversion (lack of will), causing things by natural necessity or blind causality, muteness, deafness, and blindness. The footnotes correct a printing error and explain the difference between an agent by causality and an agent by nature, both of which are impossible for Allah.

Key Terms
مستحيل
عدم
حدوث
فناء
مماثلة
وحدانية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to the passage, which of the following is NOT among the impossible attributes related to existence and oneness?
A) Non-existence
B) Temporality
C) Ignorance
D) Similarity

Key Takeaway
Allah's necessary attributes of existence, pre-eternity, everlastingness, dissimilarity, self-subsistence, and oneness logically exclude their opposites.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If Allah cannot not exist, how does that certainty shape your trust in Him when everything else in your life feels uncertain?
?
Do you ever find yourself acting as if Allah has partners—by fearing people more than Him or seeking approval from others over His? What can you do today to turn back to His oneness?
?
When you encounter suffering or loss, do you truly believe that nothing resembles Allah? How might that belief change the way you respond to hardship?

What Cannot Be: Deficiencies in Perfection
Mashāṭ enumerates the impossible attributes related to perfection.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the attributes impossible for Allah related to deficiencies in knowledge, power, will, life, and sensory perception.

Core Idea
Allah cannot lack any perfection; therefore ignorance, death, impotence, unwillingness, causality, muteness, deafness, and blindness are impossible.

Continuing the enumeration of impossible attributes, the author now lists those that contradict Allah's perfection in knowledge, power, will, life, and sensory attributes. These are the opposites of the necessary attributes of knowledge, power, will, life, and speech, hearing, and sight.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the list of eight impossible attributes.
Look for the terms: ignorance, death, impotence, aversion, causality/nature, muteness, deafness, blindness.
2
Identify the necessary opposites
For each impossible attribute, recall its necessary opposite. For example, ignorance is opposite to knowledge.
Knowledge, power, will, life, speech, hearing, sight are necessary.
3
Understand the refutation of causality and nature
The passage denies that Allah acts by causality or nature. The footnote explains the difference and provides evidence from the diversity of the world.
This is a key point in kalam: Allah is a free agent.
4
Reflect on sensory attributes
Even though Allah is not a body, He has attributes of hearing, sight, and speech. Their opposites are impossible.
This shows that Allah's attributes are not like human attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

The impossible with respect to Him, exalted. 'And the opposite of what preceded from the lofty attributes is impossible, so know.' 43. Commentary by Sheikh Hasan Muhammad al-Mishāt al-Makki al-Mālikī. When he finished the first section—which is what is obligatory for Allah—he intended to begin the impossible with respect to Him in the second section, which is what is impossible for Him, so he said: 'And the opposites of the attributes are impossible against what preceded with an alif of absolute negation, i.e., everything that contradicts what preceded for you from the essential attributes, the negative attributes, and the attributes of meanings (the lofty) meaning elevated and transcendent above temporality and its concomitants. So know: i.e., know that it is impossible for Him, exalted, to be non-existence, temporality, the occurrence of non-existence which is annihilation, similarity to contingent things in terms of being a body or an accident, not being self-subsistent, not being unique (i.e., having multiplicity in His essence or attributes, or having a partner in any action). And it is impossible for Him to be ignorant, dead, impotent, or averse (i.e., lacking will), or for anything to proceed from Him by causality or nature. And it is impossible for Him to be mute, deaf, blind—exalted is Allah above that with great exaltation. (1) In the printed edition: 'al-qidam' (eternity) and it is an error. (2) The difference between the agent by causality and the agent by nature is that causality does not depend on the existence of a condition or the absence of an impediment, while nature does. Evidence of their invalidity includes the diversity of the types of the world despite their abundance, because the effect of causality and nature does not differ.'
Source pages p. 72

Commentary
This passage is an enumeration of the attributes that are impossible for Allah. The commentator explains the line from the poem that states the opposite of the previously mentioned necessary attributes are impossible. He clarifies that 'impossible' refers to everything that contradicts the essential, negative, and attributes of meanings, which are described as 'lofty' (shāmikhāt) meaning elevated and free from temporality. He then lists the impossible attributes in detail: non-existence, temporality, annihilation, similarity to contingent beings in being a body or accident, not being self-subsistent, lacking oneness (i.e., having multiplicity or a partner), ignorance, death, impotence, aversion (lack of will), causing things by natural necessity or blind causality, muteness, deafness, and blindness. The footnotes correct a printing error and explain the difference between an agent by causality and an agent by nature, both of which are impossible for Allah.

Key Terms
جهل
موت
عجز
كراهية
بكم
صمم
عمى
تعليل
طبع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some people think that 'aversion' (karahiya) means Allah dislikes certain things as an emotion, but it means absence of will, which is impossible.
Correction: Karahiya in this context means lack of will, not an emotion. Allah's will is perfect and always present.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is an impossible attribute related to sensory faculties?
A) Ignorance
B) Death
C) Blindness
D) Impotence

Key Takeaway
Allah is free from all deficiencies in knowledge, power, will, life, and perception; any opposite is categorically impossible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Ignorance is impossible for Allah, but you are often ignorant of your own faults. When did you last admit you were wrong about something you were certain of? Will you commit to saying 'I don't know' this week as an act of humility before Allah's perfect knowledge?
?
Death is impossible for Allah, but you will die. Does that certainty shape your priorities today? What is one task you keep postponing that you would do immediately if you knew today was your last?
?
Allah cannot be deaf; He hears every whisper and thought. What secret have you been hiding that you are afraid to speak to Him? Will you pour it out in private prayer tonight, trusting that He hears and forgives?

Divine Transcendence
beginner
5 min
Believing in the Vision of Allah in Paradise
Al-Mashat defines the obligation to believe in seeing Allah in Paradise without limit or direction.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic belief in seeing Allah in Paradise as a real but transcendent vision, free from limit, place, or direction.

Core Idea
Seeing Allah in Paradise is real but without limits, place, or direction.

Al-Mashat explains a core Sunni creedal point: the vision of Allah (ru'ya) in Paradise. This passage clarifies that the vision is obligatory to believe in, yet it is utterly transcendent—without finitude, without location, and without 'how.' The author uses analogies to help us grasp that we can see something without encompassing or confining it, pointing to the incomparability of Allah.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once through, then read the literal translation. Pay attention to the key terms: ru'ya, jannat al-khuld, and tanahi.
The passage is a commentary on a poetic line; notice how the author unpacks each word.
2
Identify the three negations
The passage states that the vision is without finitude, without place/direction, and without 'how'. Find these three negations in the text and see how they are argued.
These negations remove any physicality from the vision.
3
Reflect on the analogies
Consider the analogies: knowledge of Allah (without limit), seeing the sky/earth (without encompassing), and seeing night/day (without direction). How do these help understand the vision?
The analogies show that vision can be real without confinement or location.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وَاجْزِمَ أَخِي بِرُؤْيَةِ الإِلَهِ * فِي جَنَّةِ الْخُلْدِ بِلا تَنَاهِي ثُمَّ شَرَعَ النَّاظِمُ يَذْكُرُ عَقِيدَةَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ فِي رُؤْيَةِ اللهِ تَعَالَى فِي الْجَنَّةِ وَحُكْمَهَا، فَقَالَ: (وَاجْزِمَ) وَاقْطَعْ وَاعْتَقِدْ وُجُوبًا (أَخِي) يَا أَخِي فِي الدِّينِ بِرُؤْيَةِ (الإِلَهِ تَعَالَى) أَيْ بِأَنَّ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَرَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ تَبَارَكَ وَتَعَالَى (فِي جَنَّةِ الْخُلْدِ) أَيِ الإِقَامَةِ دَوَامًا، وَذَلِكَ بِأَنْ يَكْشِفَ اللهُ عَزَّ وَجَلَّ لِمَنْ أَرَادَ لَهُ الرُّؤْيَةَ انْكِشَافًا تَامًّا بِالْبَصَرِ. وَالْمَعْنَى: أَنَّهُ يَجِبُ عَلَيْكَ أَيُّهَا الْمُؤْمِنُ أَنْ تَعْتَقِدَ رُؤْيَةَ اللهِ تَعَالَى فِي الْجَنَّةِ وَوُقُوعَهَا (بِلا) أَيْ فِي حَالِ كَوْنِ الرُّؤْيَةِ بِغَيْرِ (تَنَاهِي) لِلْمَرْئِيِّ تَعَالَى أَيْ مِنْ غَيْرِ إِحَاطَةٍ بِحُدُودِ الْمَرْئِيِّ وَنِهَايَتِهِ، لاِسْتِحَالَةِ الْحُدُودِ وَالنِّهَايَاتِ عَلَى اللهِ تَعَالَى لأَنَّ الرُّؤْيَةَ بِخَلْقِ اللهِ تَعَالَى، وَهُوَ نَوْعٌ مِنَ الإِدْرَاكِ بِخَلْقِهِ تَعَالَى لِمَنْ شَاءَ فِي أَيِّ مَحَلٍّ شَاءَ، لاَ فِي مَكَانٍ وَلاَ جِهَةٍ، وَلاَ بِاتِّصَالِ شُعَاعٍ، وَنَحْوِ ذَلِكَ مِمَّا هُوَ مِنْ سِمَاتِ الْحَوَادِثِ، فَإِنَّ اللهَ مُنَزَّهٌ عَنْهَا. وَيُنْظَرُ فِي ذَلِكَ إِلَى الْعِلْمِ، فَإِنَّهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَهُ تَعَالَى بِلاَ حَدٍّ، وَلاَ نِهَايَةَ، وَبِلاَ كَيْفَ، فَكَذَلِكَ يَرَوْنَهُ، وَكَالسَّمَاءِ وَالأَرْضِ فَإِنَّا نَعْلَمُ بِهِمَا وَنَرَاهُمَا، وَلَكِنْ لاَ نُحِيطُ بِهِمَا، مَعَ أَنَّهُمَا مَحْدُودَتَانِ فَكَيْفَ بِمَنْ لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ، وَكَاللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهَارِ فَإِنَّا نُبْصِرُهُمَا، وَلَيْسَا فِي جِهَةٍ فَكَيْفَ بِمَنْ لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ.
And be certain, my brother, of the vision of the Divine * in the garden of eternity without finitude. Then the versifier began to mention the creed of the Muslims concerning the vision of Allah the Exalted in Paradise and its ruling, and he said: (And be certain) i.e., cut off and believe obligatorily (my brother) O my brother in religion of the vision (of the Divine the Exalted) i.e., that the believers will see their Lord, Blessed and Exalted, (in the garden of eternity) i.e., of permanent abode, and that by Allah, Mighty and Majestic, causing for whomever He wills vision to uncover a complete unveiling by the eye. And the meaning: it is obligatory upon you, O believer, to believe in the vision of Allah the Exalted in Paradise and its occurrence (without) i.e., in the state of the vision being without (finitude) of the seen, Exalted, i.e., without encompassing the limits and end of the seen, due to the impossibility of limits and ends for Allah the Exalted, because the vision is by Allah's creation, and it is a type of perception by His creation for whomever He wills in any place He wills, not in a place nor a direction, nor by the connection of a ray, and the like of that which is among the characteristics of contingent things, for Allah is transcendent above them. And one looks in that to knowledge, for they know Him the Exalted without limit, and without end, and without how, so similarly they see Him. And like the sky and the earth, for we know them and see them, but we do not encompass them, despite them being limited, so how about He of whom there is nothing like Him? And like the night and the day, for we see them, and they are not in a direction, so how about He of whom there is nothing like Him?
Source pages p. 77

Commentary
This passage is a definition and explanation of the Muslim belief regarding the vision of Allah (ru'ya) in Paradise. The author begins by citing the versified line that commands affirming this vision without finitude. He then unpacks each element: 'be certain' means to hold an obligatory belief; 'my brother' addresses the reader as a fellow believer; 'vision of the Divine' means that believers will see their Lord; 'in the garden of eternity' specifies the location as Paradise where believers dwell forever. The vision occurs by Allah creating a complete unveiling in the eye for whomever He wills. The commentary emphasizes that the vision is without limits because limits are impossible for Allah. It is a created perception, not occurring in a place or direction, nor by rays—these are attributes of contingent things. The author draws an analogy to how we know Allah without limit or how, and thus we see Him similarly. Further analogies compare seeing the sky and earth without encompassing them, and seeing night and day without them being in a direction, to show that Allah's vision is even more beyond our comprehension. The central point is that the vision is real but utterly transcendent, devoid of any physical or spatial limitations.

Key Terms
رؤية
جنة الخلد
تناهي
إدراك
كيف
جهة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that seeing Allah implies physicality, direction, or limit.
Correction: The vision is without finitude, place, direction, or how; it is a created perception by Allah, reflecting His transcendence.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, how is the vision of Allah in Paradise described?
A) As a physical vision with rays and direction
B) As a vision without finitude, place, or direction, created by Allah
C) As a metaphorical vision only, not literal
D) As a vision that will cease for some believers

Key Takeaway
Believing in the vision of Allah in Paradise is obligatory, but it is a transcendent vision without limit, place, or direction, reflecting Allah's incomparability.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think of seeing Allah in Paradise, do you unconsciously picture a limit, a place, or a direction? How does this lesson challenge that mental image, and what must you now actively correct in your heart?
?
You affirm the vision without knowing 'how'. Are you truly content with that, or does your heart secretly demand a more tangible certainty? Will you train yourself to accept Allah's promise as is, without adding your own conditions?
?
If you long for the vision of Allah, does that longing change how you live today? Or is it just a distant hope? What one habit can you start this week to make that vision the driving desire of your life?

Prophethood

The Necessary Attributes of Prophets
Stanza listing necessary prophetic attributes and their opposites.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the four necessary attributes of prophets (trustworthiness, truthfulness, delivering the message, and intelligence), their opposites being impossible, their shared human traits, and that their sending is a grace and mercy from Allah.

Core Idea
Prophets are divinely protected messengers with essential perfections.

This passage from the matn concisely lists the attributes that all prophets must possess. It establishes their moral and intellectual perfection while affirming their human nature, and it grounds their mission in divine grace. Let's explore each element carefully.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic stanza once to appreciate its poetic form. Then read the English translation to understand the literal meaning.
The Arabic is a rhymed verse (rajaz), which aids memorization.
2
Identify the four necessary attributes
The passage commands to describe all messengers with four attributes: trustworthiness (amana), truthfulness (sidq), delivering (tabligh), and intelligence (fatana). Notice they are qualities that ensure the message is conveyed correctly.
3
Understand the impossible opposites and possible human traits
The opposites of these attributes are impossible for prophets—they cannot be treacherous, dishonest, conceal the message, or be foolish. However, prophets share ordinary human traits like eating, which are merely possible (ja'iz) for them.
This balances their elevated status with their humanity.
4
Reflect on the sending as grace and mercy
The passage concludes that sending prophets is a favor (tafaddul) and mercy (rahma) from Allah to all worlds. This emphasizes that prophethood is not an obligation on Allah but a gift.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وَصِفْ جَمِيعَ الرُّسُلِ بِالْأَمَانَةِ * وَالصِّدْقِ وَالتَّبْلِيغِ وَالْفَطَانَةِ . وَيَسْتَحِيلُ ضِدُّهَا عَلَيْهِمْ * وَجَائِزٌ كَالْأَكْلِ فِي حَقِّهِمْ . إِرْسَالُهُمْ تَفَضُّلٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ * لِلْعَالَمِينَ جَلَّ مُولِي النِّعْمَةِ
And describe all the messengers with trustworthiness, truthfulness, delivering, and intelligence. And their opposite is impossible for them, and (human traits) like eating are possible in their right. Their sending is a favor and mercy to the worlds, exalted is the Bestower of grace.
Source pages p. 16

Commentary
This passage outlines the essential attributes of prophets. It commands to describe all messengers ('rusul') with four necessary attributes: amana (trustworthiness), sidq (truthfulness), tabligh (delivering the message), and fatana (intelligence). The opposites of these (e.g., betrayal, lying, hiding the message, stupidity) are impossible for them. However, prophets share ordinary human characteristics (like eating) that are possible for them. Finally, it states that God's sending of prophets is a grace and mercy (tafaddul wa rahma) to all worlds, not an obligation upon God.

Key Terms

الأمانة
الصدق
التبليغ
الفطنة
تفضل
رحمة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think prophets are completely superhuman, incapable of ordinary human needs like eating or sleeping.
Correction: Prophets share normal human traits such as eating, drinking, and sleeping. These are possible for them and do not detract from their status.

Checkpoint

A) Trustworthiness
B) Truthfulness
C) Richness
D) Intelligence

Key Takeaway
The necessary attributes of prophets ensure their reliability in conveying revelation, and their humanity affirms that prophethood is a divine grace.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
The passage says sending prophets is a favor and mercy from Allah. Have you ever thanked Allah for sending the Prophet ﷺ with a heart that truly feels gratitude? If not, what is stopping you from making that a daily practice?
?
Prophets are described as possessing keen intelligence. Does your life reflect that you believe the deen is not just for simpletons but demands intellect? Where have you excused yourself from deep study because it felt too hard, and what will you do to change that?

Prophetic Rulings as Necessary Knowledge
First line: authority of prophetic rulings

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that every ruling from the Prophet is considered necessary (daruri) knowledge, meaning it is indubitably certain.

Core Idea
Prophetic rulings are certain, not probable.

This lesson examines the first line of the stanzas, which establishes the epistemological status of prophetic teachings. Imam Laqqani asserts that every ruling from the Prophet is not merely opinion but reaches the level of necessary knowledge, meaning it is indubitably true. This is a foundational principle in Sunni creed regarding the authority of the Sunnah.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Carefully read the first line of the stanzas in Arabic and its literal English translation. Notice the key term 'daruri' (necessary).
2
Understand 'daruri'
Reflect on what 'daruri' means in Islamic epistemology: knowledge that is self-evident or proven beyond doubt. The Prophet's teachings are elevated to this level of certainty.
This distinguishes prophetic rulings from mere scholarly opinions.
3
Implications for authority
Consider why this matters: if every prophetic ruling is daruri, then obeying them is obligatory and rejecting them is disbelief. This affirms the binding authority of the Sunnah.
4
Test your understanding
Answer the checkpoint question to ensure you grasp the key point.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وَكُلِّ مَا جَاءَ مِنَ الْبَشِيرِ * مِنْ كُلِّ حُكْمٍ صَارَ كَالضَّرُورِيِّ . وَيَنْطَوِي فِي كِلْمَةِ الْإِسْلَامِ * مَا قَدْ مَضَى مِنْ سَائِرِ الْأَحْكَامِ
And everything that came from the bringer of good news of every ruling has become like necessary. And there is contained in the word of Islam all that has passed of the other rulings.
Source pages p. 17

Commentary
This pair of stanzas affirms the authority of the Prophet's teachings. It states that every ruling (hukm) that came from the Prophet (al-bashir) has attained the status of daruri (necessary knowledge), meaning it is indubitably true. The second line says that the word 'Islam' encompasses all the rulings that have preceded from other prophets and the final revelation. Thus, Islam includes and confirms all previous divine commands.

Key Terms
بشير
حكم
ضروري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think prophetic rulings are only probable or open to rejection if they conflict with reason.
Correction: The text says they become 'daruri' (necessary), meaning they are certain and binding.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what status do the rulings from the Prophet have?
A) They are probable opinions
B) They are like necessary (daruri) knowledge
C) They are optional recommendations
D) They apply only to the early community

Key Takeaway
Every prophetic ruling is certain knowledge (daruri) and must be accepted without doubt.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If every prophetic ruling is as certain as knowing that fire burns, which action of the Prophet (peace be upon him) have you been treating as optional? What will you commit to doing consistently from today?
?
Does your daily schedule reflect the certainty that the Prophet's teachings are necessary knowledge? Which sunnah could you integrate this week as an act of affirming that certainty?
?
When you face a command from the Prophet that challenges your desires, do you treat it as a probability to be weighed or as an undeniable truth? What change in your heart would make submission easier?

Islam Encompasses All Previous Rulings
Second line: comprehensiveness of Islam

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the term 'Islam' encompasses all previous divine rulings, indicating that Islam is the final and comprehensive revelation.

Core Idea
Islam is the final, all-encompassing religion.

This lesson focuses on the second line of the stanzas, which highlights the comprehensive nature of Islam. It states that the word 'Islam' contains all the rulings that came before, meaning that Islam confirms and subsumes the earlier prophetic messages. This reinforces the Islamic belief in the unity and continuity of divine guidance.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the second line of the stanzas in Arabic and its literal translation. Note the phrase 'yanṭawī fī kalimat al-Islām'.
2
Understand 'yanṭawī fī'
This phrase means 'is contained in' or 'is encompassed by'. It signifies that Islam includes all previous rulings, not that it abrogates them entirely but that it confirms and perfects them.
This is a key concept in the doctrine of Islam's supremacy.
3
Connect to earlier revelations
Reflect on how Islam views earlier scriptures: they are from Allah, but the final message of Islam supersedes and incorporates their essential teachings.
This answers the misconception that Islam rejects earlier prophets.
4
Test your understanding
Answer the checkpoint question to gauge your comprehension.

Prophethood
beginner

The Obligatory Attributes of Messengers and Their Infallibility
Beginning of the section on prophethood, enumerating necessary attributes and explaining infallibility.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the four necessary attributes of messengers and the concept of prophetic infallibility as defined by the commentator, including its scope and one apparent difficulty.

Core Idea
Prophets possess four essential attributes and are protected from sin, yet may perform disliked acts for divine purposes.

This lesson introduces the second major section of Islamic theology: prophethood. The author lists the essential qualities every messenger must possess, and the commentator explains the crucial concept of 'isma (infallibility), clarifying its meaning and addressing a potential objection from the Quran.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify the four attributes
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the four attributes listed in the couplet: trustworthiness, truthfulness, conveyance, and intelligence.
The commentator explains that 'trustworthiness' includes infallibility.
2
Understand the definition of 'isma
Focus on the commentator's definition: Allah's preservation of the prophets' inward and outward from anything prohibited, even if the prohibition is one of dislike.
This definition is broader than just avoiding major sins.
3
Consider the exception for disliked acts
The commentator notes that prophets may perform disliked acts (makruh) for legislative purposes, making those acts acts of closeness to Allah.
The example from al-Maraqi illustrates this principle.
4
Reflect on the verse interpretation
The commentator addresses the apparent contradiction of Quran 48:2 ('forgive your sins') by offering two interpretations: 'security' or concealment of the sin itself for prophets, while for the ummah it is concealment of punishment.
This shows how apparent difficulties are resolved within the doctrine of infallibility.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الْوَاجِبُ مَبْحَثُ النُّبُوَّاتِ إِذِ الْوُقُوعُ جَائِزٌ بِالْعَقْلِ * وَقَدْ أَتَى فِيهِ دَلِيلُ النَّقْلِ وَصِفْ جَمِيعَ الرُّسْلِ بِالأَمَانَةَ * وَالصِّدْقِ وَالتَّبْلِيغِ وَالفَطَانَةً. وَإِلَى هُنَا انْتَهَى الْكَلَامُ عَلَى مَا أَرَادَ النَّاظِمُ رَحِمَهُ اللَّهُ تَعَالَى بِقِسْمِ الْإِلَهِيَّاتِ، وَاتَّبَعَهُ بِالْقِسْمِ الثَّانِي وَهُوَ النُّبُوَّاتُ فَقَالَ: وَصِفْ جَمِيعَ الرُّسْلِ بِسُكُونِ السِّينِ لُغَةً، أَيْ: يَجِبُ عَلَيْكَ أَيُّهَا الْمُكَلَّفُ أَنْ تَعْتَقِدَ أَنَّ جَمِيعَ رُسُلِ اللَّهِ تَعَالَى مُتَّصِفُونَ: (بِالأَمَانَةِ) وَالْعِصْمَةِ : وَهِيَ حِفْظُ اللَّهِ تَعَالَى بَوَاطِنَهُمْ وَظَوَاهِرَهُمْ مِنَ التَّلَبُّسِ بِمَنْهِيٍّ عَنْهُ، وَلَوْ نَهْيَ كَرَاهَةٍ نَعَمْ قَدْ يَفْعَلُونَ الْمَكْرُوهَ تَشْرِيعًا فَيَكُونُ فِي حَقِّهِمْ قُرْبَةً، قَالَ سَيِّدِي عَبْدُ اللَّهِ الْعَلَوِيُّ رَحِمَهُ اللَّهُ تَعَالَى فِي الْمَرَاقِي (١): وَرُبَّمَا يَفْعَلُ لِلْمَكْرُوهِ * مُبَيِّنًا أَنَّهُ لِلتَّنْزِيهِ فَصَارَ فِي جَانِبِهِ مِنَ الْقُرْبِ * كَالنَّهْيِ أَنْ يَشْرَبَ مِنْ فَمِ الْقِرَبِ وَمَا يُوهِمُ خِلَافَ ذَلِكَ كَقَوْلِهِ تَعَالَى: ﴿ لِيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللَّهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِنْ ذَنْبِكَ وَمَا تَأَخَّرَ ﴾، فَمَصْرُوفٌ عَنْ ظَاهِرِهِ بِأَنَّ الْمُرَادَ أَمَنَّهُ، أَوْ أَنْ يَغْفِرَ مَأْخُوذٌ مِنَ الْغُفْرِ، وَهُوَ السَّتْرُ، وَالسَّتْرُ تَارَةً يَكُونُ بَيْنَ الْعَبْدِ وَالذَّنْبِ، وَهُوَ فِي جَانِبِ الْأَنْبِيَاءِ، وَتَارَةً يَكُونُ بَيْنَ الذَّنْبِ وَمَا يَتَرَتَّبُ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ الْعِقَابِ، وَهُوَ الْمُرَادُ فِي جَانِبِ الْأُمَّةِ، وَاللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ.
The obligatory [section]: the chapter on prophethood. Since occurrence is possible by reason, and indeed the proof of revelation has come concerning it. And describe all the messengers with trustworthiness, truthfulness, conveyance, and intelligence. And here ends the discourse on what the poet, may Allah have mercy on him, intended in the section on divine matters, and he followed it with the second section, which is prophethood, and he said: 'And describe all the messengers' with the sukun of the sin linguistically, i.e., it is obligatory upon you, O accountable person, to believe that all the messengers of Allah, peace be upon them, are characterized by (trustworthiness) and infallibility: which is Allah's preservation of their inward and outward from being stained by anything prohibited, even if it is a prohibition of dislike. Indeed, they may do the disliked act for legislative purposes, so it becomes for them an act of closeness. Sayyidi Abdullah al-Alawi said, may Allah have mercy on him, in al-Maraqi (1): 'And perhaps he does the disliked act, clarifying that it is for disassociation, so it becomes in his side an act of closeness, like the prohibition of drinking from the mouth of the waterskin.' And what gives the impression contrary to that, like His saying, Exalted: {That Allah may forgive you your sins, past and future} (Q. 48:2), is diverted from its apparent meaning by saying that what is meant is 'security', or that 'forgive' is derived from al-ghufr, which is concealment, and concealment is sometimes between the servant and the sin, and that is on the side of the prophets, and sometimes between the sin and what results from it of punishment, and that is meant on the side of the ummah. And Allah knows best.
Source pages p. 81

Commentary
This passage begins a new section on prophethood (al-nubuwwat) after completing the section on divinity (al-ilahiyyat). The couplet states two things: first, that the occurrence (of prophethood or miracles) is rationally possible and has been proven by revelation; second, the obligation to describe all messengers with four attributes: amana (trustworthiness), sidq (truthfulness), tabligh (conveyance of the message), and fatana (intelligence). The commentator then explains that 'describing all messengers' means it is obligatory for every accountable person to believe that all messengers are characterized by amana and isma (infallibility). He defines isma as Allah's preservation of their inward and outward from committing anything prohibited, even if the prohibition is one of dislike (karaha). He notes that prophets may perform disliked acts (makruh) for the purpose of legislation (tashri'), making those acts acts of closeness (qurba) for them. He quotes a poem from Sayyidi Abdullah al-Alawi from al-Maraqi to illustrate that doing a disliked act to clarify its permissibility becomes an act of closeness, like the prohibition of drinking from the mouth of a waterskin. Finally, he addresses a potential objection from the Quranic verse 'that Allah may forgive you your sins, past and future' (Q. 48:2). He explains that this verse should not be taken literally for prophets; instead, it means either 'security' (amana) or that 'forgive' is derived from 'ghufr' meaning concealment (satr). This concealment for prophets is the protection from the sin itself, while for the ummah it is the concealment of punishment. Thus, the verse does not imply actual sin for prophets.

Key Terms
النبوات
الأمانة
العصمة
الصدق
التبليغ
الفطانه
الغفر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Prophets never perform any disliked action.
Correction: Prophets may perform disliked acts for legislative purposes, which become acts of closeness.

Checkpoint
What definition does the commentator give for isma (infallibility) of prophets?
A) They are protected from all sins, minor and major
B) Allah preserves their inward and outward from committing anything prohibited
C) They never do any disliked act
D) They are free from all human attributes

Key Takeaway
Prophets are infallible in that Allah preserves them from sin, but they may perform disliked acts to clarify the law, and apparent verses implying sin are interpreted accordingly.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Prophets may perform disliked acts for legislative purposes, yet those acts become closeness to Allah. How does this change your understanding of what is 'good' or 'bad' in your own actions? Can you think of a situation where you avoided a disliked act because you thought it was beneath you, but perhaps doing it with the right intention could bring you closer to Allah? Commit to identifying one such action this week and perform it seeking Allah's pleasure.
?
The verse about forgiveness of the Prophet is interpreted as concealment from sin itself, not punishment. Reflect on your own relationship with sin: do you seek concealment from the sin itself (i.e., never committing it) or merely concealment from punishment? Which one truly reflects faith? Pray that Allah grants you the former and helps you avoid sin altogether.
?
Allah preserves prophets from sin by His grace, not their own power. This should humble you, for you are not a prophet and are prone to sin. How often do you rely on your own strength to avoid sin? Repent for your self-reliance and ask Allah to preserve you from sin, while taking the means He has prescribed.

Prophethood and the Attribute of Trustworthiness
Introduces the section on prophethood and explains al-amanah with ismah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of prophetic trustworthiness (al-amanah) linked to infallibility (al-ismah), the exception for performing disliked acts for legislative purposes, and how to interpret the verse about forgiveness of the Prophet's sins.

Core Idea
Prophets are sinless but may perform ordinarily disliked acts to teach permissibility.

This lesson marks the transition from divine matters to the second major section of the poem: prophethood. We will focus on the first attribute mentioned: trustworthiness (al-amanah), which the commentator closely associates with infallibility (al-ismah). The passage also addresses a potential misunderstanding about prophets doing disliked actions and clarifies a Quranic verse that might otherwise suggest prophetic sin.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without too much analysis, then read the literal translation to get a sense of the author's argument.
2
Understand the definition of ismah
Identify how the commentator defines al-ismah: Allah's preservation of the prophets' inner and outer selves from anything forbidden.
Notice the phrase 'حفظ الله تعالى بواطنهم وظواهرهم'.
3
Grasp the exception for disliked acts
The text says prophets may do makruh (disliked) acts as a legislative instruction. Read the poetic quotation from al-Maraqi and understand its point.
This is to show that such acts become acts of closeness for the prophets.
4
Interpret the problematic verse
Examine the verse from Surah al-Fath (48:2) and the commentator's response. Understand that 'forgiveness' here means security or covering, not actual sin.
The commentator distinguishes between covering from sin (for prophets) and covering from punishment (for the community).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإلى هنا انتهى الكلام على ما أراد الناظم رحمه الله تعالى بقسم الإلهيات، واتبعه بالقسم الثاني وهو النبوات فقال: وصِف جميع الرسل بسكون السين لغة، أي: يجب عليك أيها المكلف أن تعتقد أن جميع رسل الله تعالى متصفون: عليهم الســ (١) (بالأمانة) والعصمة : وهي حفظ الله تعالى بواطنهم وظواهرهم من التلبس بمنهى عنه، ولو نهى كراهة نعم قد يفعلون المكروه تشريعا فيكون في حقهم قربةً، قال سيدي عبد الله العلوي رحمه الله تعالى في المراقي (١): ورُبَّمَا يَفعلُ للمَكْرُوه * مُبيِّنًا أَنَّه للتَنْزِيهِ فَصَارَ فِي جَانِبِهِ مِن القُربَ * كالنَّهِي أَنْ يَشْرَبَ مِن فَمِ القِرَب وما يوهم خلاف ذلك كقوله تعالى: ﴿ لِيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللَّهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِن ذَنْبِكَ وَمَا تَأَخَّرَ ) (٢) ، فمصروف عن ظاهره بأن المراد أمنه، أو أن يغفر مأخوذ من الغفر، وهو الستر، والستر تارة يكون بين العبد والذنب، وهو في جانب الأنبياء، وتارة بين الذنب وما يترتب عليه من العقاب، وهو المراد في جانب الأمة، والله أعلم.
And up to here ended the speech on what the author intended, may Allah have mercy on him, regarding the section of the divine matters, and he followed it with the second section, which is the prophethoods, and he said: Describe all the messengers (with sukun on the sin as a language [form]), i.e., it is obligatory upon you, O responsible person, to believe that all the messengers of Allah, peace be upon them, are described with trustworthiness and infallibility, which is Allah’s preservation of their inner selves and outer selves from engaging in anything forbidden, even if the prohibition is one of disapproval. Yes, they may do the disliked thing as a legislative instruction, and it would be in their case a means of closeness. Sayyidi Abdullah al-Alawi, may Allah have mercy on him, said in al-Maraqi: 'And perhaps he does the disliked thing, showing that it is for the purpose of declaring it permissible, so it becomes in his aspect a means of closeness, like the prohibition of drinking from the mouth of the water-skin.' And what suggests otherwise, such as His saying, may He be exalted: {That Allah may forgive you your past and future sin}, is diverted from its apparent meaning by saying that what is meant is [grant] security, or that 'forgive' is derived from al-ghufr, which is covering, and covering is sometimes between the servant and the sin, which is on the side of the prophets, and sometimes between the sin and what results from it of punishment, which is intended on the side of the community, and Allah knows best.
Source pages p. 81

Commentary
This passage marks the transition from the section on divinity (al-ilahiyyat) to the second major section of the poem: prophethood (al-nubuwwat). The author quotes the first line of the matn: 'Describe all the messengers with trustworthiness and truthfulness, delivery and intelligence.' He then focuses on the first attribute: trustworthiness (al-amanah). He explains that trustworthiness is linked to sinlessness (al-ismah), defined as Allah’s preservation of the prophets’ inner and outer selves from committing anything prohibited, even if the prohibition is one of disapproval (karahah). The author clarifies that prophets may sometimes perform actions that are ordinarily disliked (makruh) for legislative purposes, in order to show the permissibility of such actions. He cites a verse from al-Maraqi to illustrate that such actions become acts of closeness to Allah for the prophets. Then he addresses a potential difficulty: the Quranic verse that speaks of forgiveness of the Prophet’s sin (Surah al-Fath, 48:2). He explains that this verse is not to be taken literally; rather, 'forgiveness' here means security or covering. For prophets, covering means that Allah protects them from sin, whereas for the community, covering means that Allah veils the sin from punishment. Thus, the verse does not imply actual sinning by the Prophet.

Key Terms
الأمانة
العصمة
المكروه

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that prophets can never perform any action that is ordinarily disliked.
Correction: The text clarifies that prophets may perform such actions for legislative purposes to teach permissibility, and these become acts of closeness.

Checkpoint
According to the text, what does 'al-ismah' (infallibility) signify for the prophets?
A) They never commit any act that is prohibited
B) They never commit any act that is prohibited or disliked
C) Allah preserves them from engaging in anything forbidden, but they may do disliked acts for legislative purposes
D) They are incapable of sinning in any way

Key Takeaway
The trustworthiness of prophets is defined by Allah's protection from sin, but they may engage in ordinarily disliked actions for the sake of teaching the community.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read the verse about forgiveness regarding the Prophet, did you ever assume it implied sin? Now that you know it means security and protection, how does that deepen your trust in his sinlessness? Will you pause before interpreting ambiguous verses and seek the understanding of the scholars?
?
Prophets, though sinless, performed disliked actions to teach permissibility. Are there permissible actions you avoid out of excessive caution, thereby missing opportunities to teach others the breadth of Allah's mercy? How will you follow the prophetic example and embrace lawful ease?
?
If Allah’s preservation of the prophets is absolute, what does that demand of your certainty in the message they brought? Have you ever doubted the authenticity of a teaching because of a perceived flaw in the messenger? How will you strengthen your conviction that every prophetic action was guided?

Prophethood

Truthfulness of the Prophets: Definition and Divine Confirmation
Defines truthfulness and provides Quranic and miracle proofs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of truthfulness for prophets and the two proofs that establish it: the command to follow them and the divine confirmation through miracles.

Core Idea
Prophets must be truthful because Allah confirms them with miracles.

This lesson examines the attribute of truthfulness (al-sidq) that must be affirmed for all prophets. The author defines it as correspondence to reality and provides two key proofs: Allah's command to follow prophets absolutely, and His endorsement through miracles, with the Quran as the greatest enduring miracle.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation. Notice how the author defines truthfulness before giving proofs.
The definition is 'correspondence of judgment to reality'.
2
Identify the two proofs
The passage presents two arguments for why prophets must be truthful. Find them: one from Allah's command to follow, and one from miracles.
The second proof includes the Quran as the greatest miracle.
3
Reflect on the Quranic challenge
The passage describes how the Quran challenged the Arabs and they could not match it. Ask yourself why this proves the Prophet's truthfulness.
It serves as a continuous miracle confirming the Prophet.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وَصِفْ جَمِيعَ الرُسُلِ بِالأَمَانَة * والصِّدْقِ والتبليغ والفَطَانَة | ٤٩ (والصدق)، أي: صفهم بالصدق أيضًا (١) ، وهو مطابقة الحكم للواقع، قال تعالى: ﴿ وَمَا يَنطِقُ عَنِ الْهَوَى (٢)، ولأننا معشر الأمة مأمورون باتباعهم في أقوالهم وأفعالهم مطلقا، واتباعهم طاعة الله تعالى من يطع الرسول فقد أطاع الله، فلو جاز عليهم الكذب للزم الكذب في خبره ،تعالى، لأن الله تعالى صدقهم بالمعجزة (٣) التي أيدهم بها، والتي هي بمنزلة قوله: «صدق عبدي في كل ما يبلغ عني». ومحفظة نبينا صلى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ لا تُعَدّ ولا تحصى، وأعظمها القرآن العظيم، الذي هو بين أيدينا يبقى إلى يوم القيامة محفوظا من التبديل والتغيير، بفضل الله تعالى وحفظه، وقد تحدى القرآن العظيم صناديد العرب وبلغائهم المتفننين في البلاغة وأساليب الكلام، فلم يقدروا على معارضته، بل قال بعض المشركين منهم: إن له حلاوة، وإن عليه طلاوة». ) (والتبليغ) أي: وصف جميع الرسل الكرام بالتبليغ، و[هو] إيصال الأحكام الشرعية التي أمروا بتبليغها إلى أمتهم، أي: يجب عليك أيها المكلف أن تعتقد أنهم بلّغوا ما أمروا بتبليغه من الأحكام، قال تعالى: يَتأَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلّغ مَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ﴾ (٤) .
And describe all the messengers with trustworthiness, and truthfulness, and delivery, and intelligence | 49. (And truthfulness), i.e., describe them with truthfulness also, and it is the correspondence of judgment to reality. He said, may He be exalted: {And he does not speak from desire}. And because we, the community, are commanded to follow them in their sayings and actions absolutely, and following them is obedience to Allah: 'Whoever obeys the Messenger has obeyed Allah.' So if lying were possible for them, lying would necessarily follow in His report, may He be exalted, because Allah has verified them by the miracle with which He supported them, and it is like His saying: 'My servant has spoken truthfully in everything he conveys from Me.' And the miracle of our Prophet, may Allah bless him and give him peace, cannot be counted or enumerated, and the greatest of them is the Mighty Quran, which is in our hands, remaining until the Day of Resurrection, preserved from alteration and change, by the favor of Allah and His preservation. And the Mighty Quran challenged the stubborn ones of the Arabs and their eloquent ones skilled in eloquence and styles of speech, and they were unable to oppose it; rather, some of the polytheists among them said: 'It has sweetness, and upon it is beauty.' (And delivery) i.e., describe all the noble messengers with delivery, and it is conveying the legal rulings which they were commanded to convey to their community. i.e., it is obligatory upon you, O responsible person, to believe that they conveyed what they were commanded to convey of the rulings. He said, may He be exalted: {O Messenger, convey what has been sent down to you from your Lord}.
Source pages p. 82

Commentary
This passage continues the explanation of the attributes of messengers. First, the author defines truthfulness (al-sidq) as the correspondence of a statement to reality. He then provides two proofs for the necessity of truthfulness in prophets: (1) Allah commands us to follow them absolutely, and following a liar is invalid; (2) Allah confirms their truthfulness through miracles, which serve as divine endorsements that 'My servant has spoken truthfully'. Next, the author discusses miracles, particularly the greatest miracle: the Quran. He notes that the Quran is preserved from alteration and challenged the Arabs, who could not match it. Finally, he explains delivery (al-tabligh): the prophets must convey the rulings they were commanded to deliver, and it is obligatory to believe they did so, supported by Quran 5:67.

Key Terms
الصدق
المعجزة
القرآن

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that miracles are only for proving prophethood once at the beginning of a prophet's mission.
Correction: The passage shows that the Quran remains a continuous miracle, preserved and challenging until the Day of Resurrection, thus continuously proving the Prophet's truthfulness.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is given as a proof that prophets must be truthful?
A) They are the best of creation
B) Allah confirms them with miracles
C) They never make mistakes
D) The Quran says so

Key Takeaway
Prophets are truthful because Allah commands us to follow them and confirms their truthfulness through miracles, with the Quran as the greatest enduring miracle.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly follow the Prophet in every aspect of your life, or do you pick and choose what suits you? If you claim to believe he is truthful, how does your obedience reflect that belief?
?
When you recite or hear the Quran, do you feel its miraculous nature, or has familiarity dulled your awe? What would change if you treated each verse as a fresh divine challenge to your heart?
?
If someone asked you why you trust the Prophet's teachings, would you point to the Quran's challenge and its preservation, or would you rely on habit? Is your conviction built on the evidence presented here, or mere upbringing?

Delivery of the Prophets: Obligation and Quranic Proof
Defines delivery and provides Quranic proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the attribute of delivery (al-tabligh) that must be affirmed for all prophets and the obligation to believe that they fully conveyed their message.

Core Idea
Prophets must deliver all that they are commanded to convey, and we must believe they did.

This lesson focuses on the attribute of delivery (al-tabligh), which is the conveying of divine rulings to the community. The author defines it and provides the Quranic proof from Surah Al-Ma'idah (5:67) that the Prophet must deliver what was revealed.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation. The passage is short and to the point. Observe how the author defines tabligh and then supports it with a verse.
2
Understand the definition
The author defines delivery as conveying the rulings that the prophets were commanded to deliver. Note that it is an obligation upon the prophets.
The definition includes 'conveying the legal rulings to their community'.
3
Reflect on the obligation to believe
The author states it is obligatory for believers to believe that prophets delivered all that they were commanded. Ask yourself why this is important.
It ensures the completeness of revelation.

وَصِفْ جَمِيعَ الرُسُلِ بِالأَمَانَة * والصِّدْقِ والتبليغ والفَطَانَة | ٤٩ (والصدق)، أي: صفهم بالصدق أيضًا (١) ، وهو مطابقة الحكم للواقع، قال تعالى: ﴿ وَمَا يَنطِقُ عَنِ الْهَوَى (٢)، ولأننا معشر الأمة مأمورون باتباعهم في أقوالهم وأفعالهم مطلقا، واتباعهم طاعة الله تعالى من يطع الرسول فقد أطاع الله، فلو جاز عليهم الكذب للزم الكذب في خبره ،تعالى، لأن الله تعالى صدقهم بالمعجزة (٣) التي أيدهم بها، والتي هي بمنزلة قوله: «صدق عبدي في كل ما يبلغ عني». ومحفظة نبينا صلى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ لا تُعَدّ ولا تحصى، وأعظمها القرآن العظيم، الذي هو بين أيدينا يبقى إلى يوم القيامة محفوظا من التبديل والتغيير، بفضل الله تعالى وحفظه، وقد تحدى القرآن العظيم صناديد العرب وبلغائهم المتفننين في البلاغة وأساليب الكلام، فلم يقدروا على معارضته، بل قال بعض المشركين منهم: إن له حلاوة، وإن عليه طلاوة». ) (والتبليغ) أي: وصف جميع الرسل الكرام بالتبليغ، و[هو] إيصال الأحكام الشرعية التي أمروا بتبليغها إلى أمتهم، أي: يجب عليك أيها المكلف أن تعتقد أنهم بلّغوا ما أمروا بتبليغه من الأحكام، قال تعالى: يَتأَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلّغ مَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ﴾ (٤) .
And describe all the messengers with trustworthiness, and truthfulness, and delivery, and intelligence | 49. (And truthfulness), i.e., describe them with truthfulness also, and it is the correspondence of judgment to reality. He said, may He be exalted: {And he does not speak from desire}. And because we, the community, are commanded to follow them in their sayings and actions absolutely, and following them is obedience to Allah: 'Whoever obeys the Messenger has obeyed Allah.' So if lying were possible for them, lying would necessarily follow in His report, may He be exalted, because Allah has verified them by the miracle with which He supported them, and it is like His saying: 'My servant has spoken truthfully in everything he conveys from Me.' And the miracle of our Prophet, may Allah bless him and give him peace, cannot be counted or enumerated, and the greatest of them is the Mighty Quran, which is in our hands, remaining until the Day of Resurrection, preserved from alteration and change, by the favor of Allah and His preservation. And the Mighty Quran challenged the stubborn ones of the Arabs and their eloquent ones skilled in eloquence and styles of speech, and they were unable to oppose it; rather, some of the polytheists among them said: 'It has sweetness, and upon it is beauty.' (And delivery) i.e., describe all the noble messengers with delivery, and it is conveying the legal rulings which they were commanded to convey to their community. i.e., it is obligatory upon you, O responsible person, to believe that they conveyed what they were commanded to convey of the rulings. He said, may He be exalted: {O Messenger, convey what has been sent down to you from your Lord}.
Source pages p. 82

Commentary
This passage continues the explanation of the attributes of messengers. First, the author defines truthfulness (al-sidq) as the correspondence of a statement to reality. He then provides two proofs for the necessity of truthfulness in prophets: (1) Allah commands us to follow them absolutely, and following a liar is invalid; (2) Allah confirms their truthfulness through miracles, which serve as divine endorsements that 'My servant has spoken truthfully'. Next, the author discusses miracles, particularly the greatest miracle: the Quran. He notes that the Quran is preserved from alteration and challenged the Arabs, who could not match it. Finally, he explains delivery (al-tabligh): the prophets must convey the rulings they were commanded to deliver, and it is obligatory to believe they did so, supported by Quran 5:67.

Key Terms
التبليغ

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that prophets only delivered the essential parts of the message, and minor details may have been left out or forgotten.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that it is obligatory to believe that they conveyed all that they were commanded to convey. Prophets do not forget or omit any part of the revelation.

Checkpoint
What does the attribute of delivery (al-tabligh) refer to?
A) The ability to speak eloquently
B) Conveying the legal rulings to the community
C) Performing miracles
D) Being trustworthy

Key Takeaway
Prophets delivered all the rulings they were commanded to convey, and it is obligatory for Muslims to believe that they did so.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the Prophet (peace be upon him) delivered every single ruling without omission, then you have no excuse to ignore or selectively follow any part of Islam. Which command of Allah have you been neglecting that you will now commit to acting upon?
?
The verse commands the Prophet to deliver the message as a trust. As a member of this ummah, you are also entrusted to convey the truth to others. What truth about Allah or His religion have you been too shy or fearful to share with someone close to you?
?
Believing that the prophets fully delivered the revelation means you are accountable for acting on every part of it. What specific step will you take this week to learn a ruling you previously ignored and put it into practice?
