Truthfulness of the Prophets: Definition and Proof
Author defines as-siddiq and proves prophets must be truthful.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of prophetic truthfulness and the rational and scriptural proof that prophets are truthful.

Core Idea
Prophets are truthful because Allah confirms their message through miracles.

This lesson establishes the essential attribute of truthfulness for all prophets. The author first defines what truthfulness means, then provides both a Qur'anic proof and a rational argument linked to the function of miracles. Understanding this proof is key to trusting everything the prophets convey from Allah.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it, then read the translation. Get familiar with the terminology and flow.
Pay attention to the definition and the two proofs.
2
Identify the definition and proofs
Locate the precise definition of truthfulness (مطابقة الحكم للواقع) and note the two proofs: the Qur'anic verse (53:3) and the rational argument from following and miracles.
The miracle argument links prophecy directly to divine confirmation.
3
Reflect on the miracle argument
Consider how a miracle functions as Allah's implicit statement that the prophet is truthful. This makes it logically impossible for a prophet to lie.
This is a key point in Kalam: miracles are not mere wonders but divine endorsements.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وَصِفْ جَمِيعَ الرُّسُلِ بِالأَمَانَة * والصِّدْقِ والتبليغ والفَطَانَة. حد شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي هع. (والصدق)، أي: صفهم بالصدق أيضًا، وهو مطابقة الحكم للواقع، قال تعالى: ﴿ وَمَا يَنطِقُ عَنِ الْهَوَى ﴾، ولأننا معشر الأمة مأمورون باتباعهم في أقوالهم وأفعالهم مطلقا، واتباعهم طاعة الله تعالى من يطع الرسول فقد أطاع الله، فلو جاز عليهم الكذب للزم الكذب في خبره تعالى، لأن الله تعالى صدقهم بالمعجزة التي أيدهم بها، والتي هي بمنزلة قوله: «صدق عبدي في كل ما يبلغ عني». ومعجزة نبينا صلى الله عليه وسلم لا تُعَدّ ولا تحصى، وأعظمها القرآن العظيم، الذي هو بين أيدينا يبقى إلى يوم القيامة محفوظا من التبديل والتغيير، بفضل الله تعالى وحفظه، وقد تحدى القرآن العظيم صناديد العرب وبلغائهم المتفننين في البلاغة وأساليب الكلام، فلم يقدروا على معارضته، بل قال بعض المشركين منهم: إن له حلاوة، وإن عليه طلاوة.
And describe all messengers with trustworthiness, truthfulness, conveying, and intelligence. [This is] the commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mishshāt al-Makkī al-Mālikī. (And truthfulness) i.e. describe them with truthfulness as well, and it is the correspondence of judgment to reality. Allah Most High said: {Nor does he speak from [his own] inclination}. And because we, the community, are commanded to follow them in their statements and actions absolutely, and following them is obedience to Allah Most High: 'Whoever obeys the Messenger has obeyed Allah.' So if lying were possible for them, then lying would be necessary in His news, because Allah Most High confirmed them as truthful through the miracle (mu'jiza) by which He supported them, and which is in the position of His statement: 'My servant has spoken truthfully in all that he conveys from Me.' And the miracle of our Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, cannot be counted or enumerated, and the greatest of them is the Mighty Qur'an, which is before us, remaining until the Day of Resurrection, preserved from alteration and change, by the grace of Allah and His preservation. And the Mighty Qur'an challenged the champions of the Arabs and their eloquent speakers skilled in eloquence and styles of speech, but they were unable to oppose it; rather some of the polytheists among them said: 'It has sweetness, and it has charm.'
Source pages p. 82

Commentary
This passage defines and proves the attribute of truthfulness (as-siddiq) for the prophets. The author first explains that 'truthfulness' in the poem means describing all messengers with truthfulness. He gives the definition: truthfulness is the correspondence of a statement (or judgment) to reality. Then he provides a proof from the Qur'an (53:3) that the Prophet does not speak from desire, indicating his truthfulness. The author adds a rational argument: the community is commanded to follow the prophets absolutely in word and deed, and following them is obedience to Allah. If lying were possible for them, then Allah's own word would be subject to falsehood because Allah confirms their truthfulness through miracles—those miracles serve as Allah saying, 'My servant has spoken truthfully in all that he conveys from Me.' The author then emphasizes the greatest miracle of Prophet Muhammad: the Qur'an, which remains preserved and inimitable, as the Arabs themselves acknowledged its beauty and sweetness.

Key Terms
الصدق
المعجزة
اتباع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Miracles are merely impressive acts that show power, but do not prove truthfulness.
Correction: Miracles are divine testimony: they function as Allah saying 'My servant has spoken truthfully.' Thus, a prophet supported by a miracle cannot lie.

Checkpoint
According to the author, what would logically follow if lying were possible for prophets?
A) The community would not be able to follow them.
B) Allah's news would also be subject to falsehood.
C) Their miracles would be impossible.
D) The Qur'an would not be preserved.

Key Takeaway
The truthfulness of prophets is established by their miracles, which serve as Allah's testimony that they speak truthfully.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
In what area of your life are you selectively following the Prophet's example, and what does the absolute command to follow him (as confirmed by the miracle of the Qur'an) demand of you right now?
?
When you read the Qur'an, do you truly receive it as the word of the One who testified His Prophet's truthfulness? How does this affect your urgency and commitment to obeying what you read today?
?
If the miracle of the Qur'an is enough to silence the most eloquent Arabs, what excuse do you have for doubting or neglecting any of the Prophet's teachings? What specific command will you act on immediately to prove your belief in his truthfulness?

The Obligation of Prophets to Convey the Message
Defines at-tabligh and provides scriptural evidence for its obligation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of tabligh (conveying) as an essential attribute of prophets and the scriptural evidence for its obligation.

Core Idea
Prophets are defined by their duty to deliver Allah's commands fully and faithfully.

This lesson introduces the attribute of at-tabligh, which is central to prophethood. Before we can understand why prophets are sinless and trustworthy, we must first recognize that their primary mission is to convey the divine message without alteration. The author grounds this attribute in both Qur'anic command and the Prophet's own practice.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation once without analysis. Notice how the author moves from a definition to multiple proofs.
The author uses a concise definition followed by scriptural evidence.
2
Define tabligh in your own words
Paraphrase the author's definition: tabligh is the delivery of legal rulings that prophets were commanded to convey to their communities.
The definition includes 'إيصال الأحكام الشرعية التي أمروا بتبليغها'.
3
Identify the scriptural proofs
Find the Qur'anic verse (5:67) and the three hadith references. Notice how each proof reinforces the obligation.
The Prophet repeated 'Have I conveyed?' and commanded others to convey.
4
Understand the communal response
Read the supplication of the community: 'O Allah, indeed Your Prophet... conveyed the message, fulfilled the trust...' This shows the belief that the Prophet fully discharged his duty.
This supplication is recited by Muslims, affirming their conviction.
5
Reflect on the obligation
Consider why believing in the prophets' conveyance is a necessary part of faith. It protects against doubt in the completeness of revelation.
This connects to the concept of amana (trust).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(والتبليغ) أي: وصف جميع الرسل الكرام بالتبليغ، و[هو] إيصال الأحكام الشرعية التي أمروا بتبليغها إلى أمتهم، أي: يجب عليك أيها المكلف أن تعتقد أنهم بلّغوا ما أمروا بتبليغه من الأحكام، قال تعالى: ﴿ يَتأَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلّغ مَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ﴾. وقد قال عليه الصلاة والسلام يوم الحج الأكبر: «ألا فليبلغ الشاهد منكم الغائب، ألا هل بلغت»، يكرر عليه الصلاة والسلام ذلك. ويقول عليه الصلاة والسلام: بلغوا عني، أو كما قال، ويقول الله تعالى وهو من آخر ما أُنزل عليه ﴿ الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْإِسْلَامَ دِينًا ﴾. وتقول الأمة المحمدية، ونحن منها: «اللهم إن نبيك سيدنا محمدًا صلى الله عليه وسلم بلغ الرسالة، وأدى الأمانة، ونصح الأمة، وجاهد في سبيلك حق الجهاد حتى أتاه اليقين، فجزاه الله عنا أفضل ما جازي نبيا عن أمته».
(And conveying) i.e. describing all noble messengers with conveying, and it is the delivery of the legal rulings which they were commanded to convey to their community. That is, it is obligatory upon you, O responsible person, to believe that they conveyed what they were commanded to convey of the rulings. Allah Most High said: {O Messenger, convey that which has been revealed to you from your Lord}. And he, peace and blessings be upon him, said on the Day of the Greater Hajj: 'Behold, let those present convey to those absent. Behold, have I conveyed?' repeating that, peace be upon him. And he, peace be upon him, said: 'Convey from me' or as he said. And Allah Most High says, and it is among the last revealed to him: {This day I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favor upon you and have approved for you Islam as religion}. And the Muhammadan community, and we are among it, says: 'O Allah, indeed Your Prophet, our master Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, conveyed the message, fulfilled the trust, advised the community, and strove in Your path with true striving until certainty came to him. So reward him, O Allah, with the best reward You have given any prophet for his community.'
Source pages p. 82

Commentary
This passage defines the attribute of conveying (at-tabligh) for the prophets. The author explains that it means delivering the legal rulings they were commanded to convey to their nations. The believer must hold the belief that the prophets fully delivered what they were ordered. The proof is from the Qur'an (5:67) where Allah commands the Prophet to convey. Then the author cites multiple hadiths: on the Day of the Greater Hajj the Prophet asked 'Have I conveyed?' and instructed those present to inform the absent. Also the command 'Convey from me.' The author then notes the verse (5:3) about perfection of religion, indicating that the message was completely conveyed. Finally, the author quotes the traditional supplication of the community acknowledging that the Prophet conveyed the message, fulfilled the trust, advised the community, and strove until certainty.

Key Terms
التبليغ
الرسالة
الأمانة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Conveying only means speaking the words aloud.
Correction: Conveying requires ensuring the entire message reaches the community, as shown by the Prophet's repeated emphasis and the verse on completion of religion.

Checkpoint
What is the definition of 'tabligh' (conveying) according to the author?
A) Speaking eloquently to the people.
B) Delivering the legal rulings commanded to be conveyed.
C) Performing miracles to prove prophethood.
D) Advising the community in general.

Key Takeaway
Belief in the prophets' perfect conveyance of divine rulings is an essential part of faith, supported by clear scriptural evidence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the supplication 'اللهم إن نبيك بلغ الرسالة وأدى الأمانة', do you truly feel the weight of the Prophet's commitment? What specific command from Allah are you neglecting to implement, knowing that it has been fully delivered to you?
?
The Prophet repeatedly asked 'ألا هل بلغت' to confirm his delivery. If you were asked today to testify that you have conveyed the message of Islam to someone under your care (family, friend, neighbor), what honest answer would you give?
?
Belief in the prophets' perfect delivery means acknowledging that every ruling in the Quran and Sunnah was meant for you. Is there a law of Allah that you find burdensome or dismiss as outdated? How does the certainty that the Prophet conveyed it perfectly challenge that attitude?

Why Prophets Must Be Intelligent
Author defines al-fatanah and explains its necessity.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why it is obligatory to believe that the prophets possess intelligence (al-fatanah) and how this attribute is essential for their mission.

Core Idea
Prophets are intelligent by necessity: to prove truth and guide us.

The author now turns to a key attribute of the prophets: intelligence. He explains not only what it means but also why it is a required belief for all Muslims. This passage grounds the necessity of prophetic intelligence in two clear functions.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the pronunciation note on 'al-fatanah'.
2
Understand the definition
The author defines al-fatanah as 'al-tafattun', which is intelligence and perception of subtle matters. Grasp what this means.
3
Identify the two reasons
The passage gives two reasons why prophets must be intelligent. Find them and see how they connect to the prophetic mission.
These reasons show that intelligence is not optional but necessary.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(والفطانة) بفتح الفاء، وهي التفطن، وهو الذكاء وإدراك الأمور الدقيقة، فيجب في حقهم علينا أن نعتقد أنهم متصفون بالفطانة، لأنهم بعثوا لإقامة الحجج، وإبطال شبه المخالفين، ولأنا مأمورون بالاقتداء بهم في الأقوال والأفعال.
(And intelligence) with a fatha on the fa, and it is perspicacity, which is intelligence and perception of subtle matters. So it is obligatory concerning them upon us that we believe that they are characterized by intelligence, because they were sent to establish proofs and to invalidate the doubts of opponents, and because we are commanded to follow them in statements and actions.
Source pages p. 83

Commentary
This passage defines the attribute of intelligence (al-fatanah) for the prophets. The author notes the pronunciation: with a fatha on the fa (فَطَانة). He defines it as perspicacity (tafattuun), which is intelligence and perception of subtle matters. It is obligatory for believers to believe that the prophets are characterized by intelligence. Two reasons are given: first, prophets were sent to establish proofs (hujaj) and to invalidate the doubts of opponents, which requires sharp intellect; second, we are commanded to emulate them in statements and actions, so they must be intelligent to serve as proper role models.

Key Terms
الفطانة
التفطن
الذكاء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Intelligence (fatanah) is just worldly cleverness or cunning.
Correction: Rather, it is perspicacity and the ability to perceive subtle truths and refute arguments, which is essential for the prophetic mission.

Checkpoint
Why is it necessary for prophets to possess intelligence (fatanah)?
A) To perform miracles.
B) To establish proofs and refute doubts of opponents.
C) To be wealthy and influential.
D) To know the unseen.

Key Takeaway
Prophets must be intelligent because they are sent to establish proofs and to be followed in all statements and actions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly follow the Prophet in your daily speech and actions, or do you only follow when it is convenient? What subtle matters have you failed to perceive that would bring you closer to his example?
?
The prophets were sent to invalidate doubts. What doubts do you still harbor that contradict the clear proofs in the Quran and Sunnah? Will you confront them today with an honest heart?
?
If the Prophet's intelligence is necessary for establishing proofs, how can you ignore or neglect the proofs that have been established for you? Are you actively seeking to understand them and act upon them?

The Conclusion: Prophets Are Free from Anything Contrary to Dignity
Author concludes that prophets must be free from everything contrary to dignity.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the final conclusion regarding the perfection of prophets: they must be free from anything that undermines dignity and from any deficiency in their ranks.

Core Idea
Prophets must be free from everything contrary to dignity.

This brief concluding passage summarizes the entire discussion on the attributes of prophets. After considering various defects and imperfections, the author asserts that the necessary belief is that all prophets and messengers are free from anything that undermines dignity (muru'a) and from anything that leads to deficiency in their lofty ranks. This principle covers all aspects of prophetic character.

Guided Path
1
Read the concluding passage
Read the Arabic passage once and its translation. Notice how the author uses 'obligatory' (يجب) to indicate a required belief.
2
Identify the two key concepts
The passage mentions 'free from everything that undermines dignity' and 'free from everything that leads to deficiency.' Comprehend what tanzeeh and muru'a mean in this context.
Tanzeeh means declaring transcendence or freedom from imperfections.
3
Reflect on the scope
Consider why the author includes both 'undermines dignity' and 'leads to deficiency.' This covers every possible imperfection that could contradict prophetic status.
This conclusion applies to all prophets and messengers collectively.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والحاصل أنه يجب أن يعتقد في حق الأنبياء والرسل كلهم التنزيه عن كل ما يخل بالمروءة، وكل ما يؤدي إلى نقص في مراتبهم العلية عليهم الصلاة والسلام.
And the conclusion is that it is necessary to believe concerning all prophets and messengers their being free from everything that undermines dignity, and everything that leads to deficiency in their lofty ranks, peace and blessings be upon them.
Source pages p. 83

Commentary
This concluding passage summarizes the preceding discussions. The author states that the essential belief regarding all prophets and messengers is that they are free (tanzeeh) from anything that undermines dignity (muru'a) and from anything that leads to deficiency in their lofty ranks. This includes all the attributes discussed—truthfulness, trustworthiness, conveying, intelligence—and extends to every aspect of their character. The phrase 'free from everything that undermines dignity' indicates that prophets must possess moral perfection and must not do anything that would lower their standing or credibility in the eyes of their communities, as that would contradict their purpose as role models and conveyors of divine messages.

Key Terms
التنزيه
المروءة
النقص

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think muru'a only means external decency or etiquette.
Correction: Muru'a encompasses all actions and traits that affect a person's respect and dignity in society; for prophets, it covers every moral and behavioral perfection.

Checkpoint
What must be believed about prophets according to the concluding statement?
A) They are free from all sins.
B) They are free from everything that undermines dignity and leads to deficiency.
C) They are infallible in all actions.
D) They possess all perfections except knowledge of the unseen.

Key Takeaway
The essential belief is that prophets are free from anything that undermines dignity and from any deficiency in their ranks.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believe that prophets are free from everything that undermines dignity, what does that mean for how you view their actions and teachings? Do you ever allow yourself to think disrespectfully about any of them? How can you guard your heart against that?
?
The author says it is 'necessary' to believe this. Is this belief merely intellectual for you, or does it change how you follow the Prophet's example? In what area of your life do you most need to elevate your respect for the Prophet's dignity?
?
Consider your own character: Are there things you do that undermine your own dignity as a follower of the prophets? What would it take for you to align your conduct with the noble character they exemplified?

The Four Obligatory Attributes of the Prophets
The author enumerates the four obligatory attributes of prophets and provides proofs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the four obligatory attributes of the prophets and the evidence supporting them.

Core Idea
Prophets must possess trustworthiness, truthfulness, delivery, and intelligence.

This passage from al-Bahja al-Saniyya establishes the core attributes that every prophet must possess. Understanding these attributes is essential for correct belief about the messengers. The author not only lists them but also provides scriptural and rational proofs.

Guided Path
1
Read the matn line
Start by reading the opening verse that commands us to describe all messengers with the four attributes: amānah, ṣidq, tablīgh, and faṭānah.
This sets the foundation for the entire discussion.
2
Examine the proof for tablīgh
The sharh provides evidence for the attribute of delivery: the Prophet's hadith at the Greater Hajj, the command 'Convey from me', and the Quranic verse of perfection of religion.
These proofs show that the prophets must convey the message fully.
3
Understand faṭānah and the conclusion
The author defines faṭānah as intelligence and explains why it is obligatory: prophets were sent to establish proofs and be emulated. The passage concludes with the necessity of believing they are free from anything violating dignity.
This connects to the broader principle of tanzih.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وَصِفْ جَمِيعَ الرُّسُلِ بِالأَمَانَهُ * وَالصِّدْقِ وَالتَّبْلِيغِ وَالْفَطَانَةِ

قَالَ الشَّارِحُ: وَقَدْ قَالَ عَلَيْهِ الصَّلَاةُ وَالسَّلَامُ يَوْمَ الْحَجِّ الْأَكْبَرِ: «أَلَا فَلْيُبَلِّغِ الشَّاهِدُ مِنْكُمُ الْغَائِبَ، أَلَا هَلْ بَلَّغْتُ» يُكَرِّرُ عَلَيْهِ الصَّلَاةُ وَالسَّلَامُ ذَلِكَ. وَيَقُولُ عَلَيْهِ الصَّلَاةُ وَالسَّلَامُ: «بَلِّغُوا عَنِّي» أَوْ كَمَا قَالَ. وَيَقُولُ اللهُ تَعَالَى وَهُوَ مِنْ آخِرِ مَا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ: {الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْإِسْلَامَ دِينًا}. وَتَقُولُ الْأُمَّةُ الْمُحَمَّدِيَّةُ، وَنَحْنُ مِنْهَا: «اللَّهُمَّ إِنَّ نَبِيَّكَ سَيِّدَنَا مُحَمَّدًا صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ بَلَّغَ الرِّسَالَةَ، وَأَدَّى الْأَمَانَةَ، وَنَصَحَ الْأُمَّةَ، وَجَاهَدَ فِي سَبِيلِكَ حَقَّ الْجِهَادِ حَتَّى أَتَاهُ الْيَقِينُ، فَجَزَاهُ اللهُ عَنَّا أَفْضَلَ مَا جَازَى نَبِيًّا عَنْ أُمَّتِهِ». وَالْفَطَانَةُ بِفَتْحِ الْفَاءِ، وَهِيَ التَّفَطُّنُ، وَهُوَ الذَّكَاءُ وَإِدْرَاكُ الْأُمُورِ الدَّقِيقَةِ، فَيَجِبُ فِي حَقِّهِمْ عَلَيْنَا أَنْ نَعْتَقِدَ أَنَّهُمْ مُتَّصِفُونَ بِالْفَطَانَةِ، لِأَنَّهُمْ بُعِثُوا لِإِقَامَةِ الْحُجَجِ، وَإِبْطَالِ شُبَهِ الْمُخَالِفِينَ، وَلِأَنَّا مَأْمُورُونَ بِالِاقْتِدَاءِ بِهِمْ فِي الْأَقْوَالِ وَالْأَفْعَالِ. وَالْحَاصِلُ أَنَّهُ يَجِبُ أَنْ يُعْتَقَدَ فِي حَقِّ الْأَنْبِيَاءِ وَالرُّسُلِ كُلِّهِمُ التَّنْزِيهُ عَنْ كُلِّ مَا يُخِلُّ بِالْمُرُوءَةِ، وَكُلِّ مَا يُؤَدِّي إِلَى نَقْصٍ فِي مَرَاتِبِهِمُ الْعَلِيَّةِ.
And describe all of the messengers with trustworthiness, truthfulness, delivery, and intelligence. The commentator said: The Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, said on the day of the Greater Hajj: 'Behold, let the present convey to the absent. Behold, have I conveyed?' He repeated that, peace and blessings be upon him. And he said, peace and blessings be upon him: 'Convey from me,' or as he said. And God Almighty said, and it is among the last revealed to him: 'Today I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favor upon you and have approved for you Islam as religion.' And the Muhammadan community, and we are among it, says: 'O God, indeed Your Prophet, our master Muhammad, may God bless him and grant him peace, conveyed the message, fulfilled the trust, advised the community, and strove in Your cause with true striving until certainty came to him. May God reward him on our behalf with the best reward He has given any prophet on behalf of his community.' And 'al-faṭānah' with fatha of the fa', and it is 'al-tafaṭṭun', which is intelligence and perception of subtle matters. So it is obligatory for us, regarding them, to believe that they are characterized by intelligence, because they were sent to establish proofs and invalidate the doubts of opponents, and because we are commanded to emulate them in words and deeds. The conclusion is that it is obligatory to believe regarding all prophets and messengers that they are free from everything that violates dignity and everything that leads to a deficiency in their lofty ranks.
Source pages p. 83

Commentary
This passage is a definition and proof of the four obligatory attributes of prophets. The author begins with the matn line commanding us to describe all messengers with trustworthiness (amānah), truthfulness (ṣidq), delivery (tablīgh), and intelligence (faṭānah). The sharh then provides evidence: for tablīgh, the Prophet's hadith at the Greater Hajj and the command 'Convey from me', as well as the Quranic verse of perfection of religion. He then cites the supplication of the Muhammadan community affirming that the Prophet fulfilled these attributes. The author defines faṭānah as intelligence and perception of subtle matters, and explains that it is obligatory to believe in this attribute because prophets were sent to establish proofs and invalidate opponents' doubts, and because we are commanded to emulate them. The passage concludes that we must believe in the prophets' freedom from anything that violates murū'ah (dignity) or leads to deficiency in their lofty ranks.

Key Terms
أمانة
صدق
تبليغ
فطانة
مروءة
تنزيه

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think prophets are not required to have these attributes, or that they could lack intelligence or trustworthiness.
Correction: The passage shows that these attributes are obligatory based on revelation and reason; prophets must possess them to fulfill their mission.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is NOT one of the four obligatory attributes of prophets mentioned in the passage?
A) al-Amānah (trustworthiness)
B) al-Ṣidq (truthfulness)
C) al-Shajāʿah (courage)
D) al-Faṭānah (intelligence)

Key Takeaway
Prophets must be characterized by trustworthiness, truthfulness, delivery, and intelligence, and must be free from anything that violates dignity or diminishes their rank.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The Prophet repeatedly asked 'Have I conveyed?' on the day of the Greater Hajj. What message has God entrusted you to convey to those around you that you have been avoiding? What one specific action will you take this week to deliver that trust?
?
The passage says prophets are free from anything that violates dignity (murūʾah). Reflect on a time you compromised your dignity for worldly gain or approval. How can the prophets' example of complete moral integrity inspire you to make a different choice next time?
?
Prophets used their intelligence to establish proofs against falsehood. In what area of your life (belief, habit, relationship) are you currently ignoring rational evidence or failing to confront a falsehood? What step will you take today to use your intellect to align with truth?

Prophethood

The Impossible Opposites of Prophetic Attributes
Enumeration of four impossible prophetic qualities as opposites of the wajib attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the four impossible attributes for prophets – the opposites of trustworthiness, truthfulness, delivery, and intelligence – and why they are impossible.

Core Idea
Prophets are protected from every opposite of their essential attributes.

Now that we know the four attributes that prophets must have, we need to understand what they are protected from. The author lists the exact opposites that can never occur in a prophet. These are not just undesirable traits but rational impossibilities given their mission.

Guided Path
1
Review the four wajib attributes
Recall that prophets must have trustworthiness (amanah), truthfulness (sidq), delivery (tabligh), and intelligence (fatanah). This lesson covers their opposites.
If you haven't studied the wajib attributes, do that first.
2
Read the passage on impossible opposites
Read the Arabic and translation carefully. The author states each opposite and its corresponding wajib attribute.
Notice the explicit pairing: khiyanah is opposite of amanah, etc.
3
Understand why these are impossible
Reflect on why a prophet cannot lie, betray, conceal revelation, or be stupid. These would contradict their role as guides.
The commentary explains the rational basis.
4
Test your knowledge with the checkpoint
Answer the multiple choice question to see if you can identify one of these impossibilities.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وَيَسْتَحِيلُ ضِدُّهَا عَلَيْهِمْ * وَجَائِزٌ كَالْأَكْلِ فِي حَقِّهِمْ

ثُمَّ شَرَعَ يُبَيِّنُ مَا يَسْتَحِيلُ فَقَالَ: (وَيَسْتَحِيلُ ضِدُّهَا) أَيْ ضِدُّ هَذِهِ الصِّفَاتِ الْأَرْبَعَةِ الْمُتَقَدِّمَةِ (عَلَيْهِمْ). فَيَسْتَحِيلُ فِي حَقِّهِمُ الْخِيَانَةُ بِفِعْلٍ مَنْهِىٍّ عَنْهُ، وَهِيَ ضِدُّ الْأَمَانَةِ. وَيَسْتَحِيلُ أَيْضًا الْكَذِبُ وَهِيَ ضِدُّ الصِّدْقِ. وَكِتْمَانُ شَيْءٍ مِمَّا أُمِرُوا بِتَبْلِيغِهِ، وَهُوَ ضِدُّ التَّبْلِيغِ. وَكَذَا يَمْتَنِعُ مِنْهُمُ الْغَفْلَةُ وَالْبَلَاهَةُ، وَهُوَ ضِدُّ الْفَطَانَةِ. ثُمَّ أَرَادَ أَنْ يَشْرَعَ فِي الْقِسْمِ الثَّالِثِ، وَهُوَ الْجَائِزُ فَقَالَ: (وَجَائِزٌ) الْجَائِزُ فِي حَقِّ الرُّسُلِ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ الْأَعْرَاضِ الْبَشَرِيَّةِ مَا لَا يُؤَدِّي إِلَى نَقْصٍ فِي مَرَاتِبِهِمُ الْعَلِيَّةِ، عَلَيْهِمُ السَّلَامُ، بِأَنْ لَا يَكُونَ مَنْهِىًّا عَنْهُ، وَلَا مِنَ الْمُبَاحِ مَا يُزْرِي، وَلَا مِنَ الْأَمْرَاضِ مَا هُوَ مُزْمِنٌ، أَوْ تَعَافُهُ النُّفُوسُ، كَالْجُذَامِ وَالْبَرَصِ. وَأَخْبَرَ عَنِ الْجَائِزِ الَّذِي لَا يُسْتَغْنَى عَنْهُ عَادَةً بَنُو آدَمَ (كَالْأَكْلِ) أَيِ: الْأَكْلِ، وَمِثْلُهُ فِي حَقِّهِمْ، أَيِ: الشُّرْبُ وَالنَّوْمُ، وَكَذَا مَا يُسْتَغْنَى عَنْهُ كَالتَّفَكُّهِ بِنَحْوِ أَكْلِ الْفَوَاكِهِ، وَالنِّكَاحِ وَنَحْوِ ذَلِكَ، فَلَأَنَّهُ مِنَ الْجَائِزَاتِ فِي حَقِّهِمْ. وَمَا يَنْزِلُ بِهِمْ مِنْ بَعْضِ الْأَمْرَاضِ غَيْرِ الْمُنَفِّرَةِ إِنَّمَا هُوَ لِتَعْظِيمِ أُجُورِهِمْ، وَرَفْعِ دَرَجَاتِهِمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ عَزَّ وَجَلَّ، وَلِلتَّشْرِيعِ كَالسَّهْوِ مِنْهُ صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ فِي ... أَنْ تَأْنَفَ النَّفْسُ مِنَ اتِّبَاعِهِ وَالِاقْتِدَاءِ بِهِ، وَلِذَا كَانُوا مُنَزَّهِينَ عَنْ كُلِّ مَا يُخِلُّ بِالْمُرُوءَةِ، وَكُلِّ مَا يُؤَدِّي إِلَى نَقْصٍ فِي مَرَاتِبِهِمُ الْعَلِيَّةِ عَلَيْهِمْ صَلَوَاتُ اللهِ وَسَلَامُهُ.
And their opposite is impossible upon them, and permissible is like eating in their regard. Then he began to explain what is impossible and said: 'And their opposite is impossible' i.e., the opposite of these four preceding attributes (upon them). So treachery by a prohibited act is impossible in their regard, and it is the opposite of trustworthiness. Also lying is impossible, and it is the opposite of truthfulness. And concealing something of what they were commanded to deliver is impossible, and it is the opposite of delivery. Likewise, heedlessness and stupidity are prevented from them, and that is the opposite of intelligence. Then he intended to commence the third division, which is the permissible, and said: 'And permissible' — the permissible in the regard of the messengers, upon them, from human attributes is that which does not lead to deficiency in their lofty ranks, peace be upon them, such that it is not prohibited, nor from the permissible what is degrading, nor from diseases what is chronic or that souls loathe, like leprosy and vitiligo. And he informed about the permissible which the children of Adam cannot ordinarily do without (like eating), i.e., eating, and the like in their regard, i.e., drinking and sleeping, and also what can be done without, like enjoying by eating fruits, marriage and the like, for it is among the permissible things in their regard. And what befalls them of some non-repulsive diseases is only to magnify their rewards and raise their ranks with God Almighty, and for legislation, like forgetfulness from him, may God bless him and grant him peace, in ... lest the soul be repelled from following him and emulating him. Therefore, they were free from everything that violates dignity and everything that leads to a deficiency in their lofty ranks, upon them be the blessings and peace of God.
Source pages p. 84

Commentary
This passage is a classification of prophetic attributes into impossible (mustaḥīl) and possible (jāʾiz). The author first defines mustaḥīl: the opposites of the four wajib attributes. These opposites are treachery (against amānah), lying (against ṣidq), concealment (against tablīgh), and heedlessness/stupidity (against faṭānah). For each, he states that it is impossible for prophets. Then he moves to the jāʾiz category: human characteristics that do not entail deficiency in their lofty status. The permissible includes actions that are not prohibited, not degrading permissible acts, and diseases that are not chronic or repulsive like leprosy. Examples of necessary permissible acts are eating, drinking, sleeping; optional ones include enjoying fruits, marriage. The author adds that non-repulsive diseases afflicting prophets are for increasing rewards and for legislation (e.g., the Prophet's forgetfulness in prayer). The passage concludes by reiterating that prophets are free from anything contrary to dignity or that would cause deficiency.

Key Terms
يستحيل
ضد
خيانة
كذب
كتمان
غفلة
بلاهة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think prophets could commit minor sins like lying for a good cause.
Correction: This passage clearly states that lying (kidhb) is impossible for prophets, regardless of intention.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, which of the following is impossible for prophets?
A) Eating food
B) Being heedless (ghaflah)
C) Suffering from a non-repulsive disease
D) Getting married

Key Takeaway
Prophets are immune to the four opposites of their necessary attributes: treachery, lying, concealment, and stupidity.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Think of a time when you were entrusted with a secret or a responsibility. Did you ever betray that trust, even slightly? The prophets were absolutely free from treachery. What would it take for you to become someone who can be trusted completely?
?
Have you ever been in a situation where it was easier to lie than to tell the truth? The prophets never lied, not even for a good cause. Are you willing to commit to truthfulness in all your words, no matter the cost?
?
When you fail to deliver a message or task you were assigned, do you make excuses? The prophets could not conceal anything they were commanded to deliver. What message has Allah entrusted you to convey, and are you being faithful in delivering it?

Permissible Human Traits for Prophets
Definition and examples of permissible (jāʾiz) human attributes for prophets with restrictions.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand which human traits are permissible for prophets and the conditions that limit them, such as not causing deficiency or repulsion.

Core Idea
Prophets share permissible human traits, but only those that preserve their dignity.

Prophets are human, so they naturally have human needs and characteristics. However, not all human traits are permissible for them. The author defines the permissible (jāʾiz) with conditions: it must not be prohibited, degrading, or involve repulsive diseases. This lesson explains those conditions and gives examples.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on permissible traits
Read the Arabic and translation starting from 'وَجَائِزٌ'. Note the definition and conditions.
2
Identify the conditions for permissible traits
The author lists three negative conditions: not prohibited, not degrading from the mubah, and not chronic/repulsive diseases.
Understanding these conditions is key.
3
Examine the examples
Examples include eating, drinking, sleeping (necessary), and optional things like marriage. Note the diseases mentioned as repulsive.
4
Reflect on the purpose of afflictions
The author mentions that non-repulsive diseases afflict prophets for higher rewards and legislation, not as punishment.
This clarifies a common confusion.
5
Test your understanding
Answer the checkpoint to confirm you can distinguish permissible from impermissible traits.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وَيَسْتَحِيلُ ضِدُّهَا عَلَيْهِمْ * وَجَائِزٌ كَالْأَكْلِ فِي حَقِّهِمْ

ثُمَّ شَرَعَ يُبَيِّنُ مَا يَسْتَحِيلُ فَقَالَ: (وَيَسْتَحِيلُ ضِدُّهَا) أَيْ ضِدُّ هَذِهِ الصِّفَاتِ الْأَرْبَعَةِ الْمُتَقَدِّمَةِ (عَلَيْهِمْ). فَيَسْتَحِيلُ فِي حَقِّهِمُ الْخِيَانَةُ بِفِعْلٍ مَنْهِىٍّ عَنْهُ، وَهِيَ ضِدُّ الْأَمَانَةِ. وَيَسْتَحِيلُ أَيْضًا الْكَذِبُ وَهِيَ ضِدُّ الصِّدْقِ. وَكِتْمَانُ شَيْءٍ مِمَّا أُمِرُوا بِتَبْلِيغِهِ، وَهُوَ ضِدُّ التَّبْلِيغِ. وَكَذَا يَمْتَنِعُ مِنْهُمُ الْغَفْلَةُ وَالْبَلَاهَةُ، وَهُوَ ضِدُّ الْفَطَانَةِ. ثُمَّ أَرَادَ أَنْ يَشْرَعَ فِي الْقِسْمِ الثَّالِثِ، وَهُوَ الْجَائِزُ فَقَالَ: (وَجَائِزٌ) الْجَائِزُ فِي حَقِّ الرُّسُلِ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ الْأَعْرَاضِ الْبَشَرِيَّةِ مَا لَا يُؤَدِّي إِلَى نَقْصٍ فِي مَرَاتِبِهِمُ الْعَلِيَّةِ، عَلَيْهِمُ السَّلَامُ، بِأَنْ لَا يَكُونَ مَنْهِىًّا عَنْهُ، وَلَا مِنَ الْمُبَاحِ مَا يُزْرِي، وَلَا مِنَ الْأَمْرَاضِ مَا هُوَ مُزْمِنٌ، أَوْ تَعَافُهُ النُّفُوسُ، كَالْجُذَامِ وَالْبَرَصِ. وَأَخْبَرَ عَنِ الْجَائِزِ الَّذِي لَا يُسْتَغْنَى عَنْهُ عَادَةً بَنُو آدَمَ (كَالْأَكْلِ) أَيِ: الْأَكْلِ، وَمِثْلُهُ فِي حَقِّهِمْ، أَيِ: الشُّرْبُ وَالنَّوْمُ، وَكَذَا مَا يُسْتَغْنَى عَنْهُ كَالتَّفَكُّهِ بِنَحْوِ أَكْلِ الْفَوَاكِهِ، وَالنِّكَاحِ وَنَحْوِ ذَلِكَ، فَلَأَنَّهُ مِنَ الْجَائِزَاتِ فِي حَقِّهِمْ. وَمَا يَنْزِلُ بِهِمْ مِنْ بَعْضِ الْأَمْرَاضِ غَيْرِ الْمُنَفِّرَةِ إِنَّمَا هُوَ لِتَعْظِيمِ أُجُورِهِمْ، وَرَفْعِ دَرَجَاتِهِمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ عَزَّ وَجَلَّ، وَلِلتَّشْرِيعِ كَالسَّهْوِ مِنْهُ صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ فِي ... أَنْ تَأْنَفَ النَّفْسُ مِنَ اتِّبَاعِهِ وَالِاقْتِدَاءِ بِهِ، وَلِذَا كَانُوا مُنَزَّهِينَ عَنْ كُلِّ مَا يُخِلُّ بِالْمُرُوءَةِ، وَكُلِّ مَا يُؤَدِّي إِلَى نَقْصٍ فِي مَرَاتِبِهِمُ الْعَلِيَّةِ عَلَيْهِمْ صَلَوَاتُ اللهِ وَسَلَامُهُ.
And their opposite is impossible upon them, and permissible is like eating in their regard. Then he began to explain what is impossible and said: 'And their opposite is impossible' i.e., the opposite of these four preceding attributes (upon them). So treachery by a prohibited act is impossible in their regard, and it is the opposite of trustworthiness. Also lying is impossible, and it is the opposite of truthfulness. And concealing something of what they were commanded to deliver is impossible, and it is the opposite of delivery. Likewise, heedlessness and stupidity are prevented from them, and that is the opposite of intelligence. Then he intended to commence the third division, which is the permissible, and said: 'And permissible' — the permissible in the regard of the messengers, upon them, from human attributes is that which does not lead to deficiency in their lofty ranks, peace be upon them, such that it is not prohibited, nor from the permissible what is degrading, nor from diseases what is chronic or that souls loathe, like leprosy and vitiligo. And he informed about the permissible which the children of Adam cannot ordinarily do without (like eating), i.e., eating, and the like in their regard, i.e., drinking and sleeping, and also what can be done without, like enjoying by eating fruits, marriage and the like, for it is among the permissible things in their regard. And what befalls them of some non-repulsive diseases is only to magnify their rewards and raise their ranks with God Almighty, and for legislation, like forgetfulness from him, may God bless him and grant him peace, in ... lest the soul be repelled from following him and emulating him. Therefore, they were free from everything that violates dignity and everything that leads to a deficiency in their lofty ranks, upon them be the blessings and peace of God.
Source pages p. 84

Commentary
This passage is a classification of prophetic attributes into impossible (mustaḥīl) and possible (jāʾiz). The author first defines mustaḥīl: the opposites of the four wajib attributes. These opposites are treachery (against amānah), lying (against ṣidq), concealment (against tablīgh), and heedlessness/stupidity (against faṭānah). For each, he states that it is impossible for prophets. Then he moves to the jāʾiz category: human characteristics that do not entail deficiency in their lofty status. The permissible includes actions that are not prohibited, not degrading permissible acts, and diseases that are not chronic or repulsive like leprosy. Examples of necessary permissible acts are eating, drinking, sleeping; optional ones include enjoying fruits, marriage. The author adds that non-repulsive diseases afflicting prophets are for increasing rewards and for legislation (e.g., the Prophet's forgetfulness in prayer). The passage concludes by reiterating that prophets are free from anything contrary to dignity or that would cause deficiency.

Key Terms
جائز
أكل
شرب
نوم
أمراض
مروءة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think prophets cannot suffer any disease at all.
Correction: The passage states that non-repulsive, non-chronic diseases are permissible and even beneficial for increasing rewards.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is NOT considered a permissible trait for prophets according to the passage?
A) Eating and drinking
B) Sleeping
C) Suffering from leprosy
D) Marriage

Key Takeaway
Prophets share permissible human traits as long as they do not degrade their status or involve repulsive diseases.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You claim to follow the Prophet ﷺ, but do you honor his dignity by avoiding degrading actions in your own life? What is one undignified habit you will abandon this week as a mark of respect for his station?
?
The prophets endured non-repulsive illnesses for greater reward. When you fall ill, do you see it as a chance to earn reward or do you complain? Will you train yourself to say 'Alhamdulillah' and seek reward from the first moment of your next sickness?
?
You eat, drink, and sleep just as the prophets did. But are your daily routines performed with intention and gratitude, or are they mere habits? Pick one sunnah related to eating, drinking, or sleeping and commit to practicing it consistently for the next 30 days.

The Impossible Attributes for Prophets
Enumeration of the four impossible attributes for prophets

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the four impossible attributes that prophets cannot possess and why they are the opposites of their necessary attributes.

Core Idea
Prophets are protected from treachery, lying, concealment, and heedlessness — these are impossible.

The commentator explains that just as prophets must have certain necessary attributes, their opposites are absolutely impossible. This lesson defines each of the four impossible attributes, grounding the doctrine of prophetic infallibility ('isma) in clear terms.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic text and its translation to get the full list of impossible attributes and their connections to the necessary ones.
Pay attention to how each opposite is defined briefly.
2
Identify the opposite pairs
For each necessary attribute mentioned (trustworthiness, truthfulness, delivery, intelligence), find its impossible opposite as stated in the passage.
There are four pairs: trustworthiness/treachery, truthfulness/lying, delivery/concealment, intelligence/heedlessness.
3
Understand the doctrine
Grasp that these impossibilities affirm prophetic protection from sin and error in conveying revelation, a key element of Islamic belief.
This is part of the 'isma (infallibility) of prophets.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويستحيل ضدها عليهم * وجائز كالأكل في حقهم | ٥٢
شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي
2
ثم شرع يبين ما يستحيل فقال: (ويستحيل ضدها أي ضد هذه المستحيل في الصفات الأربعة المتقدمة ( عليهم). فيستحيل في حقهم الخيانة بفعل منهي عنه، وهي ضد الأمانة. ويستحيل أيضًا الكذب وهي ضد الصدق. وكتمان شيء مما أمروا بتبليغه، وهو ضد التبليغ. وكذا يمتنع منهم الغفلة والبلاهة، وهو ضد الفطانة.
And impossible is their opposite * and permissible is like eating in their case. [Verse 52] Explanation by Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mishshāt al-Makkī al-Mālikī 2 Then he began to explain what is impossible, saying: (And impossible is their opposite) i.e., the opposite of these impossibles in the four preceding attributes (upon them). So treachery by committing a forbidden act is impossible in their case, and it is the opposite of trustworthiness. Lying is also impossible, and it is the opposite of truthfulness. And concealing something that they were commanded to convey, which is the opposite of delivery. Likewise, heedlessness and dullness are prevented from them, and they are the opposite of intelligence.
Source pages p. 84

Commentary
This passage is an enumeration and definition of the impossible attributes for prophets. The commentator explains the poetic line which states that the opposites of certain attributes are impossible for prophets. He identifies the four necessary attributes: amana (trustworthiness), sidq (truthfulness), tabligh (delivery of the message), and fatana (intelligence). Their opposites are impossible: khianah (treachery), kidhb (lying), kitman (concealment), and ghafla/bulaha (heedlessness/dullness). Each is explained briefly: khianah is any forbidden act, kitman is failure to convey what was commanded, etc. This establishes the doctrine of 'isma (infallibility) of prophets in these respects.

صدق

كذب
كتمان
غفلة
بلاهة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think prophets could occasionally commit minor sins like lying or heedlessness under duress.
Correction: This passage affirms the absolute impossibility of these four attributes for prophets, establishing their infallibility in trustworthiness, truthfulness, delivery, and intelligence.

Checkpoint
What is the opposite of the attribute of trustworthiness (amana) that is impossible for prophets?
A) Sidq
B) Khianah
C) Kitman
D) Ghafala

Key Takeaway
The four impossible attributes for prophets are treachery, lying, concealment, and heedlessness — the opposites of the four necessary attributes, affirming prophetic infallibility.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If prophets must be absolutely truthful in all matters, how does your own willingness to tell 'small' lies contradict the standard set by Allah's messengers? Will you commit to complete honesty for one week, starting today?
?
The prophets are protected even from minor forbidden acts (khianah). Where in your life have you betrayed a trust—even a small one—and excused it as insignificant? How can you make amends right now?
?
Heedlessness (ghafla) is completely impossible for prophets because of their perfect intelligence (fatana). In which parts of your prayer or worship do you allow your mind to wander? What concrete step will you take this week to remain fully present before Allah?

The Permissible Attributes for Prophets
Definition and examples of permissible attributes for prophets

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand what human traits are permissible for prophets and the conditions that preserve their dignity.

Core Idea
Prophets are human but protected from anything that diminishes their dignity.

The commentator now turns to the third category of attributes: the permissible. For prophets, this includes ordinary human needs like eating and sleeping, but with important conditions that preserve their lofty status. This lesson clarifies what is allowed and what is not, and why some afflictions befall them.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Notice how the commentator structures the definition and exceptions.
Pay attention to the phrase 'لا يؤدي إلى نقص'.
2
Identify the conditions for permissible traits
The passage lists three conditions: not prohibited, not undignified, not repulsive or chronic. Understand each condition and its rationale.
3
Note examples of permissible traits
Examples include eating, drinking, sleep, eating fruits, marriage. These are necessities or permissible enjoyments.
The commentator distinguishes between what is indispensable and what is not.
4
Understand the purpose of afflictions
Non-repulsive diseases increase reward. Forgetfulness serves legislation so people can follow. This explains why prophets experience hardships.
This connects to the idea of 'muru'a' or dignity.
5
Reflect on the concept of muru'a
Prophets are purified from anything that harms their dignity. The final line reminds of the world's lowly worth as comfort.
Consider how this shapes our view of prophets.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم أراد أن يشرع في القسم الثالث، وهو الجائز فقال: (وجائز) | الجائز في حق الرسل عليهم من الأعراض البشرية ما لا يؤدي إلى نقص في مراتبهم العلية، عليهم السلام بأن لا يكون منهيًّا عنه، ولا من المباح ما يزري، ولا من الأمراض ما هو مزمن، أو تعافه النفوس، كالجذام والبرص. وأخبر عن الجائز الذي لا يستغنى عنه عادة بنو آدم (كالأكل) أي: الأكل، ومثله في حقهم، أي: الشرب والنوم، وكذا ما يُستغنى عنه كالتفكه بنحو أكل الفواكه، والنكاح ونحو ذلك، فلأنه من الجائزات في حقهم.. و ما ينزل بهم من بعض الأمراض غير المنفرة إنما هو لتعظيم أجورهم، ورفع درجاتهم عند الله عَزَّوَجَلَّ، وللتشريع كالسهو منه صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ في - أن تأنفَ النَّفْسُ من اتباعه والاقتداء به، ولذا كانوا مُنَزِّهِين عن كل ما يخلُّ بالمروءة، وكل ما يؤدي إلى نقص في مراتبهم العليَّة عليهم صلوات الله وسلامه.

الصلاة والتسلي بأحوالهم إذ نزل بنا ما نزل بهم، والتنبيه على حقارة الدنيا وخسة قدرها عند الله تعالى.
Then he intended to begin the third category, which is the permissible, and said: (And permissible is like eating) The permissible in the right of the messengers, upon them, from human accidents that do not lead to deficiency in their lofty ranks, upon them peace, by not being prohibited, nor from the permissible what diminishes, nor from diseases what is chronic or what souls detest, such as leprosy and vitiligo. And he informed about the permissible that the sons of Adam cannot do without customarily: (like eating) i.e., eating, and likewise in their right, i.e., drinking and sleep, and likewise what can be done without such as enjoyment by way of eating fruits, and marriage and the like, for it is from the permissible in their right. And what befalls them of some non-repulsive diseases is only to magnify their rewards and raise their degrees with Allah Almighty, and for legislation such as forgetfulness from him صلى الله عليه وسلم in - so that the soul would not disdain following him and emulating him. And therefore they were purified from everything that detracts from manliness (muru'a) and everything that leads to deficiency in their lofty ranks, upon them the blessings and peace of Allah. Prayer and seeking solace in their states when what befell them befalls us, and a reminder of the insignificance of the world and its lowly worth with Allah Almighty.
Source pages p. 84

Commentary
This passage defines the category of permissible (ja'iz) for prophets. The commentator explains that permissible human traits are those that do not degrade their status: they must not be forbidden acts, nor undignified permissible acts, nor chronic or repulsive diseases. Examples include eating, drinking, sleep, and even enjoyable things like eating fruit and marriage. Some diseases that are not repulsive can befall them to increase their reward. Even forgetfulness from the Prophet (e.g., in prayer) is for legislation so that people do not shy away from following him. The prophets are purified from anything that harms their dignity (muru'a). The final sentence notes that reflecting on their trials provides comfort and reminds of the world's lowly worth.

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think all human actions are permissible for prophets without restriction.
Correction: This passage clarifies that even permissible actions must be dignified and not repulsive, preserving the prophets' lofty status.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, which of the following is permissible for prophets?
A) Betrayal (khianah)
B) Chronic leprosy
C) Eating and drinking
D) Lying

Key Takeaway
Prophets are permitted human traits that do not compromise their dignity, and even afflictions serve a higher purpose.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever excuse your own disrespectful or undignified behavior by saying 'I'm only human'? How does this lesson's emphasis on prophets being human yet perfectly dignified challenge that excuse? What one change will you make today to uphold dignity in your actions?
?
When you face a trial like illness or discomfort, do you instinctively see it as punishment or inconvenience? How can you reorient your heart to see it as a potential means of reward, just as the prophets' afflictions were for elevation?
?
Prophets were protected from anything that might make people disdain following them. Is there any habit in your life that, even if 'permissible,' damages your credibility as a follower of the Prophet? What will you do to remove it?

The Sending of Messengers: Favor and Mercy, Not Obligation
Commentator explains that sending prophets is favor and mercy, not obligation

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the sending of prophets is a pure favor and mercy from Allah, not an obligation upon Him, based on His attribute as the sovereign free agent.

Core Idea
Allah sends prophets as a pure favor, not because He is obliged to.

In this passage, the commentator clarifies a fundamental point about prophethood: it is a gift from Allah, not a duty. Many ask whether Allah must send prophets; here we learn that His actions are free and unbound. The passage defines the purpose of prophets and reaffirms Allah's absolute sovereignty.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Start by reading the source text and its literal translation. Notice the emphasis on favor (tafaddul) and mercy (rahma).
The verse-like line is part of the original matn being explained.
2
Identify the key claim
The commentator states that sending prophets is 'pure favor' and 'mercy' and explicitly denies it is obligatory (wajib). Find these words in the Arabic.
Focus on the words تفضل, رحمة, واجب.
3
Understand the reason for obligation being denied
The commentator gives the reason: Allah is the free agent (al-fa'il al-mukhtar) who is not questioned about His actions. This refutes the Mu'tazila who claimed it is obligatory.
This ties the lesson to the broader topic of divine attributes.
4
Reflect on the praise of Allah
The passage ends by praising Allah as the Bestower (Mawli) of the greatest grace: faith and the sending of messengers. Note the supplication for steadfastness.
This is a moment of gratitude and worship, not just doctrine.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
إِرْسَالُهُم تَفَضُّلٌ وَرَحْمَهُ * للعالَمِينَ جَلَّ مُولِي النِّعْمَة
حد شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي
إرسالهم) أي: الأنبياء من البشر إلى الخلق من أبينا آدم إلى سيدنا محمد صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ بإدخال المبدأ أو الغاية (تفضل) (۱)، أي: محض تفضل وإحسان من الله الكريم، ليبلغوهم شريعة الله وما يحتاجون إليه، (ورحمة) منه (للعالمين قاطبة لا واجب، لما سبق لك من أن الله تعالى هو الفاعل المختار الذي لا يُسأل عما يفعل وهم يسألون. (جل) وعَظُم (مولي) بضم الميم، وكسر اللام، أي: معطي (النعمة) التي أجلها نعمة الإيمان، وبعثة الرسل عليهم الصلاة والسلام، فله الحمد والمنة على ذلك، وعلى كل حال ثبتنا الله على ذلك.
Their sending is a favor and mercy * for the worlds, majestic is the Bestower of grace. [Verse?] Limit? Explanation by Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mishshāt al-Makkī al-Mālikī (Their sending) i.e., the prophets from humankind to the creation from our father Adam to our master Muhammad صلى الله عليه وسلم, whether including the beginning or the end, (a favor) (1) i.e., pure favor and beneficence from the Generous God, so that they may convey to them the sharia of God and what they need, (and a mercy) from Him (for the worlds entirely, not an obligation, because of what has preceded for you that Allah Almighty is the sovereign agent who is not asked about what He does while they are asked. (Majestic) and exalted (the Bestower) with damm on the mim and kasr on the lam, i.e., the Giver (of grace) whose greatest is the grace of faith and the sending of the messengers, blessings and peace be upon them. To Him belongs praise and gratitude for that, and in all circumstances may Allah establish us upon that.
Source pages p. 85

Commentary
This passage is a definition and proof: the sending of prophets is a pure favor (tafaddul) and mercy (rahma) from Allah, not an obligation on Him. The commentator clarifies that the phrase 'their sending' refers to all prophets from Adam to Muhammad. He describes it as pure favor and beneficence, intended to convey God's sharia. He explicitly denies that it is wajib (obligatory), citing the principle that Allah is the free agent (al-fa'il al-mukhtar) who is not questioned about His actions. This refutes the Mu'tazila who claimed it is obligatory upon God to send prophets. Then he explains the word 'mawli' as the Giver of grace, and notes the greatest grace is faith and the sending of messengers. He ends with a supplication for steadfastness.

Key Terms
تفضل
رحمة
واجب
فاعل مختار
مولي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think Allah is obligated to send prophets (Mu'tazila view).
Correction: The passage asserts it is a favor and mercy, not an obligation, because Allah is the free agent.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, why is the sending of prophets not obligatory upon Allah?
A) Because prophets are not important
B) Because Allah is the free agent who is not questioned about His actions
C) Because it is impossible
D) Because people do not need prophets

Key Takeaway
Allah sends prophets as a pure favor and mercy, not because He is obliged, affirming His absolute free will.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the sending of prophets is a pure favor from Allah, not something He owed you, how does your heart respond to this unearned gift? Do you treat the guidance of the prophets as a burden or as a mercy designed for your own good? Pause now and express your gratitude to Allah for sending these messengers, especially Muhammad (peace be upon him).
?
The passage states that Allah is not questioned about what He does. How often do you find yourself questioning Allah's decrees or His ways, forgetting that He is the sovereign agent? What does this passage teach you about your proper position before Allah? Commit today to stop demanding answers and start trusting His wisdom.
?
If Allah's sending of prophets is mercy for the worlds, what practical steps can you take this week to show that you truly believe this? For example, how will you engage with the sunnah of the Prophet (peace be upon him) not as optional but as a mercy that leads to your salvation? Write down one concrete action you will take.

Prophethood
beginner
4 min
Sending Prophets: Favor, Not Obligation
The author explains that sending prophets is a favor and mercy, not obligatory, and refutes opposing groups.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the sending of prophets is a pure favor and mercy from Allah, not an obligation, and how this refutes opposing views.

Core Idea
Sending prophets is Allah's free gift, not His duty.

This lesson examines a foundational doctrine in Sunni theology: that Allah sends messengers not because He is obligated to, but as a pure favor and mercy. The passage explains this and refutes those who either obligate or deny prophethood.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once to get the flow, then read the translation. Notice the verse and the commentary that follows.
The verse is the matn; the rest is the sharh.
2
Understand key terms
Focus on the terms tafaḍḍul (favor), raḥmah (mercy), and wājib (obligation). Ask yourself why the author emphasizes that it is not obligatory.
This is the core of the lesson.
3
Identify the refutations
The footnote mentions two groups: the Mu'tazila who obligate sending prophets, and the Sumaniyya and Barahima who deny it. Understand how the passage refutes both.
This connects the doctrine to broader theological debates.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
إِرْسَالُهُم تَفَضُّلٌ وَرَحْمَهُ * للعالَمِينَ جَلَّ مُولِي النِّعْمَة. حد شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي: الصلاة والتسلي بأحوالهم إذ نزل بنا ما نزل بهم، والتنبيه على حقارة الدنيا وخسة قدرها عند الله تعالى. إرسالهم) أي: الأنبياء من البشر إلى الخلق من أبينا آدم إلى سيدنا محمد صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ بإدخال المبدأ أو الغاية (تفضل) (۱)، أي: محض تفضل وإحسان من الله الكريم، ليبلغوهم شريعة الله وما يحتاجون إليه، (ورحمة) منه (للعالمين قاطبة لا واجب، لما سبق لك من أن الله تعالى هو الفاعل المختار الذي لا يُسأل عما يفعل وهم يسألون. (جل) وعَظُم (مولي) بضم الميم، وكسر اللام، أي: معطي (النعمة) التي أجلها نعمة الإيمان، وبعثة الرسل عليهم الصلاة والسلام، فله الحمد والمنة على ذلك، وعلى كل حال ثبتنا الله على ذلك. (۱) فيه رد على المعتزلة الذين أوجبوا على الله تعالى إرسال الرسل بناء على قاعدتهم: من وجوب الصلاح عليه تعالى والأصلح في حق عبيده، وفيه رد كذلك على السمنية والبراهمة القائلين باستحالته.
The sending of them is a favor and mercy for the worlds. Glorified is the Bestower of grace. Commentary by Sheikh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashshāt al-Makkī al-Mālikī: The prayer and salutation regarding their states when what befell them befell us, and the reminder of the insignificance of the world and its low worth with Allah Almighty. (The sending of them) that is, the prophets from among humans to the creation from our father Adam to our master Muhammad (peace be upon him) by including the beginning or the end (is a favor) meaning pure favor and beneficence from Allah the Generous, so that they may convey to them the Sharia of Allah and what they need (and a mercy) from Him (for all the worlds, not obligatory, because of what has preceded for you that Allah Almighty is the Free Agent who is not asked about what He does, while they are asked. (Glorified) and Exalted (Bestower) with damma on the meem and kasra on the lam, i.e., the Giver (of grace) the greatest of which is the grace of faith and the sending of the messengers, peace be upon them, so praise and gratitude are due to Him for that, and in every state may Allah establish us upon that. (1) In it is a refutation of the Mu'tazila who obligated upon Allah the sending of messengers based on their principle: the obligation of doing what is best and most suitable for His servants, and in it is also a refutation of the Sumaniyya and the Barahima who assert its impossibility.
Source pages p. 85

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on the verse "Irsāluhum tafaḍḍulun wa-raḥmah" and defines the doctrine that the sending of prophets is a pure favor (tafaḍḍul) and mercy (raḥmah) from Allah, not an obligation (wājib). The author begins by quoting the verse and then gives its explanation. He clarifies that "sending them" refers to prophets from Adam to Muhammad, and that "favor" means pure beneficence, while "mercy" indicates it is not obligatory because Allah is the free agent (fāʿil mukhtār) who cannot be questioned. The phrase "Glorified is the Bestower of grace" exalts Allah as the giver of the greatest grace: faith and prophethood. The author concludes by praising Allah and asking for steadfastness. The footnote adds that this refutes the Mu'tazila who obligate Allah to send messengers based on their principle of al-ṣalāḥ wa al-aṣlaḥ, and also refutes the Sumaniyya and Barahima who deny prophethood entirely.

Key Terms
تفضل
رحمة
واجب
فاعل مختار
معتزلة
براهمة
سمنية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Sending prophets was an obligation upon Allah.
Correction: It is a pure favor and mercy, not obligatory, because Allah is the free agent and His actions are not subject to obligation.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, why is sending messengers not obligatory for Allah?
A) Because it is impossible
B) Because Allah is the free agent who is not asked about what He does
C) Because it is a favor
D) Both B and C

Key Takeaway
Sending prophets is a pure favor and mercy from Allah, not an obligation, because Allah is the free agent and His actions are not subject to obligation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you sometimes feel entitled to blessings from Allah, as if He owes you something? Reflect on the fact that even the sending of prophets was a pure favor—how should this affect your daily gratitude? Commit to thanking Allah for at least one favor you previously took for granted.
?
When you hear arguments from people who deny the prophethood or who claim Allah must act in a certain way, are you firm in your belief that Allah is free and not bound by human notions of obligation? How can you strengthen that conviction in your heart today?
?
If sending prophets is a favor, not an obligation, then every time you hear a Prophet's message, you are receiving a gift. Are you treating it as a gift or as a burden? What change will you make to honor that gift starting now?

Prophethood

Prophethood as Divine Favor and Mercy
Verse and commentary on prophethood as favor

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Allah's sending of prophets is a pure favor and mercy, not a divine obligation, and that His grace can erase sins.

Core Idea
Prophethood is a gift of grace, not a debt of justice.

In this lesson, we examine a verse from the poem that firmly establishes the nature of prophethood as an unmerited favor from Allah. The commentator emphasizes that if Allah were to apply strict justice, even the good deeds of a servant would be nullified, but through His grace, sins are wiped away. This sets the foundation for understanding prophethood as an act of mercy.

Guided Path
1
Read the verse and translation
Start by reading the Arabic verse and its literal English translation. Notice the key terms: 'tafaḍḍul' (favor) and 'raḥma' (mercy).
The verse is the primary doctrinal statement.
2
Understand the justice-grace contrast
The commentary presents two scenarios: Allah's justice would cause good deeds to vanish, while His grace eliminates sins. This contrast highlights that prophethood is purely a favor.
This shows the superiority of grace over justice.
3
Reflect on the implications
Ask yourself: If prophethood were obligatory, it would be a matter of justice. But the verse says it is favor and mercy, so it cannot be obligatory. This affirms Allah's absolute freedom and generosity.
This is a key point in Sunni theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
إِرْسَالُهُم تَفَضُّلٌ وَرَحْمَهُ * للعالَمِينَ جَلَّ مُولِي النُّعْمَة ٥٣
حد شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي 2
بسط فضله على عبد لم تبق له سيئة.
يُعطي ويمنع ما يشاء كما يَشَا *
وَهِبَاتُه ليستْ تُقَارِنُهَا الرَّشَا
يقول العبد الفقير غفرا الله له وختم له بخير آمين:
إنْ وَضَعَ الجَلِيلُ عَدْلَهُ عَلَى عَبْدِ تَلاشَتْ حَسَنَاتِه الأُلَى وإِنْ يَكُنْ قَد بَسَطَ الفَضْلَ عَلَيْهِ لَمْ يَبْقَ مِنْ سَيِّئَةِ العَبْدِ لَدَيْهِ
سُبْحَانَ رَبِّي ذِي عَطَاءٍ وَكَرَمْ
* مَا شَاءَ أَوْجَدَ وَمَا شَاءَ عَدَمْ
٨٥
Their sending is favor and mercy * to the worlds, majestic is the Bestower of grace 53. Beginning of the commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mishshāt al-Makki al-Mālikī 2. He extended His grace upon a servant whose sins are no more. He gives and withholds what He wills as He wills * His gifts are not accompanied by bribery. The poor servant, may Allah forgive him and seal his end with good, says: If the Majestic places His justice upon a servant, his former good deeds vanish. And if He extends grace upon him, none of the servant's sins remain before Him. Glory to my Lord, Possessor of giving and generosity * What He wills He brings into existence, and what He wills He annihilates. 85.
Source pages p. 86

Commentary
This passage contains the original verse line (53) from the poem, stating that Allah's sending of the prophets is a favor (tafaḍḍul) and mercy (raḥma) to all the worlds, and that Allah is the Majestic Bestower of grace. Then the commentator, Shaykh Hasan al-Mishshāt, begins his explanation. The commentary first notes that when Allah extends His grace (faḍl) upon a servant, none of the servant's sins remain. This is followed by a poetic expansion: Allah gives and withholds as He wills; His gifts are pure and not tainted by bribery. The commentator then speaks in the first person as 'the poor servant', praying for forgiveness. He elaborates on the interplay of divine justice and grace: if Allah were to apply pure justice, a servant's good deeds would vanish, but if He extends grace, sins are erased. The passage concludes with praise of Allah as the Possessor of giving and generosity, who creates and annihilates as He wills. The overall lesson is that prophethood is a pure divine favor, not something earned, and that Allah's mercy prevails over His justice.

Key Terms
إرسال
تفضل
رحمة
عدل
فضل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Sending prophets is an obligation upon God necessary for justice.
Correction: The passage affirms that sending prophets is a favor and mercy, not a duty. God's actions are free and not constrained by obligation.

Checkpoint
According to verse 53, what is the nature of Allah's sending of the prophets?
A) An obligation required by justice
B) A favor and mercy
C) A response to human merit
D) A natural consequence of creation

Key Takeaway
Allah's sending of prophets is an act of pure grace and mercy, not a response to human deserving or an obligation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that under Allah's justice even your best deeds could evaporate, how would that change the way you perform salah and charity today?
?
When you sin, do you secretly hope that Allah will overlook it because you are 'good enough'? How does this lesson's contrast between justice and grace expose that pride?
?
Prophethood is pure favor—you did nothing to earn it. What would it mean for you to approach every blessing in your life as unearned tafaḍḍul? Name one blessing you will consciously receive as a gift today, not a right.

Belief in the Prophets and the Twenty-Five
Enumeration of the twenty-five prophets and the duty of belief.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation to believe in all prophets in general and in detail, and the twenty-five specific prophets mentioned in the Qur'an.

Core Idea
Believe in all prophets – known and unknown – with both summary and detail.

This lesson focuses on the foundational duty of believing in the prophets. The text explains that belief must be both general (ijmalan) and detailed (tafsilan) for those known by name. It also clarifies that while we affirm twenty-five named prophets, we do not restrict the total number to that.

Guided Path
1
Understand the two modes of belief
Read how the passage distinguishes between believing in prophets in summary (ijmalan) and in detail (tafsilan). Notice that detailed belief applies only to those prophets whose names are known.
The verse cited shows that some prophets are mentioned in the Qur'an, others are not.
2
Reflect on the number of prophets
The passage says it is better not to restrict the number, but then lists twenty-five. Understand that this means these are the ones we know, but there are more.
This guards against the misconception that there are exactly twenty-five prophets.
3
Memorize the list of twenty-five
Go through the names in order. Say each name aloud. Notice that the list ends with Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him).
The order is traditional and includes prophets from Adam to Muhammad.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم الأنبياء يجب الإيمان بهم، عليهم وعلى نبينا الصلاة والسلام إجمالا، فيما عُلم منهم إجمالا، والأولى ترك حصرهم في عدد وهم خمسة وعشرون معين، كما قال تعالى: ﴿ مِنْهُم مَّن قَصَصْنَا عَلَيْكَ وَمِنْهُم مَّن لَّمْ نَقْصُصْ عَلَيْكَ ﴾ (۲)، وتفصيلا فيما علم تفصيلا، آدم، وإدريس ونوح وهود، وصالح، وإبراهيم، ولوط، وإسماعيل، وإسحاق ويعقوب ويوسف، وأيوب، وشعيب، وموسى وهارون وذو الكفل وداود وسليمان ،وإلياس واليسع ويونس، وزكريا ويحيى، وعيسى، وسيد الكائنات سيدنا محمد صلى الله وسلم عليه وعليهم أجمعين. وأما أولو العزم منهم أي زيادة الصبر وتحمل المشاق، فخمسة أشار إليهم بعضهم بقوله: محمد إبراهيم موسى كليمه * فعيسى فنوحُ هُمُ أولو العزمِ فَاعْلَمِ وهم في الفضل على هذا الترتيب. ويجب اعتقاد أن سيدنا محمدًا صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ أفضلهم، وأنه
Then the prophets - it is obligatory to believe in them, may blessings and peace be upon them and our Prophet, in summary regarding what is known in summary, and it is better to refrain from restricting them to a specific number, but they are twenty-five specific [prophets], as He said: 'Of them are those We have related to you and of them are those We have not related to you.' And in detail regarding what is known in detail: Adam, Idris, Nuh, Hud, Salih, Ibrahim, Lut, Isma'il, Ishaq, Ya'qub, Yusuf, Ayyub, Shu'ayb, Musa, Harun, Dhul-Kifl, Dawud, Sulayman, Ilyas, Al-Yasa', Yunus, Zakariyya, Yahya, 'Isa, and the master of all creation our master Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace and upon them all. As for the messengers of strong will (ulu al-azm) among them, i.e., those with increased patience and bearing of hardships, they are five. Some have indicated them by his saying: 'Muhammad, Ibrahim, Musa His interlocutor, then 'Isa, then Nuh - they are the ulu al-azm, so know.' And they are in virtue in this order. And it is obligatory to believe that our master Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, is the best of them, and that he...
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on the section of the poem dealing with belief in prophets (anbiya). It begins by stating that one must believe in all prophets in general (ijmalan) and in detail (tafsilan) for those known. It is better not to restrict their number, but the known ones are twenty-five. The verse from the Quran is cited: 'Of them are those We have related to you and of them are those We have not related to you.' Then the twenty-five prophets are listed in order: Adam, Idris, Nuh, Hud, Salih, Ibrahim, Lut, Isma'il, Ishaq, Ya'qub, Yusuf, Ayyub, Shu'ayb, Musa, Harun, Dhul-Kifl, Dawud, Sulayman, Ilyas, Al-Yasa', Yunus, Zakariyya, Yahya, 'Isa, and Muhammad (peace be upon them all). Next, the passage discusses the 'ulu al-azm' (the resolute messengers), who are five: Muhammad, Ibrahim, Musa, 'Isa, and Nuh. It specifies that their order of virtue is as listed. Finally, it affirms that Muhammad (peace be upon him) is the best of all prophets. The passage ends abruptly with 'and that he...' (وأنه), indicating continuation on the next page. The commentary follows the author's sequence: general belief, specific numbers, listing, special category, and ranking.

Key Terms
الأنبياء
إيمان إجمالي
إيمان تفصيلي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That there are exactly twenty-five prophets and no more.
Correction: The passage clearly states it is better not to restrict the number; the twenty-five are only those known to us.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the ruling regarding restricting the number of prophets?
A) It is obligatory to say there are exactly twenty-five.
B) It is better not to restrict them to a specific number.
C) It is forbidden to mention any number.
D) The number is exactly twenty-five and no more.

Key Takeaway
We must believe in all prophets generally and in the twenty-five known ones in detail, without limiting the total count.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You believe in the prophets you know by name, but do you truly trust in the ones Allah has not told you about? Is your faith in the unseen prophets as real and active as your faith in the ones you have heard of? What can you do today to strengthen that hidden corner of your iman?
?
The passage advises not to fix the number of prophets, reminding you that Allah's guidance is vast. Are there areas in your life where you mentally restrict Allah's mercy to what you are familiar with—your usual duas, your favorite scholars, your comfortable rituals? How can you open your heart to accept more of what Allah has revealed, even beyond your current understanding?
?
Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) is called 'the master of all creation' in this list. Have you truly placed him above all others in your heart? Is there any person or figure you respect or admire more than him? What one concrete change can you make to honor his prophethood above all else—starting today?
