The Five Ulu al-Azm and the Superiority of Prophet Muhammad
Definition and listing of the five ulu al-azm and affirmation of Prophet Muhammad's superiority.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the meaning of ulu al-azm, the five prophets included, their order of virtue, and the superiority of Prophet Muhammad over all prophets.

Core Idea
Five prophets are ulu al-azm, and Muhammad is the best of all.

This lesson focuses on the special category of prophets known as 'ulu al-azm' (the resolute messengers). The text defines them as those with increased patience and hardship, names the five, and orders them by virtue. It then affirms the absolute superiority of Prophet Muhammad over all prophets.

Guided Path
1
Understand 'ulu al-azm'
Read the definition: they are prophets with increased patience and ability to bear hardships. The term is derived from the Qur'an.
The phrase 'ulu al-azm' appears in Surah Al-Ahqaf (46:35) and elsewhere.
2
Memorize the five names
The passage gives a poetic line listing the five: Muhammad, Ibrahim, Musa, 'Isa, and Nuh. Learn them in order.
The order in the poem is also the order of their virtue.
3
Recognize the virtue order
The passage states that they are in virtue according to this order. Understand that Muhammad is the best, then Ibrahim, then Musa, then 'Isa, then Nuh.
This does not diminish the status of the other prophets; it is a specific ranking among the ulu al-azm.
4
Affirm Muhammad's superiority
The passage ends with the obligation to believe that Muhammad (peace be upon him) is the best of all prophets. Reflect on why this is a core belief.
This is based on evidence from the Qur'an and Sunnah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

Next, the passage discusses the 'ulu al-azm' (the resolute messengers), who are five: Muhammad, Ibrahim, Musa, 'Isa, and Nuh. It specifies that their order of virtue is as listed. Finally, it affirms that Muhammad (peace be upon him) is the best of all prophets. The passage ends abruptly with 'and that he...' (وأنه), indicating continuation on the next page. The commentary follows the author's sequence: general belief, specific numbers, listing, special category, and ranking.

Key Terms
أولو العزم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That all prophets are equal in rank, or that another prophet is superior to Muhammad.
Correction: The passage asserts that Muhammad is the best of all prophets, and the ulu al-azm have a specific order of virtue.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, which prophet is the best of all prophets?
A) Ibrahim
B) Musa
C) Muhammad
D) Nuh

Key Takeaway
The five ulu al-azm are Muhammad, Ibrahim, Musa, 'Isa, and Nuh, in that order of virtue, and Muhammad is the best of all prophets.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The ulu al-azm are defined by increased patience and bearing of hardships. Which specific trial are you currently facing where you can consciously increase your patience to resemble theirs? Write down one concrete action you will take this week to endure it for Allah's sake.
?
If Muhammad (peace be upon him) is the best of all prophets, does your daily life reflect that he is your ultimate role model? Identify one area where you follow your desires instead of his Sunnah, and commit to changing it starting today.
?
The order of virtue among the five prophets is from Allah's wisdom. Do you accept His decrees in your own life regarding who is given more or less? Are you free from envy towards others whom Allah has blessed with more worldly or spiritual favors?

Belief in the Prophets: Obligation and the Twenty-Five
Obligation to believe in prophets and enumeration of twenty-five named prophets.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation to believe in all prophets and the names of the twenty-five prophets specifically mentioned in revelation.

Core Idea
Belief in prophets is obligatory, and we know twenty-five by name.

This lesson introduces the foundational obligation to believe in the prophets whom Allah has sent. The author first clarifies that while we must believe in all prophets generally, we are not required to enumerate them all. Yet, revelation has specified twenty-five prophets whom we must know by name. This list includes Adam through Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon them all.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on general obligation
Read the first part of the Arabic passage that states the obligation to believe in prophets and the preference for not confining them to a specific number. Note the Quranic verse cited.
The verse is from Surah Ghafir (40:78).
2
Identify the list of twenty-five
Read the list of prophets from Adam to Muhammad. Notice the sequence and the names. This list is the detailed knowledge we are required to have.
The list ends with 'Sayed al-Ka'inat' which is a title for Prophet Muhammad.
3
Reflect on the balance
Ask yourself: why does the author say it is preferable not to limit the number, but then give a list? This teaches us to believe in all prophets generally, but we must know those specifically mentioned in revelation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم الأنبياء يجب الإيمان بهم، عليهم وعلى نبينا الصلاة والسلام إجمالا، فيما عُلم منهم إجمالا، والأولى ترك حصرهم في عدد معين، كما قال تعالى: ﴿ مِنْهُم مَّن قَصَصْنَا عَلَيْكَ وَمِنْهُم مَّن لَّمْ نَقْصُصْ عَلَيْكَ ﴾، وتفصيلا فيما علم تفصيلا، آدم، وإدريس ونوح وهود، وصالح، وإبراهيم، ولوط، وإسماعيل، وإسحاق ويعقوب ويوسف، وأيوب، وشعيب، وموسى وهارون وذو الكفل وداود وسليمان، وإلياس واليسع ويونس، وزكريا ويحيى، وعيسى، وسيد الكائنات سيدنا محمد صلى الله وسلم عليه وعليهم أجمعين. وأما أولو العزم منهم أي زيادة الصبر وتحمل المشاق، فخمسة أشار إليهم بعضهم بقوله: محمد إبراهيم موسى كليمه * فعيسى فنوحُ هُمُ أولو العزمِ فَاعْلَمِ وهم في الفضل على هذا الترتيب.
Then the prophets: it is obligatory to believe in them, upon them and upon our Prophet prayers and peace, in general regarding what is known of them in general, and it is preferable to avoid confining them to a specific number, as Allah said: 'Of them are those We have related to you and of them are those We have not related to you' (Quran 40:78), and in detail regarding what is known in detail: Adam, Idris, Noah, Hud, Salih, Abraham, Lot, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, Joseph, Job, Shu'ayb, Moses, Aaron, Dhul-Kifl, David, Solomon, Elijah, Elisha, Jonah, Zechariah, John, Jesus, and the master of the universes our master Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and upon all of them. As for the resolute among them (Ulu al-Azm), meaning those with increased patience and endurance of hardships, there are five, alluded to by some by his saying: Muhammad, Abraham, Moses His interlocutor, then Jesus, then Noah; they are the resolute, so know. And they are in merit according to this order.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage explains the obligation of belief in the prophets. It begins by stating that belief is obligatory in general for those prophets known to us generally, and it is better not to restrict them to a specific number since the Quran mentions some and not others. However, it then provides a detailed list of twenty-five prophets that are known by name from revelation. The list includes Adam through Muhammad, peace be upon them all. The passage then turns to the category of the Ulu al-Azm, the resolute prophets, who are distinguished by their greater patience and endurance. It states that there are five such prophets, and quotes a poetic line naming them in order: Muhammad, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Noah. The passage concludes by stating that they are in merit according to this order, with Prophet Muhammad being the foremost among them, as will be clarified in the next lesson.

Key Terms
الأنبياء
الإيمان بالأنبياء
الخمسة والعشرون

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One must know the exact number of all prophets and their names.
Correction: The passage clarifies that it is preferable not to confine them to a number, but we must know those specifically mentioned in revelation.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, why is it preferable not to confine the prophets to a specific number?
A) Because we cannot count them all
B) Because the Quran says there are prophets not mentioned
C) Because only twenty-five are known
D) Because it is not obligatory to believe in them

Key Takeaway
Belief in all prophets is obligatory, and we know twenty-five by name, but the total number is known only to Allah.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the names of the prophets in your daily life, do you feel a connection to each one, or are some merely names you pass over? What would it mean for you to truly believe in them all, including those whose stories you have not heard?
?
The lesson reminds you that there are prophets Allah did not mention to you. Does the thought that you are accountable to believe in them even though you don't know them trouble you? How does this expand your concept of faith?

The Ulu al-Azm: The Five Resolute Prophets
Definition and enumeration of the five Ulu al-Azm prophets with their rank.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand who the Ulu al-Azm are, why they are distinguished, and their ranking in merit.

Core Idea
Five prophets are resolute, with Prophet Muhammad as the highest.

Having learned the general list of prophets, we now turn to a special group among them: the Ulu al-Azm, or the resolute prophets. These five are distinguished by their exceptional patience and endurance in conveying the message. A poetic summary helps us remember their names and order of merit.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition of Ulu al-Azm
Read the Arabic passage that defines Ulu al-Azm as those with increased patience and endurance. Understand why they are singled out.
The term 'Ulu al-Azm' literally means 'those of resolve'.
2
Memorize the five names via the poem
Read the poetic line that names the five prophets: Muhammad, Ibrahim, Musa, Isa, Nuh. Notice the order given.
The poem is a mnemonic device.
3
Consider the ranking in merit
The passage states that they are in merit according to this order. Think about what this means for the rank of Prophet Muhammad.
This order is based on a hadith or scholarly consensus.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم الأنبياء يجب الإيمان بهم، عليهم وعلى نبينا الصلاة والسلام إجمالا، فيما عُلم منهم إجمالا، والأولى ترك حصرهم في عدد معين، كما قال تعالى: ﴿ مِنْهُم مَّن قَصَصْنَا عَلَيْكَ وَمِنْهُم مَّن لَّمْ نَقْصُصْ عَلَيْكَ ﴾، وتفصيلا فيما علم تفصيلا، آدم، وإدريس ونوح وهود، وصالح، وإبراهيم، ولوط، وإسماعيل، وإسحاق ويعقوب ويوسف، وأيوب، وشعيب، وموسى وهارون وذو الكفل وداود وسليمان، وإلياس واليسع ويونس، وزكريا ويحيى، وعيسى، وسيد الكائنات سيدنا محمد صلى الله وسلم عليه وعليهم أجمعين. وأما أولو العزم منهم أي زيادة الصبر وتحمل المشاق، فخمسة أشار إليهم بعضهم بقوله: محمد إبراهيم موسى كليمه * فعيسى فنوحُ هُمُ أولو العزمِ فَاعْلَمِ وهم في الفضل على هذا الترتيب.

And they are in merit according to this order.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage explains the obligation of belief in the prophets. It begins by stating that belief is obligatory in general for those prophets known to us generally, and it is better not to restrict them to a specific number since the Quran mentions some and not others. However, it then provides a detailed list of twenty-five prophets that are known by name from revelation. The list includes Adam through Muhammad, peace be upon them all. Prophethood

The Superiority of Prophet Muhammad and the Prophet-Angel Hierarchy
Affirms Muhammad's superiority and the hierarchy of prophets over angels.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni doctrine that Prophet Muhammad is the best of all prophets and that prophets are superior to angels.

Core Idea
Prophet Muhammad is the best of all creation, and prophets rank above angels.

This passage from the Kharida establishes a foundational belief in Sunni Islam: the absolute superiority of Prophet Muhammad over all other prophets and the hierarchy between prophets and angels. It is obligatory to hold this conviction in one's heart.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read carefully the obligation to believe in Muhammad's superiority and his being the seal of prophets. Notice the phrase 'wa annahu khatamuhum'.
2
Identify the three key affirmations
The passage makes three affirmations: (1) Muhammad is the best of them, (2) he is the seal, (3) after them are the rest of the messengers, and prophets are heads of angels. Identify each.
3
Reflect on the hierarchy
Consider the hierarchy: Muhammad, then other messengers, then prophets in general, then angels. Why does this ordering matter in Sunni theology?
This guards against the idea that angels are superior to prophets.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب اعتقاد أن سيدنا محمدًا صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ أفضلهم، وأنه خاتمهم، وبعدهم بقية الرسل، فالأنبياء فرؤوس الملائكة بلا تعيين فأصحاب النبي صلى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ ، وأفضلهم أبو بكر، فعمر، فعثمان، فعلي، فبقية العشرة، فبقية البدريين، فأهل بيته فبقية الصحابة، فالتابعين وتابع التابعين.
And it is obligatory to believe that our master Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, is the best of them, and that he is their seal, and after them are the rest of the messengers, then the prophets are the heads of the angels without specification, then the companions of the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and the best of them is Abu Bakr, then Umar, then Uthman, then Ali, then the rest of the ten, then the rest of the Badriyun, then his household, then the rest of the companions, then the followers (tabi'un), and the followers of the followers.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage establishes the necessity of believing in the preeminence of Prophet Muhammad over all other prophets and that he is the final prophet. It then outlines a hierarchy: after him are the rest of the messengers (anbiya?), then the prophets (which here seems to mean the same? Actually it says 'the prophets are the heads of the angels' meaning prophets are superior to angels, without specifying which angels). Then it ranks the companions of the Prophet in order of virtue: Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali, then the remaining ten promised Paradise (the 'ashara mubashshara), then those who fought at Badr, then the Prophet's household, then the rest of the companions, then the tabi'un, and then the tabi' al-tabi'in. This hierarchy reflects the well-known Sunni position on the superiority of the Rightly Guided Caliphs and the virtue of the early Muslims.

Key Terms
أفضلهم
خاتمهم
الرسل
الأنبياء
الملائكة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That angels are superior to prophets, or that some prophets are equal to Muhammad.
Correction: This passage affirms that Prophet Muhammad is afdaluhum (the best) and that prophets are the heads of angels, establishing a clear hierarchy.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what is the rank of prophets relative to angels?
A) Prophets are equal to angels
B) Prophets are the heads of angels
C) Angels are superior to prophets
D) No comparison is made

Key Takeaway
Prophet Muhammad is the best of all prophets, and prophets as a whole rank above angels.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You affirm that Prophet Muhammad is the best of all creation. How does this belief affect your daily choices? Is there an area where you are following a habit or figure that contradicts his example? What specific change will you make today to honor his superiority?
?
The lesson teaches that prophets rank above angels. If you have ever placed angels on a higher pedestal in your heart, why? How can you cultivate a deeper love and respect for the prophets, especially Muhammad, starting with one action this week?
?
Believing that Muhammad is the seal of prophets means that his message is the final and complete guidance. Does your life reflect that you consider the Quran and Sunnah as your primary source of direction? In what ways might you be neglecting this belief, and what step will you take to realign?

The Hierarchy of the Companions and Successors
Lists the Companions and early generations in order of virtue.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni ranking of the Companions and early Muslim generations in virtue.

Core Idea
The Companions have a specific order of virtue, beginning with the first four caliphs.

After establishing the superiority of prophets, the Kharida turns to the Companions of the Prophet. This passage lists them in order of merit, from the Rightly Guided Caliphs to the later generations, reflecting the Sunni consensus on the hierarchy of the early Muslim community.

Guided Path
1
Read the list carefully
Read the Arabic list from 'fa ash-hab al-nabi' to the end. Note the sequence: Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali, then others.
2
Identify the groups
The list includes the ten promised Paradise, the Badriyun (those who fought at Badr), the Prophet's household, the rest of the Companions, the Tabi'un, and Tabi' al-Tabi'in.
3
Understand the principle
This hierarchy is based on precedence in Islam and service. It defends the Sunni position on the caliphate.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب اعتقاد أن سيدنا محمدًا صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ أفضلهم، وأنه خاتمهم، وبعدهم بقية الرسل، فالأنبياء فرؤوس الملائكة بلا تعيين فأصحاب النبي صلى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ ، وأفضلهم أبو بكر، فعمر، فعثمان، فعلي، فبقية العشرة، فبقية البدريين، فأهل بيته فبقية الصحابة، فالتابعين وتابع التابعين.
And it is obligatory to believe that our master Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, is the best of them, and that he is their seal, and after them are the rest of the messengers, then the prophets are the heads of the angels without specification, then the companions of the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and the best of them is Abu Bakr, then Umar, then Uthman, then Ali, then the rest of the ten, then the rest of the Badriyun, then his household, then the rest of the companions, then the followers (tabi'un), and the followers of the followers.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage establishes the necessity of believing in the preeminence of Prophet Muhammad over all other prophets and that he is the final prophet. It then outlines a hierarchy: after him are the rest of the messengers (anbiya?), then the prophets (which here seems to mean the same? Actually it says 'the prophets are the heads of the angels' meaning prophets are superior to angels, without specifying which angels). Then it ranks the companions of the Prophet in order of virtue: Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali, then the remaining ten promised Paradise (the 'ashara mubashshara), then those who fought at Badr, then the Prophet's household, then the rest of the companions, then the tabi'un, and then the tabi' al-tabi'in. This hierarchy reflects the well-known Sunni position on the superiority of the Rightly Guided Caliphs and the virtue of the early Muslims.

Key Terms

الخلفاء الراشدون
العشرة المبشرون
البدريون
أهل البيت
التابعين

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That Ali is superior to Abu Bakr, or that all Companions are equal in rank.
Correction: This passage clearly orders them: Abu Bakr is the best, then Umar, then Uthman, then Ali, then others.

Checkpoint

A) Ali
B) Abu Bakr
C) Umar
D) Uthman

Key Takeaway

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
Do you secretly harbor the belief that modern scholars or your own opinions are more valuable than the understanding of the Companions? When was the last time you actively set aside your own reasoning to accept a teaching from them that you found difficult?
?
The Companions are ranked in virtue; the best of them are known. Are you content with being a follower of the average? What concrete step will you take this week to emulate the dedication of Abu Bakr or Umar in your own worship or character?

Unseen Matters
beginner
4 min
Obligatory Eschatological Beliefs
Enumerates obligatory eschatological beliefs in poetic form.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the essential eschatological events and beings that every Muslim must believe in as part of Islamic creed.

Core Idea
Belief in the afterlife includes specific events and beings — denying any is disbelief.

This lesson presents a concise catalogue of eschatological realities that every Muslim must affirm. The author enumerates them in a poetic format, covering events like the accounting and the bridge, as well as beings like angels and the houris. Understanding this list helps anchor one's faith in the unseen.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic passage and its literal translation. Notice the rhythmic structure and the number of items listed.
The passage is a poem, so repetition and rhyme are used for memorisation.
2
Categorise the items
Try grouping the items into two categories: events (e.g., accounting, gathering) and beings (e.g., jinn, prophets). This helps organise the list in your mind.
Some items like 'the pool' are places, but they are part of eschatological events.
3
Reflect on obligation
Consider why each item is obligatory to believe. The commentary explains that denying any of them constitutes disbelief. Think about how this list shapes a Muslim's worldview.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وَيَلْزَمُ الْإِيمَانُ بِالْحِسَابِ * وَالْحَشْرِ وَالْعِقَابِ وَالثَّوَابِ . وَالنَّشْرِ وَالصِّرَاطِ وَالْمِيزَانِ * وَالْحَوْضِ وَالنَّيْرَانِ وَالْجِنَانِ . وَالْجِنِّ وَالْأَمْلَاكِ ثُمَّ الْأَنْبِيَا * وَالْحُورِ وَالْوِلْدَانِ ثُمَّ الْأَوْلِيَا
And belief is necessary in the accounting, and the gathering, and the punishment, and the reward, and the resurrection, and the path, and the balance, and the pool, and the fires, and the gardens, and the jinn, and the angels, then the prophets, and the houris, and the young boys, then the friends of God.
Source pages p. 16

Commentary
This passage lists the components of eschatological belief (al-iman bi...). It begins with al-hisab (accounting), al-hashr (gathering), al-'iqab (punishment), al-thawab (reward), al-nashr (resurrection), al-sirat (the bridge), al-mizan (the balance), al-hawd (the pool of the Prophet), al-niran (the fires of hell), al-jinan (the gardens of paradise). Then it includes belief in jinn, angels, prophets, hur (houris), wildan (youthful servants), and awliya (saints). All these are obligatory to believe in as part of Islamic creed.

Key Terms
حساب
حشر
صراط
ميزان
حوض
نيران
جنان
جني
أملاك
حور
ولدان
أوليا

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some people think that only the major events like resurrection and judgment are obligatory, while details like the pool or houris are optional or symbolic.
Correction: This passage makes clear that all listed items are equally obligatory to believe in as part of Islamic faith. Denying any of them, including the Prophet's pool or the houris, is a serious error.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is NOT listed as an object of belief in this passage?
A) The balance
B) The prophets
C) The throne
D) The houris

Key Takeaway
Faith in the unseen realities of the afterlife is an integral part of Islamic belief, and each listed item must be accepted as true.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe in every single item listed in this poem, or do you find yourself dismissing some as mere metaphors? What evidence can you point to in your life that shows you take these unseen realities seriously?
?
If you were to appear before the Balance (Mizan) tomorrow, what would tip the scale—your confidence in your good deeds or your fear of your shortcomings? How does that affect how you live today?
?
The Prophet's Pool (Hawd) is promised to those who follow him steadfastly. Is your love for the Prophet and his teachings deep enough that you yearn for that drink, or have you let worldly attachments distract you?

Module 12 · Lesson 2

Unseen Matters
beginner
8 min
Proofs for the Vision of Allah from Revelation
Al-Mashat cites Quran, hadith, and poetry to prove the vision of Allah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the scriptural evidence for the vision of Allah in the afterlife and how it varies according to one's knowledge and love of Allah.

Core Idea
Seeing Allah is the ultimate bliss, varying by one's knowledge and love.

In this passage, Al-Mashat presents the doctrine of the vision of Allah (ru'ya) as the greatest reward for the people of Paradise. He cites the Quran, a prophetic hadith, and a well-known theological poem to affirm that all believers—men, women, children, even jinn—will see their Lord, and that this vision differs in magnificence based on their earthly devotion. The passage also includes a lament against those who deny this vision.

Guided Path
1
Identify who sees Allah
Read the opening lines to see which groups are included in the vision. Notice that it covers all inhabitants of Paradise: humans, jinn, women, and children.
The author emphasizes inclusivity to counter any exclusionary views.
2
Understand the variation in vision
Pay attention to how the vision differs in quantity and quality. The variation is tied to one's knowledge and love of Allah in this world.
This explains why some experience a more intense vision.
3
Analyze the poetic quote
Read the lines from Bad' al-Amali. They affirm that believers see Allah without 'how' and that this sight makes them forget all other bliss.
The poem contrasts believers with the Mu'tazila who deny the vision.
4
Examine the Quranic verse
Look at verse 75:22-23 and its direct claim that faces will look at their Lord. This is a primary proof.
5
Reflect on the hadith fragment
The hadith begins with the Companions asking about seeing Allah. The Prophet's response (cut off) likely parallels seeing the moon.
The full hadith is well-known; this fragment serves as a reminder.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قَالَ الْمُؤَلِّفُ رَحِمَهُ اللهُ تَعَالَى: وَتَقَعُ الرُّؤْيَةُ لِكُلِّ مَنْ دَخَلَ الْجَنَّةَ، مِنْ إِنْسٍ وَجِنٍّ مِنْ هَذِهِ الأُمَّةِ وَغَيْرِهَا، حَتَّى النِّسَاءِ وَالصِّبْيَانِ، وَتَتَفَاضَلُ الرُّؤْيَةُ كَمَا - أَيْ: عَدًا، وَكَيْفًا - أَيْ: قَدْرًا وَعَظَمَةً، وَلَذَّةً، عَلَى قَدْرِ عِلْمِهِمْ بِاللهِ تَعَالَى، وَحُبِّهِمْ لَهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا، حَتَّى إِنَّ الْبَعْضَ لاَ تَنْقَطِعُ عَنْهُ الرُّؤْيَةُ أَبَدًا، كَمَا أَنَّهُ كَانَ فِي الدُّنْيَا لاَ يَتَعَلَّقُ قَلْبُهُ بِغَيْرِ اللهِ تَعَالَى أَبَدًا. قَالَ فِي بَدْءِ الأَمَالِي: يَرَاهُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ بِغَيْرِ كَيْفٍ = وَضَرْبٍ وَتَشْبِيهٍ مِنْ مِثَالِ فَيَنْسَوْنَ النَّعِيمَ إِذَا رَأَوْهُ * فَيَا خُسْرَانَ أَهْلِ الاعْتِزَالِ فَإِنَّ هَذَا هُوَ النَّعِيمُ حَقًّا، وَسِوَاهُ مِمَّا أَعَدَّهُ اللهُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ فِي الْجَنَّةِ، إِنَّمَا هُوَ مَظْهَرٌ مِنْ مَظَاهِرِ هَذَا النَّعِيمِ، قَالَ اللهُ تَعَالَى: ﴿ وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَّاضِرَةٌ إِلَى رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ ﴾ قَالَ رَسُولُ اللهِ صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ وَقَدْ قَالَ لَهُ الصَّحْبُ الْكِرَامُ يَا رَسُولَ اللهِ هَلْ نَرَى رَبَّنَا يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ؟ هَلْ تُمَارُونَ» أَيْ: تَشُكُّونَ «فِي
The author, may Allah have mercy on him, said: And the vision occurs for everyone who enters Paradise, from humans and jinn of this nation and others, even women and children, and the vision differs in quantity and quality—i.e., in amount and greatness and pleasure—according to their knowledge of Allah the Exalted and their love for Him in the world, such that for some the vision is never cut off from them, just as in the world his heart was never attached to other than Allah the Exalted. He said in Bad' al-Amali: The believers see Him without how and without category and likeness from an example, so they forget bliss when they see Him. O the loss of the people of i'tizal! For this is truly the bliss, and other than it of what Allah has prepared for the believers in Paradise is only a manifestation of this bliss. Allah the Exalted said: (Faces on that day radiant, looking at their Lord). The Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, said—and the noble Companions had said to him, 'O Messenger of Allah, will we see our Lord on the Day of Resurrection?'—'Do you dispute' i.e., doubt 'in'.
Source pages p. 77

Commentary
This passage provides proofs and additional details about the vision of Allah. The author first states that the vision occurs for all inhabitants of Paradise—humans, jinn, even women and children—and that it varies in quantity and quality (number of times and intensity) according to one's knowledge and love of Allah in this world. Some never lose the vision, just as they were never attached to anything else. Then he quotes from the poem Bad' al-Amali, which affirms that believers see Allah without how, category, or likeness, and that upon seeing Him they forget all other bliss. The poem then laments the loss of the Mu'tazila who deny this. The author comments that this vision is the true bliss, and other blessings are merely manifestations of it. He then cites the Quranic verse from Surat al-Qiyama (75:22-23): 'Faces on that day radiant, looking at their Lord.' Finally, he begins to cite a hadith where the Companions ask the Prophet if they will see their Lord, and the Prophet responds by asking if they doubt seeing the moon—but the text cuts off. The overall argument is that scriptural sources and scholarly consensus confirm the reality of the vision, and it is the highest reward.

جنة
تفاضل
نعيم
اعترال
ناظرة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Only prophets or men will see Allah in Paradise.
Correction: The passage explicitly includes all inhabitants: humans, jinn, women, and children.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what does the Quranic verse 'وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَّاضِرَةٌ إِلَى رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ' indicate?
A) That faces will be bright because of good deeds
B) That believers will look at their Lord on that day
C) That only prophets will see Allah
D) That vision is metaphorical

Key Takeaway
The vision of Allah is the ultimate reward of Paradise, confirmed by the Quran, hadith, and scholarly consensus, and it varies in greatness according to one's faith and love.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The passage says the vision differs according to your knowledge and love of Allah in this world. How much are you actually seeking to know Him and love Him daily? What specific step will you take today to increase either?
?
Some believers will never have the vision cut off because their hearts were never attached to other than Allah. What worldly attachment is currently competing with your devotion to Allah? Will you break it before you meet Him?
?
The author laments the loss of those who deny the vision. Are you willing to accept all that Allah has revealed about Himself, even if your mind struggles to comprehend it? Or do you, like the Mu'tazila, explain away what seems beyond reason?

5 min
The Reality and Differentiation of the Vision of Allah
Author affirms ru'ya and explains its differentiation

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni doctrine of the vision of Allah (ru'ya) in paradise, its universality, its variation based on knowledge and love, and the refutation of the Mu'tazila denial.

Core Idea
The vision of Allah is the greatest bliss of paradise, varying in degree but always without modality.

In this passage, the author affirms the vision of Allah (ru'ya) for all inhabitants of paradise, including humans and jinn, and explains how it differs in quality and duration based on one's knowledge and love of Allah in this world. He then quotes a famous poem and a Quranic verse to emphasize that this vision is the supreme bliss and a refutation of those who deny it.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the inclusive language: everyone in paradise, even women and children.
2
Identify the key claims about ru'ya
The passage affirms ru'ya for all and explains that it varies in two aspects: quantity and quality. Underline the phrases that describe this variation.
3
Understand the quote from Bad' al-Amali
The poem states that believers see Allah without how, and that this vision makes them forget all other bliss. Note the condemnation of the Mu'tazila.
This quote reinforces the Sunni position against the Mu'tazila who deny ru'ya.
4
Reflect on the Quranic evidence
The author concludes with Quran 75:22-23. Read the verse and see how it proves that the righteous will see their Lord.
This verse is a primary proof for ru'ya.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتقع الرؤية لكل من دخل الجنة، من إنس وجن من هذه الأمة ،وغيرها، حتى النساء والصبيان، وتتفاضل الرؤية كما - أي: عدا ، وكيفا -أي: قدرًا وعظما، ولدة، على قدر علمهم بالله تعالى، وحبهم له في الدنيا، حتى إن البعض لا تنقطع عنه الرؤية أبدًا، كما أنه كان في الدنيا لا يتعلق قلبه بغير الله تعالى أبدا» (۱) ، اللهم أهلنا لرؤيتك التي إليها قلوب العارفين تُسارع منا منك وفضلا يا كريم، قال في بدء الأمالي، (۲): يرأه المؤمنونَ بِغير كيف = وضَرْبٍ وَتَشْبِيهِ (۳) مِن مِثَالِ فَيَنْسَونَ النعيمَ إِذَا رَأَوْهُ * فَيَا خُسْرانَ أَهْلِ الاعْتِزَالِ فإن هذا هو النعيم حقا، وسواه مما أعده الله للمؤمنين في الجنة، إنما هو مظهر من مظاهر هذا النعيم، قال الله تعالى: ﴿ وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَيذٍ نَّاضِرَةٌ إِلَى رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ ﴾
And the vision occurs for everyone who enters paradise, from humans and jinn of this nation and others, even women and children. And the vision varies in quantity—i.e., amount—and quality—i.e., magnitude and greatness and duration—according to their knowledge of Allah Almighty and their love for Him in the world, such that some are never cut off from the vision ever, just as in the world his heart was not attached to anything other than Allah Almighty ever. O Allah, make us worthy of Your vision towards which the hearts of the knowers hasten, as a favor from You, O Generous. He said in Bad' al-Amali: 'The believers see Him without how, and without striking and likening from any example. Then they forget [all other] bliss when they see Him. So woe to the people of i'tizal!' For this is the true bliss, and other than it from what Allah has prepared for the believers in paradise is but a manifestation of this bliss. Allah Almighty said: 'Faces on that day are radiant, looking at their Lord.'
Source pages p. 78

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal affirmation of the vision of Allah (ru'ya) in the afterlife, specifically in paradise. The author begins by stating that the vision occurs for every inhabitant of paradise, including humans and jinn, from this ummah and other nations, and even women and children. He then explains that the vision is not the same for everyone; it varies in quantity (kam) and quality (kayf), i.e., in its extent and in its greatness and duration, depending on the level of knowledge of Allah and love for Him that the person had in the world. Some believers are never cut off from this vision, just as their hearts were never attached to anything other than Allah in this life. The author then includes a supplication for himself and the readers to be made worthy of that vision. He quotes from the poem Bad' al-Amali, which states that the believers see Allah without modality (kayf), without comparing or likening to any example. The poem emphasizes that upon seeing Him, they forget all other bliss, and it pronounces woe upon the Mu'tazila who deny the vision. The author then comments that this vision is the true bliss, and all other delights of paradise are merely manifestations of this supreme bliss. Finally, he cites Quran 75:22-23 as evidence: 'Faces on that day are radiant, looking at their Lord.'

Key Terms
رؤية
كما وكيفا
بدء الأمالي
أهل الاعتزال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Mu'tazila denial that Allah can be seen in the afterlife.
Correction: The passage affirms ru'ya for all believers in paradise, citing the Quran and the consensus of Ahl al-Sunna, and refutes the Mu'tazila position.

Checkpoint
What determines the differentiation in the vision of Allah among the people of paradise according to this passage?
A) Their wealth
B) Their lineage
C) Their knowledge and love of Allah
D) Their gender

Key Takeaway
The vision of Allah is the greatest reward in paradise, varying in perfection according to one's faith and love in this life, and it is seen without modality.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
How does your current knowledge of Allah and love for Him in this world compare to the level that would grant you the highest vision in paradise? What is one step you can take today to increase both?
?
The author says some believers are never cut off from the vision because their hearts were never attached to anything other than Allah. Is your heart attached to something other than Allah that would prevent such continuous vision? What would it take for you to detach?
?
The poem says the believers forget all other bliss when they see Allah. If you were in paradise, would you be satisfied with the lesser delights, or do you truly long for the vision of Allah above all else? What does your answer reveal about your sincerity?

Hadith Evidence for the Vision of Allah
The author presents the hadith proof and explains the linguistic variants to affirm the clarity of vision.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the Prophet's analogy of seeing the moon and sun serves as a proof for the vision of Allah, and how the linguistic variants reinforce the clarity of that vision.

Core Idea
Seeing Allah is as clear as seeing the full moon — no doubt, no obstruction.

The author begins by citing a famous hadith in which the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, responds to the Companions' question about seeing Allah on the Day of Resurrection. By analogizing the vision to the sight of the moon and sun without obstruction, the hadith establishes both the possibility and clarity of ru'ya. The subsequent discussion of variant wordings further emphasizes that there will be no confusion or difficulty in that vision.

Guided Path
1
Read the hadith text
Read the entire hadith including the linguistic variants. Notice how the Prophet uses analogy.
2
Understand the variant wordings
The author discusses multiple wordings (tudārūn, tadārūn, etc.) and their meanings. Note how they all emphasize clarity.
3
Connect to the broader proof
Reflect on why this hadith is a definitive proof for ru'ya. How does it refute doubt?
This connects to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال رسول الله صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ وقد قال له الصحب الكرام يا رسول الله هل نرى ربنا يوم القيامة؟ هل تمارون» أي: تشكون «في القمر ليلة البدر، ليس دونه سحاب»، قالوا: لا يا رسول الله، قال: «فهل تمارون في الشمس ليس دونها سحاب»، قالوا: لا يا رسول الله، قال: فإنكم ترونه كذلك»، والحديث في الصحيحين» (۱). (1) أخرجه البخاري ( ح٤٥٨١) ، ومسلم ( ح ۱۸۳) من حديث أبي سعيد الخدري، وأخرجه البخاري (ح٦٥٧٣) ، ومسلم ( ح ۱۸۲) من حديث أبي هريرة رضى اللهُ عَنْهُ ، وفي الروايتين تضارون» بدل «تمارون». وفي شرح صحيح مسلم للنووي (۱۷/۳) روي «تضارون» بتشديد الراء، وبتخفيفها، والتاء مضمومة فيهما، ومعنى المشدد هل تضارون غيركم في حالة الرؤية بزحمة، أو مخالفة في الرؤية، أو غيرها لخفائه، كما تفعلون أول ليلة من الشهر، ومعنى المخفف هل يلحقكم في رؤيته ضير، وهو الضرر. وروي أيضًا تضامون بتشديد الميم، وتخفيفها، فمن شددها فتح التاء، ومن خففها ضم التاء، ومعنى المشدد هل تتضامون وتتلطفون في التوصل إلى رؤيته، ومعنى المخفف هل يلحقكم ضيم ، وهو المشقة والتعب، ومعناه لا يشتبه عليكم وترتابون فيه، فيعارض بعضكم بعضًا في رؤيته». وقد استدل على الجواز أيضًا بقوله تعالى: ﴿ رَبِّ أَرِنِي أَنظُرْ إِلَيْكَ ﴾ [الأعراف: ١٤٣]، فلو لم تكن جائزة ما طلبها سيدنا موسى عليه السلام، وإلا كان طلبها إما جهلًا بأحكام الألوهية، وإما سفها، أو عبئًا بطلب المحال، والأنبياء مُنَزَّهُونَ عن ذلك كله. وأن الله تعالى قد عَلْقَها على ممكن، وهو استقرار الجبل، والمعلق على الممكن ممكن، إذ معنى التعليق: الإخبار بوقوع المُعَلَّقِ عند ثبوت المُعَلَّقِ عليه، والمحال لا يقع على شيء من التقادير الممكنة، فلو لم تكن ممكنة، لزم الخلف في خبره تعالى، وهو محال.
The Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and grant him peace) said, while the noble Companions had said to him, 'O Messenger of Allah, will we see our Lord on the Day of Resurrection?' He said, 'Do you dispute (i.e., doubt) about the moon on the night of the full moon, without any cloud over it?' They said, 'No, O Messenger of Allah.' He said, 'Do you dispute about the sun without any cloud over it?' They said, 'No.' He said, 'Then you will see Him likewise.' And the hadith is in the two Sahihs. (1) Al-Bukhari narrated it (no. 4581) and Muslim (no. 183) from the hadith of Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, and Al-Bukhari (no. 6573) and Muslim (no. 182) from the hadith of Abu Hurayra, may Allah be pleased with him. In both narrations it is 'tudārūn' instead of 'tumārūn'. In the commentary of Sahih Muslim by al-Nawawi (17/3) it is narrated 'tudārūn' with a shadda on the ra', and with its lightening, and the ta' is damma in both. The meaning of the intensified form is: Do you crowd one another in the vision due to congestion, or disagree about it due to its subtlety, as you do on the first night of the month? The meaning of the lightened form is: Does any harm (ḍayr) befall you in seeing it? Harm. And it is also narrated 'tudāmūn' with a shadda on the mim and its lightening; whoever emphasizes it opens the ta', whoever lightens it puts damma on the ta'. The meaning of the intensified form is: Do you press together and seek delicacy in attaining the vision? The lightened form means: Does any oppression (ḍaym) befall you? i.e., hardship and fatigue. Its meaning: it is not ambiguous for you, nor do you doubt it, so that some of you contradict others concerning it.' And they also used as evidence for its permissibility the saying of Allah Almighty: 'My Lord, show me (Yourself) that I may look at You' (7:143). For if it were not permissible, our master Musa (peace be upon him) would not have asked for it, otherwise his request would have been either ignorance of the rulings of divinity, or foolishness, or a burden of asking for the impossible, and the prophets are free from all of that. And Allah Almighty made it contingent upon something possible, namely the mountain's settling, and what is contingent upon a possible thing is possible, for the meaning of condition is: informing of the occurrence of the conditioned thing upon the establishment of the condition, and the impossible does not occur under any possible circumstances. So if it were not possible, there would be a break in the information of Allah Almighty, which is impossible.
Source pages p. 78

Commentary
This passage provides two major proofs for the permissibility (and reality) of the vision of Allah (ru'ya) in the afterlife. The first proof is the hadith where the Prophet likens the believers' seeing Allah to seeing the moon and the sun without obstruction, indicating clear visibility. The author also discusses the linguistic variants of the hadith, explaining the meanings of the different wordings (tudārūn, tadārūn, tudāmūn, tadāmūn) to clarify the intent. The second proof is the rational argument from the request of Musa (alayhi salam) in the Quran (7:143): if ru'ya were impossible, a prophet would not have asked for it, as prophets are knowledgeable and wise. Furthermore, Allah made the granting of the vision contingent upon the mountain's staying put, which is a possible event; thus ru'ya itself is possible. If it were impossible, the conditional statement of Allah would be false (khulf), which is impossible. Therefore ru'ya is possible (jā'iz). This argument is used to refute the Mu'tazilite denial of the vision.

Key Terms
رؤية
تمارون
تضارون
تضامون

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The vision of Allah will be unclear or doubtful like a partial moon.
Correction: The hadith emphasizes it will be like the full moon at zenith, with no obstruction or doubt.

Checkpoint
What do the variant readings of the hadith (tudārūn, tadārūn, tudāmūn, tadāmūn) collectively indicate about the vision of Allah?
A) That there will be disagreement among believers
B) That there will be no doubt, crowding, harm, or difficulty
C) That only a few will see Allah
D) That the vision will be like seeing the sun at noon

Key Takeaway
The hadith establishes that the vision of Allah on the Day of Resurrection will be clear, certain, and free from any obstruction or confusion.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You will see Allah as clearly as you see the full moon. Does that certainty make you long for that moment? What are you doing today to prepare for that meeting?
?
The Prophet removed all doubt from the Companions' hearts. Is there any doubt in your heart about whether you will meet your Lord? What step will you take this week to strengthen your conviction?
?
The hadith promises no harm, no crowding, no difficulty in that vision. Yet you cling to this world as if it will last forever. What fear keeps you from desiring that vision more than anything else?

The Logical Proof from Musa's Request
The author presents the rational argument from Musa's request and the conditional proof that ru'ya is possible.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rational argument from Musa's request to see Allah and the conditional proof that demonstrates ru'ya is possible.

Core Idea
If vision were impossible, a prophet wouldn't ask — and Allah wouldn't condition it on a possible event.

The author now turns to a rational proof rooted in the Quranic story of Musa, peace be upon him, who asked to see Allah. Since prophets are infallible in knowledge and wisdom, such a request could not be for something impossible. Moreover, Allah's response — conditioning the vision on the mountain's stability — further proves that ru'ya is possible, for the conditioned outcome is only possible if the condition is possible.

Guided Path
1
Analyze Musa's request
Read the verse and the author's argument: Musa's request proves ru'ya is permissible because prophets only ask for possible things.
2
Examine the conditional argument
Understand the structure: Allah conditions ru'ya on the mountain's settling. Since the condition is possible, the conditioned must be possible.
3
Reflect on the impossibility of khulf
If ru'ya were impossible, Allah's conditional would be false — impossible for Allah. So ru'ya is possible.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال رسول الله صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ وقد قال له الصحب الكرام يا رسول الله هل نرى ربنا يوم القيامة؟ هل تمارون» أي: تشكون «في القمر ليلة البدر، ليس دونه سحاب»، قالوا: لا يا رسول الله، قال: «فهل تمارون في الشمس ليس دونها سحاب»، قالوا: لا يا رسول الله، قال: فإنكم ترونه كذلك»، والحديث في الصحيحين» (۱). (1) أخرجه البخاري ( ح٤٥٨١) ، ومسلم ( ح ۱۸۳) من حديث أبي سعيد الخدري، وأخرجه البخاري (ح٦٥٧٣) ، ومسلم ( ح ۱۸۲) من حديث أبي هريرة رضى اللهُ عَنْهُ ، وفي الروايتين تضارون» بدل «تمارون». وفي شرح صحيح مسلم للنووي (۱۷/۳) روي «تضارون» بتشديد الراء، وبتخفيفها، والتاء مضمومة فيهما، ومعنى المشدد هل تضارون غيركم في حالة الرؤية بزحمة، أو مخالفة في الرؤية، أو غيرها لخفائه، كما تفعلون أول ليلة من الشهر، ومعنى المخفف هل يلحقكم في رؤيته ضير، وهو الضرر. وروي أيضًا تضامون بتشديد الميم، وتخفيفها، فمن شددها فتح التاء، ومن خففها ضم التاء، ومعنى المشدد هل تتضامون وتتلطفون في التوصل إلى رؤيته، ومعنى المخفف هل يلحقكم ضيم ، وهو المشقة والتعب، ومعناه لا يشتبه عليكم وترتابون فيه، فيعارض بعضكم بعضًا في رؤيته». وقد استدل على الجواز أيضًا بقوله تعالى: ﴿ رَبِّ أَرِنِي أَنظُرْ إِلَيْكَ ﴾ [الأعراف: ١٤٣]، فلو لم تكن جائزة ما طلبها سيدنا موسى عليه السلام، وإلا كان طلبها إما جهلًا بأحكام الألوهية، وإما سفها، أو عبئًا بطلب المحال، والأنبياء مُنَزَّهُونَ عن ذلك كله. وأن الله تعالى قد عَلْقَها على ممكن، وهو استقرار الجبل، والمعلق على الممكن ممكن، إذ معنى التعليق: الإخبار بوقوع المُعَلَّقِ عند ثبوت المُعَلَّقِ عليه، والمحال لا يقع على شيء من التقادير الممكنة، فلو لم تكن ممكنة، لزم الخلف في خبره تعالى، وهو محال.
The Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and grant him peace) said, while the noble Companions had said to him, 'O Messenger of Allah, will we see our Lord on the Day of Resurrection?' He said, 'Do you dispute (i.e., doubt) about the moon on the night of the full moon, without any cloud over it?' They said, 'No, O Messenger of Allah.' He said, 'Do you dispute about the sun without any cloud over it?' They said, 'No.' He said, 'Then you will see Him likewise.' And the hadith is in the two Sahihs. (1) Al-Bukhari narrated it (no. 4581) and Muslim (no. 183) from the hadith of Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, and Al-Bukhari (no. 6573) and Muslim (no. 182) from the hadith of Abu Hurayra, may Allah be pleased with him. In both narrations it is 'tudārūn' instead of 'tumārūn'. In the commentary of Sahih Muslim by al-Nawawi (17/3) it is narrated 'tudārūn' with a shadda on the ra', and with its lightening, and the ta' is damma in both. The meaning of the intensified form is: Do you crowd one another in the vision due to congestion, or disagree about it due to its subtlety, as you do on the first night of the month? The meaning of the lightened form is: Does any harm (ḍayr) befall you in seeing it? Harm. And it is also narrated 'tudāmūn' with a shadda on the mim and its lightening; whoever emphasizes it opens the ta', whoever lightens it puts damma on the ta'. The meaning of the intensified form is: Do you press together and seek delicacy in attaining the vision? The lightened form means: Does any oppression (ḍaym) befall you? i.e., hardship and fatigue. Its meaning: it is not ambiguous for you, nor do you doubt it, so that some of you contradict others concerning it.' And they also used as evidence for its permissibility the saying of Allah Almighty: 'My Lord, show me (Yourself) that I may look at You' (7:143). For if it were not permissible, our master Musa (peace be upon him) would not have asked for it, otherwise his request would have been either ignorance of the rulings of divinity, or foolishness, or a burden of asking for the impossible, and the prophets are free from all of that. And Allah Almighty made it contingent upon something possible, namely the mountain's settling, and what is contingent upon a possible thing is possible, for the meaning of condition is: informing of the occurrence of the conditioned thing upon the establishment of the condition, and the impossible does not occur under any possible circumstances. So if it were not possible, there would be a break in the information of Allah Almighty, which is impossible.
Source pages p. 78

Commentary
This passage provides two major proofs for the permissibility (and reality) of the vision of Allah (ru'ya) in the afterlife. The first proof is the hadith where the Prophet likens the believers' seeing Allah to seeing the moon and the sun without obstruction, indicating clear visibility. The author also discusses the linguistic variants of the hadith, explaining the meanings of the different wordings (tudārūn, tadārūn, tudāmūn, tadāmūn) to clarify the intent. The second proof is the rational argument from the request of Musa (alayhi salam) in the Quran (7:143): if ru'ya were impossible, a prophet would not have asked for it, as prophets are knowledgeable and wise. Furthermore, Allah made the granting of the vision contingent upon the mountain's staying put, which is a possible event; thus ru'ya itself is possible. If it were impossible, the conditional statement of Allah would be false (khulf), which is impossible. Therefore ru'ya is possible (jā'iz). This argument is used to refute the Mu'tazilite denial of the vision.

Key Terms
جواز
تعليق
محال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The request of Musa was a mistake or indicates doubt.
Correction: The text says prophets are free from ignorance and foolishness; Musa's request was for something possible.

Checkpoint
According to the author, what is the logical consequence if ru'ya were impossible?
A) Musa would not have asked
B) The mountain would have settled
C) Allah's conditional would be false
D) Both A and C

Key Takeaway
Musa's request and Allah's conditional response together prove that the vision of Allah is rationally possible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Prophet Musa, despite his high station, burned with desire to see Allah. Do you share that longing? Or have you become content with a distant, intellectual faith? What would it take for you to genuinely cry from your heart, 'Rabbi arini anzur ilayka'?
?
The proof shows that asking for the impossible would be ignorance or foolishness. In what areas of your life do you treat the possible as impossible—assuming Allah cannot change you, forgive you, or grant what you need? What step of faith are you avoiding because you think it's beyond reach?
?
Allah's conditional promise guarantees that ru'ya is truly possible in the Hereafter. Does this certainty shape how you live today? Or is it just a distant belief? How would your priorities change if you truly expected to see your Lord?

Unseen Matters
beginner
6 min
The Proofs for Seeing Allah in the Afterlife
Al-Mashat explains proofs for ru'yat Allah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the textual and rational proofs that establish the possibility of seeing Allah in the afterlife.

Core Idea
Seeing Allah is possible and confirmed by hadith, Qur'an, and reason.

Al-Mashat presents three lines of evidence for ru'yat Allah: the prophetic hadith comparing vision to seeing the moon, the verse of Musa asking to see Allah, and a rational argument based on possible suspension. This lesson unpacks each proof and its implications.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage and translation to get an overview of the proofs presented.
Notice the poetic line at the beginning which summarizes the topic.
2
Understand the hadith proof
The Prophet compared seeing Allah to seeing the moon and sun without doubt. Reflect on why this analogy is powerful for affirming possibility.
The variant wordings (tudarun, tudamun) emphasize clarity and absence of harm.
3
Analyze the Musa verse
Musa asked to see Allah; the fact that a prophet made such a request proves it is not impossible, as prophets are free from ignorance and frivolity.
The request was conditional on the mountain settling, indicating possibility.
4
Grasp the rational argument
Allah suspended the granting of the request on a possible event (mountain settling). If seeing were impossible, such suspension would be invalid, leading to a contradiction in Allah's report.
Understand the concept of ta'liq (suspension) and its logical implications.
5
Note the final clarification
Seeing Allah in this world awake is impossible, but the Prophet saw Him with his eyesight on the Night of Isra'. This limits the claim to the afterlife.
This guards against overgeneralization.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي
القمر ليلة البدر، ليس دونه سحاب»، قالوا: لا يا رسول الله، قال: «فهل تمارون في الشمس ليس دونها سحاب»، قالوا: لا يا رسول الله، قال: فإنكم ترونه كذلك»، والحديث في الصحيحين» (۱).
(1) أخرجه البخاري ( ح٤٥٨١) ، ومسلم ( ح ۱۸۳) من حديث أبي سعيد الخدري، وأخرجه البخاري (ح٦٥٧٣) ، ومسلم ( ح ۱۸۲) من حديث أبي هريرة رضى اللهُ عَنْهُ ، وفي الروايتين تضارون» بدل «تمارون». وفي شرح صحيح مسلم للنووي (۱۷/۳) روي «تضارون» بتشديد الراء، وبتخفيفها، والتاء مضمومة فيهما، ومعنى المشدد هل تضارون غيركم في حالة الرؤية بزحمة، أو مخالفة في الرؤية، أو غيرها لخفائه، كما تفعلون أول ليلة من الشهر، ومعنى المخفف هل يلحقكم في رؤيته ضير، وهو الضرر.
وروي أيضًا تضامون بتشديد الميم، وتخفيفها، فمن شددها فتح التاء، ومن خففها ضم التاء، ومعنى المشدد هل تتضامون وتتلطفون في التوصل إلى رؤيته، ومعنى المخفف هل يلحقكم ضيم ، وهو المشقة والتعب، ومعناه لا يشتبه عليكم وترتابون فيه، فيعارض بعضكم بعضًا في رؤيته». وقد استدل على الجواز أيضًا بقوله تعالى: ﴿ رَبِّ أَرِنِي أَنظُرْ إِلَيْكَ ﴾ [الأعراف: ١٤٣]، فلو لم تكن جائزة ما طلبها سيدنا موسى عليه السلام، وإلا كان طلبها إما جهلًا بأحكام الألوهية، وإما سفها، أو عبئًا بطلب المحال، والأنبياء مُنَزِّهُونَ عن ذلك كله.
وأن الله تعالى قد عَلْقَها على ممكن، وهو استقرار الجبل، والمعلق على الممكن ممكن، إذ معنى التعليق: الإخبار بوقوع المُعَلَّقِ عند ثبوت المُعَلَّقِ عليه، والمحال لا يقع على شيء من التقادير الممكنة، فلو لم تكن ممكنة، لزم الخلف في خبره تعالى، وهو محال.
واجْزِمُ أَخِي بِرُؤيَةِ الإِلهِ * فِي جَنَّةِ الْخُلْدِ بِلا تَنَاهِي | ٤٩ ح شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي أما رؤيته تعالى في الدنيا يقظة فغير واقعة، أما في المنام فقد وقعت لكثير من الصالحين منهم الإمام أحمد بن حنبل رحمه الله تعالى، فقد نُقل عنه إنه رأى ربه عَزَوَجَلَّ مرارا كثيرة في المنام. والصحيح أن نبينا صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ رأى ربه بالبصر ليلة الإسراء لا بالقلب فقط، والله أعلم.
And affirm, my brother, the seeing of Allah * in the garden of eternity without end. Commentary of Sheikh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat al-Maliki al-Maliki. 'The moon on the night of the full moon, without cloud before it.' They said: No, O Messenger of Allah. He said: 'Do you dispute concerning the sun without cloud before it?' They said: No, O Messenger of Allah. He said: 'Then you will see Him similarly.' And the hadith is in the two Sahihs. (1) (1) Bukhari (4581) and Muslim (183) from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, and Bukhari (6573) and Muslim (182) from Abu Hurayra, may Allah be pleased with him. In both narrations 'tudarun' instead of 'tumarun'. In Sharh Sahih Muslim by al-Nawawi (17/3) it is narrated 'tudarun' with shadda on the ra', and with its tashdid, and the ta is damma in both, meaning of the shadda: do you harm others in the state of seeing due to crowding, or disagreement in seeing, or otherwise because of its hiddenness, as you do on the first night of the month. And meaning of the light: does harm befall you in seeing Him, i.e. injury. And also narrated 'tudamun' with shadda on the mim and with lightening, so whoever makes it shadda opens the ta' and whoever makes it light damma the ta', meaning of the shadda: do you crowd and show kindness in arriving at seeing Him, and meaning of the light: does oppression befall you, i.e. hardship and fatigue, meaning: it is not ambiguous to you and you do not doubt it, so some of you oppose others in seeing Him. And evidence for the permissibility is also adduced from His saying, exalted: 'My Lord, show me [Yourself] that I may look upon You' [al-A'raf 7:143]; for if it were not permissible, our master Moses, peace be upon him, would not have asked for it, otherwise his asking would have been either ignorance of the rulings of divinity, or foolishness, or a burden of asking for the impossible, and the prophets are exalted above all that. And that Allah, exalted, has suspended it upon something possible, namely the settling of the mountain, and that which is suspended upon the possible is possible, for the meaning of suspension is: informing of the occurrence of the suspended thing when that upon which it is suspended is established, and the impossible does not occur on any possible estimation, so if it were not possible, there would be a breach in the report of the exalted, and that is impossible. And affirm, my brother, the seeing of Allah * in the garden of eternity without end | 49 h Commentary of Sheikh Hasan Muhammad al-Mashat al-Makki al-Maliki. As for seeing Him, exalted, in this world awake, it does not occur. As for in sleep, it has occurred to many of the righteous, among them Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, may Allah have mercy on him, for it is reported from him that he saw his Lord, Almighty and Majestic, many times in sleep. And the correct view is that our Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, saw his Lord with his eyesight on the Night of Isra', not with the heart only, and Allah knows best.
Source pages p. 79

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on the line of poetry 'And affirm, my brother, the seeing of Allah in the garden of eternity without end'. The commentator, Hasan al-Mashat, begins by citing a well-known hadith in which the Prophet compares seeing Allah in the afterlife to seeing the moon and the sun clearly. He then discusses variant wordings in the hadith ('tudarun' vs 'tudamun') and explains their nuances, clarifying that the meaning is that there is no doubt or difficulty in seeing Allah, just as there is no doubt in seeing the moon on a clear night. Next, he adduces the Qur'anic verse where Musa asks to see Allah, arguing that Musa would not have asked if it were impossible, as prophets are infallible and do not engage in futile requests. He then presents a rational argument: Allah suspended the granting of the request on a possible event (the mountain settling), and what is suspended on a possible is itself possible; if it were impossible, it would entail a contradiction in Allah's report. Finally, he addresses the issue of seeing Allah in this world: it does not occur while awake, but it has occurred in dreams for many righteous people, such as Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, and the correct view is that the Prophet saw Allah with his eyesight on the Night of Isra', not only with the heart.

Key Terms
رؤية الله
تمارون
تضارون
تضامون
التعليق
الممكن
الخلف في الخبر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think seeing Allah is impossible or purely metaphorical.
Correction: The hadith, Qur'anic verse, and rational argument show it is possible and will occur in the afterlife. It is not possible in this world awake, but the Prophet saw Him in the Night Journey.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is NOT a proof cited by Al-Mashat for the possibility of seeing Allah?
A) The hadith about seeing the moon and the sun
B) The verse of Musa asking to see Allah
C) The miracle of the splitting of the moon
D) The argument from suspension on a possible event

Key Takeaway
Seeing Allah in the afterlife is definitively established by the Prophet's analogy, Musa's request, and the logical principle of suspension on a possible event.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed you will see Allah as clearly as the full moon, what would you change about how you live today?
?
The companions had no doubt about seeing the sun and moon — do you have any doubt about seeing Allah? Where does that doubt come from, and what does the evidence in this lesson demand you to do about it?
?
Musa asked to see Allah, and his request was not rejected but used to teach possibility. Have you ever held back from asking Allah for something you thought was impossible? What does this incident say about your expectations of His mercy and power?

Sam'iyyat: The Third Division and Belief in the Account
Introduction to Sam'iyyat and obligation of al-Hisab

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the division of Kalam into three parts and the obligation of belief in the Account (al-Hisab) as a core element of Sam'iyyat.

Core Idea
Sam'iyyat completes Kalam by addressing revealed eschatological truths.

In this passage, the commentator introduces Sam'iyyat as the third major division of Islamic theology, following Ilahiyyat and Nubuwwat. This section deals with matters heard from revelation, particularly eschatological events. The first and most fundamental of these is the belief in the Account on the Day of Judgment, which is obligatory for every morally responsible person.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Notice how the author defines the three divisions and then focuses on the Account.
The passage includes a Quranic verse to support the definition.
2
Identify the three divisions of Kalam
The passage explicitly states that this science has three sections. Find the names of all three and note which ones have been covered already.
3
Understand the nature of al-Hisab
The Account is defined as Allah stopping His servants in the Place of Gathering. The passage describes various types of accounting: easy, difficult, secret, open, by favor, by justice.
The easy account is called 'al-ʿarḍ' (presentation).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
بالحـ [ مبحث السمعيات ] ٥٤ | ويَلْزَمُ الإِيمَانُ بِالْحِسَابِ * والْحَشْرِ والعِقَابِ والثَّوَابِ
شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي عب
ثم إذا علمت هذا فاعلم أن هذا العلم على ثلاثة أقسام: الهيات ونبويات، وقد تقدما، وسمعيات وهو القسم الثالث المشار إليه بقوله: ( ويلزم) على المكلف أي ويجب عليه (الإيمان) والتصديق (بالحساب)، وهو توقيف الله عباده في المحشر على أعمالهم، قال تعالى: ﴿ وَتُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَمَةِ كِتَبًا يَلْقَهُ مَنشُورًا أَقْرَأْ كِتَبَكَ كَفَى بِنَفْسِكَ الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيبًا ) (۱) ، وكيفية الحساب مختلفة، فمنه اليسير ومنه العسير، ومنه السر والجهر والفضل والعدل، على حسب الأعمال، فيغفر لمن يشاء ويعذب من يشاء، وذلك بعد أخذهم الكتب لقوله تعالى: ﴿ فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتَابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ، فَسَوْفَ يُحَاسَبُ حِسَابًا يَسِيرًا وَيَنقَلِبُ إِلَىٰ أَهْلِهِ مَسْرُورًا ﴾ (۲).
والحساب اليسير هو المعبر عنه بالعرض، بأن يعرض الله جَلَجَلَالُهُ على عبده بعض ذنبه، ويلقي عليه كنفه وستره، فيقول الله تعالى: سَتَرْتُهَا عَلَيْكَ فِي الدُّنيا وأنا اليوم أغفرها لك»، روى الشيخان (۳) عن عائشة رضايته عنها قالت: قال النبي صلى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّم : «لَيْسَ أَحَدٌ يُحَاسَبُ
Al-Hisab [of] [the section on Sam'iyyat] 54 | And belief in the Account, the Gathering, the Punishment, and the Reward is obligatory. Commentary of Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mishshāt al-Mālikī, 'Abb. Then, when you know this, know that this science is divided into three sections: Ilāhiyyāt and Nubuwwāt, which have preceded, and Sam‘iyyāt, which is the third section referred to by his saying: (And obligatory) upon the morally responsible person, i.e., belief and affirmation (in the Account) is required of him. It is Allah's stopping of His servants in the Place of Gathering for their deeds. Allah Most High said: {And We shall bring out for him on the Day of Resurrection a book which he will see spread open. Read your book; sufficient is your soul this Day as a reckoner against you} (1). And the manner of the Account varies: some is easy, some is difficult; some is secret and open, favor and justice, according to deeds. He forgives whom He wills and punishes whom He wills, and that after they receive their books, for His saying: {Then as for him who is given his book in his right hand, he will be reckoned with an easy reckoning, and return to his family joyful} (2). And the easy account is what is called the presentation, by which Allah, may His glory be exalted, presents to His servant some of his sins and casts upon him His care and veil, and Allah says: 'I concealed them for you in the world and today I forgive you.' The Two Shaykhs narrated from ‘Ā’isha, may Allah be pleased with her, who said: The Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, said: 'None will be reckoned
Source pages p. 87

Commentary
This passage begins with the verse line (54) stating that belief in the Account (al-Ḥisāb), the Gathering (al-Ḥashr), the Punishment (al-‘Iqāb), and the Reward (al-Thawāb) is obligatory. The commentary then explains that this science (ʿilm al-kalām) is divided into three sections: Ilāhiyyāt (divine matters), Nubuwwāt (prophetic matters), which have already been covered, and Sam‘iyyāt (matters heard from revelation), which is the third section. The verse's phrase 'and obligated' means that it is incumbent upon the morally responsible person (mukallaf) to believe and affirm in the Account. The Account is defined as Allah stopping His servants in the Place of Gathering (al-Maḥshar) to inform them of their deeds. A Quranic verse from Sura al-Isrā' is cited to support this. The commentators then discuss the varieties of the Account: some are easy (yasīr), some difficult (ʿasīr), some secret (sirr), some open (jahr), some by favor (faḍl), some by justice (ʿadl), depending on deeds. After receiving their books (the records), people are either forgiven or punished. Another verse from Sura al-Inshiqāq is cited to show that those given their book in their right hand will receive an easy reckoning and be joyful. The easy account is further explained as al-ʿarḍ (presentation), where Allah presents to the servant some of his sins, covers them with His care and veil, and says He concealed them in the world and forgives them today. A hadith from al-Bukhārī and Muslim (the Two Shaykhs) is quoted, starting to mention that no one will be reckoned... but the text cuts off. The lesson thus establishes Sam‘iyyāt as a core division of Kalām and details the obligation, nature, and types of the eschatological Account.

Key Terms
سمعيات
الحساب
الحشر
العقاب
الثواب
العرض

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Only Ilahiyyat and Nubuwwat are essential; Sam'iyyat is optional or secondary.
Correction: Sam'iyyat is a distinct obligatory division of kalam concerning revealed eschatological matters, as stated explicitly in the passage.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what are the three divisions of the science of Kalam?
A) Ilahiyyat, Nubuwwat, and Sam'iyyat
B) Tawhid, Adl, and Nubuwwa
C) Usul, Furu', and Adab
D) Aqaid, Fiqh, and Tasawwuf

Key Takeaway
Belief in the Account (al-Hisab) is an obligatory element of Sam'iyyat, the third division of Kalam dealing with revealed eschatological truths.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
If the Account can be easy or difficult based on deeds, what are you doing today to ensure an easy reckoning? Name one concrete action you will take before you sleep tonight.
?
How many of your daily actions are done with the consciousness that they will be presented to Allah? If you are honest, are you living as if the Account is real? What needs to change starting now?

The Doctrine of al-Hisab: Necessity and Types
Definition of al-Hisab and its two types with evidence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic doctrine of al-Hisab (the Account), its necessity, and its two types—the easy presentation (al-‘ard) and the severe scrutiny (al-munaqasha)—based on Quran and Hadith.

Core Idea
The Account is merciful: an easy presentation for believers, not a harsh interrogation.

This lesson addresses the third division of Islamic theology: the audible matters (sam‘iyyāt). After establishing the necessity of faith in the Day of Judgment, the author now explains one of its key components—the Account (al-Ḥisāb). Through Quranic verses and prophetic hadith, we learn that the nature of the account varies and that for the righteous it is a gentle presentation leading to forgiveness.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once to get the overall structure, then read the translation. Notice how the author defines al-Hisab and then describes its variations.
The passage begins by situating the topic within the third division of kalam.
2
Identify the definition and types
Find where the author defines al-Hisab as God calling His servants to account for their deeds. Then note the different types: easy/severe, secret/public, favor/justice.
The key Quranic verses are cited to support each aspect.
3
Focus on the easy account
Read the explanation of the easy account (al-ḥisāb al-yasīr) as a presentation (al-‘arḍ). Then study the hadith from ‘Ā’isha that distinguishes this gentle review from harsh scrutiny.
The Prophet's prayer for an easy account shows its desirability.

٥٤ | ويَلْزَمُ الإِيمَانُ بِالْحِسَابِ * والْحَشْرِ والعِقَابِ والثَّوَابِ
شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط الملكي المالكي عب
ثم إذا علمت هذا فاعلم أن هذا العلم على ثلاثة أقسام: الهيات ونبويات، وقد تقدما، وسمعيات وهو القسم الثالث المشار إليه بقوله: (ويلزم) على المكلف أي ويجب عليه (الإيمان) والتصديق (بالحساب)، وهو توقيف الله عباده في المحشر على أعمالهم، قال تعالى: {وَتُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَمَةِ كِتَبًا يَلْقَهُ مَنشُورًا أَقْرَأْ كِتَبَكَ كَفَى بِنَفْسِكَ الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيبًا} (١)، وكيفية الحساب مختلفة، فمنه اليسير ومنه العسير، ومنه السر والجهر والفضل والعدل، على حسب الأعمال، فيغفر لمن يشاء ويعذب من يشاء، وذلك بعد أخذهم الكتب لقوله تعالى: {فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتَابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ، فَسَوْفَ يُحَاسَبُ حِسَابًا يَسِيرًا وَيَنقَلِبُ إِلَىٰ أَهْلِهِ مَسْرُورًا} (٢). والحساب اليسير هو المعبر عنه بالعرض، بأن يعرض الله جل جلاله على عبده بعض ذنبه، ويلقي عليه كنفه وستره، فيقول الله تعالى: سترتها عليك في الدنيا وأنا اليوم أغفرها لك، روى الشيخان عن عائشة رضي الله عنها قالت: قال النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم: «ليس أحد يحاسب إلا هلك»، قلت: يا رسول الله جعلني الله فداك، أليس الله تعالى يقول: {فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتَابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ، فَسَوْفَ يُحَاسَبُ حِسَابًا يَسِيرًا}، قال: ذاك العرض يعرضون، ومن نوقش الحساب هلك. وأخرج أحمد وغيره عن عائشة رضي الله عنها، سمعت رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم يقول في بعض صلاته: «اللهم حاسبني حسابا يسيرا»، فلما انصرف عليه الصلاة والسلام قلت يا رسول الله ما الحساب اليسير قال: «أن ينظر في كتابه فيتجاوز له عنه».
Chapter on the Audible Matters (Sam'iyyat). 54 | Faith is required in the Account, and the Gathering, and the Punishment, and the Reward. Commentary by Shaykh Hasan Muhammad al-Mishshāt al-Mālikī al-Mālikī [؟]. Then, when you know this, know that this science is of three divisions: divine matters (ilāhiyyāt) and prophetic matters (nubuwwiyāt), which have preceded, and audible matters (sam'iyyāt), which is the third division referred to by his statement: 'And required' is upon the duty-bound, i.e., required upon him is faith and affirmation in the Account (al-ḥisāb), and it is God's calling His servants to account in the place of assembly (al-maḥshar) for their deeds. He, exalted, said: {And We bring out for him on the Day of Resurrection a book which he will find spread open, [saying]: 'Read your book. Sufficient is your soul this Day against you as a reckoner'} (17:13-14). The modality of the account varies: among it is the easy and among it is the severe, and among it is secret and public, and favor and justice, according to the deeds. He forgives whom He wills and punishes whom He wills, and that after they take the books. For His statement: {Then as for he who is given his book in his right hand, he will be judged with an easy account and return to his people happy} (84:7-9). The easy account is that which is expressed by the presentation (al-‘arḍ), which is that God, may His majesty be exalted, presents to His servant some of his sins and casts upon him His protection and veil, and says: 'I covered them for you in the world and today I forgive them for you.' The two shaykhs (al-Bukhārī and Muslim) narrated from ‘Ā’isha, may God be pleased with her, she said: The Prophet, may God bless him and grant him peace, said: 'No one is called to account except that he is destroyed.' I said: 'O Messenger of God, may God ransom me with you, does not God Almighty say: {Then as for he who is given his book in his right hand, he will be judged with an easy account}?' He said: 'That is the presentation; they are presented. And whoever is scrutinized in the account is destroyed.' And Aḥmad and others narrated from ‘Ā’isha, may God be pleased with her: she heard the Messenger of God, may God bless him and grant him peace, say in some of his prayers: 'O God, call me to account with an easy account.' When he finished, peace and blessings be upon him, I said: 'O Messenger of God, what is the easy account?' He said: 'That He looks into his book and excuses him from it.'
Source pages p. 87

Commentary
This passage is from the section on Audible Matters (Sam'iyyat), which is the third division of kalam. It opens with the poem line requiring faith in the Account, Gathering, Punishment, and Reward. The commentary first defines the Account (al-hisab) as God's calling His servants to account for their deeds in the place of assembly. It cites Quran 17:13-14 to support the idea that each person will receive a book. The author then explains that the account varies in nature—easy or severe, secret or public—depending on one's deeds. He particularly focuses on the easy account, which is called 'presentation' (al-‘ard). In this presentation, God shows the servant some of his sins but covers them and forgives. Two hadiths are provided: one from Bukhari and Muslim where the Prophet distinguishes the easy account (presentation) from scrutiny (munaqasha) which leads to destruction; another from Ahmad where the Prophet explains the easy account as God looking into the book and overlooking the sins. This lesson establishes the necessity of believing in the account and clarifies its types, emphasizing God's mercy in the easy account.

العرض
اليسير
العسير
الموقشة

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think all accounting on Judgment Day is a harsh interrogation leading to punishment.
Correction: The lesson distinguishes the easy account (presentation) where sins are covered and forgiven, from the severe scrutiny (munaqasha) which leads to destruction.

Checkpoint

Key Takeaway
Belief in the Account (al-Hisab) is obligatory, and the 'easy account' is a gentle presentation of sins leading to forgiveness, as taught by the Prophet.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?

?
In this lesson, the Prophet said, 'من نوقش الحساب هلك' (whoever is subjected to scrutiny perishes). How does this motivate you to seek forgiveness and good deeds, so that you may be among those who receive the easy account? What is one change you can make today?
