Belief in al-Hashr: The Gathering
Definition of al-Hashr with Quran and hadith evidence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic doctrine of al-Hashr (the Gathering), its definition, scriptural proofs, and the varying states of people on that day.

Core Idea
Belief in the Gathering is obligatory: bodies and souls are collected and driven to the standing place after resurrection.

This passage from al-Bahja al-Saniyya explains the second requirement in the section on Audible Matters: belief in al-Hashr. The author defines it clearly and supports it with Quran and hadith, also noting that people will be in different ranks. Let's read the source text carefully.

Guided Path
1
Read the source and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation to get the literal meaning.
Notice that the passage ends mid-sentence; we focus on the core definition and proofs.
2
Identify the definition and proofs
Find the definition of al-Hashr (gathering of bodies and souls, driving to al-mawqif). Then note the two proofs: Quran 19:85-86 and the hadith about being gathered barefoot.
The author also explains the word 'ghurla' in the hadith.
3
Reflect on the varying ranks
The passage mentions that people's ranks in the gathering differ: some ride, some walk, based on deeds. Consider how this emphasizes accountability.
This shows that the gathering is not uniform; actions matter.

ويجب الإيمان بـ (الحشر) أي: جمع الأجسام والأرواح، وسوقها إلى الموقف بعد بعثهم من قبورهم المسمى بالنشر، قال الله تعالى: {يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ الْمُتَّقِينَ إِلَى الرَّحْمَنِ وَفَدًا وَنَسُوقُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ إِلَى جَهَنَّمَ وِرْدًا} (٢). وفي الحديث الشريف: «إنكم محشورون إلى الله تعالى حفاة عراة غرلا»، جمع أغرل، وهو القلفة المخرجة بالختان، والآيات والأحاديث في ذلك كثيرة جدا. ومراتب الناس في الحشر مختلفة، فمنهم الراكب ومنهم الماشي على رجليه على حسب الأعمال، وأول من تنشق عنه الأرض
And faith is required in the Gathering (al-ḥashr), i.e., the gathering of bodies and souls, and their driving to the standing place after their resurrection from their graves, which is called the Spreading (al-nashr). God Almighty said: {On the Day We gather the righteous to the Most Merciful as a delegation, and We drive the criminals to Hell as a thirsty herd} (19:85-86). And in the noble hadith: 'You will be gathered to God Almighty barefoot, naked, and uncircumcised' — 'ghurla' is the plural of 'aghralu', which is the foreskin removed by circumcision. The verses and hadiths on this are very many. The ranks of people in the gathering vary: among them are the riders and among them are the walkers on their feet, according to deeds. And the first from whom the earth splits open...
Source pages p. 88

Commentary
This passage continues the section on Audible Matters, now addressing the second element: the Gathering (al-Hashr). The author defines al-Hashr as the gathering of bodies and souls and their being driven to the standing place (al-mawqif) after resurrection from graves, which is also called al-Nashr (the spreading). He supports this with Quran 19:85-86, which mentions gathering the righteous as a delegation to the Most Merciful and driving the criminals to Hell. He then cites a hadith describing the state of people on that day: barefoot, naked, and uncircumcised, explaining the word 'ghurla'. He notes that there are many more verses and hadiths on this topic. Finally, he mentions that people's ranks in the gathering differ: some ride, some walk, based on their deeds. The text ends mid-sentence, but the point is that belief in the gathering is obligatory, and its details are known from revelation.

Faith in the Reckoning and the Easy Reckoning
Author states obligation and explains via two hadiths.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that belief in the Reckoning is obligatory and that the 'easy reckoning' is a merciful presentation rather than a harsh scrutiny.

Core Idea
The reckoning for believers is a merciful review, not a harsh interrogation.

This lesson covers a foundational belief in the Hereafter: the Reckoning. The author states the obligation of believing in it, and then the commentary clarifies its nature through two prophetic hadiths. You will learn the distinction between a mere presentation and a strict scrutiny, and how the Prophet himself prayed for an easy reckoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the source passage
Read the Arabic matn and the two hadiths carefully, then read the translation. Let the text speak for itself before analyzing.
Focus on understanding the key terminology.
2
Identify the key terms
Note the terms 'al-hisab', 'al-ard', 'al-munaqasha', and 'al-hisab al-yasir'. These are central to the doctrine.
Look up their meanings if needed.
3
Understand the distinction
The Prophet distinguishes between presentation (al-ard) where deeds are shown but not scrutinized, and scrutiny (al-munaqasha) which leads to perdition.
This distinction is the key insight of the first hadith.
4
Reflect on the easy reckoning
The easy reckoning is defined in the second hadith as Allah looking at one's book and pardoning. This shows Allah's mercy.
Consider how this defines the believer's hope.
5
Internalize the lesson
Recognize that this teaching brings hope and encourages believers to seek Allah's mercy while being mindful of their deeds.
This lesson should foster both hope and accountability.

I said: O Messenger of Allah, may Allah ransom me, does not Allah the Exalted say: 'Then as for him who is given his book in his right hand, he will be reckoned with an easy reckoning.' When he finished the prayer, peace be upon him, I said: O Messenger of Allah, what is the easy reckoning? He said: 'That He looks at his book and pardons him for it.'

Commentary
This passage defines the obligation of believing in the Reckoning (al-ḥisāb) and provides two hadiths to clarify its nature. The matn declares that faith in the Reckoning is obligatory. The commentary begins by citing a hadith where the Prophet distinguishes between the 'presentation' (al-ʿarḍ) and the 'scrutiny' (al-munāqasha). The easy reckoning (ḥisāb yasīr) is interpreted as a mere presentation where sins are overlooked. The second hadith from Aisha confirms that the Prophet himself prayed for an easy reckoning, and he explained it as Allah looking at one's book and pardoning. Thus, the nature of the reckoning is not a harsh interrogation but a merciful review for believers.

The Obligation to Believe in al-Hashr
Definition and proof of al-Hashr.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the meaning of al-Hashr and the scriptural evidence for it.

Core Idea
Al-Hashr is the physical gathering of bodies and souls after resurrection.

In this lesson, we explore the Islamic belief in the Gathering (al-Hashr). The author defines it as the assembly of bodies and souls driven to the standing place, and supports it with Quranic verses and prophetic hadith. This foundational belief is part of the sam'iyyat (matters known from revelation).

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Begin by reading the Arabic definition and proofs carefully. Note the key term الحشر and its companion النشر.
The passage starts with 'ويجب الإيمان' emphasizing obligation.
2
Identify the definition
Find the precise definition of al-Hashr: it is the gathering of bodies and souls, and their driving to the standing place after resurrection.
3
Examine the Quranic proof
Read the verse from Surah Maryam (19:85-86) and see how it contrasts the gathering of the righteous and the criminals.
This verse is a direct proof.
4
Consider the hadith proof
The hadith about being gathered barefoot, naked, and uncircumcised adds a vivid dimension. Reflect on its meaning.
The hadith emphasizes the universal and stark nature of the gathering.
5
Reflect on the multitude of evidence
The author states that verses and hadiths on this are very many, indicating the importance of this belief.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب الإيمان بـ (الحشر) أي: جمع الأجسام والأرواح، وسوقها إلى الموقف بعد بعثهم من قبورهم المسمى بالنشر، قال الله تعالى: يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ الْمُتَّقِينَ إِلَى الرَّحْمَنِ وَفَدَا وَنَسُوقُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ إِلَى جَهَنَّمَ وِرْدَا (۲) . وفي الحديث الشريف: «أنكم محشورون إلى الله تعالى حفاةً عراةٌ غُرلا» (۳)، جمع أغرل، وهو القلفة المخرجة بالختان، والآيات والأحاديث في ذلك كثيرة جدا.
ومراتب الناس في الحشر مختلفة، فمنهم الراكب ومنهم الماشي على رجليه على حسب الأعمال، وأول من تنشق عنه الأرض المصطفى عَلَيْهِ الصَّلَاةُ وَالسَّلَامُ ، ثم صاحباه ، ثم أهل البقيع، ثم أهل مكة ثم من بقي.
And it is obligatory to believe in al-ḥashr (the Gathering), i.e., the gathering of bodies and souls, and their driving to the standing-place after their being raised from their graves, which is called al-nashr (the Spreading). Allah the Exalted said: 'On the day We gather the righteous to the Most Merciful as a delegation, and We drive the criminals to Hell as a thirsty herd.' And in the noble hadith: 'You will be gathered to Allah the Exalted barefoot, naked, uncircumcised' — plural of aghral, which is the foreskin removed by circumcision. And the verses and hadiths on that are very many. And the ranks of people in the gathering are different: among them are riders, and among them are walkers on their feet according to deeds. And the first from whom the earth splits open is the Chosen One, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, then his two companions, then the people of al-Baqīʿ, then the people of Mecca, then those who remain.
Source pages p. 88

Commentary
This passage defines the obligation of believing in al-ḥashr (the Gathering), which is the assembly of bodies and souls after resurrection and their driving to the place of standing. The definition is followed by a Quranic proof from Surah Maryam, verses 85–86, describing the gathering of the righteous as honored guests and the criminals being driven to Hell. A hadith adds that people will be gathered barefoot, naked, and uncircumcised, emphasizing the stark reality of the event. The commentary then discusses the different ranks of people in the gathering: some ride, some walk, depending on their deeds. The first to be resurrected is the Prophet Muhammad, then his two companions (Abu Bakr and Umar), then the people of al-Baqīʿ (the cemetery in Medina), then the people of Mecca, and then the rest. This shows the hierarchical nature of the resurrection.

Key Terms
الحشر
النشر
الموقف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The gathering is only spiritual or metaphorical.
Correction: The text explicitly states it is the gathering of bodies and souls, and the hadith describes physical states (barefoot, naked), proving it is a physical event.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what does al-Hashr refer to?
A) The spreading of the scrolls
B) The gathering of bodies and souls to the standing place
C) The punishment of the grave
D) The weighing of deeds

Key Takeaway
Al-Hashr is the physical gathering of all humans after resurrection, proven by the Quran and hadith.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You just read that on the Day of Gathering you will be barefoot, naked, and uncircumcised – completely exposed before Allah. What are you currently hiding behind in this life that will be stripped away on that day? What concrete step can you take today to address that fault in private before it is exposed publicly?
?
The verse contrasts the righteous being honoured as delegates to the Most Merciful with criminals driven like thirsty cattle to Hell. If you were to die tonight, which group do you truly believe you would belong to – not based on hope, but on your current actions? If you are uncertain, what is one specific change you can make this week to move towards the righteous?
?
Belief in al-Hashr is obligatory, and it means you will stand before Allah in full vulnerability. If you were to live today with absolute certainty of that moment, how would it change the way you speak to others or what you look at on your phone? Commit to one action today that reflects that belief and do it within the next hour.

The Ranks of People on the Day of Gathering
Description of ranks and order of resurrection.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that people are gathered in different ranks according to their deeds, and the order of resurrection begins with the Prophet Muhammad.

Core Idea
The righteous ride, others walk; the Prophet is resurrected first.

This lesson focuses on the second part of the passage, which describes the varying ranks of people during the Gathering. Some will be riding, others walking, based on their deeds. The author also specifies the order of resurrection, beginning with the Prophet, then his companions, then others. This hierarchy reflects divine justice and honor.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the second part of the source text, starting from 'ومراتب الناس في الحشر مختلفة'.
This section is a description of ranks.
2
Identify the two categories
Notice that people are divided into riders and walkers, and that this depends on their deeds.
This shows that the manner of gathering is not uniform.
3
Learn the order of resurrection
The author lists the first to be resurrected: the Prophet, then Abu Bakr and Umar, then the people of al-Baqi', then the people of Mecca, then the rest.
This order honors the Prophet and his close companions.
4
Reflect on the significance
Consider how this rank order reflects the status of individuals and the importance of deeds.

?

Faith in Punishment: Obligation, Types, and Proof
The author explains the obligation of believing in punishment, its types, and proof from Quran.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation to believe in punishment, its types (grave, gathering), the Quranic proof for punishment in the grave, and the different outcomes for disbelievers and sinners.

Core Idea
Belief in punishment is obligatory, proven by the Quran, and varies by deed.

This lesson explores a key component of the samiyyat: belief in punishment. The author explains that punishment is an essential article of faith, occurring in the grave and on the Day of Judgment. He provides a Quranic proof and distinguishes between the eternal punishment of disbelievers and the conditional fate of sinful believers, including the possibility of intercession.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then read the English translation. Let the author's words settle in your mind before breaking them down.
2
Identify the obligation and types
The passage states that belief in punishment is obligatory. List the types of punishment mentioned: in the grave, in the gathering, and after, with examples like snakes, scorpions, beating.
Notice that punishment is for both sins and disbelief.
3
Study the Quranic proof
The proof for punishment in the grave is the verse about the family of Pharaoh (40:46). Understand why this verse is taken as evidence for barzakh punishment.
The verse mentions exposure to the Fire morning and evening, which is before the Day of Judgment.
4
Distinguish final outcomes
Disbelievers face eternal punishment. Sinful believers are subject to Allah's will: forgiveness, purification through punishment, or intercession. Note the three possibilities.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الإيمان بالعقاب والعقاب) بالجر معطوف على الحساب، أي: ومما يجب على المكلف الإيمان به واعتقاده العقاب في القبر على الذنوب والكفر، وفي الحشر وبعده بأنواع مختلفة على حسب الأعمال، فمنهم من يعاقب بالحيات والعقارب، ومنهم من يعاقب بالضرب، ومنهم من يعاقب بغير ذلك، والدليل على عقاب القبر قوله تعالى: ﴿ النَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْهَا غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوا ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ الْعَذَابِ ) (١) ، ومآل الكفار الخلود فيها، وأما أرباب المعاصي من المؤمنين فإلى مشيئة الله تعالى فمنهم من يغفر الله له، ولا يدخله النار، ومنهم من يطهر فيخرج منها بعد التطهير، ومنهم من تناله شفاعة سيدنا محمد صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ .
Faith in punishment — and 'al-ʿiqāb' (punishment) is in the genitive case, coordinated with al-ḥisāb (the reckoning) — i.e., it is among what is obligatory for the responsible person to believe in and to hold as doctrine: punishment in the grave for sins and disbelief, and in the gathering and after it in various types according to deeds. Among them are those who are punished by snakes and scorpions, and among them are those who are punished by beating, and among them are those who are punished by other than that. And the proof for punishment of the grave is His statement, the Exalted: 'The Fire — they are exposed to it morning and evening. And on the Day the Hour will be established: "Admit the family of Pharaoh to the severest punishment."' (40:46). And the final abode of the disbelievers is eternal in it. As for the sinners among the believers, it is subject to the will of Allah the Exalted: among them are those whom Allah forgives and does not admit to the Fire, and among them are those who are purified and then come out of it after purification, and among them are those who receive the intercession of our master Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace.
Source pages p. 89

Commentary
This passage explains the obligation of believing in punishment (al-ʿiqāb), which is coordinated with the reckoning in the matn. The punishment occurs in the grave for sins and disbelief, as well as on the Day of Gathering and thereafter, with various types based on deeds. The commentary gives examples: punishment by snakes, scorpions, beating, etc. The proof for punishment of the grave is the Quranic verse about the family of Pharaoh being exposed to the Fire morning and evening, which is understood as a reference to the intermediate state (barzakh). The passage then distinguishes the final outcome: disbelievers will be eternally in Hell, while sinful believers are subject to Allah's will — either forgiven and not enter, or enter to be purified and then exit, or receive the Prophet's intercession.

Key Terms
العقاب
عقاب القبر
الحيات والعقارب
الخلود
الشفاعة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That punishment in the grave is not proven.
Correction: The passage cites Quran 40:46 as clear proof for punishment in the grave (barzakh).

Checkpoint
What is the proof for punishment in the grave mentioned in the text?
A) A hadith from the Prophet
B) The Quranic verse about the family of Pharaoh
C) The consensus of scholars
D) Logical deduction

Key Takeaway
Belief in punishment is obligatory; it includes punishment in the grave proven by Quran, and its final outcomes differ for disbelievers and believers.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
This passage teaches that punishment in the grave is proven and that sins can lead to punishment even for believers. Have you ever committed a sin and thought, 'It's just a small thing, it won't matter'? How does the reality of punishment for even 'small' sins confront that assumption? Will you repent and make amends today?
?
The passage says the outcome for sinful believers is subject to Allah's will, with three possibilities: forgiveness, purification, or intercession. Which of these do you rely on? Are you living as if forgiveness is guaranteed, or do you fear the purification through punishment? What change do you need to make to avoid the latter?
?
The passage mentions intercession of the Prophet (peace be upon him) as a mercy for some. Do you feel confident that you will be among those who receive his intercession? What can you do to increase your connection to him and your hope in it, while not being complacent?

Faith in Reward and the True Bliss
Definition of reward and identification of true bliss.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic concept of reward (al-thawāb) and that the ultimate bliss is the vision of Allah.

Core Idea
True bliss is not Paradise itself, but seeing Allah.

This lesson explores the definition of al-thawāb (reward) and distinguishes between the various pleasures of Paradise and the highest spiritual joy: the vision of Allah. The passage emphasizes that all other bliss is merely a manifestation of this ultimate reality.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once and then the literal translation. Notice the grammatical connection between al-thawāb and al-ḥisāb.
2
Identify the definition of thawāb
The passage defines thawāb as recompense for deeds. Note that it includes Paradise and other types of bliss.
3
Understand the true bliss
The passage states that the true bliss is the vision of Allah's noble face. Everything else is a manifestation of this.
This corrects a common misunderstanding that Paradise's joys are purely physical.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الإيمان بالثواب والثواب بالعطف على الحساب وهو الجزاء على الأعمال بالجنة وغيرها من أنواع النعيم، والنعيم الحقيقي هو النظر لوجه الله الكريم، وما عداه فهو مظهر من مظاهر النعيم الحقيقي، كما تقدّم.
Faith in reward — and 'al-thawāb' (reward) is in the genitive case, coordinated with al-ḥisāb — and it is the recompense for deeds with Paradise and other types of bliss. And the true bliss is the vision of the noble face of Allah, and other than that is a manifestation of the true bliss, as has preceded.
Source pages p. 89

Commentary
This short passage defines faith in reward (al-thawāb). It states that reward is the recompense for deeds, consisting of Paradise and other kinds of bliss. The commentary emphasizes that the ultimate and true bliss (al-naʿīm al-ḥaqīqī) is the vision of Allah's noble face (ruʾyat Allah). Everything else is merely a manifestation of that true bliss. This point reiterates an earlier discussion.

Key Terms
الثواب
الجزاء
النعيم
النظر لوجه الله

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the joys of Paradise are purely physical and that the highest reward is the gardens and rivers.
Correction: The text clarifies that the true bliss (al-naʿīm al-ḥaqīqī) is the vision of Allah's face; all other pleasures are manifestations of this spiritual bliss.

Checkpoint
What is the true bliss (al-naʿīm al-ḥaqīqī) according to the text?
A) The gardens of Paradise
B) The rivers of milk and honey
C) The vision of Allah's face
D) The company of angels

Key Takeaway
The true bliss (al-naʿīm al-ḥaqīqī) is the vision of Allah; all other joys in Paradise are manifestations of this.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you imagine Paradise, what dominates your thoughts: the physical gardens or the vision of your Lord? How can you shift your heart's desire to the ultimate goal?
?
Do you truly long for the vision of Allah more than any worldly or paradisiacal pleasure? Or have you been content with the lesser joys?
?
If the true bliss is to see Allah, what does that mean for how you seek nearness to Him now? Are you satisfied with merely obeying commands, or do you deeply crave intimate knowledge of His essence?

Faith in Resurrection (al-Nashr)
The author defines al-nashr as resurrection and reviving the dead, with a Quranic reference.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the meaning of al-nashr (resurrection) as a necessary belief in Islam and its connection to the reviving of the dead.

Core Idea
Al-nashr is the resurrection of the dead from their graves.

The author now turns to another article of faith: the resurrection of the dead. In this passage, he defines al-nashr, explaining that it is the same as al-baʿth (the sending forth) and refers to the reviving of the dead from their graves. He also alludes to a Quranic verse as evidence, grounding this belief in divine revelation.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic passage and its literal translation. Notice how al-nashr is coordinated with al-ḥisāb (reckoning) and defined as resurrection.
The passage is short but dense; take your time with each phrase.
2
Identify the definition
Find how the author defines al-nashr: it is al-baʿth (resurrection) and means the reviving of the dead from their graves. Notice that the text says 'by it is meant' (al-murād bihi).
The term 'nashr' literally means 'spreading' but here it is a technical theological term.
3
Connect to the Quranic citation
The passage ends with a reference to Surah Ghafir, verse 46. Reflect on how the resurrection is confirmed by the Quran. Why might the author reference this specific verse?
The verse speaks of the punishment of the grave, linking resurrection to God's power.
4
Address the common misunderstanding
Consider the common misunderstanding that al-nashr is different from al-baʿth. The text explicitly equates them. Why is it important to see them as synonyms?
This ensures clarity in belief: there is one resurrection, not multiple distinct events.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الإيمان بالنشر | (والنشر) بالجر عطف على الحساب، أي: ومما يجب اعتقاده الإيمان بالنشر، أي: البعث والمراد به إحياء الموتى من قبورهم بعد (١) سورة غافر، الآية (٤٦).
Faith in al-nashr (the Spreading of the dead) — and 'al-nashr' is in the genitive case, coordinated with al-ḥisāb — i.e., among what it is obligatory to believe is faith in al-nashr, i.e., the resurrection, and by it is meant the reviving of the dead from their graves. After (1) Surah Ghafir, verse 46.
Source pages p. 89

Commentary
This passage defines the obligation of believing in al-nashr (the Spreading/Resurrection). It is coordinated with the reckoning. Al-nashr is identified with al-baʿth (the sending forth), meaning the revival of the dead from their graves. The text then references a Quranic verse as proof, though the verse itself is not quoted here; the footnote points to Surah Ghafir, verse 46 (which is the same verse used for punishment of the grave). Thus, the resurrection is the event after which the final judgment occurs.

Key Terms
النشر
البعث
إحياء الموتى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Thinking that al-nashr is a different event from al-baʿth (resurrection).
Correction: The text explicitly equates al-nashr with al-baʿth, stating 'al-nashr, i.e., al-baʿth' (any: al-baʿth). They refer to the same event: the reviving of the dead from their graves.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what does al-nashr refer to?
A) The gathering
B) The spreading of the scrolls
C) The resurrection of the dead
D) The weighing of deeds

Key Takeaway
Al-nashr is the resurrection of the dead from their graves, an obligatory belief confirmed by the Quran.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly live today as if you will be resurrected from your grave and held accountable for every action? What would change if you felt the reality of al-nashr in your heart right now?
?
The Quran affirms resurrection as a certainty. Have you ever let doubts about the afterlife creep into your mind? How will you combat those doubts with the knowledge that Allah has promised to revive the dead from their graves?
?
The author equates al-nashr with reviving the dead from their graves. When you think of your own grave, does fear or hope dominate? What step can you take today to prepare for that day of standing before your Lord?

The Gathering of Original Parts Except the Coccyx
Commentator explains resurrection gathering except the coccyx, citing Quranic proofs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that at resurrection God gathers all original body parts except the coccyx, which remains as the basis for re-creation, and that this is supported by Quranic verses.

Core Idea
The coccyx survives decay; God re-creates from it.

In this passage, the commentator explains a key detail of bodily resurrection: God gathers all the original parts of the human body except one small bone, the coccyx. This exception is important because it shows that something must remain for God to build upon. The commentator then cites several Quranic verses to affirm the power of Allah to resurrect the dead. Let's read the passage and see how the evidence is presented.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it. Then read the English translation to grasp the overall meaning.
Notice the phrase 'except the coccyx' (ما عدا عجب الذنب) as a key exception.
2
Identify the exception and its significance
The passage states that all original parts are gathered except the coccyx. Think about why this exception matters for resurrection.
The coccyx is considered the seed from which the body is recreated.
3
Recognize the Quranic proofs
The commentator cites four verses that affirm God's power to resurrect. Note how each verse emphasizes God's ability to create anew.
The verses are from Surahs 23, 36, 7, and 64.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
جمع أجزائهم الأصلية، بأن يجمعها الله تعالى بعد عدمها بالكلية، ما عدا عجب الذنب، بفتح العين قال الله تعالى: ﴿ ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ تُبْعَثُونَ ﴾ (١)، وقُلْ يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنشَأَهَا أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ ﴾ (٢)، ﴿ كَمَا بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ ﴾ (۳)، وَزَعَمَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَن لَّن يُبْعَثُوا قُل بَلَى وَرَبِّي لَمُبْعَتُنَّ ثُمَّ لَتُنَبَّؤُنَّ بِمَا عَمِلْتُمْ وَذَلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيرٌ ﴾ (٤).
The gathering of their original parts, by God gathering them after their complete non-existence, except the coccyx (ʿajab al-dhanab) – with fatḥa on the ʿayn – God said: 'Then indeed you will be resurrected on the Day of Resurrection' (Q 23:16), and 'Say: He will give them life who produced them the first time' (Q 36:79), 'As He began you, you will return' (Q 7:29), and 'Those who disbelieve claim that they will never be resurrected. Say: Yes, by my Lord, you will surely be resurrected; then you will be informed of what you did, and that is easy for God.' (Q 64:7).
Source pages p. 90

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal explanation and proof. The commentator states that at resurrection, God will gather all the original parts (اجزائهم الأصلية) of the human body after they have completely perished, except for one specific part: the coccyx (عجب الذنب). The commentator then supports this exception by citing four Quranic verses that affirm bodily resurrection. The verses are: Q 23:16, Q 36:79, Q 7:29, and Q 64:7. Each verse emphasizes God’s power to recreate the body after death. The passage establishes the theological principle that while the entire body disintegrates, the coccyx is preserved and serves as the origin for re-creation.

Key Terms
عجب الذنب
النشر
إعادة الخلق
جمع الأجزاء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think the entire body disintegrates into nothing, leaving no basis for recreation.
Correction: The commentator explains that the coccyx remains intact and serves as the foundation from which God recreates the body.

Checkpoint
According to the commentator, which part of the human body is not completely annihilated before resurrection?
A) The heart
B) The coccyx (عجب الذنب)
C) The brain
D) The bones

Key Takeaway
God preserves the coccyx and uses it as the foundation to re-create the body on the Day of Resurrection.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you consider that even your smallest bone (the coccyx) is preserved by God for resurrection, does this change how you view the significance of your every physical action? Will you now strive to make each action worthy of being resurrected?
?
Disbelievers denied resurrection. Are there moments you doubt God's power to restore what you have lost or ruined? How does this passage's affirmation of God's power over the smallest detail call you to trust Him more completely?
?
The resurrection is a certainty. If you were to be informed of your deeds tomorrow, would you be ashamed or proud? What one change can you make today to ensure your deeds are pleasing to God?

From Resurrection to the Gathering Place
Al-Siba'i begins the sequence with resurrection and gathering on a new earth.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the first two events after resurrection: being driven to the gathering place in Syria and the change of the earth as described in the Quran.

Core Idea
The Day of Judgment begins with resurrection and a literal gathering on a new earth.

The commentator begins by quoting al-Siba'i's detailed account, starting with the immediate aftermath of resurrection. This part describes how all creatures are assembled on a transformed earth, supported by a Quranic verse. Understanding this sets the stage for the subsequent events.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic text carefully, noting the words 'النشر' and 'المحشر'.
Observe the Quranic citation embedded in the exposition.
2
Identify the two main events
The passage mentions resurrection (النشر) and then being driven to the gathering place (المحشر). Notice the location and the description of the new earth.
The change of earth is a key doctrinal point.
3
Reflect on the Quranic proof
The verse from Surah Ibrahim (14:48) is cited as evidence. Consider why the commentator includes this scriptural support.
This grounds the belief in Quranic authority.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال العلامة سيدي محمد السباعي مرتبًا ما يقع في ذلك اليوم العظيم: ثم بعد النشر تساق الخلائق إلى المحشر بالشام، ويحشرون على أرض غير هذه الأرض، وهي الأرض البيضاء، قال تعالى: ﴿ يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ ﴾ (٥). والحشر القيام لرب العالمين ثم بعده العرض، ثم تنزل الملائكة وتصطف بهم وتدنو منهم الشمس، ثم تتطاير الصحف ثم أخذها بالإيمان والشمائل أي يأخذها الملك ويعطيها للمؤمن بيمينه والكافر بشماله، فيقرؤها ويعلم ما فيها، ثم يشفع فيهم النبي صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ، وهذا اليوم مقداره خمسون ألف سنة، ثم ينصرفون إلى الميزان فتوزن به أعمالهم، ثم يؤمر بهم إلى الصراط ويشربون من الحوض، والكفار لا يشربون منه وكذلك من غَيّر وبدل من الأمة، ثم يمرون على الصراط» (١). اهـ. نسأل الله تعالى السلامة والجواز، وأن يمن علينا بشربة من حوض نبينا سيدنا محمد صلى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ لا نظمأ بعدها أبدًا مع الأحباب والأولاد والذرية.
The scholar Sayyidī Muḥammad al-Sibāʿī said, arranging what occurs on that great day: Then after the resurrection (al-nashr), the creatures are driven to the place of gathering (al-maḥshar) in Syria, and they are gathered on a land other than this earth, which is the white land. God said: 'On the day the earth is changed to a different earth' (Q 14:48). And the gathering (al-ḥashr) is standing for the Lord of the worlds, then after it is the presentation (al-ʿarḍ), then the angels descend and line up with them, and the sun comes near them, then the scrolls fly, then they are taken with the right and left – meaning the angel takes them and gives them to the believer with his right hand and to the disbeliever with his left hand, and they read them and know what is in them. Then the Prophet ﷺ intercedes for them, and this day measures fifty thousand years. Then they depart to the scale (al-mīzān) and their deeds are weighed with it. Then they are ordered to the path (al-ṣirāṭ) and they drink from the basin (al-ḥawḍ), while the disbelievers do not drink from it, as well as those who changed and altered from the community. Then they pass over the path. End. We ask God for safety and passage, and that He grants us a drink from the basin of our Prophet, our master Muḥammad ﷺ, after which we will never thirst, along with loved ones, children, and offspring.
Source pages p. 90

Commentary
This passage is a narrative description and proof of the events of the Day of Judgment. The commentator quotes al-Sibāʿī, who gives a chronological order: 1) After resurrection (al-nashr), creatures are driven to the gathering place (al-maḥshar) in Syria, on a white earth (citing Q 14:48). 2) The standing (al-ḥashr) before the Lord. 3) The presentation (al-ʿarḍ) of deeds. 4) Angels descend and line up; the sun draws near. 5) Scrolls fly and are taken – believers with the right hand, disbelievers with the left; they read and know their contents. 6) The Prophet's intercession. 7) The day lasts 50,000 years. 8) They proceed to the scale (al-mīzān) for weighing deeds. 9) Then to the path (al-ṣirāṭ) and drinking from the basin (al-ḥawḍ) – disbelievers and those who changed the religion are denied. 10) Then passing over the path. The commentator ends with a prayer for safety and to drink from the Prophet's basin.

Key Terms
النشر
المحشر
الحشر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: These events are purely metaphorical or spiritual.
Correction: The commentator presents them as literal, sequential stages, citing a Quranic verse to affirm a physical change of the earth.

Checkpoint
Where are the creatures driven after resurrection according to al-Siba'i?
A) To Paradise
B) To the gathering place in Syria
C) To the scale
D) To the path

Key Takeaway
The first major event after resurrection is the gathering of all creatures on a transformed earth, as confirmed by the Quran.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the first event after resurrection is being driven to a gathering place on a strange new earth, what are you doing today to ensure you are among those who go there with peace rather than terror?
?
The earth you know will be replaced—nothing familiar will remain. What attachment in your life is so strong that its loss on that day would crush you, and how can you loosen its grip now?
?
You will be gathered with every creature, no exceptions. When you imagine standing on that white land in Syria, face to face with your Lord, what single sin are you most afraid of, and what step will you take this week to repent from it sincerely?

The Presentation, Scrolls, and Intercession
Al-Siba'i describes the standing, presentation, scrolls, and the Prophet's intercession.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the events of standing, presentation of deeds, distribution of scrolls, and the Prophet's intercession.

Core Idea
Deeds are presented, scrolls are given, and the Prophet intercedes before the weighing.

After the gathering, al-Siba'i describes a series of dramatic events: the creatures stand before God, the angels descend, the sun approaches, scrolls fly and are distributed, and finally the Prophet intercedes. This lesson covers the middle portion of the sequence, leading up to the great length of that day.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read from 'والحشر القيام' to 'خمسون ألف سنة'. Note each event in order.
The phrase 'ثم' appears repeatedly, indicating sequence.
2
List the events in order
Identify: standing, presentation, angels descending, sun near, scrolls flying, receiving scrolls with right/left, reading, intercession, duration of 50,000 years.
The scrolls are taken by a king (angel) and given to the believer with the right hand.
3
Consider the significance of intercession
Notice that the Prophet's intercession occurs before the scale and path. Reflect on its role in the sequence.
Intercession is a key mercy for the believers.

Source Text

The Scale, Path, and Basin
Al-Siba'i details the weighing of deeds, the path, and the basin, concluding with a prayer.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the final events: the weighing of deeds, the crossing of the path, and the drinking from the basin, including who is denied.

Core Idea
Deeds are weighed, believers cross the path and drink from the basin, while disbelievers and innovators are excluded.

The final part of al-Siba'i's account describes the judgment after intercession. The creatures proceed to the scale where deeds are weighed, then are ordered to the path (al-ṣirāṭ) and the basin (al-ḥawḍ). The passage notes that disbelievers and those who altered the religion cannot drink from the basin. The commentator concludes with a heartfelt prayer for safety and a drink from the Prophet's basin.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read from 'ثم ينصرفون إلى الميزان' to the end. Notice the three main events: scale, path/basin, and crossing.
The prayer at the end is the commentator's own addition.
2
List the events and the condition for drinking
Identify: departure to scale, weighing, order to path, drinking from basin (believers only), denial to disbelievers and those who changed, then passing over the path.
The phrase 'وكذلك من غَيّر وبدل من الأمة' extends denial to those who altered the religion.
3
Reflect on the prayer
The commentator prays for safety and a drink from the Prophet's basin. Consider the importance of this blessing.
The prayer 'لا نظمأ بعدها أبدًا' highlights the eternal nature of the gift.

Source Text

The Coccyx: Seed of Resurrection
Commentator defines the coccyx, cites hadith on its indestructibility, and notes divine wisdom.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the significance of the coccyx bone in Islamic eschatology, its definition, and the hadith evidence for its preservation.

Core Idea
The coccyx bone is the imperishable seed of bodily resurrection.

This lesson explores a unique element of creation: the coccyx. According to authentic hadith, this small bone at the base of the spine never decays, and from it Allah will recreate the human body on the Day of Resurrection. The commentator clarifies its definition and provides scriptural proof.

Guided Path
1
Understand the Definition
Learn what the coccyx is anatomically: a bone like a mustard seed at the end of the spinal column.
The commentator specifies its shape and location.
2
Examine the Hadith Evidence
Read the three narrations from Bukhari, Muslim, and Ibn Hibban, noting how they emphasize that only the coccyx remains.
3
Reflect on Divine Wisdom
Consider the statement that there are secrets in its preservation known only to Allah, highlighting the limits of human knowledge.
This reminds us that resurrection is beyond full human comprehension.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما ذكره الناظم رَحمَهُ الله بواو العطف التي لا تقتضي ترتيبًا لأجل النظم. أما عجب الذنب وهو عظم كالخردلة في آخر سلسلة الظهر، «فالمشهور أنه لا يفنى مع الجسم لحديث الصحيحين»(۲): «ليس من الإنسان إلا يبلى إلا عظمًا واحدًا، وهو عَجْبُ الذنب منه خلق الخلق يوم القيامة». وفي رواية مسلم (۳): كلُّ ابن آدم يأكله التراب إلا عَجْبَ الذُّنب نه خُلق ومنه يُرَكَّبُ». وفي رواية لابن حبان (٤) : وما هو يا رسول الله، قال: «هو مثل حبة خردلة منه تنشئون»، وفي بقائه أسرار لا يعلمها إلا الله تعالى (٥) . اهـ.
What the poet, may God have mercy on him, mentioned is with the conjunction wāw that does not imply order, for poetic necessity. As for the coccyx (ʿajab al-dhanab), it is a bone like a mustard seed at the end of the spinal column. 'The well-known position is that it does not perish with the body, according to the hadith of the two Ṣaḥīḥs': 'There is nothing of man that does not decay except one bone, which is the coccyx; from it creation is formed on the Day of Resurrection.' And in a narration of Muslim: 'Every son of Adam is eaten by the earth except the coccyx; from it he was created and from it he will be reconstituted.' And in a narration of Ibn Ḥibbān: 'What is it, O Messenger of God?' He said: 'It is like a mustard seed; from it you will be created anew.' And in its preservation are secrets that none knows except God. End.
Source pages p. 91

Commentary
This passage is a theological explanation and proof regarding the coccyx (عجب الذنب). The commentator first clarifies that the poet's use of the conjunction 'wāw' (و) does not imply a chronological order, but is for poetic meter. Then he defines the coccyx as a bone resembling a mustard seed at the end of the spinal column. He then presents the well-known position (المشهور) that it does not perish with the body, citing the hadith from al-Bukhārī and Muslim: 'There is nothing of man that does not decay except one bone, which is the coccyx; from it creation is formed on the Day of Resurrection.' He adds another narration from Muslim stating that every part of the child of Adam is consumed by earth except the coccyx, from which he was created and will be reconstituted. A third narration from Ibn Ḥibbān describes it as like a mustard seed, and from it people will be created anew. The commentator concludes that there are secrets in its preservation that only God knows.

Key Terms
عجب الذنب
عظم
خردلة
سلسلة الظهر
حديث الصحيحين
بلى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think the entire body perishes completely.
Correction: The hadith emphasize the coccyx remains as the basis for resurrection; the rest of the body decays.

Checkpoint
According to the hadith cited, what happens to the coccyx (عجب الذنب)?
A) It decays with the rest of the body
B) It never decays and from it creation is formed
C) It is eaten by the earth like other bones
D) It is only preserved for prophets

Key Takeaway
The coccyx is the imperishable bone from which Allah will recreate the human body on the Day of Resurrection.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The hadith says that from this small bone you will be created anew on the Day of Resurrection. Knowing that Allah has preserved this seed within you, how does that change the way you view your body right now? What will you start doing differently today to honor this trust?
?
Your coccyx is currently intact, a secret sign of your future return to life. Are you living as though this resurrection is certain? If you were to die tonight, would you be ready to meet your Lord? What is one concrete step you can take this week to prepare for that moment?
?
The commentator says that the preservation of this bone contains secrets known only to Allah. This reminds you that not everything is within your knowledge. Are you content to trust Allah with the unseen matters of your own resurrection? Or do you find yourself questioning His power? Make a sincere du'a right now asking Him to grant you certainty in the resurrection.

The Resurrection Bone (ʿAjab al-Dhanb) Does Not Decay
Al-Mashat explains the doctrine of the coccyx bone that remains undecayed for resurrection.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic doctrine that the coccyx bone remains undecayed and serves as the basis for bodily resurrection, supported by authentic hadith.

Core Idea
One small bone remains imperishable, holding the secret of resurrection.

The belief in bodily resurrection is a cornerstone of Islamic eschatology. But how can the body be rebuilt after complete decay? The Prophet ﷺ informed us about a tiny bone at the base of the spine that never decays, serving as the seed from which the entire body is recreated. In this lesson, we examine the textual evidence from authentic hadith and the theological significance of this bone.

Guided Path
1
Understand the nature of ʿajab al-dhanb
Read the definition: it is a bone like a mustard seed at the end of the spinal column. This physical description helps identify the bone in question.
2
Examine the hadith evidence
Note the three narrations: from Sahihain, Muslim, and Ibn Hibban. Each affirms that this bone does not decay and is the origin of recreation.
The hadith are mutawatir in meaning, establishing the doctrine firmly.
3
Reflect on the doctrinal implication
The mashhūr (well-known) view is that the bone remains with the body. Understand that this is a matter of samʿiyyāt (heard revelation), not rational deduction.
Al-Mashat uses the phrase 'fī baqāʾihi asrār lā yaʿlamuhā illā Allāh.'
4
Consider the deeper secrets
The passage ends by affirming that Allah alone knows the full wisdom behind this preservation. This shows humility before divine knowledge.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما ذكره الناظم رحمه الله بواو العطف التي لا تقتضي ترتيبًا لأجل النظم. أما عجب الذنب وهو عظم كالخردلة في آخر سلسلة الظهر، فالمشهور أنه لا يفنى مع الجسم لحديث الصحيحين: «ليس من الإنسان إلا يبلى إلا عظمًا واحدًا، وهو عَجْبُ الذنب منه خلق الخلق يوم القيامة». وفي رواية مسلم: «كلُّ ابن آدم يأكله التراب إلا عَجْبَ الذُّنب منه خُلق ومنه يُرَكَّبُ». وفي رواية لابن حبان: «وما هو يا رسول الله، قال: هو مثل حبة خردلة منه تنشئون»، وفي بقائه أسرار لا يعلمها إلا الله تعالى. اهـ.
And what the poet (al-Nazim) mentioned with the conjunction wāw which does not imply order, for the sake of the poem. As for ʿajab al-dhanb (the coccyx) — it is a bone like a mustard seed at the end of the spinal column. The well-known view is that it does not perish with the body, due to the hadith in the two Sahihs: 'There is nothing of a human being that does not decay except one bone, the coccyx; from it the creation will be created on the Day of Resurrection.' In a narration of Muslim: 'Every son of Adam is consumed by the earth except the coccyx; from it he was created and from it he will be reconstructed.' In a narration of Ibn Hibban: 'What is it, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'It is like a mustard seed; from it you will be raised.' And in its remaining are secrets known only to Allah Almighty. End.
Source pages p. 91

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal explanation about the coccyx bone mentioned in hadith. It begins by noting that the poet used the conjunction wāw without implying order (i.e., the list of items of faith is not arranged in a sequence). Then it defines ʿajab al-dhanb as a bone resembling a mustard seed at the end of the spine. The well-known position (al-mashhūr) is that this bone does not disintegrate with the body, based on authentic hadith. The author cites evidence from Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim stating that all of the body decays except this bone, and it is from this bone that the body will be recreated at resurrection. He also cites Ibn Hibban's narration where the Prophet (peace be upon him) describes it as like a mustard seed from which people will be raised. The passage concludes by affirming that there are deeper secrets behind its preservation known only to Allah.

Key Terms
عجب الذنب
خلق الخلق
صحيحين

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The entire human body decays completely without any remnant, making bodily resurrection impossible.
Correction: This passage affirms that a specific bone, the coccyx, never decays, and from it the body is recreated, as proven by authentic hadith.

Checkpoint
According to the hadith cited, which part of the human body does not decay?
A) The heart
B) The coccyx
C) The brain
D) The skin

Key Takeaway
The coccyx bone does not decay and is the seed from which Allah recreates the human body on the Day of Resurrection, a truth established by authentic hadith and known fully only to Allah.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You know that within your body is one small bone that will never decay, preserved by Allah as the seed of your resurrection. If you truly believed that you will be raised from that very bone, how would you live differently today? What is one action you will take right now to prepare for that appointment?
?
The Prophet ﷺ said the coccyx is like a mustard seed from which you will be created anew. Have you ever considered the immense power of Allah in preserving one tiny bone for centuries? Does that certainty make you fear His justice more or love His mercy more? What sin have you been hiding that you need to confess to Allah right now?
?
The passage says there are secrets in this bone known only to Allah. You are not required to understand everything, but you are required to submit. Is there any part of your heart that resists believing because you cannot see or comprehend? Will you ask Allah tonight to increase your iman in the unseen, even if your mind cannot grasp it?

The Ṣirāṭ: The Bridge Over Hellfire
Al-Mashat defines the Ṣirāṭ and describes the varying states of people crossing it.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the nature of the Ṣirāṭ and how the manner of crossing it reflects one's deeds in this world.

Core Idea
Your crossing speed on the Ṣirāṭ mirrors your spiritual path in this life.

Al-Mashat now turns to the topic of the Ṣirāṭ, the bridge stretched over Hellfire. This doctrine is based on sahih hadith and emphasizes that both believers and disbelievers will encounter it, but their crossing will differ dramatically. The passage provides a vivid description that connects worldly deeds to afterlife outcomes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that the passage ends with a principle linking the spiritual Ṣirāṭ to the physical one.
2
Identify the categories of people
The passage lists seven types of crossings and then mentions those who fall. Find each one and notice the comparison to natural phenomena (lightning, wind, etc.).
3
Reflect on the connection to deeds
Ask yourself: why does the author connect the speed of crossing to righteous deeds? How does this align with the concept of the straight path (ṣirāṭ mustaqīm) in this world?
This connects to the learning objective.
4
Answer the checkpoint
Complete the multiple-choice question to check your understanding of the passage's main point.
The answer is based directly on the last sentence of the passage.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(والصراط) أي: ويلزم الإيمان به، وهو جسر ممدود على متن جهنم. وفي صحيح مسلم: «هو أَدَقُّ من الشعرة وأَحَدُّ من السيف». وهو بين الموقف والجنة ترده المؤمنون والكفار للمرور عليه، والمارون عليه مختلفون فمنهم سالم بعمله ناج من الوقوع في نار جهنم، وهو أقسام: منهم من يجوزه كلمح البصر. ومنهم كالبرق الخاطف. ومنهم كالريح القاصف. ومنهم كالطير. ومنهم كالجواد السابق. ومنهم من يسعى سعيًا. ومنهم من يمشي. ومنهم من يمر عليه حبوا على قدر تفاوتهم في الأعمال الصالحة، والإعراض عن المعاصي. ومنهم غير ناج بل يسقط في نار جهنم، أعاذنا الله منها، وهم متفاوتون أيضًا على قدر جرائمهم. وبالجملة فعلى قدر الاستقامة على الصراط المعنوي في الدنيا يكون الثبات والنجاة على الصراط الحسي في الآخرة، فالإيمان به
(And al-Ṣirāṭ) i.e., belief in it is obligatory. It is a bridge stretched over the surface of Hell. In Sahih Muslim: 'It is finer than a hair and sharper than a sword.' It is between the standing-place and Paradise; both believers and disbelievers will come to it to cross over. Those who cross it vary: among them are those who cross it like the blink of an eye; some like a flash of lightning; some like a gusty wind; some like a bird; some like a swift horse; some who walk; some who crawl—according to their varying degrees in righteous deeds and avoidance of sins. And among them are those who do not succeed but fall into the fire of Hell—may Allah protect us from it—and they also vary according to their crimes. In summary, according to one's steadfastness on the spiritual Ṣirāṭ in this world will be one's firmness and salvation on the physical Ṣirāṭ in the Hereafter. So belief in it [is obligatory].
Source pages p. 92

Commentary
This passage defines the doctrine of the Ṣirāṭ (the bridge over Hell). It begins by stating that belief in it is obligatory. It then describes it as a bridge stretched over the surface of Hell, citing a hadith from Sahih Muslim that it is finer than a hair and sharper than a sword. The location is between the place of standing (al-mawqif) and Paradise. Both believers and disbelievers will approach it to cross, but the manner of crossing differs. The author enumerates seven categories of people based on their speed of crossing, ranging from the blink of an eye to crawling, corresponding to their level of righteous deeds and avoidance of sins. Those who fall are also graded according to their sins. The key point is that the ease of crossing the physical Ṣirāṭ in the afterlife is directly proportional to one's adherence to the straight path (al-ṣirāṭ al-mustaʿīm) in this world.

Key Terms
الصراط
جسر
ممدود
متن جهنم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the Ṣirāṭ is only for believers.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that both believers and disbelievers will come to the Ṣirāṭ, but disbelievers will fall into Hell.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what determines a person's speed of crossing the Ṣirāṭ?
A) Their physical strength
B) Their wealth
C) Their deeds in this world
D) Their age

Key Takeaway
The Ṣirāṭ is a real bridge over Hell, and the ease of crossing depends on one's righteousness in this life.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that your speed on the Ṣirāṭ—whether like lightning or like a crawl—is being determined right now by the choices you make regarding sins today? If so, what specific sin are you tolerating that you know will slow you down?
?
The passage says: 'according to one's steadfastness on the spiritual Ṣirāṭ in this world will be one's firmness on the physical Ṣirāṭ in the Hereafter.' As you read this, are you more concerned about the physical terror of the bridge or about the spiritual path you are on right now? Honestly, which occupies your thoughts more?
?
If you were to die tonight and stand before the Ṣirāṭ, which of the seven categories do you fear you might belong to? What would you need to change starting tomorrow to move up even one level?

Belief in the Sirat (the Bridge)
Definition of Sirat and description of crossing.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the Sirat is a real bridge over Hell and that the speed and safety of crossing depend on one's faith and righteous deeds.

Core Idea
The Sirat is a real bridge over Hell; crossing depends on one's deeds.

The commentator takes us into the reality of the Sirat, a bridge stretched over Hell that every soul must cross. He describes it in vivid detail, drawing from authentic hadith, and explains that the crossing varies dramatically from person to person based on their faith and actions. This lesson will help you grasp this essential element of Islamic eschatology and its connection to our deeds in this world.

Guided Path
1
Read the source passage
Carefully read the Arabic passage and its translation. Pay attention to how the Sirat is described and the different categories of people crossing it.
Notice the use of similes like 'finer than a hair and sharper than a sword.'
2
Understand the definition of the Sirat
Identify the definition: a bridge over Hell between the standing place and Paradise. Note the hadith about its sharpness and thinness.
This is a sensory bridge, not merely metaphorical.
3
List the categories of crossing
Find all the ways people cross: blink of an eye, lightning, wind, bird, horse, walking, crawling, and falling into Hell. Relate each to the person's deeds.
The speed reflects one's righteousness.
4
Connect to scriptural evidence
Look at the Qur'anic verse and the hadith about the hooks and the disbeliever walking on his face. How do these reinforce the reality of the Sirat?
5
Reflect on the metaphorical Sirat
The commentator says that firmness on the Sirat in this world (the straight path) leads to salvation on the sensory Sirat. Think about how your actions today affect your hereafter.
This is the key takeaway for personal application.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(والصراط) أي: ويلزم الإيمان به، وهو جسر ممدود على متن جهنم | الإيمان بالصراط وفي (صحيح مسلم» (۱): «هو أَدَقُّ من الشعرة وأَحَدُّ من السيف».اهـ. وهو بين الموقف والجنة ترده المؤمنون والكفار للمرور عليه، والمارون عليه مختلفون فمنهم سالم بعمله ناج من الوقوع في نار جهنم، وهو أقسام: منهم من يجوزه كلمح البصر. ومنهم كالبرق الخاطف. * ومنهم كالريح القاصف. ومنهم كالطير. ومنهم كالجواد السابق. ومنهم من يسعى سعيًا. ومنهم من يمشي. ومنهم من يمر عليه حبوا على قدر تفاوتهم في الأعمال الصالحة، والإعراض عن المعاصي. ومنهم غير ناج بل يسقط في نار جهنم، أعاذنا الله منها، وهم متتفاوتون أيضًا على قدر جرائمهم. وبالجملة فعلى قدر الاستقامة على الصراط المعنوي في الدنيا يكون الثبات والنجاة على الصراط الحسي في الآخرة، فالإيمان به واجب اعتقاده قال الله تعالى: ﴿ فَاسْتَبَقُوا الصِّرَاطَ ﴾، وفي الحديث الصحيح: ويضرب الصراط بين ظهراني جهنم فأكون أنا وأمتي أول من يجوزه، ولا يتكلم يومئذ إلا الرسل، ودعواهم يومئذ: اللهم سَلَم سلم، وفي جهنم كلاليب مثل شوك السعدان، غير أنه لا يعلم عظمها إلا الله ، تخطف الناس بأعمالهم، فمنهم المؤمن يؤتي بعمله، ومنهم المُجَازى حتى يُنَجِّى» . وفي الصحيحين» (۳) عن أنس رضي اللَّهُ عَنْهُ أَن رجلًا قال: يا نبي الله كيف يحشر الكافر على وجهه يوم القيامة، قال: «أليس الذي أمشاه على الرجلين قادر على أن يمشيه على وجهه يوم القيامة». اللهم اهدنا الصراط المستقيم، صراط الذين أنعمت عليهم غير المغضوب عليهم ولا الضالين، آمين.
And the Sirat: i.e., belief in it is necessary, and it is a bridge stretched over the surface of Hell. Belief in the Sirat. In Sahih Muslim (1): 'It is finer than a hair and sharper than a sword.' end quote. It is between the standing place and Paradise, traversed by believers and disbelievers to pass over it. Those who pass over it vary: some are safe by their deeds, saved from falling into Hellfire, and it has categories: some cross it in the blink of an eye; some like a flashing lightning; * some like a violent wind; some like a bird; some like a swift horse; some walk; some crawl, according to their difference in righteous deeds and avoidance of sins. And some are not saved but fall into Hellfire, may Allah protect us from it, and they also vary according to their crimes. In summary, according to one's uprightness on the metaphorical Sirat in this world is the firmness and salvation on the sensory Sirat in the Hereafter. So belief in it is obligatory as Allah said: 'Then hasten to the Sirat.' And in the authentic hadith: 'The Sirat will be set across the middle of Hell, and I and my nation will be the first to cross it. No one will speak that day except the messengers, and their supplication that day will be: O Allah, grant safety, grant safety. In Hell there are hooks like the thorns of the sa'dan plant, whose magnitude none knows but Allah, snatching people by their deeds. Some believers are given their deeds to lead them, some are requited until they are saved.' And in the two Sahihs (3) from Anas, may Allah be pleased with him, that a man said: O Prophet of Allah, how will the disbeliever be gathered on his face on the Day of Judgment? He said: 'Is He who made him walk on his feet not able to make him walk on his face on the Day of Judgment?' O Allah, guide us to the straight path, the path of those upon whom You have bestowed favor, not of those who have incurred Your wrath, nor of those who have gone astray, Amen.
Source pages p. 92

Commentary
This passage defines belief in the Sirat (the Bridge) and describes the crossing of people. The commentator begins by stating that belief in the Sirat is obligatory. He defines it as a bridge stretched over the surface of Hell, citing a hadith that it is finer than a hair and sharper than a sword. He explains that it lies between the place of standing (al-mawqif) and Paradise, and both believers and disbelievers must cross it. He then elaborates on the varying speeds of crossing based on one's deeds: some cross in the blink of an eye, some like lightning, some like a violent wind, some like a bird, some like a swift horse, some walk, some crawl, and some are not saved but fall into Hellfire, varying according to their crimes. The commentator summarizes that one's firmness on the metaphorical Sirat in this world determines their salvation on the sensory Sirat in the Hereafter. He then provides scriptural evidence: the Qur'anic verse 'Then hasten to the Sirat' and a lengthy hadith describing the crossing and the hooks in Hell that snatch people by their deeds. He also includes the hadith about the disbeliever being gathered on his face. The passage concludes with a prayer for guidance to the straight path.

Key Terms
الصراط
الجسر
جهنم
الأعمال الصالحة
الإيمان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the Sirat is merely symbolic or metaphorical.
Correction: The text emphasizes it is a real bridge over Hell, with a physical crossing described in authentic hadith.

Checkpoint
According to the commentator, what determines the speed of crossing the Sirat?
A) The person's wealth
B) The person's righteous deeds
C) The person's lineage
D) The person's age

Key Takeaway
Belief in the Sirat as a real bridge over Hell is obligatory, and one's deeds in this world directly affect the crossing in the Hereafter.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The hadith says the Sirat is finer than a hair and sharper than a sword. If you were to cross it right now, what specific deed would be the main support under your feet — or the hook dragging you down?
?
The believers cry 'O Allah, grant safety, grant safety' while crossing. When is the last time you prayed for safety on the Sirat with that same desperation — not just as a phrase, but as a cry from the depths of your heart?
?
You are among those who will cross. The passage says crossing speed depends on 'one's uprightness on the metaphorical Sirat in this world.' Look at your life today: are you crawling, walking, or flying like lightning? What is the one habit you need to change to speed up your crossing?

Belief in the Mizan (the Balance)
The commentator defines the Mizan and provides Qur'anic proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that belief in the Mizan (the Balance) is a required article of faith supported by explicit Qur'anic verses.

Core Idea
The Mizan is a real scale that will weigh deeds on Judgment Day.

The commentator begins the section on Sam‘iyyat by affirming that belief in the Mizan is obligatory. He immediately provides Qur'anic proof, citing three verses that establish the reality of the weighing. This lesson examines his argument and the textual foundations for this creedal element.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify the claim
Read the Arabic and translation to see what the commentator asserts about the Mizan. Note that he says it is 'حق ثابت' (established truth).
The key point is that belief in the Mizan is wajib.
2
Examine the Qur'anic proofs
Look at each of the three verses cited. What does each say about the weighing? How do they collectively support the obligation to believe?
The verses use the word 'weight' and 'scales' indicating a literal act.
3
Apply the lesson: Why does this matter?
Reflect on why the commentator starts with proof from revelation. What does this tell you about the source of knowledge for Sam‘iyyat?
It shows that these matters are known only through divine texts.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الإيمان بالميزان (والميزان)، أي: ومما يجب اعتقاده الميزان، والوزن وهو حق ثابت يجب الإيمان به، قال الله تعالى: ﴿ وَالْوَزْنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُّ ﴾ (٤) ، وَنَضَعُ الْمَوَازِينَ الْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ ﴾ (٥) ، ﴿ فَمَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَازِينُهُ
Belief in the Mizan (the Balance): i.e., among the things that must be believed is the Balance, and the weighing, and it is a true matter that must be believed. Allah Almighty said: 'The weighing on that Day is the truth' (4), and 'We shall set up the scales of justice for the Day of Resurrection' (5), and 'Then he whose scales are heavy...'
Source pages p. 93

Commentary
This passage affirms belief in the Mizan (the Balance) as a required article of faith. The commentator begins by stating that among the things that must be believed is the Balance and the weighing, which is a true and established matter. He then provides three Qur'anic verses as decisive proof: 'The weighing on that Day is the truth' (Qur'an 7:8), 'We shall set up the scales of justice for the Day of Resurrection' (Qur'an 21:47), and the beginning of 'Then he whose scales are heavy...' (Qur'an 23:102). These verses establish that there will be a literal weighing of deeds on the Day of Judgment, not merely a metaphorical appraisal. The passage emphasizes that this belief is obligatory (wajib al-i'tiqad).

Key Terms
الميزان
الوزن
الحق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the Balance is only a metaphor for divine justice.
Correction: The Qur'an uses concrete language of scales and weighing, and the commentator affirms it as a literal truth that must be believed.

Checkpoint
What proof does the commentator provide for belief in the Mizan?
A) Consensus of scholars
B) Rational argument
C) Qur'anic verses
D) Prophetic hadith

Key Takeaway
Belief in the Mizan is a required creedal element, proven by explicit Qur'anic verses that describe a literal weighing of deeds.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
On that Day, your deeds will be weighed on a literal scale. Are you prepared to stand before the scales of justice? What deed have you neglected that you fear will be found too light?
?
The Mizan is a true matter, not a metaphor. If you truly believed your every deed is being recorded and will be weighed, how would your actions today be different? Write one concrete change you will make.
?
You will face the scales of justice. Which sin do you most hope is outweighed by your good deeds, and what are you doing right now to tip the balance?

Belief in the Ṣirāṭ (Bridge)
The author states the obligation to believe in the Ṣirāṭ and cites Quran and hadith.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic belief in the Ṣirāṭ (Bridge) as a real physical bridge over Hell, supported by Quran and hadith, and the obligation to believe in it.

Core Idea
The Ṣirāṭ is a real bridge over Hell; belief in it is obligatory.

This lesson presents the obligation to believe in the Ṣirāṭ (Bridge) as part of the unseen realities of the Hereafter. The author cites scriptural evidence from the Quran and authentic hadith to establish its reality, including descriptions of the bridge, the hooks that snatch people according to their deeds, and the manner of resurrection. We will examine these texts and understand the importance of affirming this belief.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation to get an overview of the content.
Pay attention to the authoritative tone: 'wājib i‘tiqād'.
2
Identify the obligation
Notice that the author begins by stating that belief in the Ṣirāṭ is obligatory, then provides evidence from Quran and sunna.
3
Examine the hadith descriptions
Look at the details: the bridge over Hell, the Prophet ﷺ and his followers first to cross, the hooks that snatch people, and the resurrection on the face.
These details affirm the physical reality of the Ṣirāṭ.
4
Reflect on the supplication
The passage ends with a dua asking for guidance to the straight path, connecting to the Ṣirāṭ.
This reminds us to seek Allah's guidance.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واجب اعتقاده قال الله تعالى: ﴿ فَاسْتَبَقُوا الصِّرَاطَ ﴾، وفي الحديث الصحيح: ويضرب الصراط بين ظهراني جهنم فأكون أنا وأمتي أول من يجوزه، ولا يتكلم يومئذ إلا الرسل، ودعواهم يومئذ: اللهم سَلَم سلم، وفي جهنم كلاليب مثل شوك السعدان، غير أنه لا يعلم عظمها إلا الله، تخطف الناس بأعمالهم، فمنهم المؤمن يؤتي بعمله، ومنهم المجازى حتى ينجى. وفي الصحيحين عن أنس رضي الله عنه أن رجلًا قال: يا نبي الله كيف يحشر الكافر على وجهه يوم القيامة، قال: «أليس الذي أمشاه على الرجلين قادر على أن يمشيه على وجهه يوم القيامة». اللهم اهدنا الصراط المستقيم، صراط الذين أنعمت عليهم غير المغضوب عليهم ولا الضالين، آمين.
It is obligatory to believe in it. Allah Almighty said: ﴿Then they shall hasten to the bridge﴾, and in the authentic hadith: The bridge will be placed across the midst of Hell, and I and my followers will be the first to cross it. On that day none will speak except the messengers, and their invocation will be: O Allah, grant safety, grant safety. In Hell there are hooks like the thorns of the Sa‘dān plant, but none knows their magnitude except Allah. They snatch people according to their deeds. Among them are the believer who is rewarded for his deed, and among them are the one who is punished until he is saved. And in the two Sahihs, from Anas (may Allah be pleased with him) that a man said: O Prophet of Allah, how will the disbeliever be gathered on his face on the Day of Resurrection? He said: Is not the One who made him walk on his legs able to make him walk on his face on the Day of Resurrection? O Allah, guide us to the straight path, the path of those upon whom You have bestowed favor, not of those who have incurred [Your] wrath, nor of those who are astray. Amen.
Source pages p. 93

Commentary
This passage is a statement of obligatory belief in the Ṣirāṭ (the bridge). The author begins by declaring that belief in the Ṣirāṭ is obligatory (wājib i‘tiqād). He then provides scriptural evidence. First, a Qur’anic verse from Sūrat Yā Sīn (36:66): "Then they shall hasten to the bridge" – indicating the reality of the Ṣirāṭ. Then he cites an authentic hadith describing the Ṣirāṭ as a bridge placed over Hell. The Prophet ﷺ says he and his followers will be the first to cross. At that time only the messengers will speak, and their call will be "O Allah, grant safety" (Allāhumma sallim sallim). The hadith further describes hooks on the bridge like the thorns of the sa‘dān plant, of a size known only to Allah, which snatch people according to their deeds. Some are taken to punishment and some are saved. Next, the author cites another hadith from the two Ṣaḥīḥs (Bukhārī and Muslim) about the disbeliever being resurrected on his face. A companion asks how this is possible, and the Prophet replies that the One who made him walk on his legs is certainly able to make him walk on his face. The passage concludes with a supplication (du‘ā) asking for guidance to the straight path, which mirrors the Ṣirāṭ and the Fātiḥa prayer.

Key Terms
الصراط
الكلاليب
الحشر على الوجه

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the Ṣirāṭ is only metaphorical or symbolic.
Correction: The author affirms it as a real, physical bridge over Hell, supported by explicit Quran and hadith.

Checkpoint
According to the hadith cited in this passage, who will be the first to cross the Ṣirāṭ?
A) The prophets
B) Muhammad and his followers
C) The angels
D) The believers of all nations

Key Takeaway
Belief in the Ṣirāṭ as a real bridge over Hell is an obligatory part of Islamic creed, affirmed by Quran and authentic hadith.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The Ṣirāṭ has hooks that snatch people according to their deeds. Think of one deed you did today that you would be ashamed to have snatch you into Hell. Will you repent and change that habit tonight, or let it remain as a hook awaiting you?
?
On that bridge, only the messengers speak, and the cry is 'Allāhumma sallim sallim'. When was the last time you cried out to Allah with that urgency—not out of routine, but out of a deep sense of danger? If you cannot remember, what needs to change so that your du'a becomes a lifeline?
?
The Prophet ﷺ said he and his followers will be the first to cross. Do you truly consider yourself among his followers in the way you live? Or does your life reflect a comfortable distance from his sunnah? What is one concrete step you will take this week to draw closer to being among those first to cross?
