Belief in the Mīzān: The Scale of Deeds
The author states belief in the Mīzān, describes its two pans, and cites the hadith of the card.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic belief in the Mīzān, its two pans, its position before the Ṣirāṭ, and the significance of the hadith of the card.

Core Idea
The Mīzān is a real scale with two pans where deeds are weighed on the Day of Judgment.

In this passage from al-Bahja al-Saniyya, the author establishes the obligatory belief in the Mīzān, citing Qur’anic verses and a famous hadith. We will explore the nature of this scale, its two pans, and the profound lesson from the hadith of the card. Let’s read the source together.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic passage and its English translation to get an overall sense of the content. Notice the flow from scriptural proofs to physical description to the hadith.
Pay attention to the Quranic verses quoted.
2
Identify the scriptural proofs
Locate the three Quranic verses the author uses to prove the Mīzān. Understand that these verses establish the reality of weighing on that Day.
The verses are from Sūrat al-A‘rāf, al-Anbiyā’, and al-Mu’minūn.
3
Examine the description of the Mīzān
Note that it has two pans: a luminous one for good deeds and a dark one for evil deeds. It is placed before the Ṣirāṭ.
The author also states that it is one scale for all nations, and the plural in the verse is for glorification.
4
Analyze the hadith of the card
Read the hadith carefully. Notice the man with ninety-nine huge scrolls of sins, and a tiny card with the shahada that outweighs them all. Reflect on the lesson about Allah's mercy.
This hadith shows that what is weighed is the scroll of deeds (ṣaḥīfat al-a‘māl).
5
Reflect on theological implications
Consider what this passage teaches about justice, mercy, and the importance of sincere faith. The card's weight shows that nothing outweighs the name of Allah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الإيمان بالميزان (والميزان)، أي: ومما يجب اعتقاده الميزان، والوزن وهو حق ثابت يجب الإيمان به، قال الله تعالى: ﴿ وَالْوَزْنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُّ ﴾، ﴿ وَنَضَعُ الْمَوَازِينَ الْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ ﴾، ﴿ فَمَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَازِينُهُ فَأُولَئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ * وَمَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوَازِينُهُ فَأُولَئِكَ الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنفُسَهُمْ ﴾. وله كفتان نورانية للحسنات، وظلمانية للسيئات، وهو قبل الصراط توزن به أعمال العباد، وبلغت أحاديثه من السنة التواتر، قال المؤلف: والصحيح أنه ميزان واحد لجميع الأمم، ولجميع الأعمال، والجمع في الآية للتعظيم. ولتعلم أن الموزون هو صحيفة الأعمال، لحديث البطاقة المرفوع إلى رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم، وهو « أن الله يستخلص رجلا من أمتي على رؤوس الخلائق يوم القيامة، فينشر له تسعة وتسعون سجلا، كل سجل منها مد البصر، فيقول له: الله يا عبدي هل بقي لك حسنة؟ فيقول: لا يا ربي، فيقول الله تعالى: بلى بقى لك عندنا أمانة، فيأمر بإخراج بطاقة، وهي ورقة صغيرة قدر الأنملة، مكتوب بها لا إله إلا الله محمد رسول الله، فتوضع في كفة الحسنات فتطيش سجلات المعاصي، ولا يثقل مع اسم الله شيء، فيقول: امضوا بعبدي إلى الجنة بفضلي ومغفرتي».
Belief in the Mīzān (the scale). (And the Mīzān) means: Among what must be believed is the Mīzān, and the weighing, and it is an established truth that must be believed. Allah Almighty said: ﴿And the weighing on that day is the truth﴾, ﴿And We shall set up the scales of justice for the Day of Resurrection﴾, ﴿Then those whose scales are heavy, they are the successful. And those whose scales are light, they are the ones who have lost their own souls﴾. It has two pans: a luminous one for good deeds, and a dark one for evil deeds. It is before the Ṣirāṭ. With it the deeds of the servants are weighed. The hadiths about it from the Sunnah have reached the level of tawātur. The author said: The correct view is that it is one scale for all nations and for all deeds, and the plural in the verse is for glorification. And know that what is weighed is the scroll of deeds, according to the hadith of the card (al-biṭāqa) raised to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ: "Indeed, Allah will single out a man from my community before all creatures on the Day of Resurrection, and He will spread for him ninety-nine scrolls, each scroll extending as far as the eye can see. Then He will say: O My servant, do you have any good deed left? He will say: No, my Lord. Then Allah Almighty will say: Rather, you have a trust with Us. Then He will order the extraction of a card - a small paper the size of a fingertip, on which is written: Lā ilāha illā Allāh, Muḥammad Rasūl Allāh. So it is placed in the pan of good deeds, and it outweighs the scrolls of sins. Nothing outweighs the name of Allah. Then He will say: Take My servant to Paradise by My favor and My forgiveness."
Source pages p. 93

Commentary
This passage defines the belief in the Mīzān (the scale) as an obligatory element of Islamic creed. The author begins by stating that belief in the Mīzān and the weighing (al-wazn) is a necessary part of faith. He then provides three Qur’anic proofs: from Sūrat al-A‘rāf (7:8) that the weighing on that Day is the truth; from Sūrat al-Anbiyā’ (21:47) that Allah will set up the scales of justice; and from Sūrat al-Mu’minūn (23:102-103) about those whose scales are heavy being successful and those whose scales are light being losers. Next, the author describes the Mīzān physically: it has two pans (kaffatān) – a luminous one for good deeds and a dark one for evil deeds. The Mīzān is positioned before the Ṣirāṭ (bridge). The hadiths about it are mutawātir (massively transmitted). Then the author quotes the author of the matn (the original poem) stating that the correct view is that there is only one scale for all nations and all deeds; the plural form in the Qur’an (mawāzīn) is for glorification. Finally, the author explains that what is weighed is the scroll of deeds (ṣaḥīfat al-a‘māl), citing the famous hadith of the card (ḥadīth al-biṭāqa). In that hadith, a man has ninety-nine scrolls of sins, but a small card with the testimony of faith outweighs them all, and he is admitted to Paradise by Allah’s favor and forgiveness.

Key Terms
الميزان
الكفتان
البطاقة
صحيفة الأعمال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Mīzān is only a metaphor for divine justice.
Correction: The author affirms the Mīzān as a real physical scale with two pans, based on clear Quranic verses and mutawātir hadiths.

Checkpoint
What does the hadith of the card (biṭāqa) teach us about what is weighed on the Mīzān?
A) Only the record of deeds
B) The scrolls themselves
C) The deeds weighed intrinsically
D) The person’s soul

Key Takeaway
The Mīzān is a real scale with two pans where the scrolls of deeds are weighed, and even the smallest testimony of faith can outweigh a lifetime of sins by Allah's mercy.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The hadith describes a man with ninety-nine scrolls of sins and only one card bearing the shahada. If you were to see your own scrolls today, would you trust that your shahada is sincere enough to outweigh them? What would need to change in your heart for that card to be truly heavy?
?
You know that the Mīzān is real and that every deed is recorded. Right now, in this moment, what is the weight of your current action? Are you adding to the luminous pan or the dark one? How will you shift your next hour toward good?
?
If the card with 'Lā ilāha illā Allāh' can outweigh everything, yet you still find yourself attached to sins, ask yourself: Do I truly believe in the power of that word? Or have I taken it for granted? What would it mean for you to live today as if that card is your only hope?

The Balance and the Weight of Faith
Description of the Balance, its two pans, and the hadith of the card

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the reality of the Balance (al-Mizan), its two pans, and the powerful hadith demonstrating that the testimony of faith outweighs all sins.

Core Idea
The Balance weighs deeds, not persons, and a single card of faith outweighs mountains of sin.

This passage from al-Bahja al-Saniyya explains the nature of the Balance that will be used on the Day of Judgment. The author clarifies common misunderstandings and supports his explanation with a moving hadith. As you read, pay attention to the physical description of the Balance and the profound theological lesson in the story of the card.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once and then its English translation. Let the author's own words set the stage before you analyze it.
Notice the concise style of the matn.
2
Identify the two pans
The passage describes two pans: a luminous one for good deeds and a dark one for evil deeds. Ask yourself why these descriptions are used.
3
Understand the key doctrinal point
The author states that only one Balance exists for all nations and all deeds, and that what is weighed is the scroll of deeds, not the person. This corrects a common misunderstanding.
The plural in the Quran is for veneration.
4
Reflect on the hadith of the card
Read the hadith carefully. It shows how a small card with the testimony of faith outweighs huge registers of sins. This illustrates the power of faith and God's mercy.
Consider the lesson: 'Nothing weighs heavier than the name of God.'

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وله كفتان نورانية للحسنات، وظلمانية للسيئات، وهو قبل الصراط توزن به أعمال العباد، وبلغت أحاديثه من السنة التواتر، قال المؤلف: والصحيح أنه ميزان واحد لجميع الأمم، ولجميع الأعمال، والجمع في الآية للتعظيم. ولتعلم أن الموزون هو صحيفة الأعمال، لحديث البطاقة المرفوع إلى رسول الله صل الله عليه وعلى اله وسلم، وهو « أن الله يستخلص رجلا من أمتي على رؤوس الخلائق يوم القيامة، فينشر له تسعة وتسعون سجلا، كل سجل منها مد البصر، فيقول له: الله يا عبدي هل بقي لك حسنة؟ فيقول: لا يا ربي، فيقول الله تعالى: بلى بقى لك عندنا أمانة، فيأمر بإخراج بطاقة، وهي ورقة صغيرة قدر الأنملة، مكتوب بها لا إله إلا الله محمد رسول الله، فتوضع في كفة الحسنات فتطيش سجلات المعاصي، ولا يثقل مع اسم الله شيء، فيقول: امضوا بعبدي إلى الجنة بفضلي ومغفرتي». (١)
And it has two pans: a luminous one for good deeds and a dark one for evil deeds, and it is before the Path (al-Sirat). The deeds of servants are weighed with it, and its hadiths have reached tawatur from the Sunnah. The author said: The correct view is that it is one balance for all nations and for all deeds, and the plural in the verse is for veneration. And know that what is weighed is the scroll of deeds, according to the hadith of the card raised to the Messenger of God (peace be upon him and his family): 'Indeed, God will select a man from my nation before the heads of creation on the Day of Resurrection, and He will unfurl for him ninety-nine registers, each register as far as the eye can see. Then He will say to him: O My servant, do you have any good deed left? He will say: No, my Lord. Then God Almighty will say: Rather, you have a trust with Us. Then He will order the extraction of a card, which is a small paper the size of a fingertip, on which is written "There is no god but God, Muhammad is the Messenger of God." It will be placed in the pan of good deeds, and the registers of sins will be outweighed, and nothing weighs heavier than the name of God. Then He will say: Take My servant to Paradise by My favor and My forgiveness.'
Source pages p. 94

Commentary
This passage defines the Balance (al-Mizan) and clarifies its reality. It states that the Balance has two pans: a luminous one for good deeds and a dark one for evil deeds. It is placed before the Path (al-Sirat) and is used to weigh the deeds of servants. The author notes that hadiths about the Balance are mutawatir (massively transmitted). The correct opinion, according to the author, is that there is only one Balance for all nations and all deeds, and the plural form in the Quran (موازين) is for veneration, not multiplicity. He then explains that the object weighed is the scroll of deeds (sahifat al-a'mal), not the person themselves. He supports this with the famous hadith of the card (hadith al-bitaqa), which describes a man with ninety-nine heavy registers of sins but a single card bearing the testimony of faith that outweighs them all. This hadith demonstrates the immense weight of the declaration of faith and that God's mercy prevails.

Key Terms
الميزان
الصراط
صحيفة الأعمال
بطاقة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the Balance weighs persons rather than deeds.
Correction: The passage clarifies that what is weighed is the scroll of deeds (sahifat al-a'mal), not the person.

Checkpoint
According to the text, what is weighed on the Balance (al-Mizan)?
A) The persons themselves
B) The scroll of deeds (sahifat al-a'mal)
C) The souls of the believers
D) The intentions

Key Takeaway
The Balance (al-Mizan) weighs the scroll of deeds, and even a tiny act of faith can outweigh enormous sins by God's mercy.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that your every deed is being recorded and will be weighed? How would your actions today change if you were acutely aware of that moment?
?
The hadith shows that a tiny card of faith outweighs mountains of sin. Is there a sin you consider too great for God's mercy? How does this hadith challenge you to trust in that mercy?
?
The card says 'There is no god but God, Muhammad is the Messenger of God.' Is your shahada merely a statement you utter, or does it carry the weight of a life transformed? What would it look like for your faith to actually outweigh your sins?

The Pool of the Prophet: Belief and Description
Affirmation of belief in the Pool, its description, and correction of exclusivity misunderstanding.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation to believe in the Prophet's Pool, its description from authentic hadith, and the correct view that every prophet has a Pool.

Core Idea
The Prophet's Pool is a promised reward; every prophet has one, and it is al-Kawthar.

This lesson addresses the belief in the Pool (al-Hawd) of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), which is part of the unseen realities that Muslims must affirm. The author provides a hadith description and clarifies a common misunderstanding about whether the Pool is exclusive to the Prophet. Let's explore the textual evidence and the correct position.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage followed by the literal translation. Notice the structure: affirmation, hadith, scholarly opinion, commentary, supplication.
The hadith is from the Two Sahihs.
2
Identify the Pool's attributes
From the hadith, list the attributes: size, color, scent, number of cups, and effect of drinking.
These are sensory descriptions to encourage longing.
3
Note the correction of misunderstanding
The author states that the correct view is that every prophet has a Pool, not only Muhammad (peace be upon him). This corrects those who think it is exclusive.
This is a key doctrinal point.
4
Reflect on the supplication
The passage ends with a supplication to be allowed to drink from the Pool. Reflect on its significance as a prayer for believers.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الإيمان بالحوض | والحوض) أي: ومما يجب الإيمان به حوض النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم الذي يعطاه في الآخرة، وأحاديثه متواترة، وفي «الصحيحين» من حديث عبد الله بن عمرو بن العاص رضي الله عنهما: «حوضي مسيرة شهر، وزواياه أي نواحيه الأربع أي طوله وعرضه سواء، ماؤه أبيض من اللبن، وريحه أطيب من المسك، وكيزانه أكثر من نجوم السماء، من شرب منه لا يظمأ أبدا». واختلف والصحيح أنه لكل نبي حوض، وأنه قبل الميزان، وهو الكوثر الذي أعطيه نبينا صلى الله عليه وسلم، قال الناظم في «شرحه»: والحوض جسم مخصوص يصب فيه ميزابان من ماء الكوثر، ترده أمته عليه الصلاة والسلام، من شرب منه شربة لا يظمأ بعدها أبدا، ويطرد عنه من بدل وغير هذه الشريعة الغراء، اللهم سلم سلم. نسأل الله تعالى أن يسقينا من حوض نبينا صلى الله عليه وسلم وأحبابنا، بمنه وكرمه، آمين.
Faith in the Pool (al-Hawd). That is, among the things that must be believed is the Pool of the Prophet (peace be upon him) which he will be given in the Hereafter. Its hadiths are mutawatir. In the Two Sahihs, from the hadith of Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As (may God be pleased with them both): 'My Pool is a month's journey, its corners (i.e., its four sides) are equal, its water is whiter than milk, its scent is better than musk, its cups are more than the stars of the sky; whoever drinks from it will never thirst again.' There is a difference of opinion, but the correct view is that every prophet has a Pool, and that it is before the Balance (al-Mizan). It is al-Kawthar which was given to our Prophet (peace be upon him). The author (al-Nazim) said in his commentary: 'The Pool is a specific body into which two spouts pour water from al-Kawthar. His nation (upon him peace and blessings) will come to it; whoever drinks a single drink from it will never thirst thereafter. And he will repel those who alter and change this radiant Sharia. O God, grant peace, grant peace. We ask God Almighty to give us to drink from the Pool of our Prophet (peace be upon him) and our loved ones, by His grace and generosity. Amen.'
Source pages p. 95

Commentary
This passage affirms the obligation to believe in the Pool (al-Hawd) of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). The author states that the hadiths about it are mutawatir, indicating certainty. He then quotes a well-known hadith from the Two Sahihs describing the Pool's size (a month's journey in each direction), its water (whiter than milk), its scent (better than musk), its cups (more numerous than stars), and the effect that whoever drinks from it will never thirst again. The author notes a difference of opinion regarding whether the Pool is exclusive to the Prophet, but he affirms the correct view: every prophet has a Pool, and the Prophet's Pool is al-Kawthar given to him. He then cites a commentary describing the Pool as a physical body (jism makhsus) into which two spouts (mizaban) pour water from al-Kawthar. The nation will come to it, and those who have altered the Sharia will be repelled. The passage ends with a supplication to be allowed to drink from the Prophet's Pool.

Key Terms
الحوض
الكوثر
ميزابان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe the Pool is exclusively for the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), and no other prophet has a Pool.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that the correct view is that every prophet has a Pool (والصحيح أنه لكل نبي حوض).

Checkpoint
According to the passage, which of the following is the correct view regarding the Pool (al-Hawd)?
A) It is exclusively for the Prophet Muhammad
B) Every prophet has a Pool
C) It is only metaphorical
D) It is before the Sirat

Key Takeaway
Belief in the Prophet's Pool is obligatory; it is described vividly in authentic hadith, and every prophet has a Pool, with Muhammad's being the Kawthar.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The Prophet will repel those who change his Sharia from his Pool. Are you altering any part of the religion to suit your desires, or are you submitting completely? What is one change you need to make tonight to ensure you are not repelled?
?
You read that the water is whiter than milk and its scent better than musk. Yet you chase after worldly drinks and perfumes that fade. What is your soul truly thirsting for? What are you doing today to prepare for that eternal drink?
?
If you stood at the edge of the Prophet's Pool right now, would you be confident that you are among those who will be allowed to drink? What sin or negligence in your life stands between you and that cup? Make a firm intention tonight to remove it.

The Fire (al-Niran) and Its Seven Layers
Sanusi defines the Fire, affirms its creation, and enumerates its seven layers with inhabitants.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic belief in the Fire (Hell) as an already created reality, its seven layers, and the inhabitants of each layer as mentioned in the tradition.

Core Idea
Hell is real, created now, and has seven layers—each for a different group.

In this lesson, we examine the Islamic doctrine regarding the Fire of Hell. The author defines what al-Niran is, affirms its present existence, and then details its seven layers, specifying the inhabitants of each. This knowledge reinforces the importance of belief in the afterlife and the seriousness of disbelief and sin.

Guided Path
1
Understand the definition of al-Niran
Read the first part where al-Niran is defined as the abode of punishment and belief in it is obligatory.
The term 'al-Niran' is the plural of 'Nar'.
2
Affirm that the Fire is already created
Note the statement 'وهي مخلوقة اليوم' (it is created today). This counters the misconception that Hell is not yet created.
3
Learn the seven layers in order
Go through each layer from highest to lowest, noting the name and the group assigned to it.
The order is important: Jahannam, Ladha, al-Hutama, al-Sa'ir, Saqar, al-Jahim, al-Hawiya.
4
Reflect on the inhabitants
Notice how different religious groups and sinners are assigned to specific layers. This shows divine justice.
5
Connect to personal faith
Consider the implications of this doctrine for your own belief and actions. It motivates righteousness and caution.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الإيمان بالنيران (والنيران بكسر النون جمع نار وهي دار العقاب، أجارنا الله والجنان منها، وهي ثابتة بالكتاب والسنة، فيجب الإيمان بذلك، وهي مخلوقة اليوم، وهي طبقات أعلاها جهنم لعصاة المؤمنين، فلظى لليهود، فالحطمة للنصارى، فالسعير وفيها الصابئون، فسقر وفيها المجوس، فالجحيم وفيها عبدة الأصنام، فالهاوية وفيها المنافقون.
Faith in the Fires (al-Niran). Al-Niran (with a kasra on the nun) is the plural of Nar (Fire), which is the abode of punishment. May God protect us and the Gardens from it. It is established by the Book and the Sunnah, so faith in it is obligatory. It is created today. It has layers: its highest is Jahannam for the sinful believers, then Ladha for the Jews, then al-Hutama for the Christians, then al-Sa'ir in which are the Sabians, then Saqar in which are the Magians, then al-Jahim in which are the idol worshippers, then al-Hawiya in which are the hypocrites.
Source pages p. 95

Commentary
This passage defines the Fire (al-Niran) as the abode of punishment. The author affirms that belief in it is obligatory because it is established by the Quran and Sunnah. He states that the Fire is already created (makhluqa al-yawm), meaning it exists now. He then enumerates the seven layers of Hell, naming each and specifying its inhabitants: 1) Jahannam for the sinful believers (i.e., those Muslims who committed major sins without repentance), 2) Ladha for the Jews, 3) al-Hutama for the Christians, 4) al-Sa'ir for the Sabians, 5) Saqar for the Magians (Zoroastrians), 6) al-Jahim for idol worshippers, and 7) al-Hawiya for the hypocrites. This enumeration reinforces the reality and seriousness of the afterlife punishment and clarifies that each major group of disbelievers and sinners has a specific layer.

Key Terms
النيران
جهنم
لظى
الحطمة
السعير
سقر
الجحيم
الهاوية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think Hell is not yet created or that all disbelievers go to the same layer.
Correction: The text affirms Hell is created now, and there are distinct layers for different groups.

Checkpoint
According to the text, which layer of Hell is for the sinful believers (عصاة المؤمنين)?
A) Jahannam
B) Ladha
C) al-Hawiya
D) Saqar

Key Takeaway
The Fire of Hell is real, already created, and consists of seven distinct layers, each reserved for a specific category of disbelievers or sinful believers.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The text says the highest layer, Jahannam, is for 'sinful believers'. If you were judged solely by your sins today, would you be among them? What concrete action will you take this week to repent from a major sin you have been neglecting?
?
The lowest layer, al-Hawiya, is for hypocrites (munafiqun). Have you ever acted in a way that outwardly shows faith but inwardly doubts or disobeys? Identify one specific situation where you were a hypocrite and resolve to correct it with a sincere intention.
?
Knowing that Hell is already created and that each layer awaits a specific group, how does this urgency affect your responsibility toward non-Muslims around you? Will you pray for them and take a step to share the truth, or will you remain comfortable?

Belief in the Prophet's Basin (al-Hawd)
Definition and proof of the Prophet's Basin obligation and attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic obligation to believe in the Prophet's Basin (al-Hawd) and its described attributes based on authentic hadith.

Core Idea
The Prophet's Basin is a real, physical blessing in the Hereafter, confirmed by mutawatir hadith.

Belief in the unseen events of the Hereafter is a fundamental part of Islamic faith. Among these is the Prophet Muhammad's Basin (al-Hawd), a special body of water prepared for his followers. In this passage, we read the scriptural evidence for its reality and its detailed description, affirming that it is not a metaphor but a concrete gift from Allah.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Carefully read the Arabic text and its literal translation to grasp the original wording.
2
Identify the obligation to believe
Notice how the passage begins by stating that belief in the Basin is obligatory and that its hadiths are mutawatir.
3
Examine the description from the hadith
The hadith from the Two Sahihs gives specific dimensions, qualities, and the effect of drinking from it.
4
Understand the correct theological view
The passage clarifies that every prophet has a Basin and that this particular Basin is before the Scale (al-Mizan) and is al-Kawthar.
5
Reflect on the supplication
The passage ends with a du'a, showing the practical hope of being among those who drink from it.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الإيمان بالحوض | والحوض) أي: ومما يجب الإيمان به حوض النبي صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ الذي يعطاه في الآخرة، وأحاديثه متواترة، وفي «الصحيحين»(۱) من حديث عبد الله بن عمرو بن العاص رض اللَّهُ عَنْها: «حوضي مسيرة شهر، وزواياه أي نواحيه الأربع أي طوله وعرضه سواء، ماؤه أبيض من اللبن ، وريحه أطيب من المسك، وكيزانه أكثر من نجوم السماء، من شرب منه لا يظمأ أبدا». خلاف والصحيح أنه لكل نبي ،حوض، وأنه قبل الميزان، وهو الكوثر الذي أعطيه نبينا صلى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ ، قال الناظم في «شرحه»(۲): والحوض جسم مخصوص يَصُبُّ فيه مِيزَابَان من ماء الكوثر، ترده أمته عَلَيْهِ الصَّلَاةُ وَالسَّلَامُ ، مَن شرب منه شربة لا يظمأ بعدها أبدًا، ويطرد عنه من بدل وغيّر هذه الشريعة الغراء، اللهم سلم سلم، نسأل الله تعالى أن يسقينا من حوض نبينا صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ وأحبابنا، بمنه وكرمه، آمين.
Belief in the Basin (al-Hawd). And the Basin means: from what it is obligatory to believe in is the Basin of the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, which he is given in the Hereafter, and its hadiths are mutawatir (massively transmitted). And in the Two Sahihs (1) from the hadith of Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-Aas, may Allah be pleased with him: 'My Basin is a month's journey, its corners – i.e., its four sides – are equal, its water is whiter than milk, its scent is better than musk, its drinking vessels are more numerous than the stars of the sky; whoever drinks from it will never thirst again.' A difference [exists], and the correct [view] is that every prophet has a Basin, and that it is before the Scale (al-Mizan), and it is al-Kawthar which our Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, was given. The versifier said in his commentary (2): 'The Basin is a specific body into which two spouts from the water of al-Kawthar pour; his nation, peace and blessings be upon him, proceeds to it; whoever drinks a single drink from it will never thirst after it forever; and he is repelled from it whoever changed and altered this radiant Sharia. O Allah, grant peace, grant peace; we ask Allah Most High to give us to drink from the Basin of our Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and our beloved ones, by His bounty and generosity, Amen.'
Source pages p. 95

Commentary
This passage is a definition and proof of the obligation to believe in the Prophet’s Basin (al-Hawd). It begins by stating that belief in the Basin is obligatory, describing it as a gift given to the Prophet Muhammad in the afterlife. The hadiths about it are mutawatir (massively transmitted), specifically citing a hadith from the Two Sahihs. The hadith describes the Basin as having a distance of a month, equal sides, water whiter than milk, scent better than musk, more cups than stars, and that whoever drinks from it will never thirst. The passage then notes a difference of opinion, but affirms the correct view that every prophet has a Basin, and that this Basin is before the Scale (al-Mizan). It clarifies that the Basin is the Kawthar given to our Prophet. The commentary from the versifier (the original poet) describes the Basin as a specific body with two spouts of the water of Kawthar. The Prophet’s nation will come to it; whoever drinks once is eternally quenched; and those who changed the Sharia are repelled. The passage ends with a supplication for Allah to grant us drink from this Basin.

Key Terms
الحوض
الكوثر
الميزان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the Basin is a metaphor for spiritual reward, not a physical reality.
Correction: The passage affirms it is a real, physical body with specific dimensions and properties, and its hadiths are mutawatir, confirming its literal existence.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the correct view regarding the Basin (al-Hawd)?
A) Only Prophet Muhammad has a Basin.
B) Every prophet has a Basin, and the Prophet’s Basin is before the Scale.
C) The Basin is the same as the Scale (al-Mizan).
D) The Basin is a metaphorical concept.

Key Takeaway
Belief in the Prophet’s Basin is obligatory, described as a vast, pure, and eternal blessing for the believers, confirmed by massively transmitted hadiths.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read that those who changed the Sharia are repelled from the Basin, does a fear rise in you that any of your actions or beliefs have altered the true religion? What specific step will you take today to ensure you remain on the straight path and are among those who drink?
?
The Prophet describes his Basin as whiter than milk and sweeter than musk, yet do you find yourself longing for that drink more than you crave the temporary pleasures of this world? How will you increase your yearning for that eternal refreshment starting this week?
?
The hadith promises that whoever drinks once will never thirst again. Are you living with the conviction of that eternal promise, or are you still chasing satisfaction from things that will never truly quench your soul? What is one worldly attachment you can surrender today to demonstrate your trust in that never-ending fulfillment?

Belief in Paradise and Its Levels
Defines Paradise, affirms its existence, and lists its highest levels.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of Paradise, its reality, and the names of its highest levels.

Core Idea
Paradise is real, created now, and leads to divine companionship.

In this passage, the author affirms the existence of Paradise as an established truth from the Quran, Sunna, and consensus. He then enumerates its highest levels, culminating in the supreme abode. Understanding these basics strengthens one's iman in the unseen reward.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic text carefully, noting the plural form of janna and the description of Paradise as the abode of reward.
The word 'jinan' is the plural of 'janna'.
2
Understand the proofs
Notice that the author cites the Quran, Sunna, and consensus as evidence for Paradise's existence, and quotes a verse.
The verse 'Prepared for the righteous' confirms its reality.
3
Identify the levels
List the names of the levels in order: al-Firdaws, Jannat al-Ma'wa, etc. Note that al-Firdaws is the highest.
Above al-Firdaws is the Throne of the Most Merciful.
4
Reflect on the implication
Consider that Paradise exists today, contrary to some misconceptions. This affirms the immediate reality of the Hereafter.
This is the view of Ahl al-Sunna.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(والجنان) بكسر الجيم جمع جنة والمراد بها: دار الثواب والكرامة، وهي حق ثابت بالكتاب والسنة وإجماع الأمة، فيجب الإيمان بها، وهي موجودة اليوم، قال الله تعالى: ﴿ أُعِدَّتْ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (١) جعلنا الله منهم، أعلاها الفردوس، وفوقها عرش الرحمن، ومنها تتفجر أنهار الجنة، فجنة المأوى، فجنة الخُلد، فجنة النعيم، فجنة عدن، فدار السلام فدار الجلال.
(And the Gardens (al-Jinan)) – with a kasra on the jim, plural of Garden (janna), and what is meant by it: the abode of reward and honor. And it is true, established by the Book, the Sunna, and the consensus of the nation, so belief in it is obligatory. And it exists today. Allah Most High said: {Prepared for the righteous.} (1) May Allah make us among them. Its highest is al-Firdaws, and above it is the Throne of the Most Merciful, and from it the rivers of Paradise burst forth. Then Jannat al-Ma'wa, then Jannat al-Khuld, then Jannat al-Na'im, then Jannat 'Adn, then Dar al-Salam, then Dar al-Jalal.
Source pages p. 96

Commentary
This passage is a definition and proof of the obligation to believe in Paradise (al-Jinan). It begins by explaining the word 'al-Jinan' as the plural of 'janna' (garden), referring to the abode of reward and honor. Paradise is true (haqq), established by the Quran, Sunna, and consensus of the Muslim community, so belief in it is obligatory. It affirms that Paradise exists today (mawjuda al-yawm). A Quranic verse is cited: 'Prepared for the righteous' (preceding verse reference omitted). The passage then lists the levels of Paradise from highest to lowest: first al-Firdaws, above which is the Throne of the Most Merciful, and from al-Firdaws the rivers of Paradise spring. Then follow Jannat al-Ma'wa, Jannat al-Khuld, Jannat al-Na'im, Jannat 'Adn, Dar al-Salam, and Dar al-Jalal.

Key Terms
الجنان
الفردوس
جنة المأوى
جنة الخلد
جنة النعيم
جنة عدن
دار السلام
دار الجلال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Paradise is not yet created and will be created after the Day of Judgment.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that Paradise is already existing today, as indicated by the verse 'Prepared for the righteous' and the consensus of the Ahl al-Sunna.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the highest level of Paradise?
A) Jannat al-Ma'wa
B) Dar al-Salam
C) al-Firdaws
D) Jannat al-Khuld

Key Takeaway
Paradise (al-Jinan) is a real, created abode of reward, whose highest level is al-Firdaws, and belief in it is obligatory.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just read that Paradise exists today and its highest level is al-Firdaws, above which is the Throne. If you were asked right now, 'What have you done today that brings you closer to that highest level?' — could you honestly give a concrete answer? What will you change tonight to answer that question tomorrow?
?
The passage says Paradise is 'prepared for the righteous' (أُعِدَّتْ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ). Are you among the righteous? Don't give a general answer: list one specific act of taqwa you have neglected that you will commit to this week. Feel the weight of the preparation.
?
The source lists eight names for Paradise (from al-Firdaws to Dar al-Jalal). Each name describes a different aspect: Ma'wa (refuge), Khuld (eternity), Na'im (bliss), 'Adn (perpetual residence), Dar al-Salam (abode of peace), Dar al-Jalal (abode of majesty). Which of these names resonates most with your current longing? Why does that particular name expose a lack in your life today? Write it down as a prayer tonight.

Belief in the Jinn
Affirms the obligation to believe in jinn, their nature, and their divisions.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic doctrine regarding the existence, nature, and divisions of the jinn as established by the Quran and Sunnah.

Core Idea
Jinn are real, subtle fiery beings created by Allah, divided into believers and disbelievers.

The existence of jinn is a fundamental belief in Islam, affirmed by the Quran and Sunnah. This lesson examines the definition, nature, and categories of jinn as presented in the classical text, clarifying their reality and role in creation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the English translation to grasp the overall argument about belief in jinn.
The passage is concise; note the flow from obligation to proof to description.
2
Identify the Quranic proofs
Three Quranic verses are cited. Identify each one and how they establish the existence and nature of jinn.
The verses cover creation, address to both jinn and humans, and the story of jinn listening to the Quran.
3
Understand the nature of jinn
The passage describes jinn as subtle fiery bodies with the ability to take on any form, and the form dominates them. Consider what this implies about their capabilities.
4
Recognize the divisions among jinn
Jinn are divided into obedient and disobedient, believer and disbeliever. Among them are devils whose function is evil and misguidance.
This mirrors human divisions; note the special role of shayatin.
5
Reflect on the obligation to believe
The passage begins with 'it is obligatory' (wajib) to believe in jinn. Reflect on why belief in the unseen is a core part of Islamic creed.
This connects to the broader topic of samiyyat (matters of the unseen).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(و) يجب الإيمان بوجود (الجن) إجمالا لثبوت ذلك بالكتاب والسنة، قال الله تعالى: ﴿ وَخَلَقَ الْجَانَّ مِن مَّارِجٍ مِّن نَّارٍ ﴾ (۲)، ﴿ يَمَعْشَرَ الجن والإنس ) (۳)، ﴿ وَإِذْ صَرَفْنَا إِلَيْكَ نَفَرًا مِّنَ الْجِن يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقُرْعَانَ ) (٤). وهم أجسام لطيفة نارية لهم قدرة على التشكل بأي صورة، وتحكم عليهم الصورة، ومنهم المطيع والعاصي، والمؤمن والكافر، ومنهم الشياطين شأنهم شأن الشر والإغواء وإلقاء الناس في الفساد، بتذكير أسباب المعاصي والملذات.
(And) it is obligatory to believe in the existence of the jinn in general, because it is established by the Book and the Sunna. Allah Most High said: {And He created the jinn from a scorching fire} (2), {O assembly of jinn and humans} (3), {And when We directed to you a group of jinn listening to the Quran} (4). And they are subtle fiery bodies, having the ability to take on any form, and the form dominates them. Among them are the obedient and the disobedient, the believer and the disbeliever. And among them are the devils (shayatin); their affair is the affair of evil, misguidance, and casting people into corruption by reminding them of the causes of sins and pleasures.
Source pages p. 96

Commentary
This passage is a proof for the obligation to believe in the existence of the jinn. It begins by stating that belief in jinn is obligatory (wajib) in a general sense, because it is established by the Quran and Sunna. Three Quranic verses are cited: one describing their creation from a scorching fire, one addressing both jinn and humans, and one about a group of jinn listening to the Quran. The passage then describes the nature of jinn: they are subtle (latifa) fiery bodies; they have the ability to take on any form (tashakkul), and the form dominates them (meaning they are compelled by the form they take). They are divided into obedient and disobedient, believing and disbelieving. Among them are the devils (shayatin), whose function is evil, misguidance, and leading people into corruption by reminding them of the causes of sins and pleasures.

Key Terms
الجن
الأجسام اللطيفة النارية
الشياطين

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Jinn do not exist or are merely symbolic or imaginary beings.
Correction: The Quran and Sunnah establish their real existence as created beings with specific properties and moral accountability.

Checkpoint
What does the passage say about the ability of jinn regarding forms?
A) They have no ability to change forms.
B) They have the ability to take on any form, and the form dominates them.
C) They can take only one fixed form.
D) They can take on forms but only with permission.

Key Takeaway
Belief in jinn is obligatory; they are real, fiery, shape-shifting beings among whom there are believers and disbelievers, including devils who lead people astray.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The jinn are unseen beings who listened to the Quran and responded—some believed, some rejected. When you recite or hear the Quran, are you aware that unseen witnesses may be listening? Does that awareness change how you receive Allah's words? What will you do to be more present when the Quran is recited?
?
Among the jinn are devils whose sole mission is to remind you of sins and pleasures to lead you astray. Knowing that there are unseen enemies actively working against your faith, what specific guard will you put in place today against their whispers?
?
Jinn were created from fire, you from clay. Both are creations of Allah, yet jinn have abilities you do not. How does reflecting on the vastness of Allah's creation—including beings you cannot see—humble your heart? Will you let this humility move you to greater obedience?

The Obligation to Believe in the Jinn
Commentator states the obligation to believe in jinn and provides Quranic evidence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that belief in the existence of the jinn is obligatory based on Quranic and Sunnah proofs.

Core Idea
Belief in the jinn is a required part of Islamic faith.

The commentator begins by affirming that belief in the jinn is an obligation for every Muslim. He cites clear Quranic verses to establish this, showing that the jinn are a creation of Allah like any other. In this lesson, we will explore the textual evidence and the obligation itself.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on obligation and proof
Carefully read the Arabic and translation of the first part of the source, focusing on the phrase 'it is obligatory to believe' and the Quranic verses.
Notice that the obligation is 'in summary' meaning general belief without detailed specifics.
2
Identify the Quranic verses
Three verses are cited: one about creation from fire, one addressing the jinn together with humans, and one about jinn listening to the Qur'an. Reflect on how each supports the existence of jinn.
These verses are from Surah al-Hijr, al-An'am, and al-Ahqaf respectively.
3
Understand 'belief in summary'
The term 'ijmalan' (in summary) means believing in their existence without needing to know every detail. This is a principle in matters of samiyyat (revelational matters).
Detailed specifics may be known from further texts.
4
Reflect on the basis of obligation
The obligation is established by the Book (Qur'an) and the Sunnah. This highlights that belief in jinn is a matter of divine revelation, not reason alone.
This places jinn belief in the category of samiyyat.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(و) يجب الإيمان بوجود (الجن) إجمالا لثبوت ذلك بالكتاب والسنة، قال الله تعالى: ﴿ وَخَلَقَ الْجَانَّ مِن مَّارِجٍ مِّن نَّارٍ ﴾، ﴿ يَمَعْشَرَ الجن والإنس ﴾، ﴿ وَإِذْ صَرَفْنَا إِلَيْكَ نَفَرًا مِّنَ الْجِن يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقُرْعَانَ ﴾. وهم أجسام لطيفة نارية لهم قدرة على التشكل بأي صورة، وتحكم عليهم الصورة، ومنهم المطيع والعاصي، والمؤمن والكافر، ومنهم الشياطين شأنهم شأن الشر والإغواء وإلقاء الناس في الفساد، بتذكير أسباب المعاصي والملذات.
(And) it is obligatory to believe in the existence of the jinn in summary due to its establishment by the Book and the Sunnah. Allah Almighty said: ﴿ And He created the jinn from a smokeless flame of fire ﴾, ﴿ O company of jinn and humans ﴾, ﴿ And when We directed to you a company of the jinn listening to the Qur'an ﴾. They are subtle, fiery bodies, having the ability to take any form, and the form dominates them. Among them are the obedient and the disobedient, the believer and the disbeliever, and among them are the devils; their affair is the affair of evil and misguidance and casting people into corruption, by reminding of the causes of sins and pleasures.
Source pages p. 96

Commentary
This passage affirms the obligation to believe in the existence of the jinn in a general way, based on the Qur'an and Sunnah. The commentator cites three verses: one stating their creation from fire, another addressing them alongside humans, and a third describing their listening to the Qur'an. He then describes the jinn as subtle, fiery bodies that can assume any form, and notes that among them are both obedient and disobedient, believers and disbelievers, and that the devils are a subset of jinn who lead people astray by tempting them with sins and pleasures.

Misconception: Belief in jinn is optional or not required in Islam.
Correction: The passage explicitly states 'it is obligatory to believe in the existence of the jinn,' indicating it is a mandatory part of Islamic creed.

Key Takeaway
Belief in the jinn is an obligation upon every Muslim, firmly established by the Book and the Sunnah.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the Qur'an, do you ever pause to consider that jinn might be listening and being transformed by it, just as mentioned in the verse? How does that awareness change the way you recite?
?
Belief in jinn is obligatory, yet how often do you actively incorporate this belief into your daily thoughts and actions—like seeking refuge from them or reflecting on their creation? What would it take for this belief to become more than a mere intellectual assent?
?
The Qur'an presents the jinn as beings who responded to the truth when they heard it. If you were in their place, would your heart be as open to the Qur'an as theirs was? What is holding you back from that level of sincerity?

The Nature and Diversity of the Jinn
Commentator describes the nature, abilities, and moral diversity of jinn.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the physical nature of the jinn, their ability to take form, and their moral divisions including the devils.

Core Idea
Jinn are subtle fiery beings with free will, capable of good and evil.

After establishing the obligation to believe in jinn, the commentator describes their nature. He explains that they are created from fire, can assume any form, and include both believers and disbelievers, with the devils as a subset. This lesson unpacks this rich description.

Guided Path
1
Read the description of jinn bodies
Read the Arabic and translation describing the jinn as 'subtle, fiery bodies'. Note the contrast with human bodies.
The term 'latifa' means fine or subtle, not dense like human bodies.
2
Understand the ability to take form
The passage states they can take any form, but the form dominates them. Reflect on what this means for their interaction with the physical world.
This ability allows jinn to appear to humans in various shapes.
3
Notice the moral categories
Among jinn are obedient and disobedient, believers and disbelievers. This shows they have free will like humans.
This moral diversity means not all jinn are evil.
4
Note the role of devils
Devils are a subset of jinn whose purpose is to lead people astray by reminding them of sins and pleasures. Understand their function.
Shaytan (Iblis) is a jinn, not an angel.

Source Text

?

?
Among the jinn there are believers and disbelievers, obedient and disobedient. You share this same spectrum. Which side do your actions truly align with—not just in public, but in your private thoughts and hidden moments?
?
The devils' job is to 'cast people into corruption by reminding of sins and pleasures.' Where in your life do you notice these whispers succeeding? What specific step will you take today to guard against them?

Belief in Angels: Obligation and Nature
Affirms obligation to believe in angels and describes their nature.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation to believe in angels in summary, their nature as subtle luminous bodies, and their infallibility.

Core Idea
Angels are subtle luminous servants who never disobey Allah.

Before learning about individual angels, we must first affirm their existence and understand their nature. This passage establishes the obligatory belief in angels in general, describing them as luminous, obedient beings, and proving their existence and infallibility through Quranic verses.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on general belief
Read the Arabic passage carefully, then the translation. Note that the obligation is 'in summary' (ijmalan).
2
Understand the description of angels
The passage describes angels as 'subtle luminous bodies' capable of taking various forms, possessing knowledge, and performing arduous tasks. They are always obedient and dwell in the heavens.
This counters the idea that angels are purely spiritual without any physical aspect.
3
Learn the definition of infallibility
Infallibility ('ismah) is defined as Allah not creating sin in them while they retain ability and choice. This preserves their free will while ensuring they do not sin.
This definition avoids determinism.
4
Review the Quranic proofs
Three verses are cited: one about angels not disdaining worship, one about Allah inspiring angels, and one about Allah and angels sending blessings on the Prophet. These prove existence and infallibility.
The passage says 'other numerous verses' as well.
5
Recognize the consequence of disbelief
The passage states that denying angels constitutes disbelief (kufr).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(و) يجب على كل مكلف الإيمان بوجود (الأملاك)، جمع ملك، والمراد: الملائكة الكرام عليهم الصلاة والسلام إجمالا فيما يجب الإيمان إجمالا. وهم أجسام لطيفة نورانية قادرة على أن تتشكل بأشكال مختلفة، في العلم، والقدرة على الأعمال الشاقة، شأنها الطاعات، وسكنها السموات عباد مكرمون لا يعصون الله ما أمرهم ويفعلون ما يؤمرون، كما يجب الإيمان بعصمتهم، وهي أن لا يخلق الله الذنب في المكلف مع بقاء قدرته واختياره، قال الله تعالى: ﴿ لَّن يَسْتَنكِفَ الْمَسِيحُ أَن يَكُونَ عَبْدًا لِلَّهِ وَلَا الْمَلَيكَةُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ ﴾، وقال تعالى: إذ يُوحى رَبُّكَ إِلَى الْمَلتبِكَةِ أَنِّي مَعَكُمْ ﴾، وقال تعالى: إن الله وَمَلَتيكتَهُ يُصَلُّونَ عَلَى النَّبيُّ ﴾، إلى غير ذلك من الآيات الكثيرة الدالة على وجودهم، وعصمتهم، وعدم الإيمان بهم كفر. أما الذين تجب معرفتهم تفصيلا فهم جبريل أمين الوحي، وميكائيل الموكل بالأرزاق، وإسرافيل الموكل بالنفخ في الصور، وملك الموت الموكل بقبض الأرواح، ومنكر ونكير الموكلان بسؤال الميت في القبر، والملائكة الموكلون بكتابة ما يصدر من المكلف، لكل واحد ملكان يوصف كل منهما بأنه رقيب، أي: مراقب، وعتيد، أي: حاضر، ومالك
(And) it is obligatory upon every legally responsible person to believe in the existence of the angels, plural of malak, meaning: the noble angels, peace and blessings be upon them, in summary with regard to what must be believed in summary. They are subtle, luminous bodies, capable of taking various forms, in knowledge and ability to perform arduous tasks; their affair is obedience, and their dwelling is the heavens. They are honored servants who do not disobey Allah in what He commands them and do what they are commanded. Also, belief in their infallibility is obligatory, which is that Allah does not create sin in the responsible person while his ability and choice remain. Allah Almighty said: ﴿ The Messiah would never disdain to be a servant of Allah, nor the angels brought near ﴾, and He said: ﴿ When your Lord inspired to the angels, 'I am with you' ﴾, and He said: ﴿ Indeed, Allah and His angels send blessings upon the Prophet ﴾, and other numerous verses indicating their existence and infallibility, and disbelief in them is disbelief. As for those whose detailed knowledge is obligatory, they are: Jibril, the trustee of revelation; Mika'il, entrusted with provision; Israfil, entrusted with blowing the Trumpet; the Angel of Death, entrusted with seizing souls; Munkar and Nakir, entrusted with questioning the deceased in the grave; and the angels entrusted with recording what issues from the responsible person, for each one there are two angels, each described as raqib, i.e., watcher, and 'atid, i.e., present, and Malik...
Source pages p. 96

Commentary
This passage continues the series of obligatory beliefs by affirming the obligation to believe in angels in summary. It describes them as subtle, luminous bodies capable of assuming various forms, possessing knowledge and ability for arduous tasks, always obedient, dwelling in the heavens. Their infallibility ('ismah) is defined as Allah not creating sin in them while they retain ability and choice. The commentator cites three Quranic verses as proof, then states that disbelief in angels is kufr. He then lists the angels that must be known in detail: Jibril, Mika'il, Israfil, the Angel of Death, Munkar and Nakir, and the recording angels (raqib and 'atid). The passage ends abruptly with 'Malik' (the guardian of Hell), which is incomplete in this window.

Key Terms
الملائكة
الأملاك
أجسام لطيفة نورانية
العصمة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Angels are purely spiritual beings with no physical form.
Correction: The passage affirms they are 'subtle luminous bodies' (أجسام لطيفة نورانية), meaning they have a refined physical nature, not purely spiritual.

Checkpoint
What is the definition of infallibility ('ismah) for angels according to the passage?
A) They cannot sin because they have no free will.
B) Allah does not create sin in them while they retain ability and choice.
C) They are naturally sinless without any condition.
D) They are always in a state of worship and never err.

Key Takeaway
Belief in angels as subtle, luminous, infallible beings is an obligatory article of faith, supported by clear Quranic verses.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The angels never disobey Allah, yet they have ability and choice. You also have ability and choice. What one area of your life today have you been using your choice to disobey, and what will you commit to align with the angels’ obedience?
?
If you truly believed that angels are recording every deed—including your private thoughts—would you change your behavior right now? What is one hidden sin you need to repent from and start avoiding?
?
The passage defines infallibility as Allah not creating sin in them while their ability and choice remain. Does this challenge the excuses you make for your own sins, blaming circumstances or lack of control? How will you take full responsibility for your choices starting today?

The Angels Known in Detail
Lists specific angels that must be known in detail.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to name and describe the specific angels that must be known in detail: Jibril, Mika'il, Israfil, Angel of Death, Munkar and Nakir, and the recording angels.

Core Idea
Knowing the special duties of each angel deepens our faith.

After affirming belief in angels in general, Islam requires detailed knowledge of certain key angels and their missions. This passage lists the angels whose names and functions are obligatory to know, from Jibril, the bearer of revelation, to the recording angels who note every deed.

Guided Path
1
Read the list of specific angels
Read the Arabic passage and translation. Note that this is a detailed list (tafsil) of angels that must be known individually.
2
Learn the role of each major angel
Identify Jibril (revelation), Mika'il (provision), Israfil (Trumpet), and the Angel of Death (souls). Understand each one's unique responsibility.
These four are well-known archangels.
3
Understand Munkar and Nakir
These two angels are tasked with questioning the deceased in the grave. They are named specifically.
4
Learn about the recording angels
Every person has two angels, described as Raqib (watcher) and 'Atid (present), who record deeds.
5
Notice the incomplete mention of Malik
The passage ends with 'wa Malik', referring to the guardian of Hell. The list is cut off, but we understand its importance.
This indicates the passage is incomplete; the full list likely continues.

They are subtle, luminous bodies, capable of taking various forms, in knowledge and ability to perform arduous tasks; their affair is obedience, and their dwelling is the heavens. How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Right now, two angels are recording your every word and action. What is one thing you will do today to make sure your record is filled with good deeds?
?
If the Angel of Death were to take your soul tonight, would you be satisfied with your last day? What would you change starting now?
?
When Munkar and Nakir ask you 'Who is your Lord? What is your religion? Who is your Prophet?' in the grave, will your answer be firm? What are you neglecting in your faith that you need to strengthen today?

Respecting the Companions: Refraining from Disputes and Affirming Their Justice
Commands refraining from disputes and affirms the uprightness of all Companions.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation to refrain from discussing disputes among the Companions and the Sunni consensus that all Companions are upright (udul).

Core Idea
Honor the Companions by staying silent about their conflicts.

The passage before you addresses a sensitive area of Islamic theology: the conflicts that occurred among the Prophet's Companions. It lays down a clear rule of conduct and a foundational belief. We must refrain from investigating these disputes and must affirm the collective justice of all Sahaba. This teaching is based on the consensus of Ahl al-Sunna, and it protects the integrity of the generations that conveyed the religion to us.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic text once to get a feel for its concise style. Then read the translation alongside it.
Notice the two distinct commands: 'it is obligatory to refrain' and 'know that all Companions are upright'.
2
Identify the two rulings
The passage contains two main points. Identify the obligation to refrain from disputes and the affirmation that all Companions are udul.
The second point is reinforced by a reference to Imam al-Nawawi's agreement.
3
Reflect on the rationale
Think about why it is important to refrain from discussing these disputes. How does affirming the justice of all Companions protect the integrity of Islamic teachings?
The Companions are the transmitters of the Quran and Sunnah; questioning their integrity would undermine religious knowledge.
4
Check your understanding
Answer the checkpoint question to confirm you have grasped the ruling regarding the disputes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب الإمساك عما وقع بين الصحابة من النزاع، ولتعلم أن أصحاب رسول الله صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ كلهم عدول: وَهُم عُدُولٌ كُلُّهُم لَا يَشْتَبِهْ النَّوِوِي أَجمَعَ مَن يُعتَدُّ بِه
And it is obligatory to refrain from what occurred of disputes among the Companions, and know that the Companions of the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, are all upright (udul); they are all upright, there is no doubt, al-Nawawi agreed, from those who are considered.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage has two main points. First, it states the obligation to withhold from investigating the conflicts that happened among the Companions. This is a standard Sunni position—not to discuss the disagreements within the Sahaba in a way that leads to disrespect or criticism. Second, it affirms the integral justice (adala) of all the Companions. It adds that the opinion of Imam al-Nawawi is in agreement with this consensus. The phrase 'they are all udul' means they are all trustworthy in religion and narration, thus their narrations are accepted without questioning their character.

Key Terms
الإمساك
الصحابة
النزاع
عدول
النووي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: It is permissible to criticize the Companions for their mistakes and to investigate the rights and wrongs of their disputes.
Correction: This passage explicitly commands refraining from such investigation and affirms the uprightness of all Companions. Disputing their conflicts or impugning their character is prohibited.

Checkpoint
What is the ruling regarding the disputes that occurred among the Companions?
A) It is obligatory to investigate them
B) It is obligatory to refrain from discussing them
C) It is recommended to know them
D) It is disliked to ignore them

Key Takeaway
It is obligatory to refrain from investigating disputes among the Companions and to affirm that all of them are upright (udul) without exception.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you come across debates about the Companions' conflicts online or among friends, does your heart feel drawn to take sides? Will you now commit to stopping such discussions whenever you can?
?
Do you truly affirm in your heart that every single Companion is upright, even those whose actions you may not understand? Ask Allah to grant you this belief without doubt.
?
If a loved one criticizes a Companion, will you gently remind them of this obligation to refrain and affirm their justice, even if it means a difficult conversation?

Belief in the Houris al-Ayn
Defines Houris al-Ayn and the duty to believe in them.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the nature of the Houris al-Ayn and that belief in their existence is obligatory.

Core Idea
The Houris are real women of Paradise, not symbols.

This lesson explains a key element of Islamic eschatology: the Houris al-Ayn. The passage defines their name and physical description, emphasizing that they are actual creations in Paradise. Understanding this affirms the reality of the unseen.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic sentence describing the Houris. Note the terms 'Hur' and 'Ayn'.
The passage uses precise linguistic roots.
2
Learn the meaning of 'Hur'
The term 'Hur' refers to the intense contrast of white and black in the eyes. This is a key attribute.
3
Learn the meaning of 'Ayn'
The term 'Ayn' indicates wide eyes. Both combine to describe their beauty.
4
Understand the obligation of belief
The passage states it is obligatory (yajib) to believe in their existence. This is a matter of faith.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والحور العين، ويجب الإيمان بوجودهن وهن جمع حوراء، والحور شدة بياض العين مع شدة سوادها وهن نساء الجنة، ووصفهن بالعين لاتساع أعينهن. (والولدان) مما يجب الإيمان بوجودهم، وهم الغلمان، على صورة غلمان الدنيا، وليسوا من أولاد الدنيا الإنس، كما قاله السباعي هم خدمة أهل الجنة. ( ثم) (الأولياء) أي: يجب الإيمان بهم، جمع ولي، وهو القائم بحقوق الله تعالى، وحقوق العباد حسب الطاقة، كراماتهم، كما جاء بذلك الكتاب والسنة وأجمعت عليه الأمة.
And the Houris al-Ayn, belief in their existence is obligatory, and they are the plural of Hawra'. Al-Hur means the intense whiteness of the eye with intense blackness, and they are the women of Paradise. They are described as (al-Ayn) due to the wide opening of their eyes. (And the Wildan) are from what belief in their existence is obligatory, and they are the boys, in the form of the boys of this world, and they are not from the human children of this world, as al-Siba'i said: they are the servants of the people of Paradise. (Then) (the Awliya), i.e., belief in them is obligatory, they are the plural of Wali, and he is the one who fulfills the rights of Allah and the rights of the servants according to capacity. Their miracles (karamat) are as reported in the Book and the Sunna and the consensus of the community.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage covers three distinct aspects of Islamic eschatology and sainthood. First, the Houris al-Ayn: belief in their existence is obligatory. The term 'Hur' refers to the intense contrast of white and black in their eyes, and 'Ayn' indicates wide eyes. They are the women of Paradise created for the believers. Second, the Wildan: these are young boys created as servants in Paradise. They are not the children of humans from this world but are created directly for service, as noted by the commentator al-Siba'i. Third, the Awliya: these are the saints, individuals who fulfill the rights of Allah and of people to the best of their ability. It is obligatory to believe in them, meaning to believe in their existence and that they can be granted miraculous favors (karamat) from Allah, as attested by the Quran, Sunnah, and scholarly consensus.

Key Terms
الحور العين
حوراء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe the Houris are mere symbols or metaphors rather than real creations.
Correction: This passage affirms they are real women of Paradise, and belief in them is obligatory.

Checkpoint
What does the term 'Hur' refer to in the description of the Houris?
A) The whiteness of their skin
B) The intense whiteness of the eye with intense blackness
C) Their wide eyes
D) Their beauty

Key Takeaway
The Houris are real, physical beings in Paradise, and believing in them is part of Islamic faith.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read that the Houris are real women of Paradise with specific physical features, does your heart yearn for that reward? Or do you feel indifferent? What does your reaction reveal about the strength of your faith in the unseen?
?
The source says belief in their existence is obligatory. Have you ever consciously affirmed this belief, or do you take it for granted? What concrete step will you take today to cultivate a deeper conviction in the realities of Paradise?
?
If you were to meet a Houri based on your current deeds, would you feel joyful or ashamed? How does that thought motivate you to change your actions starting now?

Belief in the Awliya and Their Miracles
Defines Awliya and affirms the reality of their miracles.

Saints exist, and their miracles are divinely sanctioned.

The third part of the passage addresses the Awliya (saints) and the reality of their miracles. It defines a wali and asserts that their karamat are confirmed by the Quran, Sunnah, and consensus. This lesson grounds the belief in saints and their supernatural events.

Belief in Everything the Prophet Brought
States the obligation to believe in all that the Prophet informed about, which becomes like necessary knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation to believe in every ruling brought by the Prophet, and how these rulings become as certain as necessary knowledge.

Core Idea
Everything the Prophet brought is as certain as necessary knowledge.

This passage concludes the discussion on the samiyyat by emphasizing that all the Prophet's teachings must be accepted. The author explains that the rulings transmitted from the Prophet, who is the bearer of good news and warning, become so well-established that they are like necessary knowledge. We will explore how this principle solidifies the certainty of revealed knowledge.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the literal translation to grasp the meaning.
Notice the grammatical coordination with 'by the reckoning'.
2
Understand the obligation
Realize that 'each (kull)' refers to every ruling brought by the Prophet, and belief in it is obligatory.
3
Grasp the concept of 'necessary knowledge'
The phrase 'sara kal-daruri' means these rulings become as evident as necessary knowledge—certain and undeniable.
This is a key epistemological claim in kalam.
4
Reflect on the implication
Ask yourself: What does this say about the authority of the Prophet's reports?
It elevates them to the highest level of certainty.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وكل بالجر عطف على قوله بالحساب أي يجب الإيمان بكل ما جاء ونقل عن النبي البشير أي البشير لمن أطاعه بالجنة والنذير لمن عصاه بالنار من كل حكم صار كالضروري.
And 'each' (wa kull) in genitive, coordinated with His saying 'by the reckoning', meaning: it is obligatory to believe in everything that came and was transmitted from the Prophet, the bringer of good news (al-bashir) – i.e., the bringer of good news for those who obey him of Paradise, and the warner for those who disobey him of Hell – of every ruling that became like the necessary (al-daruri).
Source pages p. 100

Commentary
This passage interprets the final line of the poem: 'And everything that came from the bringer of good news / of every ruling became like the necessary.' It explains that 'each' (kull) is in the genitive case as a coordinate to 'by the reckoning' (bi al-hisab) from earlier in the poem, meaning that just as belief in the reckoning is obligatory, so is belief in every ruling brought by the Prophet. The Prophet is described as al-bashir (the bringer of glad tidings) for the obedient and al-nadhir (the warner) for the disobedient. The phrase 'became like the necessary' (sara kal-daruri) means that these rulings are so well-established that they become as evident as necessary knowledge, i.e., there is no room for doubt. This emphasizes that all the beliefs derived from the Prophet's teachings are certain and must be accepted without hesitation.

Key Terms
البشير
النذير
الضروري
الإيمان بكل ما جاء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Only some of the Prophet's reports are necessary
Correction: All reports from the Prophet become as certain as necessary knowledge.

Checkpoint
What is meant by the phrase 'sara kal-daruri' (became like the necessary)?
A) The rulings are optional
B) The rulings are so established they are certain like necessary knowledge
C) The rulings are required only for some people
D) The rulings are from the Prophet only

Key Takeaway
All the Prophet's teachings are as certain as necessary knowledge and must be believed without hesitation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you treat every teaching of the Prophet with the same certainty as you treat the fact that fire burns? Reflect on one sunnah you have been neglecting and ask yourself: does that negligence reflect a lack of conviction in its truth?
?
If you truly believed the Prophet's warnings are as certain as necessary knowledge, how would your daily choices change regarding sins you consider minor?
?
The passage says the Prophet is both a bringer of good news and a warner. Which of these roles do you pay more attention to in your life, and what does that say about your heart's condition?

Pages p. 100 · Unseen Matters
← Prev
Mark Done

← Prev
p. 100
Module 12 · Lesson 42
