The Ash'ari Response to the Mu'tazili Dilemma
The author narrates a debate between Abdul Jabbar and Abu Ishaq on divine will.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Ash'ari response to the Mu'tazili objection that if God wills all actions, He would be responsible for evil, and the key distinction between what belongs to the servant and what belongs to God.

Core Idea
God's will does not make Him responsible for evil; what is His is His favor, not a right.

This lesson presents a famous debate that captures the core difference between Mu'tazili and Ash'ari views on divine will and human responsibility. As you read the back-and-forth, notice how the Ash'ari scholar uses a precise distinction to refute the Mu'tazili's dilemma. The passage demonstrates how theological reasoning defends divine transcendence while affirming human accountability.

Guided Path
1
Read the debate carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it, then read the translation. Pay attention to the rhythm of the exchange between the two scholars.
2
Identify the turning point
Notice where the Mu'tazili poses his dilemma about God preventing guidance and decreeing perdition. This is the critical challenge.
The Ash'ari's answer hinges on the distinction between what is 'yours' and what is 'His.'
3
Understand the key distinction
Reflect on why the Ash'ari's distinction refutes the Mu'tazili. If guidance is God's favor, not a right, then withholding it is not injustice.
This distinction is central to the Ash'ari doctrine of acquisition (kasb).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
لطيفة يحكى أن عبد الجبار المعتزلي قال للأستاذ أبي إسحاق الإسفرايني، من كبار أئمة أهل السنة والجماعة، مُعَرَّضًا له: «سبحان من تنزه عن الفحشاء». فتفطن الأستاذ وقال: سبحان من لا يقع في ملكه إلا ما يشاء». فقال عبد الجبار: «يريد ربنا أن يعصى». فقال الأستاذ: «أيعصى ربنا قهرا». فقال عبد الجبار: أريت إن منعني الهدى، وقضى عليّ بالرّدى أحسن إلى أم أساء؟». فقال الأستاذ: إن منعك ما هو لك فقد أساء، وأن منعك ما هو له فذاك فضله يؤتيه من يشاء»، فبهت عبد الجبار. وقال الحاضرون: «والله ما لهذا جواب». ويقال: وقعت هذه المباحثة بين بدعي وبين سيدنا الإمام علي ابن الحسين رضي اللَّهُ عَنْهُ، فانصرف الرجل وهو يقول: الله أعلم حيث يجعل رسالته.
A subtle point: It is narrated that Abdul Jabbar al-Mu'tazili said to the master Abu Ishaq al-Isfarayini, one of the great imams of Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama'ah, alluding to him: 'Glory be to Him who is exalted above lewdness.' So the master perceived and said: 'Glory be to Him in whose dominion nothing occurs except what He wills.' Abdul Jabbar said: 'Our Lord wants to be disobeyed.' The master said: 'Is our Lord disobeyed by force?' Abdul Jabbar said: 'What do you think? If He prevented me from guidance and decreed for me perdition, did He do good or evil?' The master said: 'If He prevented you from what is rightfully yours, then He did evil; but if He prevented you from what is His, then that is His favor which He gives to whom He wills.' So Abdul Jabbar was confounded. Those present said: 'By God, there is no answer to this.' It is also said that this debate took place between an innovator and our master Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn, may God be pleased with him, and the man left saying: 'God knows best where to place His message.'
Source pages p. 62

Commentary
This passage is a theological debate in objection-and-answer form. The Mu'tazili begins by implying that the Ash'ari view (that God creates all actions, including evil) contradicts divine transcendence. The Ash'ari replies that whatever occurs in God's dominion is by His will, affirming sovereignty. The Mu'tazili objects that this means God wills disobedience. The Ash'ari retorts: 'Is God disobeyed by force?' implying human acquisition of acts. Then the Mu'tazili poses a dilemma: if God prevents guidance and decrees perdition, is He good or evil? The Ash'ari distinguishes: what is rightfully due to the servant (guidance) is a right; preventing it would be evil. But guidance is not a right; it is God's favor. So preventing what is His is not evil. This distinction destroys the Mu'tazili argument.

Key Terms
تنزه
فحشاء
ملك
مشيئة
قهر
هدى
ردى
إحسان
إساءة
فضل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: If God wills all actions, He is responsible for evil and cannot be just.
Correction: The Ash'ari response shows that not all things are due to the servant; guidance is God's favor, not a right, so withholding it is not injustice.

Checkpoint
What was the key distinction made by Abu Ishaq al-Isfarayini in response to Abdul Jabbar's question about God preventing guidance?
A) Between guidance and misguidance
B) Between what belongs to the servant and what belongs to God
C) Between good and evil deeds
D) Between will and command

Key Takeaway
The Ash'ari distinction between what belongs to the servant by right and what is God's favor resolves the dilemma of divine will and evil.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you fall short in obedience, do you ever quietly resent God for not giving you more guidance or strength? How does Abu Ishaq's distinction between rights and favor challenge that resentment?
?
Is there a specific area of your life where you feel God has 'prevented' something you think you deserved? Write down what that is, then honestly ask: was that thing ever truly yours by right, or was it always a gift?
?
If you truly believed that every moment of guidance is a pure favor (فضل) that God could withhold without injustice, how would your prayer for guidance change tonight?

The Three Groups that Are the Elite of the Ummah
The author classifies the three groups that constitute the elite of the Ummah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three groups that constitute the elite of the Muhammadan Ummah according to the author.

Core Idea
The elite of the Ummah includes scholars of fiqh, kalam, and tasawwuf.

In this passage, the author identifies the three groups that represent the highest levels of the Ummah. He begins with the four Imams of jurisprudence, then moves to the theologians who safeguarded correct beliefs, and finally the spiritual masters who practiced what they taught. This classification shows the unity of fiqh, aqida, and tasawwuf.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that the author uses a repetitive structure: 'wa firqa... wa firqa...'
2
Identify the three groups
List the three groups mentioned. What is each group's role? The first group are the four Imams of fiqh. The second group are the theologians (mutakallimun) like al-Ash'ari and al-Maturidi. The third group are the spiritual masters (like al-Junayd) who focus on works and mujahada.
3
Understand the relationship
Notice that the third group follows the first two groups in belief and practice. They are not separate but integrated. The author says they act 'according to what the two preceding groups held'.
This shows the harmony between fiqh, aqida, and tasawwuf.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
The author concludes that these three groups are the elite (khawass) and masters of the Ummah. Think about why he emphasizes this. What does it tell us about the unity of Islamic scholarship?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
منها من المذاهب المرضية إلى عصرنا هذا هم الأئمة الأربعة؛ الإمام أبو حنيفة، والإمام مالك والامام الشافعي، والامام أحمد ابن حنبل، رحمهم الله ورضي عنهم. وفرقة نصبت نفسها للاشتغال ببيان العقائد الصحيحة، التي كان عليها السلف الصالح، وهو الامام أبو الحسن، والامام أبو منصور، ومن تبعهما. وفرقة نصبت نفسها للاشتغال بالعمل والمجاهدات طبقا لما ذهبت إليه الفرقتان المتقدمتان، وهم كثيرون، منهم الامام أبو القاسم الجنيد. فهؤلاء الفرق الثلاثة هم خواص الأمة المحمدية وساداتها، رضي الله عنهم أجمعين.
Among them are from the approved schools up to our time: the four Imams: Imam Abu Hanifa, Imam Malik, Imam al-Shafi'i, and Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, may Allah have mercy on them and be pleased with them. And a group that devoted itself to the exposition of correct beliefs, which the righteous predecessors were upon, and they are Imam Abu al-Hasan (al-Ash'ari), Imam Abu Mansur (al-Maturidi), and those who followed them. And a group that devoted itself to works and spiritual struggles according to what the two preceding groups held, and they are many, among them Imam Abu al-Qasim al-Junayd. So these three groups are the elite of the Muhammadan Ummah and its masters, may Allah be pleased with them all.
Source pages p. 114

Commentary
This passage classifies the elite of the Ummah into three groups. The first group are the four Imams of the approved schools of jurisprudence. The second group are those who devoted themselves to clarifying correct beliefs (aqa'id) according to the way of the righteous predecessors, exemplified by Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari and Imam Abu Mansur al-Maturidi and their followers. The third group are those who devoted themselves to works and spiritual struggles (mujahadat) in accordance with the two preceding groups, represented by Imam Abu al-Qasim al-Junayd and many others. The author concludes that these three groups are the elite (khawass) and masters of the Muhammadan Ummah.

خواص الأمة
ساداتها

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Only the four Imams (first group) are considered the elite of the Ummah.
Correction: The author includes two other groups: the theologians (Ash'ari and Maturidi) and the spiritual masters (like al-Junayd), showing that the elite comprises scholars of fiqh, aqida, and tasawwuf.

Checkpoint

Key Takeaway
The elite of the Ummah consists of three integrated groups: the jurists (four Imams), the theologians (Ash'ari and Maturidi), and the spiritual masters (like al-Junayd), together representing the unity of Islamic knowledge and practice.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

All three groups are united under the banner of the elite. Do you tend to prioritize one over the others? How can you cultivate a balanced approach that honors jurisprudence, theology, and spirituality in your daily life, and what is the first action you will take?

Defining the Intellect: A Spiritual Faculty
The commentator defines 'aql as a spiritual faculty with its loci and development.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how classical kalam defines the intellect as a spiritual faculty with specific loci and development stages.

Core Idea
The intellect is a spiritual secret, not merely the brain.

The commentator begins by defining one of the most important tools in kalam: the intellect ('aql). Without a clear understanding of what the intellect is, the subsequent arguments about knowledge of Allah would be confused. This passage gives a precise definition, locating the intellect both in the heart and the brain, and describing its origin and perfection.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the sequence: what the intellect is, what it does, where it resides, when it begins, when it becomes perfect.
The definition is compact but rich.
2
Identify the key components
Look at each part: the nature (spiritual secret), the function (perceiving sciences), the locus (heart) and light (brain), the beginning (spirit blown), and first perfection (puberty).
3
Reflect on why this matters
Consider why the commentator emphasizes that the intellect is spiritual and not purely physical. This shapes how we understand reason in Islamic theology.
This connects to broader kalam debates.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(۱) العقل: سر روحاني، تدرك به النفس العلوم الضرورية والنظرية، ومحله القلب، ونوره في الدماغ، وابتداؤه من حين نفخ الروح في الجنين، وأول كماله عند البلوغ. انظر شرح الخريدة» (ص١٤٤).
(1) The intellect: a spiritual secret, by which the soul perceives necessary and theoretical sciences; its locus is the heart, and its light is in the brain; its beginning is from the time the spirit is blown into the fetus, and its first perfection is at puberty. See Sharh al-Kharida (p. 144).
Source pages p. 33

Commentary
This passage defines the term 'aql (intellect). It is described as a spiritual secret (sirr ruhani) by which the soul perceives necessary and theoretical sciences. Its locus is the heart, its light in the brain, and it begins at the blowing of the spirit into the fetus, reaching its first perfection at puberty. This definition clarifies that 'aql is not merely the brain but a spiritual faculty.

Key Terms
العقل
سر روحاني

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The intellect is identical to the brain and its functions.
Correction: The definition clarifies that the intellect is a spiritual faculty with its locus in the heart and its light in the brain.

Checkpoint

A) The brain
B) The heart
C) The soul
D) The spirit

Key Takeaway
The intellect is a spiritual faculty, located in the heart, that enables the soul to know, beginning at ensoulment and perfecting at puberty.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Your intellect is described as a spiritual secret, not just your brain. When you reason about your faith, do you experience it as a spiritual act or a mere mental exercise? Will you commit to approaching every act of thinking as an act of worship, seeking light from your heart?
?
The intellect begins at ensoulment and perfects at puberty, when you became responsible before Allah. Have you ever felt the weight of that responsibility? Are you using the gift of your intellect to know what Allah has made necessary and to ponder His creation, or are you neglecting it?
?
The locus of the intellect is the heart. Is your knowledge alive in your heart, or does it remain only in your mind? What would change if you truly believed that understanding is a matter of the heart? Will you ask Allah to make your intellect a light that transforms your inner self?

Logic and Technical Terms

Distinction between Milla, Sharia, and Din
Author explains the subtle differences among the terms milla, sharia, and din.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the terms milla, sharia, and din are technically distinguished based on different aspects, despite being used synonymously in common speech.

Core Idea
Three key Islamic terms differ by the aspect of divine command they emphasize.

This lesson explores a subtle terminological clarification from the author. While dictionaries may treat milla, sharia, and din as synonyms, the author points out that each term highlights a distinct facet of divine legislation: transmission, worship, or legislation itself. Understanding this nuance deepens one's grasp of Islamic legal and theological vocabulary.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing; then read the translation. Let the author's rhythm come through before you interpret.
2
Identify the three aspects
Notice the three perspectives: rulings dictated (milla), worship (din), legislation (sharia). See how each term is defined.
3
Reflect on the distinction
Consider why such terminological precision matters in Islamic scholarship, especially in kalam and usul.
This connects to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وكلام صاحب القاموس يشير إلى ترادف الملة والشريعة والدين حيث قال: «والملة بالكسر: الشريعة أو الدين...» إلى آخره. وهي في الحقيقة تختلف بالحيثيات، فمن حيث إن الأحكام الشرعية تُملى لتنقل فهي ملة، ومن حيث إنها يُتديّن بها أي يُتعبد بها فهي دين، ومن حيث إنها شُرعت أي بَيَّنها الشارع فهي شريعة.
And the statement of the author of al-Qamus indicates the synonymy of milla, sharia, and din where he said: 'Milla (with kasra): sharia or din...' etc. In reality they differ according to aspects: from the perspective that the legal rulings are dictated to be transmitted, it is milla; from the perspective that one practices religion through it, i.e., worships through it, it is din; and from the perspective that it is legislated, i.e., the Lawgiver explained it, it is sharia.
Source pages p. 52

Commentary
This passage is a terminological clarification. The author begins by citing the dictionary authority of al-Qamus, which states that milla, sharia, and din are synonymous. However, he then explains that in fact they are distinct when considered from different aspects. He gives three aspects: milla relates to the transmission of rulings; din relates to worship and religious practice; sharia relates to the act of legislation by the Lawgiver. The passage thus defines three key terms of Islamic law and theology, showing that although they may be used interchangeably in common speech, technical usage distinguishes them based on the specific relationship between the divine command and the human response.

Key Terms
الملة
الشريعة
الدين
الحيثيات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think these three terms are identical in all contexts.
Correction: The author shows they differ according to the aspect considered.

Checkpoint
According to the author, from which perspective is the term 'milla' used?
A) From the perspective of worship
B) From the perspective that rulings are dictated for transmission
C) From the perspective of legislation
D) From the perspective of divine attributes

Key Takeaway
Milla, sharia, and din are technically distinct terms emphasizing transmission, worship, and legislation respectively.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you describe yourself as a Muslim, do you unconsciously emphasize the transmitted rulings (milla) over the personal worship (din) or the divine origin (sharia)? Which aspect are you most neglecting, and what one change can you make this week to restore balance?
?
This lesson teaches that the same divine command can be viewed from three angles. Are you treating your religious practice as merely a set of rules to follow (milla) without engaging your heart in worship (din) or recognizing the authority of the Lawgiver (sharia)? Commit to a specific action that acknowledges all three aspects today.
?
You have learned that din emphasizes worship and personal devotion. Is your din alive with genuine worship, or has it become a routine of transmitted customs? What would it take for you to experience the ‘dīn’ aspect of your prayers this week?

Divine Decree
beginner
5 min
The Four Categories of Command and Will
Mashat enumerates the four categories of things based on command and will.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the four logical categories of created things based on divine command and will.

Core Idea
Divine command and divine will are not identical; they produce four distinct categories.

In this passage, Mashat presents a key doctrinal classification that distinguishes between what Allah commands and what He wills. By examining four categories and their examples, we see that some things are commanded but not willed, and vice versa. This distinction is central to Sunni theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation to get familiar with the four categories.
2
Identify the categories
Find and list the four categories as described by Mashat. Notice the pattern: commanded/willed combinations.
3
Understand the examples
Consider the examples given: Abu Bakr's faith, disbelief from Abu Bakr? faith from Abu Jahl, disbelief from Abu Jahl. Clarify the second category's example.
The second category may be ambiguous; check commentary.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فقد علمت من قول الناظم رحمه الله تعالى وقدس سره: أقسام الكائنات وكل شيء كائن... إلى آخره، أربعا أقساما في الكائنات منطوقا الإرادة والأمر ومفهوما: الأول: مأمور به ومراد، كإيمان أبي بكر رضي الله عنه. الثاني: عكسه، كالكفر منه. الثالث: مأمور به غير مراد، كالإيمان من أبي جهل. الرابع: عكسه، ككفره. فاحفظ هذا المقام على الوجه المتقدم، فإنه مذهب أهل السنة والجماعة، وقد زلت فيه أقدام المعتزلة.
You have known from the statement of the author, may God have mercy on him and sanctify his secret, 'The categories of existing things and every thing that exists... etc.,' four categories among existing things by explicit mention of will and command and by implication: First: commanded and willed, like the faith of Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him. Second: its opposite, like disbelief from him. Third: commanded but not willed, like faith from Abu Jahl. Fourth: its opposite, like his disbelief. So preserve this station in the aforementioned manner, for it is the doctrine of Ahl al-Sunna wa al-Jama'a, and the feet of the Mu'tazila have slipped in it.
Source pages p. 60

Commentary
This passage enumerates the four logical categories of things in relation to divine command (amr) and will (irada). The author derives these from the poem. The first category is what is both commanded and willed, exemplified by the faith of Abu Bakr. The second is described as its opposite, with the example of disbelief from him (Abu Jahl? unclear). The third is commanded but not willed, such as the faith that would come from Abu Jahl (i.e., Allah commands faith from him but does not will it). The fourth is the opposite of the third, i.e., not commanded but willed, exemplified by the disbelief of Abu Jahl. The author then emphasizes that this classification is the doctrine of Ahl al-Sunna and that the Mu'tazila erred by conflating command and will.

Key Terms
مأمور به
مراد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that whatever occurs must be both commanded and willed by Allah.
Correction: The classification shows that some things occur that are not commanded (like disbelief) but are willed, and some things are commanded but not willed (like faith from Abu Jahl).

Checkpoint
Which category does the faith of Abu Bakr belong to?
A) Commanded and willed
B) Commanded but not willed
C) Not commanded but willed
D) Neither commanded nor willed

Key Takeaway
Allah's command and will are distinct, creating four logical possibilities that apply to human actions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you realize that Allah commanded faith from Abu Jahl but did not will it for him, how does that shape your gratitude that He both commands and wills faith for you? What are you doing today to live in a way that honors that gift?
?
If some actions are willed by Allah but not commanded—like the disbelief of Abu Jahl—does that challenge your view of people who reject faith? How does this truth help you trust Allah's wisdom when you see evil in the world?
?
Since your faith is both commanded and willed by Allah, what specific habit or attitude would you need to change tomorrow to show you truly believe He has given you this grace for a purpose?

The Sunni Stance on Command and Will
Mashat affirms the classification as Sunni doctrine and criticizes Mu'tazila deviation.

Learning Objective

The Mu'tazila conflate command and will; Sunni theology distinguishes them.

Having learned the four categories, Mashat now emphasizes their doctrinal importance. He declares that this classification is the madhhab of Ahl al-Sunna wa al-Jama'a, and warns that the Mu'tazila have slipped in this matter. This lesson explores the sectarian significance of the distinction.

Guided Path

The third is commanded but not willed, such as the faith that would come from Abu Jahl (i.e., Allah commands faith from him but does not will it). Divine Decree
intermediate
4 min
Refutation of the Jabriyya on Divine Will
The author refutes the Jabriyya by showing their view invalidates Sharia commands.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the Jabriyya position that all human actions are directly from God's will leads to the invalidation of religious obligations.

Core Idea
Attributing all actions to God destroys the basis for commands.

In this passage, the author attacks the Jabriyya sect, who claimed that humans have no real agency and all actions are created by God. He shows that this view, far from exalting God, actually renders the entire Sharia meaningless. Let's read the passage to see how he makes this argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the structure: first critique, then their statement, then the consequence.
2
Identify the Jabriyya claim
Find the part where they refer actions to Creatorhood and claim compulsion. Understand their reasoning.
They say: 'We are a people compelled by His decree.'
3
Note the logical consequence
The author argues that if actions are compelled, then commands and prohibitions become invalid. Why? Because they would be commanding what is already decreed.
This is a reductio ad absurdum.
4
Reflect on the refutation
Think about why this argument is effective. It shows the Jabriyya position self-destructs by making Sharia meaningless.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
بجهلهم هذا أن في ذلك تنزيها لله تعالى. وأما الذين تمسكوا بالإرادة والمشيئة، فأحالوا فعلهم وعملهم على الخالقية، وقطعوا نطاق العبودية، وتبرئوا من أعمالهم، وقالوا: نحن قوم مجبورون بحكمه في قبضة قهره ، ويلزمهم على هذا إبطال الأوامر والنواهي وسائر التكاليف الشرعية». اهـ. أعاذنا الله منه بمنه وكرمه آمين.
In their ignorance of this, that in that there is an exoneration of God Almighty. As for those who clung to will and volition, they referred their action and deed to Creatorhood, cut off the realm of servitude, absolved themselves of their deeds, and said: 'We are a people compelled by His decree in the grasp of His coercion.' And it is necessary for them on this to invalidate commands and prohibitions and all other religious obligations. End. May God protect us from it by His grace and generosity, Amen.
Source pages p. 62

Commentary
This passage is a refutation. The author begins by stating that those who attribute everything to God's will are ignorant of the fact that this position actually exonerates God (by wrongly absolving humans). He then describes their position: they refer all actions to God's creating, deny human agency, and claim compulsion. Then he points out the necessary consequence: if humans have no free will, then commands, prohibitions, and all sharia obligations become invalid (because they would be commanding what is already decreed). This is a reductio ad absurdum of the Jabriyya. The author ends with a prayer for protection.

Key Terms
إرادة
مشيئة
خالقية
عبودية
جبر
أوامر
نواهي
تكاليف شرعية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think attributing all actions to God exalts Him and is a form of tawhid.
Correction: Actually, it voids accountability and makes Sharia commands meaningless, as the author argues.

Checkpoint
What is the necessary consequence of the Jabriyya position that all human actions are directly from God's will?
A) It affirms divine justice
B) It nullifies commands and prohibitions
C) It increases servitude
D) It exonerates humans

Key Takeaway
The Jabriyya belief that all actions are compelled by God necessarily makes religious obligations meaningless.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sin, do you ever catch yourself thinking, 'It was written for me' or 'It was God's will' as a way to avoid taking responsibility? How does this lesson expose that as a form of Jabriyya? Will you repent from using qadar as an excuse and instead own your actions before Allah?
?
Consider one area of your life where you feel powerless to obey a clear command of Allah—perhaps a habit you claim you cannot break. How does the argument in this passage challenge your excuse? What step will you take today to reclaim your servitude and act as if your choice matters?
?
The Jabriyya 'cut off the realm of servitude' by denying their role in actions. In what specific relationship (with Allah, family, or community) have you subtly cut off responsibility by blaming circumstances or others? What would it look like to fully re-enter the 'realm of servitude' starting tomorrow morning?

The Bedouin's Refutation of the Qadari Prayer
Story of a Bedouin refuting a Qadari's prayer, followed by a couplet affirming God's absolute will.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the story of the Bedouin and the Qadari illustrates the logical necessity of God's absolute will, and that whatever He wills occurs.

Core Idea
God's will is absolute: whatever He wills happens, no exceptions.

This lesson uses a vivid historical anecdote to expose a flaw in the Qadari view that God's will does not encompass evil. Through a simple dialogue, the Bedouin shows that the Qadari's own prayer contradicts itself, paving the way for the Ash'ari affirmation of God's universal will.

Guided Path
1
Read the story carefully
Read the Arabic passage once to understand the narrative. Then read the translation to grasp the dialogue between the Bedouin and Amr ibn Ubayd.
Notice how the Bedouin immediately spots the logical problem.
2
Identify the contradiction
Analyze the Bedouin's reasoning: if God did not will the theft, the theft occurred against His will, which is impossible. If He wills its return, it must return, so the prayer is futile.
The contradiction lies in the premise that God's will can be thwarted.
3
Connect to the couplet
Read the couplet from al-Ida'ah and see how it affirms the Ash'ari doctrine: whatever God wills occurs, no one can prevent it, and He does as He wills.
This resolves the contradiction by affirming God's absolute will.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويحكي أن أعرابيا سُرقت ناقته، فجئ به إلى عمرو بن عبيد القدري ليدعو له، فرفع يديه وقال: «اللهم إن ناقة هذا الأعرابي سُرقت ولم ترد سرقتها فَرُدّها عليه، فقال الأعرابي: بالله عليك يا شيخ كف عني من دعائك هذا»، قال: «ولم؟»، قال: «لأنه إذا لم يُرد سرقتها، وقد سرقت فيريدُ رَدّها ولا ترد». قال في «الإضاءة» (۱): وَكُلَّمَا أَرَادَ فَهُوَ كَائِنُ وَإِن نَهَى عَنْهُ وَأَخطَا المَائِنُ وَلَيسَ عَمَّ شَاءَهُ مَحِيدُ لأَنَّهُ يَفعَلُ مَا يُرِيدُ
And it is narrated that an Arab's camel was stolen, so he was brought to Amr ibn Ubayd al-Qadari to pray for him. He raised his hands and said: 'O God, this Arab's camel was stolen, and You did not will its theft, so return it to him.' The Arab said: 'By God, O shaykh, spare me from this prayer of yours.' He said: 'Why?' He said: 'Because if He did not will its theft, and it was stolen, then He wills its return but it will not return.' He said in al-Ida'ah: 'And whatever He wills, it occurs, even if He forbids it and the disparager errs. And there is no escape from what He wills, because He does what He wills.'
Source pages p. 63

Commentary
This passage is a story illustrating a logical refutation of the Qadari position. Amr ibn Ubayd al-Qadari prays that God return the stolen camel on the condition that God did not will the theft. The Bedouin immediately points out the contradiction: if God did not will the theft, then the theft occurred against His will, which is impossible; and if God wills its return, it must return, so the prayer is futile. The author then quotes a couplet from al-Ida'ah that encapsulates the Ash'ari doctrine: whatever God wills happens; no one can prevent it; He does as He wills.

Key Terms
قدر
إرادة
رد
سرقة
مائن
محيد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some people think that God's will only applies to good actions, not evil.
Correction: The couplet asserts that whatever God wills occurs, even if He forbids it. God's will encompasses all events, good and evil, though He may command good and forbid evil.

Checkpoint
Why did the Arab reject Amr ibn Ubayd's prayer?
A) Because Amr was a heretic
B) Because the prayer implied a contradiction in God's will
C) Because the camel was not important
D) Because Amr did not raise his hands properly

Key Takeaway
The story demonstrates that God's will is all-encompassing and cannot be limited by human distinctions of good and evil.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The Bedouin saw the logical inconsistency in Amr's prayer. Is there any area of your life where you act as if God's will is limited, praying as if your circumstances happened without God's permission? What would change if you truly believed that everything—including your failures and sins—occurred only by His will? Are you willing to submit to that, even when it's uncomfortable?
?
The couplet says: 'And whatever He wills, it occurs, even if He forbids it.' Does this truth make you fearful or comforted? Reflect on a recent sin you committed. Could you have committed it if God had not willed it? Does this deepen your humility before Him? Commit to asking God for forgiveness while acknowledging His sovereign will.
?
The Bedouin bluntly told the scholar to spare him from his prayer. Are there prayers you make that unconsciously assume God is limited or that your circumstances are outside His control? What would it look like to pray with full confidence that God's will is already at work in every detail of your life?

God's Grace and Justice: A Debate on Divine Will
Story of a debate between Abd al-Jabbar and an Imam on God's justice and will.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the distinction between what is owed to creatures and what is purely a gift from God, and how this distinction resolves questions about divine justice.

Core Idea
God's withholding of His grace is not injustice, for He owes nothing to anyone.

This passage recounts a powerful theological exchange that cuts to the heart of debates about divine will and justice. A questioner challenges whether God can be considered good if He withholds guidance and decrees destruction. The answer, simple yet profound, distinguishes between rights and gifts—a distinction that silences the objection.

Guided Path
1
Read the debate narrative
Read the Arabic and translation once to understand the flow of the argument. Notice the question, the answer, and the reaction of the audience.
2
Identify the key distinction
The Imam's response hinges on a distinction: withholding what belongs to another is evil, but withholding what is one's own is a grace. Identify this pivot in the text.
This distinction is the core of the argument.
3
Reflect on the implication
Consider why this distinction refutes the objection that God would be unjust. Since God is the owner of all things, He is not obligated to give guidance to anyone.
Grace is a favor, not a debt.
4
Consider the historical attribution
Note the variant that attributes this debate to Imam Ali ibn Husayn. Reflect on how this argument has been used in Islamic theological discourse.
The story shows the strength of the argument.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فقال عبد الجبار: أريت إن منعني الهدى، وقضى عليّ بالرّدى أحسن إلى أم أساء؟». فقال الأستاذ: إن منعك ما هو لك فقد أساء، وأن منعك ما هو له فذاك فضله يؤتيه من يشاء»، فبهت عبد الجبار. وقال الحاضرون: «والله ما لهذا جواب». ويقال: وقعت هذه المباحثة بين بدعي وبين سيدنا الإمام علي ابن الحسين رضي اللَّهُ عَنْهُ، فانصرف الرجل وهو يقول: الله أعلم حيث يجعل رسالته.
Then Abd al-Jabbar said: 'Did you see that if He prevents guidance for me and decrees destruction upon me, does He do good or evil?' The teacher said: 'If He prevents what is yours, then He has done evil; but if He prevents what is His, then that is His grace; He gives it to whom He wills.' So Abd al-Jabbar was dumbfounded. The attendees said: 'By God, there is no answer to this.' It is said this debate occurred between a heretic and our master Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn (may God be pleased with him), and the man left saying: 'God knows best where to place His message.'
Source pages p. 63

Commentary
This passage is a narrative illustrating a theological argument about divine will and justice. It presents a debate where Abd al-Jabbar asks whether God, if He withholds guidance and decrees destruction, is acting well or badly. The Imam (al-ustadh) responds by distinguishing between withholding what belongs to another (which would be evil) and withholding what is one's own (which is a grace). Abd al-Jabbar is silenced, and the audience acknowledges the force of the argument. The passage concludes with a variant reporting that the debate was with Imam Ali ibn Husayn, and the opponent departed acknowledging that God knows best where to place His message.

Key Terms
الهدى
الرَّدى
فضل
إساءة
إحسان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: If God withholds guidance from someone, He is being unjust.
Correction: God is not obligated to give His grace; it is purely a gift. Withholding what is not owed is not injustice.

Checkpoint
According to the Imam's response, what is the difference between God's withholding of guidance and an act of injustice?
A) God never withholds guidance from anyone.
B) If the guidance were due to the person, withholding it would be evil; if it is God's own, it is a grace.
C) God's actions are neither good nor evil.
D) Both are acts of justice.

Key Takeaway
God's actions are just because He owes nothing to any creature; His grace is a free gift.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever felt entitled to God's guidance, as if He owes it to you? How does the Imam's response—that God withholds what is His, not what is yours— challenge that feeling of entitlement? What would it mean to truly accept that guidance is a grace you do not deserve?
?
The heretic left saying, 'God knows best where to place His message.' Are you genuinely humble about your own faith, or do you secretly think you are more deserving of guidance than others? Ask yourself: if God had not guided you, would you still believe He is just?
?
If God owes you absolutely nothing, how would your prayer life change? Would you still pray for guidance, or would you give up? Identify one area in your life where you act as if God owes you something, and commit to surrendering that expectation today.

The Stolen Camel: A Lesson on Divine Will
Anecdote of an Arab correcting Amr ibn Ubayd's prayer about divine will and theft.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the logical inconsistency in the Qadari view that some events occur outside God's will, as illustrated by the anecdote of the stolen camel.

Core Idea
If God did not will the theft, His will is undone — so prayer is futile.

This lesson presents a famous anecdote that exposes a critical flaw in the Qadari position that some events happen without God's will. A bedouin's sharp objection to a prayer reveals the logical consequences of denying God's absolute will over all things. As you read, consider how the story defends the Ash'ari view that everything occurs by divine decree.

Guided Path
1
Read the story carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation once to get the narrative flow. Notice the characters and their statements.
The story is short but carries deep theological weight.
2
Identify Amr's theological assumption
Amr ibn Ubayd was a Qadari who believed human actions are not willed by God. His prayer reflects this: 'if You did not will the theft...'
The Qadari position denies that God creates evil actions.
3
Analyze the bedouin's objection
The bedouin points out the contradiction: if God did not will the theft, yet it occurred, then God's will is not effective. So asking Him to return it is pointless.
The objection hinges on the efficacy of God's will.
4
Connect to the broader doctrine
Reflect on how this story supports the Ash'ari view that all events, including evil, occur by God's will, and that denying this leads to absurdity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويحكي أن أعرابيا سُرقت ناقته، فجئ به إلى عمرو بن عبيد القدري ليدعو له، فرفع يديه وقال: «اللهم إن ناقة هذا الأعرابي سُرقت ولم ترد سرقتها فَرُدّها عليه، فقال الأعرابي: بالله عليك يا شيخ كف عني من دعائك هذا»، قال: «ولم؟»، قال: «لأنه إذا لم يُرد سرقتها، وقد سرقت فيريدُ رَدّها ولا ترد».
It is related that a bedouin's she-camel was stolen, so he was brought to Amr ibn Ubayd al-Qadari to pray for him. He raised his hands and said: 'O God, if You did not will the theft of this bedouin's she-camel, then return it to him.' The bedouin said: 'By God, O sheikh, spare me from this prayer of yours.' He said: 'Why?' He said: 'Because if He did not will its theft, and it has been stolen, then He wills to return it and it will not be returned.'
Source pages p. 63

Commentary
This passage is an anecdote that exposes the inconsistency of the Qadari doctrine. Amr ibn Ubayd, a Qadari, is asked to pray for the return of a stolen camel. He prays, 'O God, if You did not will the theft of this camel, then return it.' The Arab objects, saying that if God did not will the theft yet it happened, then God's will is not effective, so the prayer is futile. The passage thus defends the Ash'ari view that all events occur by God's will.

Key Terms
سرقة
إرادة
قدري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some events occur outside of God's will, and prayer can change them independently of God's will.
Correction: The story shows that if an event (like theft) occurs without God's will, then God's will is impotent, making prayer futile because returning it would also be against His will.

Checkpoint
What logical flaw does the bedouin find in Amr's prayer?
A) Amr should not have prayed at all.
B) If God did not will the theft, then God cannot will its return because His will is ineffective.
C) The camel was already returned.
D) God's will is not relevant.

Key Takeaway
The anecdote demonstrates that denying God's will over all events leads to a logical contradiction, affirming the Ash'ari view that everything occurs by divine decree.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When something bad happens to you, do you ever catch yourself thinking, 'This is not God's will'? How does the bedouin's rebuttal expose the hidden danger in that thought?
?
Imagine you are the bedouin: your camel is stolen, and someone prays in a way that undermines God's will. Would your faith be shaken, or like the bedouin, would you confidently reject that prayer? What does your answer reveal about your true belief in God's absolute decree?
?
This anecdote shows that even a bedouin could see the logical flaw in denying God's will. Today, are there areas in your life where you live as if God's will is not in control—your career, relationships, or plans? What one step can you take this week to submit more fully to His decree?

The Inevitability of God's Will
Poetic lines affirming God's will is always realized.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that in Sunni theology, whatever God wills inevitably occurs, and no power can thwart His will.

Core Idea
Whatever God wills must happen; nothing escapes His decree.

This short poem from al-Iḍāʼah states a core tenet of Sunni belief: the absolute power of God's will. It affirms that God's will is always realized, even if He forbids something or human protectors fail. Let's examine the lines carefully to grasp their theological weight.

Guided Path
1
Read the poem aloud
Read the Arabic lines of poetry once without worrying about translation. Let the rhythm and repetition sink in.
Notice the rhyme and the emphatic phrasing.
2
Understand the meaning
Read the literal translation and commentary. Identify the two key assertions: (1) whatever God wills comes to be, even if He forbids it; (2) there is no escape from His will because He does as He intends.
3
Reflect on the refutation
The poem directly counters the Qadari belief that humans can act independently of God's will. Ask yourself: why would these lines be necessary to affirm?
This connects to the broader debate on free will and predestination.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال في «الإضاءة» (۱): وَكُلَّمَا أَرَادَ فَهُوَ كَائِنُ وَإِن نَهَى عَنْهُ وَأَخطَا المَائِنُ وَلَيسَ عَمَّ شَاءَهُ مَحِيدُ لأَنَّهُ يَفعَلُ مَا يُرِيدُ
He said in al-Iḍāʼah: 'And whenever He wills, it comes to be, even if He forbids it and the protector errs. There is no escape from what He wills, because He does what He intends.'
Source pages p. 63

Commentary
This passage is a poetic affirmation of divine omnipotence and the impossibility of opposing God's will. The lines state that whatever God wills comes to pass, and even if He forbids something, the one who fails (to obey) has erred. Nothing can escape what God wills, because He does what He intends. This directly refutes the Qadari claim that humans can act independently of God's will.

Key Terms
إرادة
مائِن
محيد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That God can will something but it does not occur, or that human actions can escape God's will.
Correction: The poem affirms that whatever God wills inevitably comes to pass, and nothing can evade His decree. This refutes the Qadari view that humans possess independent agency.

Checkpoint
What does the poem assert about God's will?
A) It is sometimes thwarted.
B) Whatever God wills inevitably occurs.
C) God only wills good.
D) Human will determines outcomes.

Key Takeaway
God's will is always effective; nothing in creation can oppose or evade what He intends.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that everything in your life—even your mistakes and the wrongs done to you—comes from God's will? If so, why do you still resist some of His decrees as if you could change them?
?
When you sin despite knowing God has forbidden it, do you realize that your sin is still within His will? Does that thought lead you to despair or to deeper reliance on His mercy?
?
The poem says 'there is no escape from what He wills.' Are there areas of your life where you are still trying to escape or deny God's decree? What would it look like to surrender those fully to Him today?

4 min
Qada and Qadar: The Foundation of Patience
Explains that every affair is by divine decree and that this necessitates patience.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the belief in divine decree (qada and qadar) necessitates patience and submission, and what is at stake if one fails to be patient.

Core Idea
Divine decree is the foundation for patience; submission to Allah's will is the path to safety.

This lesson explores a foundational link between Islamic theology and spiritual practice: the doctrine of qada and qadar. The author explains that every event is by Allah's decree, and this very fact makes patience an obligation. The passage from the poem and commentary shows why submission is not optional—it is the only way to attain good in both worlds.

Guided Path
1
Read the poetic verses
Read the two verses of poetry (lines 63-64) carefully. They state that every matter is by decree and every predetermined thing is inescapable, then command submission.
Notice the rhyme and rhythm; poetry aids memorization.
2
Understand the definitions
In the commentary, qada is defined as Allah's eternal will specifying occurrences, and qadar as Allah's bringing things into existence accordingly. Note how these definitions set the stage for the argument.
3
Grasp the logical connection
The commentary argues that because every event is decreed and inescapable, the morally responsible person (mukallaf) must have patience and submit. Otherwise, they lose the good of both worlds.
This is the core point: decree necessitates patience.
4
Reflect on the consequence
Ponder the warning: impatience leads to loss in this life and the next. Consider how this motivates a believer to embrace submission.
The passage ends with a note that the phrase 'every predetermined thing' completes the reasoning.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فَكُلُّ أَمْرِ بِالقَضَاءِ والقَدَرْ * وكُلُّ مَقْدُورٍ فَمَا عَنْهُ مَقَرْ | ٦٣ فكُنْ لَهُ مُسَلِّمَا كَي تَسْلَما * واتَّبَعْ سَبِيلَ النَّاسِكينَ العُلَما ٦٤ شرح الشيخ حسن محمد المشاط المكي المالكي ثم علل طلب الصبر بقوله: فكلُّ أمر أي: وإنما طلب منك الصبر لأن كلَّ أمر برز في الكائنات فهو (بالقضاء) أي: بسبب قضاء الله تعالى وعلمه. و(القدر) وهو ايجاد الله تعالى الأمور على طبق ما أَرَادَهُ وعَلِمَهُ، وكلَّ مقدور قدَّره الله تعالى وأبرزه إلى عالم الوجود، وفق علمه وقضائه، فما أي: فليس عنه مفر، ولا بد من وقوعه طبقًا لما أراده من علمه. ومعنى ذلك أنه يجب على المكلف الصبر والتسليم لما قدَّره الله العليم الحكيم، فإنه إذا لم يصبر ويستسلم لذلك، فَقَدَ خَيْرَ الدنيا والآخرة. وعلم من هذا التقرير أن قول الناظم: وكل مقدور من تمام التعليل. (1) القضاء عند الأشاعرة: إرادة الله المتعلقة أزلا بتخصيص الكائنات، ببعض ما يجوز عليها، والقدر عندهم هو إيجاد الله الأمور على طبق إرادته.
For every matter is by the decree (qada) and predestination (qadar), and every predetermined thing has no escape from it. [Verse 63] So be submissive to Him so that you may be safe, and follow the path of the devout scholars. [Verse 64] Commentary by Shaykh Hasan ibn Muhammad al-Mashshāṭ al-Makkī al-Mālikī: Then he gave the reason for requiring patience by his saying: 'For every matter' - i.e., patience is required of you only because every matter that appears in existence is by the decree, i.e., by the decree of Allah the Exalted and His knowledge. And 'predestination' is Allah's bringing things into existence in accordance with what He willed and knew. And every predetermined thing that Allah predestined and brought into the world of existence according to His knowledge and decree, has no escape from it, and it must occur in conformity with what He intended by His knowledge. The meaning of that is that it is obligatory for the morally responsible person to have patience and submission to what Allah, the All-Knowing, the Wise, has predestined. For if he does not have patience and submit to that, he loses the good of this world and the Hereafter. And it is understood from this explanation that the author's saying 'and every predetermined thing' completes the reasoning. (1) According to the Ash'aris, al-qada' is Allah's eternal will specifying occurrences with some of what is possible for them, and al-qadar is Allah's bringing things into existence in accordance with His will.
Source pages p. 112

Commentary
This passage is a proof-text linking divine decree to the necessity of patience. It begins with two verses of poetry stating that every affair is by qada and qadar, and every predetermined thing cannot be escaped. The commentary explains that Allah requires patience because all events are within His decree and predestination. Qada is defined as Allah's eternal will that specifies what occurs, and qadar is the actual creation of things in conformity with that will. Since nothing can escape what Allah has decreed, the mukallaf must patiently submit; otherwise, he loses both worldly and Hereafter benefits. The author notes that the phrase 'and every predetermined thing' completes the reasoning, emphasizing that the inevitability of decree necessitates patience.

Key Terms
القضاء
القدر
التسليم
المكلّف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Belief in qada and qadar means humans have no control and are not responsible for their actions.
Correction: The passage does not negate responsibility; rather, it teaches that because divine decree is certain, patience and submission are required. Human responsibility remains, as we are commanded to be patient.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the consequence of not being patient with divine decree?
A) The decree changes
B) One loses the good of this world and the Hereafter
C) One becomes a disbeliever
D) One is punished immediately

Key Takeaway
Belief in divine decree demands patience and submission; without it, one loses both worldly and otherworldly good.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Which event in your life right now are you struggling to accept as coming from Allah's decree? How does this lesson's command to be submissive challenge your current attitude? What would it look like for you to actively submit to that decree, and what do you fear losing if you do so?
?
The commentator says that if you do not have patience, you lose the good of this world and the Hereafter. Can you think of a specific time when impatience with Allah's decree caused you to lose something valuable? What would have been different if you had submitted?
?
Are you truly convinced that every matter is by qada and qadar, or do you sometimes act as if some things happen outside Allah's control? How does that affect your level of patience? What one step can you take today to strengthen your belief in divine decree and live out the submission this lesson demands?

Divine Decree
beginner
4 min
The Fourth Principle: Contentment (Rida) and Submission to Divine Decree
Introduces the fourth principle of rida and commands submission.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the fourth principle of contentment (rida) and the command to be submissive to Allah's decree, commands, and prohibitions.

Core Idea
Contentment with divine decree is complete submission to Allah's will.

The author now turns to the fourth of the ten principles: rida, or contentment with Allah's decree. This passage explains that the seeker must be submissive to whatever Allah has predestined, decreed, commanded, and forbidden. The word 'musalliman' with shaddah emphasizes full surrender.

Guided Path
1
Identify the fourth principle
Read the passage and note that it introduces the fourth principle: rida (contentment). The author explicitly states it.
2
Understand the command to be submissive
The phrase 'wa-kun lahu musalliman' commands the seeker to be submissive to Allah's contentment. The shaddah on the lam intensifies the meaning.
3
Recognize the scope of submission
Submission covers everything: predestination, decree, commands, and prohibitions. The passage is cut off, but the intended meaning is complete surrender.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم أشار إلى الرابع من الأصول: وهو الرّضا، بقوله: وكن) أيها الطالب (له تعالى، أي: لرضا مولاك (مسلما) بتشديد اللام المكسورة، أي: في كل ما قدره وقضاه، وأمر به ونهى عنه، بأن
Then he pointed to the fourth of the principles: it is contentment (rida), by his saying: 'And be' O seeker 'to Him the Exalted', i.e., to the contentment of your Lord, 'submissive' (musalliman) with shaddah on the kasra lam, i.e., in everything that He predestined and decreed, and commanded and forbade, by that...
Source pages p. 112

Commentary
This passage identifies the fourth principle among the ten: rida (contentment with Allah’s decree). The author explains the line 'wa-kun lahu musalliman' (and be submissive to Him) as a command to the seeker to be content with what Allah has predestined and decreed, as well as with His commands and prohibitions. The word 'musalliman' is emphasized with a shaddah on the lam, indicating complete submission. The passage is cut off at 'bi-an' (by that), but the intent is clear: one must submit fully to Allah’s will.

Key Terms
الرضا
التسليم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What is the fourth principle mentioned in this passage?
A) Patience (sabr)
B) Contentment (rida)
C) Gratitude (shukr)
D) Trust (tawakkul)

Key Takeaway
The fourth principle of rida commands the seeker to be completely submissive to Allah's decree, commands, and prohibitions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Are there areas in your life where you resist Allah's decree or His commands? What would it take for you to truly be musalliman in those areas?
?
When you face a hardship, does your heart immediately submit or does it question? How can you train yourself to have rida even when it is difficult?
?
What is preventing you from full submission to Allah's commands and prohibitions? What concrete step will you take today to increase your taslim?

Divine Decree
intermediate
5 min
Understanding Qada, Qadar, and the Obligation of Patience
al-Mashat explains qada and qadar and the obligation of patience for verse 63.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the distinction between qada and qadar according to the Ash'aris and why patience and submission to Allah's decree are obligatory.

Core Idea
Qada is Allah's eternal decree; qadar is His bringing it into existence; submission is the believer's duty.

In this passage, al-Mashat unpacks a key verse from the poem: every event is by qada and qadar. He clarifies the precise meanings of these two terms and derives a powerful practical conclusion: patience and submission are obligatory. Let's walk through his explanation step by step.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the Arabic passage once to get a feel for the language. Then read the full English translation to grasp the overall argument.
Notice how the footnote provides technical Ash'ari definitions.
2
Identify the two definitions
Locate where the commentator defines qada and qadar. Write down each definition in your own words.
3
Understand the Ash'ari terms
The footnote gives the technical Ash'ari definitions. Compare them with the earlier definitions to see how they align.
4
Reflect on the obligation of patience
Consider why the commentator says patience is obligatory. What does losing the good of both worlds mean? Think about how this applies to daily life.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فكل أمر أي: وإنما طلب منك القضاء والقدر الصبر لأن كل أمر برز في الكائنات فهو (بالقضاء) أي: بسبب قضاء الله تعالى وعلمه. (و) بسبب (القدر) وهو ايجاد الله تعالى الأمور على طبق ما أَرَادَهُ وَعَلِمَهُ، وكل مقدور قدره الله تعالى وأبرزه إلى عالم الوجود، وفق علمه وقضائه، ( فما ) أي: فليس عنه مفر)، ولا بد من وقوعه طبقًا لما أراده من علمه (۱). ومعنى ذلك أنه يجب على المكلف الصبر والتسليم لما قدره الله العليم الحكيم، فإنه إذا لم يصبر ويستسلم لذلك، فَقَدَ خَيْرَ الدنيا والآخرة. وعلم من هذا التقرير أن قول الناظم: «وكل مقدور» من تمام التعليل.
(۱) القضاء عند الأشاعرة: إرادة الله المتعلقة أزلا بتخصيص الكائنات، ببعض ما يجوز عليها، والقدر عندهم هو إيجاد الله الأمور على طبق إرادته.
So every matter—that is, patience is demanded from you regarding decree and predestination, because every matter that appears in the cosmos is by decree, meaning: due to the decree of Allah Most High and His knowledge, and by predestination, which is Allah's bringing into existence of things in accordance with what He willed and knew. And every predestined thing that Allah has decreed and brought forth into the world of existence, according to His knowledge and decree, there is no escape from it, and it must occur in accordance with what He willed from His knowledge. And the meaning of that is that it is obligatory for the responsible person to be patient and submit to what Allah, the All-Knowing, the Wise, has decreed, for if he does not be patient and submit to that, he loses the good of this world and the Hereafter. And it is understood from this explanation that the poet's saying "and every predestined thing" is part of the complete reason. (1) Al-qada according to the Ash'aris is Allah's eternal will pertaining to the specification of things, with some of what is possible for them; and al-qadar according to them is Allah's bringing things into existence in accordance with His will.
Source pages p. 112

Commentary
This passage is an explanation of the first part of verse 63 of the poem, which states that every matter is by qada and qadar. The commentator (al-Mashat) begins by interpreting the line: 'So every matter' refers to the demand for patience because every event that occurs is by qada (decree) and qadar (predestination). He defines qada as Allah's decree based on His knowledge, and qadar as Allah's creation of things in accordance with His will and knowledge. He then emphasizes that nothing can escape Allah's decree; it must come to pass. The logical conclusion is that the morally responsible person (mukallaf) is obligated to be patient and submit to Allah's decree; failure to do so leads to loss in both worlds. A footnote clarifies the Ash'ari technical definitions: qada is Allah's eternal will specifying which possibilities occur, and qadar is the bringing of those things into existence accordingly. Finally, the commentator notes that the poet's phrase 'and every predestined thing' completes the justification for patience, not a separate statement.

Key Terms
القضاء
القدر
الصبر
الأشاعرة
التسليم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think qada and qadar mean the same thing.
Correction: The passage distinguishes them: qada is Allah's decree (based on knowledge), qadar is the creation (bringing into existence) in accordance with that decree.

Checkpoint
According to the commentary, what is the consequence for a person who does not practice patience and submission to Allah's decree?
A) He will be punished in the afterlife only
B) He loses the good of both this world and the Hereafter
C) He will be forgiven if he repents
D) His deeds become invalid

Key Takeaway
Qada is Allah's eternal will specifying what will be; qadar is His creation of those things; believers must patiently submit to both.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When a calamity strikes, do you truly believe that there is no escape from Allah's decree? Examine your heart: do you accept it with patience and submission, or do you feel resentment? What step can you take today to strengthen your taslim?
?
If you knew with certainty that losing patience over a trial would cause you to lose the good of both worlds, would you still allow yourself to complain? How can you train your heart to see every trial as a test from the All-Wise?
?
Think of a recent moment when you felt things were out of your control. Did you respond with sabr and trust in Allah's qada and qadar, or did you try to escape through worry and frustration? Commit to one specific action the next time you face such a situation.
